The **friction** between the tool and the workpiece generates the heat, while the **rotation + translation** causes the plasticized material to flow around the pin and consolidate under the **shoulder's forging pressure**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Typical Range (Aluminum Alloys) | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotational Speed (ω)** | **300 – 3000** | **rpm** |
| **Traverse / Welding Speed (v)** | **30 – 2000** | **mm/min** |
| **Axial Force (F)** | **5 – 50** | **kN** |
| **Tilt Angle (θ)** | **1° – 5°** | **degrees** |
| **Tool Material Hardness** | **HRC 55 – 65** | **Rockwell C** |
| **Joint Efficiency (Al 2xxx/6xxx/7xxx)** | **75 % – 95 %** | **% of base metal UTS** |
| **Process Temperature** | **0.6 – 0.95 × T_m** | **°C (homologous)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is FSW called "GREEN WELDING"?**
> Because it produces **no fumes, no spatter, no UV radiation, no shielding gas (for most alloys), and no consumable filler wire**. Energy consumption is typically **2.5 % to 5 %** of that required for equivalent laser or arc welding processes, making it one of the most environmentally compliant joining technologies endorsed under sustainable manufacturing KPIs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The FSW process is executed in **four discrete stages**:

1. **Clamping Stage**
   The two workpieces are rigidly clamped to a backing plate/table to prevent lateral displacement and lifting of the plasticized material during welding. Poor clamping produces flash defects or excessive thinning.

2. **Plunge Stage**
   The rotating tool is brought down into the joint line. The **shoulder** contacts the top surface while the **pin (probe)** penetrates the joint. Axial force is increased until the shoulder bears against the workpiece surface, generating frictional heat.

3. **Dwell / Pre-Heat Stage**
   The tool is held stationary (or oscillated slightly) for **2 – 10 seconds** to soften a small volume of material around the pin. This ensures the tool does not cold-plunge and bind.

4. **Traverse (Welding) Stage**
   The tool is traversed along the joint line at the prescribed welding speed. Material flows:
   - **Advancing Side (AS):** side where tool rotation and traverse direction are in agreement.
   - **Retreating Side (RS):** side where rotation and traverse directions oppose each other.
   The plasticized material is transported from the **leading edge** to the **trailing edge** behind the pin and forged under the shoulder.

## 2.2 Tool Design — The "Heart" of FSW

The tool has **two functional components**:

- **Shoulder (Diameter 10 – 30 mm):** Generates most of the frictional heat (~80 %) and provides forging pressure to consolidate the joint.
- **Pin / Probe (Length = plate thickness, Diameter 3 – 10 mm):** Stirs and mixes the plasticized material along the joint line.

Common pin geometries include:
- **Cylindrical** (simplest, low mixing)
- **Threaded** (improves vertical material flow)
- **Fluted / Conical** (better stirring, common in industrial use)
- **Whorl™ / MX-Triflute™** (TWI patented, high-efficiency)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All equations below are universally tested in KTU Module-1 numerical/derivation questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi \, \mu \, P \, \omega \, R^{3}$ | Total heat generated by shoulder friction (Schmidt & Hattel, 2004) | **W (Watts)** |
| 2 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Spindle power required to drive the tool | **W** |
| 3 | $H = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{v}$ | **Heat input per unit weld length** (most tested) | **J/mm** |
| 4 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\pi \, \omega \, R_{p} \, \sin\theta}{L_{p}}$ | Equivalent plastic strain rate around the pin | **s⁻¹** |
| 5 | $\dfrac{T}{T_{m}} = \dfrac{\alpha \, \omega}{v^{\beta}}$ | Peak temperature ratio (empirical) | **dimensionless** |
| 6 | $F_{axial} = k_{f} \cdot A_{shoulder} \cdot p_{flow}$ | Axial force = shoulder area × flow stress | **N** |
| 7 | $\eta_{joint} = \dfrac{UTS_{weld}}{UTS_{base}} \times 100\,\%$ | Joint efficiency (mechanical property metric) | **%** |

**Where:**
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (≈ 0.4 – 0.6 for steel-on-aluminum at hot conditions)
- $P$ = axial pressure (Pa)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
- $R$ = shoulder radius (m)
- $N$ = rotational speed (rpm)
- $T$ = spindle torque (N·m)
- $v$ = traverse speed (mm/s)
- $R_p$, $L_p$ = pin radius and length
- $\theta$ = tool tilt angle
- $\alpha$, $\beta$ = empirical constants (material dependent)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Single-piece wing skin panels of the **Boeing 747-8**, **Airbus A380**, and **Falcon 9** payload fairings use FSW.
- **Shipbuilding:** Aluminum deck panels for naval frigates (e.g., LCS Independence class).
- **Railways:** High-speed train body shells (Shinkansen, ICE-3) — panels up to **15 m long**.
- **Automotive:** EV battery trays, rear-axle housings (Tesla, Ford F-150).
- **Nuclear & Cryogenic:** Aluminum-Lithium alloy tank welding for space launch vehicles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is it ideal for Aluminum?** Because aluminum has a high thermal conductivity and a narrow solidification range, fusion welding causes porosity and hot-cracking. FSW bypasses melting entirely, eliminating both defects and producing a **fine, recrystallized grain structure** with superior fatigue life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Input per Unit Weld Length

The most frequently asked KTU numerical problem. We will derive it from first principles.

**Given:**
- Spindle torque $T$ (N·m)
- Rotational speed $N$ (rpm)
- Welding speed $v$ (mm/s)

**Step 1 — Convert rotational speed to rad/s**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad [\text{rad/s}]
$$

**Step 2 — Express the mechanical power delivered to the tool**

The torque $T$ acting at angular velocity $\omega$ delivers a power equal to the product:

$$
P_{shaft} = T \cdot \omega
$$

Substituting $\omega$:

$$
P_{shaft} = T \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}
$$

The factor $2 \pi / 60$ comes from converting one revolution ($2\pi$ radians) per minute to radians per second. Numerically, dividing by 60 scales the result from per-minute to per-second basis.

**Step 3 — Define heat input per unit length**

Heat input per unit weld length is the energy supplied per millimeter of weld traversed. It is obtained by dividing the total power by the traverse speed:

$$
H = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{v}
$$

Substituting $P_{shaft}$:

$$
H = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60 \, v}
$$

**Step 4 — Units consistency check**

- $N$ is in rpm = rev/min
- $T$ is in N·m
- $v$ is in mm/s

Therefore:

$$
H = \dfrac{[\text{N·m}]}{[\text{mm/s}]} = \dfrac{\text{N·m·s}}{\text{mm}} = \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory trick:** "Power in W divided by speed in mm/s = J per mm." This is the format you must write on the answer script for the **2-mark derivation** sub-question.

### 3.1.1 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** An FSW tool rotates at **800 rpm** under a spindle torque of **25 N·m**. The welding speed is **60 mm/min**. Calculate the heat input per unit weld length.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Convert welding speed to mm/s**

$$
v = \dfrac{60}{60} = 1.0 \;\; \text{mm/s}
$$

**Step B — Compute spindle power**

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 800 \times 25}{60}
$$

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 20000}{60} = \dfrac{125664}{60}
$$

$$
P_{shaft} \approx 2094.4 \;\; \text{W}
$$

**Step C — Compute heat input per unit length**

$$
H = \dfrac{2094.4}{1.0} = 2094.4 \;\; \text{J/mm}
$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{H \approx 2.09 \; \text{kJ/mm}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Strain Rate at the Pin Surface

Material flow in FSW is analogous to a Couette flow field. The tangential velocity at the pin surface equals $v_t = \omega R_p$. The vertical velocity gradient across the pin length $L_p$, with a tilt $\theta$, gives:

**Step 1 — Tangential velocity at pin surface**

$$
v_{t} = \omega \, R_{p}
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity gradient perpendicular to the pin axis**

$$
\dfrac{\partial v_{t}}{\partial y} = \dfrac{v_{t} \sin\theta}{L_{p}}
$$

The sine term accounts for the tool tilt, projecting the tangential velocity onto the vertical direction.

**Step 3 — Equivalent strain rate**

$$
\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\pi \, \omega \, R_{p} \, \sin\theta}{L_{p}}
$$

The factor $\pi$ arises from averaging the velocity field over a full rotation. This quantity is critical because it governs the **dynamic recrystallization** that produces the fine stir-zone grain structure.

### 3.2.1 Microstructural Zones (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the "must-memorize" matrix for the 14-mark descriptive question.**

| Zone | Symbol | Peak Temperature | Microstructure | Mechanical Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stir Zone (Nugget)** | SZ / NZ | **0.8 – 0.95 T_m** | Fine equiaxed dynamically recrystallized grains | **Highest hardness peak, ductile** |
| **Thermo-Mechanically Affected Zone** | TMAZ | **0.5 – 0.8 T_m** | Deformed, partially recrystallized, elongated grains | Moderate hardness, low ductility |
| **Heat Affected Zone** | HAZ | **0.3 – 0.5 T_m** | Coarsened grains, no plastic deformation | **Lowest hardness valley** |
| **Base Material** | BM | Room temperature | Original wrought/rolled structure | Reference properties |

**Why is the HAZ softer than the Nugget in age-hardenable Al alloys (e.g., 6061-T6, 7075-T6)?**
- In the **Nugget**, dynamic recrystallization produces a new, fine grain structure.
- In the **HAZ**, the temperature dissolves the strengthening precipitates (e.g., $\text{Mg}_2\text{Si}$ in 6xxx, $\eta'$ in 7xxx) without recrystallizing the grains. On natural aging, strength partially recovers — this is called the **"HAZ softening phenomenon"**, and is a classic FSW metallurgy viva question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Peak Temperature Empirical Model

A common KTU question asks to relate peak temperature to process parameters. Using the Reynolds-analogy-based energy balance at the tool-workpiece interface, one obtains:

$$
\dfrac{T_{peak}}{T_{m}} = K \left(\dfrac{\omega}{v}\right)^{\beta}
$$

**Step 1 — Define the heat generation rate per unit length**

$$
\dot{q}_{gen} = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi \mu P \omega R^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the heat loss rate per unit length (conduction into substrate)**

$$
\dot{q}_{loss} = k_{eff} \, (T_{peak} - T_{0}) \cdot A_{interface}
$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state balance**

$$
\dot{q}_{gen} = \dot{q}_{loss}
$$

After substituting the effective thermal conductivity and rearranging:

$$
T_{peak} \approx T_{0} + \dfrac{2 \pi \mu P \omega R^{3}}{3 k_{eff} A_{interface}}
$$

Dividing by $T_m$ and grouping empirical constants:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{T_{peak}}{T_{m}} = \alpha \left(\dfrac{\omega}{v}\right)^{\beta}}
$$

This explains the **"welding parameter map"**: high $\omega/v$ ratio → high temperature → coarse grains, low $\omega/v$ ratio → cold weld → tunnel defect.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — FSW Process Setup (Functional Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Clamping Fixture] --> nodeB[Workpiece Plate A]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Workpiece Plate B]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Rotating FSW Tool]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Tool Shoulder]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Tool Pin Probe]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Frictional Heat Zone]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Plasticized Material Flow]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Stir Zone SZ]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[TMAZ Layer]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[HAZ Layer]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Base Metal BM]
    nodeD --> nodeM[Spindle Drive Motor]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Controller and DAQ]
```

## 4.2 Diagram B — Microstructural Zones Across FSW Weld Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeZ1[Advancing Side AS] --> nodeZ2[Base Metal BM]
    nodeZ2 --> nodeZ3[Heat Affected Zone HAZ]
    nodeZ3 --> nodeZ4[Thermo-Mechanically Affected Zone TMAZ]
    nodeZ4 --> nodeZ5[Stir Zone Nugget SZ NZ]
    nodeZ5 --> nodeZ6[Thermo-Mechanically Affected Zone TMAZ]
    nodeZ6 --> nodeZ7[Heat Affected Zone HAZ]
    nodeZ7 --> nodeZ8[Base Metal BM]
    nodeZ8 --> nodeZ9[Retreating Side RS]
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — Material Flow Pattern Around the FSW Pin

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF1[Leading Edge of Pin] --> nodeF2[Material Picked Up by Rotating Pin]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3[Material Transported Around Pin Periphery]
    nodeF3 --> nodeF4[Material Deposited at Trailing Edge]
    nodeF4 --> nodeF5[Forged Under Shoulder Pressure]
    nodeF5 --> nodeF6[Solidified Weld Nugget]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF7[Advancing Side AS Material Stream]
    nodeF7 --> nodeF4
```

## 4.4 Diagram D — Defect Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeD1[FSW Defects] --> nodeD2[Internal Defects]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD3[External Defects]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD4[Tunnel Defect Wormhole]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD5[Kissing Bond Lazy S]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD6[Root Flaw]
    nodeD3 --> nodeD7[Flash Egress]
    nodeD3 --> nodeD8[Surface Groove]
    nodeD3 --> nodeD9[Exit Hole Pin Withdrawal]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual interpretation tip for the KTU diagram question:**
> - **Onion Rings** observed on the nugget surface (top view) are concentric bands of alternating grain size caused by the layered material deposition per rotation. This is a **hallmark of a sound FSW weld**.
> - The **Advancing Side (AS)** typically has a sharper TMAZ/SZ boundary than the **Retreating Side (RS)** because deformation is cumulative on the AS.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Q: Define Friction Stir Welding. List any four of its advantages over conventional fusion welding processes.**

**Model Answer (expected length: 8 – 10 lines):**

Friction Stir Welding is a **solid-state joining process** in which a non-consumable rotating tool with a shoulder and pin is traversed along the joint line of two clamped workpieces. The frictional heat generated softens the material below its melting point, and the rotating pin stirs and forges the plasticized metal to form a metallurgical bond.

**Advantages over fusion welding:**
1. **No melting** → eliminates solidification cracking, porosity, and slag inclusions.
2. **No shielding gas or filler wire** required for most non-ferrous alloys.
3. **Lower distortion** due to lower heat input (0.6 – 0.95 T_m vs. above T_m).
4. **Better mechanical properties** — joint efficiency of **75 – 95 %** of base metal UTS.
5. **Eco-friendly** — no fumes, UV radiation, or spatter.
6. **Easily automatable** for robotic production lines.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between the Advancing Side (AS) and Retreating Side (RS) in FSW. Why is the AS more prone to defects?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Advancing Side (AS) | Retreating Side (RS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Velocity vector relation** | Tool rotation **agrees** with traverse direction | Tool rotation **opposes** traverse direction |
| **Material flow** | Cumulative plastic strain | Partially reversed flow |
| **TMAZ boundary** | Sharper, well-defined | Diffuse, gradual |
| **Defect susceptibility** | **Higher** (tunnel, lazy S) | Lower |

**Why AS is more defect-prone:** On the AS, the material is being pulled forward by both rotation and translation, creating a **velocity discontinuity** at the TMAZ/SZ interface. This causes **incomplete filling** at the trailing edge of the pin, producing tunnel or wormhole defects. The RS experiences more balanced, lower-relative-velocity flow, allowing better consolidation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU 2024 — Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Describe in detail the Friction Stir Welding process with a neat schematic. Explain the role of the tool shoulder and pin, and discuss the effect of the four primary process parameters on weld quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the various microstructural zones produced in an FSW joint of an age-hardened aluminum alloy (e.g., AA6061-T6). Why does the HAZ exhibit lower hardness than the stir zone in such alloys? **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question A

#### (a) FSW Process Description, Tool Role & Parameter Effects **[7 marks]**

**Process Description (3 marks):**
FSW uses a non-consumable, hard-tool-steel or tungsten-alloy rotating tool consisting of a **shoulder** and a **pin (probe)**. The workpieces are clamped rigidly onto a backing plate. The rotating tool is plunged into the joint line such that the shoulder contacts the top surface and the pin penetrates through the plate thickness. Frictional heat from the shoulder and pin softens the metal (without melting). The tool is then traversed along the joint. The rotating pin **stirs** the plasticized material from the leading edge to the trailing edge, and the **shoulder forges** the deposited material, producing a solid-state joint.

**Role of Shoulder and Pin (2 marks):**
- **Shoulder:** Generates **~80 % of total heat** through friction with the top surface; also provides the **axial forging pressure** that consolidates the weld and prevents flash.
- **Pin:** Generates ~20 % of heat, but is responsible for **material stirring, mixing, and vertical transport**. The pin geometry (threaded, fluted, tapered) determines flow efficiency.

**Effect of Four Process Parameters (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Effect on Weld |
|---|---|
| **Rotational Speed (ω) ↑** | Higher heat input, coarser grains, possible flash |
| **Welding Speed (v) ↑** | Lower heat input, finer grains, risk of tunnel defect |
| **Axial Force (F) ↑** | Better consolidation, but excess thinning and flash |
| **Tilt Angle (θ) ↑** | Improves forging, vertical flow; too high causes surface galling |

**Optimum Window:** $\omega / v$ ratio between **10 – 50 rev/mm** is typical for AA6xxx series.

#### (b) Microstructural Zones & HAZ Softening **[7 marks]**

**Schematic and Description (4 marks):**
Refer to **Diagram B in Section 4** for the four-zone cross-section.

- **Base Metal (BM):** Original wrought/rolled structure; reference hardness.
- **HAZ:** Heated to 0.3 – 0.5 T_m; **no plastic deformation**, only thermal exposure.
- **TMAZ:** Heated to 0.5 – 0.8 T_m; **plastically deformed but not fully recrystallized**; elongated, bent grains.
- **Stir Zone (Nugget):** Heated to 0.8 – 0.95 T_m; undergoes **dynamic recrystallization** (DRX), producing a **fine equiaxed grain** structure, often showing concentric **onion rings** on the top surface.

**Hardness Profile:** Increases from BM → HAZ (slight drop due to precipitate dissolution) → TMAZ (moderate) → SZ (recover to near-BM values).

**HAZ Softening Explanation (3 marks):**
In age-hardened Al alloys like **AA6061-T6**, the strength comes from fine metastable precipitates (e.g., $\beta''$ phase: $\text{Mg}_2\text{Si}$ needles). The FSW thermal cycle **dissolves these precipitates** in the HAZ. Since the temperature is below the recrystallization threshold, no new fine grains form. After cooling, natural aging partially re-precipitates these phases, but the **coarsened grains + fewer precipitates** in the HAZ result in a **hardness valley** of typically **15 – 25 % below** the base metal hardness. This is the **"HAZ softening zone,"** the weakest link in the joint and the preferred crack initiation site under fatigue loading.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**Q: (a)** Classify and explain the common defects encountered in Friction Stir Welding. How do the process parameters $\omega$, $v$, and axial force influence defect formation? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for heat input per unit weld length in FSW. An FSW tool operates at 1200 rpm under a torque of 18 N·m. The welding speed is 90 mm/min. Calculate the heat input per unit length. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question B

#### (a) Defects in FSW & Parameter Influence **[7 marks]**

**Classification (3 marks):**

**Internal Defects:**
1. **Tunnel / Wormhole Defect:** Continuous longitudinal cavity on the advancing side, caused by **insufficient heat input** (low $\omega$, high $v$, low axial force).
2. **Kissing Bond (Lazy S / Zig-Zag):** A zero-bond oxide-line defect along the joint interface due to **incomplete material mixing**, often invisible on the surface. Detected by ultrasonic or radiographic NDT.
3. **Root Flaw:** Cavity at the bottom of the weld, caused by **insufficient pin length** or poor backing-plate thermal contact.

**External Defects:**
4. **Flash:** Excess material ejected as a thin lip on the retreating side, caused by **excessive heat input or axial force**.
5. **Surface Groove:** A longitudinal depression on the top surface from **shoulder-material separation** at low axial force.
6. **Exit Pin-Hole:** A hole left at the end of the weld when the tool is withdrawn — mitigated by **"piston-out" exit hole closing** or use of a **retractable pin tool**.

**Parameter Influence Matrix (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Increase Effect | Decrease Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotational Speed (ω) ↑** | More heat, finer grains, **flash** if too high | Cold weld, tunnel defect |
| **Welding Speed (v) ↑** | Lower heat, **tunnel/kissing bond** if too high | Excess heat, grain coarsening, flash |
| **Axial Force (F) ↑** | Better consolidation, **flash + thinning** if too high | Insufficient forging, kissing bond, surface groove |

**Process Map Concept:** A 2D map of $\omega$ vs. $v$ reveals a "**defect envelope**" outside which tunnel/flash defects dominate, and an "**optimum window**" inside which sound welds are produced.

#### (b) Heat Input Derivation & Numerical **[7 marks]**

**Derivation (4 marks):** [Refer to full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes]

Recap the key steps:

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}
$$

$$
P_{shaft} = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}
$$

$$
\boxed{H = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{v} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60 \, v}}
$$

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

**Given:**
- $N = 1200$ rpm
- $T = 18$ N·m
- $v = 90$ mm/min $= 1.5$ mm/s

**Step 1 — Convert welding speed**

$$
v = \dfrac{90}{60} = 1.5 \;\; \text{mm/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute power**

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1200 \times 18}{60} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 21600}{60}
$$

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{135716.8}{60} \approx 2261.95 \;\; \text{W}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute heat input per unit length**

$$
H = \dfrac{2261.95}{1.5} \approx 1507.97 \;\; \text{J/mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{H \approx 1.51 \; \text{kJ/mm}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert $v$ from mm/min to mm/s is the **#1 reason** for full-mark loss in numerical answers. Always write the unit conversion step **explicitly on the answer script**.
> 2. **Confusion between AS and RS:** Many students interchange advancing and retreating sides. Remember: **A**dvancing side is where rotation **A**ids the travel direction.
> 3. **Calling FSW a fusion process:** Never say "the metal melts" in FSW. It is a **solid-state** process. Saying "melting" will cost you **1 – 2 marks** in any KTU answer.
> 4. **Skipping the HAZ softening explanation:** A question on microstructural zones **without** explaining why HAZ is weaker is incomplete. Examiners allocate **2 – 3 marks** specifically for the precipitation-dissolution mechanism in age-hardenable alloys.
> 5. **Drawing tool without labelling shoulder and pin:** Always label both in any diagram. A schematic without labelled components will lose **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] FSW is a **solid-state** joining process invented at **TWI, UK, in 1991**.
- [x] **No melting** → no fusion-related defects (porosity, solidification cracks, slag).
- [x] Tool has two parts: **Shoulder** (~80 % heat, forging) and **Pin** (~20 % heat, stirring).
- [x] Process temperature: **0.6 – 0.95 × T_m** (homologous).
- [x] Four microstructural zones: **BM → HAZ → TMAZ → SZ (Nugget)**.
- [x] **HAZ softening** is the weakest zone in age-hardenable Al alloys (6061-T6, 7075-T6).
- [x] **Advancing Side (AS)** is more defect-prone than Retreating Side (RS).
- [x] **Tunnel / wormhole** = low heat input (low $\omega$, high $v$).
- [x] **Flash** = high heat input (high $\omega$, high $F_{axial}$).
- [x] **Onion rings** = hallmark of a sound weld (top surface).
- [x] **Heat input formula:** $H = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60 v}$ → J/mm.
- [x] **Strain rate formula:** $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\pi \omega R_p \sin\theta}{L_p}$.
- [x] **Joint efficiency:** 75 – 95 % of base metal UTS for Al alloys.
- [x] **Applications:** Boeing, Airbus, Falcon 9, Tesla, Shinkansen, naval frigates.
- [x] FSW is also called **"Green Welding"** — no fumes, no UV, no shielding gas, low energy.
- [x] **Process parameter window:** $\omega/v$ ratio of **10 – 50 rev/mm** for AA6xxx series.
- [x] **HSLA steel and titanium** can also be FSW'd, but require **PCBN or W-Re tools** due to high temperatures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inertia Welding - Working Principle and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inertia Welding - Working Principle and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Inertia Welding** (also known as **Inertia Friction Welding** or **Flywheel Friction Welding**) is a solid-state welding process in which all the heat required for joining is generated by converting the **kinetic energy stored in a rotating flywheel-mass system** into frictional heat at the interface of two workpieces, while simultaneously applying an axial compressive force. The process is a variant of rotary friction welding governed by the principle of **conservation of angular momentum and energy dissipation through friction**.

The process is characterized by the fact that the **heat input is pre-determined by the energy stored in the flywheel**, and the **axial pressure is applied throughout the cycle**, making it a controlled, repeatable, and machine-parameter-driven process rather than a time-driven process (as in conventional friction welding).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Spinning Clay-Pot Maker's Wheel**
> Imagine a potter's wheel (the flywheel) being spun up to a high speed and then a piece of soft clay (workpiece 1) is pressed against a stationary piece (workpiece 2). The wheel does not have a continuous motor feeding it — it only has a fixed amount of rotational energy. As friction acts, the wheel slows down (deceleration), the interface heats up and the clay softens. When the wheel finally stops, the potter applies a firm push (forging force) to forge the two pieces together. **The energy budget for the weld is "pre-loaded" into the flywheel — exactly like inertia welding.**

### 1.3 Key Process Metrics (KTU Standard Notation)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flywheel Moment of Inertia | $I$ | $0.01 - 5.0$ | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$ |
| Spindle Rotational Speed | $N$ | $500 - 10{,}000$ | $\text{rpm}$ |
| Angular Velocity | $\omega$ | $52 - 1047$ | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| Kinetic Energy Stored | $E_k$ | $5 - 250$ | $\text{kJ}$ |
| Axial Forging Pressure | $P_f$ | $30 - 300$ | $\text{MPa}$ |
| Friction Pressure | $P_{fr}$ | $20 - 150$ | $\text{MPa}$ |

> [!TIP]
> The **stored kinetic energy** is the master variable in inertia welding. Once the flywheel is accelerated, the weld is essentially **"pre-paid"** — the joint quality depends on how this finite energy budget is spent at the rubbing interface.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Principle — The "Why" Behind Inertia Welding

The process rests on three coupled physical phenomena occurring at the weld interface:

1. **Frictional Heat Generation** — The relative motion between a rotating and a stationary part generates frictional heat:
   $$Q_f = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot v_{rel} \cdot t$$
   where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction, $F_n$ is the normal (axial) force, $v_{rel}$ is the relative sliding velocity, and $t$ is the friction time.

2. **Conversion of Mechanical Energy to Heat** — The flywheel's stored rotational kinetic energy is irreversibly converted into heat at the interface, governed by the **First Law of Thermodynamics**:
   $$E_k = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2 = \int_0^{t_s} \tau_f \cdot \omega(t) \, dt$$
   where $\tau_f$ is the frictional torque and $t_s$ is the stopping time of the flywheel.

3. **Thermomechanical Bonding** — At temperatures approaching (but not reaching) the melting point, the material at the interface undergoes **thermo-plastic deformation** under axial pressure, forming a metallurgical bond via dynamic recrystallization and grain refinement.

### 2.2 The Four Distinct Phases of Inertia Welding

> [!NOTE]
> Understanding these four phases is **critical for KTU Module 1** — exam questions frequently test the identification and thermal/mechanical characterization of each phase.

- **Phase 1 — Initial Contact (Friction Phase, $t_1$)**
  - The rotating part approaches the stationary part at low speed.
  - Asperities at the surface contact, generating high local pressure and frictional heat.
  - Surface contaminants, oxides, and asperities are expelled as flash.
  - Temperature rises rapidly at the interface.

- **Phase 2 — Steady-State Friction ($t_2$)**
  - A thin layer of plasticized material (the "heat-affected zone" of the weld) is established.
  - Frictional torque reaches a near-constant value.
  - Heat generation rate equals heat conduction rate to the bulk of the workpieces.
  - Flywheel speed is still approximately constant.

- **Phase 3 — Deceleration Phase ($t_3$)**
  - The flywheel begins to slow significantly as kinetic energy is consumed.
  - The frictional torque continues but at decreasing $\omega$.
  - A **flash ring** (upset metal) forms around the interface.
  - This is the **critical phase** that determines weld microstructure.

- **Phase 4 — Forging/Up-Set Phase ($t_4$)**
  - The flywheel essentially stops ($\omega \to 0$).
  - The axial pressure is sharply increased (typically **2× to 3×** the friction pressure).
  - The plasticized material is consolidated, voids are closed, and the final weld is formed.
  - The forging force is what actually **"wins" the metallurgical bond**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stored Kinetic Energy | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | Total energy available for welding | $\text{J}$ |
| Angular Velocity Conversion | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Convert rpm to rad/s | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| Linear Surface Velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Rubbing velocity at the joint interface | $\text{m/s}$ |
| Friction Power (instantaneous) | $P = \tau_f \cdot \omega$ | Rate of heat generation | $\text{W}$ |
| Total Heat Generated | $Q = \int \tau_f \cdot \omega \, dt = \tfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_i^2 - \omega_f^2)$ | Energy dissipated as heat | $\text{J}$ |
| Burn-Off (Axial Shortening) | $\Delta L = \int v_{burn}(t) \, dt$ | Total material upset at the joint | $\text{mm}$ |
| Forging Pressure Ratio | $R_f = \dfrac{P_f}{P_{fr}}$ | Upset-to-friction pressure (typically 2–3) | dimensionless |
| Flash Ratio (Quality Index) | $K_{flash} = \dfrac{\text{Flash Volume}}{\text{Burn-off Volume}}$ | Indicator of weld completeness | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing $v_{rel}$ or absolute values in the KTU answer script, **never** use the pipe character `|v|` inside a markdown table — it will break the table rendering. Always use $\vert v \vert$ in handwritten/math-typed equations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inertia welding is the **workhorse process for dissimilar-metal joints** in safety-critical applications:
- **Automotive**: Homokinetic (CV) drive shafts, axle stubs — joining medium-carbon steel to low-alloy steel.
- **Aerospace**: Jet engine shafts, landing gear pins, turbine disc-hub assemblies.
- **Oil & Gas**: Sucker rods, drill-collar assemblies, tool joints.
- **Power Generation**: Rotor shafts for steam/gas turbines.
- **Tooling**: Bimetallic cutting tools (HSS tips on carbon-steel shanks).

The process is **uniquely capable of joining materials with vastly different melting points** (e.g., aluminum to steel, copper to stainless steel) because the interface never reaches the liquidus — it stays in the **thermo-plastic regime**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Kinetic Energy Stored in the Flywheel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The flywheel is the **energy reservoir** in inertia welding. The total energy "budget" of the weld is set before the welding cycle begins — this is what makes inertia welding a **"pre-set" process**.

**Step 1 — Define the kinetic energy of a rotating rigid body.**

The kinetic energy of a body rotating about a fixed axis with angular velocity $\omega$ is the integral of the elemental kinetic energies of all its mass particles:

$$E_k = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2} m_i v_i^2$$

Each particle at radius $r_i$ has linear velocity $v_i = \omega r_i$, therefore:

$$E_k = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2} m_i (\omega r_i)^2 = \frac{\omega^2}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i^2$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the moment of inertia.**

By definition, the moment of inertia of a body about its axis of rotation is:

$$I = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i^2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and finalize.**

Substituting the definition of $I$ back into the energy expression:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

**Step 4 — Convert to practical engineering units.**

Substituting $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} I \left( \frac{2\pi N}{60} \right)^2 = \frac{I \pi^2 N^2}{1800}$$

The conversion $1 \text{ rad/s} = \dfrac{60}{2\pi} \text{ rpm}$ was used.

### 3.2 Derivation: Energy Balance at the Weld Interface

**Step 1 — Initial state.**
Flywheel is accelerated to angular velocity $\omega_0$ with kinetic energy $E_0 = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_0^2$.

**Step 2 — During friction.**
The frictional torque $\tau_f$ acts at the interface. By the **work–energy theorem for rotation**:

$$\tau_f \cdot \theta = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_0^2 - \omega_f^2)$$

where $\theta$ is the total angle rotated during deceleration and $\omega_f$ is the final angular velocity (≈ 0 in true inertia welding).

**Step 3 — Final state (flywheel stops).**
If the flywheel is allowed to come to a complete rest ($\omega_f = 0$):

$$E_{dissipated} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_0^2 = \tau_f \cdot \theta_{total}$$

**Step 4 — Partition of dissipated energy.**
The dissipated energy is split into useful heat for bonding ($Q_{useful}$) and losses to the surroundings ($Q_{loss}$):

$$\frac{1}{2} I \omega_0^2 = Q_{useful} + Q_{loss}$$

Typically, $70$–$90\%$ of the stored energy is converted to useful heat at the interface, with the remainder conducted into the bulk of the workpieces.

### 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A flywheel of moment of inertia $I = 2.5 \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$ is accelerated to $N = 3000 \text{ rpm}$ for inertia welding of two low-alloy steel shafts of diameter $D = 50 \text{ mm}$. Calculate: (i) the stored kinetic energy, (ii) the rubbing velocity at the interface, and (iii) the average frictional torque if the weld forms in $\theta = 250$ rad of rotation.

**Solution:**

**(i) Stored kinetic energy:**

$$E_k = \frac{I \pi^2 N^2}{1800} = \frac{2.5 \times \pi^2 \times (3000)^2}{1800}$$

$$E_k = \frac{2.5 \times 9.8696 \times 9 \times 10^6}{1800} = \frac{2.2217 \times 10^8}{1800}$$

$$E_k \approx 1.234 \times 10^5 \text{ J} = 123.4 \text{ kJ}$$

**(ii) Rubbing velocity at the interface (radius $r = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$):**

$$v = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.05 \times 3000}{60} = \frac{471.24}{60}$$

$$v \approx 7.854 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Average frictional torque:**

Using the work–energy theorem $\tau_f \cdot \theta = E_k$:

$$\tau_f = \frac{E_k}{\theta} = \frac{1.234 \times 10^5}{250} = 493.6 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Phase-by-Phase Process Map

| Phase | Duration | $\omega$ Behavior | $\tau_f$ Behavior | $P$ (Power) | Material State | Visual Cue |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Initial Contact | Very short | Near-constant | Sharp rise | Spikes high | Elastic → plastic | Sparks, oxide flash |
| 2. Steady-State | Medium | Constant | Near-constant | High & steady | Viscoplastic layer | Bright flash ring |
| 3. Deceleration | Medium | Monotonic drop | Decaying | Drops | Heavily plasticized | Thick flash spirals |
| 4. Forging (Upset) | Short | $\approx 0$ | Force-controlled | Very low (static) | Bond consolidation | Final upset geometry |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Source and Motor Drive] --> B[Clutch Assembly]
    B --> C[Flywheel Energy Storage Unit]
    C --> D[Spindle with Rotating Chuck]
    D --> E[Workpiece A - Rotating]
    F[Hydraulic / Pneumatic Actuator] --> G[Stationary Chuck with Axial Thrust]
    G --> H[Workpiece B - Stationary]
    E --> I[Friction Interface - Weld Zone]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Heat Generation via Friction]
    J --> K[Material Plasticization]
    K --> L[Deceleration of Flywheel]
    L --> M[Forging Pressure Application]
    M --> N[Completed Solid-State Weld]
    O[Control Panel with Sensors] --> A
    O --> F
    P[Encoder and Speed Sensor] --> C
    Q[Load Cell for Axial Force] --> F
    R[Thermocouple and Infrared Pyrometer] --> I
```

### 4.2 Four-Phase Process Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1([Start: Clamp Workpieces]) --> S2[Accelerate Flywheel to Preset N rpm]
    S2 --> S3[Phase 1: Initial Contact and Asperity Heating]
    S3 --> S4[Phase 2: Steady-State Friction and Viscoplastic Layer]
    S4 --> S5[Phase 3: Flywheel Deceleration and Flash Formation]
    S5 --> S6[Phase 4: Apply Upset Forging Pressure]
    S6 --> S7[Hold and Cool Under Pressure]
    S7 --> S8([End: Release and Unclamp Weldment])
```

### 4.3 Energy Conversion Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[Electrical Energy from Motor] --> E2[Rotational Kinetic Energy in Flywheel]
    E2 --> E3[Frictional Heat at Interface]
    E3 --> E4[Thermal Energy in Plasticized Material]
    E4 --> E5[Plastic Deformation Work]
    E5 --> E6[Metallurgical Bond Formation]
    E3 --> E7[Heat Loss to Bulk of Workpieces]
    E3 --> E8[Heat Loss to Environment and Flash]
```

### 4.4 Equipment Schematic (Block-Level Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MachineFrame["Welding Machine Frame"]
        M1[Headstock Assembly]
        M2[Tailstock Assembly]
        M3[Bed and Guideways]
    end
    subgraph DriveSystem["Drive and Energy System"]
        D1[AC Servo Motor]
        D2[Clutch and Brake Unit]
        D3[Flywheel Disc]
        D4[Spindle and Bearings]
    end
    subgraph PressureSystem["Axial Thrust System"]
        P1[Hydraulic Cylinder]
        P2[Pressure Control Valve]
        P3[Load Cell Sensor]
    end
    subgraph ControlSystem["CNC Control System"]
        C1[Programmable Logic Controller]
        C2[Human Machine Interface HMI]
        C3[Data Acquisition and Logging]
    end
    M1 --- D4
    D1 --- D2
    D2 --- D3
    D3 --- D4
    M2 --- P1
    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3
    C1 --- D1
    C1 --- P2
    C1 --- C2
    C3 --- C1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define inertia welding. Why is it classified as a solid-state welding process?**

**Model Answer:**
Inertia welding is a solid-state rotary friction welding process in which the heat required for joining is generated entirely by converting the kinetic energy stored in a rotating flywheel into frictional heat at the interface of two workpieces, under an applied axial pressure. It is classified as a solid-state process because the parent metal at the interface never melts during the welding cycle; bonding is achieved through thermo-plastic deformation and dynamic recrystallization in the temperature range of $0.6$–$0.9\,T_m$ (where $T_m$ is the melting temperature in Kelvin). The absence of a liquid phase eliminates solidification-related defects such as porosity, hot cracking, and segregation.

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
> **List any four advantages of inertia welding over conventional fusion welding processes.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Dissimilar-metal joining**: Capable of joining metals with widely different melting points (e.g., aluminum to steel, copper to stainless steel).
2. **No filler metal or shielding gas required**: Process is self-sufficient in terms of consumables.
3. **Excellent repeatability**: Energy budget is pre-set by the flywheel, making it highly suitable for automation.
4. **No HAZ liquation cracking**: As a solid-state process, it avoids fusion-zone defects.
5. **High joint strength**: Weld efficiencies of **90–100%** of parent metal are routinely achieved.
6. **Low operator skill required**: Process is parameter-controlled, not skill-controlled.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **(a)** Explain the working principle of inertia welding with a neat schematic. Discuss the role of the flywheel as an energy-storage element. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a labeled diagram, describe the four distinct phases of the inertia welding cycle. Plot a typical curve of spindle speed versus time and mark the phases. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Working Principle:**

Inertia welding is a **pre-set, energy-controlled** rotary friction welding process. The working principle is as follows:

1. **Energy Loading Stage:** A flywheel (mounted on the spindle) is accelerated by a motor to a preset rotational speed $N$ (or angular velocity $\omega_0$). The flywheel stores rotational kinetic energy $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_0^2$.

2. **Engagement Stage:** The motor is declutched, and the rotating chuck holding Workpiece A is brought into axial contact with Workpiece B (held stationary) under a programmed friction pressure $P_{fr}$.

3. **Frictional Heating Stage:** Relative sliding at the interface generates frictional heat. The flywheel begins to decelerate as its kinetic energy is dissipated as heat at the interface.

4. **Upset/Forging Stage:** Once the flywheel has stopped (or reached a preset lower speed), the axial pressure is sharply increased to the forging pressure $P_f$ (typically $2$–$3 \times P_{fr}$), which consolidates the plasticized material and forms the metallurgical bond.

**Role of the Flywheel as Energy Storage:**
- The flywheel acts as a **mechanical capacitor** — it pre-stores a fixed amount of energy.
- This **decouples** the energy source (motor) from the welding process (heat generation).
- The **total heat available for welding is fixed by the flywheel parameters** ($I$ and $\omega_0$), making the process repeatable and predictable.
- Different weld sizes (cross-sections) can be accommodated by selecting flywheels of appropriate $I$ — a major advantage over continuous-drive friction welding.

**Part (b) — Four Phases and Spindle-Speed vs Time Plot:**

**Phase 1 – Initial Contact (Friction Phase):** The rotating part touches the stationary part; asperities deform and heat the interface rapidly. Spindle speed remains nearly constant at $\omega_0$.

**Phase 2 – Steady-State:** A thin layer of plasticized material forms at the interface; the frictional torque reaches a quasi-steady value. Spindle speed still nearly constant.

**Phase 3 – Deceleration:** The flywheel loses kinetic energy rapidly; spindle speed drops sharply to zero. The interface temperature peaks in this phase.

**Phase 4 – Forging/Up-Set:** Flywheel is essentially stationary; axial pressure is increased to forge the plasticized material. The bond is finalized.

**Typical Spindle Speed vs Time Plot:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega(t) &=
\begin{cases}
\omega_0, & 0 \le t \le t_1 + t_2 \quad \text{(constant)} \\[4pt]
\omega_0 \cdot e^{-(t - t_1 - t_2)/\tau}, & t_1 + t_2 \le t \le t_1 + t_2 + t_3 \quad \text{(exponential decay)} \\[4pt]
0, & t > t_1 + t_2 + t_3 \quad \text{(forging phase)}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

The four phases can be marked on the $\omega$–$t$ curve:
- **Phase 1 and 2:** Flat plateau at $\omega_0$
- **Phase 3:** Exponential decay from $\omega_0$ to $0$
- **Phase 4:** Flat at $\omega = 0$ (with pressure rise)

> **Q3(B).** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the kinetic energy stored in a rotating flywheel used in inertia welding. A flywheel of moment of inertia $I = 1.8 \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$ is spun to $N = 2500 \text{ rpm}$. Calculate the energy available for welding. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss in detail the industrial applications of inertia welding. Why is it the preferred process for joining dissimilar metals? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation and Numerical Calculation:**

The derivation follows the standard work–energy theorem for a rotating body. Summing the kinetic energy of all elemental masses of the flywheel:

$$E_k = \sum \frac{1}{2} m_i v_i^2 = \sum \frac{1}{2} m_i (\omega r_i)^2 = \frac{\omega^2}{2} \sum m_i r_i^2$$

Recognizing the moment of inertia $I = \sum m_i r_i^2$:

$$\boxed{E_k = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2}$$

Converting to rpm using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$E_k = \frac{I \pi^2 N^2}{1800}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Substituting $I = 1.8 \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$ and $N = 2500 \text{ rpm}$:

$$E_k = \frac{1.8 \times \pi^2 \times (2500)^2}{1800}$$

$$E_k = \frac{1.8 \times 9.8696 \times 6.25 \times 10^6}{1800}$$

$$E_k = \frac{1.1103 \times 10^8}{1800} = 61{,}683 \text{ J} \approx 61.7 \text{ kJ}$$

**Part (b) — Industrial Applications and Dissimilar-Metal Joining:**

**Industrial Applications:**

| Sector | Component Joined | Material Combination |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive | Drive shafts, axle tubes, gear blanks | Medium-carbon steel / low-alloy steel |
| Aerospace | Engine shafts, landing gear pins, turbine discs | Titanium / steel, nickel alloys / steel |
| Oil & Gas | Drill pipes, sucker rods, tool joints | Carbon steel / alloy steel |
| Power Generation | Rotor shafts, turbine assemblies | Low-alloy steel / Cr-Mo-V steels |
| Tooling | Bimetallic tools (drill bits, reamers) | HSS / carbon steel shank |
| Construction | Rebar couplers | Low-carbon steel / low-carbon steel |

**Why Inertia Welding is Preferred for Dissimilar Metals:**

1. **No Melting Involved:** The interface stays in the **thermoplastic regime** ($0.6$–$0.9\,T_m$). This means that even if the two metals have very different melting points, neither one reaches its liquidus — eliminating the formation of brittle intermetallics that plague fusion welding of dissimilar pairs.

2. **Short Interface-Time at High Temperature:** The high-temperature zone exists for only **1–5 seconds**, minimizing diffusion-driven intermetallic growth.

3. **Mechanical Upset Expels Oxides:** The forging phase physically extrudes brittle oxide films and intermetallic reaction products into the flash ring, leaving a clean metallurgical bond.

4. **Precise Energy Control:** The pre-stored energy budget can be tuned to provide just enough heat for plasticization without overheating either of the two dissimilar materials — which is critical when one material has a much lower hot-strength.

5. **No Filler Material Issues:** The absence of filler metal means there is no third-material dilution of the joint.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing inertia welding with conventional (continuous-drive) friction welding.** The key distinction is the **flywheel energy storage** and **fixed energy budget** — examiners deduct heavily if the answer treats inertia welding as a continuous motor-driven process.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the unit of angular velocity in rad/s** when using $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$. This is a classic 1-mark trap.
> 3. **Skipping the forging phase** when describing the four phases. Many students describe only three — losing up to 2 marks.
> 4. **Writing $v_{rel}$ as `|v|` inside a table** — it breaks the markdown table structure in the digital answer script. Always use $\vert v \vert$ or simply $v$ in scripts.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- Inertia welding is a **solid-state, rotary, friction-based** welding process.
- The **flywheel is the energy source** — energy is pre-stored as $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$.
- The **energy budget is finite** — once the flywheel stops, no more heat is available.
- **Four phases**: Initial contact → Steady-state friction → Deceleration → Forging/upset.
- **Phase 4 (forging) is what actually forms the bond** — phases 1–3 only prepare the material.
- **Forging pressure is typically 2–3× the friction pressure** — remember this ratio for numerical problems.
- **Kinetic energy formula in rpm**: $E_k = \dfrac{I \pi^2 N^2}{1800}$ — memorize this directly.
- **Angular velocity conversion**: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \text{ rad/s}$ — always convert N from rpm before using $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$.
- **No melting at the interface** — this is what allows joining of dissimilar metals (e.g., Al–steel, Cu–SS).
- **Flash formation is normal and desirable** — it indicates complete expulsion of contaminants and oxides.
- **No filler metal, no shielding gas, no flux** — a "green" process.
- **Process is repeatable and automation-friendly** — common in mass-production lines (automotive, aerospace).
- **Weld efficiencies of 90–100%** of parent-metal strength are typical.
- **Equipment**: headstock (with spindle, flywheel, clutch), tailstock (with stationary chuck and thrust cylinder), control system, and energy-storage flywheel.
- **Applications** to remember: drive shafts, axle tubes, sucker rods, drill collars, landing gear pins, bimetallic tools, rebar couplers, turbine shafts.
- **For numerical problems**, the **work–energy theorem** $\tau_f \cdot \theta = E_k$ is the key relation linking frictional torque, deceleration angle, and stored energy.
- **Common pitfall**: confusing "inertia welding" with "friction welding" — the former is a specific variant of the latter.
- **For KTU Module 1**, focus areas are: definition, principle, four phases, energy equation, and dissimilar-metal applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Explosive Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Explosive Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Explosive Welding (EXW / EXWELD)** is classified by the American Welding Society (AWS) as a *solid-state welding process* in which the controlled detonation of an explosive charge propels one metal component (the **flyer plate**) against a stationary component (the **parent / base plate**) at extremely high velocity along an **oblique collision angle**, producing a metallurgical bond at the interface without the constituents ever reaching their liquidus temperature. The bond is formed in a few microseconds by a combination of high-velocity plastic deformation, hydrodynamic **jetting** of surface material, and mechanical interlocking of the wavy interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Explosive welding is a **solid-state** (no melting) joining process. Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ), solidification cracks, and phase transformations of the bulk are therefore absent. It is one of the few processes capable of joining **dissimilar, immiscible, and physically incompatible metals** (e.g., Al–Steel, Ti–Steel, Cu–Al, Nb–Steel).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Snap of a Click-Belt"

Imagine taking a flat steel ruler and placing it on a table with one end held down. Now flick the free end *very* hard with your finger: the ruler snaps down and strikes the table with such speed that it bonds (in a perceptual sense) to the surface for a fraction of a second, leaving a tiny mark. The loud "snap" is the energy release, the violent whip is the *flyer*, and the table is the *base plate*. The key idea is **velocity, not heat**: the faster the impact, the better the bond. In explosive welding, the finger is replaced by a precisely calculated sheet of explosive, and the ruler is replaced by a metal plate that travels at **200–800 m/s**.

### 1.3 Standard Quantitative Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Detonation velocity of explosive, $V_d$ | **2000 – 8000** | m/s |
| Flyer plate velocity, $V_p$ | **200 – 800** | m/s |
| Collision angle, $\beta$ | **5° – 25°** | degree |
| Standoff distance, $s$ | **0.5 – 3 × flyer thickness** | mm |
| Bonding (welding) time | **1 – 10** | µs |
| Weldable area per charge | up to **~30 m²** | m² |
| Interface strain rate | **10⁴ – 10⁶** | s⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for the board exam:** A 14-mark question frequently begins with a line-drawing of the EXW set-up followed by the line *"Define explosive welding and state any four process parameters."* Memorize the three primary variables — **$V_d$, $V_p$, $\beta$** — and one metric for the welding window.

### 1.4 Position of EXW in the Welding Family

> [!NOTE]
> EXW belongs to the family of **Solid-State Welding Processes** (alongside Friction Stir Welding, Ultrasonic Welding, Diffusion Bonding, Cold Pressure Welding, and Forge Welding). The heat generated at the interface is the by-product of plastic deformation, not the source of bonding.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage Mechanism of Bond Formation

When the detonation front sweeps across the explosive, the flyer plate bends progressively and strikes the parent plate along a moving **collision point (stagnation point)**. Bonding occurs in five sequential, overlapping micro-stages:

1. **Energy Release Stage** — Detonation of the explosive charge converts chemical energy (~**4 – 6 kJ/g** for typical ANFO/Composition-B) into a shock wave travelling at $V_d$.
2. **Acceleration Stage** — The flyer is plastically accelerated by the detonation gases to velocity $V_p$. Pressure on the flyer surface is in the order of **10 – 30 GPa**.
3. **Oblique Impact Stage** — The flyer strikes the parent at the dynamic bend angle $\beta$, generating an extremely high strain rate ($\dot{\varepsilon} \approx 10^4 - 10^6 \, s^{-1}$).
4. **Jetting Stage** — A thin metallic **jet** is ejected from the apex of the collision. This jet removes oxide films, contaminants, and adsorbed gases, exposing nascent, chemically clean metal on both surfaces — *this is the fundamental reason a bond can form.*
5. **Wave Formation & Interlock Stage** — The trapped jet destabilizes the interface, producing a characteristic **sinusoidal wave** (period $\lambda \approx 0.2 - 1.0 \,\text{mm}$). Wavy interfaces lock the two plates mechanically; flat interfaces give weaker bonds.

### 2.2 The Welding Window — "Why Some Combinations Work and Others Don't"

Not every combination of $V_p$ and $\beta$ produces a bond. There is a bounded region in the $V_p$-$\beta$ plane inside which a *weldable jet* forms. Outside this region, one of three failure modes occurs:

- **Below lower bound** → *No jetting.* Insufficient energy to break surface oxides → no bond.
- **Above upper bound** → *Melt / blow-through.* The interface melts and re-solidifies as a brittle cast structure or the flyer is perforated.
- **Excessively large $\beta$** → *Mach stem formation* and shock spalling.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four equations below are the most frequently asked expressions in KTU ESE for explosive welding. Memorize the symbols, the physical meaning, and the unit — the examiner frequently tests dimensional awareness.

| # | Equation | Meaning of Symbols | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $V_p \approx 2 \, V_d \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right)$ | $V_p$ = flyer plate velocity (m/s), $V_d$ = detonation velocity (m/s), $\beta$ = dynamic bend / collision angle (rad) | Sets the flyer velocity for a chosen explosive & stand-off |
| **2** | $\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right) \ge \dfrac{k}{1 + \sqrt{\rho_p/\rho_f}}$  (Deribas, 1968) | $\rho_f$ = flyer density, $\rho_p$ = parent density, $k \approx 0.6 - 1.0$ | **Lower bound** of the welding window |
| **3** | $\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right) \le \dfrac{2}{k\,\bigl(1 + \sqrt{\rho_f/\rho_p}\bigr)}$ | Same symbols as Eq. 2 | **Upper bound** of the welding window |
| **4** | $V_c \ge k\,(H/\rho_f)^{1/2}$  (Wittman, 1975) | $V_c$ = critical collision-point velocity (m/s), $H$ = Vickers hardness of flyer (HV), $k \approx 0.6 - 1.2$ | Minimum $V_p$ to ensure jetting for a given hardness |

**Rule of Thumb (Babul, 1974) — upper bound expressed in velocity form:**

$$V_c^{\,max} \approx 0.5\,\sqrt{\dfrac{H}{\rho_f}}\!\left(\dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_p}+1\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> A *weldable combination* requires that the **lower bound $\le \sin(\beta/2) \le$ upper bound** *AND* $V_p \ge V_c$ (Wittman). Many KTU problems give you $H$, $\rho_f$, and $\rho_p$ and ask you to find the permissible range of $V_p$.

### 2.4 Process Variables — Engineering Levers

- **Detonation velocity $V_d$** — controlled by the *type* of explosive (e.g., ANFO ≈ 3000 m/s, Composition B ≈ 8000 m/s, Pentolite ≈ 7500 m/s) and the *thickness* of the charge (per unit flyer area, $R$, the *mass ratio*).
- **Standoff distance $s$** — gap between flyer and parent. Larger $s$ → higher $V_p$ but also higher $\beta$; usually $s = 0.5\,t$ to $3\,t$, where $t$ is flyer thickness.
- **Collision angle $\beta$** — set geometrically by the stand-off. Decreased $\beta$ → smoother interface; increased $\beta$ → wavier interface, more jetting.
- **Mass ratio $R = m_{explosive} / m_{flyer}$** — the *master* parameter. Typical range: **0.5 – 3.0** for clad plates.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Reason EXW is Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| Oil & Gas / Chemical | Bi-metallic tubes (Al-bronze/Steel, Cu-Ni/Steel) | Corrosion resistance + structural strength |
| Marine / Ship-building | Al–Steel transition joints | Eliminates galvanic corrosion at hulls |
| Nuclear | Zircaloy–Steel end-plugs | Avoids HAZ in irradiated materials |
| Power Generation | Ti–Steel condenser tubes | Resists seawater corrosion |
| Aerospace | Al–Ti honeycomb face-sheets | Dissimilar, immiscible pair |
| Railways | Cu–Steel catenary wire earthing | High conductivity + mechanical strength |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Flyer Velocity $V_p = 2\,V_d \sin(\beta/2)$

**Premise (Bethe's Ideal Detonation Model):** The detonation front moves with velocity $V_d$ in the still explosive. Immediately behind the front, the detonation products (a high-pressure gas) push the flyer plate. The flyer, the detonation wave, and the collision point all move together, forming a steady-state oblique jet.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the moving triple point.**
Let the detonation wave propagate along the parent plate. At a given instant, the flyer makes a *dynamic bend angle* $\beta$ with the parent. The collision point moves with a *collision point velocity* $V_c$ along the parent surface.

**Step 2 — Velocity triangle.**
The flyer plate's tip velocity $V_p$ can be decomposed into a component along the detonation front ($V_d$) and a component normal to it. By the geometry of oblique reflection, the resultant flyer velocity vector is:

$$V_p = \dfrac{V_d}{\cos(\beta/2)} \cdot \sin(\beta/2) \cdot 2 = 2\,V_d \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right)$$

> The factor **2** arises because the flyer is being pushed from *behind* by the detonation gases *and* being bent downward by the parent's reaction — both effects add.

**Step 3 — Useful limiting cases.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) } \beta \to 0 \;&\Rightarrow\; V_p \to 0 \quad &\text{(no bending, no impact)} \\
\text{(b) } \beta = 90^\circ \;&\Rightarrow\; V_p = \sqrt{2}\,V_d \quad &\text{(maximum practical velocity)}\\
\text{(c) } \beta = 30^\circ \;&\Rightarrow\; V_p \approx 0.518\,V_d &
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Wittman Lower-Bound Equation

**Premise:** A jet forms only if the kinetic energy density of the collision-point region exceeds the surface energy + plastic dissipation required to rupture the surface.

**Step 1 — Energy balance at the collision point.**
The pressure at the stagnation point is approximately $\frac{1}{2}\rho_f V_c^{\,2}$ (Bernoulli). The yield stress of the flyer in dynamic conditions is proportional to its Vickers hardness, $H$.

**Step 2 — Dimensional analysis.**
For jetting to begin, $\frac{1}{2}\rho_f V_c^{\,2} \ge k \cdot H$, where $k$ is a non-dimensional constant accounting for strain-hardening and temperature rise.

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_c$.**

$$\dfrac{1}{2}\rho_f V_c^{\,2} = k H \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; V_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2kH}{\rho_f}}$$

Absorbing the factor $\sqrt{2k}$ into a single empirical constant $k' \approx 0.6 - 1.2$:

$$\boxed{\,V_c \ge k' \sqrt{\dfrac{H}{\rho_f}}\,}$$

This is the **Wittman lower bound**. The form is useful because it depends only on *flyer* properties — independent of the parent — and gives an immediate go/no-go criterion.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** An attempt is being made to explosively weld an aluminium flyer ($\rho_f = 2700 \,\text{kg/m}^3$, $H = 35 \,\text{HV}$) onto a low-carbon steel parent ($\rho_p = 7850 \,\text{kg/m}^3$). The chosen explosive has a detonation velocity of $V_d = 4000 \,\text{m/s}$ and the stand-off geometry gives a collision angle of $\beta = 12^\circ$. Use $k = 0.9$ throughout.

**(a) Compute the flyer plate velocity $V_p$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_p &= 2\,V_d \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right) \\
    &= 2 \times 4000 \times \sin(6^\circ) \\
    &= 8000 \times 0.10453 \\
    &= 836.2 \;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Verify the Wittman lower bound.** 

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= 0.9 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{35}{2700}} \quad \text{(note: $H$ in HV and $\rho$ in kg/m³ are taken as a normalized ratio)} \\
    &= 0.9 \times \sqrt{0.01296} \\
    &= 0.9 \times 0.1139 \\
    &= 0.103 \;\text{(normalized units)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> In SI consistent units where $H$ is converted to Pa ($35 \,\text{HV} \approx 3.43 \times 10^8 \,\text{Pa}$):

$$
V_c = 0.9 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{3.43 \times 10^{8}}{2700}} = 0.9 \times 356.5 \approx 321 \,\text{m/s}
$$

Since $V_p = 836 \,\text{m/s} \gg V_c = 321 \,\text{m/s}$: **the lower-bound is satisfied** — jetting will occur.

**(c) Check the Deribas upper bound.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right) &= \sin(6^\circ) = 0.1045 \\
\text{Upper bound:}\quad \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right) &\le \dfrac{2}{0.9\,(1+\sqrt{2700/7850})} \\
&= \dfrac{2}{0.9\,(1+0.586)} \\
&= \dfrac{2}{1.428} \\
&= 1.40 \;\text{(physically impossible — bound is non-binding)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The lower bound is binding (jetting is guaranteed) and the upper bound is non-binding. The chosen parameters are inside the welding window. **A successful bond is predicted.**

> [!NOTE]
> The dimensionless ratios matter. KTU often tests *which bound controls* — the lower bound controls for hard flyers, the upper bound for soft flyers.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Bond-Quality Predictors

| Quantity | Symbol | Direction of "Good Weld" | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flyer velocity | $V_p$ | $V_c \le V_p \le V_c^{max}$ | 200 – 800 m/s |
| Collision angle | $\beta$ | 5° – 25° | — |
| Mass ratio | $R$ | 0.5 – 3.0 | — |
| Stand-off | $s$ | $0.5t$ – $3t$ | mm |
| Wave amplitude / wavelength | $a/\lambda$ | 0.1 – 0.4 | dimensionless |
| Interface hardness rise | $\Delta H$ | Minimal (≤ 30 % of bulk) | HV |
| Bond shear strength | $\tau$ | $\ge 0.8 \times$ weaker parent | MPa |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Schematic — Parallel-Plate EXW Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Before Detonation]
        direction TB
        expl["EXPLOSIVE CHARGE"]
        flyer["FLYER PLATE - v_p = 200-800 m/s"]
        stand["STAND-OFF DISTANCE - s"]
        base["PARENT PLATE - STATIONARY"]
        anvil["RIGID ANVIL - SAND BED"]
        expl --> flyer
        flyer --> stand
        stand --> base
        base --> anvil
    end
    subgraph B[After Detonation Sweep]
        direction TB
        bond["METALLURGICAL BOND - WAVY INTERFACE"]
        jet["METALLIC JET - EJECTED AHEAD OF COLLISION"]
        rem["REMAINS OF EXPLOSIVE GASES"]
        flyer2["DEFORMED FLYER"]
        base2["ELASTICALLY RECOVERED PARENT"]
        bond --> jet
        bond --> flyer2
        bond --> base2
        rem --> flyer2
    end
    A -- DETONATION FRONT V_d --> B
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Process Variable Interactions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["SELECT EXPLOSIVE TYPE"] --> A2["V_d fixed"]
    A3["SELECT STAND-OFF DISTANCE"] --> A4["geometry fixed"]
    A2 --> A5["COMPUTE BETA - dynamic bend angle"]
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6["V_p = 2 V_d sin beta/2"]
    A6 --> A7{"INSIDE WELDING WINDOW?"}
    A8["MATERIAL PROPERTIES H, rho_f, rho_p"] --> A9["WITTMAN V_c = k sqrt H/rho_f"]
    A9 --> A7
    A10["DERIBAS bounds on sin beta/2"] --> A7
    A7 -- YES --> A11["EXECUTE WELD"]
    A7 -- NO --> A12["ADJUST s OR EXPLOSIVE LOAD"]
    A12 --> A3
    A12 --> A1
    A11 --> A13["INSPECT BOND - ultrasonics, metallography"]
    A13 --> A14{"WAVY + NO DEFECTS?"}
    A14 -- YES --> A15["ACCEPTED CLAD PLATE"]
    A14 -- NO --> A12
```

### 4.3 Interface Wave Pattern — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WAVE["Wavy Interface - wavelength lambda, amplitude a"]
        W1["FLYER"] --> W2["WAVE CREST 1"]
        W2 --> W3["WAVE TROUGH 1"]
        W3 --> W4["WAVE CREST 2"]
        W4 --> W5["WAVE TROUGH 2"]
        W5 --> W6["PARENT PLATE"]
    end
    WAVE2["a/lambda approx 0.1 to 0.4 for good bond"] -.-> WAVE
```

### 4.4 Failure Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1["RUN EXPLOSIVE WELD"] --> F2{"VELOCITY RATIO?"}
    F2 -- "V_p < V_c lower" --> F3["NO BOND - cold weld"]
    F2 -- "V_p > V_c upper" --> F4["MELT OR BLOW-THROUGH"]
    F2 -- "inside window" --> F5{"ANGLE RATIO?"}
    F5 -- "beta too small" --> F6["FLAT INTERFACE - low strength"]
    F5 -- "beta too large" --> F7["MACH STEM - brittle"]
    F5 -- "inside window" --> F8["WAVY BOND - acceptable"]
    F3 --> F9["increase explosive charge"]
    F4 --> F10["reduce explosive charge or V_d"]
    F6 --> F11["increase stand-off"]
    F7 --> F12["decrease stand-off"]
```

### 4.5 Equipment Layout (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["Detonation Velocity Probe - pin oscillograph"] --> P2["Data Logger"]
    P3["High Speed Camera - 10^5 fps"] --> P2
    P2 --> P4["V_p and V_c determined"]
    P5["Sand Bed / Anvil"] --> P6["Test Piece Assembly"]
    P7["Explosive Magazine"] --> P6
    P6 --> P8["Detonation Trigger"]
    P8 --> P1
    P8 --> P3
    P9["Ultrasonic C-Scan"] --> P10["Bond Quality - % bonded area"]
    P11["Metallographic Polisher + Etch"] --> P12["Interface Wave Geometry - a, lambda"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q. Define explosive welding. List four process parameters that influence the bond quality.**

**Model Answer:**
Explosive welding is a *solid-state welding process* in which the controlled detonation of an explosive charge propels a flyer plate against a parent plate at high velocity along an oblique collision angle, forming a metallurgical bond without melting the constituents. The four governing parameters are: **(i) detonation velocity $V_d$ of the explosive, (ii) flyer plate velocity $V_p$, (iii) collision angle $\beta$, and (iv) stand-off distance $s$** (or equivalently, mass ratio $R$). **Weldability window** is determined by lower bound (Wittman) and upper bound (Deribas).

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q. What is "jetting" in explosive welding, and why is it essential for bond formation?**

**Model Answer:**
Jetting is the high-velocity ejection of a thin stream of surface metal from the apex of the collision point between flyer and parent. It is essential because the jet **scavenges the surface oxide layers, adsorbed gases, and contaminants** from both plates, exposing nascent, chemically clean metal that comes into intimate atomic contact under the enormous contact pressure (10 – 30 GPa), enabling metallurgical bonding. Without jetting, the persistent oxide film prevents atomic contact and no bond forms.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks)**
> *(a)* With a neat schematic, describe the **parallel-plate explosive welding** process. Explain the formation of a **wavy bond interface** and state its significance. (7 Marks)
> *(b)* Derive the **Wittman lower-bound equation** for explosive welding. An aluminium flyer (Hardness 30 HV, density 2700 kg/m³) is to be welded to a steel parent. Using $k = 0.9$, determine the *minimum* collision-point velocity required for jetting. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Process description (7 marks):**

The parallel-plate set-up is shown in Section 4.1. The key steps are:

1. The base plate is supported on a sand bed or anvil.
2. The flyer plate is placed parallel to the base plate at a stand-off distance $s$.
3. A uniform layer of explosive is spread on the upper surface of the flyer.
4. Detonation is initiated at one edge; the detonation front propagates at $V_d$.
5. The flyer is progressively bent and accelerated, striking the parent at angle $\beta$.
6. A metallic jet is ejected, cleaning the surfaces.
7. A wavy bond forms behind the collision point.

The **wavy interface** has wavelength $\lambda$ and amplitude $a$. Its formation is explained by the **Askeland–Bahar–Compton hydrodynamic instability theory**: the jet cannot escape entirely; the trapped portion destabilizes the interface like a Kelvin–Helmholtz shear layer. **Significance:** wavy interfaces have *higher mechanical interlock* and *larger bonded area* than flat ones, raising the bond shear strength by **2 – 3×**. The ratio $a/\lambda \approx 0.1 - 0.4$ is the indicator of a *good* weld. (7 marks)

**(b) Wittman derivation + numerical (7 marks):**

The derivation is given in §3.2. For the numerical part:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= 30 \;\text{HV} = 30 \times 9.807 \times 10^{6} \;\text{Pa} = 2.94 \times 10^{8} \;\text{Pa} \\
\rho_f &= 2700 \;\text{kg/m}^3 \\
k &= 0.9 \\
V_c &= k\sqrt{\dfrac{H}{\rho_f}} = 0.9 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2.94 \times 10^{8}}{2700}} \\
    &= 0.9 \times \sqrt{1.089 \times 10^{5}} \\
    &= 0.9 \times 330.0 \\
    &\approx 297 \;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum collision-point velocity = 297 m/s.** (7 marks)

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative choice)*
> *(a)* Explain the concept of the **weldability window** in explosive welding. Derive the **Deribas lower-bound condition** and discuss its engineering significance. (7 Marks)
> *(b)* With a schematic, describe **angle-plate explosive welding** and contrast it with the parallel-plate configuration. List **five dissimilar metal combinations** successfully joined by EXW. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Weldability window (7 marks):**

The weldability window is a bounded region in the $V_p$–$\beta$ plane (or equivalently $\sin(\beta/2)$ – $V_c$ plane) inside which a *metallurgical bond with a jet* forms. Outside it, one obtains either a *no-bond* region (kinetic energy too low) or a *melt / blow-through* region (kinetic energy too high). The classical Deribas lower bound is:

$$\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right) \ge \dfrac{k}{1+\sqrt{\rho_p/\rho_f}}$$

**Derivation sketch:** Equating the *kinetic energy* of the jet to the *surface + plastic work* required to rupture the oxide layer, and using a Bernoulli estimate of the pressure at the collision point, one arrives at the above bound (with $k \approx 0.6 - 1.0$). **Engineering significance:** it gives a *closed-form* design rule — for any pair of densities, the permissible range of $\beta$ (and hence $V_p$, via $V_p = 2 V_d \sin(\beta/2)$) can be calculated *before* the experiment. (7 marks)

**(b) Angle-plate configuration + dissimilar pairs (7 marks):**

In the *angle-plate* configuration, the flyer is initially inclined at a fixed angle $\alpha$ to the parent, eliminating the stand-off gap. As the detonation sweeps across, the geometry dynamically evolves, giving a wider $\beta$ range and a more uniform collision point velocity. This is preferred for **tube-to-tube-plate welding** and for **very thin** flyers.

| # | Combination | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aluminium – Steel | Ship hulls, automotive panels |
| 2 | Titanium – Steel | Nuclear, condenser tubes |
| 3 | Copper – Steel | Electrical transition joints |
| 4 | Zircaloy – Stainless Steel | Nuclear end-plugs |
| 5 | Tantalum – Steel | Chemical reactors |

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. *Forgetting the factor of 2* in $V_p = 2 V_d \sin(\beta/2)$. Writing $V_p = V_d \sin(\beta/2)$ is a 2-mark cut.
> 2. *Unit mismatch* in Wittman equation — $H$ in HV must be converted to Pa (× 9.81 × 10⁶) for SI consistency.
> 3. *Confusing lower and upper bounds* — Wittman is the *lower* bound (minimum velocity to form a jet). Deribas has *both* a lower and an upper expression; the upper one is for the *blow-through* limit.
> 4. *Stating "EXW is a fusion process"* — **wrong.** EXW is **solid-state**. This single error can cost 3 marks outright.
> 5. *Missing the "jetting" rationale* — without jetting, no bond. Most valuation keys allocate **2 mandatory marks** to this concept.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⚡ **EXW is solid-state** — no melting, no HAZ, no solidification cracks.
- ⚡ **The bond driver is velocity, not heat** — typical $V_p = 200 - 800 \,\text{m/s}$.
- ⚡ **The three master variables** are $V_d$, $\beta$, and stand-off $s$.
- ⚡ **$V_p = 2 V_d \sin(\beta/2)$** — flyer's velocity, derived from oblique geometry.
- ⚡ **Wittman lower bound:** $V_c \ge k\sqrt{H/\rho_f}$ — minimum velocity to ensure a *jet*.
- ⚡ **Deribas bounds** — enclose the **welding window** in $\sin(\beta/2)$ space.
- ⚡ **Jet formation** is the *fundamental mechanism* — it strips oxides and creates nascent metal contact.
- ⚡ **Wavy interface** is the *signature* of a good EXW bond; $a/\lambda \approx 0.1 - 0.4$.
- ⚡ **Dissimilar-metal joining** is the unique strength of EXW: Al–Steel, Ti–Steel, Cu–Steel, Zr–Steel, Ta–Steel, Nb–Steel, etc.
- ⚡ **Configurations:** parallel-plate (most common for clad plates) and angle-plate (used for tubes and thin flyers).
- ⚡ **Applications span** oil & gas, marine, nuclear, power, aerospace, and railways.
- ⚡ **Inspection methods:** ultrasonic C-scan (for bond-area fraction) and metallography (for wave geometry).
- ⚡ **Failure modes** to remember for exam: *no jet* (below Wittman), *melt/blow-through* (above Deribas upper), *Mach stem* (excessively large $\beta$).
- ⚡ **Mnemonic — "J-S-W-B"**: **J**etting, **S**urface cleaning, **W**ave formation, **B**onding — the four steps of EXW.
- ⚡ **Examiner-favourite line:** *"Hence EXW can join combinations that are unweldable by fusion processes."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffusion Bonding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Diffusion Bonding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diffusion Bonding (DB)** is a *solid-state joining process* in which two nominally flat surfaces are coalesced at an elevated temperature (typically **0.5 – 0.8 T_m**, where T_m is the melting point in Kelvin) under an applied pressure (usually **1 – 100 MPa**) for a controlled dwell time, **without macroscopic melting of the parent material**. The metallic continuity at the interface is achieved by *atomic diffusion*, grain boundary migration, and recrystallization across the original interface.

The process is conducted in a vacuum or inert atmosphere to suppress oxide formation and contamination. It is governed by the classical three-stage mechanism proposed by King and Owczarski (1968), and is formally classified by **AWS C3.2 "Standard Method for Evaluating the Strength of Brazed Joints"** as a precision solid-state joining technique.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Warm Butter Sandwich"** 🍞
>
> Imagine two slices of bread smeared with a thin layer of cold butter. Now imagine pressing them together for hours in a warm oven (just below the boiling point of water — never hot enough to *melt* the bread or burn it). What happens? The butter slowly seeps into the bread, and the bread-to-bread contact gradually becomes indistinguishable. **No ingredient melted. No flame was used. Yet, after enough time and gentle pressure, the two slices are now one loaf.**
>
> In Diffusion Bonding:
> - **The two metal surfaces** = the two slices of bread
> - **Heat below the melting point** = the warm oven
> - **Sustained pressure** = the gentle hand pressing
> - **Atomic diffusion across the interface** = the butter seeping through
> - **Vacuum atmosphere** = a clean environment free of dust/oxides

After sufficient dwell time, the original faying surface (the line where the two parts met) **disappears completely** under metallurgical examination — the joint becomes *microstructurally continuous* with the parent metal.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following constants are repeatedly used in diffusion bonding analysis and must be memorized:

- **Gas constant, R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Typical bonding temperature ratio: T_bond / T_m = 0.5 – 0.8**
- **Typical bonding pressure: P = 1 – 100 MPa**
- **Typical dwell time: t = 15 min – 24 h**
- **Required vacuum level: 10⁻² – 10⁻⁵ Pa** (to avoid oxidation)
- **Acceptable surface roughness: Ra ≤ 0.4 μm** (mirror finish preferred)

> [!IMPORTANT]
