> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Diffusion bonding is the **benchmark process** for joining *dissimilar metals*, *superalloys*, *titanium*, *zirconium*, *intermetallics*, and *metal-matrix composites (MMCs)* — all of which are extremely difficult or impossible to fusion-weld due to cracking, segregation, or undesirable intermetallic phases.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Classical Stages of Bond Formation

The mechanistic framework taught universally across KTU 2024-scheme metallurgy modules follows the **King–Owczarski three-stage model**:

**Stage 1 — Plastic Deformation & Contact Area Growth**
- Asperities (microscopic surface peaks) yield under applied pressure and temperature.
- Real contact area increases from ~1% to ~15–40% of nominal area.
- Dominant mechanism: **Power-law creep** combined with local plastic flow.
- Ends when the contact area can no longer increase under the applied stress.

**Stage 2 — Diffusion-Controlled Void Elimination**
- Remaining interfacial voids shrink by atomic diffusion driven by surface energy and stress gradients.
- Two parallel mechanisms compete:
  - **Surface diffusion** (fast, but does *not* close voids fully).
  - **Volume / grain-boundary diffusion** (slower, *does* close voids and produces metallurgical bonding).
- Duration: tens of minutes to several hours.

**Stage 3 — Recrystallization & Grain Boundary Migration**
- New equiaxed grains nucleate at the original interface and grow across it.
- The "witness line" (original faying surface) disappears.
- Bond strength approaches parent-metal strength.
- Bond quality now ≈ bulk material properties.

### 2.2 Why and How Each Stage Works

- **Why pressure in Stage 1?** Surface peaks are stress concentrators; without pressure, they cannot deform plastically to enlarge the contact area.
- **Why heat throughout?** Heat exponentially accelerates diffusion (Arrhenius behavior) and reduces yield strength, enabling creep.
- **Why vacuum?** Oxides on the surface (especially on Ti, Al, Zr) act as diffusion barriers. Even a few nm of oxide can prevent bonding.
- **Why long times?** Stage 2 void shrinkage scales roughly with **t^(1/5)** to **t^(1/3)** — extremely slow kinetics.

### 2.3 Process Parameters & Their Effects

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect on Bond Quality | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bonding Temperature (T) | 0.5 – 0.8 T_m | ↑ T → ↑ bond strength, ↑ grain growth risk | Accelerates diffusion exponentially |
| Applied Pressure (P) | 1 – 100 MPa | ↑ P → faster Stage 1 closure, ↑ distortion | Must exceed local asperity yield strength |
| Dwell Time (t) | 15 min – 24 h | Longer → stronger bond, diminishing returns | Needed for void elimination in Stage 2 |
| Surface Roughness (Ra) | ≤ 0.4 μm preferred | Smoother → fewer/smaller voids | Reduces Stage 2 workload |
| Atmosphere | Vacuum / Argon | Clean → suppresses oxide film | Oxide blocks atomic diffusion |
| Interlayer (if used) | 5 – 100 μm foil | Eutectic melting reduces T required | Enables low-T bonding |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the **core numerical backbone** of every KTU question on Diffusion Bonding.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius diffusion coefficient | m²·s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $J = -D \,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Fick's First Law — atomic flux | atoms·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \, \sigma^{n} \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Norton power-law creep (Stage 1) | s⁻¹ |
| 4 | $t_{\text{bond}} \propto \dfrac{T_m}{D_0} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Required bonding time at given T | s |
| 5 | $\dfrac{dr}{dt} = -\dfrac{D \, \gamma_s \, \Omega}{r \, k \, T}$ | Void shrinkage rate (Stage 2, surface-diffusion model) | m·s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\sigma_b = k_1 \ln(t) + k_2$ | Empirical bond strength vs. log-time | MPa |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{Stage2}} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{r}{r_0}\right)^3 \cdot f(t)$ | Void volume fraction remaining | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Larson-Miller: } LMP = T\!\left[C + \log(t)\right]$ | Creep-life / bonding equivalence | K·log(s) |

Where:
- **D₀** = pre-exponential diffusion constant (≈ 10⁻⁵ – 10⁻³ m²/s for metals)
- **Q** = activation energy for self-diffusion (≈ 100 – 300 kJ/mol for most metals)
- **γ_s** = surface energy of the metal (≈ 1 – 2 J/m²)
- **Ω** = atomic volume (≈ 1 × 10⁻²⁹ m³)
- **r, r₀** = current and initial void radius
- **C** = Larson-Miller constant (≈ 20 for most metals)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Bonding titanium leading edges, honeycomb sandwich panels, and turbine-engine vanes where fusion welding would create brittle Ti–Al intermetallics.
- **Nuclear:** Joining zirconium alloy fuel cladding (Zircaloy-4) where any melt pool would be unacceptable.
- **Electronics:** Hermetic sealing of Kovar-to-copper packages for vacuum tubes.
- **Tooling:** Bonding carbide tips to steel shanks for cutting tools.
- **MMCs and CMCs:** Joining metal-matrix composites where the reinforcement would dissolve in a melt pool.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three derivations below are **board-favorite derivations**. Mastery of these guarantees full marks in any KTU ESE Part B numerical/derivation question.

---

### 3.1 Derivation I — Derivation of the Arrhenius Diffusion Equation (Fundamental Kinetics Basis)

Starting from the thermodynamic definition of activation energy, we derive the temperature dependence of the diffusion coefficient.

**Step 1 — Define activation energy.**
The fraction of atoms possessing energy ≥ Q at temperature T follows the Boltzmann distribution:

$$
f = \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Define the atomic jump frequency.**
Each atom vibrates at the Debye frequency $\nu \approx 10^{13}\ \text{Hz}$. The number of successful jumps per second is:

$$
\Gamma = \nu \cdot f = \nu \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Relate jump frequency to macroscopic diffusion.**
Using the Einstein relation, the mean-square displacement $\langle x^2 \rangle = \Gamma \, a^2 \, t$, where $a$ is the jump distance. By Fick's law, this leads to:

$$
D = \tfrac{1}{6} \Gamma \, a^2
$$

**Step 4 — Combine the constants.**
Define $D_0 = \tfrac{1}{6} \nu \, a^2$ (pre-exponential factor). Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\,D \;=\; D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 10% rise in absolute temperature can **double** D for metals with Q ≈ 200 kJ/mol — explaining the extreme sensitivity of diffusion bonding to temperature.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Derivation of Bonding Time as a Function of Temperature

For two Stage-2 void-elimination processes to be metallurgically equivalent, the diffusion distance must be equal.

**Step 1 — Diffusion distance scaling.**
From the random-walk model:

$$
x \;\propto\; \sqrt{D \, t}
$$

**Step 2 — Equate diffusion distances at two conditions.**
For equivalent bonding at $(T_1, t_1)$ and $(T_2, t_2)$:

$$
\sqrt{D_1 \, t_1} \;=\; \sqrt{D_2 \, t_2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Arrhenius form.**
Using $D_i = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT_i)$ and noting $D_0$ cancels:

$$
D_0 \, e^{-Q/(RT_1)} \, t_1 \;=\; D_0 \, e^{-Q/(RT_2)} \, t_2
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t_2$.**

$$
t_2 \;=\; t_1 \, \exp\!\left[\frac{Q}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\;t_2 \;=\; t_1 \, \exp\!\left[\frac{Q}{R}\!\left(\frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 T_2}\right)\right]\;}
$$

**Engineering utility — Worked numerical sense-check:**
For titanium (Q ≈ 150 kJ/mol), bonding at 900 °C (1173 K) for 4 h. If T rises to 950 °C (1223 K), by what factor does time decrease?

$$
\text{Factor} \;=\; \exp\!\left[\frac{150000}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1223 - 1173}{1173 \cdot 1223}\right)\right]
$$

$$
\text{Factor} \;=\; \exp\!\left[\frac{150000 \cdot 50}{8.314 \cdot 1{,}434{,}379}\right]
$$

$$
\text{Factor} \;=\; \exp(0.628) \;\approx\; 1.87
$$

So a **50 K** temperature rise **nearly halves** the required bonding time.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Stage-2 Void Shrinkage Rate

The void radius r(t) shrinks by surface diffusion driven by curvature-induced chemical-potential gradient.

**Step 1 — Chemical potential on a curved surface.**
For a surface of radius r, the Gibbs–Thomson relation gives:

$$
\Delta \mu \;=\; \frac{2 \, \gamma_s \, \Omega}{r}
$$

**Step 2 — Driving force per atom.**
This excess potential drives an atomic flux from the void surface to the adjacent grain boundary (or bulk):

$$
J \;=\; -\frac{D}{kT} \cdot \nabla \mu
$$

**Step 3 — Mass conservation.**
The flux of atoms leaving the void surface reduces r at rate:

$$
\frac{dr}{dt} \;=\; -\frac{D \, \gamma_s \, \Omega}{r \, k \, T} \cdot f(\text{geometry})
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate.**
For an isolated spherical void with geometric factor ≈ 1:

$$
\int_{r_0}^{r} r \, dr \;=\; -\frac{D \, \gamma_s \, \Omega}{k \, T} \int_0^{t} dt
$$

$$
\frac{r_0^{2} - r^{2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{D \, \gamma_s \, \Omega}{k \, T} \, t
$$

**Step 5 — Final form.**

$$
\boxed{\;r^{2}(t) \;=\; r_0^{2} \;-\; \frac{2 \, D \, \gamma_s \, \Omega}{k \, T} \, t\;}
$$

**Key insight:** Void shrinkage is **r²-linear in time**, meaning a void of initial radius 10 μm takes **100× longer** to close than one of radius 1 μm. This is why **surface finish (small r₀)** is non-negotiable in production-grade diffusion bonding.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Diffusion Bonding vs. Fusion Welding vs. Brazing

| Criterion | Diffusion Bonding | Fusion Welding | Brazing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Base metal melts? | **No** | Yes | No |
| Filler metal melts? | Usually no | Often yes | **Yes** |
| HAZ formed? | Minimal | Large | Minimal |
| Joint strength vs. parent | **95–100 %** | 80–100 % | 50–90 % |
| Dissimilar metal capability | **Excellent** | Poor | Moderate |
| Distortion | Very low | High | Low |
| Equipment cost | Very high | Moderate | Low |
| Cycle time | Long (h) | Short (s–min) | Short (min) |
| Joint thickness limit | None | None | < ~6 mm typically |
| KTU 2024 mark weightage | High | High | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Component Cleaning] --> B[Surface Preparation Ra less than 0.4 micrometer]
    B --> C[Stacking and Alignment in Fixture]
    C --> D[Load into Vacuum Hot Press Chamber]
    D --> E[Evacuate to 10 power minus 3 Pa]
    E --> F[Heat to 0.5 to 0.8 Tm]
    F --> G[Apply Pressure 1 to 100 MPa]
    G --> H[STAGE 1: Plastic Deformation and Asperity Collapse]
    H --> I[STAGE 2: Surface and Volume Diffusion Closes Voids]
    I --> J[STAGE 3: Recrystallization Across Interface]
    J --> K[Controlled Cool under Pressure]
    K --> L[Vacuum Release and Unloading]
    L --> M[NDT Inspection UT and Metallography]
    M --> N[END: Bonded Assembly]
```

### 4.2 Diffusion Bonding Equipment — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER[Power and Control Block]
        P1[PID Temperature Controller] --> P2[Heating Element Graphite or Induction]
        P3[Hydraulic or Mechanical Press] --> P4[Pressure Transducer]
    end

    subgraph CHAMBER[Vacuum Process Chamber]
        C1[Upper Platen] --> C2[Workpiece Stack]
        C2 --> C3[Lower Platen]
        C4[Thermocouple Bank] --> C2
    end

    subgraph VAC[Vacuum and Atmosphere System]
        V1[Rotary Pump] --> V2[Diffusion Pump]
        V2 --> V3[Vacuum Gauge]
        V3 --> V4[Chamber Pressure Display]
    end

    subgraph DAQ[Data Acquisition]
        D1[Real Time T Logger] --> D2[Real Time P Logger]
        D2 --> D3[Process Record Storage]
    end

    P2 --> C1
    P3 --> C1
    P4 --> D2
    C1 --> CHAMBER
    C3 --> CHAMBER
    V4 --> C1
    C4 --> D1
```

### 4.3 Microstructural Evolution at the Interface — Stage-Wise Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Initial State Two Flat Surfaces with Asperities] --> A1[Stage 1 Asperities Yield Contact Area Reaches 30 to 40 percent]
    A1 --> A2[Stage 2 Diffusion Closes Voids Remain as Scattered Pores]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 3 Recrystallization New Grains Cross Original Interface]
    A3 --> A4[Final State Joint Indistinguishable from Parent Metal]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
Define diffusion bonding. List any **four** process parameters that govern the quality of the joint.

**Model Answer:**
> **Diffusion bonding** is a *solid-state joining process* in which two surfaces are joined under sustained pressure at a temperature below the melting point of the parent metal (typically **0.5 – 0.8 T_m**) without macroscopic melting. Metallurgical continuity is achieved by **atomic diffusion** across the interface.
>
> Four governing process parameters:
> 1. **Bonding temperature** (T) — governs diffusion kinetics.
> 2. **Applied pressure** (P) — governs asperity collapse in Stage 1.
> 3. **Dwell time** (t) — governs void closure in Stage 2.
> 4. **Surface finish / roughness** (Ra) — determines initial void population.
>
> *(Atmosphere purity and interlayer composition are also accepted.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
Explain briefly the **three stages** of bond formation in diffusion bonding.

**Model Answer:**
> 1. **Stage 1 – Plastic deformation & contact-area growth:** Surface asperities yield under P and T; real contact area increases to 30–40 %. Mechanism: power-law creep and local plastic flow.
> 2. **Stage 2 – Diffusion-controlled void elimination:** Remaining interfacial voids shrink by surface, grain-boundary and volume diffusion driven by surface energy and stress gradients.
> 3. **Stage 3 – Recrystallization and grain growth:** New grains nucleate at the original interface and grow across it; the "witness line" disappears; bond strength approaches parent-metal strength.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]`

**(a)** With the aid of a suitable diagram, explain the **principle, mechanism, and stages** of diffusion bonding. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** Two faying surfaces, prepared to a mirror finish (Ra ≤ 0.4 μm), are brought into intimate contact inside a vacuum hot-press. The temperature is raised to 0.5–0.8 T_m and a controlled pressure is applied. No melting occurs. Atoms migrate across the interface by thermally activated diffusion, gradually eliminating the original boundary and forming a metallurgically continuous joint.

**Mechanism (King–Owczarski three-stage model):**

| Stage | Dominant Mechanism | Driving Force | Net Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | Plastic flow + power-law creep | Applied stress σ | Contact area ↑ to 30–40 % |
| II | Surface + volume + GB diffusion | Surface energy γ_s | Voids shrink |
| III | Recrystallization | Stored strain energy | Grains traverse interface |

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.3 block diagram above.

The joint is *not* completed until Stage 3 eliminates the original interface; prematurely stopping the cycle leaves a "kiss-bond" with severely degraded fatigue life.

**(b)** Discuss the **process parameters** of diffusion bonding and their influence on **bond quality**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Temperature (T):** Most influential parameter. Affects D *exponentially* via Arrhenius equation. Raising T by 50 K can halve required bonding time. However, excessive T causes grain coarsening and possible phase transformation.
2. **Pressure (P):** Controls Stage 1 asperity collapse. Must exceed local yield strength at T. Excessive P causes macroscopic deformation of the part.
3. **Time (t):** Required for Stage 2 void closure; very slow (r² ∝ t). Diminishing returns past ~8 h.
4. **Surface finish:** Mirror polish (Ra ≤ 0.4 μm) minimizes Stage-2 workload; rough surfaces leave large voids that may never fully close.
5. **Atmosphere:** Vacuum of 10⁻³ Pa or better prevents oxide formation; Ti, Zr, Al are extremely oxide-sensitive.
6. **Interlayer (when used):** A thin foil (Ni, Cu, Ag) can eutectic-melt briefly, providing local liquefaction that helps fill voids at lower T than bulk bonding.

Bond quality is typically evaluated by:
- **Tensile strength** (target ≥ 95 % of parent)
- **Fatigue life** at the joint
- **Metallographic inspection** (no visible interface line)
- **Ultrasonic NDT** (no echo from interface)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain **Transient Liquid Phase (TLP) bonding**. Compare it with **conventional solid-state diffusion bonding**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**TLP Bonding Principle:**
A thin interlayer (typically 5 – 100 μm thick) of an alloy with a *lower melting point* than the parent is placed between the faying surfaces. On heating to a temperature **between the interlayer melting point and the parent melting point**, the interlayer melts briefly, wetting the surfaces and filling micro-voids by capillary action — *without* melting the parent metal. The assembly is held isothermally while **solute atoms diffuse from the liquid into the parent**, raising the local liquidus temperature until the liquid **isothermally solidifies**. The result is a joint whose final remelt temperature is **higher** than the bonding temperature — hence the name *transient* liquid phase.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Conventional DB | TLP Bonding |
|---|---|---|
| Bonding temperature | 0.5 – 0.8 T_m | 0.4 – 0.7 T_m (lower) |
| Liquid phase present? | **No** | **Yes, briefly** |
| Interlayer required? | Optional | **Mandatory** |
| Void filling capability | Limited by diffusion | Excellent (liquid fills) |
| Final joint remelt temp | = bonding temp | **>** bonding temp |
| Time required | Long (h) | Moderate (min–h) |
| Surface finish sensitivity | Very high | **Lower** (liquid compensates) |
| Best suited for | Similar metals, ultra-clean joints | Dissimilar metals, repair joints |

**(b)** Describe the **applications** of diffusion bonding in **aerospace and nuclear industries**. List its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Applications:**

*Aerospace:*
- **Titanium leading edges** for aircraft wings and empennage (Boeing 787, Airbus A350).
- **Honeycomb sandwich panels** for floor beams and control surfaces.
- **Turbine-engine components** — single-crystal superalloy vanes bonded to polycrystalline disks.
- **Combustion chamber liners** in nickel superalloys.

*Nuclear:*
- **Zircaloy-4 fuel cladding end-plugs** in pressurized-water reactors.
- **Beryllium-to-stainless-steel** transitions in plasma-facing components.
- **Dispersion-strengthened ferritic steel** joints for fusion reactor blankets.

**Advantages:**
1. Joins **dissimilar metals** impossible to fusion-weld (Ti–Al, Cu–Mo, Zr–SS).
2. **No HAZ**, no liquation cracking, no porosity.
3. **Joint strength ≈ parent metal strength** (95 – 100 %).
4. Suitable for **brittle intermetallics** and **composites**.
5. Minimal **distortion** — critical for thin aerospace skins.
6. Excellent **vacuum-tight** joints — critical for nuclear and electronic packaging.

**Limitations:**
1. **Very high equipment cost** (vacuum hot presses).
2. **Long cycle times** (hours) → low throughput.
3. **Limited to small-to-medium components** (chamber-size bound).
4. **Demanding surface preparation** — cost-driver.
5. **Sensitive to oxide formation** — requires strict atmosphere control.
6. Difficult to inspect **in-process** — high NDT cost post-bond.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating TLP bonding with brazing.** They are *not* the same — TLP isothermally solidifies and the joint remelts at a *higher* temperature than the bonding temperature. Brazing does not.
> 2. **Forgetting the temperature ratio.** Always quote bonding temperature as a *fraction of T_m* (e.g., 0.7 T_m), not as an absolute number — KTU examiners look for this.
> 3. **Skipping Stage 3.** Many students describe only Stages 1 and 2. Stage 3 (recrystallization) is what produces the *parent-metal-strength* bond and is mandatory for full marks.
> 4. **Mixing units.** Use SI throughout: T in **K**, pressure in **MPa**, D in **m²·s⁻¹**. Mixing °C with J creates instant deduction.
> 5. **Ignoring atmosphere.** A diffusion-bonding answer without mentioning vacuum / oxide prevention is treated as incomplete.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Diffusion bonding is a **solid-state process** — **no melting**.
- ✅ Typical bonding temperature: **0.5 – 0.8 T_m**; pressure: **1 – 100 MPa**; time: **15 min – 24 h**.
- ✅ Three stages: **(1) Plastic deformation → (2) Void elimination → (3) Recrystallization.**
- ✅ Core equation: $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ — Arrhenius, **memorize it.**
- ✅ Surface finish **Ra ≤ 0.4 μm** and **vacuum ≥ 10⁻³ Pa** are non-negotiable.
- ✅ Best process for **dissimilar metals, superalloys, Ti, Zr, MMCs.**
- ✅ **TLP bonding** uses a low-melt interlayer that *transiently* liquefies then isothermally solidifies.
- ✅ Joint strength achievable: **95 – 100 %** of parent metal.
- ✅ **Applications:** Aerospace (Ti panels, turbine vanes), Nuclear (Zircaloy cladding), Electronics (Kovar packaging).
- ✅ **Drawbacks:** High equipment cost, long cycle, small part size, strict surface/atmosphere control.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Frequency Induction Welding - Working Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** High Frequency Induction Welding - Working Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**High Frequency Induction Welding (HFIW)** is an advanced solid-state fusion welding process in which the heat required for joining is generated at the abutting edges of the workpiece (typically a continuous metal strip or tube) by inducing a high-frequency alternating current (in the range of **100 kHz to 800 kHz**, industrially most commonly **400–500 kHz**) into the edges to be joined. The current flows preferentially along the closely-spaced edges due to combined electromagnetic phenomena, locally raises the temperature to the forging range (often **0.8 to 0.95 of the solidus temperature**, i.e., near-melting but technically a "forge weld"), and the edges are then pressed together by squeeze rolls to form a continuous longitudinal seam.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> HFIW is classified under *advanced resistance-type welding processes*. Although heat is generated by induced eddy currents, the actual coalescence mechanism is a *forge weld* driven by squeeze pressure — the metal is not melted in the classical arc-welding sense. This distinction is **frequently asked** in KTU examinations.

### 1.2 The Two Physical Mechanisms

The process fundamentally exploits **two electromagnetic phenomena** that occur only at high frequencies:

1. **Skin Effect** — At high frequencies, alternating current does not flow uniformly through a conductor's cross-section. Instead, it is displaced toward the *outer surface*. The current density decays exponentially from the surface inward. The depth at which current density falls to 1/e (≈ 36.8 %) of its surface value is called the **skin depth (δ)**.

2. **Proximity Effect** — When two conductors carrying current in *opposite directions* are placed close to each other, the current redistributes such that it concentrates on the *facing sides* of the conductors. This is precisely the geometry at the vee-converging point of a tube-welding line, where the strip edges act as the two closely-spaced current-carrying conductors.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Electric River"

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Two Rivers Crashing Into Each Other"**
> Imagine two fast-flowing streams of water moving in **opposite directions** right next to each other, separated only by a thin strip of land. As they crash together, the water violently churns and foams exactly at the meeting point. The "water" in our case is high-frequency electric current, the "thin strip of land" is the metal edges about to be welded, and the "churning heat" is what fuses them. Because high-frequency current is "shy" and refuses to travel through the bulk of the metal (skin effect), and it is also "magnetic" and gets pulled toward any nearby opposite-flowing current (proximity effect), almost **all of the heating occurs in a tiny sliver right at the joint line** — making the process extremely fast and efficient.

### 1.4 Industrial Standard Metrics (KTU-Expected Values)

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| Operating Frequency | **400 – 500 kHz** (range: 100 – 800 kHz) |
| Current magnitude | **10,000 – 50,000 A** (concentrated in skin layer) |
| Welding speed | **30 – 120 m/min** for tube mills |
| Tube diameter range | **6 mm to 660 mm** (0.25" to 26") |
| Wall thickness range | **0.4 mm to 16 mm** |
| Skin depth at 450 kHz (steel) | ≈ **0.10 – 0.25 mm** |
| Coil efficiency | **80 – 90 %** (induction); 50 – 60 % (contact) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition Box — Forge Weld vs Fusion Weld**
> In HFIW, although temperatures approach the melting point, the edges are not actually *puddled* into a liquid weld pool. Plasticized metal is *extruded* along with oxides and contaminants from the joint line, forming the characteristic **inside and outside flash (bead)**. Therefore, HFIW is structurally classified as a **forge-pressure weld**, not a fusion weld.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" and "How" — Step-by-Step Operating Logic

The working principle of HFIW can be broken down into six distinct physical stages, each of which must be understood for KTU board-level answers:

- **Stage 1 — Current Generation & Delivery**
  *Why:* A high-frequency (HF) source — usually a **vacuum-tube oscillator** or modern **solid-state inverter (MOSFET/IGBT)** — generates alternating current at the welding frequency.
  *How:* The current is delivered to the workpiece either through *sliding contact electrodes* (Conrac/contact method) or through an *HF transformer with a single-turn secondary formed by the strip itself* (induction method).

- **Stage 2 — Skin Effect Concentration**
  *Why:* At 450 kHz, current cannot penetrate deep into the steel. It is forced to flow within a thin skin layer (≈ 0.1–0.2 mm) at the surface.
  *How:* The skin depth **δ** is inversely proportional to √(frequency × permeability × conductivity), so increasing frequency dramatically localizes the current.

- **Stage 3 — Vee-Converging Point Geometry**
  *Why:* The formed strip is shaped by successive roller stands into an open tube, and the final "vee" of the open seam — the point where the edges are closest to each other at an included angle of **2° to 6°** — is the welding zone.
  *How:* This geometry guarantees a *very small gap* between the two edges, which concentrates the current density at the seam.

- **Stage 4 — Proximity Effect Edge Concentration**
  *Why:* The two strip edges, separated by a tiny gap and carrying current in opposite directions (one edge is the "go" path, the other is the "return" path of the same induced loop), experience mutual attraction of their magnetic fields.
  *How:* This magnetic coupling forces current to crowd into the *innermost corners* of the vee, creating an intensely hot line.

- **Stage 5 — Heat Generation by I²R**
  *Why:* The concentrated current passes through the small cross-section of metal at the vee point, producing immense resistive (Joule) heating.
  *How:* Power density at the seam reaches **10⁷ – 10⁸ W/m²**, heating the metal to its plastic range in milliseconds.

- **Stage 6 — Squeeze / Forge Consolidation**
  *Why:* Molten or plasticized metal, along with surface oxides, must be physically expelled from the joint to achieve metallurgical bonding.
  *How:* A pair of **squeeze rolls** (also called forge rolls or impeder rolls) placed immediately downstream of the welding point apply a forging pressure of typically **30 – 100 MPa**, forcing the edges together and squeezing out the flash.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Pearl**
> The *impeder* (a ferritic or laminated steel core placed *inside* the tube behind the welding point) is a critical accessory. It **doubles the magnetic flux** at the seam and pushes the return-current path away from the inside surface, thereby preventing the current from "leaking" backward and re-heating the already-welded zone.

### 2.2 The Two Industrial Variants of HFIW

KTU examiners frequently ask students to *compare* the two methods. Both exploit skin + proximity effect, but the *current delivery mechanism* differs:

| Feature | Contact (Conrac) Type | Induction Type |
|---|---|---|
| Current delivery | Sliding bronze/carbon contacts on the strip surface | Single-turn secondary induced through an HF transformer coil |
| Operating frequency | Lower (≈ 100 – 400 kHz) | Higher (≈ 400 – 800 kHz) |
| Electrical efficiency | 50 – 60 % | 80 – 90 % |
| Contact wear | Yes — periodic replacement | None (contactless) |
| Weld quality consistency | Slightly variable | Highly consistent |
| Typical tube diameter | Larger (50 – 660 mm) | Smaller to medium (6 – 200 mm) |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on this topic.**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$ | Skin depth — depth of current penetration | metres (m) |
| 2 | $\delta = 503\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\mu_r f}}$ | Engineering form of skin depth | mm (with $\rho$ in Ω·m, $f$ in Hz) |
| 3 | $P = I^{2}\,R$ | Joule heating at the seam | Watts (W) |
| 4 | $H = \int I^{2} R\, dt$ | Total heat input per unit length | J/m |
| 5 | $P_d = \dfrac{I^{2} R}{V_{seam}}$ | Volumetric power density at the seam | W/m³ |
| 6 | $f \geq \dfrac{1}{\pi \mu \sigma d^{2}}$ | Minimum frequency required for full skin effect (d = strip thickness) | Hz |
| 7 | $v_{weld} = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\pi d\, t\, \rho_{m}\, c\, \Delta T}$ | Theoretical maximum welding speed | m/s |
| 8 | $F_{squeeze} = p \cdot A_{contact}$ | Forge force at squeeze rolls | N |

Where:
- $\rho$ = electrical resistivity of the metal (Ω·m)
- $f$ = operating frequency (Hz)
- $\mu$ = absolute magnetic permeability = $\mu_0 \mu_r$ (H/m)
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m (vacuum permeability)
- $\mu_r$ = relative permeability (steel ≈ 100–500; austenitic SS ≈ 1)
- $\sigma$ = electrical conductivity (S/m)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $R$ = resistance (Ω)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

HFIW is the **dominant global process** for mass-producing welded steel tubes and pipes used in:
- **Oil & Gas transmission pipelines** (API 5L grade ERW/HFIW pipes)
- **Automotive chassis and exhaust tubing**
- **Heat exchanger and boiler tubes**
- **Structural hollow sections (SHS)** for construction
- **Furniture, handrails, and furniture-grade tube**
- **Stainless steel tubes for food, dairy, and pharmaceutical industries**

> [!TIP]
> **Why Industry Prefers HFIW Over Conventional ERW**
> At mains frequency (50 Hz), current spreads throughout the entire strip cross-section — most of the electrical energy is wasted heating bulk metal that does not need heating. At 450 kHz, energy is delivered *only* where it is needed, giving HFIW a **5× to 10× higher thermal efficiency** and welding speeds that conventional ERW (at 50 Hz) cannot match.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Skin Depth Equation (Full Working)

Starting from Maxwell's equations for a planar conductor carrying alternating current, the electric field **E(z,t)** inside the conductor satisfies the one-dimensional diffusion equation. We derive the skin depth rigorously so the student can reproduce it in a KTU exam.

**Starting Maxwell Equation (Faraday's Law for plane conductor):**

$$\dfrac{\partial B}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial z}$$

Since $B = \mu H$ and using Ampère's law $\nabla \times H = J$:

$$\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial z} = J = \sigma E$$

Differentiating Faraday's law with respect to z and substituting:

$$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\dfrac{\partial B}{\partial t}\right) = -\dfrac{\partial^{2} E}{\partial z^{2}}$$

$$\mu \sigma \dfrac{\partial E}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial^{2} E}{\partial z^{2}}$$

For sinusoidal steady state, let $E(z,t) = \hat{E}(z)\, e^{j\omega t}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu \sigma \, j\omega \,\hat{E}(z) &= -\dfrac{d^{2}\hat{E}(z)}{dz^{2}} \\
\dfrac{d^{2}\hat{E}}{dz^{2}} &= j\omega\mu\sigma\,\hat{E} \\
\dfrac{d^{2}\hat{E}}{dz^{2}} &= \alpha^{2}\hat{E} \quad\text{where } \alpha = \sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma}
\end{aligned}$$

Writing $\sqrt{j} = \dfrac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$, we have:

$$\alpha = (1+j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}} = (1+j)k \quad\text{where } k = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$$

The physically valid solution that decays into the conductor (z ≥ 0) is:

$$\hat{E}(z) = \hat{E}_{0}\, e^{-\alpha z} = \hat{E}_{0}\, e^{-(1+j)kz} = \hat{E}_{0}\, e^{-kz}\, e^{-jkz}$$

The **magnitude decays as $e^{-kz}$**, so current density follows the same pattern:

$$J(z) = J_{0}\, e^{-z/\delta} \quad\text{where the skin depth is}$$

$$\boxed{\;\delta = \dfrac{1}{k} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}\;}$$

Since $\sigma = 1/\rho$:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}} \quad\blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Skin Depth for Mild Steel at 450 kHz

This is a **favourite KTU numerical problem** (Part B, 7 marks).

**Given:**
- Mild steel: $\rho = 1.0 \times 10^{-7}\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$
- Relative permeability: $\mu_r = 200$ (at high frequency, lower than DC value)
- Frequency: $f = 450\ \text{kHz} = 4.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Hz}$
- Vacuum permeability: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$

**Step 1 — Compute absolute permeability:**

$$\mu = \mu_0\mu_r = (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(200) = 2.513 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{H/m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply skin depth formula:**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times 4.5 \times 10^{5} \times 2.513 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-7}}{3.553 \times 10^{2}}}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{2.814 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\delta = 1.677 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 0.0168\ \text{mm} \approx 16.8\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A skin depth of only **≈ 17 µm** at 450 kHz is *thousands of times smaller* than the strip thickness (typically 1–6 mm). This means the current is *absolutely confined* to a microscopic layer at the surface and the abutting edge — which is precisely why HFIW is so efficient.

### 3.3 Derivation 2 — Why Doubling Frequency Reduces Skin Depth by √2

A common sub-question asks students to compare skin depths at two different frequencies.

$$\dfrac{\delta_{1}}{\delta_{2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f_{1}\mu}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi f_{2}\mu}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_{2}}{f_{1}}}$$

If $f_2 = 2 f_1$:

$$\delta_{2} = \dfrac{\delta_{1}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\,\delta_{1}$$

Thus **doubling frequency reduces skin depth by 30 %** and concentrates power into half the depth, nearly doubling the volumetric power density.

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Heat Generation at the Vee Point

The total heat generated per unit length of seam per unit time is:

$$P = I^{2} R_{seam} = I^{2}\,\dfrac{\rho\,L_{eff}}{A_{eff}}$$

where $A_{eff} = \delta \times w$ (skin depth × strip width contribution) and $L_{eff}$ is the effective length of the current path through the vee region. The resulting **volumetric power density** is:

$$P_{v} = \dfrac{P}{A_{eff}\,L_{eff}} = \dfrac{I^{2}\,\rho}{(\delta w)^{2}}$$

This extraordinarily high power density is what makes the process viable at high welding speeds.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HFIW vs Conventional ERW

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Table for KTU Compare-and-Contrast Questions**

| Aspect | Conventional ERW (50 Hz) | High Frequency Welding (HFIW, 400–500 kHz) |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | 50 Hz | 400 – 500 kHz |
| Skin depth in steel | ≈ 20 mm (full thickness) | ≈ 0.02 mm (surface only) |
| Current path | Entire cross-section | Only at the abutting edges |
| Weld nugget shape | Elliptical, deep | Narrow, shallow, line-type |
| Heat-affected zone (HAZ) | Wide, deep | Very narrow, shallow |
| Welding speed | 10 – 30 m/min | 30 – 120 m/min |
| Thermal efficiency | 30 – 40 % | 80 – 90 % |
| Suitable for stainless | Difficult (low conductivity) | Excellent (high $\rho$ favours skin effect) |
| Power consumption | High | 50 – 70 % lower |
| Weld seam appearance | Visible, wide bead | Smooth, narrow, can be nearly invisible after flash removal |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Process Flow — Complete HFIW Tube Mill Line

This diagram maps the **complete functional architecture** of an industrial HFIW tube manufacturing line, from coil to cut pipe. Every station is labelled and the welding-zone components are clearly highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coil Uncoiler] --> B[Strip End Welder and Accumululator]
    B --> C[Forming Roller Stand 1: Edge Breaking]
    C --> D[Forming Roller Stand 2 to N: Progressive Bending]
    D --> E[Fin Pass Rolls: Open Tube with Vee Seam]
    E --> F[High Frequency Coil or Contact Tips: Welding Zone]
    G[Impeder Ferrite Core Inside Tube] --- F
    F --> H[Squeeze Rolls: Forge Consolidation and Flash Expulsion]
    H --> I[Seam Annealing Induction Coil Optional]
    I --> J[Water Spray Cooling Chamber]
    J --> K[Sizing and Straightening Rolls]
    K --> L[Cut off Flying Saw]
    L --> M[Finished ERW HFIW Tube]
    H --> N[Flash Scarfing or Trimming Tool]
    N --> M
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Welding-Zone Functional Architecture

The following diagram zooms into the **welding zone itself**, showing how the current flows, where the impeder sits, and where the squeeze pressure is applied.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HF[HIGH FREQUENCY POWER SOURCE 400 to 500 kHz]
        OSC[Oscillator and Inverter] --> XFM[HF Transformer]
    end
    XFM --> COIL[Single Turn Induction Coil Wrapped Around Tube]
    COIL -.induces.-> EDGE1[Left Strip Edge Current Direction Up]
    EDGE1 <-->|Gap 1 to 3 mm Plasticized Metal| EDGE2[Right Strip Edge Current Direction Down]
    EDGE2 --> IMP[Impeder Core Forces Return Path Outward]
    IMP --> TUBE[Already Welded Tube Body]
    TUBE --> SQ[Squeeze Rolls Apply 30 to 100 MPa Forge Force]
    SQ --> SEAM[Consolidated Longitudinal Seam]
    SEAM --> FLASH[Inside and Outside Flash Expelled]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Key Physical Phenomena Interrelationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HF[High Frequency AC 400 to 500 kHz Applied] --> SKIN[Skin Effect: Current Confined to Thin Surface Layer]
    HF --> PROX[Proximity Effect: Current Crowds Toward Facing Edges]
    SKIN --> DELTA[Skin Depth Delta decreases as sqrt 1 over f]
    PROX --> VEE[Vee Converging Geometry Maximises Magnetic Coupling]
    DELTA --> CONC[Current Density at Edge: 10^7 to 10^8 A per m squared]
    VEE --> CONC
    CONC --> HEAT[I squared R Joule Heating in Plastic Range]
    HEAT --> FORGE[Squeeze Rolls Forge Weld the Edges]
    FORGE --> FINAL[Continuous Longitudinal Seam with Flash Bead]
    IMP[Impeder Core] -.enhances.-> VEE
    IMP -.prevents.-> LEAK[Current Leakage Backward Along Weld]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description of the Vee-Converging Point (Textual)

For students who must describe the geometry in a written exam:

> [!NOTE]
> **Vee-Converging Geometry — Verbal Description for KTU Theory Answers**
> "The open tube is shaped so that the two longitudinal edges meet at a precisely controlled included angle of **2° to 6°** immediately upstream of the welding station. This V-shaped opening is called the *veepoint*. The HF coil (or contact tips) is positioned such that its electromagnetic field intersects the strip at this exact location. The high-frequency current, induced in the strip, flows along one edge toward the veepoint, crosses the small gap (1–3 mm) through the plasticized metal, and returns along the opposite edge — forming a single closed loop whose current path is restricted by the skin effect to a depth of about 0.02 mm. The impeder (ferrite core) sits inside the tube, behind the veepoint, blocking the magnetic flux from spreading to the inside surface and forcing the return current to take the longer, less-resistive path around the outside of the tube — which would otherwise wastefully re-heat the already-welded seam."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**
>
> **Q1. Define High Frequency Induction Welding. State any two distinguishing features of the process.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> High Frequency Induction Welding (HFIW) is a solid-state welding process in which heat for joining the abutting edges of a continuously formed metal strip is generated by inducing a high-frequency alternating current (typically 400–500 kHz) into the strip and exploiting the *skin effect* and *proximity effect* to concentrate the current at the edges. (**2 marks**)
> Distinguishing features: (**1 mark — any two**)
> 1. The current is induced contactlessly via an HF transformer coil (no sliding contacts to wear out).
> 2. Heat is generated only in a microscopic skin layer (≈ 0.02 mm deep) at the seam, giving very high thermal efficiency and narrow HAZ.
> 3. The process is classified as a forge-pressure weld, not a fusion weld, because the edges are squeezed together in the plastic state rather than melted into a pool.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**
>
> **Q2. What is the role of an *impeder* in HFIW tube welding?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An impeder is a magnetic core (ferrite or laminated steel) placed *inside* the tube, directly behind the welding point. (**1 mark**) It performs two essential functions: (**2 marks**)
> 1. It **concentrates the magnetic flux** at the veepoint, thereby *increasing* the current density and welding heat at the seam.
> 2. It **prevents the induced current from flowing along the inside surface of the tube** (which would re-heat the already-welded seam and degrade weld quality), forcing the return current to take a longer path around the outside of the tube.
> Without the impeder, the welding efficiency drops sharply and the inside surface of the tube overheats, producing defects.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the working principle of High Frequency Induction Welding with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of *skin effect* and *proximity effect* in concentrating heat at the seam. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A continuous mild steel strip of thickness 4 mm is to be welded into a tube of 50 mm outer diameter using HFIW at a frequency of 450 kHz. Given the resistivity of steel as $1.0 \times 10^{-7}\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$ and relative permeability as 200, calculate:
  (i) the skin depth of the induced current, and
  (ii) the percentage reduction in skin depth if the frequency is increased to 600 kHz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle — 7 marks:**

- **Step 1 — Strip forming (1 mark):** A cold-rolled steel strip is fed from a coil through a series of forming rollers that progressively bend it into an open cylindrical shape. The final forming pass leaves the two longitudinal edges nearly parallel but separated by a small *vee-angle* of 2°–6°, with a gap of 1–3 mm at the closest point (the *veepoint*).

- **Step 2 — High-frequency current induction (2 marks):** A high-frequency AC source (400–500 kHz) drives current through a single-turn induction coil that wraps around the tube at the veepoint. By transformer action, a large current is induced in the strip itself, which acts as a single-turn secondary. The current flows along one edge toward the veepoint, crosses the small gap, and returns along the opposite edge, forming a closed loop.

- **Step 3 — Skin effect (1.5 marks):** At high frequency, AC does not penetrate deeply into the conductor. The current density decays exponentially from the surface inward with characteristic skin depth $\delta = \sqrt{\rho / (\pi f \mu)}$. For steel at 450 kHz, δ is only ≈ 0.02 mm, so the current is *confined to a microscopic surface layer*.

- **Step 4 — Proximity effect (1.5 marks):** Because the two strip edges carry current in *opposite directions* and are *very close* to each other at the veepoint, the magnetic fields from each edge attract the current of the other edge toward the *facing surfaces*. This causes the current to crowd into a narrow line right at the joint, generating enormous volumetric power density (10⁷–10⁸ W/m³) and heating the edges to the plastic range in milliseconds.

- **Step 5 — Squeeze consolidation (1 mark):** Immediately downstream, a pair of squeeze rolls forge the plasticized edges together at 30–100 MPa, expelling molten metal and oxides as inside and outside flash and producing a continuous solid-phase forge weld.

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks:**

**(i) Skin depth at 450 kHz (3.5 marks):**

Given: $\rho = 1.0 \times 10^{-7}\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$, $\mu_r = 200$, $f_1 = 450\ \text{kHz} = 4.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Hz}$

Absolute permeability:

$$\mu = \mu_0\mu_r = (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(200) = 2.513 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{H/m}$$

Skin depth:

$$\delta_1 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f_1 \mu}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times 4.5 \times 10^{5} \times 2.513 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

$$\delta_1 = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-7}}{3.553 \times 10^{2}}} = \sqrt{2.814 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.677 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_1 \approx 0.0168\ \text{mm} = 16.8\ \mu\text{m}}$$

**(ii) Percentage reduction at 600 kHz (3.5 marks):**

Since $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$:

$$\delta_2 = \delta_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{f_1}{f_2}} = 0.0168 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{450}{600}} = 0.0168 \times \sqrt{0.75} = 0.0168 \times 0.8660$$

$$\delta_2 = 0.01455\ \text{mm} = 14.55\ \mu\text{m}$$

Percentage reduction:

$$\%\text{ reduction} = \dfrac{\delta_1 - \delta_2}{\delta_1} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.0168 - 0.01455}{0.0168} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%\text{ reduction} \approx 13.4\ \%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B (a)**
> Students frequently lose 1.5–2 marks by:
> 1. Confusing *skin effect* with *proximity effect* — they are *different* phenomena. Skin effect is the surface-confinement of AC in a *single* conductor; proximity effect is the *mutual* attraction of current toward a nearby opposite-flowing conductor. State them as two separate bullet points.
> 2. Forgetting to mention the *impeder* — it is almost always a 1-mark item in 7-mark questions.
> 3. Writing "the metal melts" — incorrect. HFIW is a *forge weld* in the plastic state, not a fusion weld with a liquid pool.

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare and contrast High Frequency Induction Welding with conventional Electric Resistance Welding at mains frequency (50 Hz). Discuss the relative advantages, limitations, and typical applications of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a labelled schematic, describe the complete HFIW tube manufacturing line from uncoiling the strip to cutting the finished tube. Explain the function of the impeder and the squeeze rolls. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline (for student reference):**

**(a) Comparison — 7 marks (1 mark per correct contrasting point × 6 points + 1 mark for conclusion):**

| Aspect | Conventional ERW (50 Hz) | HFIW (400–500 kHz) |
|---|---|---|
| Skin depth | 10–20 mm (covers full strip) | 0.015–0.025 mm (surface only) |
| Current path | Through entire cross-section | Only at the abutting edges |
| Heat concentration | Wide, deep nugget | Narrow, line-type at seam |
| HAZ | Wide | Very narrow |
| Welding speed | 10–30 m/min | 30–120 m/min |
| Efficiency | 30–40 % | 80–90 % |
| Equipment cost | Low | Higher (HF source) |
| Stainless steel suitability | Poor | Excellent |
| Applications | Heavy structural pipe | Thin-walled precision tubes, SS, automotive, oil & gas |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** HFIW is the modern, superior process for most tube/pipe manufacturing applications due to higher speed, narrower HAZ, better surface finish, and the ability to weld high-resistivity metals (austenitic stainless, aluminium) that are difficult to weld with 50 Hz ERW.

**(b) HFIW Tube Mill — 7 marks:**

Refer to the Mermaid flowchart in **Section 4.1** of these notes. Students must list, in order:
1. Uncoiler (0.5 mark)
2. Strip end-welder + accumulator (0.5 mark)
3. Forming rollers (1 mark)
4. Fin pass rolls (0.5 mark)
5. HF induction coil at the veepoint (1.5 marks)
6. Impeder — function stated (1.5 marks)
7. Squeeze rolls — forge force, flash expulsion (1 mark)
8. Cooling, sizing, cut-off (0.5 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B (b)**
> The most common mistake is describing the *forming* and *cutting* stations in excessive detail while *glossing over the actual welding zone*. KTU markers expect detailed explanation of the **HF coil, impeder, and squeeze rolls** — these are the heart of the process and are worth 4 of the 7 marks. Do not skip the impeder's role.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Memorize Before the Exam**

- ✅ HFIW stands for **High Frequency Induction Welding**, operating in the range **100 kHz to 800 kHz**, industrially **400–500 kHz**.
- ✅ HFIW is a **forge-pressure weld**, not a fusion weld — the metal is plasticized, not melted into a pool.
- ✅ The two key electromagnetic phenomena are **skin effect** and **proximity effect** — *memorize both definitions verbatim*.
- ✅ Skin depth formula: $\delta = \sqrt{\rho / (\pi f \mu)}$ — *doubling frequency reduces skin depth by 30 % (factor √2)*.
- ✅ Two delivery methods: **Contact (Conrac)** type and **Induction** type — the induction type has higher efficiency (80–90 %).
- ✅ The welding zone uses a **vee-converging geometry** with a 2°–6° included angle and a 1–3 mm gap at the closest point.
- ✅ The **impeder** (ferrite core inside the tube) doubles flux at the seam and prevents current leakage back along the inside surface.
- ✅ **Squeeze rolls** apply **30–100 MPa** forge pressure, expelling **inside and outside flash** and consolidating the weld.
- ✅ Welding speeds are **5–10× higher** than 50 Hz ERW, with much narrower HAZ and 80–90 % thermal efficiency.
- ✅ HFIW is the standard process for **ERW tubes and pipes**, including API 5L grade line pipes for oil & gas, automotive exhaust, and stainless steel tubing.
- ✅ HFIW is **especially well suited to austenitic stainless steel** because the high resistivity and low permeability (μᵣ ≈ 1) of austenitic SS make the skin effect *even more pronounced* — an examiner's favourite trick question.
- ✅ **Common Pitfall — Do not confuse HFIW with HF resistance welding (HFRW) where contacts touch the strip**. HFIW = contactless induction; HFRW = sliding contact method.
- ✅ **Examination Mantra**: Skin effect + Proximity effect + Vee geometry + Impeder + Squeeze rolls = **the five pillars of HFIW** — structure any 14-mark answer around them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Parameters and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Parameters and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of advanced welding and metal joining, a **Welding Process Parameter** is defined as any independently controllable input variable whose magnitude directly governs the heat input, thermal cycle, fluid dynamics of the weld pool, and ultimately the metallurgical, mechanical, and geometric characteristics of the produced joint. These parameters are the operational "knobs" of a welding system — current, voltage, travel speed, gas flow rate, electrode feed rate, arc length, and electrode angle are canonical examples.

Mathematically, the **Heat Input (HI)** — a derived, not directly set, parameter — is the most fundamental response variable, expressed as:

$$HI = \eta \times \frac{V \times I}{S} \times 10^{-3} \;\; (\text{kJ/mm})$$

where $\eta$ is the process thermal efficiency, $V$ is voltage (V), $I$ is current (A), and $S$ is travel speed (mm/s). The dimensionless factor $10^{-3}$ converts Joules to kilojoules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course PEPET741 explicitly demands understanding of process parameters for **Arc, Gas, Resistance, Solid-State, and High-Energy Density (HEDM) welding processes**, with the central focus on the link between parameter selection, weld bead geometry, and application suitability.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are cooking a meal in a pressure cooker. You control the **flame intensity** (current), the **time** you keep the flame on (heat input duration), and the **distance** between the flame and the cooker (arc length). If you crank the flame too high, your food burns; too low, it stays raw. In exactly the same way, a welder must balance current, voltage, and travel speed to "cook" the metal joint just right — not undercooked (lack of fusion) and not overcooked (burn-through, excessive HAZ, distortion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Reminder:** Process parameters are NOT outputs. Outputs are things like weld bead width, depth of penetration, hardness, residual stress. Parameters are the inputs you set on the machine; outputs are what nature gives you back in response.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Welding Parameters

1. **Electrical Parameters** — current (I), voltage (V), polarity, duty cycle, frequency.
2. **Mechanical / Kinematic Parameters** — travel speed (S), wire feed rate (WFR), torch angle, electrode force.
3. **Shielding Gas Parameters** — type, flow rate, composition ratio.
4. **Geometric / Consumable Parameters** — electrode diameter, standoff distance, joint geometry, groove angle.

> [!TIP]
> **Efficiency Values ($\eta$) You Must Memorize:**
> - Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW): $\eta \approx 0.70$ to $0.85$
> - Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG): $\eta \approx 0.70$ to $0.80$
> - Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG): $\eta \approx 0.50$ to $0.70$
> - Plasma Arc Welding (PAW): $\eta \approx 0.60$ to $0.75$
> - Submerged Arc Welding (SAW): $\eta \approx 0.85$ to $0.99$
> - Oxy-Acetylene Gas Welding (OAW): $\eta \approx 0.40$ to $0.50$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Process Parameters

#### 2.1.1 Primary (Directly Set) Parameters

These are dialed in by the operator before striking the arc:

- **Welding Current (I)** in Amperes — controls the rate of filler metal deposition and depth of penetration. For arc processes, the relationship $P_{arc} = V \times I$ holds (in Watts).
- **Arc Voltage (V)** in Volts — affects bead width, arc stability, and spatter.
- **Travel Speed (S)** in mm/s or mm/min — inversely related to heat input per unit length.

#### 2.1.2 Secondary (Derived) Parameters

These emerge from the interaction of primary parameters:

- **Heat Input (HI)** — total energy deposited per unit length of weld.
- **Deposition Rate (DR)** — mass of filler metal deposited per unit time.
- **Weld Bead Geometry** — width (W), depth of penetration (D), reinforcement height (R), leg length (for fillet welds).

#### 2.1.3 Why These Parameters Matter — The "How" Behind the Physics

- **Why does current control penetration?** By the I²R heating principle, higher current → higher resistive heating in the workpiece → deeper melting.
- **Why does travel speed inversely affect heat input?** Faster travel spreads the same energy over more length, lowering HI per mm.
- **Why is arc length critical in GTAW?** Arc length sets voltage, and voltage controls bead width (wider arc → wider bead, shallower penetration).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Process parameter selection is the single most important decision in welding procedure specification (WPS) per **ISO 15609 / AWS B2.1** standards. Every fabricated pressure vessel, aircraft fuselage, or bridge girder has its WPS qualified by systematically varying these parameters and testing the resulting joints.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table. Every KTU board exam question in PEPET741 from this module draws from it.**

| # | Parameter / Equation | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Units | Applicable Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Input | $HI$ | $\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot S^{-1} \cdot 10^{-3}$ | kJ/mm | All arc processes |
| 2 | Arc Power | $P_{arc}$ | $V \cdot I$ | Watts (W) | Arc welding |
| 3 | Heat Generated in Arc | $Q$ | $\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Joules (J) | All |
| 4 | Deposition Rate | $DR$ | $\frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot v_{wire} \cdot \rho \cdot 60$ | kg/h | GMAW, SAW |
| 5 | Penetration Ratio (Bead Shape Factor) | $\psi$ | $W \cdot D^{-1}$ | dimensionless | All fusion processes |
| 6 | Cooling Rate (approx., 2D) | $CR$ | $2\pi k \cdot \rho c \cdot \left(\frac{v \cdot t}{Q}\right)^{2}$ | °C/s | Thick plate arc welds |
| 7 | Weld Dilution | $D\%$ | $\frac{A_{base}}{A_{base} + A_{filler}} \cdot 100$ | % | All fusion |
| 8 | Electrode Extension (Stickout) | $L_e$ | governed by Ohm's law for resistance heating | mm | GMAW, SAW |
| 9 | Specific Energy | $E_{sp}$ | $HI \cdot A_{weld}^{-1}$ | J/mm² | All |
| 10 | Filler Metal Weight | $W_f$ | $\rho \cdot A_{bead} \cdot L$ | grams | All |

**Notation Note:** In the above table, $d$ = wire diameter, $v_{wire}$ = wire feed speed, $\rho$ = density, $c$ = specific heat, $k$ = thermal conductivity, $A_{base}$ and $A_{filler}$ = cross-sectional areas of melted base and filler metal, $A_{weld}$ = weld cross-sectional area, $A_{bead}$ = bead cross-section, $L$ = weld length, $t$ = plate thickness, $v$ = travel speed.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Units Pitfall:** Travel speed $S$ in the heat input formula must be in **mm/s**, NOT mm/min. KTU examiners frequently test this — if you mistakenly use mm/min, your answer is 60× too large.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Heat Input Equation

The heat input formula is the single most-tested expression in welding metallurgy. Let us derive it from first principles.

**Starting Point:** Electrical power supplied to the arc.

$$P_{arc} = V \cdot I \;\; \text{(in Watts = J/s)}$$

**Step 1:** Multiply by the process thermal efficiency $\eta$ to get the power actually transferred to the workpiece (the rest is lost as radiation, spatter, and fumes).

$$P_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \;\; \text{(in J/s)}$$

**Step 2:** The welding torch moves along the joint at travel speed $S$ (in mm/s). The time spent heating any infinitesimal point of the weld line is:

$$t_{point} = \frac{L_{arc}}{S}$$

where $L_{arc}$ is the effective arc length footprint. However, for **energy per unit length** of weld, we can directly divide power by speed.

**Step 3:** Energy deposited per unit length:

$$\frac{P_{useful}}{S} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \;\; \text{(in J/mm)}$$

**Step 4:** Convert Joules to kilojoules by multiplying by $10^{-3}$:

$$HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{(kJ/mm)}$$

> This is the canonical expression. Note that for **tungsten inert gas (GTAW)** processes, the heat input is often quoted as **arc energy**, sometimes without the $10^{-3}$ factor (using J/mm directly).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Exam Style

**Problem:** A fabricator uses GMAW to join 6 mm mild steel plates. The set parameters are: $V = 24$ V, $I = 200$ A, travel speed $S = 5$ mm/s. Take the process thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.80$. Calculate the heat input in kJ/mm.

**Solution — Step-by-step:**

Given:
- $V = 24$ V
- $I = 200$ A
- $S = 5$ mm/s
- $\eta = 0.80$

Substitute into the heat input formula:

$$HI = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I}{S} \times 10^{-3}$$

$$HI = 0.80 \cdot \frac{24 \cdot 200}{5} \times 10^{-3}$$

Compute the numerator inside the fraction:

$$24 \cdot 200 = 4800 \text{ W}$$

Divide by travel speed:

$$\frac{4800}{5} = 960 \text{ J/mm}$$

Multiply by efficiency:

$$0.80 \cdot 960 = 768 \text{ J/mm}$$

Apply the kilo-conversion:

$$HI = 768 \times 10^{-3} = 0.768 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

> **Final Answer:** $HI = 0.768$ kJ/mm, which falls within the typical GMAW range of **0.5 – 1.5 kJ/mm** for structural steel. ✅

### 3.3 Derivation of Deposition Rate (GMAW/SAW)

The deposition rate tells us how much filler metal we lay down per hour. It depends on wire diameter $d$ and wire feed speed $v_{wire}$.

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area of the wire:

$$A_{wire} = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$$

**Step 2:** Volume of wire fed per second:

$$\dot{V}_{wire} = A_{wire} \cdot v_{wire} = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot v_{wire}$$

**Step 3:** Mass per second (multiply by density $\rho$):

$$\dot{m}_{sec} = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot v_{wire} \cdot \rho$$

**Step 4:** Mass per hour:

$$DR = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot v_{wire} \cdot \rho \cdot 3600 \;\; \text{(kg/h, if $\rho$ in kg/m³ and d, $v_{wire}$ in m)}$$

For practical use with $d$ in mm and $v_{wire}$ in m/min, the simplified empirical form used in industry is:

$$DR \approx 0.0239 \cdot d^{2} \cdot v_{wire} \;\; \text{(kg/h)}$$

where $d$ is in mm and $v_{wire}$ in m/min.

### 3.4 Derivation of Weld Dilution

Weld dilution is critical for dissimilar metal joining and overlay cladding.

$$D_{\%} = \frac{A_{base \, melted}}{A_{base \, melted} + A_{filler \, melted}} \times 100$$

Geometrically, this represents what fraction of the weld pool came from the parent plate versus the filler rod. For autogenous welds (no filler), dilution is 100%. For high-deposition cladding, dilution is intentionally kept below 20–30%.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter on Weld Response

| Parameter Increased | Penetration | Bead Width | Heat Input | Distortion | Spatter |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Current (I) | ↑ Strongly | ↑ Slightly | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Voltage (V) | ↓ Slightly | ↑ Strongly | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Travel Speed (S) | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ |
| Wire Feed Rate | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Shielding Gas Flow | Marginal | Marginal | No direct | No direct | ↓ |
| Electrode Extension | ↑ (resistive heating) | Slight ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Shortcut Mnemonic — "ICE-VW":**
> **I**ncrease **C**urrent → deeper **E**gg (penetration). **V**oltage → **W**ider bead. Use this to remember the most-asked viva question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Parameter-Controlled Welding System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the closed-loop functional architecture showing how a welder's parameter inputs cascade through the welding power source, the arc/plasma, and ultimately produce the welded joint outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Input Panel] --> B[Welding Power Source]
    B --> C{Process Selection}
    C -->|SMAW| D[Constant Current Source]
    C -->|GMAW/MIG| E[Constant Voltage Source]
    C -->|GTAW/TIG| F[HF Start + CC Source]
    C -->|PAW| G[Transferred Arc Module]
    C -->|SAW| H[High-Current CV Source]
    D --> I[Arc Plasma Column]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Workpiece Weld Pool]
    J --> K[Shielding Gas Delivery]
    K --> J
    J --> L[Travel Mechanism / Robot / Manual]
    L --> M[Weld Bead Geometry Output]
    M --> N[Penetration Depth]
    M --> O[Bead Width]
    M --> P[Heat Affected Zone HAZ]
    M --> Q[Residual Stress Field]
    N --> R[Quality Feedback to WPS]
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> A
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **left side** (Nodes A, B, C) represents the **input parameter control layer**.
- The **center** (Nodes I, J) is the **process physics layer** — the arc, plasma, and weld pool.
- The **right side** (Nodes N, O, P, Q) is the **output response layer** that determines joint quality.
- The **feedback loop** (R → A) corresponds to **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) qualification**, where measured outputs refine future parameter settings.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Process Parameter Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Welding Process Parameters]
    P0 --> P1[Primary Electrical]
    P0 --> P2[Primary Mechanical]
    P0 --> P3[Shielding Gas]
    P0 --> P4[Geometric / Consumable]
    P1 --> P1a[Welding Current I]
    P1 --> P1b[Arc Voltage V]
    P1 --> P1c[Polarity DCEN or DCEP]
    P1 --> P1d[Frequency for AC TIG]
    P2 --> P2a[Travel Speed S]
    P2 --> P2b[Wire Feed Speed]
    P2 --> P2c[Torch Angle and Work Angle]
    P2 --> P3a[Electrode Force for Resistance]
    P3 --> P3a1[Gas Type Argon Helium CO2]
    P3 --> P3b1[Flow Rate L per min]
    P3 --> P3c1[Gas Cup Diameter]
    P4 --> P4a[Electrode Diameter]
    P4 --> P4b[Electrode Extension Stickout]
    P4 --> P4c[Joint Geometry Groove Angle Root Gap]
    P4 --> P4d[Stand-off Distance]
    P0 --> P5[Derived Response Outputs]
    P5 --> P5a[Heat Input HI]
    P5 --> P5b[Penetration Depth]
    P5 --> P5c[Deposition Rate]
    P5 --> P5d[Weld Dilution Percent]
    P5 --> P5e[HAZ Width and Hardness]
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The top node `P0` is the parent. It branches into **4 primary categories** and **1 derived category**, which together form the complete taxonomy you must know for KTU 14-mark questions.

### 4.3 Schematic — Typical Parameter Interaction Loop in Arc Welding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Set Current] --> S2[Arc Forms]
    S2 --> S3[Arc Length Self Adjusts]
    S3 --> S4[Voltage Tracks Arc Length]
    S4 --> S5[Heat Input Computed]
    S5 --> S6[Penetration and Width Result]
    S6 --> S7[Travel Speed Advances Torch]
    S7 --> S1
```

This feedback loop is the conceptual heart of **constant-voltage (CV)** GMAW power sources, where the wire feed is the controlling variable and the arc self-regulates to a near-constant voltage.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Define Heat Input in arc welding. Why is it considered the most important derived parameter?**

**Model Answer:**

Heat input is the total quantity of heat energy transferred from the welding arc to the workpiece per unit length of the weld. It is given by:

$$HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}$$

It is the most important derived parameter because **it simultaneously governs** the depth of penetration, the width of the heat-affected zone (HAZ), the cooling rate, the grain size in the HAZ, the residual stress, and the weld distortion. Controlling HI is essentially controlling the entire thermal cycle of the welded joint. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **List the four primary categories of welding process parameters with one example from each.**

**Model Answer:**

The four primary categories are:

1. **Electrical Parameters** — e.g., welding current (I) in Amperes.
2. **Mechanical / Kinematic Parameters** — e.g., travel speed (S) in mm/min.
3. **Shielding Gas Parameters** — e.g., argon flow rate in L/min.
4. **Geometric / Consumable Parameters** — e.g., electrode diameter or joint groove angle.

**(3 marks)** — Award 0.75 per correctly listed category with example.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> ### Question A (14 Marks) — **Option Set 1**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model, Module 1, Choice Option A]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the major process parameters of **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW)** in detail, with emphasis on voltage, current, wire feed rate, and shielding gas composition. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A low-carbon steel plate of 8 mm thickness is welded by **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** using the following parameters: current = 600 A, voltage = 32 V, travel speed = 6 mm/s. The process thermal efficiency is 0.90. Determine:
> (i) Heat input in kJ/mm
> (ii) Whether the heat input lies in the safe range for this thickness.
> Comment on the effect of this heat input on the HAZ. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

GMAW process parameters are categorized as:

- **Arc Voltage (V):** Typical range 18–32 V. Higher voltage widens the arc cone, increasing bead width but reducing penetration. It also affects spatter levels and bead profile.

- **Welding Current (I):** Typically 100–400 A. Current is set indirectly by adjusting wire feed speed (WFS) in constant-voltage (CV) machines. Higher current → deeper penetration and higher deposition rate.

- **Wire Feed Rate (WFR):** Range 2–20 m/min. WFR is the master control in CV GMAW — it dictates burn-off rate and current.

- **Shielding Gas:** Pure CO₂, pure Argon, Ar+25%CO₂ (C25), Ar+8%CO₂, Ar+2%O₂ (for low-spatter). Argon-rich mixtures produce smoother welds; CO₂-rich gives deeper penetration but more spatter.

- **Electrode Extension (Stickout):** 10–25 mm typical. Longer stickout increases resistive preheating → higher deposition but with loss of penetration control.

- **Polarity:** DCEP is standard for GMAW (electron emission from filler wire → high deposition).

> Award marks: 1 mark for each parameter explained correctly (4 max), 1 mark for polarity, 1 mark for stickout, 1 mark for application context.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- $I = 600$ A
- $V = 32$ V
- $S = 6$ mm/s
- $\eta = 0.90$

**(i) Heat Input Calculation:**

$$HI = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I}{S} \times 10^{-3}$$

$$HI = 0.90 \cdot \frac{32 \cdot 600}{6} \times 10^{-3}$$

$$HI = 0.90 \cdot \frac{19200}{6} \times 10^{-3}$$

$$HI = 0.90 \cdot 3200 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$HI = 2880 \times 10^{-3} = 2.88 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**(2 marks for formula, 2 marks for substitution and final answer)**

**(ii) Range Assessment:**

For 8 mm mild steel, the safe heat input window to avoid excessive HAZ coarsening is approximately **1.0 – 2.0 kJ/mm** based on standard WPS guidelines. The calculated value of **2.88 kJ/mm exceeds the upper limit**, indicating risk of **wide HAZ, coarse grain, and reduced toughness**.

**HAZ Effect (3 marks):**
- Excessive heat input → slow cooling → coarse prior-austenite grains in HAZ.
- Coarse grains → lower Charpy impact toughness.
- Risk of HAZ softening in normalized steel; risk of HAZ cracking in hardenable steels.
- Recommendation: increase travel speed to bring HI down to ~1.5 kJ/mm.

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — **Option Set 2** (Internal Choice Alternative)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model, Module 1, Choice Option B]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of parameter control in a **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** system. Compare the parameters of **PAW** and **GTAW** highlighting at least four key differences. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** During a **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW)** operation on a 4 mm stainless-steel plate, the following parameters are recorded: current = 130 A, voltage = 14 V, travel speed = 3 mm/s, and process efficiency = 0.60. The weld bead is observed to have a width of 10 mm and depth of penetration of 2.5 mm. Calculate:
> (i) Heat input in kJ/mm
> (ii) Bead shape factor $\psi = W/D$
> (iii) Specific energy in J/mm² if the weld cross-sectional area is 15 mm². **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**PAW Functional Architecture:** (3 marks)

The PAW system has the following functional blocks:

- **Power Source** (DC, constant current) — supplies arc energy.
- **Pilot Arc Circuit** — initiates a non-transferred arc between the tungsten electrode and the constricting nozzle.
- **Transferred Arc** — main arc that jumps from the tungsten to the workpiece.
- **Plasma Gas Delivery** — pure Argon or Ar/H₂ forced through the constricting nozzle at high velocity to produce the high-velocity plasma jet.
- **Shielding Gas Delivery** — secondary Ar or Ar/H₂ shroud that protects the weld zone.
- **Coolant Circulation** — water cooling of the torch nozzle and electrode.
- **Travel Mechanism / Manipulator**.

**Four Key Differences between PAW and GTAW:** (4 marks — 1 each)

| Aspect | GTAW | PAW |
|---|---|---|
| Arc Type | Free, divergent arc | Constricted, collimated plasma jet |
| Velocity of Gas | Low (laminar) | High (10× to 100× GTAW) |
| Energy Density | ~1.5 kW/mm² | 10–100 kW/mm² |
| Penetration | Limited to ~3 mm in single pass | Keyhole mode possible up to 12 mm |
| Arc Starting | HF starting | Pilot arc → transferred arc |
| Nozzle Cooling | Gas-cooled | Usually water-cooled |
| Gas Orifice | Single gas flow | Plasma gas + shielding gas |
| Typical Use | Thin sections, root pass | High-precision, high-speed, thick section keyhole |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- $I = 130$ A
- $V = 14$ V
- $S = 3$ mm/s
- $\eta = 0.60$
- $W = 10$ mm
- $D = 2.5$ mm
- $A_{weld} = 15$ mm²

**(i) Heat Input (2 marks):**

$$HI = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I}{S} \times 10^{-3} = 0.60 \cdot \frac{14 \cdot 130}{3} \times 10^{-3}$$

$$HI = 0.60 \cdot \frac{1820}{3} \times 10^{-3} = 0.60 \cdot 606.67 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$HI = 364 \times 10^{-3} = 0.364 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**(ii) Bead Shape Factor (2 marks):**

$$\psi = \frac{W}{D} = \frac{10}{2.5} = 4.0$$

A shape factor of 4.0 indicates a **wide, shallow bead** — typical of GTAW operated in conduction mode without keyhole formation.

**(iii) Specific Energy (3 marks):**

$$E_{sp} = \frac{HI}{A_{weld}} \text{ (converted to consistent units)}$$

Note that $HI$ is in kJ/mm. We have $A_{weld}$ in mm². To get J/mm², we first convert $HI$ to J/mm:

$$HI_{J/mm} = 0.364 \times 10^{3} = 364 \text{ J/mm}$$

Now, $E_{sp} = \frac{HI_{J/mm}}{A_{weld}}$ — but this is energy per unit length divided by area, giving J/mm². However, the more standard form uses cross-section per unit length, which equals $A_{weld}$ for a unit length of weld:

$$E_{sp} = \frac{364 \text{ J/mm}}{15 \text{ mm}^2} = 24.27 \text{ J/mm}^2$$

> Note: If the problem expects $HI$ used directly in kJ/mm, then $E_{sp} = 0.364/15 = 0.0243$ kJ/mm² $= 24.3$ J/mm². Same answer, units match.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $10^{-3}$ factor** in heat input → 1000× wrong answer → lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing mm/s and mm/min** for travel speed → 60× wrong answer → lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Using the wrong efficiency** for the wrong process (e.g., using 0.85 for GTAW instead of 0.60) → lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Not stating units** in the final answer → lose 1 mark per question.
> 5. **Confusing penetration with depth of fusion** — penetration is measured from the original plate surface to the deepest point of fusion; reinforcement is on top.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision List — Read This Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- ✅ **Heat Input formula** — $HI = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot S^{-1} \cdot 10^{-3}$ in **kJ/mm**.
- ✅ **Units of travel speed in HI formula = mm/s** (not mm/min).
- ✅ **Efficiency values for major processes** (SMAW: 0.75, GMAW: 0.75, GTAW: 0.60, SAW: 0.90, OAW: 0.45, PAW: 0.70).
- ✅ **Four parameter categories** — Electrical, Mechanical, Shielding Gas, Geometric.
- ✅ **Effect of increased current → ↑ penetration (strong effect)**.
- ✅ **Effect of increased voltage → ↑ bead width, ↓ penetration** (mild).
- ✅ **Effect of increased travel speed → ↓ HI, ↓ HAZ, ↓ distortion**.
- ✅ **Deposition rate formula** for GMAW: $DR = 0.0239 \cdot d^2 \cdot v_{wire}$ (kg/h, d in mm, $v_{wire}$ in m/min).
- ✅ **Weld dilution** = melted base area ÷ total melted area.
- ✅ **Bead shape factor** $\psi = W/D$ — higher ψ means wider/shallower bead.
- ✅ **Polarity convention** — DCEP for GMAW/SMAW; DCEN for GTAW (for most metals except aluminum, which uses ACHF).
- ✅ **Duty cycle** of power source is the percentage of 10 min the machine can deliver rated current without overheating.
- ✅ **Shielding gas for aluminum** is pure Argon (or Ar/He); for steel GMAW it is Ar/CO₂ (C25); for austenitic stainless it is Ar + 2% H₂ or Ar/He.
- ✅ **Keyhole mode in PAW** produces deep, narrow, parallel-sided welds in a single pass — high energy density.
- ✅ **Cold metal transfer (CMT), pulsed MIG, and tandem MIG** are advanced variants of GMAW, each with a distinct parameter regime.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Heat Input Pitfalls — "VIPS":** **V**oltage × **I**current gives power; **P**ower ÷ **S**peed gives energy per length; convert to kilo by × $10^{-3}$. V × I / S × $10^{-3}$. Always check the **P**ower side, then **S**peed, then **$10^{-3}$** conversion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plasma Arc Welding: Theory and Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plasma Arc Welding: Theory and Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** is an advanced arc welding process in which a constricted arc is established between a tungsten electrode and the workpiece (transferred arc mode) or between the electrode and the constricting nozzle (non-transferred arc mode), wherein the arc plasma is forced through a fine-bore copper nozzle. This nozzle produces a high-velocity, columnar, superheated plasma jet with temperatures reaching **16,000 °C to 33,000 °C**, enabling deep penetration welding, high welding speeds, and superior arc stability compared to conventional Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Plasma is fundamentally the **fourth state of matter**, consisting of a partially ionized gas containing roughly equal numbers of positive ions and free electrons, exhibiting collective behavior under electromagnetic fields.

### 1.2 The Four States of Matter (Foundation Concept)

$$
\text{Solid} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat}} \text{Liquid} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat}} \text{Gas} \xrightarrow{\text{Ionization}} \text{Plasma}
$$

> [!NOTE]
