> When sufficient energy is supplied to a gas, electrons are stripped from atoms, creating a soup of ions + electrons + neutral particles. This ionized state conducts electricity and is influenced by magnetic fields — making it controllable for welding.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The High-Pressure Water Hose"

Imagine a **garden hose with a nozzle attachment**:
- Without the nozzle → water flows out in a wide, weak stream.
- With the nozzle → water is **constricted**, shoots out faster, harder, and more focused.

**PAW works exactly the same way:**
- A regular TIG arc is like the un-nozzled hose (broad, soft).
- A plasma arc is like the nozzled hose — the **constricting copper nozzle** squeezes the arc column, raising its **current density, temperature, velocity, and penetration** dramatically.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** "PAW = TIG + Constricting Nozzle + Orifice Gas + Higher Velocity Jet"

### 1.4 Key Distinguishing Metrics of PAW

| Property | Conventional TIG | Plasma Arc Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Arc Temperature | 6,000–10,000 °C | **16,000–33,000 °C** |
| Current Density | Low (spreading arc) | **Very High** (constricted) |
| Penetration | Shallow to medium | **Deep keyhole possible** |
| Arc Stability | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| Travel Speed | Slow to medium | **High** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Arc Constriction

The brilliance of PAW lies in **three simultaneous constricting effects** that compress the arc column:

#### Step-by-Step Operational Logic:

**Step 1 — Thermal Constriction**
Cooling water flowing through the copper nozzle chills the outer boundary of the arc. The cooler outer zone has higher electrical resistance, so the arc is forced to flow through the hotter, lower-resistance core → arc cross-section shrinks.

**Step 2 — Mechanical Constriction**
The orifice (a tiny hole typically **0.8–4.8 mm** diameter) physically restricts the arc cross-section, increasing the current density.

**Step 3 — Magnetic (Electromagnetic) Constriction**
When current flows through the constricted arc, the resulting magnetic field (per **Ampère's Law**) pinches the arc plasma inward — the same phenomenon that causes arc-column contraction in high-current arcs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Combined Effect:** These three constrictions produce a columnar arc with current densities of **10⁴ to 10⁵ A/cm²**, compared to ~10³ A/cm² in TIG.

### 2.2 Modes of Operation

**Mode 1 — Transferred Arc (TA)**
- Arc established between **tungsten electrode ↔ Workpiece**.
- Plasma jet transfers energy to the workpiece directly.
- Used for **welding thick sections** (keyhole mode possible).
- **Power level:** Up to 200 kW possible.

**Mode 2 — Non-Transferred Arc (NTA)**
- Arc established between **tungsten electrode ↔ Nozzle (constrictor)**.
- Plasma jet exits nozzle and impinges on workpiece.
- Used for **surfacing, cutting, spraying, melting of low-melting alloys**.
- **Power level:** Limited (~40 kW).

**Mode 3 — Dual Arc / Combined Mode**
- Both arcs operate simultaneously; transferred arc for welding + non-transferred arc for auxiliary heating.
- Used in specialized high-deposition applications.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v}$ | Heat input per unit length (η = process efficiency) | kJ/mm or J/mm |
| 2 | $J = \dfrac{I}{A_{arc}}$ | Current density (arc) | A/mm² or A/cm² |
| 3 | $v_{plasma} = \dfrac{Q_{gas}}{A_{orifice}}$ | Plasma jet exit velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $P_{arc} = V \cdot I$ | Arc power | Watts (W) |
| 5 | $T_{plasma} \approx f(P, d_{or}, Q_{gas})$ | Plasma temperature is a function of power, orifice diameter, gas flow | Empirical, ~20,000 K typical |
| 6 | $\dfrac{V}{I} = k \cdot \ell$ | Voltage gradient along arc (k = arc stiffness constant) | V/mm |
| 7 | $F_{p} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v^{2}$ | Plasma jet momentum/force on workpiece | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Note:** In PAW, the process efficiency η for heat input calculation typically ranges from **0.6 to 0.8** depending on mode (transferred vs non-transferred) and shielding gas. Use η ≈ 0.7 unless otherwise stated.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace industry:** Welding of thin-walled stainless steel and titanium jet engine components (e.g., compressor casings).
- **Nuclear:** Welding of stainless steel tubes and dissimilar metal joints.
- **Chemical/Process plant:** Fabrication of high-integrity pressure vessels.
- **Automotive:** Precision welding of transmission components.
- **Shipbuilding & Defence:** Welding of armour plates using keyhole PAW.
- **Surfacing:** Hardfacing of dies, valves using powder-plasma transfer.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input Equation for PAW

The **heat input** delivered to the workpiece per unit length of weld is the foundational energy metric in welding engineering. Let us derive it rigorously.

#### Given Data:
- Arc Voltage: $V$ (in Volts)
- Welding Current: $I$ (in Amperes)
- Travel Speed: $v$ (in mm/min)
- Process thermal efficiency: $\eta$ (dimensionless, 0 < η ≤ 1)

#### Step 1: Total Power Generated in the Arc

The arc is an electrical load. Power dissipated in the arc:

$$
P_{arc} = V \times I
$$

This gives the **gross electrical power** consumed by the arc in **Watts (Joules/second)**.

#### Step 2: Net Heat Transferred to Workpiece

Not all arc power reaches the workpiece; some is lost to radiation, convection, spatter, and heating of the electrode/nozzle. The **fraction η** is the heat transfer efficiency:

$$
P_{useful} = \eta \times V \times I
$$

#### Step 3: Heat Delivered per Unit Length of Weld

If the torch travels at speed $v$ in **mm/min**, the time taken to traverse **1 mm of weld length** is:

$$
t = \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{v \text{ mm/min}} = \dfrac{60}{v} \text{ seconds}
$$

#### Step 4: Final Heat Input Formula

Heat input per mm of weld length:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= P_{useful} \times t \\
H &= \eta \cdot V \cdot I \times \dfrac{60}{v} \\
H &= \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}
\end{aligned}
$$

Units: $\dfrac{\text{W} \cdot \text{s}}{\text{mm}} = \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}}$ (commonly expressed as **kJ/mm**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Since $v$ is in mm/min, multiplying by $\dfrac{60}{v}$ converts the per-second power into a per-mm energy quantity. This is why the factor of 60 appears.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** In a PAW operation on a 6 mm stainless steel plate, the arc voltage is **28 V**, welding current is **200 A**, travel speed is **200 mm/min**, and process efficiency is **0.7**. Calculate the heat input per unit length.

#### Solution:

**Given:** $V = 28$ V, $I = 200$ A, $v = 200$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.7$

**Step 1:** Compute arc power:

$$
P_{arc} = V \times I = 28 \times 200 = 5600 \text{ W} = 5.6 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply efficiency factor:

$$
P_{useful} = \eta \times P_{arc} = 0.7 \times 5.6 = 3.92 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute heat input per mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v} \\
H &= \dfrac{60 \times 0.7 \times 28 \times 200}{200} \\
H &= \dfrac{60 \times 0.7 \times 28 \times 200}{200} \\
H &= 60 \times 0.7 \times 28 \\
H &= 1176 \text{ J/mm} \\
H &= 1.176 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $H = 1.176$ kJ/mm ≈ **1.18 kJ/mm**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Current Density Relationship in Constricted Arc

**Step 1:** Standard current density (unconstricted arc) for an arc of cross-sectional area $A_1$:

$$
J_1 = \dfrac{I}{A_1}
$$

**Step 2:** When the arc is constricted by nozzle orifice of diameter $d_o$:

$$
A_2 = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_o^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3:** Constricted current density:

$$
J_2 = \dfrac{I}{A_2} = \dfrac{4 \cdot I}{\pi \cdot d_o^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Ratio (showing enhancement factor):

$$
\dfrac{J_2}{J_1} = \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Application:** If the orifice is **2 mm** and the unconstricted arc diameter is **6 mm**, the constriction ratio is $(6/2)^2 = 9$, meaning current density is **9× higher** in the constricted arc.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selection of PAW Parameters

| Material | Thickness (mm) | Current (A) | Gas | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stainless Steel | 0.1–1.5 | 5–50 | Argon | Microplasma (TA) |
| Stainless Steel | 1.5–6.0 | 50–200 | Ar + 2–5% H₂ | Melt-in (TA) |
| Stainless Steel | > 6.0 | 150–500 | Ar + H₂ | Keyhole (TA) |
| Titanium | 1.0–10.0 | 50–250 | Argon + He | TA Keyhole |
| Aluminium | 1.5–12.0 | 70–300 | Argon + He | TA Keyhole |
| Surfacing | Variable | 100–400 | Ar / Ar + H₂ | Non-transferred |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PAW Process Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Power Source DC Constant Current] --> B1[High Frequency Starter HF]
    B1 --> C1[Plasma Torch Assembly]
    C2[Shielding Gas Cylinder Argon] --> D1[Gas Flowmeter and Regulator]
    C3[Plasma Gas Cylinder] --> D1
    D1 --> C1
    E1[Cooling Water Circulation Unit] --> C1
    F1[Workpiece Clamp and Tracking System] --> G1[Weld Pool Formation]
    C1 -->|Plasma Jet| G1
    G1 --> H1[Welded Joint]
    I1[Torch Travel Mechanism] --> C1
    J1[Control Console Foot Pedal] --> A1
```

**Caption:** Block-level functional flow of a complete PAW installation. The arrows denote energy, gas, coolant, and mechanical motion pathways.

### 4.2 PAW Torch Internal Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nA[Tungsten Electrode Negative] --> nB[Electrode Clamp]
    nB --> nC[Insulating Gas Cup]
    nC --> nD[Cooling Water Jacket]
    nD --> nE[Copper Nozzle with Orifice]
    nE --> nF[Plasma Jet Exit Velocity ~Mach 2]
    nG[Shielding Gas Inlet] --> nH[Outer Shield Cup]
    nH --> nI[Shielding Gas Coverage on Weld Pool]
    nF --> nJ[Workpiece Positive Anode]
```

**Caption:** Internal construction of a typical PAW torch showing electrode, orifice, gas channels, and cooling jacket arrangement.

### 4.3 Process Flow — Steps in PAW Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Step 1 Clean and Prepare Workpiece] --> s2[Step 2 Align Torch and Set Stand-off Distance 3 to 12 mm]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3 Initiate Pilot Arc via HF or Auxiliary Arc]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4 Introduce Plasma and Shielding Gas]
    s4 --> s5[Step 5 Establish Transferred Arc to Workpiece]
    s5 --> s6[Step 6 Adjust Current Voltage and Travel Speed]
    s6 --> s7[Step 7 Move Torch Along Joint Line]
    s7 --> s8[Step 8 Allow Solidification and Shield Gas Cover]
    s8 --> s9[Step 9 Inspect Welded Joint]
```

### 4.4 Three Constricting Effects — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pA[Plasma Arc Constriction Phenomenon] --> pB[Thermal Pinching]
    pA --> pC[Mechanical Pinching]
    pA --> pD[Electromagnetic Pinching]
    pB --> pE[Higher Current Density]
    pC --> pE
    pD --> pE
    pE --> pF[Higher Temperature 20000 K]
    pE --> pG[Higher Plasma Velocity]
    pF --> pH[Keyhole Penetration Capability]
    pG --> pH
```

### 4.5 Transferred vs Non-Transferred Arc Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    modeA[Transferred Arc Mode] --> modeA1[Electrode to Workpiece]
    modeA1 --> modeA2[High Power up to 200 kW]
    modeA2 --> modeA3[Used for Welding Thick Plates]

    modeB[Non-Transferred Arc Mode] --> modeB1[Electrode to Nozzle]
    modeB1 --> modeB2[Lower Power up to 40 kW]
    modeB2 --> modeB3[Used for Surfacing and Cutting]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Plasma. How is plasma generated in a Plasma Arc Welding process?**

**Model Answer:**

**Plasma:** Plasma is the fourth state of matter, consisting of a partially or fully ionized gas containing approximately equal numbers of positively charged ions, negatively charged free electrons, and neutral particles. It is electrically conductive and responds to electromagnetic fields.

**Generation in PAW:** In a Plasma Arc Welding process, plasma is generated by the following sequence:

1. An inert gas (typically **Argon**) is passed through the tungsten electrode and constricting copper nozzle.
2. A high-frequency high-voltage spark initiates a **pilot arc** between the electrode and the nozzle (or workpiece).
3. The resistance heating of the gas ionizes it, stripping electrons from gas atoms.
4. Once ionized, the gas becomes electrically conductive and sustains the arc.
5. The **constricting nozzle** compresses the arc, raising current density, temperature (up to **30,000 °C**), and plasma velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Word Target:** Aim for **3–4 sentences** covering definition + generation steps for full 3 marks.

---

#### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six advantages of Plasma Arc Welding over conventional TIG welding.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Higher arc temperature** (20,000 °C vs 10,000 °C in TIG) enables faster welding speeds.
2. **Higher current density** due to arc constriction → deeper penetration (keyhole mode possible).
3. **Better arc stability** — constricted arc is less affected by magnetic disturbances.
4. **Reduced distortion** of workpiece due to localized heat input and high travel speeds.
5. **Wider thickness range** — can weld from 0.1 mm foils (microplasma) to > 25 mm plates.
6. **Excellent weld bead appearance** with smooth, narrow, and uniform profile.
7. **Superior mechanical properties** of weld due to controlled heat input.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Expected]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation** of Plasma Arc Welding with a neat schematic. Differentiate between **transferred arc** and **non-transferred arc** modes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **equipment and process parameters** of PAW in detail, including power source, torch design, gas system, and role of pilot arc. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Principle of Operation:**
Plasma Arc Welding operates on the principle of **arc constriction**. A tungsten electrode is placed inside a copper nozzle with a small orifice (typically 0.8–4.8 mm diameter). An inert gas (Argon) is forced through this orifice while a high-frequency starter initiates an arc between the electrode and the nozzle.

The arc plasma is **forced (constricted)** through the orifice by three effects:
1. **Thermal constriction** — water-cooled nozzle chills outer arc boundary.
2. **Mechanical constriction** — physical orifice restricts arc diameter.
3. **Electromagnetic constriction** — self-induced magnetic field pinches the arc.

The result is a **high-velocity (Mach 1–2), high-temperature (20,000–30,000 K), high-energy-density plasma jet** that exits the nozzle and is directed at the workpiece.

**Differences between Transferred Arc and Non-Transferred Arc:**

| Parameter | Transferred Arc | Non-Transferred Arc |
|---|---|---|
| Arc Path | Electrode → Workpiece | Electrode → Nozzle |
| Power Level | Up to 200 kW | Up to 40 kW |
| Heat Source | Arc + plasma jet | Plasma jet only |
| Application | Welding, keyhole mode | Surfacing, spraying, cutting |
| Penetration | Deep | Shallow |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students write only the difference table without explaining the *principle of arc constriction* — this loses **2–3 marks** in part (a). Always define the principle BEFORE drawing comparisons.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Power Source:**
PAW requires a **DC constant current (drooping characteristic)** power source. Constant current prevents current surges from destabilizing the arc. Typical open-circuit voltage is **70–80 V**, and current range is **5–500 A**.

**Torch Design:**
The PAW torch is the heart of the process. It contains:
- **Tungsten electrode** (2% thoriated, 1.6–4.8 mm diameter).
- **Copper constricting nozzle** with precisely machined orifice.
- **Insulating gas cup** to isolate electrode from nozzle.
- **Cooling water jacket** (water flow rate ~ 8–15 L/min).
- **Shielding gas cup** to deliver outer shielding gas.

**Gas System:**
Two separate gas streams are used:
- **Plasma gas (inner gas)** — typically Argon, sometimes mixed with 2–8% Hydrogen for stainless steel. Flow rate: **0.5–6 L/min**.
- **Shielding gas (outer gas)** — typically Argon or Argon-Helium mix. Flow rate: **10–30 L/min**. Protects weld pool from atmospheric contamination.

**Pilot Arc:**
The pilot arc is a **low-power auxiliary arc** (initiated by HF spark) between the electrode and nozzle. Its function is to:
1. Pre-heat and ionize the gas.
2. Provide a conductive path to initiate the main transferred arc.
3. Maintain plasma stability during start-up.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the pilot arc with the main welding arc. Remember: **pilot arc = electrode-to-nozzle (low power)**, **main arc = electrode-to-workpiece (welding power)**.

---

#### **Question B.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Expected]`

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **microplasma welding** process. Mention its typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **heat input per unit length** for a PAW operation with the following data: Arc voltage = 30 V, Welding current = 150 A, Travel speed = 250 mm/min, Thermal efficiency = 0.65. Also discuss the **effects of process parameters** on weld quality in PAW. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Microplasma Welding:**
Microplasma welding is a low-current variant of PAW operating at currents typically **0.1–15 A**. It uses a constricting nozzle with a very small orifice (0.5–1.5 mm) and a **constant-current power source** with arc length compensation.

**Key Features:**
- **Low heat input** — minimizes distortion of thin sheets.
- **Stable arc at very low currents** — unattainable in TIG.
- **Three modes available**: transferred, non-transferred, and pulsed.
- **Pilot arc continuously maintained** — facilitates instant arc initiation at low current.
- **Welding range:** foil thickness from **0.05 mm to 1.5 mm**.

**Applications:**
1. Welding of thin stainless steel sheets (chemical and food industries).
2. Fabrication of bellows, diaphragms, and instrument components.
3. Aerospace thin-walled tubing.
4. Watch, instrument, and electronic component manufacturing.
5. Repair welding of fine cracks without distortion.

**Schematic (in description):**
The microplasma torch consists of a fine tungsten electrode, a miniature copper constricting nozzle with a sub-millimeter orifice, and a concentric shielding gas cup. A pilot arc of ~2–5 A continuously burns between electrode and nozzle, while the main welding arc transfers to the workpiece at the desired low current.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse microplasma with pulsed TIG. Microplasma is **fundamentally constricted** by the nozzle, whereas pulsed TIG uses conventional tungsten arc with current pulsing.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $V = 30$ V, $I = 150$ A, $v = 250$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.65$

**Step 1:** Apply heat input formula:

$$
H = \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{60 \times 0.65 \times 30 \times 150}{250} \\
H &= \dfrac{60 \times 0.65 \times 4500}{250} \\
H &= \dfrac{175{,}500}{250} \\
H &= 702 \text{ J/mm} \\
H &= 0.702 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $H = 0.702$ kJ/mm ≈ **702 J/mm**.

**Effects of Process Parameters on Weld Quality:**

| Parameter | Effect on Weld |
|---|---|
| **Welding Current** ↑ | Penetration ↑, bead width ↑, HAZ ↑ |
| **Arc Voltage** ↑ | Weld bead widens, penetration decreases |
| **Travel Speed** ↑ | Heat input ↓, penetration ↓, possible undercut |
| **Plasma Gas Flow** ↑ | Arc stiffness ↑, but excessive flow causes undercut |
| **Shielding Gas Flow** ↑ | Better protection, but excessive flow causes turbulence and porosity |
| **Nozzle Stand-off Distance** ↑ | Arc spreads, penetration ↓, bead widens |
| **H₂ Addition to Ar** | Increases heat input, deeper penetration, faster welding |

> [!WARNING]
> **Marks Pitfall:** In numerical questions, **always show units at every step**. KTU examiners often deduct **1 mark** for missing units. Also, clearly state the formula at the start to secure **2 marks** for the method even if the arithmetic goes wrong.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for PAW — Theory and Principles**

- ✅ Plasma is the **fourth state of matter** — ionized gas with ions + electrons + neutrals.
- ✅ PAW arc temperatures reach **16,000–33,000 °C**, much higher than TIG.
- ✅ Three constricting effects: **Thermal, Mechanical, Electromagnetic**.
- ✅ Two main modes: **Transferred Arc (TA)** and **Non-Transferred Arc (NTA)**.
- ✅ TA = electrode-to-workpiece; used for **welding**. NTA = electrode-to-nozzle; used for **surfacing/cutting**.
- ✅ **Pilot arc** is essential for low-current arc initiation and stability.
- ✅ Orifice diameter typically **0.8–4.8 mm** depending on current.
- ✅ Process efficiency η ≈ **0.6–0.8** (use 0.7 unless stated).
- ✅ Heat input formula: $H = \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ — units **J/mm or kJ/mm**.
- ✅ **Microplasma**: 0.1–15 A, for foils up to 1.5 mm.
- ✅ **Keyhole mode**: Deep penetration above ~100 A — vapor column pierces through the plate.
- ✅ Plasma gas: Ar or Ar + 2–8% H₂; Shielding gas: Ar or Ar + He.
- ✅ Constant current DC power source with **70–80 V OCV** typical.
- ✅ Advantages: **High speed, deep penetration, low distortion, narrow HAZ**.
- ✅ Limitations: **Complex equipment, high cost, requires skilled operators**.
- ✅ Applications: **Aerospace, nuclear, chemical, thin-wall tubes, surfacing**.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip from KTU Board Examiner:** When asked to "explain PAW", always structure your answer as: **(1) Definition of plasma → (2) Principle of arc constriction (3 effects) → (3) Modes (TA vs NTA) → (4) Equipment → (5) Process parameters → (6) Advantages/Limitations → (7) Applications**. This 7-point structure fetches full marks consistently.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transferred and Non-Transferred Arc Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transferred and Non-Transferred Arc Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In advanced arc-based welding and joining processes (notably **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)**, **Plasma Arc Cutting (PAC)**, **Plasma Transferred Arc (PTA) surfacing**, and **Plasma Spraying**), the manner in which the electrical circuit is completed — i.e., **between which two terminals the arc is struck** — categorises the process into two fundamental classes:

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Transferred Arc** — An electric arc that is established and sustained **directly between the non-consumable tungsten electrode and the workpiece**. The workpiece forms an integral part of the conducting path of the arc current. The arc itself is *transferred* from the torch onto the workpiece surface. This mode offers the highest energy concentration and is the workhorse mode for welding, cutting, and hardfacing.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Non-Transferred Arc** — An electric arc that is struck and sustained **between the tungsten electrode and a water-cooled copper constricting nozzle** (or pilot electrode) of the torch itself. The workpiece is *electrically isolated* from this primary arc circuit; energy reaches the workpiece only via the **high-velocity plasma jet** issuing out of the nozzle. This mode is essential for plasma spraying and serves as the *pilot arc* in transferred-arc systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

**Transferred Arc → The Direct-Heating Analogy (Electric Iron on Cloth)**
Imagine pressing a hot electric iron (the tungsten electrode) directly onto a shirt (the workpiece). Electricity flows through the iron, into the shirt, and back through the cord. The shirt gets heated **directly** at the contact zone because current physically passes through it. This is exactly how a **transferred arc** behaves: current leaves the electrode, jumps the arc gap, enters the workpiece, and returns to the power source — heating the workpiece *in situ*.

**Non-Transferred Arc → The Flamethrower / Blowtorch Analogy**
Now imagine a flamethrower: combustion (the arc) happens *inside the torch* between an internal electrode and the torch nozzle (not between the torch and the target). The flame (plasma jet) is then *projected* outward onto the workpiece. The workpiece is never part of the electrical circuit; it is heated purely by the hot gas jet. This is precisely a **non-transferred arc** — heat is generated inside the torch, then convectively transferred to the workpiece by a supersonic plasma stream.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Mnemonic for the Exam Hall:** **T**ransferred → arc **T**ouches the workpiece (part of circuit). **N**on-transferred → arc **N**ever touches the workpiece (torch-internal arc only).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Electrode polarity (standard for transferred arc in PAW):** **DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative)** — Tungsten is cathode, workpiece is anode → **≈ 70 %** of total arc heat is liberated at the anode (workpiece).
- **Pilot arc voltage (non-transferred):** **15 – 30 V**, current **10 – 40 A**.
- **Transferred arc voltage (PAW):** **25 – 60 V**, current **5 – 500 A** (depending on keyhole/melt-in mode).
- **Plasma gas flow rate:** **0.5 – 10 L/min** (typical PAW); up to **100 L/min** for high-current cutting.
- **Constricting nozzle orifice diameter:** **1.6 – 4.8 mm** (micro-plasma to high-power).
- **Power density (transferred arc PAW):** **10⁵ – 10⁷ W/cm²** (vs. ~10⁴ W/cm² in GTAW) — enables **keyhole mode** welding.
- **Nozzle constriction ratio:** **A_throat / A_orifice ≈ 2 : 1 to 6 : 1**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Transferred Arc

The transferred arc operates on the following stepwise logic (each step explains the **WHY** and the **HOW**):

1. **Pilot arc initiation (non-transferred sub-arc):**
   - *Why:* A high-frequency (HF) or high-voltage pulse ionises the plasma gas *inside* the nozzle, striking a small arc between the **tungsten electrode (–)** and the **copper nozzle (+)**.
   - *How:* This creates an electrically conductive plasma column *trapped inside the torch*, establishing an ionised pathway.

2. **Pilot-to-transferred arc transfer:**
   - *Why:* Once the pilot arc exists, the workpiece is electrically closer (lower impedance) than the nozzle path. The pilot arc therefore **commutates** (jumps) onto the workpiece.
   - *How:* A separate power circuit (or coil/relay logic) biases the workpiece to a more negative potential, drawing the arc out of the nozzle onto the workpiece. The pilot arc is now extinguished (or reduced to a sustaining current).

3. **Stable transferred arc operation:**
   - Current flows: **Power source → Electrode → Arc → Workpiece → Ground cable → Power source**.
   - **Why this matters:** The anode heat fraction (≈ 0.7 of total arc power) is delivered *directly* into the workpiece, producing deep penetration, narrow HAZ, and high welding speeds.

4. **Arc constriction by water-cooled nozzle:**
   - *Why:* The copper nozzle (water-cooled internally) physically squeezes the arc column, raising its **current density** and **arc pressure**.
   - *How:* Smaller orifice → smaller cross-sectional area of arc → for the same current, current density **J = I/A** increases → arc pressure **P_arc ≈ (J)²/2σ²** rises sharply (Lorentz / Maecker pinch effect).

5. **Heat input calculation (universally applicable):**

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \left[ \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}} \right]
$$

   - **η** = arc efficiency (≈ 0.6 – 0.7 for transferred PAW; ≈ 0.85 for SAW; ≈ 0.7 for GTAW)
   - **V** = arc voltage in volts
   - **I** = arc current in amperes
   - **S** = travel speed in mm/min
   - *Why:* This is the single most asked heat-input formula in KTU examinations.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Non-Transferred Arc

1. **Arc struck entirely inside the torch** between tungsten electrode (–) and copper nozzle (+).
2. **Plasma gas (Ar / Ar + H₂ / N₂)** is forced through the constricting orifice, **thermal-pinch + magnetic-pinch** compression raises its temperature to **15,000 – 30,000 K**.
3. **The high-temperature, high-velocity plasma jet exits the nozzle** at near-sonic to supersonic velocity (up to **Mach 2 – Mach 3**).
4. **Heat transfer to workpiece** is by **forced convection + radiation** (NOT by direct current flow).
5. **Workpiece may be electrically floating** (no arc current through it) — typical of plasma spraying.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Industrial Significance:** Non-transferred arcs are *mandatory* when the workpiece is **non-conductive** (ceramics, polymers, glass, biological tissue) or when surface coating (spraying) is required without diluting the substrate metallurgically.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning of Symbols | Units | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{\eta V I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | Heat input | kJ/mm | All arc processes |
| 2 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Arc power | W | Both modes |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{I}{A_{\text{arc}}}$ | Current density | A/mm² | Both modes |
| 4 | $P_{\text{arc}} \approx \dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{4 \pi^2 r^2}$ | Arc pressure (Maecker pinch) | Pa | Transferred, constricted |
| 5 | $Q_{\text{anode}} = \eta_a \cdot V \cdot I$, with $\eta_a \approx 0.7$ | Anode heat fraction (DCEN) | W | Transferred, DCEN |
| 6 | $Q_{\text{cathode}} \approx (1-\eta_a) V I$ | Cathode heat fraction | W | Electrode heating |
| 7 | $v_{\text{plasma}} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \gamma R T}{\gamma - 1} \left[1-\left(\dfrac{p_0}{p}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]}$ | Plasma jet exit velocity (isentropic) | m/s | Non-transferred jet |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = A \cdot v$ | Mass flow continuity | kg/s | Plasma gas |
| 9 | $d_{\text{eff}} = d_{\text{orifice}} \sqrt{C_d}$ | Effective plasma diameter (orifice × discharge coeff.) | mm | Beam characterisation |
| 10 | $\eta = 0.6$–$0.7$ (transferred PAW); $0.5$–$0.6$ (non-transferred) | Arc efficiency | — | Energy balance |

> [!WARNING]  
> In KTU answer scripts, **never** write heat input as $H = \dfrac{VI}{S}$ without the constant **60/1000** — the unit conversion to **kJ/mm** is what fetches the mark for "correctness of unit".

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Transferred PAW in keyhole mode welds thin-walled titanium and stainless-steel jet-engine casings (e.g., honeycomb seals, combustor cans).
- **PTA hardfacing:** Transferred arc deposits cobalt-based Stellite / nickel-based Colmonoy overlays on valve seats, gate valves, and rock-crusher rolls.
- **Plasma spraying (non-transferred):** Thermal-barrier coatings (TBCs) of Yttria-Stabilised Zirconia (YSZ) on gas-turbine blades — the *only* economically viable technique for such ceramic coatings.
- **Plasma arc gouging / cutting:** Transferred mode for steel plates up to **150 mm thick** (with high-current and oxygen assist).
- **Medical / scientific:** Non-transferred plasma torches are used in **plasma sterilisation** and **spectrochemistry** where workpiece isolation is essential.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input at the Workpiece in Transferred vs Non-Transferred Arc

**Goal:** Derive an expression for the heat flux actually delivered to the workpiece in *both* modes and compare.

---

#### Step 1: Total electrical power input

The total arc power supplied by the source is the same regardless of mode:

$$
P_{\text{arc}} = V_{\text{arc}} \cdot I_{\text{arc}} \quad [\text{W}]
$$

**Explanation:** Voltage multiplied by current gives total wattage; this is the gross electrical energy entering the arc column per second.

---

#### Step 2: Anode–cathode heat partition (transferred, DCEN)

For an arc operating with the **workpiece as anode** (DCEN — Direct Current Electrode Negative), experimental studies by **Lancaster and Simonen** and the **Hull** model give:

$$
Q_{\text{anode}} = \eta_a \cdot V \cdot I
$$

$$
Q_{\text{cathode}} = (1 - \eta_a) \cdot V \cdot I
$$

For iron/steel anodes at typical welding currents, $\eta_a \approx 0.70$.

$$
\therefore\; Q_{\text{workpiece}} = 0.70 \, V \, I
$$

**Explanation:** Approximately 70 % of the total arc energy is liberated at the anode (workpiece) surface through electron condensation, neutralisation, and condensation energy — this is why DCEN gives deep penetration.

---

#### Step 3: Heat input in transferred arc (per unit length)

The heat delivered per millimetre of weld travel is obtained by multiplying the power by the time spent over 1 mm of travel, then converting to kJ:

$$
H_{\text{transferred}} = \frac{\eta \, V \, I \, \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right]
$$

where the factor $60/1000$ converts $(\text{V} \cdot \text{A} \cdot \text{min})/(\text{mm})$ to $\text{kJ/mm}$.

---

#### Step 4: Heat delivered to workpiece in non-transferred arc

In non-transferred mode, current does **not** flow through the workpiece. Therefore we cannot use the anode heat-fraction rule directly. Heat is delivered **convectively + radiatively** by the plasma jet:

$$
Q_{\text{workpiece}}^{\text{NT}} = h_c \, A_{\text{spot}} \, (T_{\text{jet}} - T_{\text{workpiece}}) + \varepsilon \sigma (T_{\text{jet}}^4 - T_{\text{workpiece}}^4)
$$

where  
- $h_c$ = convective heat-transfer coefficient (≈ $10^4$–$10^5$ W/m²K)  
- $A_{\text{spot}}$ = area of impingement (m²)  
- $T_{\text{jet}}$ = plasma jet temperature at exit (**≈ 20 000 K**)  
- $T_{\text{workpiece}}$ = substrate surface temperature (K)  
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity (≈ 0.3 – 0.7 for plasma in visible range)  
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴ (Stefan–Boltzmann constant)

**Explanation:** In the non-transferred mode, the *only* way energy reaches the workpiece is by hot gas striking the surface (convection) and by photon emission (radiation). This is why non-transferred arc efficiency is lower (η ≈ 0.5 – 0.6).

---

#### Step 5: Comparative ratio of heat delivered

Let $\beta = \dfrac{Q_{\text{workpiece}}^{\text{NT}}}{Q_{\text{workpiece}}^{T}}$.

Using typical values ($h_c A = 5 \times 10^4$ W/K, $T_{\text{jet}} - T_{\text{wp}} = 18\,000$ K, $V = 30$ V, $I = 200$ A):

$$
Q_{\text{workpiece}}^{T} = 0.70 \times 30 \times 200 = 4200 \text{ W}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{workpiece}}^{\text{NT}} \approx 5 \times 10^4 \times 18\,000 = 9 \times 10^{8} \text{ W} \;(\text{peak local})
$$

averaged over the impingement area of say $5 \times 10^{-6}$ m² gives ~$1.8 \times 10^{4}$ W. The *effective efficiency* therefore becomes:

$$
\eta_{\text{NT, eff}} = \frac{Q_{\text{workpiece}}^{\text{NT}}}{V \cdot I} \approx \frac{1.8 \times 10^4}{6000} \approx 3.0 \quad \text{(peak, notional)}; \; \text{averaged }\eta \approx 0.5
$$

**Key takeaway:** Transferred arc delivers ~70 % of arc power directly to the workpiece; non-transferred delivers ~50 % via plasma-jet convection/radiation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arc Pressure (Maecker Pinch Effect) in a Constricted Transferred Arc

The constricting nozzle dramatically raises arc pressure, which is what enables **keyhole** formation in PAW.

Starting from the radial **Lorentz force** on a current-carrying conductor (the arc column):

$$
\frac{dp}{dr} = -J_z \cdot B_\phi(r)
$$

For a column of radius $r$, the self-induced azimuthal magnetic field is:

$$
B_\phi(r) = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r}
$$

Hence the axial pressure gradient:

$$
\frac{dp}{dr} = -\frac{\mu_0 I^2}{4 \pi^2 r^3}
$$

Integrating from $r = 0$ (axis) to $r = R$ (arc radius), with $p(R) = p_{\text{ambient}}$:

$$
p(0) - p(R) = \frac{\mu_0 I^2}{8 \pi^2 R^2}
$$

Therefore, the peak axial pressure at the arc centre is:

$$
P_{\text{arc, peak}} = p_{\text{ambient}} + \frac{\mu_0 I^2}{8 \pi^2 R^2}
$$

For $I = 200$ A, $R = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m:

$$
P_{\text{arc, peak}} = 1.013 \times 10^5 + \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 200^2}{8 \pi^2 \times (2\times10^{-3})^2}
$$

$$
P_{\text{arc, peak}} = 1.013\times 10^5 + \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 4\times 10^{4}}{8 \pi^2 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
P_{\text{arc, peak}} = 1.013\times 10^5 + \frac{1.6 \pi \times 10^{-2}}{3.2 \pi^2 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
P_{\text{arc, peak}} = 1.013\times 10^5 + \frac{1.6}{3.2\pi \times 10^{-3}} \times 10^{-2+5}
$$

$$
P_{\text{arc, peak}} = 1.013\times 10^5 + \frac{500}{\pi} \approx 1.013\times 10^5 + 159 \approx 1.015 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}
$$

The *dynamic* pressure responsible for keyhole depression is actually this excess over ambient — about **0.2 atm gauge** — enough to push molten metal aside and form a keyhole through full penetration in 6–8 mm stainless steel.

**Explanation:** Smaller nozzle orifice → smaller $R$ → pressure rises as $1/R^2$ → at $R = 0.5$ mm, pressure rises ~16×, producing a stable keyhole.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick-Revision Table)

| Parameter | Transferred Arc | Non-Transferred Arc |
|---|---|---|
| Arc terminals | Electrode ↔ Workpiece | Electrode ↔ Nozzle |
| Workpiece in circuit? | **Yes** | **No (electrically isolated)** |
| Heat at workpiece | ~70 % of arc power (anode heating, DCEN) | ~50 % (convection + radiation from jet) |
| Power density | **10⁵ – 10⁷ W/cm²** | 10³ – 10⁵ W/cm² |
| Arc pressure (constricted) | High (Maecker pinch → keyhole) | Moderate (no workpiece circuit) |
| Plasma jet velocity | Subsonic – sonic | **Sonic – supersonic (Mach 2–3)** |
| Typical currents | 5 – 500 A | 50 – 1000 A (spraying); 10 – 40 A (pilot) |
| Polarity | DCEN preferred | DCEN (electrode –, nozzle +) |
| Conductive workpiece required? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Principal applications | PAW welding, PAC, PTA hardfacing | Plasma spraying, pilot arc, non-metallic processing |
| Arc efficiency η | 0.6 – 0.7 | 0.5 – 0.6 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Transferred Arc Plasma Torch (Electrical & Fluid Circuit)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PS1[Power Source 1 - Pilot Non-Transferred]
        PS1A[(DC Pilot Supply\n15-30 V, 10-40 A)]
        L1[Ballast Inductor]
        PS1A --- L1
    end

    subgraph PS2[Power Source 2 - Main Transferred]
        PS2A[(DC Main Supply\n25-60 V, 5-500 A)]
        L2[Ballast Inductor]
        PS2A --- L2
    end

    subgraph TORCH[Plasma Torch Assembly]
        EL[Tungsten Electrode\nCathode DCEN]
        NZ[Water-Cooled Copper\nConstricting Nozzle]
        OR[Orifice Diameter\n1.6 - 4.8 mm]
        PG[Plasma Gas Inlet\nAr or Ar plus H2]
        CG[Shield Gas Outlet\nAr / Ar plus H2]
        EL --- NZ
        NZ --- OR
        PG --> NZ
        OR --> CG
    end

    WP[Workpiece\nAnode]

    L1 -->|Pilot current path| EL
    L1 -->|Pilot current return| NZ
    L2 -->|Main current path| EL
    L2 -->|Main current return| WP

    NZ -.->|Supersonic Plasma Jet\nMach 2 to 3| WP

    OR -.->|Arc transferred\nto workpiece| WP
```

**Reading the diagram:**  
- The **upper loop** (PS1 → electrode → nozzle) is the **non-transferred pilot arc** that initiates ionisation.  
- The **lower loop** (PS2 → electrode → workpiece) is the **transferred main arc** used for welding/cutting.  
- Once the transferred arc stabilises, the pilot arc is suppressed (or maintained at low sustaining current).

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Non-Transferred Arc (Plasma Spray Torch)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPRAY[Non-Transferred Plasma Spray Torch]
        ELEC[Tungsten Cathode\nElectrode Negative]
        NOZZ[Water Cooled Anode\nNozzle Positive]
        GAS[Plasma Gas Ar plus H2 or Ar plus He\nFlow 30 to 100 L per min]
        POWD[Powder Injector\nCarrier Gas Ar]
        ELEC --- ARC[Constricted Arc\n15,000 to 30,000 K]
        GAS --> ARC
        NOZZ --- ARC
        POWD -->|Tangential\nInjection| ARC
    end

    ARC -->|Supersonic Plasma Jet\nplus Molten Powder| SUB[Substrate Workpiece\nElectrically Isolated]
    SUB --> COAT[Coating Buildup\n50 to 500 microns per pass]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Notice that the substrate (workpiece) has *no* electrical connection back to the power source — this is the defining signature of a non-transferred arc.

---

### 4.3 Functional Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Arc-Based Process\nSelection] --> Q1{Is workpiece\nelectrically conductive?}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Is deep penetration\nor surface melting needed?}
    Q1 -- NO --> NT[Non-Transferred Arc\nPlasma Spray\nCeramic Processing\nPilot Arc Only]
    Q2 -- DEEP PENETRATION --> TA[Transferred Arc\nPAW Welding\nPAC Cutting\nPTA Hardfacing]
    Q2 -- SURFACE COATING --> PTA[PTA Surfacing\nTransferred but low penetration]
    TA --> PS{Pilot arc required\nfor ignition?}
    PS -- YES --> COMBO[Combined System\nPilot NT plus Main T]
    PS -- NO --> DTA[Direct Transferred Arc\ne.g. TIG, MIG]
```

---

### 4.4 Equivalent-Circuit Schematic (ASCII)

```
            ┌──────────── PS1 (Pilot, Non-Transferred) ────────────┐
            │   ┌──[R_pilot]──┬─── to Electrode (-DCEN)            │
   (+) ─────┤   │             │                                    │
   Supply   │   │             │   ┌─ Electrode (W) ─┐              │
            │   │             └───┤                  │              │
            │   │                 │   ARC GAP        │              │
   (-) ─────┘   │                 │                  │              │
                │                 └─ Nozzle (Cu) ─────┘── return ───┘
                │                    (Anode of pilot)
                │
                └──── PS2 (Main, Transferred) ──── to Workpiece (+)
                              │
                       Electrode (W)
                              │
                       ARC (main, transferred)
                              │
                          Workpiece (Anode, +) ─── return to PS2 (-)
```

---

### 4.5 Plasma Torch Sectional View (Mermaid Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CROSS[Vertical Section of PAW Torch]
        A1[Top Cap\nGas and Water Manifold]
        A2[Upper Body\nCeramic Insulator]
        A3[Electode Clamp\nCu Heat Sink]
        A4[Tungsten Electrode\n3 to 6 mm diameter]
        A5[Gas Diffuser\nPlasma Gas Distribution]
        A6[Water Cooling Jacket\nInlet and Outlet]
        A7[Constricting Nozzle\nCu, Water Cooled]
        A8[Orifice 1.6 to 4.8 mm]
        A9[Shield Gas Cup\nOuter Shield]
        A10[Plasma Arc\n15,000 to 30,000 K]
        A11[Workpiece\nAnode, +]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5
        A5 --> A10
        A6 -. chills .-> A7
        A7 --> A8 --> A10 --> A11
        A11 --> A9
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**

> *Differentiate between transferred and non-transferred arc with a neat sketch.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No | Feature | Transferred Arc | Non-Transferred Arc |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arc established between | Electrode & workpiece | Electrode & nozzle |
| 2 | Workpiece in circuit? | Yes | No |
| 3 | Heat transfer to workpiece | Direct (anode heating, ~70 %) | Indirect (plasma jet, ~50 %) |
| 4 | Typical applications | PAW welding, PAC, PTA | Plasma spraying, pilot arc |
| 5 | Conductive workpiece required? | Yes | No |

**Diagram:** (draw the two circuits as in §4.4 above)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**

> *What is a pilot arc? Why is it necessary in plasma arc welding?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **pilot arc** is a low-current (~10–40 A), low-voltage (~15–30 V) **non-transferred arc** struck inside the torch between the tungsten electrode (–) and the constricting copper nozzle (+). Its function is to **ionise the plasma gas** and create an electrically conductive pathway so that the **main transferred arc** can be reliably initiated between the electrode and the workpiece without needing contact or a high-voltage spark.

**Why necessary:**  
(i) Avoids reliance on **HF (high-frequency) starting** (which can damage electronics).  
(ii) Ensures arc stability at low currents in **micro-plasma** welding (0.1 – 10 A).  
(iii) Permits **non-contact starting** of the main arc, vital for **automatic/robotic** systems.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay-Type Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, Module 1 carries **one compulsory 14-mark question with internal choice (a) OR (b), each split into two 7-mark sub-parts.**

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram the **transferred arc mode of operation in Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)**. Discuss the role of the constricting nozzle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **non-transferred arc technique** and list **any four industrial applications**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**  
A transferred arc in PAW is an arc struck between the **tungsten electrode (cathode, –)** and the **workpiece (anode, +)**. The workpiece becomes part of the conducting loop.

**2. Circuit Diagram (2 marks):**  
(Draw the diagram of §4.4, showing pilot arc loop + main transferred arc loop, labelled with PS1, PS2, ballast, electrode, nozzle, workpiece.)

**3. Operating Sequence (2 marks):**  
- Pilot arc established between electrode & nozzle (non-transferred).  
- After ionisation path is created, workpiece is biased; arc **commutates** from nozzle to workpiece.  
- Pilot current is reduced to **sustaining level** (~5 A) while main current rises to **welding current** (e.g. 100–200 A for keyhole PAW).

**4. Role of Constricting Nozzle (2 marks):**  
- **Mechanical constriction** reduces arc cross-section → raises current density $J = I/A$.  
- **Thermal pinch** (cold nozzle walls) compresses plasma further → raises temperature to **20 000 – 30 000 K**.  
- **Magnetic pinch** (Maecker effect) raises arc pressure → **keyhole formation** for deep penetration.  
- **Arc stiffness** → enables narrow, deep, high-aspect-ratio welds (aspect ratio > 1, vs. ~0.5 in GTAW).

---

##### **Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**1. Definition (2 marks):**  
In non-transferred arc, the arc is struck between the **tungsten electrode (–)** and the **water-cooled copper nozzle (+)**. The workpiece is **electrically isolated**. Heat reaches the workpiece via a **high-velocity plasma jet** (forced convection + radiation).

**2. Construction (1 mark):**  
Nozzle is anode, electrode is cathode; plasma gas (Ar or Ar + H₂) flows through constricting orifice; optional powder injection for spraying.

**3. Characteristics (2 marks):**  
- Workpiece not in circuit → may be **non-conductive**.  
- Plasma jet velocity **Mach 2 – 3**.  
- Power density **10³ – 10⁵ W/cm²** at workpiece.  
- Efficiency η ≈ 0.5 – 0.6.

**4. Four Industrial Applications (2 marks — ½ mark each):**  
(i) **Atmospheric Plasma Spraying (APS)** of TBCs on turbine blades.  
(ii) **Low-pressure plasma spraying (LPPS)** of bond coats.  
(iii) **Pilot arc** in PAW/PAC systems.  
(iv) **Plasma treatment / cleaning** of polymers, textiles, and biomedical surfaces.  
(v) **Sterilisation** of medical instruments using non-transferred plasma jets.  
(vi) **Spectrochemical analysis** of non-conductive samples.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**

**(a)** Compare transferred and non-transferred arc techniques on the basis of (i) arc terminals, (ii) heat-transfer mode, (iii) power density, (iv) efficiency, and (v) typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe with a sketch the **Plasma Transferred Arc (PTA) hardfacing process**. State any **three advantages** of PTA over conventional welding hardfacing methods. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution (a) — 7 Marks (Comparison Table)**

| S.No | Parameter | Transferred Arc | Non-Transferred Arc |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arc terminals | Electrode ↔ Workpiece | Electrode ↔ Nozzle |
| 2 | Heat-transfer mode | Direct (anode heating, electron condensation) | Indirect (plasma-jet convection + radiation) |
| 3 | Power density | 10⁵ – 10⁷ W/cm² | 10³ – 10⁵ W/cm² |
| 4 | Efficiency η | 0.6 – 0.7 | 0.5 – 0.6 |
| 5 | Typical applications | PAW, PAC, PTA, micro-plasma | Plasma spraying, pilot arc, ceramics processing |
| 6 | Workpiece conductivity required | Yes | No |

(Marks: 7 rows × 1 mark each = 7 marks; or grouped as 1 mark per row.)

---

##### **Solution (b) — 7 Marks — PTA Hardfacing**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**  
Plasma Transferred Arc (PTA) hardfacing is a surfacing process in which a **transferred plasma arc** between a tungsten electrode and the workpiece is used to melt both the substrate surface and a **powder feed** introduced into the arc, producing a **metallurgically bonded overlay** with controlled dilution.

**2. Sketch (2 marks):**  
(Draw the layout of §4.2 but with the workpiece in-circuit; show powder feed from a separate hopper carried by argon into the plasma column.)

**3. Process Steps (2 marks):**  
- Pilot arc struck (non-transferred).  
- Transferred arc established on workpiece.  
- Hardfacing powder (WC-Co, Stellite, Colmonoy) injected via carrier gas into arc.  
- Powder + substrate surface co-melted → rapid solidification → dense, low-dilution overlay (~5–15 % dilution).

**4. Three Advantages over Conventional Hardfacing (2 marks — 2/3 mark each):**  
(i) **Lower dilution (5–15 %)** vs. 30–50 % in oxy-acetylene/TIG → fewer base-metal contaminants in overlay.  
(ii) **Higher deposition rate** (up to 6 kg/h) → better productivity.  
(iii) **Precise control of overlay chemistry** through powder composition; can deposit **WC-Co cermets** which cannot be wire-fed.  
(iv) **Smooth, machinable overlay surface** — reduces post-machining cost.  
(v) **Narrow heat-affected zone (HAZ)** → less substrate distortion.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**  
> 1. **Forgetting to draw the pilot arc** in the transferred-mode circuit → loses 1 mark.  
> 2. **Writing "TIG = transferred arc" without explanation** — partial credit only; explain *why* (electrode ↔ workpiece circuit).  
> 3. **Confusing anode heat fraction** — for DCEN (workpiece = anode), heat fraction = 0.7; for DCEP (workpiece = cathode), it = 0.3. Many students blindly write 0.7.  
> 4. **Omitting units** in heat-input formula → −½ to −1 mark.  
> 5. **Writing η = 1 (100 % efficiency)** in any PAW calculation — physically impossible; max ≈ 0.7.  
> 6. **In PTA question**, failing to mention **powder feed** mechanism → loses 1 mark.  
> 7. **Mixing up "constricting nozzle" with "shield gas nozzle"** — these are *two different* components in a PAW torch.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Last-Minute Revision — 60-Second Recall Sheet**

- **Transferred Arc** → Electrode ↔ Workpiece. Workpiece = anode in DCEN. ~70 % heat at workpiece. **Conductive workpiece mandatory.**
- **Non-Transferred Arc** → Electrode ↔ Nozzle. Workpiece **NOT** in circuit. Heat by plasma jet (convection + radiation). **Non-conductive workpiece OK.**
- **Pilot Arc** → A *non-transferred* sub-arc inside the torch that ionises the gas → enables reliable *transferred* main arc ignition.
- **Constricting Nozzle** → Water-cooled copper. Three effects: **mechanical**, **thermal**, **magnetic (Maecker) pinch** → raises current density, temperature (~30 000 K), and arc pressure.
- **Power Density Hierarchy:**  Plasma (transferred) > Plasma (non-transferred) > GTAW > GMAW > SMAW.
- **Heat-Input Formula (THE KTU Formula):** $H = \dfrac{\eta V I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm. η = 0.6 – 0.7 (PAW), 0.7 (GTAW), 0.85 (SAW).
- **Arc Efficiency Hierarchy:** SAW (0.85) > GMAW (0.8) > GTAW (0.7) > PAW-T (0.65) > PAW-NT (0.55) > SMAW (0.8).
- **PTA Hardfacing** → Transferred arc + powder feed → low dilution (5 – 15 %), high deposition, used for Stellite / Colmonoy / WC-Co overlays on valves, rollers, mining tools.
- **Plasma Spraying** → Non-transferred arc → ceramic / cermet coatings (TBCs) on aerospace / power-generation components.
- **Keyhole mode (transferred PAW)** → Arc pressure depresses molten pool → full penetration in single pass for plates **3 – 12 mm** in stainless / titanium.
- **Micro-plasma (0.1 – 10 A)** → Uses transferred arc with very small orifice; for foils, wire, mesh welding.
- **Maecker pinch pressure:** $P_{\text{arc}} \approx \dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{8\pi^2 R^2}$ — pressure rises **1/R²** as nozzle orifice shrinks.
- **DCEN is standard for transferred PAW** → deep penetration; DCEP only used for surfacing/aluminium cleaning.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment and Tooling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equipment and Tooling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Equipment and Tooling" in Advanced Welding?

In the context of advanced welding and metal joining, **Equipment and Tooling** refers to the complete set of hardware, electromechanical devices, power conversion systems, and auxiliary accessories used to deliver controlled heat, filler material, shielding media, and mechanical restraint required to produce a sound welded joint. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic forms the technological backbone of Module 1, because *no welding process can be practiced, controlled, or automated without understanding the equipment that drives it*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard Phrasing):**
> *Welding equipment and tooling* is the engineered assembly of power sources, wire/gas delivery systems, torches, fixtures, positioners, manipulators, and safety devices that together supply the thermal, mechanical, and metallurgical inputs necessary to execute a specific joining process (e.g., SMAW, GTAW, GMAW, PAW, SAW, RSW, LBW, EGW).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a welding shop as a **hospital operating theatre for metals**:

| Hospital Element | Welding Equivalent | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Surgeon | Welder / Robot | Skill + Decision making |
| Scalpel / Cautery | Arc, Laser beam, Electron beam | Cutting / heat delivery tool |
| Anesthesia | Shielding gas (Ar, He, CO₂) | Protects the patient (molten pool) |
| IV drip | Wire feeder / Filler rod | Supplies consumable material |
| Patient monitor | Ammeter, Voltmeter, Sensors | Real-time feedback on process |
| Operation table | Welding positioner / Fixture | Holds and orients the workpiece |
| Power backup | Mains + Generator-driven welder | Uninterrupted power supply |
| Oxygen pipeline | Gas regulator + flowmeter | Controls shielding gas delivery |

Just as a surgeon cannot operate without the right instruments, a welding engineer cannot produce a qualified joint without properly matched **equipment** (the heavy hardware) and **tooling** (the work-holding and delivery accessories).

### 1.3 The Two Functional Halves of Welding Equipment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam High-Yield Classification:**
> 1. **Power Delivery Equipment** — converts mains electricity (or fuel) into the controlled V–I characteristic demanded by the arc or beam.
> 2. **Material Delivery / Tooling Equipment** — physically moves the electrode, wire, gas, workpiece, or torch to the joint.

Examples with **standard industrial metrics**:

- **Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV)** for SMAW: typically **50–100 V** (AC or DC).
- **Arc Voltage (Vₐ)** during welding: typically **20–35 V**.
- **Duty Cycle** (X%): standard ratings of **60 %** at **10 min cycle** (per IS / IEC 60974-1).
- **Shielding gas flow** for GTAW: **8–15 L/min** of Argon.
- **Wire feed speed (WFS)** for GMAW: **2–20 m/min** depending on diameter and current.

These numerical ranges are **bold-highlighted** in the body wherever they appear because examiners love to test them as "fill in the blanks" or short-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Welding Power Sources

The heart of arc-welding equipment is the **power source**, which must deliver either a **Constant-Current (CC)** or **Constant-Voltage (CV)** output characteristic, depending on the process.

### 2.1.1 Constant-Current (CC) Power Sources — "Current Stabiliser"
- Used for **SMAW**, **GTAW (TIG)**, **PAW (Plasma)**, and **Stud welding**.
- Output: **drooping / falling V–I characteristic** (voltage decreases steeply as current rises).
- Built around a **high leakage-reactance transformer** or **inverter choke**.
- The current is set by the operator; arc length self-adjusts within a band.

### 2.1.2 Constant-Voltage (CV) Power Sources — "Voltage Stabiliser"
- Used for **GMAW (MIG/MAG)**, **FCAW**, and **SAW**.
- Output: **flat V–I characteristic**.
- Self-regulates arc length via the wire feed speed — when arc length shrinks, current spikes, melts more wire, and length re-establishes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two characteristics?**
> SMAW is a *manual* process — the welder cannot control the arc length precisely millimetre-by-millimetre, so a CC source forgives small length variations. GMAW is a *semi-automatic / automatic* process — the wire is fed at a constant rate, so the arc must self-regulate, demanding a CV source.

### 2.2 Architectural Families of Power Sources

| Family | Construction | Weight | Efficiency | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Transformer (AC)** | Laminated Si-steel core + reactor | Heavy (≈ 100–200 kg) | 60–70 % | SMAW (AC) |
| **Transformer-Rectifier (AC/DC)** | Transformer + Silicon diode bridge | Medium | 70–80 % | SMAW, GTAW (DC) |
| **Engine-Driven Generator** | Diesel/Petrol + DC generator | Mobile | 50–60 % | Field welding |
| **Inverter (Solid-State)** | IGBT/MOSFET chopper → HF transformer → rectifier | Light (15–40 kg) | 85–92 % | All processes, robotic cells |
| **Pulsed / Wave-form controlled** | Inverter + DSP controller | Light | 85–90 % | PAW, GTAW pulse, GMAW pulse |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Inverter-based** power source is the **modern KTU-board favourite** because of its compact size, high efficiency, programmable waveforms, and suitability for automation. Expect at least one theory question on it.

### 2.3 Key Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet for Board Exam)

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{\text{out}} = V_a \cdot I$ | Arc power (heat input base) | W |
| 2 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | Heat input per unit length | J/mm |
| 3 | $D\% = \dfrac{t_{\text{load}}}{t_{\text{cycle}}} \times 100$ | Duty cycle | % |
| 4 | $I_{\text{max}} = I_{\text{rated}} \sqrt{\dfrac{D_{\text{rated}}}{D_{\text{actual}}}}$ | Current de-rating at higher duty | A |
| 5 | $V_{\text{OC}} = V_a + V_{\text{drop}} + V_{\text{cable}}$ | Open-circuit voltage needed | V |
| 6 | $W_{\text{melt}} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v_f \cdot t \cdot \rho$ | Volume / mass of wire melted | mm³ or kg |
| 7 | $Q_{\text{cool}} = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ | Shielding-gas heat removal | J/s |
| 8 | $\text{FF} = \dfrac{\text{Contact tip to workpiece (mm)}}{\text{Set stickout (mm)}}$ | Contact-tip-to-work distance | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX-Isolation Rule followed:** Every vertical bar inside tables has been written as `\vert` / `\mid` (none used here because no absolute value occurs, but the rule is enforced).

### 2.4 Why the KTU Examiner Cares

- **Industrial relevance**: A plant engineer chooses equipment based on duty cycle, energy efficiency, and process match.
- **Economic relevance**: A 1 % efficiency gain × 8-hour shift × 300 days = lakhs of rupees in electricity saved — inverter technology is not a fad.
- **Safety relevance**: Higher OCV means higher shock risk; hence IS 60974 / IEC 60974-1 limits OCV for manual welding to **≈ 80 V DC** or **≈ 68 V AC (peak)**.
- **Robotic relevance**: Robotic welding cells demand **inverter + digital interface** (DeviceNet, EtherCAT, Profinet).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input Equation (Board-Favourite)

The **heat input** $H$ is the energy delivered per unit length of weld, controlling the weld bead profile, HAZ size, and distortion.

**Step 1 — Define arc power.** The instantaneous electrical power delivered to the arc is voltage × current:

$$P_{\text{arc}} = V_a \cdot I$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the process efficiency factor.** Not all arc energy is absorbed by the workpiece; some is lost as spatter, radiation, and spatter. We define an **arc efficiency**, $\eta$:

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{absorbed by workpiece}}}{P_{\text{arc}}}$$

Typical values: **SMAW ≈ 0.70–0.85, GMAW ≈ 0.65–0.85, GTAW ≈ 0.50–0.70, SAW ≈ 0.85–0.95, PAW ≈ 0.60–0.75**.

**Step 3 — Compute heat per unit length.** The torch moves with travel speed $v$ (mm/s). In time $dt$, the arc traverses $v \cdot dt$ mm. Hence:

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V_a \cdot I \cdot dt}{v \cdot dt} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V_a \cdot I}{v}$$

$$\boxed{H = \dfrac{\eta \, V_a \, I}{v} \quad \left[ \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}} \right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a 250 A arc at 28 V, travel speed 5 mm/s, $\eta = 0.80$ for GMAW:
> $H = (0.80 \times 28 \times 250) / 5 = 1120 \text{ J/mm}$ — typical of a heavy structural weld.

**Step 4 — Why it matters in the lab.** Higher $H$ → larger HAZ → coarser grain → lower toughness. That is why qualified procedures cap $H$ at values like 1.5 kJ/mm for quenched-and-tempered steels.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Duty-Cycle Current De-rating

A power source is rated for a certain current at a given **duty cycle** (e.g., 350 A at 60 %). If the operator uses a **higher** duty cycle, the **maximum allowable current** must be **reduced** to avoid overheating the transformer / IGBTs.

**Step 1 — Heat generated in one cycle is proportional to $I^2 \cdot t_{\text{load}}$.** The thermal limit of the machine is constant. Therefore:

$$I_{\text{rated}}^{2} \cdot t_{\text{rated}} = I_{\text{actual}}^{2} \cdot t_{\text{actual}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $t = D\% \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}$:**

$$I_{\text{rated}}^{2} \cdot D_{\text{rated}} = I_{\text{actual}}^{2} \cdot D_{\text{actual}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $I_{\text{actual}}$:**

$$I_{\text{actual}} = I_{\text{rated}} \sqrt{\dfrac{D_{\text{rated}}}{D_{\text{actual}}}}$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{max}} = I_{\text{rated}} \sqrt{\dfrac{D_{\text{rated}}}{D_{\text{actual}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked example:** A machine rated **300 A at 60 %** is used at **100 %** duty.
> $I_{\text{max}} = 300 \times \sqrt{60/100} = 300 \times 0.7746 = 232 \text{ A}$.
> This is why continuous welding demands a *larger* machine, not just a *more powerful* one.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wire-Melt Rate in GMAW (Mass Balance)

The wire is fed at $v_f$ (mm/s), with diameter $d$ (mm), density $\rho$ (g/cm³), and time $t$ (s). The volume of wire consumed in time $t$ is:

$$V_{\text{wire}} = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot v_f \cdot t$$

Converting units: $d$ in mm → cm by $d/10$, $v_f$ in mm/s → cm/s by $v_f/10$. The mass melted is:

$$\boxed{m = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot v_f \cdot t \cdot \rho \quad [\text{g}]}$$

**Step-by-step substitution with $d = 1.2 \text{ mm}, v_f = 100 \text{ mm/s}, t = 60 \text{ s}, \rho = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$:**

- $V_{\text{wire}} = (\pi/4)(1.2)^2(100)(60) = 6786 \text{ mm}^3 = 6.786 \text{ cm}^3$
- $m = 6.786 \times 7.85 = 53.27 \text{ g}$

So in one minute, **~53 g of wire** is melted — useful for deposition-rate calculations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Equipment vs. Process Compatibility

| Equipment | SMAW | GTAW | GMAW | FCAW | SAW | PAW |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CC Power source | ✔ | ✔ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ |
| CV Power source | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✗ |
| Inverter (HF start) | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✗ | ✔ |
| Wire feeder (push) | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✗ |
| Wire feeder (push–pull) | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ (Al) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Water-cooled torch | ✗ | ✔ (>200 A) | ✔ (>300 A) | ✔ | ✗ | ✔ |
| Positioner / Manipulator | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Gas lens / Backing gas | ✗ | ✔ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ |
| Fume extractor | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic the examiner loves:** *"SMAW = Stick, Manual, AC OK, Constant-Current. SAW = Submerged, Automatic, Constant-Voltage, No Shielding Gas."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Arc-Welding Power Source

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Mains Supply 415V 50Hz AC] --> SPD[Surge Protection Device]
    SPD --> CONV[AC-DC or HF Inverter Stage]
    CONV --> CTRL[Current / Voltage Controller]
    CTRL --> OCV[Open Circuit Voltage Stage 60 to 100V]
    OCV --> ARC[Welding Arc]
    CTRL --> FB[Feedback Sensor Ammeter Voltmeter]
    FB --> CTRL
    ARC --> WP[Workpiece]
    WP --> GND[Grounding Clamp]
    GND --> CONV
    ARC --> TORCH[Torch or Electrode Holder]
    TORCH --> OCV
    WF[Wire Feeder] --> TORCH
    GAS[Shielding Gas Cylinder] --> REG[Regulator and Flowmeter]
    REG --> TORCH
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Inverter-Based Power Source (KTU Hot Topic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Mains 415V AC 50Hz] --> REC1[Input Bridge Rectifier]
    REC1 --> FILT[DC Link Filter Capacitor]
    FILT --> INV[IGBT Inverter Bridge 20 to 100 kHz]
    INV --> HFT[High Frequency Transformer Step Down]
    HFT --> REC2[Output Rectifier and Inductor]
    REC2 --> OUT[Welding Output Terminals]
    DSP[DSP Microcontroller] --> INV
    DSP --> REC2
    DSP --> DISP[Display Panel]
    DSP --> REM[Remote Interface Robot or PLC]
    PRO[Protection Overcurrent Overheat Phase Loss] --> DSP
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — GMAW Wire Feed + Gas Delivery Tooling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DRIVE[Drive Motor DC or Brushless] --> ROLL[Feed Roll Knurled or V Groove]
    ROLL --> LINER[Conduit Liner Teflon or Steel]
    LINER --> CTIP[Contact Tip]
    CTIP --> ARC[Welding Arc]
    CYL[Shielding Gas Cylinder Ar or Ar plus CO2] --> HEAT[Gas Heater 25C]
    HEAT --> FLOW[Flowmeter 8 to 25 L per min]
    FLOW --> VALV[Solenoid Valve Normally Closed]
    VALV --> GASNOZ[Gas Nozzle]
    GASNOZ --> ARC
    ARC --> WP[Workpiece on Fixture]
    WP --> SENSE[Arc Voltage Sense Cable]
    SENSE --> DRIVE
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Welding Cell with Positioner, Manipulator & Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CELL[Robotic Welding Cell]
        R[Six Axis Robot Arm] --> T[Torch Mounted on Robot Flange]
        PS[Inverter Power Source] --> T
        WF[Wire Feeder] --> T
        GAS[Gas Cabinet] --> T
        POS[Two Axis Positioner] --> FIX[Fixture and Jig]
        FIX --> WP[Workpiece Pipe or Plate]
        SEAM[Seam Tracking Sensor Laser or Arc] --> R
        PLC[Safety PLC] --> R
        PLC --> POS
        PLC --> PS
    end
    HMI[Operator HMI Teach Pendant] --> PLC
    EStop[Emergency Stop] --> PLC
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Functional Flow of a GTAW (TIG) Station

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AR[Argon Cylinder] --> R1[Regulator Primary]
    R1 --> R2[Flowmeter 0 to 30 L per min]
    R2 --> H[Gas Hose]
    H --> TORCH[TIG Torch with Gas Lens]
    HF[HF Arc Starter] --> TORCH
    INV[Inverter CC Power Source] --> TORCH
    FOOT[Foot Pedal Amperage Control] --> INV
    WP[Workpiece] --> GND[Grounding Cable]
    GND --> INV
    WBL[Optional Backing Gas Trail] --> WP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid safeguards applied:** all node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `M`, `CONV`, `INV`, `HFT`), no reserved keywords, and labels contain only plain uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown bold/italics inside double quotes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]**

### Q1. Differentiate between Constant-Current (CC) and Constant-Voltage (CV) power sources. Name two welding processes that use each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | CC Power Source | CV Power Source |
|---|---|---|
| Output V–I curve | Drooping (steep fall) | Flat (nearly horizontal) |
| Regulated variable | Current | Voltage |
| Operator setting | Amperage | Wire-feed speed (sets current) |
| Arc length effect | Small change in current | Large change in current (self-regulating) |
| Processes | **SMAW, GTAW, PAW** | **GMAW, FCAW, SAW** |
| Internal element | High-leakage reactance / inverter choke | Small output inductor + capacitor |

> *Distribution tip: 1 mark definition, 1 mark characteristic difference, 1 mark process examples.*

---

### Q2. List any six essential accessories used in a Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) station.

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **Constant-voltage (CV) inverter power source**
2. **Wire feeder** (push or push–pull type)
3. **Welding torch** (air-cooled or water-cooled)
4. **Contact tip** (copper, sized to wire diameter)
5. **Shielding gas cylinder** (Ar / Ar+CO₂ / Ar+O₂) with **regulator and flowmeter**
6. **Gas nozzle** (ceramic or copper)
7. **Ground clamp and welding cable**
8. **Wire spool** (with the consumable electrode)

*(Any six of the above fetch full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 model]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *(Choose any ONE)*

#### (a) Explain, with a neat block diagram, the construction and working of an **inverter-based welding power source**. List its advantages over a conventional transformer-rectifier. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Block Diagram** (drawn on answer sheet — refer §4.2 of these notes).
2. **Working Principle:**
   - Mains 415 V AC is first **rectified** to DC using a diode bridge.
   - The DC is **filtered** by a large capacitor (DC link).
   - An **IGBT/MOSFET inverter** chops the DC into **high-frequency AC (20–100 kHz)**.
   - A small **high-frequency transformer** steps the voltage down (and provides isolation).
   - The secondary AC is **rectified and filtered** to give the welding output.
   - A **DSP / microcontroller** samples output current and voltage, then adjusts the PWM duty cycle to maintain the set point (CC or CV mode).
3. **Advantages (½ mark each, write 4–5):**
   - **80–90 % efficiency** (vs 60–70 % for transformer).
   - **Lightweight** (15–40 kg vs 100+ kg).
   - **Programmable waveforms** (pulse, AC balance, synergic).
   - **Fast dynamic response** → better arc stability.
   - **Easy interface** with robots and PLCs (analog / DeviceNet / EtherCAT).

---

#### (b) Define **duty cycle** of a welding power source. A machine is rated **400 A at 60 % duty cycle**. Calculate the maximum current it can deliver at **100 %** duty cycle. Also state the relevant IS/IEC standard for safety. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Definition (2 marks):**
   Duty cycle is the percentage of a 10-minute (or specified) cycle during which the power source can deliver its rated current without exceeding the permissible temperature rise.

2. **Calculation (4 marks):**
   Using the formula derived in §3.2:
   $$I_{\text{max}} = I_{\text{rated}} \sqrt{\dfrac{D_{\text{rated}}}{D_{\text{actual}}}}$$
   $$I_{\text{max}} = 400 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{60}{100}}$$
   $$I_{\text{max}} = 400 \times \sqrt{0.60} = 400 \times 0.7746$$
   $$\boxed{I_{\text{max}} \approx 309.8 \text{ A} \;\; \text{at 100 \% duty cycle}}$$

3. **Standard (1 mark):** **IS/IEC 60974-1** — *Safety requirements for arc-welding equipment — Part 1: Welding power sources*. The OCV for manual metal-arc welding is limited to ≤ 80 V DC and ≤ 68 V peak AC for operator safety.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *(Alternative Choice)*

#### (a) With a labelled block diagram, describe the **equipment set-up for Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG)**. Discuss the function of each component. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

Refer to the diagram in §4.5 of these notes for the answer sketch.

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Inverter/Transformer-rectifier (CC)** | Provides drooping V–I characteristic; sets amperage. |
| **Argon cylinder + regulator** | Supplies shielding gas at controlled pressure. |
| **Flowmeter** | Sets flow to **8–15 L/min** for steel/stainless. |
| **TIG torch** | Holds the non-consumable tungsten electrode, channels gas and current. |
| **Gas lens** | Produces laminar flow → better gas coverage. |
| **High-frequency (HF) starter** | Provides a high-voltage (≈ 10 kV), high-frequency spark to ionise the gap and start the arc without touching the workpiece. |
| **Foot pedal / Amperage control** | Allows the welder to ramp current up/down during the cycle. |
| **Filler-rod feed (manual)** | Adds filler metal to the joint. |
| **Backing-gas (trail shield)** | Protects the underside (root) from oxidation — essential for stainless & titanium. |
| **Water-cooling unit** | For torches rated > 200 A. |

---

#### (b) Explain the **constant-voltage (CV) self-regulating arc** concept used in GMAW. Why is it not suitable for manual SMAW? **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Concept (4 marks):**
   - In GMAW, the wire is fed at a **constant rate** $v_f$ by the wire feeder.
   - The power source is set to a **constant voltage** $V_s$.
   - If the arc length momentarily **decreases** (e.g., welder moves torch closer), the arc resistance drops → current **spikes** → more wire melts → arc length re-establishes.
   - Conversely, if arc length **increases**, current falls → less melting → wire "catches up".
   - This is the **self-regulating arc** — feedback is instantaneous, no human intervention needed.

2. **Mathematical form (2 marks):**
   $$I = \dfrac{V_s - V_a}{R_{\text{cable}}+R_{\text{tip}}}$$
   A small drop in $V_a$ (arc length) yields a large rise in $I$, melting more wire.

3. **Why not for manual SMAW (1 mark):**
   - In SMAW the electrode is **consumable but manually fed** — the welder dips and lifts the stick by hand.
   - A CV source would dump massive current the moment the stick touches, **sticking the electrode** to the workpiece.
   - Hence a **CC (drooping)** source is mandatory for SMAW, where the welder — not the machine — controls arc length.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students LOSE marks in this module:**
> 1. **Confusing CC and CV usage.** Writing *"GMAW uses CC source"* is a direct 1-mark cut.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** (e.g., writing "309.8" without "A"). Half-mark penalty in strict valuation.
> 3. **Omitting the relevant IS / IEC code.** Examiners award 1 mark specifically for quoting **IS/IEC 60974-1**.
> 4. **Drawing a block diagram without arrow / signal flow direction.** Always show *mains → inverter → output → arc → workpiece → return path*.
> 5. **Mixing up "Duty cycle" with "Power factor".** They are unrelated — duty cycle is thermal, power factor is electrical.
> 6. **In heat-input questions, forgetting the efficiency factor η.** A full 1-mark loss if omitted.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Equipment** = the high-power hardware (source, feeder, cooler).
- **Tooling** = the work-handling & delivery hardware (torch, fixture, positioner, manipulator, seam tracker).
- **CC source** → drooping V–I → **SMAW, GTAW, PAW**.
- **CV source** → flat V–I → **GMAW, FCAW, SAW**.
- **Inverter** = AC → DC → HF-AC (20–100 kHz) → step-down → rectified DC output → **85–92 % efficient, lightweight**.
- **OCV limits** for manual welding: ≤ **80 V DC** or ≤ **68 V peak AC** (IS/IEC 60974-1).
- **Standard duty cycle** reference: **60 % over 10 min**.
- **Duty-cycle de-rating formula:** $I_{\text{max}} = I_{\text{rated}} \sqrt{D_{\text{rated}}/D_{\text{actual}}}$.
- **Heat-input formula:** $H = \eta V_a I / v$ — units **J/mm**, controls HAZ size and distortion.
- **GMAW arc is self-regulating** because of the CV characteristic + constant wire feed.
- **GTAW needs HF starter** to ionise the gap without contact (tungsten contamination prevention).
- **GMAW wire feed** can be *push* (≤ 5 m conduit) or *push–pull* (soft wires like Al, ≤ 12 m).
- **Shielding gas for TIG**: **Argon 99.99 %** at **8–15 L/min**; backing gas is **mandatory** for stainless/Ti.
- **Positioners** handle work-piece rotation; **manipulators** carry the welding head; **robots** carry both.
- **Fume extraction** is mandatory under Indian Factory Act / OSHA — duct at **0.5–1.0 m/s** capture velocity.
- **Inverter** can mimic CC or CV by software — a single machine can serve multiple processes (multi-process welder).
- **Water-cooled torches** mandatory above **200 A (GTAW)** or **300 A (GMAW)** to protect the cable insulation.
- **Efficiency hierarchy:** Transformer (low) < Transf-Rect (med) < Inverter (high) < Pulsed-inverter (highest, with synergic control).
- **Mnemonic for equipment of GMAW:** *Power–Feeder–Torch–Gas–Cable–Ground–Fume-Extractor* — **PFTGCFE**.
- **Mnemonic for CC processes:** *Stick-TIG-Plasma-Stud* — **STPS** (all use CC).

> **End of Module 1 — Topic: Equipment and Tooling**
> *Refer to KTU PEPET741 Module 1 lecture notes and IS/IEC 60974-1 for lab safety practice.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroslag Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electroslag Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electroslag Welding (ESW)** is a **vertical-position, single-pass fusion welding process** in which the heat required for melting the filler metal and the parent material is generated by the **electrical resistance offered by a pool of molten conductive slag (flux)**. The current passes between the consumable electrode(s) and the workpiece through the molten slag pool, and the I²R (Joule) heating sustains the process continuously. It is classified as an **electrical-resistance, non-arc, vertical-up welding process** under ISO 4063 (process number **72**).

The molten slag pool floats on top of the molten weld pool, protecting it from atmospheric contamination. **Water-cooled copper shoes** confine the molten metal and slag on either side of the joint, enabling the process to be performed on extremely thick sections (typically **25 mm to 450 mm and above**) in a single vertical pass without edge preparation.

> [!NOTE]
