> **Historical Note for KTU Exam**: Electroslag Welding was invented at the **E.O. Paton Electric Welding Institute, Kyiv, Ukraine (then USSR)** in **1952** by Professors K.V. Lyubavsky and B.E. Paton. It was introduced to heavy industry in the late 1950s.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Electric Heated Soup"**

Imagine you are cooking a very thick stew (the weld pool) in a deep, narrow pot. Instead of a gas stove flame, you plunge **two electrodes** into the broth and pass a strong electric current through it. Because the salty, mineral-rich broth conducts electricity (just like a thick **saltwater solution**), it heats up uniformly — *no flame, no sparks, just hot liquid*. To keep the broth from spilling and the heat from escaping, you slide two **sliding cold copper walls (the copper dams)** upward as the level rises.

In ESW:
- The **molten slag** = the "electrically heated broth"
- The **consumable wire electrode** = the heating element melting into the broth
- The **copper shoes** = the cold sliding walls
- The **weld pool** = the stew being cooked below the slag

No visible arc exists during steady-state ESW — the process is **silent, slag-bath driven**, and capable of joining plates as thick as a **one-storey building wall** in a single upward pass.

## 1.3 Standard Process Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Welding Position | Vertical-up (3G / PA equivalent) | — |
| Plate Thickness Range | **25 to 450 (and up to 1000)** | mm |
| Voltage | **25 – 50** | V (AC) |
| Current | **400 – 1000** | A |
| Welding Travel Speed | **1.0 – 3.0** | m/hr |
| Slag Pool Temperature | **~ 2000** | °C |
| Weld Pool Temperature | **~ 1750 – 1800** | °C |
| Slag Depth (Molten) | **40 – 60** | mm |
| Thermal Efficiency (η) | **~ 0.80 – 0.90** | — |
| Process Code (ISO 4063) | **72** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: ESW is treated as a high-deposition-rate, **non-arc, slag-bath resistance process** — distinct from Electrogas Welding (EGW, process code **73**), which still uses an external arc shielded by gas/flux.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The process is best understood as a **four-stage sequence** rather than a single steady-state event:

### Stage 1 — Starting Arc Phase (Transient)
- Plates are clamped vertically with a root gap of **20 – 35 mm**.
- A starting block (run-off tab) is placed at the bottom.
- **Granulated flux (slag)** is poured into the gap.
- The electrode is lowered to touch the base plate, and an **arc is struck**.
- The arc melts the flux to form a **molten slag pool** (~ 25 – 40 mm deep).

### Stage 2 — Arc Extinction (Transition)
- Once the slag pool is deep enough to **submerge the electrode tip**, the arc is **extinguished (quenched)**.
- The **arc voltage** drops to zero, and the current now flows through the **conductive molten slag** between the electrode tip and the weld pool.
- **Joule heating (I²R)** in the slag takes over as the dominant heat source.

### Stage 3 — Steady-State Resistance Welding
- Current path: **Electrode (+/–) → molten slag → weld pool → base plate**.
- The high current (~ 600 A) flowing through a small cross-section of resistive molten flux generates intense heat.
- The electrode and the parent metal edges continuously melt.
- The **molten weld pool** is supported from below by the **solidifying weld metal** and from the sides by the **water-cooled copper shoes**.

### Stage 4 — Upward Progression
- The welding head (electrode guide, current contact tube, slag hopper) **rises vertically** at a controlled speed.
- The copper shoes slide upward behind the weld head, exposing the solidified weld.
- New flux is fed continuously to maintain slag depth.
- The process continues to the top run-off tab.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no arc survives in steady state?**
> In ESW, the **open-circuit voltage (OCV)** is set intentionally **LOW (~ 40 – 50 V)** — below the arc re-ignition threshold once the slag covers the electrode tip. This is the fundamental electrical difference from SAW and EGW.

## 2.2 Role of the Molten Slag (Flux)

The slag is **not just a shielding medium** in ESW — it is the **heating element itself**.

| Function | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Electrical Resistance** | Molten flux has a resistivity of **~ 0.4 – 1.0 Ω·cm** at 2000 °C, sufficient to dissipate I²R heat. |
| **Thermal Insulation** | Slag floats above the weld pool, slowing heat extraction and promoting deep penetration. |
| **Metallurgical Cleaning** | Reacts with dissolved O, S, P; refines grain structure. |
| **Shape Confinement** | Holds the weld pool within the copper dams. |

Common flux systems (analogous to SAW fluxes): **Fused fluxes** (e.g., type FES — Iron oxide, silica, alumina, lime, magnesia, fluorspar).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **high-yield** for KTU numerical/analytical questions on ESW.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (HI)** | $HI \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | η = thermal efficiency, V = volts, I = amperes, S = travel speed (mm/min) |
| 2 | **Heat Input (alt form)** | $HI \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ | J/mm | S in mm/s |
| 3 | **Deposition Rate (DR)** | $DR \;=\; \dfrac{\pi d^{2} \cdot v_{f} \cdot \rho}{4 \cdot 1000}$ | kg/hr | d = wire dia (mm), v_f = wire feed (m/min), ρ = density (kg/m³) |
| 4 | **Joule Heat in Slag** | $P_{slag} \;=\; I^{2} R_{slag} \;=\; V I$ | W (or J/s) | R_slag = effective resistance of slag column |
| 5 | **Cross-Sectional Area of Weld** | $A_{weld} \;=\; t \cdot g$ | mm² | t = plate thickness, g = root gap |
| 6 | **Weld Time per Pass** | $t_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{H}{v_{w}}$ | hr | H = plate height (m), v_w = welding speed (m/hr) |
| 7 | **Specific Energy** | $E_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{HI}{t}$ | kJ/mm² | per unit cross-section |
| 8 | **Resistivity of Slag** | $\rho_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{R \cdot A_{s}}{L_{s}}$ | Ω·cm | A_s = slag cross-section, L_s = slag column length |

> [!NOTE]
> For ESW, **η (thermal efficiency) is exceptionally high (≈ 0.85 – 0.90)** because there is no arc plasma radiation or spatter loss — almost all electrical energy is converted to heat within the slag.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

ESW is the **go-to process** when:
- A single-pass, full-penetration weld is required on **sections thicker than ~ 50 mm** in the vertical position.
- Distortion must be minimized despite high heat input.
- Productivity on heavy fabrication is critical (ship hulls, nuclear vessels, hydraulic turbine shafts).

Major industrial users: **heavy shipbuilding** (deck-to-side shell joints, bulkhead-to-shell), **boiler and pressure vessel fabrication**, **nuclear reactor containment vessels**, **dam penstock pipes**, and **bridge box-girder diaphragms**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input in Electroslag Welding

The **governing energy-balance equation** for ESW is derived from the principle of conservation of electrical energy and its conversion to thermal energy within the slag bath.

### Step 1: Electrical Power Dissipated in the Slag

The instantaneous electrical power delivered to the slag column from the power source is given by the product of the welding voltage and welding current.

$$
P \;=\; V \cdot I
$$

where $P$ is in watts, $V$ is the RMS welding voltage (V), and $I$ is the RMS welding current (A).

### Step 2: Useful Thermal Power

Because some of the electrical energy is lost to the water-cooled copper shoes, radiation from the slag surface, and conduction into the unmelted base metal, only a fraction of the electrical power contributes to melting. This fraction is the **process thermal efficiency** $\eta$.

$$
P_{th} \;=\; \eta \cdot V \cdot I
$$

For ESW, empirical measurements give $\eta \approx 0.85$ to $0.90$.

### Step 3: Heat Input per Unit Length of Weld

To express the heat input on a per-length basis, divide the useful thermal power by the **vertical travel speed** $S$ of the welding head (expressed in mm/s for SI consistency, or in mm/min for engineering practice).

$$
HI \;=\; \dfrac{P_{th}}{S} \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}
$$

with units J/mm when $S$ is in mm/s.

### Step 4: Conversion to the Standard kJ/mm Form Used in KTU

The KTU standard convention reports welding speed in **mm/min**. Converting 1 mm/s = 60 mm/min introduces a factor of 60 in the numerator, and dividing by 1000 converts J to kJ. The final form is:

$$
HI \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}
$$

with $HI$ in **kJ/mm**, $V$ in V, $I$ in A, and $S$ in mm/min.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: For ESW, although $V$ is low (~ 40 V) compared to arc processes, the very high current (~ 600 – 900 A) and high $\eta$ make $HI$ in ESW among the **highest of all welding processes** — often 5 – 10 × higher than in shielded metal arc welding. This explains the **coarse grain** and the **wide heat-affected zone (HAZ)** characteristic of ESW joints.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Resistance of the Slag Column

### Step 1: Definition of Resistivity

The resistance of any conductor is given by:

$$
R \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}
$$

where $\rho$ is the resistivity of the material, $L$ is the length of the current path, and $A$ is the cross-sectional area perpendicular to the current.

### Step 2: Slag Geometry

The slag pool in ESW is a roughly rectangular vertical column sandwiched between the weld pool below and the electrode tip above. Its cross-section is the **gap area between the plates**:

$$
A_{s} \;=\; t \cdot g
$$

where $t$ = plate thickness (mm) and $g$ = root gap (mm). Its length is the **electrode immersion depth** into the slag, $L_{s} \approx 25$ to $40$ mm.

### Step 3: Effective Slag Resistance

$$
R_{slag} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_{s} \cdot L_{s}}{A_{s}} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_{s} \cdot L_{s}}{t \cdot g}
$$

For $\rho_{s} \approx 0.6$ Ω·cm, $L_{s} = 30$ mm = 3 cm, $t = 100$ mm, $g = 25$ mm:

$$
R_{slag} \;=\; \dfrac{0.6 \cdot 3}{100 \cdot 25} \cdot 10^{-2}
$$

Wait — the area must be in cm²: $A_{s} = 100 \times 25 = 2500$ mm² = 25 cm².

$$
R_{slag} \;=\; \dfrac{0.6 \cdot 3}{25} \;=\; 0.072 \; \Omega
$$

### Step 4: Power Dissipated

$$
P \;=\; I^{2} \cdot R_{slag} \;=\; (700)^{2} \cdot 0.072 \;\approx\; 35{,}280 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 35.3 \;\text{kW}
$$

This matches typical ESW operating powers and confirms that the **slag pool acts as a controlled resistive heating element**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Weld Time for a Single ESW Pass

### Step 1: Definition of Welding Speed

The welding head rises at a constant vertical travel speed $v_{w}$ (m/hr), also written as $S$ (mm/min) with $1$ m/hr $= 1000/60 \approx 16.67$ mm/min.

### Step 2: Time to Weld a Plate of Height H

$$
t_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{H}{v_{w}}
$$

For $H = 3$ m and $v_{w} = 1.5$ m/hr:

$$
t_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{3}{1.5} \;=\; 2 \;\text{hr}
$$

### Step 3: Comparison With Multi-Pass Arc Welding

For the same 100 mm thick × 3 m high joint done by **SAW multi-pass**, total weld length required (V-groove) easily exceeds 30 – 40 m of weld bead, taking 8 – 12 hours. **ESW is therefore 4 – 6× faster on thick vertical joints** — its principal commercial advantage.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ESW vs Related Processes

| Feature | ESW | EGW | SAW | GTAW |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heat source | Slag resistance | Arc (CO₂) | Arc (under flux) | Arc (TIG) |
| Steady-state arc | **No** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Process code (ISO 4063) | **72** | 73 | 12 | 14 |
| Plate thickness | 25 – 1000 mm | 6 – 50 mm | 6 – 100 mm | 0.5 – 10 mm |
| Position | Vertical-up | Vertical-up | All | All |
| η (efficiency) | 0.85 – 0.90 | 0.75 – 0.85 | 0.85 – 0.95 | 0.40 – 0.60 |
| Typical V | 25 – 50 V | 28 – 35 V | 28 – 38 V | 10 – 18 V |
| Typical I | 400 – 1000 A | 300 – 700 A | 400 – 1200 A | 60 – 350 A |
| Slag/gas shielding | Slag | Gas (CO₂/Ar) | Slag | Argon |
| Single-pass thick weld | **Yes** | Limited | Limited | No |
| Outdoor wind tolerance | Excellent | Poor | Good | Poor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction**: Students frequently confuse ESW with EGW. The mnemonic — **"ESW = Slag is the Heater"** vs **"EGW = Gas shields an Arc"** — is exam-ready.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Process Flow — Steady-State ESW Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Plates clamped vertically] --> B[Pour granulated flux into root gap]
    B --> C[Lower consumable electrode to base plate]
    C --> D[Strike starting arc arc]
    D --> E[Arc melts flux forming molten slag pool]
    E --> F{Slag deep enough to submerge electrode tip?}
    F -- No --> E
    F -- Yes --> G[Arc extinguished, current flows through slag]
    G --> H[Joule heating I squared R in slag]
    H --> I[Electrode tip melts into weld pool]
    I --> J[Base plate edges melt into weld pool]
    J --> K[Copper shoes slide upward]
    K --> L[New flux fed from hopper]
    L --> M[Slag and weld pool rise with head]
    M --> N{Top run-off tab reached?}
    N -- No --> G
    N -- Yes --> O[Stop current, retract electrode]
    O --> P[Cut off start and end tabs]
    P --> Q[Finished single-pass vertical weld]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The graph above is a **closed-loop cycle** — once the arc transitions to slag-resistance heating (node G), the system stays in steady state (G → H → I → J → K → L → M) until the top of the joint is reached.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — ESW Equipment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerSource[AC Power Source]
        PS[Step-down welding transformer 3 phase]
    end
    subgraph WeldingHead[Moving Welding Head]
        EH[Electrode holder contact tube]
        FH[Flux hopper]
    end
    subgraph VerticalJoint[Vertical Joint Assembly]
        CS1[Water-cooled copper shoe left]
        CS2[Water-cooled copper shoe right]
        SP[Molten slag pool 2000 C]
        WP[Molten weld pool 1800 C]
        WM[Solidified weld metal]
        BP1[Base plate left]
        BP2[Base plate right]
    end
    subgraph GuideSystem[Guide and Travel System]
        GR[Vertical guide rails]
        DR[Drive carriage motor]
    end
    subgraph CoolingSystem[Cooling System]
        CR[Chiller unit]
    end

    PS --> EH
    EH --> SP
    SP --> WP
    WP --> WM
    CS1 --- SP
    CS2 --- SP
    BP1 --- WP
    BP2 --- WP
    FH --> SP
    GR --> DR
    DR --> EH
    CS1 --> CR
    CS2 --> CR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation**: This block diagram shows the **functional architecture** rather than a physical drawing. The **electrical current path** is PS → EH → SP → WP → BP1/BP2 → back to PS. The **mechanical path** is DR → GR → EH. The **thermal path** is SP → WP → WM. The **coolant loop** is CR ↔ CS1/CS2.

## 4.3 Mermaid Slag-Bath Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical energy V times I] --> B[Molten slag resistive element]
    B --> C[Conduction to electrode tip]
    B --> D[Conduction to weld pool]
    B --> E[Conduction to base metal]
    B --> F[Loss to copper shoes]
    B --> G[Surface radiation loss]
    C --> H[Electrode melting - filler addition]
    D --> I[Parent metal melting]
    E --> J[HAZ formation]
    H --> K[Weld bead build-up]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    F --> L[Removed by cooling water]
    G --> M[Lost to atmosphere]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

> **Q: Define Electroslag Welding (ESW). List any two of its principal industrial applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Electroslag Welding is a **vertical-up, single-pass fusion welding process** in which the heat for melting the filler metal and the parent material is generated by the **electrical resistance (I²R heating) of a molten conductive slag pool**. The arc, used only momentarily at start, is extinguished once the slag submerges the electrode, and the current flows through the slag column between the consumable electrode and the base metal. The molten slag floats on the weld pool and is retained by **water-cooled copper shoes**.

**Applications (1 mark — any two):**
1. Fabrication of **thick-walled pressure vessels and nuclear reactor containment shells**.
2. **Heavy shipbuilding** — longitudinal and transverse bulkhead-to-shell joints.
3. **Hydraulic turbine shafts, dam penstock pipes**, and large-diameter pipeline girth welds.
4. **Bridge box-girder diaphragm and flange-to-web** thick-plate joints.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

> **Q: Differentiate between Electroslag Welding (ESW) and Electrogas Welding (EGW) in any three aspects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per valid difference):**

| S.No. | Aspect | Electroslag Welding (ESW) | Electrogas Welding (EGW) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat source** | I²R resistance heating through molten slag (no arc in steady state) | Continuous electric arc between consumable electrode and weld pool |
| 2 | **Shielding medium** | Molten slag (flux) — same flux used in SAW | External shielding gas (CO₂ or Ar/CO₂ mix) |
| 3 | **Slag / Gas handling** | Slag is the resistor and the shield; must be replenished | Gas is supplied externally; no molten slag pool sustains arc |
| 4 | **Thickness range** | 25 – 1000 mm (single pass) | Typically 6 – 50 mm |
| 5 | **ISO 4063 code** | 72 | 73 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part A**: Students often write *"ESW uses slag to protect the weld"* without mentioning **resistance heating**. Half a mark is lost. Always state the **heat-generation mechanism** first, the **shielding role** second.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Both alternatives A and B are full 14-mark questions. KTU students answer ONE.**

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

> **(a)** Explain the **principle and working of the Electroslag Welding process** with the help of a neat sketch. Describe the role of the **molten slag pool** and the **water-cooled copper shoes**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **process parameters, advantages, limitations, and applications** of Electroslag Welding. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 marks]

**Principle (2 marks):** Electroslag Welding works on the principle of **Joule heating (I²R effect)** in a **molten conductive slag bath**. Once a starting arc melts the flux into a deep slag pool, the arc is extinguished, and the welding current (~ 400 – 1000 A) passes through the slag column between the **consumable electrode** and the **workpiece**. The electrical resistance of the molten slag (~ 0.4 – 1.0 Ω·cm at 2000 °C) dissipates the electrical energy as heat, which melts the continuously fed electrode wire and the abutting edges of the base plates.

**Working Sequence (3 marks):**
1. **Setup:** Two thick plates are clamped in the **vertical position** with a root gap of 20 – 35 mm. **Water-cooled copper shoes** are placed on both faces of the joint, sealed against the plates with a refractory gasket. A starting "run-off" block is fitted at the bottom.
2. **Flux deposition:** Granulated fused flux is poured into the gap between the plates from the hopper.
3. **Arc initiation:** The consumable electrode (typically 2.4 – 3.2 mm dia) is lowered to touch the plate, and a short arc is struck (OCV ≈ 50 V).
4. **Slag formation and arc extinction:** The arc melts the flux, creating a molten slag pool (~ 25 – 40 mm deep). When the slag fully covers the electrode tip, the **arc is quenched** because the open-circuit voltage is below the re-ignition threshold.
5. **Steady-state resistance heating:** Current flows through the slag. Heat melts the electrode (deposited as filler) and the parent plate edges. The weld pool forms below the slag and solidifies against the copper shoes.
6. **Upward progression:** The welding head, shoes, and hopper all rise together at a controlled speed. New flux is fed continuously. The solidified weld exits behind the copper shoes.
7. **Termination:** On reaching the run-off tab at the top, the current is cut off, the head retracts, and start/end tabs are removed.

**Role of Molten Slag Pool (1 mark):**
- Acts as the **electrical resistance heating element**.
- Provides **thermal insulation** over the weld pool, enabling deep penetration.
- Performs **metallurgical refining** (deoxidation, desulfurization).
- **Shapes** and **confines** the weld pool within the copper dams.

**Role of Water-Cooled Copper Shoes (1 mark):**
- Contain the molten metal and slag, preventing breakout.
- Extract excess heat from the sides, controlling the weld-pool width and the **HAZ size**.
- Provide a sliding mould that moves upward with the welding head, enabling continuous welding.

### Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 marks]

**Process Parameters (2 marks):**
| Parameter | Range |
|---|---|
| Welding current | 400 – 1000 A (DC or AC, usually AC) |
| Welding voltage | 25 – 50 V |
| Welding speed | 1 – 3 m/hr |
| Electrode diameter | 2.4 – 3.2 mm (often 2 or 3 wires) |
| Slag pool depth | 25 – 50 mm |
| Root gap | 20 – 35 mm |
| Plate thickness | 25 – 1000 mm |

**Advantages (2 marks — any 4 to score 2):**
1. **Single-pass joining** of very thick plates, eliminating the need for V/U edge preparation.
2. **High deposition rate** (often 10 – 30 kg/hr per head).
3. **Low distortion** compared to multi-pass arc welding, since the weld is symmetric and the heat input is concentrated vertically.
4. **No arc flash, spatter, or UV radiation** — safe for operators and suitable for indoor fabrication.
5. **Excellent slag shielding** — no need for external gas cylinders.
6. **Deep penetration** ensures full fusion across the entire plate thickness.

**Limitations (2 marks — any 4 to score 2):**
1. **Limited to vertical or near-vertical position** only.
2. **Coarse, columnar grain structure** in the weld and a **wide HAZ** — lower toughness and fatigue life.
3. **Hot-cracking susceptibility** in high-sulphur or high-carbon steels.
4. **High heat input** may cause grain growth in the parent HAZ — restricts use in quenched-and-tempered grades.
5. **Initial capital cost** of the ESW machine and copper-shoe tooling is high.
6. **Slag inclusions and starting porosity** if slag chemistry or arc-start sequence is not tightly controlled.

**Applications (1 mark — any 2):**
1. Heavy shipbuilding (deck-to-side shell, bulkhead-to-shell).
2. Nuclear reactor pressure vessels and steam generator shells.
3. Hydroelectric penstock pipes and dam outlet liners.
4. Thick-walled boiler drums and chemical-reactor shells.
5. Large hydraulic press cylinders and rolling-mill housings.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

> **(a)** Describe the **metallurgical aspects of Electroslag Welding**, including the role of slag chemistry, the **weld-metal solidification behaviour**, and the **microstructural changes** in the heat-affected zone (HAZ). **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare Electroslag Welding (ESW) with **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** in terms of principle, heat source, position capability, metallurgical effects, and typical applications. Highlight the **toughness and hot-cracking issues** specific to ESW. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 marks]

**1. Slag Chemistry and its Role (2 marks):**
The flux used in ESW is typically a **fused flux** (FES type) of composition similar to SAW flux, containing SiO₂, MnO, CaO, MgO, Al₂O₃, CaF₂, and FeO. In the molten state (≈ 2000 °C), the slag:
- **Transfers oxygen** between the atmosphere, weld pool, and electrode (O potential gradient).
- **Removes sulphur and phosphorus** by forming stable compounds.
- **Alloys with the weld pool** — Mn and Si from the slag reduce FeO, increasing the Mn/Si content of the deposit (typical weld metal: 0.6 – 1.0% Mn, 0.2 – 0.5% Si).
- **Controls inclusion morphology** — finely dispersed silicate inclusions improve toughness if well-distributed.

**2. Solidification Behaviour (2 marks):**
- The weld pool in ESW is **very large and slow-cooling** due to the insulating slag cap.
- Solidification proceeds **epitaxially** from the base metal grains upward along the heat-flow direction (steepest thermal gradient), producing **coarse, long columnar grains** aligned with the welding direction.
- The **solidification structure** in ESW of carbon steel is typically: thin chill zone → coarse columnar austenite grains → Widmanstätten ferrite + aligned pearlite in the as-welded condition.
- Centreline segregation of **S, P, C, Mn** can occur, raising **hot-cracking susceptibility** in single-pass ESW.

**3. HAZ Microstructure (2 marks):**
- Peak temperatures adjacent to the fusion line exceed **1450 °C**; the coarse-grain HAZ (CGHAZ) experiences **austenite grain coarsening**, reducing notch toughness.
- For C-Mn and low-alloy steels, the CGHAZ typically shows **coarse-grained ferrite + pearlite** with a hardness of ~ 180 – 220 HV.
- Sub-critical HAZ regions undergo **tempering** in quenched-and-tempered base plates, causing **softening** (a *soft zone* of concern in HY-80, HY-100, etc.).
- Because the heat input is very high and cooling is slow, **toughness** in the CGHAZ and weld metal is **lower** than in equivalent multi-pass arc welds — a critical issue in offshore and nuclear service.

**4. Toughness and Hot-Cracking Concerns (1 mark):**
- **Hot cracking** (solidification cracking) arises from centreline segregation of S and P combined with low-Mn/S ratio; mitigated by using **low-S, low-P base plates** or by adding Mn/Si deoxidants through the flux.
- **Reheat / underclad cracking** may appear in low-alloy Cr-Mo-V steels in the HAZ.
- **Lamellar tearing** risk in T-joints with through-thickness restraint.

### Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Criterion | ESW | SAW |
|---|---|---|
| ISO 4063 code | 72 | 12 |
| Heat source | Molten-slag resistance (no steady-state arc) | Arc submerged under granular flux |
| Arc during welding | No (after start) | Yes (continuous) |
| Position | **Vertical only** (up) | All positions (1G, 2F for high currents) |
| Plate thickness | **25 – 1000 mm** in one pass | 6 – 100 mm; thick plates need multi-pass |
| Deposition rate | Very high (10 – 30 kg/hr) | High (5 – 15 kg/hr) |
| η (efficiency) | 0.85 – 0.90 | 0.85 – 0.95 |
| Edge preparation | Usually **not required** (square butt) | V- or U-groove for thick plates |
| Filler metal | Usually 1 – 3 wires or strip electrode | Wire + flux |
| Equipment cost | Higher (special head & shoes) | Lower, more flexible |
| Operator skill | Moderate | High for thick multi-pass |

**Toughness Issues Specific to ESW (2 marks):**
- **Coarse columnar grains** in the as-welded weld metal lower Charpy impact energy.
- **Wide CGHAZ** with grain-coarsening reduces HAZ toughness.
- Slow cooling promotes **Widmanstätten ferrite** in C-Mn steels, lowering CVN.
- For critical service (offshore, nuclear), **post-weld normalizing** or **tempering** is often required to refine the microstructure.

**Hot-Cracking Issues in ESW (2 marks):**
- Centreline **solidification cracking** from S, P, and C segregation.
- **Liquation cracking** in the HAZ of high-strength low-alloy (HSLA) plates.
- Mitigation: low-S/P base metal (S ≤ 0.010%), Mn/S ≥ 20, controlled heat input, weaving oscillation of the electrode, and **use of austenitic filler** for difficult grades.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B**:
> 1. **Diagrams are mandatory.** A question on ESW asked without a labelled sketch can lose **2 – 3 marks** at the examiner's discretion. Always draw the **vertical joint, copper shoes, slag pool, weld pool, electrode, and run-off tab** with arrows for slag level and current path.
> 2. **Numerical substitution** is expected in heat-input derivations. Writing $HI = \eta V I / S$ without units or sample numbers loses a step-mark.
> 3. **Distinguish ESW from EGW explicitly** — confusing the two is a common cause of zero-mark answers.
> 4. **Mention the inventor (Paton Institute) and the year (1952)** wherever a "history/development" sub-part is asked.
> 5. **Do not overstate "no arc"** — clarify that the arc exists only in the *transient start phase*, and the *steady-state* operation is arc-free.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Electroslag Welding (ESW)**

- **Definition (1-liner):** ESW is a **vertical-up, single-pass, arc-extinction resistance welding process** in which heat is generated by **I²R heating in a molten conductive slag** between a consumable electrode and the workpiece.
- **Inventor / Year:** Paton Electric Welding Institute, **Kyiv, Ukraine, 1952** (K.V. Lyubavsky & B.E. Paton).
- **ISO 4063 Code:** **72** (do not confuse with 73 = EGW).
- **Heat source:** **Resistance (Joule) heating in molten slag** — not an arc.
- **Slag pool temperature:** **~ 2000 °C**; **Weld pool:** ~ 1750 – 1800 °C.
- **Voltage:** 25 – 50 V; **Current:** 400 – 1000 A (typically AC).
- **Travel speed:** 1 – 3 m/hr; **Deposition:** 10 – 30 kg/hr.
- **Plate thickness range:** 25 – 1000 mm in **one pass**.
- **Position:** **Vertical-up only**.
- **Key equipment:** AC power source, water-cooled copper shoes/dams, electrode guide & oscillator, flux hopper, vertical travel carriage, cooling-water chiller.
- **Slag functions (Mnemonic — "RTS-MS"):** **R**esistor, **T**hermal insulator, **S**hield, **M**etallurgical refiner, **S**hape container.
- **Heat input formula (KTU standard):** $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm, with $\eta \approx 0.85$ for ESW.
- **Slag resistance formula:** $R_{slag} = \dfrac{\rho_{s} L_{s}}{t \cdot g}$.
- **Major advantages:** single-pass for thick sections, low distortion, no arc/spatter, high deposition.
- **Major limitations:** vertical-only, coarse grain, wide HAZ, hot-cracking tendency, high capital cost.
- **ESW vs EGW:** ESW = no arc, slag resistance; EGW = arc, gas shield. Codes 72 vs 73.
- **ESW vs SAW:** ESW = vertical, no arc, single-pass; SAW = all-position, arc submerged, multi-pass for thick.
- **Top applications:** nuclear vessels, ship hulls, penstock pipes, pressure vessels, dam gates.
- **Metallurgical concerns:** centreline S/P segregation → solidification cracking; CGHAZ coarsening → low toughness; HAZ softening in Q&T steels.
- **Mitigations:** low-S/P base metal (Mn/S ≥ 20), post-weld normalizing, controlled heat input, oscillating electrode, austenitic filler for crack-prone grades.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electron Beam Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electron Beam Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** is a non-conventional fusion welding process in which the joint between two base metals is produced by the **localized melting and fusion** caused by the **kinetic energy of a highly focused, high-velocity stream of electrons** striking the workpiece under vacuum conditions. The kinetic energy of the accelerated electrons is rapidly converted into thermal energy upon impact, generating extremely high power density (typically **10⁶ to 10⁹ W/mm²**) at the point of incidence.

## 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bullet vs. Hammer" Intuition

Imagine two ways to melt a slab of butter on a freezing cold day:

- **The Hammer (Conventional Arc Welding):** You bring a giant hot iron (the arc) and *push* heat into the butter. The surface gets hot first, then the heat slowly *conducts* downward. This is slow, spreads wide, and burns the top.
- **The Bullet (Electron Beam Welding):** Instead, imagine firing millions of tiny invisible "bullets" (electrons) moving at near the **speed of light** directly into the butter. The bullets bury themselves deep and dump ALL their motion energy into the butter in a tiny, narrow column. The butter melts *deep* before the sides even get warm.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of EBW Power Density:**
> 1. **Vacuum Environment** — Prevents electrons from colliding with air molecules (otherwise they'd scatter like a hose without a nozzle).
> 2. **High Velocity** — Electrons are accelerated to roughly **0.5 to 0.7 times the speed of light**.
> 3. **Magnetic Focusing** — The electron beam is squeezed to a spot as small as **0.1 to 1.0 mm** in diameter.

## 1.2 Historical Context & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (KTU Board Standard) |
|---|---|
| **Power Density** | **10⁶ to 10⁹ W/mm²** |
| **Accelerating Voltage** | 30 kV to 200 kV (commonly 60–150 kV) |
| **Beam Diameter at Focus** | 0.1 mm to 1.5 mm |
| **Vacuum Level Required** | **10⁻² to 10⁻⁴ torr** (High Vacuum EBW) |
| **Depth-to-Width Ratio of Weld** | Up to **20 : 1** or even **50 : 1** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why the deep narrow weld?** Because the electrons are stopped within a few micrometres of the surface and deposit their energy in a near-cylindrical column rather than spreading sideways. This is why EBW is called a **"keyhole" welding process**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Six-Stage Chain of Events

The EBW process is governed by a precise sequence of physical phenomena. KTU examiners frequently test the *sequence* itself, so memorize this order:

1. **Electron Emission (Thermionic Emission):** A tungsten filament (cathode) is heated to roughly **2500 °C**. Electrons overcome the work function and "boil off" the surface (Richardson–Dushman emission).
2. **Acceleration:** A high potential difference (typically **60–150 kV**) between the cathode and anode creates a strong electric field. Electrons are accelerated to approximately half the speed of light.
3. **Magnetic Focusing:** Electromagnetic coils (magnetic lens) squeeze the diverging electron stream into a tight, coherent beam of diameter < 1 mm.
4. **Beam Deflection & Guidance:** Magnetic deflection coils steer the beam precisely along the joint line.
5. **Workpiece Interaction (Kinetic-to-Thermal Conversion):** High-velocity electrons strike the workpiece and undergo abrupt deceleration, converting kinetic energy into heat (>90% efficiency).
6. **Vapour-Cavity (Keyhole) Formation:** The intense localised heat vaporises the metal, creating a deep, narrow vapour-filled cavity called the **keyhole**. The molten metal flows around this cavity, and as the beam moves on, the molten walls solidify to form a deep, narrow weld.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Keyhole" Effect:**
> The vapour cavity acts like a moving tunnel. The full depth of the metal is melted *simultaneously* as the beam drills down, rather than melting progressively from the top down. This is why EBW achieves such extraordinary depth-to-width ratios.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master These)

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P = V \cdot I$ | Beam Power | Watts (W) | Total electrical power delivered to the beam |
| $E_k = eV$ | Kinetic energy gained per electron | Joules (J) | Fundamental electron–field interaction |
| $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | Heat Input per unit length | J/mm | Compares EBW to arc welding |
| $D \approx k \cdot \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v}$ | Approximate Weld Depth | mm | Used for process planning |
| $\dfrac{D}{W} \approx 10 \text{ to } 50$ | Depth-to-Width Ratio | dimensionless | Hallmark of EBW |
| $v_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 e V}{m_e}}$ | Electron velocity | m/s | Shows relativistic effects at high V |
| $q = \dfrac{0.0012 \, V}{d}$ | Charge density criterion | C/m | Beam current regulation |

Where:
- $V$ = Accelerating Voltage (volts)
- $I$ = Beam Current (amperes)
- $v$ = Travel Speed (mm/s)
- $\eta$ = Process efficiency (typically **0.85 to 0.95** for EBW)
- $e$ = Electron charge = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- $m_e$ = Electron mass = **9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg**
- $D$ = Weld depth (mm); $W$ = Weld width (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Note — The Efficiency Term η:**
> In **Electron Beam Welding**, $\eta \approx 0.9$ (very high because beam loss is minimal in vacuum). This is much higher than **GTAW (η ≈ 0.6)** or **SMAW (η ≈ 0.8)**. KTU questions often ask you to compare these.

## 2.3 Equipment Architecture — The EBW Machine

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to label a block diagram. Memorise these five subsystems:

1. **Electron Gun Assembly** — Contains the cathode (filament), anode, grid cup, and bias supply.
2. **High-Voltage Power Supply** — Provides 30–200 kV DC accelerating potential.
3. **Focusing & Deflection Coil System** — Magnetic lenses for beam shaping and steering.
4. **Vacuum Chamber & Pumping System** — Maintains **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁵ torr**; includes diffusion pump, mechanical pump, and seals.
5. **Workpiece Manipulation System** — CNC X-Y-Z table inside the vacuum chamber.

> [!TIP]
> **Why vacuum is non-negotiable:** At atmospheric pressure, electrons travelling at 0.5c would collide with nitrogen/oxygen molecules within millimetres, scattering the beam completely. The mean free path of an electron in vacuum at 10⁻⁴ torr is roughly **50 metres** — more than enough to reach the workpiece.

## 2.4 Classification of EBW (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Type | Vacuum Level | Application | Beam Power |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High-Vacuum EBW** | **10⁻⁴ torr** | Aerospace, nuclear, critical welds | 100 W – 100 kW |
| **Partial / Soft Vacuum EBW** | **10⁻¹ to 10⁻² torr** | Larger components, automotive | Up to 60 kW |
| **Non-Vacuum EBW** | Atmospheric | Heavy engineering (requires special gun) | > 100 kW |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Velocity of an Accelerated Electron

A fundamental relationship in EBW theory. Given the kinetic energy gained by an electron traversing the accelerating voltage $V$:

$$E_k = e V$$

Using classical kinetic energy (valid for $V < 100\,\text{kV}$; relativistic corrections needed above this):

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m_e v_e^2$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$\frac{1}{2} m_e v_e^2 = e V$$

Solving for electron velocity $v_e$:

$$v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m_e}}$$

### Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU 2-Mark Question)

**Problem:** An EBW machine operates at an accelerating voltage of **$V = 60\,\text{kV}$**. Calculate the velocity of the electrons.

**Given:**
- $V = 60 \times 10^3\,\text{V}$
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}$
- $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\,\text{kg}$

**Step 1 — Substitute into the formula:**

$$v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 60 \times 10^3}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the numerator:**

$$2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 60 \times 10^3 = 1.9224 \times 10^{-14}\,\text{J}$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $m_e$:**

$$\frac{1.9224 \times 10^{-14}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}} = 2.110 \times 10^{16}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root:**

$$v_e = \sqrt{2.110 \times 10^{16}} = 1.453 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$:**

$$\frac{v_e}{c} = \frac{1.453 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^8} \approx 0.484$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The electron reaches about **48.4 % of the speed of light**. This justifies the high kinetic energy available for conversion into heat on impact.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Heat Input per Unit Weld Length

This is the most-tested equation in KTU 2024 EBW module. The total beam power is:

$$P = V \cdot I$$

where $V$ is the accelerating voltage and $I$ is the beam current. The actual heat delivered to the workpiece is reduced by the **process efficiency** $\eta$:

$$P_{\text{useful}} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I$$

If the workpiece moves at a travel speed $v$ (in mm/s), the heat input *per unit length of weld* is:

$$H = \frac{P_{\text{useful}}}{v} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$$

The units of $H$ are $\text{J/mm}$. This is the **same form** as the heat input formula for any arc welding process, with $\eta$ replaced by the appropriate process efficiency.

### Numerical Worked Example (Full 7-Mark Style)

**Problem:** In an EBW operation, the accelerating voltage is **$V = 80\,\text{kV}$**, the beam current is **$I = 50\,\text{mA}$**, and the travel speed is **$v = 5\,\text{mm/s}$**. If the process efficiency is **$\eta = 0.9$**, determine:

(a) The beam power
(b) The heat input per unit length

**Given Data:**
- $V = 80 \times 10^3\,\text{V}$
- $I = 50 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A}$
- $v = 5\,\text{mm/s}$
- $\eta = 0.9$

### Solution to Part (a) — Beam Power

$$P = V \cdot I$$

$$P = (80 \times 10^3) \times (50 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$P = 4000\,\text{W} = 4\,\text{kW}$$

### Solution to Part (b) — Heat Input per Unit Length

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$$

$$H = \frac{0.9 \times 4000}{5}$$

$$H = \frac{3600}{5}$$

$$H = 720\,\text{J/mm}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Comparison anchor:** A typical arc weld gives $H \approx 200\text{–}1000\,\text{J/mm}$. EBW at modest settings sits comfortably in this range, *but the energy is delivered in a far narrower region*, producing a much deeper, narrower weld.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Relating Beam Power to Power Density

The **power density** (W/mm²) is the key metric that distinguishes EBW from other processes. It is:

$$\rho_P = \frac{P}{A_{\text{spot}}}$$

where $A_{\text{spot}}$ is the area of the focused beam spot on the workpiece. For a circular spot of diameter $d$:

$$A_{\text{spot}} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

Therefore:

$$\rho_P = \frac{4 V I}{\pi d^2}$$

### Quick Numerical Illustration

If $P = 4\,\text{kW}$ and $d = 0.5\,\text{mm}$:

$$\rho_P = \frac{4 \times 4000}{\pi \times (0.5)^2} = \frac{16000}{0.7854} \approx 2.04 \times 10^4\,\text{W/mm}^2$$

For sharper focus ($d = 0.1\,\text{mm}$), the same power yields:

$$\rho_P = \frac{16000}{\pi \times 0.01} \approx 5.09 \times 10^5\,\text{W/mm}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is *orders of magnitude* higher than arc welding (typically $\sim 10^2\,\text{W/mm}^2$). This is why EBW can vaporise metal and form a keyhole, whereas arc welding only melts it.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 14-Mark Favourite)

| Feature | EBW | GTAW (TIG) | Plasma Welding |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power Density (W/mm²)** | 10⁶–10⁹ | 10²–10³ | 10⁴–10⁵ |
| **Heat Source** | Kinetic energy of e⁻ | Electric arc | Constricted arc |
| **Depth/Width Ratio** | 20:1 to 50:1 | 1:1 to 2:1 | 2:1 to 5:1 |
| **HAZ Width** | Very narrow | Wide | Moderate |
| **Atmosphere Required** | **Vacuum mandatory** | Inert gas shield | Inert gas shield |
| **Efficiency (η)** | **0.85–0.95** | 0.5–0.7 | 0.6–0.75 |
| **Weldable Material Thickness (single pass)** | Up to 100 mm | Up to 6 mm | Up to 15 mm |
| **Capital Cost** | Very High | Moderate | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — EBW Process Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** A physical cross-section of an EBW chamber requires complex CAD drawings. The **Mermaid block below** renders a *functional architecture flow* showing the **subsystem interactions** — this is the format KTU examiners use in 14-mark question diagrams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HVPS[High Voltage Power Supply 60 to 150 kV] --> GUN
    FIL[Heated Tungsten Filament 2500 C Cathode] --> GUN
    GRID[Grid Cup with Bias Voltage] --> GUN
    GUN[Electron Gun Assembly] -->|Stream of Electrons| ACCL
    ACCL[Acceleration Region Cathode to Anode] -->|0.5 c velocity| FOC
    FOC[Magnetic Focusing Coils Beam Diameter less than 1 mm] -->|Collimated Beam| DEF
    DEF[Magnetic Deflection Coils Beam Steering] -->|Targeted Path| WP
    WP[Workpiece inside Vacuum Chamber]
    VAC[Vacuum Pumping System 10 power minus 4 torr] --> CHAM
    CHAM[Vacuum Chamber Sealed Enclosure] --> WP
    CNC[CNC X Y Z Manipulator Table] --> WP
    MON[Operator Console with Weld Parameter Display] --> HVPS
    MON --> CNC
    MON --> VAC
    WP -->|Vapour Keyhole forms| KHOLE[Vapour Filled Keyhole Cavity]
    KHOLE -->|Molten Walls Solidify| SEAM[Deep Narrow Weld Seam D to W greater than 20]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Reading Guide for KTU

When asked to "draw and label the EBW process," ensure you include these **mandatory labels** (each worth 1 mark in board valuation):

1. **Electron gun / cathode** — labelled with operating temperature
2. **Anode** — show as perforated plate at ground potential
3. **Accelerating voltage source** — annotate value range (e.g., 150 kV)
4. **Magnetic focusing lens** — show coil symbol
5. **Magnetic deflection coil** — show two orthogonal pairs (X & Y steering)
6. **Vacuum chamber** — show sealed enclosure
7. **Workpiece** — show at the beam focus point
8. **Vapour keyhole** — show narrow column extending through the workpiece thickness

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget to show the **vacuum chamber boundary** or fail to indicate that the **workpiece is inside the vacuum**. This loses 2–3 marks outright.

## 4.3 Beam–Workpiece Interaction (Conceptual Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BEAM[High Velocity Electron Beam] -->|strikes| SURF[Workpiece Surface]
    SURF -->|Energy converted to heat| MELT1[Top Melt Zone Narrow]
    MELT1 -->|Keyhole extends| KEY[Deep Vapour Filled Keyhole]
    KEY -->|Lateral conduction| MELT2[Side Melt Zone Thin]
    MELT2 -->|Heat dissipates| HAZ[Heat Affected Zone Minimal]
    HAZ -->|Unaffected base metal| BASE[Parent Metal]
    KEY -->|Solidifies as beam moves| SEAM[Narrow Deep Solidified Weld]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading this diagram:** Notice how the HAZ (Heat Affected Zone) is **minimal** because energy is deposited as a *line* (the keyhole) rather than spread over a wide surface. This is the structural reason EBW produces low-distortion welds — a frequent 3-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Electron Beam Welding. State any two advantages of EBW over conventional arc welding.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Electron Beam Welding (EBW) is a fusion welding process in which the heat required for joining is generated by accelerating a high-velocity stream of electrons in a vacuum, focusing them onto the joint, and converting their kinetic energy into thermal energy upon impact with the workpiece.

**Two advantages:**

1. **Extremely high power density** (10⁶–10⁹ W/mm²) — produces very deep, narrow welds with depth-to-width ratios up to 50:1 in a single pass.
2. **Minimal Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) and distortion** — because heat is deposited in a narrow column, surrounding material is barely affected, making EBW ideal for thin and heat-sensitive alloys.
3. *(Bonus valid point)* No filler metal or shielding gas required; welds in reactive metals like titanium, zirconium, and niobium.

---

### Question A2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is vacuum essential in Electron Beam Welding? Mention the vacuum level used in high-vacuum EBW.**

**Model Answer:**

**Why vacuum is essential:**

1. Electrons accelerated to high velocities would collide with air molecules if present, causing **beam scattering** and loss of energy.
2. Vacuum prevents **oxidation and contamination** of the weld pool — critical for reactive metals (titanium, niobium, zirconium).
3. The mean free path of electrons in vacuum is sufficient to deliver the beam unattenuated from the gun to the workpiece.

**Vacuum level:** High-vacuum EBW is performed at **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁵ torr** (typically ~10⁻⁴ torr).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, each Module typically contains an **internal choice**. Two alternative 14-mark questions are provided below. Pick **ONE** to attempt.

### Question Choice A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 + Adapted]**

**A.** (a) With the help of a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of Electron Beam Welding. Label all major subsystems. **(7 Marks)**

**(b) In an EBW operation, the accelerating voltage is 100 kV, the beam current is 80 mA, the travel speed is 4 mm/s, and the process efficiency is 0.9. Calculate:**
  (i) The velocity of the electrons.
  (ii) The beam power.
  (iii) The heat input per unit length of weld. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Working Principle (Stepwise):**

1. **Electron Generation:** A tungsten filament (cathode) is heated to roughly 2500 °C by an AC heater supply. Electrons are emitted via thermionic emission.
2. **Acceleration:** A high DC voltage (here 100 kV) applied between the cathode and the grounded anode accelerates the emitted electrons through a perforated anode. The electrons gain kinetic energy $E_k = eV$.
3. **Focusing:** A magnetic lens (electromagnetic coil) focuses the diverging electron stream into a fine beam (~0.5 mm diameter).
4. **Deflection:** A pair of magnetic deflection coils steers the focused beam along the desired weld path with high precision.
5. **Vacuum Environment:** The entire process occurs inside a vacuum chamber maintained at ~10⁻⁴ torr, ensuring the beam reaches the workpiece unattenuated.
6. **Keyhole Formation & Solidification:** On impact, ~90% of the kinetic energy is converted to heat in a tiny spot. The metal vaporises, forming a deep, narrow vapour cavity (the **keyhole**). Molten metal flows around the keyhole; as the beam moves on, the cavity collapses and the weld solidifies into a deep, narrow seam.

**Block Diagram (as drawn):**
Refer to the Mermaid functional block in Section 4.1 of these notes. The examiner expects: Electron Gun → Accelerating Anode → Magnetic Focus Coil → Magnetic Deflection Coils → Workpiece in Vacuum Chamber → Keyhole → Solidified Weld.

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Given:**
- $V = 100 \times 10^3\,\text{V}$
- $I = 80 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A}$
- $v = 4\,\text{mm/s}$
- $\eta = 0.9$
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}$
- $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\,\text{kg}$

**(i) Electron velocity:**

$$v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m_e}}$$

$$v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 100 \times 10^3}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v_e = \sqrt{\frac{3.204 \times 10^{-14}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}} = \sqrt{3.518 \times 10^{16}}$$

$$v_e \approx 1.876 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$$

This is about **62.5 % of the speed of light**.

**(ii) Beam power:**

$$P = V \cdot I = (100 \times 10^3)(80 \times 10^{-3}) = 8000\,\text{W} = 8\,\text{kW}$$

**(iii) Heat input per unit length:**

$$H = \frac{\eta V I}{v} = \frac{0.9 \times 8000}{4} = \frac{7200}{4} = 1800\,\text{J/mm}$$

---

### Question Choice B — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 + Adapted]**

**B.** (a) Explain the formation of the **keyhole** in Electron Beam Welding. State any four advantages and two limitations of EBW. **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Compare Electron Beam Welding with Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW) on the basis of: (i) heat source, (ii) power density, (iii) depth-to-width ratio, (iv) operating atmosphere, (v) weldable thickness in a single pass, and (vi) process efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Keyhole Formation:**

When the high-velocity electron beam strikes the workpiece, the localised power density is so high (10⁶–10⁹ W/mm²) that the metal at the point of incidence **vaporises** almost instantly. The escaping metal vapour creates a high pressure that pushes molten metal radially outward, forming a deep, narrow, vapour-filled cavity called the **keyhole**.

This keyhole is essentially a long, thin cylinder of vapour extending through the entire thickness of the workpiece. The beam penetrates down this cavity, melting the walls progressively. As the beam moves along the joint, the molten metal behind the keyhole flows back in and solidifies, producing a **deep, narrow weld with a very high depth-to-width ratio (often 20:1 or greater)**.

**Four Advantages of EBW:**

1. **Deep penetration** in a single pass (up to 100 mm in steel; even thicker in some alloys).
2. **Minimal Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** — low distortion, suitable for precision assemblies.
3. **No contamination** — vacuum environment eliminates oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen pickup; ideal for reactive metals.
4. **Welding of dissimilar metals** — the localised heat allows joining of metals with very different melting points.

**Two Limitations of EBW:**

1. **Very high capital cost** — EBW machines cost 5–10 times more than conventional arc welding equipment.
2. **Strict vacuum requirement** — large or porous structures (castings) are difficult to weld; cycle time is high due to pump-down.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — Comparison Table

| S.No. | Parameter | EBW | GTAW (TIG) |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Heat source** | Kinetic energy of accelerated electrons | Electric arc between W electrode and workpiece |
| (ii) | **Power density (W/mm²)** | 10⁶ – 10⁹ | 10² – 10³ |
| (iii) | **Depth-to-Width ratio** | 20 : 1 to 50 : 1 | 1 : 1 to 2 : 1 |
| (iv) | **Operating atmosphere** | High vacuum (~10⁻⁴ torr) | Inert gas shield (Argon) at atmospheric pressure |
| (v) | **Single-pass weldable thickness** | Up to 100 mm (steel) | Up to 6 mm |
| (vi) | **Process efficiency (η)** | 0.85 – 0.95 | 0.50 – 0.70 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the efficiency term η** in the heat input formula — examiners deduct **2 marks** because students write $H = VI/v$ without $\eta$.
> 2. **Confusing Power Density with Power** — Power is in Watts, Power Density is in **W/mm²**. This is a classic 2-mark trap.
> 3. **Vacuum level units** — Always express as **torr** or **Pascal (Pa)**, not as a fraction of atmospheric pressure, unless specifically asked. KTU expects the SI/standard unit.
> 4. **In numericals, students forget to convert mA → A and kV → V** before substitution. Always show unit conversion as the **first step**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!TIP]
> **One-page Revision Checklist for the Day Before the KTU Exam:**

- [ ] **EBW = vacuum + high-velocity electrons + magnetic focusing + keyhole welding.**
- [ ] Accelerating Voltage: **30–200 kV**; Beam current: few mA to amps.
- [ ] Power density: **10⁶–10⁹ W/mm²** (highest among all welding processes).
- [ ] Depth-to-width ratio: **20:1 to 50:1** (single pass).
- [ ] Vacuum level (high-vacuum EBW): **10⁻⁴ torr**.
- [ ] Process efficiency: **η ≈ 0.9** (compare to 0.6 for GTAW).
- [ ] Electron velocity at 60 kV ≈ **0.48 c**; at 100 kV ≈ **0.63 c**.
- [ ] **Key formula (memorise):** $v_e = \sqrt{2eV/m_e}$.
- [ ] **Key formula (memorise):** $H = \eta V I / v$ (J/mm).
- [ ] **Key formula (memorise):** $P = V \cdot I$ (W).
- [ ] **Power density:** $\rho_P = 4 P / (\pi d^2)$.
- [ ] Subsystems of EBW machine: **Electron Gun, HV Power Supply, Focusing Coil, Deflection Coil, Vacuum Chamber, Work Manipulator**.
- [ ] **Three types of EBW:** High Vacuum / Partial Vacuum / Non-Vacuum.
- [ ] **Weldable materials:** Reactive metals (Ti, Zr, Nb), refractory metals, dissimilar metal combinations, thick sections.
- [ ] **Limitations:** High cost, vacuum requirement limits size, requires skilled operator, X-ray shielding needed.
- [ ] **Applications:** Aerospace (jet engine blisks, rocket nozzle welds), nuclear (fuel rod end caps), automotive (gears, transmission components), electronics (vacuum-sealed packages).

> **End of Notes — Module 2: Electron Beam Welding | PEPET741 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Background of the Process and Welding Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Background of the Process and Welding Equipment # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** is a solid-state joining process invented and patented by **The Welding Institute (TWI), United Kingdom, in 1991**, where a non-consumable rotating tool with a specially designed pin and shoulder is plunged into the joint line between two workpieces and traversed along the joint. The frictional heat generated between the tool shoulder and the workpiece plasticizes the material without reaching its melting point, allowing the softened material to be mechanically intermixed and forged together, forming a high-integrity solid-state joint. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Friction Stir Welding is the flagship topic of Module 2 of PEPET741. The phrase "Background of the Process" specifically refers to the **historical evolution (1991 to present)**, the **limitation of fusion welding** that motivated its invention, and the **primary metallurgical advantage** of operating in the solid state below the melting point of the parent material. ### Formal Classification Table | Property | Classification | |---|---| | Process Type | Solid-state welding | | Heat Source | Frictional + Plastic deformation heat | | Melting Occurs | **No** (parent material does not melt) | | Joining Mechanism | Hot plastic deformation + Mechanical mixing | | Typical Temperature Range | **0.6 – 0.9 × Tm** (Tm = melting point) | | Filler Metal Required | **No** (Autogenous) | | Shielding Gas Required | **Usually not** (except reactive metals) | | Inventor | Wayne Thomas, TWI, UK, **1991** | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you have two bars of **soft clay** lying side by side on a table. Now take a **hot butter knife** (a heated, blunt tool) and press it down at the seam while moving it back and forth in a circular motion, then push it slowly along the joint. The heat and pressure from the knife will soften the clay on both sides, mix it together, and "stitch" the two pieces into one continuous piece — without ever liquefying the clay into a puddle. That is exactly what Friction Stir Welding does — but with metals such as **aluminium, magnesium, copper, titanium, and steel**. The "butter knife" is the **FSW tool**, the "circular motion" is **tool rotation**, the "slow push" is the **traverse/welding speed**, and the "soft clay" is the **plasticized metal** that flows around the pin and consolidates under the shoulder. ### Why was FSW invented? (The "Background" story) Conventional **fusion welding** (like TIG, MIG, SMAW) melts the parent metal to form a joint. This causes several problems in **soft alloys** such as aluminium and magnesium: 1. **Porosity** — gas bubbles trapped in the weld pool 2. **Solidification cracking** — hot tears in the weld bead 3. **Distortion** — large thermal expansion and contraction 4. **Loss of temper / strength** in heat-treated alloys 5. **Fume and spatter** — environmental and safety concerns FSW solves **all five** of these issues because the metal never melts. The background of the process is therefore the story of how a UK research team, while working on a project for **Norsk Hydro (Norway) to weld aluminium ship panels**, invented a completely new way of joining metals using friction heat instead of an electric arc. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for KTU viva:** *"FSW was born because **f**usion welding **f**ailed on **a**luminium — so TWI found a way to **s**tir, not **s**eal."* ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Inventor:** Wayne Thomas at TWI, Cambridge, UK - **Patent year:** **1991** (UK Patent GB 2 325 891 B) - **First commercial use:** **1996** (Marine applications, by Marine Aluminium, Norway) - **Maximum commercial weld length:** Exceeds **30 m** (e.g., in aerospace fuselage panels) - **Typical rotational speed range:** **100 – 3000 rpm** - **Typical traverse speed range:** **10 – 1000 mm/min** - **Normal downward forging force:** **5 – 50 kN** depending on material thickness # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Why Friction Generates Heat When the FSW tool shoulder rotates and presses against the workpiece surface, two heat-generation mechanisms occur simultaneously: 1. **Sticking friction (pre-heating stage):** Initially, the tool and workpiece surfaces are in full contact, and the relative motion is zero at the interface. The frictional shear stress equals the **shear yield stress (k)** of the workpiece material. Heat is generated at a rate proportional to the product of shear stress, contact area, and sliding velocity. 2. **Sliding friction (steady-state stage):** Once the surface temperature rises sufficiently, a thin layer of plasticized material forms. The tool then slides over this layer. Heat generation drops because the effective friction coefficient decreases. ### 2.2 Analytical Heat-Generation Expression The volumetric heat-generation rate at the tool-workpiece interface can be expressed as: $$q_{gen} = \tau_{yield} \cdot \omega \cdot r \cdot A_{contact}$$ Where the symbols carry the following meaning: - **qgen** — Volumetric heat generation rate (W/m³) - **τyield** — Shear yield strength of workpiece at interface temperature (Pa) - **ω** — Angular velocity of the tool (rad/s) - **r** — Radial distance from the tool axis (m) - **Acontact** — Effective contact area between shoulder and workpiece (m²) The angular velocity is related to the rotational speed **N (rpm)** by: $$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$$ The shear yield strength at elevated temperature is often modelled using a temperature-dependent expression: $$\tau_{yield}(T) = \tau_{0} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{T - T_{room}}{T_{m} - T_{room}} \right)$$ Where: - **τ0** — Shear yield strength at room temperature (Pa) - **T** — Local temperature at the interface (K) - **Troom** — Ambient room temperature (**298 K**) - **Tm** — Melting point of the parent material (K) > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** This equation shows that as the local temperature T approaches the melting point Tm, the shear yield strength drops toward zero, which is why FSW never crosses into the melting regime — process control parameters (ω and traverse speed) are designed to keep the peak temperature at approximately **0.6 to 0.9 × Tm**. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Process Logic The FSW process is logically broken down into four sequential operational stages: 1. **Plunging Stage** — The rotating tool is brought into contact with the workpiece. The shoulder contacts the top surface, and the pin is driven into the joint line. Heat is generated rapidly, and a downward forging force is applied to consolidate the plasticized material. 2. **Dwell (Soaking) Stage** — The tool is held stationary for a few seconds at the plunge position. This allows the temperature to rise to a stable steady-state value and the material beneath the shoulder to reach a uniform plasticized state. 3. **Traverse (Welding) Stage** — The tool is moved along the joint line at a constant traverse speed. Material is picked up on the **advancing side** (where tool rotation direction matches traverse direction), plasticized, swept around the pin, and deposited on the **retreating side**, where it cools and consolidates. 4. **Retraction Stage** — At the end of the weld, the tool is withdrawn. A characteristic **exit-hole (keyhole)** is left at the termination point, which must be cut off and is the most well-known geometric feature of FSW joints. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Symbol | Expression | Description / Units | |---|---|---| | Heat input rate | $P = \tau_{yield} \cdot \omega \cdot r \cdot A$ | Power dissipated at shoulder interface (W) | | Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Convert rpm to rad/s | | Total heat input per unit length | $H = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | J/mm — controls weld quality | | Spindle power | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T_{sp}}{60}$ | Tsp = spindle torque (N·m) | | Forge force (rule of thumb) | $F \approx 3 - 5 \times k \times A_{pin}$ | Empirical estimate (N) | | Peak temperature estimate | $T_{peak} \approx 0.6 - 0.9 \times T_{m}$ | Empirical (K) | | Stir zone width | $W_{SZ} \approx 1.1 \times D_{shoulder}$ | Approximate geometric relation (m) | | Pitch (advance per revolution) | $p = \dfrac{v}{N}$ | mm/rev (controls heat input) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary / process window condition:** For a sound FSW joint, the dimensionless parameter **ω / v** (rotational speed divided by traverse speed, with units of rev/mm) must fall within a material-specific window. For **AA6061-T6 aluminium**, the typical safe window is **ω/v ≈ 5 – 15 rev/mm**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Why FSW is preferred | |---|---| | **Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus)** | Low distortion, no porosity — critical for fuselage skin panels of the **Boeing 787** and **SpaceX Falcon 9** fuel tanks | | **Marine / Shipbuilding** | Long, straight, single-pass welds for aluminium hull plates up to 30 m long | | **Automotive** | Lightweight aluminium and magnesium chassis components, battery trays for EVs | | **Railway** | High-speed train body shells and floor panels | | **Nuclear / Defence** | Joining of reactive metals (zirconium, beryllium) where fusion welding is impossible | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Steady-State Heat Generation under the FSW Tool Shoulder ### Step 1 — Define the differential ring element on the shoulder Consider a small annular ring on the shoulder at radius **r**, with width **dr**. The local area of this ring is: $$dA = 2 \pi r \, dr$$ ### Step 2 — Write the local sliding velocity Because the shoulder rotates at angular velocity **ω**, a point at radius r moves with linear (tangential) speed: $$v_{local} = \omega r$$ ### Step 3 — Write the local heat generation rate (frictional power per unit area) In the **sticking regime**, the local heat flux (power per unit area) is the product of the local shear yield stress and the local sliding velocity: $$dq'' = \tau_{yield} \cdot \omega r$$ ### Step 4 — Integrate over the full shoulder area To get the total frictional power generated under the entire shoulder (from inner radius **Rpin** to outer radius **Rshoulder**), integrate: $$P_{shoulder} = \int_{R_{pin}}^{R_{shoulder}} \tau_{yield} \cdot \omega r \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr$$ ### Step 5 — Carry out the integration Assuming τyield and ω are approximately constant over the shoulder for a simplified analytical model: $$P_{shoulder} = 2 \pi \tau_{yield} \omega \int_{R_{pin}}^{R_{shoulder}} r^{2} \, dr$$ $$P_{shoulder} = 2 \pi \tau_{yield} \omega \left[ \frac{r^{3}}{3} \right]_{R_{pin}}^{R_{shoulder}}$$ $$P_{shoulder} = \frac{2 \pi \tau_{yield} \omega}{3} \left( R_{shoulder}^{3} - R_{pin}^{3} \right)$$ ### Step 6 — Result and physical interpretation $$\boxed{P_{shoulder} = \frac{2 \pi \tau_{yield} \omega}{3} \left( R_{shoulder}^{3} - R_{pin}^{3} \right)}$$ - The cubic dependence on the **shoulder radius Rshoulder** shows why a larger shoulder dramatically increases heat generation — this is why FSW tool designers prefer **large-diameter shoulders**. - The term **Rpin³** is small compared to **Rshoulder³** for typical tool geometries, so the shoulder accounts for **> 80% of total heat input**. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Sample Calculation) > [!NOTE] > **KTU-Style Problem:** A friction stir welding tool has a shoulder diameter of 20 mm and a pin root diameter of 6 mm. The rotational speed is 800 rpm and the shear yield strength of the workpiece at interface temperature is 90 MPa. Calculate the heat generation rate under the shoulder. **Given:** - Dshoulder = 20 mm → Rshoulder = 0.010 m - Dpin = 6 mm → Rpin = 0.003 m - N = 800 rpm - τyield = 90 × 10⁶ Pa **Step 1 — Convert rotational speed to angular velocity:** $$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 800}{60} = \frac{1600 \pi}{60} = \frac{80 \pi}{3} \approx 83.78 \; rad/s$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the derived formula:** $$P_{shoulder} = \frac{2 \pi \times (90 \times 10^{6}) \times 83.78}{3} \left( (0.010)^{3} - (0.003)^{3} \right)$$ **Step 3 — Compute the cubic radius difference:** $$(0.010)^{3} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \; m^{3}$$ $$(0.003)^{3} = 2.7 \times 10^{-8} \; m^{3}$$ $$\Delta R^{3} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} - 2.7 \times 10^{-8} = 9.73 \times 10^{-7} \; m^{3}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the numerical factor:** $$\frac{2 \pi \times 90 \times 10^{6} \times 83.78}{3} = 1.581 \times 10^{10}$$ **Step 5 — Multiply:** $$P_{shoulder} = 1.581 \times 10^{10} \times 9.73 \times 10^{-7}$$ $$\boxed{P_{shoulder} \approx 1.54 \times 10^{4} \; W = 15.4 \; kW}$$ **Interpretation:** The shoulder dissipates ~15.4 kW of frictional power, which is sufficient to plasticize an aluminium joint of moderate thickness in a single pass. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: How the Background of the Process Informs Equipment Selection | Process Need (from "Background" study) | Resulting Equipment Requirement | |---|---| | Solid-state joining → no fusion | No shielding gas supply required for aluminium | | Frictional heat → need high axial force | **Heavy-duty servo-hydraulic Z-axis** (≥ 50 kN capacity) | | Continuous plasticized flow → tool wear | **High-temperature tool material** (tool steel, W-Re, polycrystalline cubic boron nitride) | | Long, straight welds (marine, aerospace) | **Gantry-type CNC machine** with travel ≥ 10 m | | Torsional load on spindle | **High-torque, low-rpm spindle** (vs. high-rpm routers) | | Temperature control for quality | **Tool-embedded thermocouples** + spindle load monitoring | | Reactive metals (Ti, Zr) | **Argon-purged glove-box FSW** variant | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an FSW Machine The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a complete FSW workstation, showing how each subsystem interacts during the process. This block diagram is the standard answer for "explain the welding equipment" questions in KTU exams. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph CTRL[Control Subsystem] CNC[CNC Motion Controller] PLC[PLC and Safety Interlocks] HMI[Operator HMI and Teach Pendant] end subgraph MECH[Mechanical Subsystem] FRAME[Heavy Welded Steel Machine Frame] XAXIS[X-Axis Linear Gantry with Servo Motor] YAXIS[Y-Axis Cross Slide] ZAXIS[Z-Axis Vertical Column with Force Actuator] CLMP[Hydraulic Pneumatic Workpiece Clamping Table] end subgraph SPIN[Spindle and Tooling Subsystem] SPINDLE[High Torque Tilting Spindle 100 to 3000 rpm] TOOLHOLDER[Quick Change Tool Holder] TOOL[FSW Tool Pin plus Shoulder Assembly] BACKING[Backing Plate and Anvil] end subgraph MON[Monitoring Subsystem] TORQUE[Spindle Torque Sensor] AXIALF[Axial Force Load Cell] TIR[Tool Infrared Pyrometer] THLOG[Data Acquisition and Logging PC] end HMI --> CNC PLC --> CNC CNC --> XAXIS CNC --> YAXIS CNC --> ZAXIS ZAXIS --> SPINDLE SPINDLE --> TOOLHOLDER TOOLHOLDER --> TOOL TOOL --> WORKPIECE1[Workpiece 1] TOOL --> WORKPIECE2[Workpiece 2] CLMP --> WORKPIECE1 CLMP --> WORKPIECE2 BACKING --> WORKPIECE1 BACKING --> WORKPIECE2 SPINDLE --> TORQUE ZAXIS --> AXIALF TIR --> TOOL TORQUE --> THLOG AXIALF --> THLOG TIR --> THLOG ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram of the FSW Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Step 1 Plunging
Rotating tool lowered into joint] --> B[Step 2 Dwell
3 to 10 seconds soak] B --> C[Step 3 Traverse
Tool moves along joint line] C --> D[Step 4 Retraction
Tool withdrawn leaves keyhole] D --> E[Finished Solid State Weld] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram of FSW Tool Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD SH[Shoulder Convex or Scroll or Concave] --> PIN[Pin Cylindrical or Tapered or Threaded] PIN --> NECK[Neck Reduced Diameter for Soft Materials] NECK --> SHANK[Shank Held in Tool Holder] SHANK --> DRIVE[Drive Adaptor Connected to Spindle Collet] ``` ## 4.4 Equipment Subsystem Description Table (for narrative answer writing) | Subsystem | Primary Function | Typical Component Specification | |---|---|---| | Machine Frame | Resist bending under high traverse force | Welded steel, ≥ 2 tonnes for aerospace gantries | | Spindle | Provide torque and rotation | 50 – 100 N·m torque, 0 – 3000 rpm, ±5° tilt (for 3D contours) | | Z-axis actuator | Apply and control plunge + forge force | Servo-hydraulic, 50 kN capacity, 0.01 mm positioning accuracy | | Work-holding table | Clamp plates to prevent sliding | Hydraulic clamps, **0.05 mm flatness** across bed | | Backing plate | Support the underside of the weld | Tool-steel insert, water-cooled for thick sections | | Tool | Generate heat and stir the metal | Tool-steel for Al, **W-Re or PCBN for steel**, **tungsten carbide for Mg** | | Control system | Coordinate motion, force, rotation | Fanuc / Siemens CNC with macro programs for plunge-dwell-retract cycles | | Sensors | Monitor process for quality assurance | Load cell, torque transducer, IR pyrometer, displacement LVDT | | Cooling | Protect spindle bearings from heat | Water-cooled spindle housing | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Friction Stir Welding and list any two of its advantages over conventional fusion welding.** **Model Answer:** - **Definition:** Friction Stir Welding (FSW) is a **solid-state joining process** in which a non-consumable rotating tool consisting of a shoulder and a pin is plunged into the joint between two workpieces. Frictional heat generated by the rotating tool plasticizes the material below the melting point, and the tool's traverse along the joint mechanically mixes and forges the softened material to form a metallurgical bond. - **Advantage 1:** No fusion occurs, so defects such as porosity, solidification cracking, and liquation cracking — common in fusion welding of aluminium — are eliminated. - **Advantage 2:** No filler metal, no shielding gas, and no harmful UV/IR arc radiation are required, making the process environmentally cleaner and more energy-efficient. - **Advantage 3 (bonus):** Joint strength is often higher than the parent material in age-hardenable aluminium alloys because the thermo-mechanically affected zone experiences natural artificial ageing effects. ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State the function of the (i) shoulder and (ii) pin of an FSW tool.** **Model Answer:** - **(i) Function of the shoulder:** 1. Generates the **majority of the frictional heat** (typically > 80% of total heat input) because of its large contact area with the workpiece surface. 2. Acts as an **escape seal**, confining the plasticized material beneath it and preventing it from being thrown out (flash). 3. Provides the **downward forging pressure** that consolidates the stirred material into a sound weld. - **(ii) Function of the pin:** 1. Acts as a **mechanical stirrer** that drags the plasticized material from the advancing side to the retreating side around the joint line. 2. Penetrates into the workpiece to the desired depth, ensuring **through-thickness mixing** of the joint interface. 3. Generates a portion of the frictional heat (secondary to the shoulder) along its cylindrical surface. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 internal choice]** **(a)** Explain the **historical background** of the Friction Stir Welding process. Discuss the metallurgical limitations of fusion welding of aluminium alloys that motivated its invention. (7 Marks) **(b)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **principal subsystems of an FSW machine** and explain the role of the spindle, the Z-axis actuator, and the backing plate. (7 Marks) #### Model Solution for (a): 1. **Invention (1991):** Friction Stir Welding was invented by **Mr. Wayne Thomas** and his team at **The Welding Institute (TWI), Cambridge, UK, in 1991**. The original motivation came from a project commissioned by **Norsk Hydro (Norway)** to develop a reliable welding method for **aluminium ship panels**, which suffered from severe porosity and distortion when joined by conventional gas metal arc welding (GMAW). 2. **Patent:** The fundamental patent (GB 2 325 891 B) was filed in 1991 and granted in the mid-1990s. TWI then made FSW widely available to industry through licensing. 3. **Commercial deployment (1996 onwards):** The first commercial use was by **Marine Aluminium** in Norway for ship panels. By the 2000s, FSW was adopted by **Boeing (Delta II rocket and 787 fuselage), NASA, Alstom (railway), Hydro (automotive)**, and others. 4. **Metallurgical limitations of fusion welding of aluminium:** - Aluminium has a high thermal conductivity (≈ 235 W/m·K), so heat is rapidly conducted away, producing wide heat-affected zones. - The solidification range of Al-Mg-Si and Al-Zn-Mg-Cu alloys is wide, making them **highly susceptible to hot cracking**. - Hydrogen is highly soluble in molten aluminium and forms **porosity** upon solidification. - Heat-treated (T6) alloys lose their temper in the HAZ, reducing joint efficiency. - FSW solves all of these issues because the metal remains in the **solid state**. #### Model Solution for (b): Refer to **Section 4.1 (Block Diagram)** for the schematic. The principal subsystems and their roles are: - **Spindle:** Provides the **rotary motion** (100 – 3000 rpm) and a **constant-torque drive** to the FSW tool. It must be robust to handle high lateral bending moments during traverse and must have a **tilt mechanism (±5°)** to allow welding on curved surfaces. - **Z-axis actuator:** This is a **heavy-duty servo-hydraulic or servo-electric actuator** that (i) controls the **plunge depth** of the pin, (ii) applies and maintains the **downward forging force** (typically 5 – 50 kN), and (iii) provides positional feedback via a load cell for process control. - **Backing plate:** Sits directly beneath the joint line and serves three functions: (i) it **supports the upward forging pressure** of the shoulder, preventing the workpiece from deflecting, (ii) it acts as a **heat sink** (often water-cooled for thick sections) to extract excess heat, and (iii) it defines the **root surface finish** of the weld. ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 internal choice]** **(a)** Derive an expression for the **heat generation rate under the FSW tool shoulder**, treating the tool–workpiece interface as a sticking-friction regime. (7 Marks) **(b)** A friction stir welding tool has a shoulder diameter of 25 mm and a pin root diameter of 8 mm. The tool rotates at 1200 rpm while the shear yield strength of the workpiece at the interface is 70 MPa. Estimate the **heat generation rate** at the shoulder. State any assumptions clearly. (7 Marks) #### Model Solution for (a): The complete derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes.
