The result is: $$P_{shoulder} = \frac{2 \pi \tau_{yield} \omega}{3} \left( R_{shoulder}^{3} - R_{pin}^{3} \right)$$ #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** - Dshoulder = 25 mm → Rshoulder = 0.0125 m - Dpin = 8 mm → Rpin = 0.0040 m - N = 1200 rpm - τyield = 70 × 10⁶ Pa **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state, sticking-friction regime at the shoulder-workpiece interface. 2. τyield and ω are radially constant over the shoulder. 3. The pin's contribution to heat generation is neglected; only the shoulder is considered. **Step 1:** Angular velocity: $$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 1200}{60} = 40 \pi \approx 125.66 \; rad/s$$ **Step 2:** Cubic radius difference: $$R_{shoulder}^{3} = (0.0125)^{3} = 1.953 \times 10^{-6} \; m^{3}$$ $$R_{pin}^{3} = (0.0040)^{3} = 6.4 \times 10^{-8} \; m^{3}$$ $$\Delta R^{3} = 1.953 \times 10^{-6} - 6.4 \times 10^{-8} = 1.889 \times 10^{-6} \; m^{3}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute: $$P_{shoulder} = \frac{2 \pi \times 70 \times 10^{6} \times 125.66}{3} \times 1.889 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Step 4:** Compute: $$P_{shoulder} = 1.842 \times 10^{10} \times 1.889 \times 10^{-6} \approx 3.48 \times 10^{4} \; W$$ $$\boxed{P_{shoulder} \approx 34.8 \; kW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common error #1 (Unit mismatch):** Students frequently forget to convert **rpm to rad/s** using ω = 2πN/60. This alone causes a **2-mark deduction** in the final answer. > 2. **Common error #2 (Pin radius not subtracted):** Several students erroneously use only Rshoulder³ without subtracting Rpin³. The pin occupies the central region, and the shoulder does not contact the workpiece there. > 3. **Common error #3 (Missing assumption statement):** In a 7-mark derivation/numerical problem, **not listing the assumptions** costs 1 mark, even if the numerical answer is correct. > 4. **Common error #4 (Ignoring the cubic dependence):** Students sometimes write heat input proportional to R (linear) or R² instead of R³. Examiners specifically look for the correct **R³ dependence** as it is the most important conceptual outcome of the derivation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-revision checklist (must memorize for KTU exams):** - **Process type:** Solid-state welding; no melting; no filler; no shielding gas (for Al). - **Inventor:** Wayne Thomas, **TWI, UK, 1991**. - **Tool components:** **Shoulder** (heat + forge + seal) and **Pin** (stir + heat). - **Tool materials:** Tool-steel for aluminium, **W-Re alloys or polycrystalline CBN** for steel, tungsten carbide for magnesium. - **Key process parameters:** Rotational speed **N (rpm)**, traverse speed **v (mm/min)**, tilt angle (typically **2° – 3°**), plunge depth, and dwell time. - **Process zones in a typical FSW joint cross-section:** **Stir zone (SZ / nugget), Thermo-mechanically affected zone (TMAZ), Heat-affected zone (HAZ), and unaffected base metal**. - **Asymmetric features:** **Advancing side** (rotation and traverse in the same direction) has sharper boundary; **retreating side** has a more diffuse boundary. - **Characteristic defect:** **Exit keyhole** at the end of the weld — a hallmark of FSW. - **Heat generation formula:** $P_{shoulder} = \dfrac{2 \pi \tau_{yield} \omega}{3}\left( R_{shoulder}^{3} - R_{pin}^{3} \right)$ - **Peak temperature:** $T_{peak} \approx 0.6 - 0.9 \times T_{m}$ - **Angular velocity conversion:** $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ - **Process window rule:** $\omega / v \approx 5 - 15 \; rev/mm$ for AA6061-T6. - **Major industrial users:** Boeing, Airbus, SpaceX, Alstom, Hydro, Marine Aluminium. - **Limitations of FSW:** Difficulty with **hard materials at room temperature** (steel > 6 mm thick needs special tools and preheating), **exit-keyhole defect**, **welding speed generally lower than fusion welding** for thick sections, and **strong clamping/backing requirements** for thin sheets. - **Common variants:** **FSW**, **Friction Stir Spot Welding (FSSW)**, **Friction Stir Processing (FSP)**, **Self-Reacting FSW (SR-FSW)**, **Bobbin Tool FSW (BT-FSW)** — all are part of the "friction stir family" frequently tested in PEPET741. - **Significance of the "Background":** The historical motivation (fusion welding's failure on aluminium) is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**, so always remember to mention **Norsk Hydro, TWI, 1991, and the year 1996 for first commercial use.**

topics.title: Types of Lasers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Lasers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **laser** (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation) is a device that emits a highly coherent, monochromatic, and collimated beam of electromagnetic radiation through the process of *optical amplification* based on the stimulated emission of photons. In the context of advanced welding and metal joining, a **laser beam** functions as a concentrated, high-energy-density heat source capable of producing keyhole-mode or conduction-mode welds in metallic and non-metallic workpieces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Lasers used in materials processing are classified primarily by the **active lasing medium** (gas, solid-state, fiber, semiconductor) and by their characteristic **wavelength (λ)**, which determines the beam–material interaction (absorption and reflectivity) in the joint zone."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a stadium full of people clapping randomly — that is **ordinary light** (incoherent, multi-directional, many wavelengths). Now imagine a military marching band where every drummer strikes the drum at the **exact same time, in the exact same direction, at the exact same pitch** — that is **laser light**. Every photon marches in step, producing an extraordinarily focused, intense, and powerful beam that can melt even the toughest metals within a fraction of a second.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in Welding:**
> The intensity of a laser beam at the focal point can exceed **10⁶ W/mm²**, which is what enables **deep penetration welding** (keyhole mode) — a single pass that can join thick plates without the need for beveling or multi-pass techniques used in conventional arc welding.

### 1.3 Primary Classification of Lasers (KTU Framework)

Industrial welding lasers are broadly classified into **four families**:

| Family | Active Medium | Typical λ | Beam Delivery |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gas Lasers** | CO₂, He–Ne, Argon | 10.6 µm (CO₂) | Mirror-based (rigid) |
| **Solid-State Lasers** | Nd:YAG, Nd:YVO₄, Ruby | 1.064 µm | Fiber or mirror |
| **Fiber Lasers** | Yb-doped silica fiber | 1.07 µm | Flexible fiber (10–30 m) |
| **Semiconductor / Diode Lasers** | GaAs, InGaAs | 0.8–1.0 µm | Fiber or direct |

### 1.4 Key Beam Quality Metric

The suitability of a laser for precision welding is quantified by the **Beam Parameter Product (BPP)**, given in **mm·mrad**. A lower BPP means a better focusability. The formula is:

$$\text{BPP} = w_0 \cdot \theta$$

where $w_0$ is the beam waist radius (in **mm**) and $\theta$ is the half-divergence angle (in **mrad**). For high-power welding, a BPP of **< 8 mm·mrad** is considered excellent.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Constant to Remember:**
> Speed of light in vacuum, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; used to relate wavelength $\lambda$ and frequency $\nu$ through $c = \lambda \nu$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Study of Each Laser Type

#### 2.1.1 CO₂ Laser (Gas Laser)
- **Active Medium:** A mixture of CO₂, N₂, and He gases.
- **Pumping Mechanism:** Electrical discharge excites N₂ molecules, which transfer energy to CO₂ via resonant collision.
- **Wavelength:** **10.6 µm** (far-infrared region).
- **Power Range:** From a few watts up to **45 kW** (in industrial systems).
- **Beam Delivery:** Mirrors and rigid articulated arms (the beam is invisible and highly absorbed by standard optical fibers made of silica).
- **Key Welding Feature:** High reflectivity on most metals (≈ 90% at room temperature for Al, Cu) — requires a surface absorption-enhancing coating (e.g., graphite spray) for non-ferrous metals.

#### 2.1.2 Nd:YAG Laser (Solid-State Laser)
- **Active Medium:** Neodymium-doped Yttrium Aluminum Garnet (Y₃Al₅O₁₂) crystal.
- **Wavelength:** **1.064 µm** (near-infrared).
- **Power Range:** Up to **6 kW** in continuous wave (CW) mode.
- **Beam Delivery:** Can be coupled into a fiber (limited to ~600 W through a 600 µm fiber per fiber in older systems; modern systems can deliver multi-kW through bundled fibers).
- **Key Welding Feature:** Shorter wavelength gives **better absorption** in metals compared to CO₂. Especially useful for welding **aluminum, copper, and dissimilar metals**.

#### 2.1.3 Fiber Laser
- **Active Medium:** Ytterbium (Yb)-doped double-clad silica fiber.
- **Wavelength:** **1.07 µm**.
- **Power Range:** From 50 W up to **100 kW** (the highest CW power of any industrial laser).
- **Beam Quality:** Very high (BPP ≈ 0.5–4 mm·mrad).
- **Beam Delivery:** Directly through the same flexible fiber that also pumps the gain medium — extremely robust and energy-efficient (**wall-plug efficiency ≈ 30–40%**).
- **Key Welding Feature:** The current industry workhorse for **automotive, shipbuilding, and aerospace** welding.

#### 2.1.4 Disc (Disk) Laser
- **Active Medium:** Yb:YAG thin disc, mounted on a heat sink.
- **Wavelength:** **1.03 µm**.
- **Power Range:** Up to **16 kW** per disc; scalable by multiplexing.
- **Key Welding Feature:** Excellent beam quality at high power, low thermal lensing, high efficiency.

#### 2.1.5 Diode Laser (Direct Diode)
- **Active Medium:** Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) semiconductor p–n junction.
- **Wavelength:** **0.8–0.98 µm** (near-infrared).
- **Power Range:** Up to **15 kW** (with beam combining).
- **Key Welding Feature:** Highest electrical-to-optical efficiency (≈ 50–60%), but lower beam quality.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities are in **SI units** unless stated otherwise.

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = h \nu = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy (Planck–Einstein) | J (eV if $h$ in eV·s) |
| 2 | $I = \dfrac{P}{A_{focus}}$ | Power density at focal spot | W/m² or W/mm² |
| 3 | $\text{BPP} = w_0 \cdot \theta$ | Beam Parameter Product | mm·mrad |
| 4 | $A = 1 - R$ | Absorptivity = 1 − Reflectivity | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta_{abs} = 1 - \exp(-\alpha \ell)$ | Beer–Lambert absorption in depth $\ell$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $d_{focus} = \dfrac{4 \lambda f}{\pi D}$ | Focused spot diameter (Gaussian) | m |
| 7 | $P_{th} = A_{21} \cdot \dfrac{h \nu \cdot N_2}{V}$ | Threshold pump power (Einstein relation) | W |
| 8 | $\text{Stability} \Rightarrow 0 < g_1 g_2 < 1$ | Resonator stability condition | dimensionless |

Where:
- $h$ = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- $c$ = speed of light = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $w_0$ = beam waist radius (mm); $\theta$ = half-divergence (mrad)
- $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient (m⁻¹); $\ell$ = depth in the material (m)
- $f$ = focal length of lens; $D$ = raw beam diameter
- $g_i = 1 - \dfrac{L}{R_i}$ resonator geometry parameters

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Remote laser welding of tailored blanks (fiber + scanner heads), and battery cell tab welding (diode/fiber).
- **Shipbuilding:** Thick-plate (≥ 10 mm) single-pass keyhole welding using **CO₂ or fiber lasers** at 20–50 kW.
- **Aerospace:** Ti-alloy and Inconel welding using **disk and fiber lasers** in vacuum or inert chambers.
- **Electronics:** Diode lasers for hermetic sealing of micro-electronic packages.
- **Medical devices:** Nd:YAG pulsed lasers for fine spot welding of stents and catheters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Absorbed Power Density for a Focused Laser Beam

The single most important equation in laser-welding design is the **power density** delivered at the workpiece. We derive it rigorously.

**Step 1 — Definition of Power Density.**
Power density $I$ is the average optical power per unit area at the focal plane:

$$
I \;=\; \frac{P_{laser}}{A_{focus}}
$$

**Step 2 — Geometry of the Focused Spot.**
A diffraction-limited Gaussian TEM₀₀ beam, focused by a lens of focal length $f$, produces a circular spot. The spot diameter is given by the standard Gaussian optics formula:

$$
d_{focus} \;=\; \frac{4 \, \lambda \, f}{\pi \, D}
$$

where $D$ is the diameter of the incident collimated beam on the lens, $\lambda$ is the laser wavelength, and $f$ is the focal length.

**Step 3 — Compute the Spot Area.**
Assuming a circular focal spot of diameter $d_{focus}$, the area is:

$$
A_{focus} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \, d_{focus}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Spot Diameter.**

$$
A_{focus} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \left( \frac{4 \lambda f}{\pi D} \right)^{2} \;=\; \frac{4 \lambda^{2} f^{2}}{\pi D^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Write the Power Density Expression.**

$$
I \;=\; \frac{P_{laser}}{A_{focus}} \;=\; \frac{\pi \, D^{2} \, P_{laser}}{4 \, \lambda^{2} \, f^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Absorbed (Effective) Power Density.**
The workpiece only absorbs a fraction $A$ of the incident light. The *effective* power density that actually heats the metal is:

$$
I_{abs} \;=\; A \cdot I \;=\; \frac{\pi \, A \, D^{2} \, P_{laser}}{4 \, \lambda^{2} \, f^{2}}
$$

This equation immediately explains the design rule of thumb in laser welding: **shorter wavelength lasers (smaller λ) give higher absorbed intensity for the same optical setup**, which is why fiber and disc lasers dominate aluminum and copper welding over CO₂.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A 2 kW Nd:YAG laser ($\lambda = 1.064 \mu m$, $A = 0.30$ on stainless steel) is focused through a 100 mm focal-length lens with an incident beam diameter of 10 mm. Calculate (a) the focused spot diameter, (b) the incident power density, and (c) the absorbed power density.

**Solution (a) — Spot diameter:**

$$
d_{focus} \;=\; \frac{4 \cdot (1.064 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (100 \times 10^{-3})}{\pi \cdot (10 \times 10^{-3})}
$$

$$
d_{focus} \;=\; \frac{4.256 \times 10^{-7}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-2}} \;=\; 1.354 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \;=\; 13.54 \text{ μm}
$$

**Solution (b) — Incident power density:**

$$
I \;=\; \frac{2000}{\frac{\pi}{4}(13.54 \times 10^{-6})^{2}} \;=\; \frac{2000}{1.440 \times 10^{-10}} \;\approx\; 1.39 \times 10^{13} \text{ W/m}^{2}
$$

Equivalently, $\approx 1.39 \times 10^{7}$ **W/mm²** — well into the keyhole regime (keyhole threshold is roughly $10^6$ W/mm²).

**Solution (c) — Absorbed power density:**

$$
I_{abs} \;=\; 0.30 \times 1.39 \times 10^{13} \;=\; 4.17 \times 10^{12} \text{ W/m}^{2}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting a Laser for a Welding Task

| Application | Recommended Laser | Wavelength | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thick plate steel (≥ 6 mm) | **CO₂ or High-power Fiber** | 10.6 / 1.07 µm | Highest available CW power (kW–tens of kW) |
| Aluminum & Copper (high reflectivity) | **Fiber / Disc / Diode** | 1.0–1.07 µm | High absorption at short λ |
| Deep penetration single-pass | **Fiber (up to 100 kW)** | 1.07 µm | Excellent BPP + power density |
| Micro-welding (electronics) | **Pulsed Nd:YAG / Diode** | 1.064 / 0.9 µm | Precise low-energy pulses |
| Aerospace Ti-alloy | **Disk / Fiber** | 1.03 / 1.07 µm | Beam quality + low HAZ |
| Remote / Scanner welding | **Fiber (low BPP)** | 1.07 µm | Fiber delivery + small focus |
| Polymer / non-metal joining | **CO₂** | 10.6 µm | Strong absorption in polymers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Threshold for Mode of Welding:**
> - Conduction mode: $I_{abs} \lesssim 10^6$ W/mm²
> - **Keyhole mode:** $I_{abs} \gtrsim 10^6$ W/mm² (deep, narrow, high depth-to-width)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Industrial Laser System Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodePS[Power Supply Unit] --> nodeDRV[Diode Pump Driver]
    nodeDRV --> nodeMED[Active Lasing Medium]
    nodeMED --> nodeRES[Optical Resonator Mirrors]
    nodeRES --> nodeCOUPL[Coupling Optics]
    nodeCOUPL --> nodeFIB[Fiber or Articulated Arm]
    nodeFIB --> nodeFOC[Focusing Head]
    nodeFOC --> nodeWP[Workpiece / Weld Joint]
    nodeWP --> nodeFEED[Wire Feeder Optional]
    nodeWP --> nodeGAS[Shielding Gas Nozzle]
    nodeGAS --> nodeCTRL[Process Controller and Sensors]
    nodeCTRL --> nodePS
    nodeCTRL --> nodeFOC
```

### 4.2 Classification Flow of Welding Lasers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeROOT[Industrial Welding Lasers]
    nodeROOT --> nodeGAS[Gas Lasers]
    nodeROOT --> nodeSOLID[Solid State Lasers]
    nodeROOT --> nodeFIBER[Fiber Lasers]
    nodeROOT --> nodeSEMI[Semiconductor Diode Lasers]

    nodeGAS --> nodeCO2[CO2 Laser at 10.6 micrometer]
    nodeSOLID --> nodeND[Nd YAG at 1.064 micrometer]
    nodeSOLID --> nodeDISC[Disc Yb YAG at 1.03 micrometer]
    nodeFIBER --> nodeYB[Yb Doped Fiber at 1.07 micrometer]
    nodeSEMI --> nodeDIODE[GaAs Diode at 0.8 to 0.98 micrometer]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Laser Beam Interaction with Metal

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeINC[Incident Laser Beam]
    nodeINC --> nodeREF[Reflected Fraction R]
    nodeINC --> nodeABS[Absorbed Fraction A in Metal]
    nodeABS --> nodeCOND{Absorbed Intensity Iabs}
    nodeCOND --> nodeLOW[Iabs less than 1e6 W per mm squared]
    nodeCOND --> nodeHIGH[Iabs greater than 1e6 W per mm squared]
    nodeLOW --> nodeCM[Conduction Mode Welding]
    nodeHIGH --> nodeKH[Keyhole Mode Welding]
    nodeCM --> nodeWIDE[Wide Shallow Weld Bead]
    nodeKH --> nodeDEEP[Deep Narrow Weld Bead]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Parameter Dashboard (Engineering Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeC1[CO2 Laser] --> nodeP1[Power 0.5 to 45 kW]
    nodeC1 --> nodeW1[Wavelength 10.6 micrometer]
    nodeC1 --> nodeB1[Delivery Mirror only]
    nodeC1 --> nodeE1[Wall Plug 10 percent]

    nodeC2[Nd YAG] --> nodeP2[Power 0.05 to 6 kW]
    nodeC2 --> nodeW2[Wavelength 1.064 micrometer]
    nodeC2 --> nodeB2[Delivery Fiber up to 600 W]
    nodeC2 --> nodeE2[Wall Plug 3 to 5 percent]

    nodeC3[Fiber Laser] --> nodeP3[Power 0.05 to 100 kW]
    nodeC3 --> nodeW3[Wavelength 1.07 micrometer]
    nodeC3 --> nodeB3[Delivery Fiber to 30 m]
    nodeC3 --> nodeE3[Wall Plug 30 to 40 percent]

    nodeC4[Diode Laser] --> nodeP4[Power 0.05 to 15 kW]
    nodeC4 --> nodeW4[Wavelength 0.8 to 0.98 micrometer]
    nodeC4 --> nodeB4[Delivery Fiber or direct]
    nodeC4 --> nodeE4[Wall Plug 50 to 60 percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above satisfy the KTU requirement of mapping the system architecture and process flow without attempting to draw the physical shape of the laser cavity or the molten pool, which is best illustrated in the classroom.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*List any three industrial laser types used in metal joining and state their characteristic emission wavelengths.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **CO₂ laser** — wavelength **10.6 µm** (far-infrared).
2. **Nd:YAG laser** — wavelength **1.064 µm** (near-infrared).
3. **Fiber laser (Yb-doped)** — wavelength **1.07 µm** (near-infrared).
*(Each correct laser with its wavelength = 1 mark, total 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**
*Define the term "Beam Parameter Product" (BPP). Why is a low BPP preferred in welding?*

**Model Answer:**
- The Beam Parameter Product is defined as $BPP = w_0 \cdot \theta$, where $w_0$ is the beam waist radius (mm) and $\theta$ is the half-divergence angle (mrad). Its unit is **mm·mrad**. *(1 mark)*
- It is a conserved quantity (étendue) of a laser beam and is **invariant under perfect optics**. *(1 mark)*
- A **lower BPP** means the beam can be focused to a **smaller spot** for the same divergence, producing a higher power density at the workpiece — necessary for **keyhole-mode deep penetration welding**. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *Lasers in Welding: Classification and Performance*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified Past Pattern]**

**(a)** Classify the major industrial laser types used in welding. Discuss the **operating principle, active medium, and typical wavelength** of CO₂, Nd:YAG, and fiber lasers. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the above three laser systems with respect to (i) beam delivery method, (ii) wall-plug efficiency, (iii) suitability for aluminum and copper welding, and (iv) maximum commercial CW power. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

| Laser Type | Active Medium | Pumping | Wavelength | Operating Principle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | CO₂ + N₂ + He gas mix | Electrical discharge | **10.6 µm** | N₂ transfers energy to CO₂ molecules by resonant collision; population inversion in CO₂ vibrational levels produces lasing at 10.6 µm. |
| Nd:YAG | Nd³⁺ doped YAG crystal | Flash lamp or diode | **1.064 µm** | Nd³⁺ ions are excited to a metastable state; spontaneous and stimulated emission at 1.064 µm is amplified in an optical cavity. |
| Fiber | Yb³⁺ doped silica fiber | Injected laser diode (915/975 nm) | **1.07 µm** | Pump light propagates in the cladding; Yb³⁺ ions in the core emit at 1.07 µm and the fiber itself acts as the gain medium. |

*(Award 1 mark for correct classification; 1 mark per laser for active medium, wavelength, and principle — totaling 7 marks.)*

**Model Solution to (b):**

| Parameter | CO₂ | Nd:YAG | Fiber |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Beam Delivery | Mirrors, rigid arm | Fiber (limited) or mirror | Flexible fiber, up to 30 m |
| (ii) Wall-Plug Efficiency | ~ 10 % | ~ 3–5 % | ~ 30–40 % |
| (iii) Al / Cu Welding | Poor (low absorption) | Good | Excellent |
| (iv) Max CW Power | 45 kW | 6 kW | 100 kW |

*(Award 1.75 marks per row × 4 = 7 marks. If a student presents a paragraph instead of a table, accept equivalent technical content.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Quantitative Laser Welding Design*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified Past Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **focal spot diameter** of a Gaussian laser beam focused by a thin lens. Use this to derive the **power density** at the workpiece. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 4 kW fiber laser ($\lambda = 1.07 \mu m$, absorptivity on steel $A = 0.35$) is focused through a 150 mm lens. The raw beam diameter at the lens is 20 mm. Calculate (i) the focal spot diameter, (ii) the incident power density, and (iii) the absorbed power density. State the welding mode (conduction or keyhole). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

The minimum spot size for a diffraction-limited TEM₀₀ Gaussian beam of waist $w_0$ focused by a lens of focal length $f$ is:

$$
2 w_f \;=\; \frac{4 \lambda f}{\pi D}
$$

where $D$ is the diameter of the collimated beam incident on the lens. The factor 4 comes from the $1/e^2$ intensity definition and the standard Gaussian beam formulas. *(3 marks for stating and explaining the formula)*

The focal spot area for a circular spot:

$$
A_{focus} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \left( \frac{4 \lambda f}{\pi D} \right)^{2} \;=\; \frac{4 \lambda^{2} f^{2}}{\pi D^{2}}
$$

The incident power density is power divided by spot area:

$$
I \;=\; \frac{P}{A_{focus}} \;=\; \frac{\pi D^{2} P}{4 \lambda^{2} f^{2}}
$$

*(4 marks for the derivation. Award 1 mark extra for clearly identifying each step.)*

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Focal spot diameter:**

$$
d_{focus} \;=\; \frac{4 \cdot (1.07 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (0.150)}{\pi \cdot (0.020)} \;=\; \frac{6.42 \times 10^{-7}}{6.283 \times 10^{-2}} \;\approx\; 1.022 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \;\approx\; 10.2 \text{ μm}
$$

**(ii) Incident power density:**

$$
I \;=\; \frac{4000}{\frac{\pi}{4}(1.022 \times 10^{-5})^{2}} \;\approx\; \frac{4000}{8.20 \times 10^{-11}} \;\approx\; 4.88 \times 10^{13} \text{ W/m}^{2}
$$

**(iii) Absorbed power density:**

$$
I_{abs} \;=\; 0.35 \times 4.88 \times 10^{13} \;\approx\; 1.71 \times 10^{13} \text{ W/m}^{2} \;\approx\; 1.71 \times 10^{7} \text{ W/mm}^{2}
$$

**Welding mode:** Since $I_{abs} \approx 1.71 \times 10^7$ W/mm² ≫ $10^6$ W/mm² (the keyhole threshold), the welding will be in the **keyhole mode**, producing a deep, narrow penetration weld. *(1.5 + 1.5 + 2 + 2 = 7 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Many students forget to convert mm → m and µm → m, leading to spot diameters off by a factor of 10³. Always write units at every step.
> 2. **Confusing the threshold:** Conduction mode threshold is *much* lower than keyhole. Stating the threshold as $10^3$ W/mm² (instead of $10^6$ W/mm²) is a frequent error.
> 3. **Wrong formula for spot area:** Using $A = \pi r^2$ with $r$ being the spot *radius* but plugging the *diameter* in the denominator is a recurring mistake. Use $A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$ if $d$ is the diameter.
> 4. **Omitting absorptivity:** The absorptivity $A$ must be multiplied with incident $I$ to get absorbed $I_{abs}$. The examiner awards a separate mark for this step.
> 5. **Misstating BPP definition:** BPP is a *product*, not a ratio. Writing "BPP = θ / w₀" costs 0.5 mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **laser** is characterized by **monochromaticity, coherence, and directionality** — produced by *stimulated emission* in a *population-inverted* medium.
- Industrial welding lasers are classified as **Gas (CO₂), Solid-State (Nd:YAG, Disc), Fiber (Yb-doped), and Semiconductor (Diode)**.
- **Wavelength** is the single most important parameter governing metal absorption:
  - CO₂: **10.6 µm** → highly reflected by Al/Cu, requires surface treatment.
  - Nd:YAG / Fiber / Disc: **1.0–1.07 µm** → 3–5× better absorption on non-ferrous metals.
- **Beam Parameter Product (BPP)** quantifies focusability; **lower BPP = smaller spot = higher intensity**.
- **Power density** dictates the welding mode:
  - $< 10^6$ W/mm² → **Conduction** mode (shallow, wide).
  - $> 10^6$ W/mm² → **Keyhole** mode (deep, narrow).
- **Wall-plug efficiency** ranking: **Diode > Fiber > Disc > CO₂ > Nd:YAG (lamp-pumped)**.
- **Fiber lasers** are the modern industry workhorse due to high power, high efficiency, flexible fiber delivery, and excellent beam quality.
- The focused spot diameter of a Gaussian beam: $d = \dfrac{4 \lambda f}{\pi D}$.
- The absorbed power density: $I_{abs} = \dfrac{\pi \, A \, D^{2} \, P}{4 \, \lambda^{2} \, f^{2}}$.
- For **aluminum and copper welding**, prefer short-λ lasers (fiber, disc, or diode) and consider surface absorption enhancers if using CO₂.
- The threshold for **deep penetration keyhole welding** is conventionally taken as $I \approx 10^6$ W/mm².

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory hook for the exam:**
> *"CO₂ goes far (10.6 µm), Nd:YAG and Fiber go near (1.0–1.07 µm); shorter wavelength = better absorption = better for shiny metals."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of advanced welding and metal joining techniques, **process parameters** are the independently controllable input variables of a welding process whose magnitudes directly govern the energy delivery, thermal cycle, metal transfer mode, weld pool dynamics, and ultimately the metallurgical and mechanical integrity of the produced joint. They constitute the *primary control levers* between the operator/machine settings and the resultant weld bead geometry and quality.

As per KTU 2024 scheme syllabus, process parameters are classified into **Primary (Independent) Parameters**, **Secondary (Dependent) Parameters**, and **Tertiary (Resultant) Parameters**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Process parameters are the variables that must be set on the welding equipment to achieve a specific weld. They are the *cause* parameters, while weld bead geometry and mechanical properties are the *effect* (output) parameters.

### 1.2 Classification of Process Parameters

| Parameter Type | Definition | Examples |
|----------------|------------|----------|
| **Primary / Independent** | Set directly by the operator on the machine | Welding current, Arc voltage, Welding speed, Electrode feed rate |
| **Secondary / Dependent** | Adjust based on primary parameters to maintain stability | Shielding gas flow rate, Inductance, Pulse frequency |
| **Tertiary / Resultant** | Outputs that arise from the combination of primary parameters | Heat input, Weld bead width, Depth of penetration, Dilution, HAZ width |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine you are cooking **dosa** on a tawa (a traditional Indian analogy):
- The **flame intensity (heat source)** is your *welding current*
- The **distance between the ladle and tawa (arc gap)** is your *arc voltage*
- The **speed at which you spread the batter (travel speed)** is your *welding speed*
- The **amount of batter poured (filler deposition)** is your *wire feed rate*

If you keep the tawa too long at one spot, it burns (excessive heat input → burn-through). If you move too fast, the batter doesn't cook properly (lack of fusion). The *recipe* that gives the perfect crispy golden dosa is exactly analogous to the *parameter window* that gives a sound weld.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a master dosa-maker intuitively knows the parameter window, a skilled welding engineer knows how to balance these independent variables to produce a defect-free weld.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Heat Input (HI)** unit: **Joules/mm** or **kJ/mm**
- **Welding current (I)**: measured in **Amperes (A)**
- **Arc voltage (V)**: measured in **Volts (V)**
- **Welding speed (v)**: measured in **mm/min** or **mm/s**
- **Thermal efficiency (η)**: dimensionless, typically **0.6 to 0.85** for arc welding processes
- **Specific energy consumption**: **kWh/kg** of deposited metal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that **Heat Input** is the *single most critical resultant parameter* because it directly influences the Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) microstructure, grain growth, residual stresses, and distortion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Primary Process Parameters — The Big Four

#### 2.1.1 Welding Current (I)
- **Definition:** The magnitude of electric current flowing through the electrode and the workpiece.
- **Range:** Typically **50 A to 500 A** for conventional arc welding processes.
- **Effect on Weld:**
  - Increases → Higher deposition rate, deeper penetration, wider bead
  - Decreases → Shallow penetration, narrower bead, risk of lack of fusion
- **Control mechanism:** Varies the heat generated at the arc: $Q = I^2 R t$

#### 2.1.2 Arc Voltage (V)
- **Definition:** The potential difference maintained between the electrode tip and the workpiece (arc length).
- **Range:** Typically **18 V to 35 V** for GMAW/GTAW.
- **Effect on Weld:**
  - Increases → Wider bead, flatter profile, more dilution
  - Decreases → Taller bead (more convex), narrower profile, risk of short-circuiting
- **Control mechanism:** Directly sets the arc length when using Constant Voltage (CV) power sources.

#### 2.1.3 Welding Speed / Travel Speed (v)
- **Definition:** The linear velocity at which the welding torch travels along the joint.
- **Range:** Typically **100 mm/min to 1000 mm/min** depending on process.
- **Effect on Weld:**
  - Increases → Less heat input, shallower penetration, narrower bead, possible undercut
  - Decreases → Excess heat input, wider HAZ, possible burn-through, distortion

#### 2.1.4 Electrode / Wire Feed Rate (WFR)
- **Definition:** The linear rate at which the consumable electrode is fed into the arc.
- **Range:** Typically **2 m/min to 15 m/min** for GMAW.
- **Effect on Weld:** Controls deposition rate; coupled with voltage, determines current in self-regulated CV systems.

> [!NOTE]
> In **synergic (synergic pulsed) MIG/MAG** welding (a KTU 2024 advanced topic), the wire feed rate is the *master* parameter — voltage and current are automatically adjusted by the machine once WFR is set.

### 2.2 Secondary Process Parameters

| Parameter | Process | Function |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| Shielding Gas Flow Rate | GMAW, GTAW, PAW | Protects molten pool from atmospheric contamination (typically **10–20 L/min**) |
| Inductance (in waveform control) | GMAW short-circuit transfer | Controls rate of current rise → affects spatter and bead shape |
| Pulse Frequency | Pulsed GMAW, PAW | Refines droplet detachment; typical range **50–500 Hz** |
| Polarity (DCEN/DCEP) | All arc processes | Affects penetration (DCEP = deep penetration in GMAW) |
| Electrode Extension (Stick-out) | GMAW | Influences preheating of wire → affects current |
| Torch Angle | All processes | Affects bead shape and penetration asymmetry |

### 2.3 Heat Input — The Master Resultant Parameter

Heat Input (HI) is the **amount of thermal energy supplied per unit length of weld**. It is the mathematical combination of all primary parameters.

**Heat Input Formula (KTU Board Standard):**

$$
\text{HI} = \frac{\eta \times V \times I \times 60}{v \times 1000} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}} \right]
$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = thermal efficiency of the process (dimensionless)
- $V$ = arc voltage in Volts
- $I$ = welding current in Amperes
- $v$ = travel speed in mm/min
- 60 and 1000 are unit conversion factors (seconds and joules)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Efficiency (η) Values for KTU 2024 Reference:**
> - Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW): **0.70 – 0.85**
> - Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG): **0.70 – 0.80**
> - Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG): **0.40 – 0.60**
> - Submerged Arc Welding (SAW): **0.85 – 0.99**
> - Plasma Arc Welding (PAW): **0.60 – 0.80**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Equation | Units | Application |
|--------------|----------|-------|-------------|
| Heat Input | $HI = \frac{\eta V I}{v} \times 0.06$ | kJ/mm | General arc welding |
| Heat Input (kJ/in) | $HI = \frac{\eta V I \times 60}{v}$ (v in in/min) | kJ/inch | US-coded problems |
| Deposition Rate | $DR = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \times WFR \times \rho$ | kg/h | Wire consumption |
| Penetration Ratio | $PR = \frac{\text{Penetration Depth}}{\text{Bead Width}}$ | dimensionless | Quality check |
| Dilution | $D = \frac{A_{BM}}{A_{BM} + A_{FM}} \times 100$ | % | Cladding/surfacing |
| Volumetric Heat Generation | $Q = V I$ | Watts (J/s) | Power calculation |
| HAZ Cooling Time (t8/5) | $t_{8/5} = \frac{Q}{2\pi \lambda c_p \rho} \left[ \frac{1}{700 - T_0} - \frac{1}{800 - T_0}\right]$ | seconds | CCT diagram use |
| Bead Area | $A = \frac{1}{2}(w \times h)$ | mm² | Geometry (triangular approx.) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Trap:** Students often forget to convert **mm/min to mm/s** (divide by 60) and **W to kW** (divide by 1000). The combined conversion factor is **0.06**. Always show unit cancellation in your answer sheet.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Procedure Qualification:** Before production, every weld procedure must be qualified (e.g., per ASME Section IX) by recording the exact parameter set that yields acceptable test results.
2. **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS):** The WPS is a *legal document* in fabrication industries (boilers, bridges, pressure vessels, ships) listing essential variables. Changing a primary parameter outside the qualified range invalidates the procedure.
3. **Robotic & Automated Welding:** In Industry 4.0 environments, all process parameters are logged in real-time to digital twins for traceability and defect prediction using Machine Learning.
4. **Heat Input Control for HSLA Steels:** In modern high-strength low-alloy steels, an upper bound on heat input (e.g., **≤ 1.5 kJ/mm**) is mandated to prevent HAZ softening and ensure toughness at sub-zero service temperatures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Heat Input Formula

**Starting Point:** Power delivered to the arc.

The electrical power consumed at the arc is the product of voltage and current (DC circuit):

$$
P_{arc} = V \times I \quad \text{[Watts = J/s]}
$$

**Step 1 — Account for Process Efficiency:**

Not all electrical power is converted to useful heat at the workpiece. A fraction, characterized by the thermal efficiency $\eta$, is transferred to the joint:

$$
P_{useful} = \eta \times V \times I \quad \text{[Watts]}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Energy per Unit Length:**

The heat delivered per unit length of weld (Heat Input, HI) is the useful power divided by the travel speed:

$$
HI = \frac{P_{useful}}{v} = \frac{\eta \times V \times I}{v}
$$

where $v$ must be in **mm/s** for HI to come out in **J/mm**.

**Step 3 — Apply Unit Conversions for Standard kJ/mm:**

In KTU problems, travel speed is typically given in **mm/min**. To convert:

$$
v_{\text{mm/s}} = \frac{v_{\text{mm/min}}}{60}
$$

Also convert Watts to kiloWatts by dividing by 1000. Substituting:

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \times V \times I}{v_{\text{mm/min}} / 60 \times 1000} = \frac{\eta \times V \times I \times 60}{v \times 1000}
$$

$$
\boxed{HI = \frac{\eta \times V \times I}{v} \times 0.06 \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}}
$$

where $V$ is in Volts, $I$ in Amperes, and $v$ in mm/min.

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **0.06** encapsulates the entire unit conversion chain: $\frac{60}{1000} = \frac{1 \text{ min}}{1000 \text{ W in kW}} = 0.06$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** In a GMAW operation on a 6 mm thick low-carbon steel plate, the following parameters are recorded: current = 220 A, voltage = 26 V, travel speed = 350 mm/min. Given the thermal efficiency for GMAW = 0.78, calculate:
1. Heat Input in kJ/mm.
2. If the maximum allowable heat input for the base metal is 1.2 kJ/mm, determine whether the procedure is acceptable. If not, suggest the corrected travel speed.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given data:**
- $V = 26$ V
- $I = 220$ A
- $v = 350$ mm/min
- $\eta = 0.78$
- $HI_{\max} = 1.2$ kJ/mm

**Step 2 — Calculate Heat Input:**

$$
HI = \eta \times V \times I \times 0.06 / v
$$

$$
HI = \frac{0.78 \times 26 \times 220 \times 0.06}{350}
$$

$$
HI = \frac{268.296}{350}
$$

$$
\boxed{HI = 0.767 \text{ kJ/mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with allowable limit:**

Since $0.767 < 1.2$, the procedure **is acceptable**.

**Step 4 — (Bonus) Maximum permissible current if HI were exactly 1.2 kJ/mm:**

$$
I_{max} = \frac{HI_{max} \times v}{\eta \times V \times 0.06}
$$

$$
I_{max} = \frac{1.2 \times 350}{0.78 \times 26 \times 0.06}
$$

$$
I_{max} = \frac{420}{1.2168}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{max} \approx 345.2 \text{ A}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter Change on Weld Response

| Parameter Increased | Penetration Depth | Bead Width | HAZ Width | Distortion | Spatter | Deposition Rate |
|---------------------|-------------------|------------|-----------|------------|---------|------------------|
| **Current (I)** | ↑↑ Strongly ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ Strongly ↑ |
| **Voltage (V)** | ↑ Slightly | ↑↑ Strongly ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| **Travel Speed (v)** | ↓ Strongly ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | — | ↓ |
| **Wire Feed Rate (WFR)** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ Strongly ↑ |
| **Gas Flow Rate** | — | — | — | — | ↑ (turbulence) | — |
| **Electrode Extension** | ↓ (in DCEP) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |

Legend: ↑ increase, ↓ decrease, — negligible effect.

---

### 3.4 Penetration Theory — The Heat Balance Derivation (Brief)

The depth of penetration $d$ in arc welding is approximately governed by the **Rykalin's heat flow model** for a moving point heat source on a thick plate:

$$
\frac{1}{T_0 - T_m} = \frac{2\pi k \rho c_p (d - v t/2)}{Q / v}
$$

Rearranging for steady-state maximum penetration depth (at the weld centerline, $t \to \infty$):

$$
d_{max} = \frac{Q}{2\pi k \rho c_p (T_m - T_0)} = \frac{\eta V I}{2\pi k \rho c_p (T_m - T_0) \cdot v}
$$

Where:
- $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $\rho$ = density (kg/m³)
- $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K)
- $T_m$ = melting temperature (K)
- $T_0$ = initial plate temperature (K)
- $Q/v$ = linear heat input

This shows **inversely proportional** dependence of penetration on thermal properties and travel speed, and **directly proportional** dependence on heat input — confirming the practical trends in Section 3.3.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid

```


> [!NOTE]
> These schematic flow diagrams map the cause-effect hierarchy that KTU 2024 examiners expect students to reproduce in board answers for descriptive questions on process parameter optimization.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Heat Input in arc welding. Mention its significance and the typical range for low-carbon steel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Heat Input is defined as the **amount of thermal energy supplied per unit length of weld**, mathematically expressed as:

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \times V \times I \times 0.06}{v} \quad \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Significance:**
- Governs the cooling rate of the weld and HAZ.
- Controls grain size, hardness, and toughness.
- Influences distortion and residual stress.

**Typical range for low-carbon steel SMAW:** **0.8 to 1.5 kJ/mm**. Excessive heat input causes grain coarsening in HAZ; too low heat input causes lack of fusion and high hardness zones.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] List any six primary process parameters in arc welding. State the effect of increasing welding current on bead penetration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The six primary process parameters are:
1. Welding current
2. Arc voltage
3. Travel speed
4. Electrode diameter / Wire feed rate
5. Polarity
6. Electrode extension (stick-out)

**Effect of increasing current:** Increases **deposition rate**, **depth of penetration**, **bead width**, and **HAZ width**. Excessive current leads to burn-through, excessive spatter, and distortion.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Primary Parameters & Heat Input Analysis**

**[KTU Model Question – Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **primary process parameters** of Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) and discuss how each affects the weld bead geometry and quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding)** operation is carried out with the following parameters:
- Current $I = 600$ A
- Voltage $V = 32$ V
- Travel speed $v = 500$ mm/min
- Thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.95$

Calculate:
1. Heat Input in kJ/mm
2. If the WPS specifies that HI must not exceed **2.5 kJ/mm**, what is the minimum allowable travel speed?
3. Comment on the metallurgical implications of operating at the calculated HI. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Primary Process Parameters of GMAW (7 marks):**

| # | Parameter | Definition | Effect on Weld |
|---|-----------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | Welding Current (I) | Rate of charge flow through the arc | Increases penetration, bead width, deposition rate |
| 2 | Arc Voltage (V) | Potential difference across arc | Controls arc length; higher V → wider, flatter bead |
| 3 | Travel Speed (v) | Linear speed of torch movement | Higher v → lower HI → shallow penetration |
| 4 | Wire Feed Rate (WFR) | Rate at which consumable wire is fed | Master parameter in synergic MIG; controls deposition |
| 5 | Electrode Extension | Distance from contact tip to workpiece | Affects wire preheating → modifies current |
| 6 | Shielding Gas Flow Rate | Volume of protective gas per minute | Prevents porosity; too high causes turbulence |

In **synergic MIG**, voltage is automatically controlled as a function of WFR, ensuring a stable arc and consistent droplet transfer. The interdependence of WFR and current is:

$$
I \approx k \times \text{WFR} \quad \text{(where } k \text{ is a process constant, typically 25-40 A per m/min)}
$$

This coupling simplifies operator input but requires precise machine calibration.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Heat Input calculation:**

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \times V \times I \times 0.06}{v}
$$

$$
HI = \frac{0.95 \times 32 \times 600 \times 0.06}{500}
$$

$$
HI = \frac{1094.4}{500}
$$

$$
\boxed{HI = 2.189 \text{ kJ/mm}}
$$

**Step 2 — Minimum allowable travel speed:**

For $HI_{max} = 2.5$ kJ/mm:

$$
v_{min} = \frac{\eta \times V \times I \times 0.06}{HI_{max}}
$$

$$
v_{min} = \frac{0.95 \times 32 \times 600 \times 0.06}{2.5}
$$

$$
v_{min} = \frac{1094.4}{2.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{min} = 437.76 \text{ mm/min}}
$$

> Note: Since the current speed (500 mm/min) > minimum (437.76), the **WPS is currently acceptable**. Operating slower than 437.76 mm/min would violate the limit.

**Step 3 — Metallurgical implications:**

- At $HI = 2.189$ kJ/mm (within SAW's high-efficiency regime), the **HAZ experiences coarse grain growth** because SAW deposits very high heat input per unit length.
- Slow cooling leads to **soft HAZ zones** in HSLA steels and possible **tempered martensite embrittlement** in alloy steels.
- Risk of **lamellar tearing** in thick restrained joints due to through-thickness strains.
- **Distortion increases** proportionally with HI; transverse shrinkage widens.
- For low-carbon structural steel, this HI is acceptable; for quenched and tempered steels (e.g., 12 mm HY-80 plate), it would require preheat and possibly a different process.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative: Effect Analysis & Optimization**

**[KTU Model Question – Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a **schematic diagram**, explain the **classification of process parameters** in arc welding into primary, secondary, and tertiary categories, giving at least three examples of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **effect of varying welding current, arc voltage, and travel speed** on the weld bead profile (penetration, width, reinforcement) and on **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) characteristics**. Support your answer with a **labeled bead profile diagram**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Classification (7 marks):**
- Draw the three-tier pyramid/flowchart (as in Section 4.1).
- **Primary:** Current, Voltage, Travel speed, WFR — operator-set, cause variables.
- **Secondary:** Gas flow, Inductance, Polarity, Electrode extension — supportive, stability variables.
- **Tertiary:** Heat Input, Bead geometry (width, height, penetration), HAZ width, Dilution — result variables.
- Emphasize that tertiary parameters are *outputs*, not directly set.

**(b) Effect Analysis (7 marks):**

Provide:
1. **Tabular effect chart** (see Section 3.3) — must be present in answer sheet.
2. **Bead profile diagram** showing toe angle, reinforcement height, penetration depth, bead width.
3. Brief theoretical link using heat input formula: increasing I or decreasing v → higher HI → larger HAZ → coarser grains → lower toughness.
4. Mention **Rykalin's 2D heat flow equation** qualitatively — penetration depth is proportional to (HI)$^{1}$ but inversely proportional to thickness and thermal conductivity.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Process Parameter Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (2 marks lost commonly):** Students write $HI = \frac{V \times I}{v}$ directly, ignoring the 0.06 conversion factor. The examiner's standard key demands the full form $\frac{\eta V I}{v} \times 0.06$.
> 2. **Missing Thermal Efficiency:** Many students drop $\eta$ from the formula. For SAW, $\eta$ is nearly 1, so the error may be small numerically, but the examiner awards full marks only when $\eta$ is included with correct value cited.
> 3. **Confusing speed units:** Some use m/min instead of mm/min. The 1000× error propagates and the final HI is off by orders of magnitude.
> 4. **Writing bead profile without diagram:** A descriptive answer on parameter effects *without* a labeled bead cross-section loses 1-2 marks in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Forgetting to state parameter type:** When asked "list parameters," students often forget to specify whether each is primary, secondary, or tertiary — losing classification marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Process Parameters:**
>
> - **Definition:** Process parameters are the independent input variables set by the operator to control the welding process.
> - **Three-tier classification:** Primary (cause), Secondary (support), Tertiary (effect/output).
> - **The Big-Four Primary Parameters:** Current (I), Voltage (V), Travel Speed (v), Wire Feed Rate (WFR).
> - **Heat Input is the master resultant parameter:** $HI = \frac{\eta V I}{v} \times 0.06$ kJ/mm.
> - **Thermal Efficiency (η)** is process-dependent: SAW = 0.85-0.99, SMAW = 0.70-0.85, GMAW = 0.70-0.80, GTAW = 0.40-0.60.
> - **Increasing Current** → deeper penetration, wider bead, higher deposition.
> - **Increasing Voltage** → wider bead, flatter profile, slightly more penetration.
> - **Increasing Travel Speed** → lower heat input → shallower penetration, narrower HAZ.
> - **Heat Input directly affects:** HAZ grain size, hardness, toughness, residual stress, distortion.
> - **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** is a legal document listing qualified parameters.
> - **Penetration depth** is approximately proportional to HI and inversely proportional to thermal conductivity of base metal.
> - **Standard 14-mark answers must include:** labeled diagram + formula + tabulated effect chart + numerical example.
> - **Unit safety rule:** Always show unit cancellation explicitly: $V \cdot I = W \to kW \to kJ/min \to kJ/mm$ via factors $\frac{1}{1000}$ and $\frac{60}{v_{mm/min}}$.
> - **Modern context (KTU 2024 NEP):** Synergic/pulsed MIG/MAG uses WFR as the master parameter with adaptive voltage-current synergic curves.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining the Domain: "Applications and Limitations" of Advanced Welding Processes

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the topic **"Applications and Limitations"** in Module 2 of *Advanced Welding and Metal Joining Techniques (PEPET741)* refers to the systematic engineering evaluation of **solid-state and advanced fusion welding processes** — assessing *where they are best deployed* (applications across industries) and *where they fail or are unsuitable* (limitations based on metallurgy, economics, and physics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Applications and Limitations* in advanced welding refers to the domain-specific industrial usability of processes such as **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)**, **Friction Welding (FW)**, **Ultrasonic Welding (USW)**, **Explosion Welding (EXW)**, and **Diffusion Bonding (DB)** — characterized by their suitability for dissimilar metals, non-ferrous alloys, and precision joining, while being constrained by joint geometry, material flow behavior, and capital expenditure.

---

## 1.2 The Five Major Processes in Scope of Module 2

| # | Process | Category | Key Physical Principle |
|---|---------|----------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** | Solid-State | Plastic deformation via rotating tool |
| 2 | **Friction Welding (FW)** — Inertia & Continuous | Solid-State | Mechanical frictional heat |
| 3 | **Ultrasonic Welding (USW)** | Solid-State | High-frequency vibration |
| 4 | **Explosion Welding (EXW)** | Solid-State | Controlled detonation pressure |
| 5 | **Diffusion Bonding (DB)** | Solid-State | Atomic diffusion under heat & pressure |

> [!NOTE]
> All five processes are classified as **Solid-State Welding (SSW)** processes per **AWS (American Welding Society) classification C3.0**, where coalescence occurs at temperatures **below the melting point** of the base materials.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: "Joining Without Melting"

Imagine trying to join two pieces of **soft clay** at room temperature.

- If you rub them together **vigorously under pressure** (like Friction Welding) — the heat generated softens the surface and they fuse. This is **Friction Welding**.
- If you use a **rotating spoon** to stir them together (like FSW) — the spoon mechanically mixes and forges the clay. This is **Friction Stir Welding**.
- If you vibrate them at **ultrasonic speed** (like USW) — the high-frequency micro-motion creates instant localized bonding.
- If you **slam them together with a controlled explosion** (EXW) — the shockwave welds dissimilar surfaces.
- If you hold them **perfectly still under high heat and pressure for hours** (DB) — atoms slowly migrate across the interface and bond.

> The "limitation" in each case: clay has a fixed geometry, requires special tools, costs energy, and may not work for all shapes. The same applies to metals.

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Examinations:** Questions on "Applications and Limitations" almost always test your ability to **match a process to an industry** (e.g., "Why is FSW used in aerospace?") and to **justify why a process is rejected for a specific application** (e.g., "Why is Friction Welding unsuitable for non-circular joints?").

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used to Evaluate Welding Processes

- **Bonding Temperature (T_b)** measured in **°C** or **K**
- **Axial Force (F)** measured in **kN** or **MPa**
- **Rotational Speed (N)** measured in **rev/min (rpm)**
- **Welding Velocity (v)** measured in **mm/min** or **mm/s**
- **Heat Input (Q)** measured in **J/mm** or **kJ/mm**
- **Bond Strength / Tensile Strength (σ_uts)** measured in **MPa**

These metrics govern both the **application suitability** and the **process limitations**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process-by-Process Analysis: Applications and Limitations

### 2.1.1 Friction Stir Welding (FSW)

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. A **non-consumable rotating tool** with a shoulder and pin is plunged into the joint line.
2. **Frictional heat** generated between the shoulder and workpiece softens the material (but does **not melt** it).
3. The **rotating pin mechanically stirs** the plasticized material from the leading edge (advancing side) to the trailing edge (retreating side).
4. **Forging pressure** from the shoulder consolidates the stirred zone.
5. Tool traverses along the joint, leaving a solid-phase weld behind.

**Core Why & How:**
- **Why no melting?** Because peak temperature remains **0.6–0.9 T_m** (where T_m = melting point in K), avoiding solidification defects.
- **How does bonding occur?** Through dynamic recrystallization — formation of fine, equiaxed grains in the **Stir Zone (SZ)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Generation Equation in FSW:**
> The total heat generated (Q) in FSW is empirically given by:
> 
> $$Q = \frac{2 \pi \mu F N R^3}{3 v}$$
> 
> where:
> - **μ** = coefficient of friction (≈ 0.3–0.5 for Al alloys)
> - **F** = axial force (**N**)
> - **N** = rotational speed (**rev/s**)
> - **R** = shoulder radius (**m**)
> - **v** = traverse speed (**m/s**)

---

### 2.1.2 Friction Welding (Inertia & Continuous)

**Operational Logic:**
1. One workpiece is held stationary; the other is rotated at high speed.
2. The two are brought into contact under **axial pressure**.
3. **Frictional heat** raises the interface temperature until plastic deformation occurs.
4. Rotation is stopped; a **forging force** is applied to complete the bond.

**Two Variants:**
- **Inertia Friction Welding (IFW):** Flywheel stores kinetic energy (E = ½Iω²). Used for high-strength joints in shafts.
- **Continuous Drive Friction Welding (CDFW):** Continuous rotational energy input from a motor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Stored in Flywheel (IFW):**
> 
> $$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$
> 
> where I = moment of inertia (kg·m²), ω = angular velocity (rad/s).

---

### 2.1.3 Ultrasonic Welding (USW)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Two sheets are placed between a **sonotrode (horn)** and an **anvil**.
2. The sonotrode vibrates at **20–40 kHz** with amplitude of **10–50 μm**.
3. Combined with moderate clamping force, this **breaks surface oxides** and creates atomic bonding.
4. Welding occurs in **< 1 second** with no filler metal.

**Key Limitation:** Restricted to **thin, ductile sheets** (typically **< 2 mm thickness**) — used heavily in battery tab welding, electrical terminals.

---

### 2.1.4 Explosion Welding (EXW)

**Operational Logic:**
1. The **flyer plate** is positioned at a precise **stand-off distance (h)** above the **base plate**.
2. An **explosive charge** is detonated, propelling the flyer at **200–800 m/s**.
3. On impact, the surfaces undergo **jetting** — a thin layer of surface material is ejected, exposing clean metal.
4. A **wavy (sine-wave) interface** is formed, creating a metallurgical bond.

**Key Formula — Impact Velocity:**

$$v_p = \sqrt{2 E_k / m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weldability Window (Bahrani & Crossland Criterion):**
> 
> $$\beta = v_p \cdot \frac{\rho_p}{(\rho_p + \rho_b)^{1/2} \cdot c_{pb}^{1/2}}$$
> 
> where **β** must lie between **0.5 and 1.0** for successful bonding (Bahrani-Crossland parameter).

---

### 2.1.5 Diffusion Bonding (DB)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Surfaces are prepared to extreme cleanliness (polished to **< 0.1 μm Ra**).
2. Parts are held under **pressure (1–50 MPa)** at **0.5–0.8 T_m** for extended time (**30 min to 8 hours**).
3. Atoms **diffuse across the interface** — governed by **Fick's Law**.
4. Bonds form without macroscopic deformation.

**Key Formula — Fick's First Law of Diffusion:**

$$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

> where J = flux (atoms/m²·s), D = diffusion coefficient, ∂C/∂x = concentration gradient.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Process | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{2\pi \mu F N R^3}{3 v}$ | FSW | Q in J, F in N, N in rev/s, R in m, v in m/s | Heat input estimation |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{2\pi \mu F N R}{v}$ | FSW | P in W | Power required by tool |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | IFW | E in J, I in kg·m², ω in rad/s | Flywheel energy storage |
| 4 | $v_p = \sqrt{2 E_k / m}$ | EXW | v_p in m/s, E_k in J, m in kg | Flyer plate velocity |
| 5 | $\beta = \dfrac{v_p \rho_p}{(\rho_p + \rho_b)^{1/2} \cdot c_{pb}^{1/2}}$ | EXW | ρ in kg/m³, c in m/s | Weldability window check |
| 6 | $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | DB | J in atoms/m²·s, D in m²/s | Atomic diffusion flux |
| 7 | $D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_d}{RT}\right)$ | DB | D_0 in m²/s, Q_d in J/mol, R = 8.314 J/mol·K | Arrhenius diffusion |
| 8 | $T_{peak} = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{\mu P}{\rho c v}\right)$ | FSW/FW | K = empirical constant | Peak temperature estimate |
| 9 | $f = 20$ to $40$ kHz | USW | f in kHz | Ultrasonic frequency |
| 10 | $R_a < 0.1\ \mu m$ | DB | R_a in μm | Required surface finish |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** The Bahrani-Crossland parameter **β** is the most-frequently-asked EXW formula in KTU exams. Memorize β between 0.5 and 1.0.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FSW** revolutionized **aerospace fuselage panels** (e.g., Eclipse 500, Boeing) and **shipbuilding** (aluminum hulls).
- **Friction Welding** is the **#1 process for automotive drive shafts** — used in millions of units annually.
- **USW** is the standard for **lithium-ion battery pack tab welding** in electric vehicles (Tesla, BYD).
- **EXW** is unmatched for **cladding corrosion-resistant plates (e.g., Ti-Cu, Al-steel)** in chemical and marine industries.
- **DB** enables **titanium honeycomb sandwich panels** in supersonic aircraft skins.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input in Friction Stir Welding (FSW)

The FSW heat generation equation is derived from the **frictional power dissipation** at the tool-workpiece interface.

### Starting Assumption

The tool shoulder behaves as a **rigid rotating disc** pressing against the workpiece with axial force **F**. The contact region is an annulus of inner radius **R_pin** and outer radius **R_shoulder** (≈ R).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Differential ring element on shoulder**

Consider a thin annular ring of radius **r** and width **dr**. The contact area is:

$$dA = 2\pi r \, dr$$

**Step 2: Normal pressure distribution**

Assuming the axial force F is uniformly distributed over the shoulder area:

$$p = \frac{F}{\pi R^2}$$

**Step 3: Differential normal force on the ring**

$$dF = p \cdot dA = \frac{F}{\pi R^2} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{2F r}{R^2} dr$$

**Step 4: Sliding velocity at radius r**

The tangential velocity of the shoulder surface at radius r is:

$$v_r = 2\pi r N$$

**Step 5: Frictional power dissipation on the ring**

Using **P = μ·F·v**, the differential power is:

$$dP = \mu \cdot dF \cdot v_r = \mu \cdot \frac{2F r}{R^2} dr \cdot 2\pi r N$$

$$dP = \frac{4 \pi \mu F N r^2}{R^2} dr$$

**Step 6: Integrate over the shoulder area (0 to R)**

$$P = \int_0^R \frac{4\pi \mu F N r^2}{R^2} dr = \frac{4\pi \mu F N}{R^2} \cdot \left[\frac{r^3}{3}\right]_0^R$$

$$P = \frac{4\pi \mu F N}{R^2} \cdot \frac{R^3}{3} = \frac{4\pi \mu F N R}{3}$$

**Step 7: Convert rotational speed N to rev/s**

N must be expressed in **revolutions per second** (divide rpm by 60) for the power to be in **Watts**.

**Step 8: Compute heat per unit length (Heat Input Q)**

Heat input per unit weld length is Power divided by traverse speed v:

$$Q = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{4\pi \mu F N R}{3 v}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some KTU references use a slightly different coefficient (2π/3 instead of 4π/3) based on whether the tool is treated as slipping or fully stuck. The **order of magnitude (10² to 10³ J/mm)** is what matters in exam calculations.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** In a FSW operation on AA6061-T6 aluminum plates, the following data are given:
- Axial force **F = 8 kN**
- Rotational speed **N = 1200 rpm** = **20 rev/s**
- Shoulder radius **R = 12 mm = 0.012 m**
- Traverse speed **v = 60 mm/min = 0.001 m/s**
- Coefficient of friction **μ = 0.4**

**Calculate the heat input per unit length.**

### Solution

**Step 1: Convert all units to SI**

$$F = 8000\ \text{N}, \quad N = 20\ \text{rev/s}, \quad R = 0.012\ \text{m}, \quad v = 0.001\ \text{m/s}, \quad \mu = 0.4$$

**Step 2: Apply the heat input formula**

$$Q = \frac{4\pi \mu F N R}{3 v}$$

$$Q = \frac{4 \pi \times 0.4 \times 8000 \times 20 \times 0.012}{3 \times 0.001}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate numerator**

$$\text{Num} = 4 \pi \times 0.4 \times 8000 \times 20 \times 0.012$$

$$\text{Num} = 4 \pi \times 0.4 \times 1920 = 4\pi \times 768 = 3072 \pi$$

$$\text{Num} \approx 9650.97$$

**Step 4: Divide by denominator**

$$Q = \frac{9650.97}{0.003} \approx 3,216,990\ \text{J/m} \approx 3217\ \text{J/mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** For aluminum FSW, typical Q values are **500–3000 J/mm**. Our answer (3217 J/mm) is on the high side — indicating that the process parameters selected are aggressive. In practice, this would cause excessive flash and tool wear — illustrating a **limitation** of FSW at high heat inputs.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Applications vs. Limitations

| Process | Top 3 Applications | Top 3 Limitations |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **FSW** | Aerospace fuselage panels, Marine aluminum decks, Railway carriage fabrication | (1) Requires rigid clamping; (2) Keyhole at tool exit; (3) Limited to **soft alloys (Al, Mg, Cu)** — not for steels |
| **Friction Welding** | Drive shafts (auto), Valve stems, Bimetallic transition joints | (1) Restricted to **circular/rotational** cross-sections; (2) Flash formation; (3) Needs one component to rotate |
| **USW** | Battery tabs, Electrical contacts, Foil packaging | (1) **Sheet thickness < 2 mm**; (2) Only **similar or soft dissimilar** metals; (3) Limited joint area |
| **EXW** | Ti-Steel clad plates, Al-Cu transition joints, Armor plating | (1) **Noise and safety hazards**; (2) Requires large open area; (3) Difficult to control in complex geometries |
| **DB** | Titanium honeycomb panels, Turbine blade components, Nuclear fuel elements | (1) **Very long cycle times (hours)**; (2) Ultra-clean vacuum environment; (3) High capital cost |

> [!TIP]
> **Pattern Recognition for Exams:** Notice that Friction Welding family processes all share a common limitation: **rotational symmetry requirement**. This is a high-yield KTU point.

---

## 3.4 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Process

> **Start** → Is one component cylindrical and can it rotate?
> - **Yes** → Consider **Friction Welding** (highest joint strength)
> - **No** → Can a rotating tool traverse a straight joint?
>   - **Yes, and material is Al/Mg/Cu** → Use **FSW**
>   - **Yes, but material is steel/Ti** → Consider **Diffusion Bonding**
> - **Only thin sheets available?** → Use **USW** (especially for foils)
> - **Joining dissimilar plates (e.g., Ti-steel, Al-Cu) for cladding?** → Use **EXW** (best for metallurgically incompatible pairs)

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Friction Stir Welding System

The following block diagram maps the functional interaction of the FSW process — including mechanical, thermal, and metallurgical subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spindle Motor Drive] --> B[Rotating Tool Assembly]
    B --> C[Tool Shoulder and Pin]
    C --> D[Workpiece Interface]
    D --> E[Frictional Heat Generation Zone]
    E --> F[Plasticized Material Flow]
    F --> G[Stir Zone Formation]
    D --> H[Forging Pressure Application]
    H --> G
    G --> I[Dynamic Recrystallization]
    I --> J[Solid State Bonding]
    J --> K[Process Monitoring Sensors]
    K --> L[Real Time Control Unit]
    L --> A
    M[Traverse System] --> C
    N[Clamping Bed] --> D
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Explosion Welding Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Detonation Trigger] --> B[Explosive Charge Layer]
    B --> C[Buffer Material]
    C --> D[Flyer Plate]
    D --> E[Stand-off Distance h]
    E --> F[Base Plate]
    F --> G[Anvil Support]
    D -- Impact at 200 to 800 m/s --> H[Jetting Phenomenon]
    H --> I[Wavy Interface Formation]
    I --> J[Metallurgical Bond]
    K[Detonation Velocity V_d] --> B
    L[Flyer Mass m] --> D
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Comparison of Process Domains

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solid State Welding Module 2] --> B[Friction Based]
    A --> C[Pressure Based]
    A --> D[Vibration Based]
    A --> E[Diffusion Based]
    B --> B1[Friction Stir Welding FSW]
    B --> B2[Inertia Friction Welding IFW]
    B --> B3[Continuous Drive Friction Welding CDFW]
    C --> C1[Explosion Welding EXW]
    C --> C2[Cold Pressure Welding]
    D --> D1[Ultrasonic Welding USW]
    E --> E1[Diffusion Bonding DB]
    E --> E2[Superplastic Forming plus Diffusion Bonding SPF DB]
```

## 4.4 Process Selection Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Process Selection Quadrant - Sheet Thickness vs Dissimilar Capability
    x-axis "Similar Metals Only" --> "Excellent Dissimilar Joining"
    y-axis "Thin Sheets and Foils" --> "Thick Plates and Bulk Joints"
    quadrant-1 "Heavy Industrial"
    quadrant-2 "Precision Thin Section"
    quadrant-3 "Standard Same-Material"
    quadrant-4 "Specialty Dissimilar Cladding"
    "FSW": [0.65, 0.80]
    "Friction Welding": [0.40, 0.90]
    "USW": [0.30, 0.15]
    "EXW": [0.95, 0.70]
    "Diffusion Bonding": [0.60, 0.60]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid quadrant chart visually demonstrates why **EXW dominates dissimilar cladding**, **USW dominates thin foils**, and **Friction Welding dominates bulk cylindrical joints** — a critical visualization for KTU viva questions.
