# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Heat Input (Most Repeated KTU Derivation)

The **heat input** is the amount of **arc energy** delivered **per unit length of weld**. It governs the **weld bead geometry, HAZ size, residual stress, and distortion.** This derivation is worth **7 – 8 marks** in any KTU Part B question.

### 3.1.1 Starting Definition

By definition, the **arc energy** delivered to the workpiece is the product of the **arc voltage**, the **welding current**, and the **time for which the arc is sustained.**

$$E_{arc} = V \cdot I \cdot t$$

where:
- $E_{arc}$ = total arc energy in **Joules (J)**
- $V$ = arc voltage in **Volts (V)**
- $I$ = welding current in **Amperes (A)**
- $t$ = time in **seconds (s)**

### 3.1.2 Conversion to Energy Per Unit Length

The KTU convention defines heat input as **energy per unit length of weld.** To convert, we divide $E_{arc}$ by the **travel distance** $L$:

$$HI_{raw} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{L}$$

Since travel speed $S$ is defined as distance per unit time, $S = L/t$, therefore $t/L = 1/S$. Substituting:

$$HI_{raw} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S}$$

The unit of this expression is:
$$\dfrac{V \cdot A}{mm/s} = \dfrac{W \cdot s}{mm \cdot s} \cdot s = \dfrac{J}{mm}$$

### 3.1.3 Conversion to kJ/mm (Standard Unit)

To express heat input in the standard KTU unit of **kJ/mm**, multiply numerator by 60 (to convert per-second to per-minute basis if $S$ is in mm/min) and divide by 1000 (to convert J to kJ):

$$HI_{raw} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \left[ \dfrac{J}{mm} \right] = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \left[ \dfrac{kJ}{mm} \right]$$

### 3.1.4 Inclusion of Arc Efficiency

Not all of the electrical energy supplied is actually transferred to the workpiece — some is lost as spatter, radiation, convection, and fume. This is captured by the **process efficiency (transfer efficiency) $\eta$**:

$$\boxed{HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \left[ \dfrac{kJ}{mm} \right]}$$

For GMAW on steel, $\eta \approx 0.70$; for GMAW on aluminum, $\eta \approx 0.85$.

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A GMAW operation is performed on a 6 mm mild steel plate with the following parameters:
- Arc voltage: $V = 28 \text{ V}$
- Welding current: $I = 220 \text{ A}$
- Travel speed: $S = 350 \text{ mm/min}$
- Process efficiency: $\eta = 0.70$

**Calculate the heat input in kJ/mm.**

**Solution:**

Substitute into the heat input formula:

$$HI = \dfrac{0.70 \times 28 \times 220 \times 60}{350 \times 1000}$$

Compute the numerator step by step:

$$0.70 \times 28 = 19.6$$

$$19.6 \times 220 = 4312$$

$$4312 \times 60 = 258720$$

Compute the denominator:

$$350 \times 1000 = 350000$$

Final division:

$$HI = \dfrac{258720}{350000} = 0.7392 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

$$\boxed{HI \approx 0.74 \text{ kJ/mm}}$$

This value is within the typical range for GMAW of thin-to-medium steel sections, confirming the answer is physically realistic.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Deposition Rate (DR)

The **deposition rate** is the **mass of filler metal deposited per unit time.** This is critical for **productivity estimation and weld-cost calculation** in industry.

### 3.2.1 Volumetric Wire Feed

The volume of wire fed per minute is the wire cross-sectional area times the wire feed speed:

$$V_{wire} = A_{wire} \cdot WFS = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4} \cdot WFS$$

where:
- $d$ = wire diameter (m) — *if using SI units end-to-end*
- $WFS$ = wire feed speed (m/min)

### 3.2.2 Mass of Wire Fed Per Hour

Multiplying by density $\rho$ (kg/m³) gives mass per minute, then $\times 60$ for per hour:

$$M_{wire,hr} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4} \cdot WFS \cdot 60 \cdot \rho \quad [kg/hr]$$

### 3.2.3 Application of Deposition Efficiency

Only a fraction $\eta_D$ of the consumed wire actually ends up in the weld (the rest is spatter, stub loss, and over-spray):

$$\boxed{DR = \eta_{D} \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4} \cdot WFS \cdot 60 \cdot \rho \quad [kg/hr]}$$

### 3.2.4 Mixed-Unit Practical Formula (KTU Board Style)

For KTU numerical problems, diameters and WFS are typically given in **mm and m/min**, while $\rho$ is in **kg/m³**. Converting $d$ from mm to m ($d_{m} = d_{mm} \times 10^{-3}$):

$$DR = \eta_{D} \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot (d_{mm} \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4} \cdot WFS \cdot 60 \cdot \rho$$

Simplifying the $10^{-6}$ factor:

$$DR = \eta_{D} \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_{mm}^{2}}{4} \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot WFS \cdot 60 \cdot \rho$$

### 3.2.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A robotic GMAW cell uses a 1.2 mm mild steel wire (density $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$) at a WFS of 8 m/min. Assuming a deposition efficiency of 93%, calculate the deposition rate in kg/hr.

**Solution:**

Substitute into the formula with $\eta_D = 0.93$, $d = 1.2$ mm, $WFS = 8$ m/min, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³:

$$DR = 0.93 \times \dfrac{\pi \times (1.2)^{2}}{4} \times 10^{-6} \times 8 \times 60 \times 7850$$

Compute step by step:

- $d^{2} = 1.44$
- $\dfrac{\pi \times 1.44}{4} = \dfrac{4.5239}{4} = 1.1310$
- $\times 10^{-6}$ gives $1.1310 \times 10^{-6}$
- $\times 8 = 9.048 \times 10^{-6}$
- $\times 60 = 5.429 \times 10^{-4}$
- $\times 7850 = 4.262 \text{ kg/min}$

Now multiply by deposition efficiency and convert to per-hour:

$$DR = 0.93 \times 4.262 \times 60 = 0.93 \times 255.7 = 237.8 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$\boxed{DR \approx 237.8 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

This is consistent with high-deposition GMAW robotic cells used in the automotive industry.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Decision Chart (KTU Favorite)

| Parameter | **Short-Circuit** | **Globular** | **Spray** | **Pulsed Spray** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Current range | 50 – 200 A | 200 – 250 A | 200 – 500 A | 50 – 300 A (avg) |
| Voltage range | 14 – 20 V | 22 – 28 V | 28 – 35 V | 18 – 28 V (avg) |
| Heat input | **Lowest** | High | Highest | **Low (controllable)** |
| Spatter | Moderate | **Very high** | None | None |
| Bead appearance | Rough | Poor | **Excellent** | **Excellent** |
| Position | **All positions** | Flat only | Flat/H | **All positions** |
| Sheet thickness | 0.6 – 3 mm | ≥ 4 mm | ≥ 3 mm | 0.8 – 10 mm |
| Productivity | Low | Medium | **Highest** | High |
| Modern relevance | High (root pass) | **Obsolete** | High (heavy fab) | **Industry 4.0 standard** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Answer Trigger Words:** If a question asks "which mode for a thin vertical-up weld?" → **Short-circuit.** "Which mode for high-deposition flat welding of thick plate?" → **Spray.** "Which mode for robotic automation with no spatter?" → **Pulsed spray.**

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — GMAW Equipment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PS[Constant Voltage Power Source] -->|Negative Lead| WP[Workpiece Clamp]
    PS -->|Positive Lead| CT[Contact Tip Holder]
    WS[Wire Spool] --> WF[Wire Feeder Motor Drive Unit]
    WF -->|Push-Pull Conduit| CT
    CT -->|Electrode Wire| ARC[Arc Zone Between Wire Tip and Workpiece]
    GC[Shielding Gas Cylinder Ar or Ar+CO2] --> RG[Pressure Regulator]
    RG --> FM[Gas Flow Meter]
    FM --> GH[Gas Hose]
    GH --> GN[Gas Nozzle Concentric Around Contact Tip]
    GN -->|Gas Shield| ARC
    ARC --> WP
    WP --> WP1[Welded Joint Solidifying Weld Pool]
    CT --> CT1[Current Pickup Point - Stickout Region]
    CT1 -->|Resistive Heating| ARC
    WF -.->|Setpoint Reference WFS| PS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Reading Tip:** In the diagram above, the **Constant Voltage power source** is the only mandatory electrical component. The **wire feeder** is mechanically driven but electrically acts as the **feedback element** — the stick-out resistance regulates arc current automatically.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Mode of Transfer Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Sets WFS or Current] --> B{Voltage and Current Level}
    B -->|Low I below Transition Current, Low V| C[Short-Circuit Transfer]
    B -->|Moderate I, V 22 to 28 V, 100 percent CO2| D[Globular Transfer]
    B -->|High I above Transition Current, V above 28 V| E[Spray Transfer]
    B -->|Pulse Unit Active Synergic| F[Pulsed Spray Transfer]
    C --> C1[Thin sheets, all-position, root pass]
    D --> D1[Flat position, thick plate, obsolete]
    E --> E1[Thick plate, high productivity, flat and horizontal]
    F --> F1[Robotic automation, all-position, aluminum, stainless]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Self-Regulation Mechanism (CV Feedback Loop)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant PS as CV Power Source
    participant W as Wire Stickout
    participant A as Arc
    participant P as Weld Pool
    Op->>PS: Set Wire Feed Speed (WFS)
    Op->>PS: Set Reference Voltage
    PS->>A: Energize arc at preset OCV
    A->>P: Wire tip melts, droplet transfers
    Note over W: External disturbance tip touches pool
    W->>A: Stickout decreases
    A->>PS: Arc current rises
    PS->>A: Voltage drops (CV slope)
    A->>W: Wire melts back, restoring stickout
    Note over A: New steady-state arc length achieved
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Shielding Gas Flow Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High-Pressure Cylinder 200 bar] --> B[Two-Stage Regulator 200 to 5 bar]
    B --> C[Flowmeter 10 to 25 L per min]
    C --> D[Gas Hose 5 m Long]
    D --> E[Torch Inlet Manifold]
    E --> F[Gas Nozzle Annular Exit]
    F --> G[Shielding Envelope Over Weld Pool]
    G --> H[Atmospheric Discharge]
    G -.->|Exclusion Zone| I[Air and Nitrogen Blocked]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on Shielding Gas Math:** The volumetric flow rate $Q$ (L/min) and the torch travel speed $S$ (mm/s) together determine the **gas coverage time.** A slow travel speed with a low flow rate gives **laminar shielding** (good); a high travel speed with a low flow rate leads to **turbulent air entrainment** → porosity.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**"Distinguish between GMAW and GTAW with respect to: (a) electrode type, (b) shielding mechanism, and (c) automation suitability."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | GMAW (MIG/MAG) | GTAW (TIG) |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Electrode type** | Continuous **consumable** solid / metal-cored wire fed automatically through contact tip | **Non-consumable** tungsten electrode held in a collet; filler rod (if needed) fed **manually** by the welder's other hand |
| **(b) Shielding mechanism** | Shielding gas (inert or active) supplied **externally** through the nozzle, plus the vaporizing electrode provides a small partial pressure | **Pure inert gas** (Ar or He) supplied externally; tungsten electrode does **not** vaporize and contribute to shielding |
| **(c) Automation suitability** | **Highly suitable** — self-regulating CV power source + continuous wire feed → ideal for **robotic, orbital, and seam-tracking automation** | **Less suitable** — manual filler-rod addition; precise tungsten-to-work gap control is difficult to automate; used in **specialized mechanized TIG** only |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse GMAW and GTAW because **both use shielding gas.** The single most distinguishing feature is **electrode consumption: GMAW = consumable wire; GTAW = non-consumable tungsten.** Mention this in the first line of your answer for full marks.

The four modes of metal transfer in GMAW are:

| Mode | Current Range | Voltage Range |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Short-Circuit Transfer (SCT)** | 50 – 200 A | 14 – 20 V |
| 2. **Globular Transfer (GT)** | 200 – 250 A | 22 – 28 V |
| 3. **Spray Transfer (ST)** | 200 – 500 A (above transition current) | 28 – 35 V |
| 4. **Pulsed Spray Transfer (PST)** | 50 – 300 A (average) | 18 – 28 V (average) |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

#### **Q.A.(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of GMAW** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **constant voltage power source** and **self-regulation** in the process. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition & Setup (2 marks):**
Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) is an arc welding process in which a continuous consumable wire electrode is fed at a controlled rate through a contact tip. An electric arc is established between the wire tip and the workpiece. A shielding gas (inert or active) is supplied concentrically through a nozzle to protect the molten weld pool and the arc from atmospheric contamination. The standard AWS classification is AWS A5.18 for carbon steel electrodes.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
The shielding gas cylinder feeds gas through a regulator and flowmeter → gas hose → nozzle → forms a protective envelope over the arc. The wire feeder pushes the spooled wire at a preset wire feed speed (WFS) through the conduit and contact tip. The arc melts the wire tip and the base metal. The molten droplets transfer across the arc to the weld pool by one of four transfer modes. As the torch moves along the joint, the weld pool solidifies, forming a continuous weld bead.

**Constant Voltage Power Source & Self-Regulation (3 marks):**
A constant voltage (CV) power source — also called a **constant potential (CP)** source — is mandatory for GMAW. Its open-circuit voltage is typically 18 – 40 V, and its V–I characteristic is **nearly flat (slope ≈ 0 V per 100 A) or slightly drooping.** The wire feed speed is **fixed mechanically** (no current-coupled control). 

The self-regulation mechanism works as follows: Any unintended change in **arc length** or **stick-out** changes the **electrical resistance** of the electrode extension. This changes the **current** drawn from the CV source. Because voltage is constant, the change in current instantly changes the **wire melting rate**, restoring the arc length to its equilibrium value within milliseconds. This **automatic feedback** is what gives GMAW its characteristic stability and ease of automation.

**Block Diagram (refer to §4.1 of these notes):** The key blocks are: Power Source → Contact Tip → Arc → Workpiece; Wire Spool → Wire Feeder → Conduit → Contact Tip; Gas Cylinder → Regulator → Flowmeter → Hose → Nozzle → Arc Zone.

---

#### **Q.A.(b)** A **6 mm thick mild steel plate** is welded by GMAW with the following parameters:
- Wire diameter: 1.2 mm
- Wire feed speed: 6 m/min
- Arc voltage: 26 V
- Welding current: 240 A
- Travel speed: 400 mm/min
- Wire density: 7850 kg/m³
- Deposition efficiency: 92%
- Process efficiency: 0.70

**Calculate: (i) Heat input in kJ/mm, (ii) Deposition rate in kg/hr.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Heat Input (3.5 marks):**

The KTU standard heat input formula for arc welding is:

$$HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \left[ \dfrac{kJ}{mm} \right]$$

Substitute $\eta = 0.70$, $V = 26$ V, $I = 240$ A, $S = 400$ mm/min:

$$HI = \dfrac{0.70 \times 26 \times 240 \times 60}{400 \times 1000}$$

Compute numerator:

$$0.70 \times 26 = 18.2$$
$$18.2 \times 240 = 4368$$
$$4368 \times 60 = 262080$$

Denominator: $400 \times 1000 = 400000$

$$HI = \dfrac{262080}{400000} = 0.6552$$

$$\boxed{HI \approx 0.655 \text{ kJ/mm}}$$

**(ii) Deposition Rate (3.5 marks):**

The deposition rate formula (mixed-unit, KTU style) is:

$$DR = \eta_{D} \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4} \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot WFS \cdot 60 \cdot \rho \quad [kg/hr]$$

Substitute $\eta_D = 0.92$, $d = 1.2$ mm, $WFS = 6$ m/min, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³:

$$DR = 0.92 \times \dfrac{\pi \times (1.2)^{2}}{4} \times 10^{-6} \times 6 \times 60 \times 7850$$

Compute step by step:

- $d^{2} = 1.44$
- $\dfrac{\pi \times 1.44}{4} = 1.1310 \times 10^{-6}$
- $\times 6 = 6.786 \times 10^{-6}$
- $\times 60 = 4.072 \times 10^{-4}$
- $\times 7850 = 3.1965$ kg/min

Convert to per hour and apply deposition efficiency:

$$DR = 0.92 \times 3.1965 \times 60 = 0.92 \times 191.79 = 176.45$$

$$\boxed{DR \approx 176.45 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

#### **Q.B.(a)** Compare the **four modes of metal transfer** in GMAW with respect to current/voltage range, position suitability, heat input, spatter level, and typical application. (7 marks)

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

A tabular comparison is the cleanest KTU-style answer:

| Parameter | **Short-Circuit (SCT)** | **Globular (GT)** | **Spray (ST)** | **Pulsed Spray (PST)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Current range** | 50 – 200 A | 200 – 250 A | 200 – 500 A (above $I_t$) | 50 – 300 A (avg) |
| **Voltage range** | 14 – 20 V | 22 – 28 V | 28 – 35 V | 18 – 28 V (avg) |
| **Heat input** | **Lowest** (low I, low V) | High | **Highest** (high I, high V) | **Low & controllable** |
| **Spatter** | Moderate | **Very high** | Negligible | Negligible |
| **Position** | **All positions** (incl. vertical, overhead) | **Flat only** | Flat & horizontal | **All positions** |
| **Bead appearance** | Rough, slight ripples | Poor, irregular | **Smooth, uniform** | **Smooth, uniform** |
| **Drop size** | Equal to wire dia. | 2 – 3× wire dia. | **Smaller than wire dia.** | One drop per pulse |
| **Detachment mechanism** | Surface tension / pinch (after short) | **Gravity** | **Axial electromagnetic pinch** | **Synergic pulse** |
| **Shielding gas** | Ar + 25% CO₂ or 100% CO₂ | 100% CO₂ | Ar + 2–5% O₂ or Ar + 5–25% CO₂ | Ar + 2–5% O₂ + He |
| **Application** | Thin sheets 0.6 – 3 mm; root pass; out-of-position | Obsolete; legacy CO₂ welding on thick plate | Thick sections ≥ 3 mm; high-deposition structural | Robotics; thin-to-thick; aluminum; stainless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write "**spray transfer can be used in all positions**." This is **incorrect.** Spray transfer is limited to **flat and horizontal positions** because the high heat input causes the molten weld pool to **sag** in vertical/overhead positions. Pulsed spray is the variant that **overcomes this limitation** and is used for all-position robotic welding.

---

#### **Q.B.(b)** With the help of a **schematic diagram**, explain the **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) process** and discuss the **selection criteria for shielding gases** used in GMAW of carbon steel, stainless steel, and aluminum. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Schematic (refer §4.1)** – The main elements are: CV power source, wire feeder, contact tip, shielding gas supply, nozzle, arc, and workpiece. (2 marks for diagram + explanation)

**Process Explanation (2 marks):**
In GMAW, a continuous solid wire is fed through a contact tip at a preset WFS. An arc is established between the wire and the workpiece, melting both. The molten metal transfers across the arc as droplets and solidifies as the torch moves. Shielding gas, fed through the nozzle, displaces air and prevents oxidation/nitridation of the weld pool and arc.

**Shielding Gas Selection (3 marks):**

| Base Material | Recommended Shielding Gas | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Carbon steel (MAG)** | **Ar + 15–25% CO₂** (C25) or **Ar + 5–10% CO₂ + 2–5% O₂** | Active CO₂/O₂ stabilizes arc, increases penetration, gives wetting action; pure CO₂ is cheap but spattery |
| **Stainless steel (MIG)** | **Ar + 2–3% CO₂ + 1–2% H₂** or **Ar + 30% He + 2% CO₂** | Inert-rich mix preserves chromium, prevents carbide sensitization, low carbon pickup, bright bead |
| **Aluminum (MIG)** | **100% Ar** (or **Ar + 25–75% He** for thick sections) | Pure Ar prevents oxide-layer formation; He adds heat input for thick plate (better penetration) |
| **Copper / non-ferrous** | **100% Ar** or **Ar + 50% He** | High thermal conductivity needs higher arc energy; He provides the extra heat |

**Selection Criteria** are: arc stability, weld-pool wetting, depth of penetration, bead shape, mechanical properties of the weld, post-weld corrosion resistance, and cost of the gas mixture.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on GMAW Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the arc efficiency factor $\eta$** in the heat input formula — examiners specifically test whether you know $\eta$ is NOT 1.0 for GMAW.
> 2. **Mixing up MIG vs. MAG** — MIG = inert gas (Al, Cu, SS); MAG = active gas (carbon steel). Stating "MIG welding of mild steel" is a half-mark deduction.
> 3. **Conflating FCAW with GMAW** — the KTU syllabus treats flux-cored as a related but distinct process; do not call it "flux-cored MIG" without qualification.
> 4. **Unit confusion in deposition rate** — kJ/mm for heat input, **kg/hr** for deposition rate. Writing DR in kg/min is a unit error.
> 5. **Saying "spray transfer is all-position"** — false. Only **pulsed spray** is all-position; conventional spray is flat/horizontal only.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **GMAW = continuous consumable wire + external shielding gas + CV power source** — three pillars, memorize them.
- ✅ **AWS process number 131 – 138** (depending on transfer mode); ISO 4063 equivalent.
- ✅ **DCEP is the default polarity** for steel; 70% of heat is at the anode (workpiece in DCEP) → deep penetration.
- ✅ **Four transfer modes: Short-Circuit, Globular, Spray, Pulsed Spray** — know current, voltage, position, and gas for each.
- ✅ **Heat input formula:** $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm — **the single most repeated formula in KTU welding papers.**
- ✅ **Deposition rate formula:** $DR = \eta_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot WFS \cdot 60 \cdot \rho$ kg/hr.
- ✅ **Self-regulation** is the heart of GMAW — it depends on stick-out resistance and CV characteristic.
- ✅ **Shielding gas selection:** Argon-rich for non-ferrous, CO₂-bearing for carbon steel, Ar + H₂ for austenitic stainless.
- ✅ **Pulsed spray = 1 pulse → 1 droplet = synergic control** = modern Industry 4.0 / robotic standard.
- ✅ **Globular transfer is obsolete** — know it for the exam but recognize it has been replaced by spray and pulsed spray.
- ✅ **Arc efficiency for GMAW:** $\eta \approx 0.70$ (steel) and $\eta \approx 0.85$ (aluminum) — memorize these two values.
- ✅ **FCAW-G and MCAW** are variants of GMAW equipment-wise, but technically classified as separate AWS processes.

---

**End of Module 3 — GMAW Notes | PEPET741 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW) - Equipment Used
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW) - Equipment Used

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW)

**Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW)**, universally designated by the **American Welding Society (AWS) nomenclature as GTAW** and popularly called **TIG (Tungsten Inert Gas) welding**, is a non-consumable electrode arc welding process in which fusion of metals is produced by heating them with an **electric arc maintained between a non-consumable tungsten electrode and the workpiece**. The molten weld pool, the tungsten electrode tip, and the adjacent base metal in the immediate vicinity of the arc are **completely shielded from atmospheric contamination by an inert shielding gas** (typically Argon, Helium, or a mixture of both).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEPET741 / Module 3):**
> The process operates under the classification of **non-consumable electrode arc welding with external shielding gas supply**. It is classified under **AWS A5.01 / ISO 4063 designation 141**.

The process can be performed with or without the addition of **filler metal**, depending on the joint design and base material thickness. When filler is added, it is introduced **separately into the leading edge of the weld pool** by manual feeding — it never passes through the arc (unlike GMAW/MIG).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Tungsten Electrode — Why Tungsten?**
> Tungsten is selected as the electrode material because it possesses the **highest melting point of all pure metals (3422 °C / 3695 K)**, combined with excellent electrical conductivity at elevated temperatures. This permits it to sustain the arc with minimal erosion and deformation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pen-Calligrapher of Welding"

Imagine you are an artist painting a precise line on a thin sheet of paper using a **very fine brush with a steady, controlled stream of protective mist** sprayed exactly where the brush touches the surface.

- The **tungsten electrode** is your **fine brush** — it never wears out, just as the brush hairs stay intact.
- The **shielding gas** is the **protective mist** — it prevents dust (oxygen and nitrogen from air) from ruining your painting.
- The **arc** is the **tip of the brush** — producing intense, localised heat exactly where you want fusion.
- The **filler rod** (if used) is the **paint pigment** — you dip your other hand into it and dab it into the freshly wetted area.
- The **foot pedal / current control** is your **pressure-sensitive trigger** — you control the heat precisely without lifting your hand off the work.

Just as a calligrapher produces **clean, oxidation-free, museum-quality strokes**, GTAW produces **clean, defect-free, high-purity welds** — making it the preferred process for **aerospace, nuclear, food-grade stainless steel, and critical thin-section applications**.

## 1.3 Classification & Position in Welding Taxonomy

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Position of GTAW (KTU Module-3 reference framework):**
> Welding → Arc Welding → Non-Consumable Electrode Process → **GTAW (Process 141)**

## 1.4 Industrial Significance & Typical Applications

GTAW is the **process of choice** whenever weld quality, aesthetics, and metallurgical purity outweigh deposition-rate considerations. Major application domains include:

- **Aerospace:** Engine components, turbine blades, thin-wall tubes (Ti, Inconel, stainless steels).
- **Nuclear:** Stainless steel and Zircaloy fuel-cladding tubes.
- **Cryogenic:** LNG vessels, vacuum-insulated panels (304L/316L SS).
- **Food / Pharma:** Sanitary tubing using orbital GTAW.
- **Automotive (high-end):** Aluminium and magnesium chassis components.
- **Art / Sculpture:** Architectural stainless steel artwork.

The disadvantages are low deposition rate, high operator skill requirement, and sensitivity to contamination — all of which place **equipment selection** at the heart of process success.

---

## 1.5 Introduction to GTAW Equipment — The Core Module Topic

The Module-3 topic **"Equipment Used"** in GTAW refers to the **integrated set of hardware components** that together deliver the four essential process inputs:

1. **Electrical energy** (controlled current/voltage).
2. **Arc-sustaining electrode** (tungsten).
3. **Shielding gas envelope** (inert gas delivery).
4. **Optional filler metal** (rod feeding) and **operator interface** (current control).

The principal equipment units are:

| # | Equipment Unit | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Welding Power Source (Constant-Current) | Delivers controlled arc current |
| 2 | TIG Torch (Electrode Holder) | Holds tungsten, delivers gas & current |
| 3 | Tungsten Electrode | Non-consumable cathode that sustains the arc |
| 4 | Shielding Gas Supply System | Cylinder, regulator, flowmeter, gas hose |
| 5 | Gas Lens / Nozzle Assembly | Shapes the shielding envelope |
| 6 | Filler Rod Feed (Manual / Cold-wire / Hot-wire) | Adds filler metal into the weld pool |
| 7 | High-Frequency (HF) Starter / Lift-Arc Unit | Initiates arc without contact |
| 8 | Water / Gas Cooling System | Removes heat from torch |
| 9 | Foot Pedal / Remote Current Control | Modulates current during operation |
| 10 | Work Lead & Ground Clamp | Completes the electrical circuit |

Each unit will be analysed rigorously in **SECTION 2**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Equipment Block-1: Welding Power Source (Constant-Current Machine)

The **welding power source** is the **DC electrical energy supply** that maintains the arc between the tungsten electrode and the workpiece. GTAW specifically requires a **Constant-Current (CC)** characteristic — *not* constant-voltage — because the arc length in GTAW fluctuates due to manual torch manipulation, and a CC machine prevents current runaway.

### 2.1.1 Operating Modes

- **DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative)** — also called **DCSP (Straight Polarity)**: Tungsten is the **cathode (−)**, workpiece is **anode (+)**. Produces **~70 % of heat at the workpiece** → deep, narrow penetration. Used for **steel, stainless steel, titanium**.
- **DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive)** — also called **DCRP (Reverse Polarity)**: Tungsten is **anode (+)**, workpiece is **cathode (−)**. Produces **~70 % of heat at the tungsten tip** → cleans the oxide layer (e.g., Al₂O₃ on aluminium) but **erodes the electrode rapidly**.
- **AC (Alternating Current)** — square-wave or sine-wave: Combines oxide-cleaning (DCEP half-cycle) with deep penetration (DCEN half-cycle). **Essential for aluminium and magnesium.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** For **Aluminium GTAW**, the **only** viable current mode is **AC (with HF stabilisation)** — never DCEP alone, because DCEP destroys the tungsten tip rapidly.

### 2.1.2 Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) and Arc Voltage

The power source has two voltage levels:

$$V_{OC} = 60 \text{ to } 90 \text{ V (typical)}$$

$$V_{arc} = 8 \text{ to } 18 \text{ V (typical, load condition)}$$

The **OCV** is required for arc initiation (especially for HF or scratch start) and is **substantially higher** than the working arc voltage.

## 2.2 Equipment Block-2: TIG Torch (Electrode Holder)

The TIG torch performs **three simultaneous functions**:

1. **Mechanical gripping** of the tungsten electrode.
2. **Electrical conduction** of welding current to the electrode.
3. **Shielding-gas delivery** through the nozzle to the arc zone.

### 2.2.1 Torch Classification by Cooling

| Torch Type | Current Rating | Cooling Medium | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Air-cooled** | ≤ 200 A | Ambient air | Light fabrication, hobbyist |
| **Water-cooled** | 200–600 A | Circulating coolant | Heavy industrial, aerospace |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Beyond **~200 A**, air-cooled torches overheat. Industrial GTAW above 200 A **must** use water-cooled torches to protect the **current-carrying copper collet and nozzle**.

### 2.2.2 Torch Head Geometry — Gas Nozzle

The **ceramic gas nozzle** (typically **fused alumina, zirconia, or pure alumina**) shapes and directs the shielding gas. The nozzle bore diameter $D_n$ follows the empirical rule:

$$D_n \approx (2 \text{ to } 3) \times d_{electrode}$$

where $d_{electrode}$ is the tungsten electrode diameter (mm).

Common nozzle sizes are: **No.4 (6.4 mm)**, **No.5 (7.9 mm)**, **No.6 (9.5 mm)**, **No.7 (11.1 mm)**, **No.8 (12.7 mm)**.

## 2.3 Equipment Block-3: Tungsten Electrode

### 2.3.1 Electrode Classification (AWS A5.12)

| AWS Code | Composition | Colour Code | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **WP** | Pure Tungsten (≥ 99.5 %) | Green | AC welding of Al/Mg |
| **WCe-20** | 2 % Ceria (CeO₂) | Orange | AC/DC — Low current, orbital |
| **WLa-10** | 1 % Lanthana (La₂O₃) | Black | DC/AC — General purpose |
| **WLa-15** | 1.5 % Lanthana | Gold | DC/AC — Excellent arc start |
| **WTh-10** | 1 % Thoria (ThO₂) | Yellow | DC — Best electron emission |
| **WTh-20** | 2 % Thoria | Red | DC — High current |
| **WZr-8** | 0.8 % Zirconia (ZrO₂) | Brown | AC — Non-sagging for high current |

> [!WARNING]
> **Thoriated (WTh) Tungsten — KTU Examiner Pitfall:**
> Thoria (ThO₂) is **radioactive** (low-level alpha emitter). Modern industry prefers **Lanthanated (WLa) or Ceriated (WCe)** as safer replacements. **WTh-20 is being phased out under EU REACH regulations.** Examiner often asks: *Why is WTh-10 still used despite radioactivity?* → Because of its **superior arc-starting and arc-stability characteristics**.

### 2.3.2 Electrode Current Capacity — Empirical Formula

The maximum safe DC current capacity of a tungsten electrode of diameter $d$ (in mm) is given empirically as:

$$I_{max} = K \cdot d^{1.5}$$

where $K$ is an electrode-dependent constant, summarised below.

**Approximate $I_{max}$ vs Electrode Diameter (DCEN, WTh-2%):**

| d (mm) | I (A) DCEN | I (A) DCEP | I (A) AC |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 30–60 | 10–20 | 20–30 |
| 1.6 | 60–115 | 15–30 | 45–80 |
| 2.4 | 100–180 | 25–40 | 70–125 |
| 3.2 | 150–250 | 40–55 | 110–170 |
| 4.0 | 200–320 | 55–80 | 150–220 |
| 4.8 | 275–400 | 70–100 | 200–275 |
| 6.4 | 400–600 | 100–150 | 300–400 |

### 2.3.3 Electrode Tip Geometry

The shape of the **electrode tip** profoundly affects arc shape and penetration.

- **Pointed tip (30°–60° cone):** Narrow, focused arc → deep penetration → used for **DCEN thin materials**.
- **Truncated tip (small flat land):** Slightly wider arc → balanced heat distribution.
- **Balled tip (round end, ~2×d):** **AC welding of aluminium** — naturally formed by DCEP half-cycle cleaning action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Key:** For **AC aluminium welding**, the tip is allowed to form a **natural hemisphere** by molten tungsten. Grinding to a sharp point under AC is unnecessary and incorrect.

## 2.4 Equipment Block-4: Shielding Gas Supply System

### 2.4.1 Components

1. **Cylinder** (Argon at ~150 bar pressure, blue cylinder; Helium at ~200 bar, brown cylinder).
2. **Two-stage pressure regulator** (reduces 150 bar → 1–4 bar delivery).
3. **Flowmeter / flow-regulator** (rotameter or electronic mass-flow controller).
4. **Gas hose** (typically black rubber, gas-specific fittings — right-hand thread for Argon).
5. **Solenoid valve** (controlled by torch switch).
6. **Purge line and post-flow timer.**

### 2.4.2 Typical Flow Rate Rule

$$Q_{gas} = (8 \text{ to } 15) \text{ L/min for Argon (manual welding, indoor)}$$

$$Q_{gas} = (15 \text{ to } 25) \text{ L/min for Helium (because of lower density)}$$

Empirical volumetric relation:

$$Q_{gas} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_n^2 \cdot v_g$$

where $D_n$ = nozzle bore diameter (m), $v_g$ = gas exit velocity (~ 5–15 m/s typical).

### 2.4.3 Shielding Gas Selection Logic

| Gas | Arc Characteristics | Cost | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure Argon (Ar)** | Stable, low heat input | Low | SS, Ti, Cu, low-current Al |
| **Pure Helium (He)** | Hot arc, high heat input | High | High-speed, thick Al/Cu |
| **Ar + 25–75 % He** | Intermediate | Moderate | Thick-section aluminium |
| **Ar + 2–5 % H₂** | Reducing, deeper fusion | Moderate | SS (austenitic) — never for Ti! |

> [!WARNING]
> **Argon-Hydrogen mixtures MUST NOT be used for Titanium, Zirconium, or Magnesium.** Hydrogen embrittles these metals catastrophically.

## 2.5 Equipment Block-5: Filler Rod Feed Mechanism

| Method | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual cold-wire feed** | Operator dips filler rod by hand | Most common, low-cost |
| **Semi-automatic cold-wire feeder** | Motor-driven, mechanically fed | Longer welds, repeatable |
| **Hot-wire GTAW** | Filler pre-heated by resistance heating → ~3× faster deposition | Thick-section, automation |

### 2.5.1 Filler Rod Selection Rule

For **austenitic stainless steel** (e.g., AISI 304 base → 308L filler):

$$\text{ER 308L} : \text{Cr} \approx 20\%, \text{Ni} \approx 10\%$$

For **aluminium** (e.g., 6061-T6 base → 4043 or 5356 filler):

$$\text{ER 4043} : 5\% \text{ Si-Al (better fluidity, lower cracking)}$$

$$\text{ER 5356} : 5\% \text{ Mg-Al (higher strength, sea-water resistant)}$$

## 2.6 Equipment Block-6: Arc Initiation Systems

| Method | Principle | Electrode Wear | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scratch / Lift-Arc Start** | Tungsten touched to workpiece, then lifted | Moderate | Light-duty, hobby |
| **High-Frequency (HF) Start** | 100 kHz–2 MHz high-voltage burst ionises gap | Minimal | **Industrial GTAW standard** |
| **Capacitor Discharge (Pilot Arc)** | Auxiliary arc from pilot electrode | Minimal | Automated / orbital |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HF Start Voltage:** typically **2,000–10,000 V RMS at 1–2 MHz** — high enough to ionise a 2–4 mm air gap without physical contact.

## 2.7 Equipment Block-7: Cooling System (Water-Cooled Torches)

For torches > 200 A:

$$Q_{cool} \geq 1.0 \text{ L/min at } T_{in} \leq 30\,°C$$

Heat removed by cooling water:

$$Q_{removed} = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

with $c_{p,w} = 4.18 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input** $HI$ | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm | $\eta$ = arc efficiency (0.6 for GTAW); $V$ in volts; $I$ in amps; $S$ in mm/min |
| 2 | **Heat Input (alternative)** | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ kJ/mm when $S$ in mm/s | — |
| 3 | **Electrode Current Capacity** | $I_{max} = K \cdot d^{1.5}$ | $K \approx 40$ (WTh, DCEN); $d$ in mm |
| 4 | **Nozzle Diameter Rule** | $D_n = (2 \text{ to } 3) d_{electrode}$ | Both in mm |
| 5 | **Shielding Gas Volume** | $Q_g = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_n^{2} \cdot v_g$ | $D_n$ in m, $v_g$ in m/s → m³/s |
| 6 | **Cooling Load** | $Q_{rem} = \dot{m}_w c_{p,w} (T_{out}-T_{in})$ | $\dot{m}_w$ in kg/s |
| 7 | **AC Frequency Effect** | $f_{bal} \approx 70\text{–}250$ Hz (modern square-wave) | Higher = narrower arc, deeper penetration |
| 8 | **Penetration Depth (approx.)** | $P \approx k_1 \dfrac{I}{v} - k_2$ | Empirical; $I$ in A, $v$ in mm/min |
| 9 | **Deposition Rate (manual)** | $R_{dep} = \dfrac{\pi d_f^{2}}{4} \cdot v_f \cdot \rho_f / t$ | $d_f$ = filler dia, $v_f$ = feed speed, $\rho_f$ = density |
| 10 | **Tungsten Mass Erosion Rate** | $\dot{m}_W \propto \dfrac{I \cdot t}{A_{tip}}$ | $A_{tip}$ = cross-section of tip |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equipment-level specifications directly feed into **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** documents mandated by **ASME Section IX, ISO 15614-1, AWS D1.1, and EN 1011**. Engineers must select:

- **Power source** to match required current profile.
- **Electrode type and diameter** to balance arc stability vs cost.
- **Shielding gas** to balance heat input vs weld-metal chemistry.
- **Torch cooling** to maintain production-cycle uptime.

In aerospace GTAW of titanium jet-engine compressor blades (e.g., Ti-6Al-4V), **every parameter above** is locked into a qualified procedure; any deviation requires **re-qualification per NADCAP** — emphasising why equipment mastery is non-negotiable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation-1: Heat Input Equation for GTAW

### 3.1.1 Statement

The heat input $HI$ delivered per unit length of weld is fundamental to **weld metallurgy**, **HAZ width**, and **residual stress control**. Derive the standard KTU/ISO heat-input formula.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Instantaneous electrical power delivered to the arc.**

The instantaneous arc power is the product of arc voltage $V$ (volts) and welding current $I$ (amperes):

$$P = V \cdot I$$

The unit of $P$ is the **watt (W = J/s)**.

**Step 2 — Energy delivered in unit time.**

Energy delivered in time $t$ (seconds) is:

$$E = P \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t \quad [\text{in joules}]$$

**Step 3 — Account for arc transfer efficiency.**

Not all electrical energy reaches the workpiece. The **arc efficiency** $\eta$ (dimensionless, $0 < \eta < 1$) accounts for losses (radiation, spatter, fume). For GTAW, $\eta \approx 0.60$.

$$E_{effective} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Convert time $t$ into weld length $L$.**

The travel speed $S$ is defined as:

$$S = \frac{L}{t} \implies t = \frac{L}{S}$$

Substituting:

$$E_{effective} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{L}{S}$$

**Step 5 — Normalise to per-unit-length heat input.**

Divide by $L$:

$$\frac{E_{effective}}{L} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$$

This is the **heat input per unit length**, denoted $HI$:

$$HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \quad \left[\frac{\text{J}}{\text{m}}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Unit conversion for engineering practice.**

Because travel speed is commonly expressed in **mm/min** in KTU/Indian industrial practice, we substitute:

$$S \, [\text{m/s}] = \frac{S \, [\text{mm/min}]}{60 \cdot 1000} = \frac{S}{60000}$$

Plugging in:

$$\begin{aligned}
HI \left[\frac{\text{J}}{\text{m}}\right] &= \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{60000}{S \, [\text{mm/min}]} \\
&= \frac{60000 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \, [\text{mm/min}]} \quad \left[\frac{\text{J}}{\text{m}}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to kJ/mm (the most common unit on KTU question papers).**

Since $1 \text{ kJ} = 1000 \text{ J}$ and $1 \text{ m} = 1000 \text{ mm}$:

$$1 \, \frac{\text{J}}{\text{m}} = \frac{1}{10^6} \, \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
HI \left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right] &= \frac{60000 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \, [\text{mm/min}] \cdot 10^6} \\
&= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \, [\text{mm/min}] \cdot 1000}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final boxed result (KTU standard form):**

$$\boxed{HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{1000 \cdot S} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}}$$

where $V$ is in volts, $I$ is in amps, $S$ is in mm/min, $\eta$ is the arc efficiency.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example

> **[KTU Numerical Style Problem]**
> In a GTAW operation on 6 mm AISI 304L stainless steel, the welding current is **150 A**, arc voltage is **12 V**, travel speed is **80 mm/min**, and GTAW arc efficiency is **0.60**. Calculate the heat input in kJ/mm.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= 0.60 \\
V &= 12 \, \text{V} \\
I &= 150 \, \text{A} \\
S &= 80 \, \text{mm/min}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting into the heat-input formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
HI &= \frac{0.60 \times 12 \times 150 \times 60}{1000 \times 80} \\
&= \frac{0.60 \times 12 \times 150 \times 60}{80000} \\
&= \frac{64800}{80000} \\
&= 0.81 \, \text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to kJ/m for comparison:

$$HI = 0.81 \, \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}} \times 1000 \, \frac{\text{mm}}{\text{m}} = 810 \, \text{kJ/m}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A heat input of 0.81 kJ/mm lies within the ASME-recommended envelope for austenitic stainless steel (≤ 1.5 kJ/mm) to avoid **sensitisation** and **weld-metal hot cracking**.

## 3.2 Derivation-2: Tungsten Electrode Current-Capacity Index

### 3.2.1 Statement

The empirical relationship between electrode diameter and safe DCEN current is required for **machine-parameter setup** in WPS documents. Derive and apply $I_{max} = K \cdot d^{1.5}$.

### 3.2.2 Justification of the $d^{1.5}$ Exponent

The electrode cross-sectional area scales as $A \propto d^2$. The **current density** $J$ must remain below a critical value to prevent tip melting. Since:

$$I = J \cdot A \propto d^2$$

But empirical observations show the current **does not scale linearly with $d^2$** because of:

- Increasing **radiative heat loss** (Stefan–Boltzmann $\propto d^2$).
- Decreasing **aspect-ratio-related** cooling efficiency.

Empirical fit across decades of industrial data yields:

$$I_{max} = K \cdot d^{1.5}$$

where $K \approx 40$ for 2 % Thoriated tungsten under DCEN.

### 3.2.3 Worked Example — Selecting Electrode Diameter

> **[KTU Numerical Style Problem]**
> A fabricator needs to weld 3 mm thick aluminium plate using AC-GTAW at a current of **180 A** with 2 % Lanthanated tungsten. Recommend the smallest standard electrode diameter that will not overheat.

**Solution:**

Rearrange the formula to solve for $d$:

$$d_{min} = \left( \frac{I}{K} \right)^{2/3}$$

For WLa-15 (1.5 % Lanthanated), the empirical constant $K \approx 38$ (slightly lower than WTh due to lower electron emission per unit mass).

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{min} &= \left( \frac{180}{38} \right)^{2/3} \\
&= (4.737)^{0.667} \\
&\approx 2.83 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Selecting the next standard size up: **$d = 3.2 \text{ mm}$**.

**Verification:** From the standard current-capacity table (SECTION 2.3.2), a 3.2 mm WLa electrode under AC is rated **110–170 A**. The required current (180 A) **exceeds this range** → we must step up to **$d = 4.0 \text{ mm}$** (rated 150–220 A AC).

**Final recommendation: 4.0 mm WLa-15 tungsten electrode.**

## 3.3 Derivation-3: Shielding Gas Mass-Flow Requirement

### 3.3.1 Statement

A fabricator wishes to use a **No.6 nozzle** ($D_n = 9.5 \text{ mm}$) with an Argon exit velocity of $v_g = 8 \text{ m/s}$. Determine the volumetric shielding-gas flow rate in L/min.

### 3.3.2 Derivation

The volumetric flow rate through the nozzle annulus (approximating the bore as the effective flow area):

$$Q_g = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_n^{2} \cdot v_g$$

Converting units: $D_n = 9.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0095 \text{ m}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_g &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.0095)^2 \times 8 \\
&= 0.7854 \times 9.025 \times 10^{-5} \times 8 \\
&= 0.7854 \times 7.220 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 5.67 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to L/min (1 m³/s = 60 000 L/min):

$$Q_g = 5.67 \times 10^{-4} \times 60000 = 34.0 \, \text{L/min}$$

**Interpretation:** The required flow rate is **higher than the typical 8–15 L/min** rule-of-thumb, indicating either the nozzle is oversized for the chosen velocity or the velocity estimate is conservative. In practice, the **rotameter flow setting** is empirically tuned to **10–12 L/min for a No.6 nozzle**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Equipment Selection Logic (Decision Tree in Tabular Form)

| Process Variable ↓ | Power Source | Electrode | Shielding Gas | Torch | Filler Rod |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mild steel, 2 mm** | DCEN, 50 A, 11 V | WTh-10, 1.6 mm, pointed | Pure Ar, 8 L/min | Air-cooled, No.5 | ER70S-2, 1.2 mm |
| **SS 304, 6 mm** | DCEN, 150 A, 12 V | WLa-15, 2.4 mm, pointed | Ar + 2 % H₂, 12 L/min | Water-cooled, No.7 | ER308L, 1.6 mm |
| **Al 6061, 4 mm** | AC 200 A, 18 V, 120 Hz | WCe-20, 3.2 mm, balled | Pure Ar, 15 L/min | Water-cooled, No.7 | ER4043, 2.4 mm |
| **Ti-6Al-4V, 3 mm** | DCEN, 90 A, 11 V | WLa-10, 1.6 mm, pointed | Pure Ar + trailing shield | Water-cooled, No.6 | ERTi-6Al-4V, 1.6 mm |
| **Cu (deoxidised), 5 mm** | DCEN, 220 A, 14 V | WTh-20, 3.2 mm, pointed | Pure He, 20 L/min | Water-cooled, No.8 | ERCu, 2.4 mm |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PEPET741]

**Q: List and briefly explain the **four** primary functional units of GTAW equipment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU marking scheme):**

The four primary functional units of Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW) equipment are:

1. **Welding Power Source (1 mark):** A constant-current (CC) DC or AC/DC power source that delivers controlled arc voltage and current. It supplies energy for arc maintenance and provides high open-circuit voltage (60–90 V) for arc initiation.

2. **TIG Torch Assembly (1 mark):** A handheld or automated device that holds the tungsten electrode, conducts current to it, and delivers shielding gas through a ceramic nozzle to the weld zone. Available in **air-cooled (≤ 200 A)** and **water-cooled (200–600 A)** versions.

3. **Shielding Gas Delivery System (0.5 mark):** Comprises a gas cylinder (Argon/Helium), a two-stage regulator, a flowmeter, a solenoid valve, and gas hose. Delivers inert gas at a controlled flow rate (typically 8–15 L/min for Ar) to shield the molten pool from atmospheric contamination.

4. **Tungsten Electrode (0.5 mark):** A non-consumable electrode made of pure or oxide-alloyed tungsten (WTh, WLa, WCe) that serves as the cathode and sustains the arc without melting. Available in various diameters (1.0 to 6.4 mm) and tip geometries.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (3-mark questions):** Students often **omit the High-Frequency (HF) starter unit** or the **filler-rod feeding system**. If the question uses "primary" instead of "all", credit these omissions appropriately — but listing only 3 units loses 1 mark.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Why is a **constant-current (CC)** power source preferred over a constant-voltage (CV) source for GTAW?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU marking scheme):**

A constant-current (CC) power source is preferred for GTAW because:

1. **Arc-length insensitivity (1 mark):** In manual GTAW, the operator's hand inevitably varies the arc length. With CC output, the **current remains nearly constant** (within ± 2 %) despite arc-length variation, ensuring **stable heat input** and preventing burn-through or lack-of-penetration.

2. **Prevention of current runaway (1 mark):** If the tungsten momentarily touches the workpiece (short-circuit), a CV source would deliver **massive current surge** that **destroys the electrode tip** and contaminates the weld pool. A CC source self-limits the current to the set value, **protecting the tungsten electrode**.

3. **Constant heat input = Constant weld metallurgy (1 mark):** GTAW is used for **critical, certified welds** (aerospace, nuclear, ASME Section IX). A CC source gives **reproducible heat input**, which is mandatory for WPS qualification.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Module 3 carries 14 marks, with internal choice between two questions.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample PEPET741 Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block schematic, describe the **complete GTAW equipment setup** and explain the **function of each major component**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GTAW operation is performed on a 5 mm thick AISI 304L stainless-steel plate with the following parameters: arc voltage = 13 V, welding current = 175 A, travel speed = 100 mm/min, arc efficiency = 0.6. Calculate the **heat input** and comment on whether it falls within the recommended envelope for austenitic SS to avoid sensitisation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Block schematic (3 marks):** Draw the GTAW equipment block diagram showing the **interconnection** between the **power source → torch → electrode → arc → workpiece → ground → back to power source**, plus the **shielding-gas cylinder → regulator → flowmeter → hose → nozzle**, plus the **cooling water loop (if used)**. *(Refer to SECTION 4.1 for the schematic.)*

**Description of each component (4 marks — allocate 0.5 mark per component × 8 components):**

1. **Power Source:** Constant-current DC/AC, provides arc voltage (8–18 V) and current (5–600 A). Used in DCEN mode for SS, AC mode for Al.
2. **Tungsten Electrode:** Non-consumable, typically WLa or WTh, diameter 1.6–4.8 mm, sharpened to a point for DCEN.
3. **TIG Torch:** Holds electrode, conducts current, delivers gas. Air-cooled < 200 A; water-cooled > 200 A.
4. **Gas Nozzle:** Ceramic (alumina/zirconia), directs shielding gas over the arc and weld pool.
5. **Shielding Gas Cylinder:** Argon (150 bar, blue cylinder) or Helium (200 bar, brown cylinder).
6. **Pressure Regulator & Flowmeter:** Two-stage regulator reduces cylinder pressure to working pressure (1–4 bar); flowmeter sets volumetric flow (8–15 L/min Ar).
7. **Solenoid Valve & Post-Flow Timer:** Controls gas on/off; post-flow keeps shielding gas over hot tungsten after arc extinction.
8. **HF Starter:** Provides 2,000–10,000 V at 1–2 MHz to ionise the gap and start the arc without contact.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Identify given data (0.5 mark):**

$$\eta = 0.6, \quad V = 13 \, \text{V}, \quad I = 175 \, \text{A}, \quad S = 100 \, \text{mm/min}$$

**Step 2 — Apply heat-input formula (2 marks):**

$$HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{1000 \cdot S}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and compute (1.5 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
HI &= \frac{0.6 \times 13 \times 175 \times 60}{1000 \times 100} \\
&= \frac{0.6 \times 13 \times 175 \times 60}{100000} \\
&= \frac{81900}{100000} \\
&= 0.819 \, \text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kJ/m for comparison (0.5 mark):**

$$HI = 0.819 \times 1000 = 819 \, \text{kJ/m}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation (2.5 marks):**

The ASME Section IX and AWS D1.6 recommended maximum heat input for austenitic stainless steel (304L/316L) to avoid **sensitisation** (chromium-carbide precipitation at grain boundaries leading to intergranular corrosion) is generally **≤ 1.0 kJ/mm** (or 1000 kJ/m).

- The calculated $HI = 0.819$ kJ/mm lies **safely below** this threshold.
- **Conclusion:** The procedure is **acceptable** from a heat-input perspective. However, additional precautions such as using **L-grade filler (ER308L)**, **inter-pass temperature ≤ 150 °C**, and **post-weld solution annealing** may still be specified.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU PEPET741]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification, composition, and selection criteria of tungsten electrodes** used in GTAW. Discuss the **rationale for using 2 % Thoriated Tungsten (WTh-20)** despite its radioactivity, and identify the modern safer alternatives. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fabricator plans to GTA-weld a 3 mm thick aluminium alloy (6061-T6) plate using AC current of 175 A at 18 V, travel speed 120 mm/min, and pure Argon shielding gas at 14 L/min. **(i)** Recommend the **electrode type and diameter** with justification. **(ii)** Compute the **heat input** and assess whether **water-cooling of the torch** is required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Classification (2 marks):** Per **AWS A5.12**, tungsten electrodes are classified into:
- **Pure Tungsten (WP)** — Green tip.
- **Oxide-alloyed Tungsten:** WCe (Ceria), WLa (Lanthana), WTh (Thoria), WZr (Zirconia).

**Composition Table (1.5 marks):**

| Code | Oxide | Colour | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| WP | None | Green | AC Al/Mg |
| WCe-20 | 2 % CeO₂ | Orange | Low-current, orbital |
| WLa-10/15 | 1–1.5 % La₂O₃ | Black/Gold | General purpose, DC/AC |
| WTh-10/20 | 1–2 % ThO₂ | Yellow/Red | DC, best emission |
| WZr-8 | 0.8 % ZrO₂ | Brown | AC, non-sagging |

**WTh-20 rationale (2 marks):**
- **Lowest work function** among oxide-alloyed tungstens → easiest electron emission.
- **Superior arc-starting** — important for manual and automated production.
- **Stable arc column** at high current density.
- **Drawback:** ThO₂ is a **low-level alpha emitter** → inhalation of grinding dust is hazardous. Modern industry uses WLa as the closest substitute.

**Modern safer alternatives (1.5 marks):**
- **WLa-15 (1.5 % Lanthana):** Comparable arc-stability, no radioactivity, better AC performance.
- **WCe-20 (2 % Ceria):** Excellent low-current arc-starting, preferred for orbital tube welding and thin sheets.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Electrode selection (3 marks):**

For AC aluminium GTAW, the best choice is a **Ceriated (WCe-20) or Lanthanated (WLa-15) tungsten electrode**, with a **balled (rounded) tip** formed naturally during the DCEP half-cycle of AC.

For the required current $I = 175$ A (AC):

Applying the current-capacity empirical formula:

$$I_{max} = K \cdot d^{1.5}$$

With $K \approx 35$ for WCe/WLa under AC:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{min} &= \left( \frac{175}{35} \right)^{2/3} \\
&= (5.0)^{0.667} \\
&= 2.92 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Selecting the next **standard** size: **$d = 3.2$ mm**.

Cross-checking against the standard table (AC current capacity of 3.2 mm WLa = 110–170 A): **insufficient** → step up to **$d = 4.0$ mm** (rated 150–220 A AC). ✓

**Recommendation: 4.0 mm WCe-20 tungsten with a balled tip.**

**(ii) Heat input and torch cooling assessment (4 marks):**

Heat input:

$$\begin{aligned}
HI &= \frac{0.6 \times 18 \times 175 \times 60}{1000 \times 120} \\
&= \frac{113400}{120000} \\
&= 0.945 \, \text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

- $HI = 0.945$ kJ/mm = **945 kJ/m**.

**Torch cooling assessment:**

- Required current $I = 175$ A.
- From the cooling classification: **air-cooled torches are rated up to 200 A**; **water-cooled torches above 200 A**.
- Since 175 A is **below 200 A**, an **air-cooled torch is technically adequate**.
- However, for sustained production (continuous duty cycle > 60 %), the heat dissipation may be marginal — recommending a **water-cooled torch** for industrial safety margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls in 14-mark Part-B questions):**
> 1. **Heat-input unit errors:** Forgetting to convert mm/min → mm/s (factor 60/1000 = 0.06). Most common 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Missing $\eta$:** GTAW arc efficiency = 0.6, not 1.0. Including $\eta = 1$ gives double the actual heat input — examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong polarity assumption:** Not specifying DCEN for steel/SS or AC for aluminium loses 1 mark in equipment justification.
> 4. **Electrode rounding:** Always select the **next standard size up** — never the calculated fractional size.
> 5. **HF starter omission:** In schematic questions, forgetting to label the **High-Frequency arc starter** costs 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Cooling classification:** Air-cooled < 200 A vs water-cooled > 200 A is a **standard KTU remembered fact** — missing this loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Quick-Recall Bullet Block (Examination Day Revision)**

- **GTAW = Tungsten Inert Gas welding = Process 141 (AWS).** Non-consumable tungsten electrode + external inert shielding gas.
- **Equipment block list:** Power source (CC) + Torch + Tungsten electrode + Shielding-gas system + Filler-rod feed + HF starter + Cooling system + Foot pedal.
- **Power source:** Constant-Current (CC), DCEN for steel/SS/Ti, DCEP for cleaning (rarely used alone), **AC for aluminium/magnesium** (square-wave 70–250 Hz modern).
- **Tungsten:** WTh-20 (best but radioactive → phase-out), **WLa-15 (modern standard)**, WCe-20 (low-current), WP (pure, AC only), WZr-8 (high-current AC non-sagging).
- **Electrode size rule of thumb:** $I_{max} = K \cdot d^{1.5}$ with $K \approx 40$ (WTh, DCEN). Always round **up** to next standard diameter.
- **Tip geometry:** **Pointed** for DCEN thin materials; **balled** for AC aluminium.
- **Shielding gas:** **Argon** (most common, 8–15 L/min), Helium (hotter, thicker sections), Ar+He (mixed), Ar+H₂ (austenitic SS, never for Ti).
- **Cooling:** **Air-cooled ≤ 200 A**, **water-cooled > 200 A**.
- **HF starter:** 2–10 kV at 1–2 MHz for non-contact arc ignition.
- **Heat-input formula:** $\boxed{HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{1000 \cdot S}}$ kJ/mm with $\eta_{GTAW} \approx 0.6$.
- **Nozzle rule:** $D_n = (2\text{–}3) \cdot d_{electrode}$. Standard sizes: No.4 through No.8.
- **Process limitation:** Low deposition rate, high operator skill, expensive — but **highest weld quality**.
- **Safety:** Adequate ventilation (ozone + UV from arc), HF interference shielding, water leak prevention in water-cooled torches, **radioactive-dust precautions when grinding Thoriated tungsten**.
- **Industry WPS standards:** ASME Section IX, AWS D1.1, ISO 15614-1, EN 1011, NADCAP (aerospace).

---

*End of Module-3 Topic Notes — Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW) — Equipment Used*
*PEPET741 — Advanced Welding and Metal Joining Techniques | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Working Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **PEPET741 – Advanced Welding and Metal Joining Techniques**, the term **"Working Principles"** in Module 3 refers to the **fundamental physical, electrical, thermal, and mechanical mechanisms** that govern the operation of advanced joining processes — primarily **Resistance Welding** and **Friction Welding** processes. It encompasses the energy conversion chain: from input energy (electrical, mechanical, or thermal) → to interfacial heat generation → to plastic deformation → to metallurgical bond formation (solid-state or fusion) at the joint interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus Areas (Working Principles):**
> 1. **Resistance Welding** – Spot, Seam, Projection, Butt, Flash welding
> 2. **Friction Welding** – Continuous Drive, Inertia (Stored Energy), Friction Stir, Linear Friction Welding
> 3. **Solid-State Joining Fundamentals** – heat generation, upset mechanisms, bond formation theory

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition – Working Principle:**
> The *working principle* of a welding process is the **controlled, repeatable energy-input mechanism** that produces sufficient **localized heat and/or deformation** at the faying surfaces to achieve **atomic-level bonding** (metallurgical continuity) between two workpieces, **with or without filler metal and with or without melting of the base metal**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are trying to **join two plastic sticks** by rubbing their ends together *very fast*:
- Your **hands provide mechanical energy** (analogous to rotational/linear force in friction welding).
- The **friction at the interface** converts mechanical energy into heat.
- The plastic **softens**, deforms, and **fuses** together.
- The cycle ends when you stop applying force and let it cool.

This is **exactly** the working principle of **Friction Welding**.

Now imagine you are trying to bond two sheets of metal by:
- Holding them tightly with a clamp (analogous to **electrode force**).
- Passing a **huge electric current** through the contact area (analogous to **welding current**).
- The contact resistance generates intense **localized heat**, melting the interface.

This is precisely the working principle of **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** All advanced welding processes are essentially **controlled energy-input mechanisms**. The *working principle* describes *how* the energy is delivered, *where* it goes, and *how* it produces a bond.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | $\mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, W/m^2K^4}$ | Used in radiation heat loss |
| Mechanical equivalent of heat | $J$ | $\mathbf{4.186 \, J/cal}$ | Energy conversion factor |
| Electrical resistivity of steel | $\rho$ | $\mathbf{1.7 \times 10^{-7} \, \Omega \cdot m}$ | Process parameter |
| Specific heat of steel | $c_p$ | $\mathbf{486 \, J/kg \cdot K}$ | Heat balance calculations |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of Resistance Welding

**Resistance welding** is a **pressure welding** process in which the heat required for coalescence is generated by the **resistance to the flow of electric current** through the workpieces held under mechanical pressure.

#### 2.1.1 The Three Essentials (The "Welding Triangle")

1. **Heat Generation** → from $I^2R$ (Joule heating)
2. **Pressure (Force)** → applied via electrodes
3. **Time** → controlled current flow duration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Generation Equation (Joule's Law):**
> The total heat generated at the joint interface in resistance welding is governed by:
> 
> $$Q = I^2 R t$$
> 
> where $Q$ = heat generated (Joules), $I$ = welding current (Amperes), $R$ = interfacial electrical resistance (Ohms), and $t$ = current flow time (seconds).

#### 2.1.2 The Four Resistances in Series

The total resistance $R$ in the welding circuit is the sum of four individual resistances:

$$R_{total} = 2R_{electrode} + 2R_{contact} + 2R_{bulk} + R_{interface}$$

- $R_{electrode}$: resistance of the electrode material
- $R_{contact}$: contact resistance between electrode and workpiece
- $R_{bulk}$: bulk resistance of the workpiece
- $R_{interface}$: resistance at the faying (welding) interface — **this is where most useful heat is generated**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $R_{interface}$ is critical:** In a properly designed joint, the interface resistance is the **highest** of the four, ensuring that maximum heat is concentrated exactly where the weld is needed.

### 2.2 Working Principle of Friction Welding

In **friction welding**, the heat is generated by **mechanical friction** between two workpieces — one held stationary, the other rotated (or moved linearly) and pressed against it.

#### 2.2.1 Stages of the Friction Welding Cycle

1. **Stage 1 – Initial Contact:** The two surfaces touch, real contact area is small, friction is high.
2. **Stage 2 – Heating Phase:** Continued rubbing softens the material; interface temperature rises rapidly to the **forging temperature** (typically $0.7$ to $0.9 \times T_{melting}$, i.e., **sub-solidus temperatures**).
3. **Stage 3 – Upset (Forging) Phase:** Rotation stops; axial force is increased sharply, expelling the softened plasticized material (called **flash**) and forging the joint.
4. **Stage 4 – Cooling & Solidification:** The forged joint cools under pressure, forming a **solid-state metallurgical bond** with a refined, fine-grained weld zone.

#### 2.2.2 Heat Generation in Friction Welding

$$Q_{friction} = \mu F_n v_{rel} t$$

where $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $F_n$ = normal (axial) force, $v_{rel}$ = relative velocity at interface, $t$ = friction time.

For a rotating workpiece of radius $r$ rotating at $N$ rpm:

$$v_{rel} = 2 \pi r N / 60$$

### 2.3 Comparison of Energy Sources

| Feature | Resistance Welding | Friction Welding |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy form** | Electrical (I²R) | Mechanical (friction) |
| **Source of heat** | Electrical resistance at interface | Relative motion + normal force |
| **Weld type** | Fusion + Pressure (mostly fusion) | Solid-state (no melting) |
| **Typical processes** | Spot, Seam, Projection, Butt, Flash | Continuous drive, Inertia, FSW, LFW |
| **Filler metal** | Not required | Not required |
| **Key parameter** | Current, Force, Time | Rotational speed, Axial force, Burn-off |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = I^2 R t$ | Joule heat generated in resistance welding | J |
| 2 | $R_{total} = \sum R_i$ | Total resistance = sum of four resistances | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | $H = \dfrac{I^2 R t}{V}$ | Heat per unit volume (heat input density) | $J/m^3$ |
| 4 | $P = I^2 R$ | Instantaneous electrical power | W |
| 5 | $Q_f = \mu F_n v t$ | Frictional heat energy | J |
| 6 | $v = \dfrac{2 \pi r N}{60}$ | Tangential velocity at radius $r$ | m/s |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{total}}$ | Thermal efficiency of the process | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T_{forge} = (0.7 \text{ to } 0.9) \times T_{melt}$ | Forging (interface) temperature in friction welding | K |
| 9 | $P_{mech} = 2 \pi N T$ | Mechanical power input in friction welding | W |
| 10 | $t_w = \dfrac{H_{req} \cdot V}{I^2 R \cdot \eta}$ | Required welding time | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For all KTU numerical problems:** Always check whether the question gives **current, resistance, time** (resistance welding) OR **force, velocity, friction coefficient, time** (friction welding). Identify the process **first**, then apply the right formula.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Resistance Spot Welding** → Used in **automotive body-in-white assembly** (5,000–10,000 welds per vehicle — Tesla, Toyota, Maruti-Suzuki production lines).
- **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** → Used in **aerospace fuselage panels** (Boeing, Airbus), **shipbuilding** (aluminum hull sections), and **railway tank car fabrication**.
- **Flash Butt Welding** → Used in **rail manufacturing** (joining rail segments up to 120 m long) and **chain link production**.
- **Inertia Friction Welding** → Used in **driveline components** (axles, gear blanks) in automotive and aerospace.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Required to Raise Workpiece Temperature to Forging Temperature

This is a **classic KTU numerical** — the student is given material properties, mass, and a temperature rise, and must find the heat input required.

**Problem Statement (Generic):** A steel workpiece of mass $m = 2 \, kg$ is to be friction-welded. The specific heat capacity is $c = 486 \, J/kg \cdot K$. The interface must be raised from ambient $T_1 = 30 \, °C$ to forging temperature $T_2 = 1100 \, °C$. Find the minimum heat required.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing energy equation.**

The minimum heat required to raise the temperature of a body (without phase change) is given by the **sensible heat equation**:

$$Q_{min} = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Determine the temperature difference.**

$$\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 1100 - 30 = 1070 \, °C = 1070 \, K$$

(Note: A temperature *difference* is the same in °C and K.)

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{min} &= (2 \, kg) \times (486 \, J/kg \cdot K) \times (1070 \, K) \\
&= 972 \times 1070 \\
&= 1,040,040 \, J \\
&\approx 1.04 \, MJ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kilojoules or kiloWatt-hours for engineering context.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{min} &= 1040.04 \, kJ \\
&= \dfrac{1040.04}{3600} \, kWh \\
&\approx 0.289 \, kWh
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The minimum heat required is **$Q_{min} \approx 1.04 \times 10^6 \, J$** (1.04 MJ).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Welding Time in Resistance Spot Welding

**Problem Statement:** A resistance spot welding operation is performed on two $1.5 \, mm$ thick low-carbon steel sheets. The current is $I = 9000 \, A$, the interfacial resistance is $R = 120 \, \mu\Omega = 120 \times 10^{-6} \, \Omega$, and the thermal efficiency is $\eta = 0.8$. If the heat required to form a nugget of volume $V = 25 \, mm^3$ is $H_{req} = 2.0 \times 10^5 \, J/m^3 \times V$, determine the **welding time** $t_w$.

**Step 1 — Express the total heat required to form the nugget.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{req} &= h \times V \\
&= (2.0 \times 10^5 \, J/m^3) \times (25 \times 10^{-9} \, m^3) \\
&= 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \, J
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the energy balance (useful heat = efficiency × supplied heat).**

$$
H_{req} = \eta \cdot I^2 R \cdot t_w
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the welding time.**

$$
t_w = \frac{H_{req}}{\eta \cdot I^2 R}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_w &= \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.8 \times (9000)^2 \times (120 \times 10^{-6})} \\
&= \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.8 \times 81 \times 10^6 \times 120 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.8 \times 81 \times 120} \\
&= \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{7776} \\
&\approx 6.43 \times 10^{-7} \, s
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This unrealistically small time** is because the volume unit was misinterpreted. In real RSW, the welding time is typically **$0.1$ to $0.5$ seconds**. The standard formula used in KTU problems is:
> 
> $$t_w = \frac{H_{req} \cdot V}{\eta \cdot I^2 R}$$
> 
> where $H_{req}$ is given in $J/m^3$ and $V$ in $m^3$, *but* if the question is dimensionally inconsistent, use the **standard KTU answer pattern**:
> 
> $$t_w = \frac{Q}{\eta \cdot I^2 R}$$
> 
> where $Q$ is the **total heat required in Joules** (compute $Q = m c \Delta T$ first, or use the given heat in Joules).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Frictional Power and Welding Time in Continuous Drive Friction Welding

**Problem Statement:** A circular steel bar of diameter $D = 30 \, mm$ ($r = 15 \, mm = 0.015 \, m$) is rotated at $N = 1500 \, rpm$ and pressed against a stationary bar with axial force $F_n = 30 \, kN$. The coefficient of friction at the interface is $\mu = 0.4$. If the heat required is $Q = 1500 \, kJ$ and the thermal efficiency is $\eta = 0.7$, find the **frictional power**, the **average interface velocity**, and the **welding time**.

**Step 1 — Average tangential velocity at the interface.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{avg} &= \frac{2 \pi r N}{60} \\
&= \frac{2 \pi \times 0.015 \times 1500}{60} \\
&= \frac{141.37}{60} \\
&\approx 2.356 \, m/s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Frictional power input.**

The mechanical power dissipated as frictional heat is:

$$
P_{fric} = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot v_{avg}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{fric} &= 0.4 \times (30 \times 10^3) \times 2.356 \\
&= 12{,}000 \times 2.356 \\
&\approx 28{,}274 \, W \\
&\approx 28.27 \, kW
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Useful heat per second (= useful power).**

$$
P_{useful} = \eta \cdot P_{fric} = 0.7 \times 28.27 \approx 19.79 \, kW
$$

**Step 4 — Welding time.**

$$
t_w = \frac{Q}{P_{useful}} = \frac{1500 \times 10^3}{19.79 \times 10^3} \approx 75.8 \, s
$$

**Result:** Welding time $\approx 75.8$ seconds (about 1.26 minutes) — consistent with industrial continuous drive friction welding cycles.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Working Principle Characteristics of Common Processes

| Process | Energy Source | Heat Generation Mechanism | Solid-State / Fusion | Weld Time | Typical Current / Force |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RSW** | Electrical | $I^2 R$ Joule heating | Fusion + Pressure | $0.1 - 1.0$ s | $5 - 15$ kA |
| **Projection Welding** | Electrical | $I^2 R$ at contact projections | Fusion | $0.1 - 0.5$ s | $8 - 20$ kA |
| **Seam Welding** | Electrical | Continuous $I^2 R$ with rolling electrodes | Fusion | Continuous | $5 - 12$ kA |
| **Flash Butt Welding** | Electrical | $I^2 R$ at flashing interface | Fusion + Forging | $5 - 30$ s | Low V, high I |
| **Continuous Drive FW** | Mechanical | Friction $\mu F v$ | Solid-state | $5 - 90$ s | $20 - 200$ kN |
| **Inertia FW** | Mechanical (stored kinetic) | Decelerating flywheel friction | Solid-state | $< 1 - 5$ s | $40 - 300$ kN |
| **Friction Stir Welding** | Mechanical | Stirring tool shoulder + pin | Solid-state | Continuous | $5 - 50$ kN |
| **Linear Friction Welding** | Mechanical | Oscillatory linear motion | Solid-state | $1 - 30$ s | $50 - 400$ kN |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Resistance Spot Welding – Working Principle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Sheets clamped between electrodes] --> A1[Apply Electrode Force F]
    A1 --> A2[Squeeze Time: Allow pressure to stabilize]
    A2 --> A3[Pass Welding Current I for Time t]
    A3 --> A4{I2 R heat at interface}
    A4 --> A5[Interface Temperature rises above T melt]
    A5 --> A6[Formation of Molten Nugget]
    A6 --> A7[Hold Time: Current OFF, Force ON for cooling]
    A7 --> A8[Nugget solidifies under pressure]
    A8 --> A9[Electrode Retraction]
    A9 --> A10[End: Solid-state nugget welds the sheets]
```

### 4.2 Friction Welding – Working Principle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Start: Workpiece 1 fixed, Workpiece 2 on chuck] --> B1[Apply Axial Force F along axis]
    B1 --> B2[Spindle accelerates Workpiece 2 to N rpm]
    B2 --> B3[Move Workpiece 2 axially to contact Workpiece 1]
    B3 --> B4[Friction Heat at interface: mu times F times v]
    B4 --> B5[Interface temperature rises to 0.8 Tmelt]
    B5 --> B6[Material at interface becomes plasticized]
    B6 --> B7[Stop rotation: Brake spindle]
    B7 --> B8[Forging Phase: Increase Axial Force sharply]
    B8 --> B9[Plastic flash expelled from interface]
    B9 --> B10[Cool under pressure: Solid-state bond forms]
    B10 --> B11[Release force and retract]
    B11 --> B12[End: Completed Friction Welded Joint]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Resistance Welding System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[Power Supply 3 Phase AC] --> TR[Step Down Transformer]
    TR --> SW[Static / Ignitron Contactor Switch]
    SW --> CU[Current Regulator and Timing Circuit]
    CU --> EL[Upper Electrode]
    EL --> WK[Workpiece Assembly]
    WK --> EL2[Lower Electrode]
    EL2 --> FR[Force Application System Pneumatic or Hydraulic]
    FR --> WK
    CU --> CTL[Control Console with timers and pressure gauges]
    CTL --> CU
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Friction Welding Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MTR[AC Induction Motor] --> CL[Clutch System]
    CL --> SP[Spindle and Chuck Assembly]
    SP --> W2[Rotating Workpiece 2]
    W2 --> IF[Faying Interface Friction Zone]
    IF --> W1[Stationary Workpiece 1]
    W1 --> HYD[Hydraulic Cylinder for Axial Force]
    HYD --> HYD2[Pressure Control Valve and Gauge]
    BRK[Brake System for Spindle Stop] --> SP
    CTL2[Control Panel Speed Force Time] --> MTR
    CTL2 --> BRK
    CTL2 --> HYD
```

### 4.5 Schematic – Resistances in the Welding Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EL1[Electrode 1] --> R1[R electrode 1]
    R1 --> RC1[R contact electrode 1 to sheet]
    RC1 --> RB1[R bulk sheet 1]
    RB1 --> RI[R interface faying surface]
    RI --> RB2[R bulk sheet 2]
    RB2 --> RC2[R contact sheet 2 to electrode 2]
    RC2 --> R2[R electrode 2]
    R2 --> EL2[Electrode 2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The current flows from Electrode 1 → through the resistances in series → to Electrode 2. The total resistance $R_{total}$ is the algebraic sum. The interface resistance $R_{interface}$ is the **targeted high-resistance zone** where the weld nugget forms.

---
