# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define weldability of steels. Mention the role of carbon equivalent in weldability assessment.** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**Model Answer:**

Weldability is the capacity of a steel to be joined by welding under specified conditions to form a structure that performs satisfactorily in service (IIW definition). It is judged by the soundness, mechanical integrity, and absence of defects (cracks, porosity, embrittlement) in the welded joint.

The **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** is a numerical index that quantifies the combined effect of alloying elements (C, Mn, Cr, Mo, V, Ni, Cu) on **hardenability**. The IIW formula is:

$$
CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15}
$$

A higher CE indicates **greater martensite formation tendency** in the HAZ, raising the risk of **hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC)** and reduced toughness. Hence CE is the primary screening parameter for procedure qualification.

### **Q2. List the four sub-zones of the HAZ in a single-pass arc weld of plain-carbon steel.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**Model Answer:**

The HAZ consists of four thermally distinct sub-zones:

1. **Coarse-Grain HAZ (CGHAZ):** Peak temperature > 1100 °C — austenite grains coarsen, hard martensite/bainite forms on cooling.
2. **Fine-Grain HAZ (FGHAZ):** Peak temperature between A₃ (~900 °C) and 1100 °C — complete re-austenitization with grain refinement, gives best toughness.
3. **Intercritical HAZ (ICHAZ):** Peak temperature between A₁ (~727 °C) and A₃ — partial austenitization leads to M-A (Martensite-Austenite) islands.
4. **Subcritical HAZ (SCHAZ):** Peak temperature below A₁ — only tempering occurs, slight softening.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the microstructure evolution across the HAZ of a low-carbon steel arc weld. Explain why the CGHAZ is considered the **weakest link** in welded joints.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A structural steel plate of 30 mm thickness has the composition: C = 0.16%, Mn = 1.30%, Cr = 0.20%, Mo = 0.08%, V = 0.03%, Ni = 0.25%, Cu = 0.15%. For a submerged arc welding process using $V = 30$ V, $I = 550$ A, and $S = 350$ mm/min, calculate: (i) Heat input (kJ/mm), (ii) Carbon Equivalent (IIW), (iii) Recommended preheat temperature.

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**HAZ Microstructure Evolution:**

The HAZ of a single-pass arc weld exhibits a **continuous gradient of peak temperatures**, producing four distinct sub-zones:

| Sub-Zone | Peak T (°C) | Microstructure on Cooling | Properties |
|---|---|---|---|
| CGHAZ | > 1100 | Coarse prior-austenite grain → coarse bainite/martensite | **Lowest toughness**, crack-prone |
| FGHAZ | A₃ → 1100 | Fine equiaxed austenite → fine ferrite + pearlite | **Best toughness** |
| ICHAZ | A₁ → A₃ | Partial austenitization → M-A islands in ferrite matrix | Local embrittlement |
| SCHAZ | < A₁ | Tempered parent microstructure | Slight softening |

**Why CGHAZ is the Weakest Link:**

1. **Grain Coarsening:** Above 1100 °C, austenite grains grow rapidly (driven by grain-boundary energy minimization). Coarse austenite → coarse transformation products → **lower grain-boundary area** to impede crack propagation.
2. **Martensite/Bainite Formation:** Fast cooling from high peak T produces brittle phases (HV 350–450) prone to **HIC** when hydrogen is present.
3. **Coarse Carbide Dissolution:** Strengthening carbides (NbC, VC, TiC) dissolve and do not re-precipitate on cooling → **reduced toughness**.
4. **No Strain Recovery:** Unlike the weld metal (with refining additions), the CGHAZ undergoes no metallurgical refining — it is "parent metal in its worst state".

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Heat Input Calculation:**

$$
E = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{1000 \cdot S} = \frac{30 \times 550 \times 60}{1000 \times 350}
$$

Numerator: $30 \times 550 = 16{,}500$; $16{,}500 \times 60 = 990{,}000$

Denominator: $1000 \times 350 = 350{,}000$

$$
E = \frac{990{,}000}{350{,}000} = 2.83 \quad \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Carbon Equivalent (IIW):**

$$
CE = 0.16 + \frac{1.30}{6} + \frac{0.20 + 0.08 + 0.03}{5} + \frac{0.25 + 0.15}{15}
$$

$$
CE = 0.16 + 0.2167 + \frac{0.31}{5} + \frac{0.40}{15}
$$

$$
CE = 0.16 + 0.2167 + 0.0620 + 0.0267
$$

$$
CE = 0.465 \quad (\text{rounded to 3 decimals})
$$

**Step 3 — Preheat Temperature:**

Using empirical formula:

$$
T_p = 350\sqrt{CE - 0.25} - T_0
$$

Assuming ambient $T_0 = 20$ °C:

$$
T_p = 350\sqrt{0.465 - 0.25} - 20 = 350\sqrt{0.215} - 20
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.215} = 0.4637
$$

$$
T_p = 350 \times 0.4637 - 20 = 162.3 - 20 = 142.3 \quad ^{\circ}C
$$

**Recommendation:** Preheat to **~150 °C**, use **basic low-H electrode (e.g., AWS F7A2-EM12K + basic flux)**, and limit interpass to **≤ 200 °C**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the major metallurgical factors affecting the weldability of steels? Explain how **cooling time t₈/₅** is used to assess HAZ microstructure and crack susceptibility.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (HIC)** and **Reheat Cracking**, with reference to: (i) temperature range of occurrence, (ii) mechanism, (iii) preventive measures, and (iv) typical affected steel grades.

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Major Metallurgical Factors Affecting Steel Weldability:**

1. **Chemical Composition** — particularly C, Mn, Cr, Mo, V, Ni, Cu and impurity elements (S, P).
2. **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** — quantitative index of hardenability.
3. **Thermal Cycle** — peak temperature, dwell time above A₁, and cooling rate (t₈/₅).
4. **HAZ Microstructure** — fraction of martensite, bainite, M-A constituents.
5. **Hydrogen Content** — from electrodes, fluxes, atmosphere, and moisture.
6. **Joint Restraint** — internal stress amplification by external clamping.
7. **Plate Thickness & Heat Sink** — governs 2-D vs 3-D cooling behaviour.
8. **Prior Microstructure** — banded pearlite, deformed grains affect HAZ response.

**Cooling Time t₈/₅ — Engineering Significance:**

The cooling time between **800 °C and 500 °C** corresponds to the austenite decomposition window. Using the 3-D heat flow relation:

$$
t_{8/5} = \left(\frac{E}{13.5}\right)^2 \cdot F_3 \cdot \left[\frac{1}{(500-T_0)^2} - \frac{1}{(800-T_0)^2}\right]
$$

**Application Matrix:**

| t₈/₅ Range | HAZ Microstructure | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| < 5 s | Martensite-dominated | **High HIC**, hard brittle zone |
| 5 – 15 s | Mixed bainite + ferrite | Acceptable for structural steels |
| 15 – 30 s | Fine bainite + ferrite | **Best toughness window** |
| > 30 s | Coarse bainite, grain growth | Softening, lower strength |

For each steel grade, the **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** specifies allowable heat input $E$ and preheat $T_0$ such that t₈/₅ falls in the optimal window.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Differentiating HIC and Reheat Cracking:**

| Aspect | Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (HIC) | Reheat Cracking (Stress-Relief Cracking) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Temperature Range** | Occurs at ambient up to ~150 °C, hours after welding | Occurs during **PWHT** at 500–700 °C |
| **(ii) Mechanism** | Diffusible hydrogen trapped at martensite interfaces + tensile residual stress → decohesion | Creep cavitation at grain boundaries; V/Nb carbides re-precipitate → intragranular strengthening → strain localizes at grain boundaries |
| **(iii) Preventive Measures** | Preheat, low-H consumables, low heat input, PWHT | Avoid susceptible compositions, controlled PWHT ramp rates, low V + Nb total |
| **(iv) Affected Steels** | High-strength quenched & tempered steels, low-alloy pressure vessel steels (e.g., A514, 4130) | Creep-resistant steels: 1Cr-1Mo-V, **P91/P92**, 2.25Cr-1Mo |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students commonly **confuse HIC with lamellar tearing** — HIC is **time-delayed transverse cracking** in HAZ, while lamellar tearing is **step-like cracking** in through-thickness direction due to sulfide inclusions.
> 2. In the heat-input calculation, missing the **60/1000 conversion** is a 1-mark deduction **every time**.
> 3. For CE, students forget to **include Cu** — Cu is present in many structural steels for atmospheric corrosion resistance and **must** be added.
> 4. For preheat, do not confuse **interpass temperature** (maximum) with **preheat temperature** (minimum). Both must be controlled.
> 5. Always specify the **thermal cycle reference temperature** (e.g., "t₈/₅ between 800–500 °C") — examiners deduct marks if the band is omitted.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Weldability of Steels (Module 3):**

- **CE (IIW)** is the cornerstone metric: $CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15}$
- **Ito-Bessyo PCM** is preferred for modern low-C, low-alloy steels — remember to include B (boron) term if applicable.
- **Heat Input** $E$ in **kJ/mm** = $\frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{1000 \cdot S}$ — never skip the conversion factor.
- **t₈/₅** between 800–500 °C is the universal HAZ cooling parameter; 3-D and 2-D flow equations differ by the **k constant** (13.5 vs 6.7).
- **CGHAZ is the weakest HAZ zone** due to grain coarsening + martensite formation + carbide dissolution.
- **M-A (Martensite-Austenite) islands** in ICHAZ are the **local embrittlement centres**.
- **HIC** requires three conditions: **(i) sensitive microstructure, (ii) hydrogen source, (iii) tensile residual stress** — eliminate any one to prevent cracking.
- **Reheat cracking** is governed by V + Nb content and PWHT temperature ramp rate — relevant for creep-resistant steels (P91/P92).
- **Empirical preheat** $T_p = 350\sqrt{CE - 0.25} - T_0$ °C is a KTU favourite; also know EN 1011-2 and AWS D1.1 alternatives.
- **TMCP steels (X70, X80)** have low CE but suffer **HAZ softening** due to dissolution of nano-precipitates — opposite trend to conventional steels.
- **Quenched & Tempered (Q&T) steels** with CE > 0.50 **always** require preheat ≥ 175 °C and **PWHT is mandatory**.
- **Lorenz hardness formula** $HV_{max} = 802 C_{eq}^{0.5} + 130$ is the empirical HAZ hardness estimator — KTU may ask to compute predicted HAZ hardness from CE.
- **Weldability is conditional**, not absolute — the **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** links CE → preheat → t₈/₅ → HAZ microstructure → mechanical property verification.
- **Hardness limit 350 HV (10 kg load)** is the universal cap for sour-service / hydrogen-charged environments per NACE / AWS.
- **Classification thresholds (memorize):** CE ≤ 0.40 → easy; 0.40–0.60 → conditional; > 0.60 → difficult.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cast Iron
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Cast Iron # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition** > **Cast iron** is a broad class of **ferrous alloys** containing more than **2.14% carbon** (typically **2.5% – 4.5%** by weight), along with significant amounts of **silicon (1% – 3%)**, manganese, sulfur, and phosphorus. The high carbon and silicon content promote the precipitation of free **graphite** (rather than iron carbide, Fe₃C) during solidification, giving cast iron its characteristic microstructure, brittleness, and excellent castability. In the context of **PEPET741 – Advanced Welding and Metal Joining Techniques**, cast iron occupies a critical position because welding of cast iron is widely performed for **repair, reclamation, and joining of engineering components** such as machine tool beds, engine blocks, pump housings, gear blanks, and pressure-bearing castings. However, cast iron is considered **one of the most difficult commercial ferrous alloys to weld** due to its high carbon content, brittle microstructure, and extreme sensitivity to thermal gradients. > [!NOTE] **Classification Basis** > Cast irons are systematically classified in **Module 3** based on the **morphology of free carbon (graphite)** present in the matrix, which directly dictates weldability, mechanical properties, and the choice of joining procedure. ## 1.2 Five Major Engineering Classes of Cast Iron | Class | Graphite Form | Matrix | Typical Weldability | |-------|---------------|--------|---------------------| | **Gray Cast Iron (GCI / CI)** | Flake graphite | Ferrite / Pearlite | **Poor** – most common, brittle | | **White Cast Iron (WCI)** | No free graphite (all as Fe₃C) | Cementite | **Very Poor / Usually Unweldable** | | **Malleable Cast Iron (MCI)** | Temper carbon (irregular rosettes) | Ferrite / Pearlite | **Fair to Good** | | **Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron (DCI / SG Iron)** | Spheroidal graphite | Ferrite / Pearlite | **Moderate (with precautions)** | | **Compacted Graphite Iron (CGI)** | Short, thick interconnected flakes | Ferrite / Pearlite | **Moderate** | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] **Intuitive Analogy: "The Brittle Chocolate Cake"** > Imagine you are trying to **join two pieces of a dry, brittle chocolate cake using hot molten chocolate as "weld metal"**. If you heat the cake locally with a torch, the heat travels very fast through the dry porous structure, the cake **cracks violently** along its grain boundaries (because cocoa solids and sugar crystals expand unevenly), and any rapid cooling shatters the joint. **Cast iron behaves identically during welding.** > - The **"chocolate"** (graphite flakes in gray iron) acts like pre-existing cracks. > - The **"sugar crystals"** (massive cementite in white iron) make the metal ultra-hard and brittle. > - **Slow, uniform heating (preheat)** + **soft, ductile filler metal** + **peening the bead while hot** = the only way to "glue" the cake back without shattering it. > This is the **entire philosophical foundation** of cast iron welding. ## 1.4 Why Cast Iron Welding is Taught in PEPET741 > [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Significance** > 1. **Repair Economy**: Replacing a cracked CI machine bed costs **₹ 2–10 lakhs**, whereas in-situ weld repair costs **< ₹ 20,000**. > 2. **Heritage / Legacy Equipment**: Kerala's coir, rubber, and traditional engineering industries still operate **1950s–1980s cast iron machinery**. > 3. **Critical Components**: Pump casings, compressor housings, gear boxes, valve bodies. > 4. **Joining to Steel**: Dissimilar welds (CI to mild steel) are routine in maintenance shops. --- **Standard Metric Reminder:** All compositions are in **weight % (wt%)** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Temperature units follow **°C** as per KTU convention. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation – Why Cast Iron is Difficult to Weld The welding difficulty of cast iron arises from **four concurrent metallurgical phenomena**, all rooted in its high carbon equivalent. ### 2.1.1 The Carbon Equivalent (CE) Problem Carbon equivalent is a **numerical index** that expresses the combined hardening effect of carbon plus alloying elements relative to plain carbon steel. The **IIW (International Institute of Welding)** formula is universally adopted in KTU evaluation. > [!IMPORTANT] **The IIW Carbon Equivalent Formula** > > $$\text{CE}_{IIW} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$ > > where all symbols denote **wt %** of the respective element in the base metal. ### 2.1.2 The Martensite Formation Hazard When the heat-affected zone (HAZ) of cast iron is rapidly cooled from peak welding temperature (typically **1000–1200 °C** in the HAZ adjacent to the fusion zone), the dissolved carbon cannot diffuse out and instead forms **hard, brittle martensite** plus retained austenite. > [!WARNING] **Hardness Threshold** > HAZ hardness exceeding **350 HV (Vickers)** or **~35 HRC** almost guarantees **cold cracking** (hydrogen-assisted cracking) and **delamination** along graphite flakes. ### 2.1.3 Graphite Depletion and the "White Iron Zone" Adjacent to the fusion line, the **graphite flakes dissolve into the austenite matrix** during heating. On rapid cooling, carbon cannot re-precipitate as graphite and instead forms **iron carbide (cementite, Fe₃C)**. This creates a thin, ultra-hard **"white iron zone"** (sometimes called the **"unmelted white iron layer"**), which is the primary site for crack initiation. ### 2.1.4 Differential Solidification Shrinkage Cast iron has **~1.6% volumetric shrinkage** on solidification. When constrained by the surrounding cold parent metal, this generates **solidification shrinkage stresses** that combine with thermal contraction stresses (≈ **1.0 × 10⁻⁵ /°C**) to crack the weld. ## 2.2 The Welding Procedure Selection Logic > [!NOTE] **Five Levers for Successful Cast Iron Welding** > 1. **Lever 1 – Preheat** to slow cooling rate (typically **200–300 °C** for gray CI). > 2. **Lever 2 – Low Heat Input** per pass to limit dilution and HAZ size. > 3. **Lever 3 – Soft / Compatible Filler Metal** (Ni-Fe, pure Ni, austenitic stainless, or low-carbon steel). > 4. **Lever 4 – Peening** the bead immediately while hot (>500 °C) to relieve stresses. > 5. **Lever 5 – Slow Post-Weld Cooling** (insulate with vermiculite, sand, or ceramic blanket). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Application | |---|---------------------|------------|----------------------|-------------| | 1 | **IIW Carbon Equivalent** | $CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15}$ | wt% | Weldability index | | 2 | **Pcm (WES formula, low-alloy)** | $P_{cm} = C + \frac{Si}{30} + \frac{Mn + Cu + Cr}{20} + \frac{Ni}{60} + \frac{Mo}{15} + \frac{V}{10} + 5B$ | wt% | Cold-cracking susceptibility | | 3 | **Volumetric Shrinkage on Solidification** | $\Delta V / V \approx 1.6\%$ for cast iron | % | Stress estimation | | 4 | **Linear Thermal Expansion (Gray CI)** | $\alpha \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ | /°C | Thermal stress calc. | | 5 | **Cooling Time t₈/₅ (Rykalin 2D)** | $t_{8/5} = \frac{Q}{2\pi \lambda c \rho} \left[ \frac{1}{(500 - T_0)^2} - \frac{1}{(800 - T_0)^2} \right]$ | seconds | HAZ microstructure control | | 6 | **Heat Input Q** | $Q = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \times 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Procedure design | | 7 | **Martensite Start (Mₛ) – Empirical** | $M_s(°C) \approx 539 - 423 C - 30.4 Mn - 12.1 Cr - 7.5 Mo$ | °C | HAZ hardness prediction | | 8 | **Critical Preheat (Gray CI)** | $T_p \approx 200 + (CE - 1.0) \times 150$ | °C | Empirical guide | | 9 | **Recommended Electrode Polarity (SMAW)** | DCEP (Reverse) for graphite-rich, DCEN for carbide-rich | — | Heat distribution control | | 10 | **Filler Ni Content for Crack-Free Weld** | $\geq 95\%$ Ni (ENi-CI) **OR** ≥ 53% Ni (ENiFe-CI) | wt% | Ductility in weld metal | > **Notation used in the table:** $Q$ = heat input (kJ/mm), $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $v$ = travel speed (mm/min), $k$ = thermal efficiency (0.6–0.8 for SMAW), $\lambda$ = thermal conductivity (W/mm·°C), $c$ = specific heat (J/g·°C), $\rho$ = density (g/mm³), $T_0$ = preheat or interpass temperature (°C). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The IIW CE equation is the **single most important diagnostic** in any KTU examination or industrial weldability assessment. For cast iron: - **CE > 4.0%**: Considered virtually unweldable without exotic procedures. - **CE = 3.0 – 4.0%**: Weldable only with strict preheat + austenitic filler. - **CE < 3.0%**: Standard procedures with low-carbon steel filler may be attempted. In **aerospace, automotive, and heavy engineering** sectors, the same CE equation governs the weldability of high-strength low-alloy (HSLA) steels, making it a **transferable, lifelong engineering skill**. --- **Section Take-away:** Cast iron welding is dominated by **carbon-equivalent metallurgy**, and the entire procedural framework exists to **defeat the four failure mechanisms** (martensite, white iron, shrinkage, residual stress). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate** > Every algebraic and logical step is shown explicitly. No shortcut phrases such as "it follows that" or "obviously" are used. Each transition is justified. ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Determining Weldability Class from Chemical Composition ### 3.1.1 Problem Statement (KTU-Style) > A sample of cast iron used for a pump-casing repair has the following chemical composition (in wt %): **C = 3.20, Mn = 0.65, Si = 2.40, S = 0.08, P = 0.20, Cr = 0.15, Ni = 0.05, Mo = 0.02, V = 0.01, Cu = 0.30.** > (a) Calculate the IIW carbon equivalent. > (b) Comment on weldability. > (c) Recommend a preheat temperature range. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution #### Step 1 — Identify the alloying elements that enter the IIW CE equation. The IIW formula is: $$CE_{IIW} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$ Phosphorus (P) and Sulfur (S) **do not appear** in the IIW CE formula, although they strongly influence fluidity and hot tearing. Silicon (Si) is also excluded (it is captured implicitly by the high carbon content). > **Logic Note:** Phosphorus and sulfur are neglected in the CE term because they do not contribute to martensite hardness, but the student must remember they still affect **hot shortness** (P) and **porosity / solidification cracking** (S). #### Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values into each term. The constant term (carbon alone) is: $$C = 3.20 \, \text{wt\%}$$ The manganese term: $$\frac{Mn}{6} = \frac{0.65}{6} = 0.1083 \, \text{wt\%}$$ The chromium + molybdenum + vanadium term: $$\frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} = \frac{0.15 + 0.02 + 0.01}{5} = \frac{0.18}{5} = 0.0360 \, \text{wt\%}$$ The nickel + copper term: $$\frac{Ni + Cu}{15} = \frac{0.05 + 0.30}{15} = \frac{0.35}{15} = 0.0233 \, \text{wt\%}$$ #### Step 3 — Sum all the terms. $$ \begin{aligned} CE_{IIW} &= 3.20 + 0.1083 + 0.0360 + 0.0233 \\ &= 3.3676 \, \text{wt\%} \\ &\approx 3.37 \, \text{wt\%} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic Note:** All terms are dimensionless when expressed in wt %, so direct summation is valid. #### Step 4 — Interpret weldability. The IIW CE value of **3.37%** falls in the band **3.0 – 4.0%**, which the KTU framework classifies as: > **"Weldable only with strict preheat + austenitic / nickel-based filler."** Plain low-carbon steel filler (such as E6013) is **not recommended** because it would pick up carbon by dilution and form a hard, crack-prone HAZ. #### Step 5 — Recommend preheat temperature using empirical relation. Using the empirical relation $T_p \approx 200 + (CE - 1.0) \times 150$: $$ \begin{aligned} T_p &\approx 200 + (3.37 - 1.0) \times 150 \\ &= 200 + (2.37) \times 150 \\ &= 200 + 355.5 \\ &= 555.5 \, °C \end{aligned} $$ This theoretical value is **excessively high** and would cause metallurgical problems (graphite dissolution, oxidation). Industrial practice caps the practical preheat for gray CI at **≈ 250 – 300 °C**, accepting that full elimination of martensite is impossible and instead relying on **soft austenitic filler + peening + slow cooling** to absorb residual stress. > **Final Recommendation:** Preheat to **≈ 260 °C**, interpass **≤ 200 °C**, filler **ENiFe-CI** (≈ 53% Ni), peen each pass while hot, slow-cool under vermiculite blanket. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Calculating Heat Input and Selecting Travel Speed ### 3.2.1 Problem Statement > A fabricator uses **SMAW** to weld a gray cast iron pump cover. The selected parameters are: **Current I = 110 A, Arc Voltage V = 23 V, Travel Speed v = 90 mm/min, Thermal Efficiency k = 0.75**. > Calculate the heat input per unit length in kJ/mm and the t₈/₅ cooling time, assuming preheat T₀ = 250 °C, λ = 0.042 W/mm·°C, cρ = 0.0044 J/mm³·°C. ### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Heat Input Q The standard heat-input equation for arc welding: $$Q = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \times 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}$$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= \frac{0.75 \times 23 \times 110 \times 60}{90 \times 1000} \\ &= \frac{0.75 \times 23 \times 110 \times 60}{90{,}000} \\ &= \frac{113{,}850}{90{,}000} \\ &= 1.265 \, \text{kJ/mm} \end{aligned} $$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** A heat input of **~1.27 kJ/mm** is appropriately **low to moderate**, suitable for gray cast iron. Higher values (>2.0 kJ/mm) would dissolve excessive graphite and widen the white-iron zone. ### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Cooling Time t₈/₅ (Rykalin 2-D Model) The Rykalin two-dimensional heat-flow equation for cooling between 800 °C and 500 °C (the critical martensite-formation window) is: $$t_{8/5} = \frac{Q}{2\pi \lambda c \rho} \left[ \frac{1}{(500 - T_0)^2} - \frac{1}{(800 - T_0)^2} \right]$$ Substituting Q = 1.265 J/mm, λcρ = (0.042 × 0.0044) = 1.848 × 10⁻⁴ W·s/mm³·°C = 1.848 × 10⁻⁴ J/mm·°C·s: > **Unit-check:** Q is in J/mm, 2πλcρ is in J/mm·°C·s, so the prefactor Q/(2πλcρ) is in (°C² · s). Each bracketed term is in (1/°C²), so the product is in seconds. $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{Q}{2\pi \lambda c \rho} &= \frac{1.265}{2\pi \times 1.848 \times 10^{-4}} \\ &= \frac{1.265}{1.161 \times 10^{-3}} \\ &= 1089.5 \, °C^2 \cdot s \end{aligned} $$ Bracket term: $$ \begin{aligned} \left[ \frac{1}{(500-250)^2} - \frac{1}{(800-250)^2} \right] &= \left[ \frac{1}{250^2} - \frac{1}{550^2} \right] \\ &= \left[ \frac{1}{62{,}500} - \frac{1}{302{,}500} \right] \\ &= (1.600 \times 10^{-5}) - (3.306 \times 10^{-6}) \\ &= 1.269 \times 10^{-5} \, °C^{-2} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} t_{8/5} &= 1089.5 \times 1.269 \times 10^{-5} \\ &= 0.01383 \, s \quad ... \text{(per pass, very small)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ > **Reality Correction:** The t₈/₅ value of 13.8 ms is unrealistically small because Rykalin 2-D assumes a thick plate. For a multi-pass repair weld on a heavy casting, the **effective t₈/₅ is governed by the cumulative thermal mass** of the casting and typically lies in the range **20 – 120 seconds**, sufficient to avoid full martensite transformation when austenitic filler is used. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Filler Selection vs. Cast Iron Type | Cast Iron Type | Recommended Filler (AWS) | Preheat (°C) | Interpass (°C) | Post-Weld Heat Treatment | Typical Application | |----------------|--------------------------|--------------|----------------|--------------------------|--------------------| | **Gray (Class 30)** | ENi-CI / ENiFe-CI | 250–300 | ≤ 200 | Stress-relief 600 °C (optional) | Machine beds, engine blocks | | **Gray (Class 40+)** | ENiFe-CI | 300–350 | ≤ 250 | Mandatory stress-relief | High-strength CI housings | | **White / Chilled** | **NOT RECOMMENDED** — replacement preferred | — | — | — | Rollers, crusher jaws | | **Malleable** | ENiFe-CI or ESt (low-C steel) | 150–200 | ≤ 150 | Optional | Automotive yokes | | **Ductile (DCI)** | ENiFe-CI (55% Ni) | 200–250 | ≤ 200 | Stress-relief 595 °C | Crankshafts, gears | | **Compacted Graphite (CGI)** | ENiFe-CI | 250–300 | ≤ 200 | Stress-relief 600 °C | Diesel engine blocks | | **CI ↔ Mild Steel dissimilar** | ENiFe-CI on CI side, E7018 on steel side | 250 (CI side only) | ≤ 200 | Optional | Bracket repair | --- ## 3.4 Complete Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) — Gray Cast Iron Pump Cover > [!NOTE] **Illustrative WPS — KTU Examiner's Gold Standard** > > | WPS Parameter | Specification | > |---------------|---------------| > | **Process** | Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) | > | **Base Metal** | ASTM A48 Class 30 Gray Cast Iron | > | **Plate Thickness** | 25 mm | > | **Joint Type** | Single-V groove, 60° included angle, 2 mm root gap | > | **Filler** | AWS ENiFe-CI (Ø 3.15 mm), 55% Ni – 45% Fe | > | **Pre-Heat** | 260 °C (uniform, torch + temperature-indicating crayon) | > | **Interpass** | ≤ 200 °C | > | **Polarity** | DCEP (Electrode Positive) | > | **Current** | 90 – 110 A | > | **Voltage** | 22 – 25 V | > | **Travel Speed** | 80 – 100 mm/min | > | **Heat Input** | 1.0 – 1.3 kJ/mm | > | **Peening** | Yes, immediately after each pass, while bead is > 500 °C (judged by straw color of oxide) | > | **Cooling** | Slow cool under vermiculite blanket for ≥ 6 hours | > | **PWHT** | Stress-relief at 595 ± 10 °C for 1 hour (if service demands dimensional stability) | > | **NDT** | 100 % Visual + Dye-Penetrant Test (DPT) after cooling | --- **Section Take-away:** The CE number, heat-input equation, and t₈/₅ model together form the **analytical backbone** of any cast-iron weldability assessment. Every KTU numerical problem will reduce to these three equations. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied** > All node IDs are alphanumeric (prefixed with letters), labels contain no markdown formatting symbols, and no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node names. ## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Cast Iron Welding Decision Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A0([START: Identify Cast Iron Grade]) --> A1{Is the casting
White or Chilled?} A1 -- YES --> A2[STOP: Replace part
or use arc-cast build-up
NOT recommended] A1 -- NO --> A3{Is the grade
Ductile CGI or SG?} A3 -- YES --> A4[Use ENiFe-CI filler
Preheat 250 C
Strict interpass control] A3 -- NO --> A5{Is the grade
Malleable Iron?} A5 -- YES --> A6[Use ESt or ENiFe-CI
Preheat 150 to 200 C
Low heat input] A5 -- NO --> A7[Default: GRAY CAST IRON
Proceed to procedure selection] A7 --> B1{Calculate IIW
Carbon Equivalent} B1 --> B2{CE greater than 4.0?} B2 -- YES --> B3[Reject standard welding
Use mechanical fastening
or brazing] B2 -- NO --> B4{CE between 3.0 and 4.0?} B4 -- YES --> B5[Use austenitic Ni-Fe filler
Preheat 260 C
Peen every pass] B4 -- NO --> B6[CE below 3.0
Low-carbon steel filler
acceptable for non-critical repairs] B5 --> C1[Execute WPS
SMAW preferred
DCEP polarity] B6 --> C1 C1 --> C2[Monitor interpass
less than 200 C] C2 --> C3[Peen bead while hot] C3 --> C4[Slow cool under blanket] C4 --> C5([END: Perform DPT
and visual inspection]) ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cast Iron Microstructure Map ```mermaid flowchart LR BM[Base Metal: Cast Iron] --> CE[Compute CE IIW] CE --> C1{CE classification} C1 -- Low CE under 3.0 -- P1[Path: Mild steel filler] C1 -- Medium CE 3.0 to 4.0 -- P2[Path: NiFe austenitic filler] C1 -- High CE above 4.0 -- P3[Path: Brazing or replacement] P1 --> HAZ1[HAZ Risk: Low] P2 --> HAZ2[HAZ Risk: Controlled via soft filler] P3 --> HAZ3[HAZ Risk: Severe cracking] HAZ1 --> OUT1[Acceptable weld] HAZ2 --> OUT2[Reliable weld] HAZ3 --> OUT3[Recommend no-arc joining] ``` ## 4.3 Process-Selection Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD S0([START: Choose Welding Process]) --> S1{Available
equipment?} S1 -- Field repair only --> S2[Use SMAW with Ni electrodes] S1 -- Workshop available --> S3{Component
geometry?} S3 -- Thin section -- S4[Use GMAW with Ni-Fe wire] S3 -- Heavy section -- S5[Use SMAW with ENiFe-CI] S3 -- Large area build-up -- S6[Use FCAW with Ni-Fe cored wire] S2 --> OUT([Recommended WPS]) S4 --> OUT S5 --> OUT S6 --> OUT ``` --- ## 4.4 Schematic Description — HAZ Microstructure in Gray Cast Iron Because a physical drawing of the HAZ microstructure cannot be rendered in Mermaid, the following **annotated block-level text schematic** is provided for exam-answer insertion. ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ GRAY CAST IRON WELD JOINT CROSS-SECTION │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ ZONE 1: Unaffected Base Metal │ │ [Pearlite + Ferrite + Graphite Flakes] │ │ Hardness ≈ 180 – 220 HV │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ ZONE 2: PARTIAL MELT ZONE (the "White Iron Zone") │ │ [Fe3C + Martensite + Retained Austenite] │ │ Hardness ≈ 450 – 600 HV ⚠ HARD & BRITTLE │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ ZONE 3: HAZ — AUSTENITIZED REGION │ │ [Martensite + Retained Austenite + Graphite] │ │ Hardness ≈ 350 – 450 HV ⚠ CRACK PRONE │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ ZONE 4: FUSION ZONE (Weld Metal) │ │ [Austenitic Ni-Fe solid solution — ductile] │ │ Hardness ≈ 160 – 200 HV ✓ SOFT, COMPLIANT │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ ZONE 5: Unaffected Base Metal (other side) │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` > **Key Engineering Insight:** The **Fusion Zone (Zone 4)** is intentionally made **softer than the parent metal** so that any shrinkage or thermal stress is accommodated by **plastic deformation of the weld**, not by cracking of the brittle HAZ. This is the central design philosophy of every cast-iron welding procedure. --- **Section Take-away:** Visual reasoning is essential — the HAZ "White Iron Zone" is the *bottleneck* of every cast iron repair, and the entire procedure exists to keep it thin, soft, or absent. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] **"List any three metallurgical reasons why gray cast iron is considered difficult to weld."** **Model Answer (3 × 1 = 3 Marks):** 1. **High Carbon Equivalent (typically 3 – 4.5 % CE):** causes hard, brittle martensite formation in the HAZ upon rapid cooling, leading to cold cracking. *(1 mark)* 2. **Graphite flake morphology:** acts as pre-existing stress concentrators (notch effect); during welding, heat dissolves flakes and re-precipitates them as brittle cementite (Fe₃C) forming the hard "white iron" zone adjacent to the fusion line. *(1 mark)* 3. **Low ductility and high shrinkage stresses:** cast iron has very low ductility (≤ 1 %) and ~1.6 % solidification shrinkage; combined with thermal contraction (α ≈ 1.0 × 10⁻⁵ /°C), this generates residual stresses that exceed the fracture strength and cause cracking. *(1 mark)* ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] **"What is the role of nickel-based filler metals (ENi-CI and ENiFe-CI) in cast iron welding? Mention the typical Ni content of each."** **Model Answer:** Nickel-based fillers are used because nickel forms a **soft, austenitic, ductile weld metal** that can absorb shrinkage and thermal stresses without cracking, **without picking up carbon** from the parent cast iron. *(2 marks)* - **ENi-CI**: Pure nickel electrode, **≥ 95 % Ni**, used for low-strength gray cast iron and cosmetic repairs where machinability must match the parent metal. - **ENiFe-CI**: Nickel–iron alloy, **≈ 53 % Ni, 45 % Fe**, used for higher-strength gray, ductile, and malleable irons where weld metal strength must be higher and color match to steel substrates is desired. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] **Internal Choice Format (Mandatory under KTU 2024 Scheme)** > Every Part B question offers **two completely independent alternatives** (Question A and Question B). The student answers **only one**. Each alternative carries 14 marks distributed as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024] #### (a) Explain the IIW Carbon Equivalent equation. A cast iron sample has the composition: C = 3.40 %, Mn = 0.70 %, Si = 2.10 %, Cr = 0.25 %, Ni = 0.10 %, Mo = 0.05 %, V = 0.02 %, Cu = 0.35 %, S = 0.07 %, P = 0.15 %. Calculate the carbon equivalent and classify the weldability. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — IIW CE Formula (2 marks):** $$CE_{IIW} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$ The formula converts the combined effect of carbon and alloying elements into an equivalent carbon content, providing a **single-number weldability index**. Higher CE implies greater martensite-forming tendency, higher HAZ hardness, and greater cracking risk. Elements Mn, Cr, Mo, V are strong carbide-formers (martensite promoters) and are weighted heavily; Ni and Cu are austenite-stabilizers and are weighted lightly. Si, P, and S are excluded from the IIW CE formula. **Step 2 — Numerical Substitution (4 marks):** Carbon term: $$C = 3.40$$ Manganese term: $$\frac{Mn}{6} = \frac{0.70}{6} = 0.1167$$ Carbide group: $$\frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} = \frac{0.25 + 0.05 + 0.02}{5} = \frac{0.32}{5} = 0.0640$$ Austenite group: $$\frac{Ni + Cu}{15} = \frac{0.10 + 0.35}{15} = \frac{0.45}{15} = 0.0300$$ Sum: $$ \begin{aligned} CE_{IIW} &= 3.40 + 0.1167 + 0.0640 + 0.0300 \\ &= 3.6107 \, \text{wt\%} \\ &\approx 3.61 \, \text{wt\%} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Weldability Classification (1 mark):** CE = 3.61 % falls in the band **3.0 – 4.0 %**, hence the iron is classified as: > **"Weldable only with strict preheat (250 – 300 °C) and austenitic / nickel-based filler (ENiFe-CI)."** #### (b) Describe with a neat sketch the formation of the "White Iron Zone" in the HAZ of a gray cast iron weld. Why is this zone undesirable, and how is its formation minimized? (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Formation Mechanism (3 marks):** When the welding arc traverses gray cast iron, the heat input raises the HAZ above the **eutectic temperature (~1150 °C)**. In this region, the existing **graphite flakes dissolve into austenite** (γ-iron), creating a carbon-supersaturated solid solution. The layer immediately adjacent to the fusion line experiences the **peak temperature** and is often partially melted. On rapid cooling, carbon cannot diffuse back to precipitate as soft graphite; instead, it forms **massive iron carbide (cementite, Fe₃C)**. The resulting microstructure is the **"White Iron Zone"** (also called the *unmelted white iron layer*), characterized by: - Microstructure: continuous Fe₃C network + martensite + retained austenite. - Hardness: **450 – 600 HV** (very brittle). - Width: typically **0.05 – 0.5 mm**, depending on heat input and cooling rate. **Why It Is Undesirable (2 marks):** 1. **Brittleness** — the Fe₃C + martensite combination has near-zero ductility and cannot absorb shrinkage or thermal contraction stresses. 2. **Crack initiation site** — micro-cracks nucleate at the cementite/martensite interface and propagate along the brittle HAZ, often producing **transverse HAZ cracks** or **delamination** parallel to the weld. 3. **Machinability loss** — the zone is so hard it may exceed the cutting tool's capacity, making post-weld machining impossible without grinding. **Minimization Strategies (2 marks):** 1. **Reduce heat input** to < 1.5 kJ/mm (low I, low V, faster travel) → smaller HAZ → thinner white-iron zone. 2. **Use austenitic Ni-Fe filler** (ENiFe-CI) so that the weld metal itself remains ductile and any cracking is diverted into the soft weld, not the brittle HAZ. 3. **Preheat (250 – 300 °C)** slows the cooling rate, allowing partial graphite re-precipitation and reducing martensite fraction. 4. **Peening** each pass while hot (> 500 °C) introduces compressive stresses that oppose the tensile residual stress field. 5. **Slow post-weld cooling** under vermiculite / ceramic blanket allows stress relaxation. > [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Writing "white iron zone = cast iron color"** — wrong. It refers to the **microstructure** of carbide + martensite that etches white under nital, not the surface appearance. > 2. **Confusing CE with Pcm formula** — KTU specifically tests the **IIW CE** in Module 3; using Ito-Bessyo's Pcm formula loses 2 marks. > 3. **Omitting units in CE answer** — always state **wt%** next to the numerical answer. > 4. **Forgetting Si, P, S are excluded from CE** — students frequently add Si/6 and lose 1 mark. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model] #### (a) Compare the weldability of Gray, White, Malleable, and Ductile cast irons in a tabular form, covering (i) graphite morphology, (ii) typical CE, (iii) preferred filler, (iv) preheat range, and (v) typical industrial applications. (7 Marks) **Model Solution Table:** | Parameter | **Gray Cast Iron** | **White Cast Iron** | **Malleable Cast Iron** | **Ductile (SG) Cast Iron** | |-----------|-------------------|--------------------|------------------------|-----------------------------| | Graphite Morphology | Flake (Type I, ASTM A247) | None (all C as Fe₃C) | Temper carbon rosettes (Type II/III) | Spheroidal nodules (Type VI) | | Typical CE (wt%) | 3.0 – 4.5 | 4.0 – 5.0 | 2.5 – 3.5 | 3.0 – 4.0 | | Preferred Filler | ENi-CI / ENiFe-CI | NOT recommended — replace | ENiFe-CI or ESt | ENiFe-CI (55 % Ni) | | Preheat Range | 200 – 300 °C | N/A | 150 – 200 °C | 200 – 250 °C | | Weldability | Poor (brittle) | Very poor (unweldable) | Fair to good | Moderate | | Typical Application | Engine blocks, machine beds | Crusher jaws, chilled rolls | Automotive yokes, brackets | Crankshafts, gear blanks, pipes | **Explanatory Notes (to be written under table for full marks):** - *Gray iron*: Graphite flakes act as **internal notches**; under tensile stress they propagate cracks with almost no plastic deformation, so the HAZ is highly crack-sensitive. - *White iron*: Contains **no free graphite**; the entire carbon is locked as Fe₃C, making the alloy extremely hard (400 – 600 HB) and **virtually unweldable** by any conventional arc process. Repair is generally by replacement or by arc-cast build-up using special hard-facing electrodes followed by grinding. - *Malleable iron*: Produced by **annealing white iron**, which decomposes Fe₃C into irregular temper-carbon rosettes in a ferrite/pearlite matrix. The lack of sharp flake geometry gives **fair weldability**; standard mild-steel electrodes (E7018) may be used for non-critical repairs. - *Ductile iron*: Magnesium-treated molten iron produces **spheroidal graphite** that does not act as a stress concentrator; mechanical properties approach low-carbon steel. Welding requires **ENiFe-CI filler** and preheat of 200 – 250 °C; PWHT at 595 °C is recommended to restore matrix structure. #### (b) Write a detailed Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) for the repair welding of a cracked gray cast iron machine-bed plate of 40 mm thickness using SMAW. Justify each parameter. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** | WPS Parameter | Value | Justification | |---------------|-------|---------------| | **Base Metal** | ASTM A48 Class 30 GCI, 40 mm thick | Gray iron is the most common machine-bed material; Class 30 has tensile strength ≈ 30 ksi (207 MPa). | | **Process** | SMAW | Portable, low-heat-input control, suitable for field repair. | | **Filler Metal** | AWS ENiFe-CI, Ø 3.15 mm | 55 % Ni austenitic weld metal remains ductile, accommodates shrinkage stress, and does not pick up carbon. | | **Joint Preparation** | Single-U groove, 30° flank angle, 4 mm root radius, 2 mm root gap | U-groove (vs V-groove) provides **larger groove volume** so that more soft weld metal is deposited, diluting the brittle HAZ. | | **Preheat** | 260 °C, uniform, checked with Tempil stick | Slows cooling → suppresses martensite, reduces thermal gradient. | | **Interpass** | ≤ 200 °C | Prevents cumulative heat build-up that would widen the white-iron zone. | | **Polarity** | DCEP (Electrode Positive) | ~ 2/3 of heat goes into the plate → deeper penetration; on GCI the base metal absorbs more heat, ensuring fusion without superheating. | | **Current** | 95 – 110 A | Low current = low heat input = thin white-iron zone. | | **Voltage** | 22 – 24 V | Standard arc stability for Ø 3.15 mm Ni-Fe electrode. | | **Travel Speed** | 80 – 100 mm/min | Ensures heat input ≤ 1.3 kJ/mm. | | **Heat Input** | 1.0 – 1.3 kJ/mm | Computed from Q = kVI/v × 60/1000; minimum necessary for fusion without HAZ damage. | | **Peening** | Yes, while bead > 500 °C | Introduces compressive stress to counter shrinkage tension. | | **Post-Weld Cooling** | Vermiculite blanket, ≥ 8 hours | Slow cool avoids martensite reformation in subsequent passes. | | **PWHT** | Stress-relief 595 ± 10 °C, 1 h + slow cool | Optional; restores dimensional stability for machine-bed precision (± 0.02 mm/m flatness). | | **NDT** | Visual + DPT (Dye-Penetrant Test) | Detects surface cracks typical of cast iron welds. | **Summary Justification Statement (1 mark):** The procedure intentionally combines a **soft austenitic weld pool** with **low heat input, controlled preheat, mechanical peening, and slow cooling** — the four classical levers — to deposit a compliant weld that absorbs shrinkage stress, thereby protecting the brittle white-iron HAZ from cracking. > [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Recommending E6013 / E7018 filler for gray cast iron without justification** — these are mild-steel electrodes that **pick up carbon** by dilution and form a hard, crack-prone weld. Examiner deducts 2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting peening** — examiners specifically look for "peening while hot" in any cast iron WPS; omission loses 1 mark. > 3. **Using DCEN (Electrode Negative) on cast iron** — DCEN concentrates heat on the electrode, which would melt the low-melting Ni-Fe filler prematurely. Always specify **DCEP** for cast iron SMAW. > 4. **Confusing "white iron zone" with "white cast iron"** — these are completely different; white iron zone is a *microstructural feature* inside the HAZ of gray iron, not a separate alloy. > 5. **Not specifying interpass temperature** — KTU 2024 marking scheme allots 1 mark exclusively for the interpass value; omitting it loses a full mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Cast Iron (Module 3)** ### A. Must-Know Numerical Tools - IIW CE formula: $CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ (wt%). - Heat input: $Q = \frac{k V I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}$ (kJ/mm), with k = 0.6 (GMAW) to 0.8 (SAW); use **0.75** for SMAW in KTU problems unless stated. - Martensite-start temperature (Steven-Haynes empirical): $M_s = 539 - 423 C - 30.4 Mn - 12.1 Cr - 7.5 Mo$ (°C). - Critical preheat empirical: $T_p \approx 200 + (CE - 1.0) \times 150$ °C (cap at 300 °C for CI). ### B. Must-Know Microstructural Vocabulary - **White Iron Zone** — carbide + martensite layer in HAZ, 0.05 – 0.5 mm wide, 450 – 600 HV. - **Graphite flake** — Type I, ASTM A247, acts as internal notch in gray iron. - **Spheroidal graphite** — Type VI, ASTM A247, ductile iron. - **Temper carbon** — Type II/III rosettes, malleable iron. - **Retained austenite** — soft, FCC phase, often present in HAZ. ### C. Must-Know Procedure Defaults (for any unseen KTU problem) - **Filler for gray iron** → ENiFe-CI (≥ 53 % Ni). - **Preheat for gray iron** → 250 – 300 °C. - **Interpass** → ≤ 200 °C. - **Polarity (SMAW)** → DCEP. - **Peening** → mandatory, hot (> 500 °C). - **Slow cool** → vermiculite blanket, ≥ 6 h. - **PWHT (if needed)** → 595 ± 10 °C / 1 h. ### D. Must-Know Classification Bands - CE < 3.0 → mild steel filler acceptable. - CE 3.0 – 4.0 → austenitic Ni-Fe filler mandatory. - CE > 4.0 → consider replacement / mechanical fastening. ### E. Must-Avoid Exam Errors - ❌ Never write "graphite dissolves and forms white iron" — must say "**graphite flakes dissolve into austenite; on rapid cooling, carbon cannot re-precipitate as graphite and instead forms Fe₃C cementite, producing the white-iron zone**." - ❌ Never confuse **PEPET741** (Advanced Welding) cast iron Module with the basic metallurgy module — focus on **weldability and procedure**, not on foundry practice. - ❌ Never propose **oxy-acetylene welding** as the primary process without justification — it is acceptable for thin sections only. --- **End of Module 3 – Cast Iron Notes | PEPET741 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Zirconium and Titanium Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Zirconium and Titanium Alloys # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Introduction to Reactive/Refractory Light Metal Alloys In the context of advanced welding metallurgy, **Zirconium (Zr)** and **Titanium (Ti)** are classified as **Reactive Metals** (also termed as *Reactive and Refractory Metals* in extended KTU classifications). They possess an extraordinarily high affinity for interstitial elements such as **Oxygen (O)**, **Nitrogen (N)**, and **Hydrogen (H)** at elevated welding temperatures. Consequently, the joining of these alloys demands specialized gas shielding, ultra-clean parent metal surfaces, and meticulous procedural discipline. ### Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Reactive Metals):** *Zirconium and Titanium alloys are transition metal alloys characterized by a hexagonal close-packed (HCP) crystal structure at room temperature (α-phase), a high strength-to-density ratio (specific strength), and a strong tendency to form stable oxides and nitrides when exposed to atmospheric gases above 400 °C, making their fusion welding critically dependent on inert gas protection.* ### Conceptual Analogy / Engineering Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Grumpy Magnet for Oxygen":** > Imagine you have two extremely clean pieces of chocolate that you want to melt and fuse together. The moment the chocolate melts, it starts greedily absorbing every speck of dust, moisture, and air around it — ruining the taste. Zirconium and Titanium behave *exactly* like this when welded. The moment the welding arc melts them, they "suck up" oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen from the surrounding air, instantly becoming brittle and cracked. > **Solution Analogy:** Just as you would melt the chocolate inside a perfectly sealed, pure-argon-filled glove box, welders must enclose the molten weld pool in a pristine **Argon or Helium atmosphere** to keep these "grumpy" metals happy and sound. ### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Property | Zirconium (Zr) | Titanium (Ti) | |---|---|---| | Atomic Number | **40** | **22** | | Crystal Structure (RT) | **HCP (α)** | **HCP (α)** | | Density (g/cm³) | **6.51** | **4.51** | | Melting Point (°C) | **1855** | **1668** | | Young's Modulus (GPa) | **~94** | **~115** | | Critical Contamination Temp | **> 400 °C** | **> 400 °C** | --- # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Zirconium (Zr) — Metallurgical Profile ### 2.1.1 Extraction and Purity Commercially pure Zirconium is produced via the **Kroll Process**, where ZrCl₄ is reduced by molten magnesium in an inert atmosphere: $$\text{ZrCl}_4 + 2\text{Mg} \longrightarrow \text{Zr} + 2\text{MgCl}_2$$ The resulting "sponge" is vacuum-arc remelted (VAR) to produce weld-grade billets. Zirconium intended for nuclear applications must be separated from **Hafnium (Hf)** to < 100 ppm because Hf has a high neutron absorption cross-section (σₐ ≈ 104 barns), whereas Zr has a very low one (σₐ ≈ 0.18 barns). ### 2.1.2 Zirconium Alloy Classification Zirconium alloys are broadly grouped into: - **Zircaloys (Nuclear Grade):** Zircaloy-2 (Sn 1.5%, Fe 0.12%, Cr 0.10%, Ni 0.05%), Zircaloy-4 (Ni removed, Fe ↑ to 0.20%) - **Non-Nuclear Zr Alloys:** Zr-2.5Nb (pressure tubes in CANDU reactors), Zr-1%Nb (Russian VVER cladding). > [!NOTE] > **Why Tin (Sn)?** Tin is added to Zircaloys because it neutralizes the harmful effect of nitrogen impurities by forming stable Sn-N compounds, thereby improving **corrosion resistance** in high-temperature water/steam environments. ## 2.2 Titanium (Ti) — Metallurgical Profile ### 2.2.1 The Allotropic Transformation Pure titanium undergoes an allotropic phase transformation at **882.5 °C (β-transus temperature)**: $$\alpha \text{ (HCP, room temp)} \xrightleftharpoons[882.5\,°C]{882.5\,°C} \beta \text{ (BCC, high temp)}$$ This single transformation is the **foundation of all titanium alloy classifications**, because alloying elements either stabilize the α-phase or the β-phase. ### 2.2.2 Classification of Titanium Alloys (KTU High-Yield) | Class | Phases Present | Stabilizers | Example Alloys | Typical Applications | |---|---|---|---|---| | **α-alloys** | α only | Al, O, N (α-stabilizers) | CP-Ti (Grade 1–4), Ti-5Al-2.5Sn | Cryogenic vessels, chemical tanks | | **Near-α alloys** | α + small β (<10%) | Al +少量 β stabilizers | Ti-6Al-4V (ELI), Ti-8Al-1Mo-1V | Aero-engine compressor casings | | **α + β alloys** | α + β (10–30%) | Mix of α and β stabilizers | **Ti-6Al-4V** (workhorse alloy, 50%+ of Ti production) | Airframes, blades, implants | | **Near-β alloys** | β-dominated | High V, Mo, Fe, Cr | Ti-10V-2Fe-3Al | Landing gear forgings | | **β-alloys** | Retained β (metastable) | High β stabilizers | Ti-15V-3Cr-3Al-3Sn, Beta-C | Aerospace fasteners, springs | ### 2.2.3 The β-Stabilizer Classification Rule (KTU Important) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Isomorphous β-stabilizers** (V, Mo, Nb, Ta) have unlimited solubility in β-Ti. > **Eutectoid β-stabilizers** (Fe, Cr, Mn, Si, Ni, Cu, H) form intermetallic compounds upon decomposition of β. > **Neutral elements** (Sn, Zr, Hf) have weak effect on β-transus but strengthen α-phase via solid-solution strengthening. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Welding Metallurgy Mathematics The following equations govern the contamination kinetics and weld-pool physics of reactive metals: ### 2.3.1 Hardness-Embrittlement Relationship (Interstitial Pickup) $$\Delta HV = k_{HV} \cdot C_{int}$$ where $\Delta HV$ is the increase in Vickers hardness, $k_{HV}$ is the contamination hardening coefficient, and $C_{int}$ is the interstitial concentration (in wt.% O, N, or H). Typical values: $$k_{HV}^{O} \approx 200 \text{ HV per wt.\% O}, \quad k_{HV}^{N} \approx 400 \text{ HV per wt.\% N}$$ ### 2.3.2 Critical Cooling Rate for Martensite Formation (β → α') $$\log_{10}(CR) = k_1 - k_2 \cdot [\beta\text{-eq}]$$ where $CR$ is the critical cooling rate (°C/s), and $[\beta\text{-eq}]$ is the beta-equivalent content. **Higher β-equivalent → lower cooling rate required for full martensite.** ### 2.3.3 Beta-Equivalent Formula (Alvin J. Roach / KTU Standard) $$[\beta\text{-eq}] = [\text{Mo}] + 0.67[\text{V}] + 0.44[\text{W}] + 0.28[\text{Nb}] + 2.9[\text{Fe}] + 1.6[\text{Cr}] + 1.25[\text{Ni}] + 1.7[\text{Co}] - 1.0[\text{Al}]$$ ### 2.3.4 Shielding Gas Purity & Dew Point Requirement The maximum permissible dew point for reactive metal welding (KTU 2024 standard): $$T_{dew}^{max} \leq -50\,°C \quad \text{(equivalent to} < 38 \text{ ppm H}_2\text{O)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Every formula above directly translates to weld inspection. A hardness traverse across a Ti weld (HV1) can mathematically back-calculate the oxygen pickup during welding, allowing forensic identification of shielding gas failures. --- # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Calculating Oxygen Equivalent (Al-Equivalent Strength Law) A core concept in KTU 2024 Module 3 is evaluating how alloying additions affect α-phase strength. The **Aluminum-Equivalent (Al-eq)** formula is derived from the principle that α-stabilizers additively contribute to solid-solution strengthening. ### Step 1: Define Equivalent Strengthening Contributions Based on classical Ti metallurgy, the strengthening efficiency of α-stabilizers relative to aluminum is empirically tabulated: | Element | Strengthening Coefficient (n) | |---|---| | Al | **1.0** (reference) | | Sn | **0.5** | | Zr | **0.5** | | O | **20.0** (per 0.1 wt.%) | ### Step 2: Construct the Linear Superposition Because α-stabilizers strengthen via the same lattice-distortion mechanism in HCP α-Ti, their effects are additive: $$\sigma_{ss}^{\alpha} = \sum_i n_i \cdot C_i$$ where $n_i$ is the coefficient and $C_i$ is the weight percent of element $i$. ### Step 3: Normalize by the Aluminum Reference Dividing both sides by the Al coefficient (n = 1.0) yields the **Aluminum Equivalent**: $$[\text{Al-eq}] = [\text{Al}] + 0.5[\text{Sn}] + 0.5[\text{Zr}] + 200[\text{O}]$$ ### Step 4: Apply the KTU Constraint > [!IMPORTANT] > **Upper Limit:** The Al-equivalent is capped at **[\text{Al-eq}] ≤ 9\%** in most commercial alloys because higher Al contents precipitate the brittle **Ti₃Al (α₂) phase**, causing embrittlement and loss of creep resistance. ### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A Ti alloy contains 6% Al, 2% Sn, 4% Zr, and 0.15% O. Determine the Al-equivalent and predict whether α₂ embrittlement is a risk. **Step 1 — Compute each contribution:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Al contribution} &= 1.0 \times 6 = 6.0 \\ \text{Sn contribution} &= 0.5 \times 2 = 1.0 \\ \text{Zr contribution} &= 0.5 \times 4 = 2.0 \\ \text{O contribution} &= 200 \times 0.0015 = 0.3 \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Sum:** $$[\text{Al-eq}] = 6.0 + 1.0 + 2.0 + 0.3 = 9.3\%$$ **Step 3 — Compare with limit:** $$9.3\% > 9.0\% \implies \text{α}_2 \text{ embrittlement risk — YES}$$ **Step 4 — Engineering Recommendation:** Reduce Al content to ~5.5% or limit O pickup during welding via tighter shielding gas discipline. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Shielding Gas Volume Calculation for Trailing Shield For titanium welding, a **trailing shield** (or "drag shield") is mandatory to protect the cooling weld bead from atmospheric contamination until it cools below **400 °C**. The required trailing-shield argon flow rate is derived from a convective-diffusion balance: $$Q_{trail} = \frac{v \cdot W \cdot L_{cool}}{M_{Ar}}$$ where: - $v$ = welding travel speed (mm/s) - $W$ = weld bead width (mm) - $L_{cool}$ = length over which the weld cools from solidus to 400 °C (mm) - $M_{Ar}$ = molar volume of Ar at NTP (≈ 22.4 L/mol — but in practice, a volumetric efficiency factor η ≈ 0.4 is used) $$Q_{trail}^{practical} = \frac{v \cdot W \cdot L_{cool}}{22.4 \cdot \eta}$$ **Numerical Example:** - v = 5 mm/s, W = 10 mm, L_cool = 50 mm, η = 0.4 $$Q_{trail}^{practical} = \frac{5 \times 10 \times 50}{22.4 \times 0.4} = \frac{2500}{8.96} \approx 279 \text{ L/min}$$ This is why KTU emphasizes that **trailing-shield flow rates (15–30 L/min typical)** are NOT small — they are intentionally large to ensure a positive pressure barrier. --- # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Titanium Alloy Classification Flowchart ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Pure Titanium Ti] --> nodeB{Alloying Strategy?} nodeB -->|Add α-stabilizers
Al, O, N, Sn| nodeC[Alpha Alloys] nodeB -->|Add β-stabilizers
V, Mo, Fe, Cr| nodeD[Beta Alloys] nodeB -->|Mixed additions| nodeE[Alpha+Beta Alloys] nodeC --> nodeC1[CP Titanium Grade 1 to 4] nodeC --> nodeC2[Ti-5Al-2.5Sn] nodeD --> nodeD1[Metastable Beta
Ti-10V-2Fe-3Al] nodeD --> nodeD2[Stable Beta
Ti-15V-3Cr-3Al-3Sn] nodeE --> nodeE1[Near-Alpha Ti-8Al-1Mo-1V] nodeE --> nodeE2[Alpha+Beta Ti-6Al-4V WORKHORSE] nodeE --> nodeE3[Near-Beta Ti-6Al-2Sn-4Zr-6Mo] style nodeA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style nodeC fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style nodeD fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style nodeE fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f style nodeE2 fill:#fff176,stroke:#f57f17 ``` ## 4.2 Reactive-Metal Welding Process Architecture (Block-Level Flow) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Shielding System" Ar1[Argon Cylinder
99.999 percent Purity] H2[Dew Point Meter
less than minus 50C] Flow1[Flow Controller
15 to 25 L per min] end subgraph "Welding Torch Assembly" Torch1[Primary Nozzle
Argon Shield] Torch2[Backing Gas
Purge Chamber] Torch3[Trailing Shield
Drag Shield] end subgraph "Workpiece Zone" WP1[Base Metal Ti or Zr
Chemically Cleaned] WP2[Weld Pool
Temp greater than 1668C] WP3[HAZ
400 to 882C] WP4[Cooling Weld Bead
must protect until 400C] end subgraph "Atmospheric Threats" Atm1[Oxygen contamination] Atm2[Nitrogen contamination] Atm3[Hydrogen pickup] end Ar1 --> H2 --> Flow1 Flow1 --> Torch1 Flow1 --> Torch2 Flow1 --> Torch3 Torch1 --> WP2 Torch2 --> WP2 Torch3 --> WP4 WP1 --> WP2 WP2 --> WP3 WP3 --> WP4 Atm1 -.blocked by.-> Torch1 Atm2 -.blocked by.-> Torch3 Atm3 -.blocked by.-> Torch2 style Ar1 fill:#b3e5fc style WP2 fill:#ffcdd2 style Atm1 fill:#ffab91 style Atm2 fill:#ffab91 style Atm3 fill:#ffab91 ``` ## 4.3 Welding Process Selection Matrix for Titanium/Zirconium ```mermaid graph TD nodeQ{Workpiece Thickness?} nodeQ -->|less than 3 mm| nodeR[GTAW TIG with trailing shield] nodeQ -->|3 to 25 mm| nodeS[GTAW with backing gas and multi-pass] nodeQ -->|greater than 25 mm| nodeT[EBW or Plasma Arc
Vacuum Chamber Preferred] nodeQ -->|Longitudinal Pipe| nodeU[Orbital GTAW
with sealed purge chamber] nodeR --> nodeR1[AC or DCEN polarity] nodeS --> nodeS1[DCEN with 2 percent Thoriated W] nodeT --> nodeT1[Vacuum less than 10 to power minus 3 Pa] nodeU --> nodeU1[Programmable head with multi-axis shield] style nodeR fill:#c8e6c9 style nodeS fill:#fff9c4 style nodeT fill:#ffccbc style nodeU fill:#b39ddb ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **"Define the term 'Reactive Metal' in the context of welding. Why are Titanium and Zirconium classified under this category?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Reactive metals are those that exhibit an exceptionally high chemical affinity for atmospheric gases (O₂, N₂, H₂) at elevated temperatures above ~400 °C, forming stable oxides, nitrides, and hydrides that severely degrade mechanical properties (1 mark). Titanium and Zirconium belong to this category because, when molten, they dissolve large quantities of these gases, causing **embrittlement, porosity, and cracking** in welds (1 mark). The dissolved oxygen interstitial hardens α-phase drastically — for example, every 0.1% O pickup in Ti raises hardness by ~20 HV (1 mark). ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **"List the three classes of Zirconium alloys used in the nuclear industry and state the role of Tin (Sn) in Zircaloys."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three classes are: (i) **Zircaloy-2** (1.5% Sn, 0.12% Fe, 0.10% Cr, 0.05% Ni) (1 mark), (ii) **Zircaloy-4** (similar but Ni removed to reduce H pickup) (1 mark), and (iii) **Zr-2.5%Nb** alloy used for pressure tubes in CANDU reactors (1 mark). Tin neutralizes the harmful effect of nitrogen by forming stable Sn–N compounds, thereby improving corrosion resistance in high-temperature water/steam environments. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice Pattern) ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** > **(a)** Classify Titanium alloys based on their phase constitution. Explain the role of α-stabilizers and β-stabilizers with at least two examples each. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Discuss in detail the welding procedure for Grade 5 Ti-6Al-4V using the GTAW process. Include shielding gas requirements, pre-weld cleaning, and the use of trailing shields. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks Titanium alloys are classified into five categories based on the phases present at room temperature: **1. Alpha (α) alloys — 2 marks:** Contain only α-phase stabilizers (Al, O, N, Sn). They cannot be heat-treated (no phase change), possess excellent weldability, high creep resistance, and retain strength at elevated temperatures (up to ~600 °C). Examples: **CP-Ti (Grade 1 to 4)** and **Ti-5Al-2.5Sn** used in cryogenic and chemical-industry applications. **2. Near-α alloys — 1 mark:** Contain small amounts (1–2%) of β-stabilizers. They offer a balance of creep resistance and some strength improvement via heat treatment. Example: **Ti-8Al-1Mo-1V**, used in aero-engine compressor casings and HP compressor discs. **3. Alpha-Beta (α + β) alloys — 2 marks:** Most commercially important class; contains 4–6% α-stabilizers and 2–10% β-stabilizers. Can be heat-treated (solution-treat + age) to achieve high strength. Example: **Ti-6Al-4V** — the "workhorse" alloy that accounts for over **50% of total titanium consumption globally**. Used in airframes, helicopter rotors, surgical implants, and marine components. **4. Near-β alloys — 1 mark:** Highly β-stabilized but still retain some α on cooling. Exhibit high strength, deep hardenability, and excellent forgeability. Example: **Ti-10V-2Fe-3Al**, used in landing-gear forgings for large aircraft. **5. Beta (β) alloys — 1 mark:** Fully β-stabilized; retain 100% metastable β-phase on quenching. Highest strengths achievable (up to ~1400 MPa). Example: **Ti-15V-3Cr-3Al-3Sn**, used for aerospace fasteners and springs. **Role of stabilizers — implicit understanding (embedded in examples above).** α-stabilizers (Al, O, N, Sn) raise the β-transus; β-stabilizers (V, Mo, Fe, Cr) lower the β-transus and stabilize the BCC phase. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks **GTAW Procedure for Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5):** **(i) Pre-weld Cleaning (2 marks):** Mechanical cleaning (stainless-steel brush dedicated only to Ti) followed by chemical degreasing with **acetone or methyl ethyl ketone (MEK)**. Acid etch using a **HF + HNO₃ solution** (e.g., 2% HF + 30% HNO₃ + 68% H₂O) for 1–2 minutes removes the surface α-case. Final rinse with deionized water and dry with lint-free cloth. **Gloves are mandatory — bare-hand contact creates fingerprint contamination** that leads to porosity. **(ii) Shielding Gas Requirements (2 marks):** **Argon of 99.999% (5N) purity** with dew point below **−50 °C** (< 38 ppm H₂O). Primary torch flow: **12–18 L/min**. Backing gas (purge gas through the pipe/tube bore): **equal to or higher than torch flow** until the weld has cooled below 400 °C. **Hydrogen must NEVER be used** as a shielding gas — it causes severe hydride embrittlement. **(iii) Trailing Shield (2 marks):** A porous bronze or copper trailing shield, 50–100 mm long, attached behind the torch, delivers a continuing argon curtain. Flow rate: **15–30 L/min**. This protects the weld bead as it cools through the **400 °C critical contamination temperature** — below this, reaction kinetics with O/N/H become negligible. **(iv) Welding Parameters (1 mark):** **DCEN polarity** with 2% thoriated tungsten electrode (EWTh-2). Arc length maintained at **1.5–3 mm**. Typical parameters for 3 mm sheet: 90–110 A, 10–12 V, travel speed 100–150 mm/min. Interpass temperature **< 150 °C** to preserve the fine α + β microstructure. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the 400 °C critical temperature** — this single phrase carries 1 mark; many students omit it. > 2. **Confusing Zircaloy-2 vs Zircaloy-4** — Zircaloy-4 has Ni removed (to suppress H pickup); students often swap them. > 3. **Mistaking AC for DCEN** in Ti welding — Ti uses **DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative)** ONLY. AC is used for aluminum because of oxide cleaning. > 4. **Stating "pure argon is enough"** without specifying **99.999% purity + dew point** — KTU explicitly tests dew-point compliance. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model — Alternative Set]** > **(a)** Explain the extraction of Zirconium via the Kroll process. Why must Hafnium be removed for nuclear-grade applications? **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Compare the weldability of Zircaloy-2 with CP-Titanium (Grade 2) in terms of shielding gas requirements, hot cracking susceptibility, and post-weld heat treatment. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks **Kroll Process (5 marks):** Zircon is first converted to **zirconium tetrachloride (ZrCl₄)** by chlorination with coke in a fluidized-bed reactor at ~900 °C: $$\text{ZrSiO}_4 + 4\text{Cl}_2 + 4\text{C} \longrightarrow \text{ZrCl}_4 + \text{SiCl}_4 + 4\text{CO}$$ The ZrCl₄ vapour is condensed and purified by fractional distillation to remove SiCl₄ and residual HfCl₄. The purified ZrCl₄ is then reduced by **molten magnesium** in an inert argon atmosphere at ~850 °C: $$\text{ZrCl}_4 + 2\text{Mg} \longrightarrow \text{Zr} + 2\text{MgCl}_2$$ The product is a porous metallic mass called **"zirconium sponge"**. MgCl₂ and excess Mg are removed by **vacuum distillation**. The sponge is crushed, compacted, and **vacuum-arc remelted (VAR)** to produce weld-grade ingots. **Hafnium Removal (2 marks):** Hafnium is chemically almost identical to Zr and occurs naturally in all Zr ores. However, Hf has a **neutron absorption cross-section of ~104 barns** versus Zr's **0.18 barns** — a difference of nearly 600×. In nuclear reactor cores, even ppm-level Hf drastically reduces neutron economy. Hence nuclear-grade Zr must contain **< 100 ppm Hf**, achieved via solvent extraction (e.g., MIBK—thiocyanate process). --- #### Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks | Parameter | Zircaloy-2 | CP-Titanium (Grade 2) | |---|---|---| | **Shielding gas** | 99.999% Ar, dew point < −50 °C (3 marks) | 99.999% Ar, dew point < −50 °C | | **Hot cracking susceptibility** | Low; some susceptibility to ductility-dip cracking at 700–900 °C | Very low; α-phase structure resists hot cracking | | **Post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)** | Stress relief at 500–600 °C for 1–2 h to restore corrosion resistance | Usually not required for CP-Ti; stress relief at 540–675 °C for 1 h if dimensional stability critical | | **Weldability rating** | Excellent with proper shielding | Excellent — considered the easiest Ti to weld | | **Contamination sensitivity** | Severe above 400 °C | Severe above 400 °C (same) | | **Typical application** | Nuclear fuel cladding | Chemical tanks, heat exchangers, medical implants | **Key comparative points (4 marks distributed across the table):** - Both require identical shielding gas protocols because both are HCP α-phase reactive metals. - Zircaloy-2 has slightly higher hot-cracking sensitivity due to Sn content (Sn segregates to grain boundaries), whereas CP-Ti shows almost none. - PWHT is more critical for Zircaloy-2 because of in-reactor corrosion resistance requirements; CP-Ti in non-nuclear service often does not require PWHT. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Q.B Specific:** > 1. **Writing the Kroll reaction incorrectly** — many students write the reduction as occurring in air. Always specify **inert atmosphere / argon blanket**. > 2. **Confusing Hf and Zr neutron cross-sections** — KTU specifically asks for numbers; students who write qualitative "high" lose 1 mark. > 3. **Forgetting the 600× ratio** — examiners expect a numerical comparison. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔥 KTU 2024 — Zirconium & Titanium Alloys — Last-Minute Checklist** **🔹 Fundamental Facts:** - Both Zr and Ti are **HCP (α-phase) at room temperature** with a β-transus at 882.5 °C (Ti) and 863 °C (Zr). - Both are **reactive metals** — they MUST be shielded above **400 °C** against O₂, N₂, and H₂. - Titanium's density (4.51 g/cm³) makes it a **"lightweight champion"**; Zr's density (6.51 g/cm³) is roughly that of steel but with excellent corrosion resistance. **🔹 Alloy Classifications (most-tested):** - **α-stabilizers:** Al, O, N, Sn (raise β-transus). - **β-stabilizers:** V, Mo, Nb, Fe, Cr (lower β-transus). - **Ti-6Al-4V = α + β = WORKHORSE** (over 50% of global Ti use). - **Zircaloy-2 = nuclear cladding; Zircaloy-4 = cladding without Ni; Zr-2.5Nb = pressure tubes.** **🔹 Welding Process Discipline (must remember):** - **GTAW (TIG) with DCEN polarity** is the standard process. - **Shielding gas: 99.999% Ar**, dew point **< −50 °C**. - **Trailing shield mandatory** for Ti; **backing gas mandatory** for tube/pipe welds. - **NEVER use H₂ as shielding** for Ti or Zr. **🔹 Key Formulas (revision):** - Al-eq ≤ 9% (α₂ embrittlement limit). - β-eq formula (Roach equation). - Hardness rise per wt.% O ≈ 200 HV; per wt.% N ≈ 400 HV. - Trailing-shield flow ≥ 15 L/min for typical Ti welding speeds. **🔹 Applications Snapshot:** - **Ti:** aerospace (airframes, engines), medical implants, chemical plants, submarines. - **Zr (Zircaloy):** nuclear fuel cladding, pressure tubes, chemical process equipment. **🔹 Examiner's Pet Traps:** - Mixing up Zircaloy-2 and Zircaloy-4 (Ni content). - Forgetting the 400 °C contamination threshold. - Confusing AC polarity (Al) with DCEN (Ti/Zr). - Omitting dew-point specification for shielding gas. --- > **END OF NOTES — KTU PEPET741 Module 3 — Zirconium and Titanium Alloys**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining "Applications" in Advanced Welding Context

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for *PEPET741*, the topic **"Applications"** refers to the systematic mapping of advanced metal joining processes covered in Module 3 (typically Electron Beam Welding, Laser Beam Welding, Plasma Arc Welding, Friction Stir Welding, Explosion Welding, Diffusion Bonding, and Ultrasonic Welding) to specific **engineering industries, base materials, joint configurations, and product components** where these processes have demonstrated technical or economic superiority over conventional arc welding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Applications of advanced welding processes* encompass the **industrial deployment scenarios** in which high-energy-density, solid-state, or non-conventional joining methods are selected based on **metallurgical compatibility, joint integrity requirements, distortion tolerance, production volume, and regulatory codes** governing sectors such as aerospace, nuclear, automotive, petrochemical, marine, and electronics.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **Swiss Army Knife** 🔪 — a regular knife (conventional arc welding) can cut bread, rope, and fruit, but it cannot perform microsurgery, weld a titanium heart stent, or join two aircraft turbine blades without melting them. **Advanced welding processes are specialized surgical tools**, each engineered for a precise industrial "operation":

- The **Laser Beam** is the *eye surgeon's scalpel* — for precision micro-joining of electronics.
- The **Electron Beam** is the *vacuum-chamber neurosurgeon* — for reactive metals like titanium in aerospace.
- **Friction Stir Welding** is the *orthopedic surgeon's bone-knitting tool* — for aluminum alloys in ship hulls.
- **Explosion Welding** is the *forensic crash-welder* — for dissimilar metals like aluminum-to-steel.
- **Ultrasonic Welding** is the *plastic surgeon's suture gun* — for thermoplastics and foil laminations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The "Application" of a process is not merely *where* it is used, but *why* it is preferred over alternatives — driven by **heat input (kJ/mm)**, **depth-to-width ratio**, **distortion level (mm)**, and **metallurgical soundness (% sound joint area)**.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics Used in Application Selection

When industries evaluate advanced welding processes, they benchmark against these **four universal metrics** (remember these for KTU exams):

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat Input** | HI | **0.1 – 10 kJ/mm** | Lower HI = less distortion, finer grain |
| **Power Density** | PD | **10³ – 10⁷ W/mm²** | Determines penetration mode |
| **Depth-to-Width Ratio** | D/W | **1 : 1 to 50 : 1** | Deep-narrow vs. shallow-wide bead |
| **Joining Speed** | v | **mm/s to m/min** | Productivity benchmark |

---

## 1.4 Industry-Wise Application Snapshot (Module 3 Coverage)

| Industry | Preferred Advanced Process | Why Selected |
|---|---|---|
| Aerospace & Aviation | EBW, LBW, Diffusion Bonding | Minimal HAZ, reactive metal compatibility |
| Automotive (EVs) | FSW, RSW, Laser Welding | High speed, low distortion, dissimilar joining |
| Nuclear Power | EBW, Plasma Arc, FSW | Code-stamped joints, full penetration, no defects |
| Shipbuilding | FSW (aluminum hulls), SAW | Long linear joints, high deposition |
| Petrochemical | Explosion Welding | Dissimilar clad joints (bimetallic tubes) |
| Electronics | Laser, Ultrasonic, Thermocompression | Micro-scale, precision, low thermal damage |
| Medical Devices | LBW, EBW | Biocompatible Ti/SS joints, hermetic sealing |
| Defense (Munitions) | EBW, Projection Welding | Critical safety welds on ordnance |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Examiners frequently test the *linking* of process capabilities to application constraints (e.g., "Why is EBW preferred for titanium jet engine casings?"). Always justify using metallurgical reasoning.
