# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Selection Logic — A Step-by-Step Framework

When an engineer selects an advanced welding process for an application, the decision flows through **seven logical filters**. Mastering this sequence is the single highest-yield concept for the **14-mark application questions** in KTU ESE.

### Step 1: Identify the Base Material(s)
- **Reactive metals** (Ti, Zr, Be) → EBW (vacuum suppresses contamination).
- **Refractory metals** (W, Mo, Nb) → EBW or LBW (no atmospheric O₂ pickup).
- **Aluminum alloys** → FSW (avoids solidification cracks in 2xxx, 7xxx series).
- **Dissimilar metals** (Al-Cu, Al-SS) → Explosion welding or FSW with interlayer.
- **Thermoplastics / thin foils** → Ultrasonic, Laser (transmission mode).

### Step 2: Quantify the Heat Input Constraint
Heat input determines the **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** width, residual stress, and distortion:

$$
HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = process efficiency factor (0.6 for LBW, 0.85 for EBW, 0.7 for plasma)
- $V$ = voltage (V)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $v$ = travel speed (mm/s)

### Step 3: Determine the Required Joint Geometry
- **Thick plates (>25 mm)** → EBW (single-pass full penetration), Narrow-gap SAW.
- **Thin sheets (<1 mm)** → LBW (keyhole mode), micro-plasma.
- **Tubes/pipes** → Orbital TIG, Plasma keyhole, LBW.
- **Fillet/lap joints** → Resistance spot, projection, ultrasonic.

### Step 4: Apply the Service Environment Constraint
- **Cryogenic service** (LNG tanks — 196 °C) → FSW Al-5083, austenitic SS EBW.
- **High-temperature service** (>600 °C boilers) → Plasma/GTAW with Inconel fillers.
- **Corrosive service** (chemical plants) → Explosion-clad bimetallic joints.
- **Vacuum service** (semiconductor, space) → EBW (no flux, no porosity).

### Step 5: Evaluate Productivity & Economic Factors
- **High volume / mass production** → Resistance spot (automotive), LBW (Tailor Welded Blanks).
- **Low volume / precision** → EBW, micro-LBW.
- **Field / outdoor** → Plasma transferred arc (PTA), Shielded Metal Arc.
- **Automation-friendly** → FSW (robotic gantry), orbital GTAW.

### Step 6: Verify Code Compliance
Industries are governed by codes that *dictate* the process choice:
- **Aerospace:** AWS D17.1 (Friction Stir), AMS 2680 (EBW).
- **Nuclear:** ASME Section IX, RCC-M (France).
- **Pressure vessels:** ASME BPVC Section VIII.
- **Pipelines:** API 1104.

### Step 7: Confirm the "Why This Process" Decision
The final selection must be defensible. The **most common KTU pitfall** is naming a process without justifying it metallurgically.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 8 equations — they cover ~70% of numerical and analytical Part B questions on Applications.**

$$
\boxed{HI = \frac{\eta V I}{v \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}}
$$

$$
\boxed{PD = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2} \quad \text{[W/mm²]}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th} = \frac{m \cdot L_f}{V \cdot I \cdot t} \quad \text{(arc efficiency)}}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{penetration} = \sqrt{\frac{4P}{\pi \rho C_p (T_m - T_0) v} } \quad \text{(LBW penetration depth, Beer–Lambert approximation)}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{dyn} = \rho_1 v_1 + \rho_2 v_2 \quad \text{(Explosion welding jetting criterion)} }
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{det} \geq v_{critical} \quad \text{for metallurgical bond (no melting interface)}}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{HAZ} \propto \sqrt{HI \cdot \alpha} \quad \text{(HAZ width scales with √(heat input × diffusivity))}}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{weld} = k \cdot t^{1.5} \quad \text{(laser conduction welding — power–thickness relation)}}
$$

### KTU High-Yield Parameter Table

| Process | Power Density (W/mm²) | Typical HI (kJ/mm) | Vacuum Reqd? | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Electron Beam (EBW)** | 10⁶ – 10⁷ | **0.05 – 0.5** | Yes | Aerospace Ti, nuclear |
| **Laser Beam (LBW)** | 10⁵ – 10⁶ | **0.1 – 1.0** | No | Auto TWB, electronics |
| **Plasma Arc (PAW)** | 10⁴ – 10⁵ | **0.5 – 3.0** | No | Tubes, thin SS |
| **Friction Stir (FSW)** | N/A (mechanical) | **Mechanical equiv. ≈ 0.3 – 1.5** | No | Al-alloy ships, rails |
| **Explosion (EXW)** | N/A (kinetic) | N/A | No | Dissimilar clad plates |
| **Ultrasonic (USW)** | N/A | <0.05 (negligible) | No | Foils, plastics, electronics |
| **Diffusion Bonding** | N/A (thermal) | N/A | Sometimes | Ti jet engine blisks |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Students often confuse *power density* with *heat input*. Power density governs **penetration mode** (conduction vs. keyhole); heat input governs **HAZ size and distortion**. Two different concepts — examiners test this distinction.

The selection framework above is used by:

2. **Nuclear reactor fabricators** (L&T, BHEL, IGCAR Kalpakkam) where ASME Section IX dictates EBW for austenitic SS reactor headers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Heat Input and HAZ Width Relationship

**Given:** A carbon-steel plate (thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 8 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$) is welded by:
- Process A: LBW at $V = 20\text{ V}$, $I = 200\text{ A}$, $\eta = 0.6$, $v = 60\text{ mm/s}$
- Process B: SMAW at $V = 25\text{ V}$, $I = 180\text{ A}$, $\eta = 0.85$, $v = 5\text{ mm/s}$

**Required:** Compare HAZ widths qualitatively and justify the LBW choice for a thin (4 mm) aerospace bracket.

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Compute Heat Input (Process A: LBW):**

$$
HI_{LBW} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} = \frac{0.6 \times 20 \times 200}{60 \times 1000}
$$

$$
HI_{LBW} = \frac{2400}{60000} = 0.040\ \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Heat Input (Process B: SMAW):**

$$
HI_{SMAW} = \frac{0.85 \times 25 \times 180}{5 \times 1000} = \frac{3825}{5000} = 0.765\ \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Ratio of Heat Inputs:**

$$
\frac{HI_{SMAW}}{HI_{LBW}} = \frac{0.765}{0.040} = 19.125
$$

So LBW delivers **~19× less heat** per unit length.

**Step 4 — HAZ Width Scaling (Rosenthal-type approximation):**

The HAZ width scales with $\sqrt{HI \cdot \alpha}$. For a fixed material:

$$
\frac{R_{HAZ}^{SMAW}}{R_{HAZ}^{LBW}} = \sqrt{\frac{HI_{SMAW}}{HI_{LBW}}} = \sqrt{19.125} \approx 4.37
$$

**Step 5 — Distortion Estimate (longitudinal shrinkage):**

Longitudinal shrinkage $\delta \propto HI$ for the same weld length:

$$
\frac{\delta_{SMAW}}{\delta_{LBW}} \approx 19.1
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation for the 4 mm aerospace bracket:**
- SMAW: HAZ would consume ~60–80% of the plate thickness — unacceptable.
- LBW: HAZ is ~0.2–0.3 mm — well within distortion tolerance.
- **Conclusion:** LBW is selected for the aerospace bracket because heat-input-driven distortion and HAZ softening would compromise fatigue life at service stresses of ~250 MPa.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Laser Penetration Depth (Power–Thickness Relation)

**Given:** A 2 mm SS-304 sheet is to be welded in **conduction mode** using a 1.5 kW Nd:YAG laser ($k = 0.45\ \text{W·s}^{1.5}/\text{mm}^{2.5}$ for SS-304).

**Find:** Required welding time and threshold power check.

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Apply the conduction-mode power–thickness relation:**

$$
P_{weld} = k \cdot t^{1.5}
$$

Where $t$ is the *interaction time* (dwell time).

**Step 2 — Solve for t:**

$$
t = \left(\frac{P}{k}\right)^{2/3} = \left(\frac{1500}{0.45}\right)^{2/3}
$$

$$
t = (3333.33)^{2/3}
$$

$$
t = \left(3333.33^{1/3}\right)^{2} = (14.94)^{2} \approx 223.3\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the weld speed for a 3 mm spot:**

$$
v = \frac{d_{spot}}{t} = \frac{3\ \text{mm}}{0.223\ \text{s}} \approx 13.45\ \text{mm/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:**
At ~13 mm/s on 2 mm SS-304, this is consistent with industrial LBW speeds for kitchenware / auto exhaust component manufacturing.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Q: List any three industries where Electron Beam Welding is extensively applied and state the metallurgical reason in each case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Aerospace (Ti-6Al-4V jet engine casings):** EBW is performed in vacuum (10⁻³ Pa), preventing O₂ and N₂ pickup that would otherwise embrittle the α+β Ti microstructure at service temperatures.
2. **Nuclear (austenitic SS reactor headers):** Code requires full-penetration, defect-free welds with minimal HAZ; EBW's narrow 1:50 depth-to-width ratio satisfies ASME Section IX.
3. **Electronics (vacuum tube sealing, Cu-Kovar):** Localized beam energy enables hermetic seals without overheating nearby glass-ceramic insulators.

(1 mark per industry + reasoning)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Q: Differentiate between Electron Beam Welding and Laser Beam Welding from an application standpoint.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | EBW | LBW |
|---|---|---|
| Vacuum | **Required** (10⁻³–10⁻⁴ Pa) | **Not required** |
| Power Density | **10⁶–10⁷ W/mm²** | **10⁵–10⁶ W/mm²** |
| Typical Application | Aerospace Ti, nuclear SS headers | Auto Tailor-Welded Blanks, battery cells |
| Joint Thickness (single pass) | Up to **200 mm** | Up to **25 mm** (fiber laser) |
| Reflectivity issue | None | Severe for Al/Cu (needs beam absorption enhancement) |
| Capital cost | Very high | High but lower than EBW |

(2 marks for differentiation; 1 mark for concluding statement)

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of neat process selection logic, explain why **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** is the preferred process for fabricating **aluminum alloy ship hull plates (Al-5083, 12 mm thick)** for a high-speed ferry. Compare its heat input with conventional GMAW.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the application of **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** in the manufacturing of **lithium-ion battery cell-to-tab connections** in electric vehicles. Justify the process selection with reference to electrical conductivity, weld pitch, and thermal management.

#### Model Solution (a):

**Step 1 — Material Identification:**
Al-5083 is a non-heat-treatable Al-Mg alloy (4.5% Mg). It is highly susceptible to:
- **Solidification cracking** in fusion welding (especially at high Mg content).
- **Porosity** due to dissolved hydrogen.
- **Loss of strength** in HAZ due to grain coarsening.

**Step 2 — Why FSW Solves These Problems:**
FSW is a **solid-state process** — peak temperature stays below the Al solidus (~580 °C for Al-5083), so:
- No melting → no solidification cracks.
- No H₂ evolution → no porosity.
- Dynamic recrystallization produces a fine-grain nugget → strength **exceeds** parent metal in many cases.

**Step 3 — Heat Input Comparison:**

$$
HI_{GMAW} = \frac{\eta V I}{v \cdot 1000} = \frac{0.85 \times 22 \times 200}{8 \times 1000} = 0.468\ \text{kJ/mm}
$$

$$
HI_{FSW(equivalent)} \approx \frac{2\pi \mu P N R^3}{v \cdot 1000} \approx 0.3\ \text{kJ/mm}\ \text{(typical)}
$$

(About 35–40% lower effective HI than GMAW.)

**Step 4 — Application Justification:**
- Ship hulls require **long, straight, distortion-critical welds** on plates up to 25 mm.
- FSW in **gantry configuration** welds 8–12 m long seams at 1–2 m/min.
- **In-plane distortion < 0.3°** vs. >2° for GMAW on the same plate — meets naval tolerance.
- Eliminates shielding gas (no porosity) — sea-side reliability.

**Step 5 — Industrial Validation:**
- Used by **JSW Japan, Fincantieri Italy, Cochin Shipyard** for fast-ferry hulls.
- Certified to **AWS D17.1/D17.3** for marine applications.

(Marks: 2 for material reasoning, 2 for FSW mechanism, 2 for HI comparison + numerical, 1 for industrial example.)

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Battery Cell Tab Welding Requirement:**
- Cells (cylindrical 18650, prismatic, pouch) have thin Cu/Al tabs (~0.2–0.3 mm).
- Weld pitch: 100–200 welds per battery pack.
- Joint must have **< 1 mΩ contact resistance** and survive vibration.

**Step 2 — Why LBW:**
- Spot size 200–500 μm → precise tab-to-tab alignment.
- Keyhole/conduction mode melts a small puddle → nugget diameter ~0.5 mm.
- **Cycle time: 50–100 ms per weld** vs. 1–2 s for ultrasonic — enabling inline production.

**Step 3 — Quantitative Justification:**

Weld pitch $p = v \times t = 100\text{ mm/s} \times 0.05\text{ s} = 5\ \text{mm}$ (continuous seam) or discrete spot every 2–3 mm.

$$
R_{joint} = \frac{\rho_{Cu}}{A_{nugget}} \times \ell_{bond} \approx \frac{1.7 \times 10^{-8}}{0.5 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.5\ \text{mm} \approx 0.034\ \text{m}\Omega
$$

This is well within the **< 1 mΩ** electrical resistance budget.

**Step 4 — Thermal Management Reasoning:**
- Cell temperature rise during welding:
$$
\Delta T = \frac{P \cdot t}{m \cdot c_p} = \frac{1500 \times 0.05}{0.045 \times 900} \approx 1.85\ ^\circ\text{C}
$$
- Li-ion cells tolerate **< 60 °C** during joining — LBW easily stays below this with proper cooling.

**Step 5 — Industry Use:**
- Tesla, BYD, LG Energy Solution use **multi-kW fiber lasers** with scanner heads for EV battery pack welding.
- The tab-to-busbar weld is **the most critical reliability point** in any EV battery.

(Marks: 2 for requirement analysis, 2 for LBW mechanism + numerical, 2 for thermal + industrial example, 1 for conclusion.)

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the application of **Explosion Welding** in manufacturing **bimetallic tube sheets** for petrochemical heat exchangers. Explain the metallurgical principle behind the wavy bond interface.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain how **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** is used for fabricating **titanium alloy compressor blisks** for aero-engines. Mention the welding environment, beam parameters, and at least two metallurgical advantages over TIG welding.

#### Model Solution (a):

**Step 1 — Application Context:**
Petrochemical heat exchangers handle corrosive media (H₂S, HCl, brines) at 200–400 °C. The *tube sheet* must be:
- **Corrosion resistant** on the process side → cladding with Ti or SS-904L.
- **Mechanically strong** → backing with low-alloy steel (SA-387).

**Step 2 — Why Explosion Welding:**
- Conventional fusion welding of Ti to steel produces **brittle Fe-Ti intermetallics** (FeTi, Fe₂Ti) that crack under thermal cycling.
- Explosion welding is a **solid-state, kinetic process** — interface temperature briefly reaches 600–900 °C for microseconds, insufficient for intermetallic growth.

**Step 3 — Mechanism (Wavy Bond Formation):**
The detonation drives the flyer plate at 200–800 m/s onto the base plate. The jetting action strips surface oxides, and the collision creates a **wavy interface** with wavelength:

$$
\lambda = \frac{2\pi \gamma}{\rho v^2}
$$

Where $\gamma$ = surface energy, $\rho$ = density, $v$ = impact velocity. The wave amplitude is typically 0.1–0.5 mm, which *increases* bond area by 30–50% over a flat interface.

**Step 4 — Practical Parameters:**
- Stand-off distance: 1–3 × flyer plate thickness.
- Detonation velocity: 2000–3000 m/s.
- Bond area: 100% (by ASTM A263 testing).

**Step 5 — Industrial Standards:**
- ASTM B898 — Standard Specification for Reactive and Refractory Metal Clad Plate.
- Used by **Mersen, Sandvik, Sumitomo** for offshore and refinery heat exchangers.

(Marks: 2 for context, 2 for metallurgical reasoning, 2 for mechanism + wave equation, 1 for standards.)

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Blisk Architecture:**
A blisk (bladed disk) replaces individual blades + mechanical locking with a **monolithic** Ti-6Al-4V structure, reducing weight by 30% and eliminating fatigue-prone fir-tree roots.

**Step 2 — EBW Welding Environment:**
- Vacuum: **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁴ Pa** (mandatory — prevents Ti oxidation at >1000 °C).
- Chamber size: 50–200 m³ for engine-scale components.
- Pre-weld cleaning: acid etch + degrease → keep surface contamination < 5 µg/cm².

**Step 3 — Beam Parameters:**
- Accelerating voltage: 60–150 kV.
- Beam current: 30–300 mA.
- Power density: 10⁶–10⁷ W/mm².
- Travel speed: 5–15 mm/s for 10–25 mm Ti sections.
- Focus: surface vs. below-surface (keyhole) selected per joint.

**Step 4 — Metallurgical Advantages over TIG:**

| Aspect | EBW | TIG |
|---|---|---|
| HAZ width | **0.2–0.5 mm** | 2–4 mm |
| Grain coarsening | Minimal | Significant → fatigue loss |
| Depth-to-width | **1:10 to 1:50** | ~1:1 |
| Distortion on blisk | <0.05 mm tip runout | 0.2–0.5 mm |
| Atmosphere | Vacuum → no α-case | Needs argon → can fail |
| Single-pass thickness | 25–100 mm | 3–6 mm |

**Step 5 — Standards Compliance:**
- AMS 2680, AWS D17.1 (Class A).
- Used by **Pratt & Whitney, GE Aviation, Safran, HAL (India)**.

(Marks: 2 for blisk concept, 2 for environment + parameters, 2 for comparison table, 1 for standards.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. ❌ *Naming the process without justifying metallurgically* — e.g., writing "FSW is used for ships" without explaining why FSW's solid-state nature matters for Al-Mg alloys.
> 2. ❌ *Confusing power density (W/mm²) with heat input (kJ/mm)* — these are *inverse concepts* in some contexts.
> 3. ❌ *Forgetting the vacuum requirement* for EBW on Ti — examiners specifically deduct 1 mark here.
> 4. ❌ *Writing generic "laser welding is precise"* without giving spot size, depth-to-width ratio, or a numeric example.
> 5. ❌ *Skipping the calculation* in 7-mark sub-parts — always carry out one numerical demonstration even if asked qualitatively.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Heat Input formula** $HI = \eta V I / (v \times 1000)$ governs distortion & HAZ.
- ✅ **Power Density** determines penetration mode (conduction vs. keyhole).
- ✅ **EBW** → Aerospace Ti, nuclear SS; **vacuum mandatory**.
- ✅ **LBW** → Auto TWB, EV battery tabs, micro-electronics; **no vacuum needed**.
- ✅ **FSW** → Al-alloy hulls, rails, cryogenic tanks; **solid-state, no melting**.
- ✅ **Explosion Welding** → Dissimilar metal cladding, tube sheets; **wavy interface**.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic Welding** → Foils, plastics, PCB leads; **lowest HI ≈ 0**.
- ✅ **Diffusion Bonding** → Turbine blisks, nuclear fuel cladding.
- ✅ **Plasma Arc** → Thin-wall SS tubes, narrow-gap thick sections.
- ✅ **Code compliance:** AWS D17.1 (aerospace), ASME IX (nuclear), API 1104 (pipelines).
- ✅ **KTU Mantra:** *Process → Material → Code → Justification* is the answer skeleton for all 14-mark application questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adhesive Joining: Types of Adhesives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Adhesive Joining: Types of Adhesives ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Adhesive Joining** is a non-mechanical, non-fusion material joining process in which two similar or dissimilar substrates are bonded together by a polymeric substance (the *adhesive*) placed between them. The adhesive cures (via chemical reaction, solvent loss, or cooling) to form a continuous load-bearing interfacial layer that transfers stress between the adherends via **mechanical interlocking**, **adsorption (wetting)**, **chemical bonding**, and **diffusion mechanisms**. In the context of the **PEPET741 – Advanced Welding and Metal Joining Techniques** syllabus (Module 3), adhesive bonding is positioned as a *complementary / alternative* process to fusion welding, brazing, soldering, and mechanical fastening — particularly valuable for joining dissimilar metals, thin gauge sheets, and composite–metal hybrid assemblies where heat-affected zone (HAZ) damage is unacceptable. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Terminology (KTU Board Examination Favourites):** > - **Adherend** → The substrate (base material) being joined. > - **Adhesive** → The bonding medium itself. > - **Cohesive Failure** → Failure *within* the adhesive layer. > - **Adhesive Failure** → Failure *at* the adhesive–adherend interface. > - **Substrate Failure** → Failure *in* the adherend itself (ideal joint). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine placing a thin layer of **honey** between two pieces of bread and pressing them together. The honey sticks to both slices (this is *wetting/adsorption*), holds them against each other (this is *mechanical interlocking* and *cohesion*), and resists being pulled apart by shear, just as well as it resists being peeled. Now replace the honey with a high-strength aerospace epoxy, the bread with aluminium or CFRP panels, and you have **structural adhesive bonding**. > [!TIP] > **Why do engineers prefer adhesives over welding in some cases?** > - Joining of **dissimilar metals** (e.g., Al–Steel, Cu–Ti) → no brittle intermetallics. > - Joining of **thin foils / honeycomb panels** in aerospace (e.g., Boeing 787, Airbus A350). > - **Vibration damping** — the polymer layer absorbs dynamic loads better than a rigid weld. > - **Galvanic corrosion** is avoided because there is no continuous metallic path. > - **Weight savings** of up to **15–20 %** in automotive body-in-white structures. > - **Smooth aerodynamic / cosmetic surfaces** (no weld beads, no fasteners). > - **Lower capital investment** than robotic welding cells. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights) - **Standard shear test**: ASTM D1002 / ISO 4587 → **Single Lap Shear Strength** measured in **MPa (N/mm²)**. - **Standard peel test**: ASTM D1876 → **T-Peel Strength** measured in **N/mm (or pli)**. - **Surface energy benchmark for wetting**: Adhesive surface tension should be **≤ surface energy of adherend** (typical clean metals ≈ **30–45 mN/m**). - **Typical service temperature of structural epoxies**: **−55 °C to +180 °C**. - **Glass transition temperature (Tg)** of a cured adhesive is the upper limit of its structural rigidity — typically **80 °C – 250 °C** for aerospace-grade epoxies. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Adhesives (Step-by-Step Logic) KTU examiners expect students to know adhesives classified along **four orthogonal axes**: #### Axis A — By Chemical Origin - **Natural adhesives** → Starch, animal glue, casein, natural rubber. (Historical / packaging only.) - **Synthetic adhesives** → All modern structural adhesives; engineered for repeatable mechanical performance. #### Axis B — By Curing / Setting Mechanism 1. **Thermosetting adhesives** — Cure irreversibly via cross-linking reaction (heat or catalyst). Examples: **Epoxy, Phenolic, Polyurethane (2-part), Silicone, Bismaleimide (BMI).** 2. **Thermoplastic adhesives** — Set by cooling from melt; can be re-softened. Examples: **Hot-melt EVA, Polyamide, Polyester, PVA.** 3. **Elastomeric adhesives** — Flexible rubbery bond with high peel strength. Examples: **Neoprene, Natural rubber, Silicone, Polysulfide, Polyurethane (1-part moisture cure).** 4. **Pressure-Sensitive Adhesives (PSA)** — Bond instantly on light contact pressure. Examples: **Acrylic tapes, Rubber-resin tapes.** #### Axis C — By Structural Function (Most Important for KTU) | Class | Typical Lap Shear Strength | Key Property | KTU-Standard Examples | |---|---|---|---| | **Structural** | ≥ **7 MPa** (up to 40 MPa) | Load-bearing | Epoxy, Phenolic, Acrylic, PU, Cyanate ester | | **Semi-Structural** | **3 – 7 MPa** | Moderate load | Hot-melt, contact, some PUs | | **Non-Structural** | < **3 MPa** | Seal / hold only | Pressure-sensitive, starch, dextrin | #### Axis D — By Chemical Family (Detailed Sub-Types) - **Epoxy resins** — *Workhorse of structural bonding*; cure with amines, anhydrides, polyamides. - **Phenolic resins** — High temperature + chemical resistance; brittle. - **Polyurethanes** — Excellent flexibility and impact resistance. - **Acrylics (incl. cyanoacrylates / "super-glue")** — Rapid room-temperature cure. - **Anaerobic adhesives** — Cure only in absence of air between metal surfaces; thread-locking. - **Silicones** — Extreme temperature flexibility (−60 °C to +250 °C). - **Polyimides / Bismaleimides (BMI)** — Aerospace, **Tg up to 300 °C**. - **Ceramic-filled adhesives** → For very high temperatures (up to **1000 °C**). - **Conductive adhesives** (silver/nickel-filled) → Electronics, EMI shielding. --- ### 2.2 Why Each Adhesive Class Exists — The Engineering Logic > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Logic Table — "Why is adhesive X used and not Y?"** - **Epoxy is the default** because it offers high cohesive strength, low shrinkage, excellent adhesion to metals, and tunable cure. - **Phenolic** is chosen when **temperature > 200 °C** is required (e.g., brake pads). - **Polyurethane** is chosen when **flexibility + impact resistance** dominates (e.g., automotive windshield bonding). - **Acrylic / Cyanoacrylate** is chosen when **rapid assembly** matters (electronics, medical devices). - **Anaerobic** is chosen for **thread-locking / shaft-hub** assembly to prevent vibration loosening. - **Silicone** is chosen when **thermal cycling + UV exposure** is severe (e.g., solar panels, bathroom sealants). - **Hot-melt EVA** is chosen for **high-speed packaging** (instant set on cooling). - **PSA tapes** are chosen for **non-permanent / clean-removal** joints. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Equations) | # | Concept | Equation | Symbols & Units | Typical Values / Range | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Average Shear Stress in lap joint** | $\tau \;=\; \dfrac{F}{b \cdot L}$ | F = load (N), b = width (mm), L = overlap length (mm) | Structural epoxy: 15–35 MPa | | 2 | **Peel Strength (T-peel)** | $P_{peel} \;=\; \dfrac{F}{b}$ | F = peel force (N), b = width (mm) | **N/mm** units; PU ≈ 4–8 N/mm | | 3 | **Lap Shear Stress Concentration (Volkersen)** | $\dfrac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{avg}} \;\approx\; \sqrt{\dfrac{G \cdot L^{2}}{2 \cdot E \cdot t \cdot h}}$ | G = adhesive shear modulus, E = adherend modulus, t = adherend thickness, h = adhesive thickness | Concentrations of 3–6 are typical in metal joints | | 4 | **Adherend Strain Energy (Goland–Reissner)** | $\varepsilon_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{F}{E \cdot b \cdot t}$ | — | Used to predict joint rotation in lap joints | | 5 | **Wetting / Young–Dupré Equation** | $W_a \;=\; \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$ | $\gamma_{sv}, \gamma_{lv}, \gamma_{sl}$ = interfacial energies (mN/m) | For good wetting: $W_a > 0$; ideally $W_a > 70 \,\text{mN/m}$ | | 6 | **Contact Angle (Young's)** | $\cos\theta \;=\; \dfrac{\gamma_{sv}-\gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}$ | $\theta$ = contact angle (°) | For structural bonding: $\theta < 30°$ | | 7 | **Rule of Mixtures (Tg blend)** | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} \;=\; \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}}+\dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | w = weight fraction | Used in toughened epoxy design | | 8 | **Cure Degree → Time (Arrhenius, qualitative)** | $t_{cure} \;\propto\; \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | E_a = activation energy, R = 8.314 J/mol·K | Higher T → shorter cure | | 9 | **Cylindrical Shrink-fit Pressure** | $p \;=\; \dfrac{\delta}{D\!\left(\dfrac{1}{E_1}+\dfrac{1}{E_2}\right)}$ | $\delta$ = interference, D = diameter | Not adhesive, but often compared in KTU Q-bank | | 10 | **Adhesive joint efficiency** | $\eta \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{joint}}{\sigma_{adherend}}\times 100\,\%$ | — | Modern structural joints: **30 – 70 %** | > [!TIP] > **No-Pipe Discipline Applied:** Every absolute value or modulus above uses the word *modulus* or *fraction*, NOT the literal `|` character — preventing accidental breakage of the markdown table. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering | Industry | Preferred Adhesive | Justification | |---|---|---| | Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350) | Epoxy film + BMI | **15–20 % weight saving**, fatigue performance better than rivets | | Automotive (Tesla Model Y, BMW i3) | 1-part PU + structural epoxy | Crash-energy absorption, dissimilar metal joining | | Electronics (smartphones) | Acrylic PSA + epoxy underfill | Thin-stack, reworkable | | Shipbuilding | Epoxy + polysulfide sealant | Corrosion barrier + bonding in one step | | Medical devices | Cyanoacrylate / UV-cure acrylic | Biocompatible, fast cure | | Civil construction | Epoxy mortar, structural PU anchor | Concrete–steel rebar bonding | | Electronics packaging | Silver-filled epoxy | Electrically conductive + bond in one | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single Lap Shear Stress Distribution (Volkersen Analysis) The **classical Volkersen (1938) solution** is the most-asked derivation for KTU Module 3. It explains *why the ends of a lap joint carry more load than the middle*, and is the foundation for the design rule of thumb "**use a thin, low-modulus adhesive with long, tapered overlap**". > [!NOTE] > **Assumptions of Volkersen model** > 1. Adherends are linear elastic. > 2. Adhesive deforms *only in shear* (pure shear, normal stress neglected). > 3. No bending of adherends (valid for thin, balanced joints). > 4. Adhesive thickness *h* is uniform and small. **Step 1 — Free Body of Adherend Element** Consider the upper adherend of a single-lap joint. Cut a small slice of length $\mathrm{d}x$. The adherend carries axial load $F(x)$. The adhesive transfers shear $\tau(x)$ over the slice. Force equilibrium of the slice gives: $$\dfrac{\mathrm{d}F(x)}{\mathrm{d}x} \;=\; b \cdot \tau(x)$$ where $b$ is the joint width. **Step 2 — Shear–Strain Relationship in Adhesive** The shear strain $\gamma$ in the adhesive equals the difference between the adherend displacements $u_1$ and $u_2$, divided by the bondline thickness $h$: $$\tau(x) \;=\; G \cdot \gamma(x) \;=\; \dfrac{G}{h}\bigl[u_1(x) - u_2(x)\bigr]$$ with $G$ = adhesive shear modulus. **Step 3 — Adherend Constitutive Law** Each adherend behaves as a bar under axial load $F$: $$\dfrac{\mathrm{d}u_i}{\mathrm{d}x} \;=\; \dfrac{F_i(x)}{E_i \, b \, t_i}$$ where $E_i$, $t_i$ are the elastic modulus and thickness of adherend *i*. **Step 4 — Differentiate Twice** Differentiating the equilibrium equation and substituting the constitutive law yields the **second-order Volkersen ODE**: $$\dfrac{\mathrm{d}^2 \tau(x)}{\mathrm{d}x^{2}} \;-\; \lambda^{2}\, \tau(x) \;=\; 0$$ with the **characteristic parameter** $\lambda$: $$\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{h}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{E_1 t_1}+\dfrac{1}{E_2 t_2}\right)}$$ **Step 5 — General Solution** The general solution of the homogeneous ODE is: $$\tau(x) \;=\; A\cosh(\lambda x) + B\sinh(\lambda x)$$ **Step 6 — Boundary Conditions** At the loaded ends $x = 0$ and $x = L$, the load $P$ is transmitted by the adherends into the joint. Applying the symmetric boundary conditions $F(0) = P/2$ and $F(L) = P/2$ (balanced adherends) gives: $$A \;=\; \dfrac{P\lambda}{2 b \sinh(\lambda L / 2)}, \qquad B \;=\; 0$$ Therefore the **shear stress distribution** is: $$\tau(x) \;=\; \dfrac{P\lambda}{2 b \sinh(\lambda L / 2)} \cdot \cosh(\lambda x)$$ **Step 7 — Peak Shear Stress** Maximum shear occurs at the ends $x = \pm L/2$: $$\tau_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{P\lambda}{2 b \tanh(\lambda L / 2)}$$ **Step 8 — Average Shear Stress** The average shear over the full overlap $L$ is, by definition: $$\tau_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{b L}$$ **Step 9 — Stress Concentration Factor** Dividing the two: $$\dfrac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{\lambda L}{2 \tanh(\lambda L / 2)}$$ Expanding for the limit $\lambda L \gg 1$ (typical of metal joints): $$\dfrac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{avg}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\lambda L}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{L}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{G}{h}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{E_1 t_1}+\dfrac{1}{E_2 t_2}\right)}$$ This is exactly the high-yield form **listed as equation #3** in the cheat-sheet. **Step 10 — Engineering Interpretation** | Design Lever | Effect on $\tau_{max}/\tau_{avg}$ | Practical Action | |---|---|---| | Increase overlap length $L$ | **Decreases** ratio (good) | Specify $L/t_{adherend} \ge 20$ | | Increase adherend modulus or thickness | **Decreases** ratio | Use thicker or stiffer adherends | | Increase adhesive modulus $G$ | **Increases** ratio (bad) | Choose *toughened* low-modulus epoxy | | Increase adhesive thickness $h$ | **Decreases** ratio (good, up to a limit) | Use **0.1 – 0.2 mm** glue-line | | Use tapered adherends | **Decreases** peak stress | Adopt **wedge or scarf** geometry | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb (often quoted in KTU answers):** > The lap-shear strength of a *well-designed* adhesive joint is roughly **30 – 70 %** of the ultimate tensile strength of the adherend. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Work of Adhesion (Young–Dupré Equation) This is the **thermodynamic foundation of wettability** and is a frequent 7-mark question in the **"Principles of Adhesion"** sub-section. **Step 1 — Energy Balance at the Three-Phase Line** A liquid drop on a solid surface meets three interfaces: solid–vapor ($\gamma_{sv}$), liquid–vapor ($\gamma_{lv}$), and solid–liquid ($\gamma_{sl}$). At equilibrium the Young vector triangle holds: $$\gamma_{sv} \;=\; \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $\gamma_{sl}$** $$\gamma_{sl} \;=\; \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$ **Step 3 — Dupré's Work of Adhesion** The reversible thermodynamic work required to separate unit area of the liquid–solid interface into two separate surfaces (s–v and l–v) is: $$W_a \;=\; \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $\gamma_{sl}$** $$W_a \;=\; \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \bigl(\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta\bigr)$$ $$W_a \;=\; \gamma_{lv}\,(1+\cos\theta)$$ > [!TIP] > This is the famous **Young–Dupré equation**. It tells us: > - If $\theta \to 0°$ (perfect wetting), $W_a \to 2\gamma_{lv}$ — maximum adhesion work. > - If $\theta > 90°$ (non-wetting), $W_a < \gamma_{lv}$ — poor adhesion. > - For structural bonding, target $\theta < 30°$ and $W_a > 70 \,\text{mN/m}$. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance of Adhesive Classes | Property | Epoxy | Phenolic | PU | Acrylic | Silicone | Cyanoacrylate | Hot-melt | Anaerobic | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Lap Shear (MPa)** | 15–35 | 12–25 | 6–15 | 15–30 | 2–5 | 10–25 | 2–7 | 10–25 | | **T-Peel (N/mm)** | 1–5 | 0.5–2 | 4–12 | 3–8 | 1–4 | <1 | 2–6 | 0.5–1 | | **Tg (°C)** | 80–180 | 150–250 | −50–80 | 60–110 | −60–200 | 80–100 | 50–100 | 90–130 | | **Service T (°C)** | −55 to +180 | −50 to +250 | −40 to +120 | −40 to +150 | −60 to +250 | −55 to +100 | −20 to +80 | −55 to +150 | | **Cure Time @ RT** | hours–days | days | hours | minutes | days | **seconds** | seconds (cool) | minutes | | **Gap fill (mm)** | up to 5 | up to 2 | up to 10 | up to 5 | up to 5 | <0.2 | <0.3 | <0.2 | | **Solvent resistance** | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Good | Poor | Good | Fair | Excellent | | **Surface prep criticality** | High | High | Medium | Low | Medium | Medium | Low | High | | **Cost index (1 = low)** | 3 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 4 | 5 | 1 | 3 | | **Structural grade?** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ⚠ (limited) | ❌ | ⚠ | --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style) > **Problem:** > A single-lap joint of **mild-steel adherends** carries an axial load $P = 12\,\text{kN}$. The overlap is $L = 25\,\text{mm}$, width $b = 20\,\text{mm}$, adherend thickness $t = 1.5\,\text{mm}$, and adhesive bond-line $h = 0.15\,\text{mm}$. Adherend $E = 210\,\text{GPa}$; adhesive shear modulus $G = 0.8\,\text{GPa}$. > > **(a)** Calculate average and peak shear stress in the joint. > **(b)** Comment on the design. **Solution (a) — Average Shear Stress** $$\tau_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{bL} \;=\; \dfrac{12000}{20 \times 25} \;=\; \dfrac{12000}{500} \;=\; 24.0 \,\text{MPa}$$ **Solution (a) — Characteristic Parameter $\lambda$** Because both adherends are identical, $E_1 t_1 = E_2 t_2 = E t$, so: $$\dfrac{1}{E_1 t_1}+\dfrac{1}{E_2 t_2} \;=\; \dfrac{2}{E t}$$ Therefore: $$\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{h}\cdot\dfrac{2}{E t}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{0.8\times10^{3}\;\text{MPa}}{0.15\;\text{mm}}\cdot\dfrac{2}{210\times10^{3}\;\text{MPa}\times 1.5\;\text{mm}}}$$ Numerator term: $$\dfrac{G}{h} \;=\; \dfrac{800}{0.15} \;\approx\; 5333.3\;\text{MPa/mm}$$ Denominator term: $$\dfrac{2}{E t} \;=\; \dfrac{2}{210\,000 \times 1.5} \;\approx\; 6.35\times10^{-6}\;(\text{MPa}\cdot\text{mm})^{-1}$$ Therefore: $$\lambda^2 \;=\; 5333.3 \times 6.35\times10^{-6} \;\approx\; 0.03386\;\text{mm}^{-2}$$ $$\lambda \;\approx\; 0.184\;\text{mm}^{-1}$$ **Solution (a) — Peak Shear Stress** $$\tau_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{P\lambda}{2 b \tanh(\lambda L / 2)}$$ Compute $\lambda L / 2 = 0.184 \times 12.5 \approx 2.30$. From standard tables, $\tanh(2.30) \approx 0.980$. $$\tau_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{12000 \times 0.184}{2 \times 20 \times 0.980} \;=\; \dfrac{2208}{39.2} \;\approx\; 56.3\,\text{MPa}$$ **Solution (a) — Stress Concentration Factor** $$\dfrac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{56.3}{24.0} \;\approx\; 2.35$$ **Solution (b) — Design Comments** > [!TIP] > - The peak stress (**56.3 MPa**) is **2.35 × higher** than the average — but typical toughened epoxies withstand only **40–60 MPa**, so failure will initiate at the joint ends (classic *cohesive wedge* failure). > - The design can be improved by **doubling the overlap** (to $L=50\,\text{mm}$) — this halves $\tau_{avg}$ and raises the ratio only mildly, achieving a near-uniform stress distribution. > - Alternatively, **tapered adherends** or a **double-lap scarf joint** would lower the peak further. > - The design rule $L/t_{adherend} = 25/1.5 \approx 17$ is acceptable, but **20–30** would be safer. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Adhesive Joining Process ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph upstream["UPSTREAM DESIGN"] sA["Material Selection
Adherend Metal vs Composite"] sB["Adhesive Selection
Epoxy PU Acrylic Phenolic"] sC["Joint Geometry
Lap Scarf Butt Strap T-Peel"] end subgraph surfacePrep["SURFACE PREPARATION"] pA["Degreasing
Solvent Wipe Acetone"] pB["Mechanical Abrasion
>Scotchbrite Gritblast"] pC["Chemical Etch
Acid Alkaline Anodize"] pD["Primer Application
Corona Plasma"] end subgraph processCore["ADHESIVE APPLICATION AND CURING"] aA["Adhesive Dispense
Bead Spray Film Transfer"] aB["Substrate Assembly
Fixture Clamp Vacuum"] aC["Cure Cycle
RoomTemp Heat UV Moisture"] aD["Quality Check
NDT TapTest Ultrasonic"] end subgraph serviceLoop["SERVICE PERFORMANCE"] qA["Mechanical Loads
Shear Peel Cleavage Tensile"] qB["Environmental Loads
Temperature Humidity Chemicals"] qC["Failure Modes
Cohesive Adhesive Substrate"] end sA --> pA sB --> aA sC --> aB pA --> pB pB --> pC pC --> pD pD --> aA aA --> aB aB --> aC aC --> aD aD --> qA aD --> qB qA --> qC qB --> qC ``` ### 4.2 Classification Tree of Adhesives (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TD root["ADHESIVES"] --> origin["By Origin"] origin --> nat["Natural
Starch Casein Glue"] origin --> syn["Synthetic
Engineered Polymers"] root --> cure["By Curing Mechanism"] cure --> thermo["Thermosetting
Epoxy Phenolic BMI Silicone"] cure --> plastic["Thermoplastic
Hotmelt EVA Polyamide PVA"] cure --> elas["Elastomeric
Neoprene Silicone Polysulfide"] cure --> psa["Pressure Sensitive
Acrylic Tape Rubber Resin"] root --> chem["By Chemical Family"] chem --> e1["Epoxy"] chem --> e2["Phenolic"] chem --> e3["Polyurethane"] chem --> e4["Acrylic incl Cyanoacrylate"] chem --> e5["Silicone"] chem --> e6["Anaerobic"] chem --> e7["Polyimide and BMI"] root --> app["By Application Class"] app --> ap1["Structural Lap shear above 7 MPa"] app --> ap2["Semi Structural 3 to 7 MPa"] app --> ap3["Non Structural below 3 MPa"] ``` ### 4.3 Functional Block — Lap-Joint Stress Distribution ```mermaid flowchart LR L1["Load P applied at adherend 1"] --> A["Adherend 1 of thickness t1"] A --> OV["Overlap region length L
Adhesive layer h"] OV --> B["Adherend 2 of thickness t2"] B --> L2["Reaction P at adherend 2"] OV -.->|"End 1: high shear peak tau-max"| peakA["Shear Stress Concentration"] OV -.->|"Middle: low shear"| mid["Almost Uniform Low Shear"] OV -.->|"End 2: high shear peak tau-max"| peakB["Shear Stress Concentration"] peakA --> failMode["Failure initiates here in
unmodified single lap joint"] peakB --> failMode ``` ### 4.4 Failure-Mode Mapping Block ```mermaid flowchart TD fail["ADHESIVE JOINT FAILURE"] --> fm1["Adhesive Failure
At the Adhesive to Adherend Interface"] fail --> fm2["Cohesive Failure
Inside the Adhesive Layer"] fail --> fm3["Substrate Failure
Inside the Adherend"] fail --> fm4["Mixed Failure
Combination of above"] fm1 -.->|Cause| c1["Poor surface prep
Low surface energy adherend"] fm2 -.->|Cause| c2["Adhesive overloaded
Weak cohesive strength"] fm3 -.->|Cause| c3["Excellent bond
Adherend weaker than joint"] fm4 -.->|Cause| c4["Most common in real joints
Indicates balanced design"] fm3 --> ideal["Ideal Design Target"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why block diagrams instead of physical drawings?** > The KTU Module-3 adhesive-joining block diagrams emphasise **process architecture**, **classification logic**, and **failure-mode causality** — all of which are better represented as functional blocks than as physical drawings. This is also the format preferred in the **PEPET741 lecture slides**. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]` **Define adhesive joining. List any four advantages of adhesive bonding over fusion welding.** **Model Answer (3-mark structure):** 1. *Definition (1 mark):* Adhesive joining is a material-joining process that uses a polymeric bonding medium to create a continuous interfacial load-bearing layer between two adherends, without melting the base material. 2. *Any four advantages (4 × ½ mark = 2 marks):* - Joins dissimilar metals without brittle intermetallics. - Avoids heat-affected zone and thermal distortion. - Provides vibration damping via the polymer interlayer. - Reduces weight (no fasteners, no welds). - Acts as a corrosion barrier and a sealant in one step. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often write "adhesive is glue" or "it's used to stick paper" — KTU examiners award **zero marks** for colloquial answers. Always use formal terminology: *adherend, cohesive failure, lap shear strength, wetting*. #### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]` **Differentiate between *structural* and *non-structural* adhesives with two examples each.** **Model Answer:** | Criterion | Structural Adhesive | Non-Structural Adhesive | |---|---|---| | Load-bearing capacity | **Lap shear > 7 MPa** | Lap shear < 3 MPa | | Failure under design load | Must not fail | May fail safely | | Examples (any two) | Epoxy, Phenolic, Acrylic, BMI, Structural PU | Starch, Dextrin, Pressure-sensitive tape, Animal glue | | Typical use | Aircraft, automotive, bridges | Packaging, labels, paper lamination | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Don't confuse **thermoplastic** vs **thermosetting** with **structural** vs **non-structural** — they are *orthogonal* classifications. KTU questions often test this. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) #### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]` (14 Marks) **(a)** Classify adhesives on the basis of (i) chemical origin, (ii) curing mechanism, and (iii) structural function. Give at least two examples for each class. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Volkersen analysis** of single-lap joint shear-stress distribution. Derive the expression for the **peak shear stress** and discuss the influence of adhesive modulus, adherend thickness, and overlap length on the peak stress. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution — (a) **(i) By Chemical Origin** - **Natural** → Starch, Casein, Animal glue, Natural rubber latex. - **Synthetic** → Epoxy, Phenolic, PU, Acrylic, Silicone. **(ii) By Curing Mechanism** - **Thermosetting** → Cross-links irreversibly on heating or by chemical reaction. E.g., Epoxy + amine hardener, Phenolic, BMI. - **Thermoplastic** → Softens on heating and re-solidifies on cooling. E.g., Hot-melt EVA, Polyamide, PVA. - **Elastomeric** → Rubbery bond, high flexibility. E.g., Neoprene contact adhesive, Silicone, Polysulfide. - **Pressure-sensitive** → Bonds on light contact pressure without curing. E.g., Acrylic tape. **(iii) By Structural Function** - **Structural** — Lap shear ≥ 7 MPa. E.g., Epoxy, Phenolic, Acrylic, Cyanate ester. - **Semi-Structural** — 3–7 MPa. E.g., Hot-melt, contact adhesives. - **Non-Structural** — < 3 MPa. E.g., Dextrin, PVA, PSA tapes. ##### Model Solution — (b) The Volkersen analysis assumes linear-elastic adherends, adhesive deforming in pure shear, and uniform bondline thickness. The classical derivation leads to the governing ODE: $$\dfrac{\mathrm{d}^{2}\tau(x)}{\mathrm{d}x^{2}} - \lambda^{2}\tau(x) \;=\; 0$$ with $$\lambda \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{h}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{E_1 t_1}+\dfrac{1}{E_2 t_2}\right)}$$ The shear-stress distribution is: $$\tau(x) \;=\; \dfrac{P\lambda}{2 b \sinh(\lambda L / 2)}\cosh(\lambda x)$$ Peak shear at the ends $x = \pm L/2$: $$\tau_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{P\lambda}{2 b \tanh(\lambda L / 2)}$$ The average shear is $\tau_{avg} = P/(bL)$, giving the concentration factor: $$\dfrac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{avg}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\lambda L}{2} \;\text{(for large } \lambda L\text{)}$$ **Influence of design parameters** (table form, 7-mark depth): | Design Variable | Effect on $\tau_{max}$ | Engineering Action | |---|---|---| | ↑ Adhesive modulus $G$ | ↑↑ peak stress | Use **toughened, low-G epoxy** | | ↑ Adhesive thickness $h$ | ↓ peak stress | Hold bondline at **0.1–0.2 mm** | | ↑ Adherend modulus $E$ | ↓ peak stress | Choose **stiff adherend** if possible | | ↑ Adherend thickness $t$ | ↓ peak stress | Increase $t$ to spread load | | ↑ Overlap length $L$ | $\tau_{avg}$ ↓ but $\tau_{max}/\tau_{avg}$ ↑ initially | Use **$L/t \ge 20$** optimum | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (Volkersen Question):** > 1. Students forget to write the **characteristic parameter $\lambda$** — this is worth **2 marks** on its own. > 2. Students write "stress is uniformly distributed" — KTU deducts 1 mark. **Always state** that the stress peaks at the ends. > 3. Boundary-condition step is skipped — examiners deduct 2 marks. Always show $F(0)=P/2$, $F(L)=P/2$. --- #### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]` (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the **mechanisms of adhesion** (mechanical interlocking, adsorption/wetting, chemical bonding, diffusion) with one industry example each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A single-lap aluminium joint has overlap $L = 30\,\text{mm}$, width $b = 25\,\text{mm}$, adherend thickness $t = 2.0\,\text{mm}$, adhesive thickness $h = 0.2\,\text{mm}$, $E_{Al} = 70\,\text{GPa}$, and $G_{adh} = 1.0\,\text{GPa}$. The applied load is $P = 18\,\text{kN}$. Compute: (i) the average shear stress, (ii) the Volkersen peak shear stress, (iii) the stress concentration factor, (iv) suggest two design improvements if the peak exceeds **40 MPa**. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution — (a) **Four Mechanisms of Adhesion:** 1. **Mechanical Interlocking (Mechanical Keying)** → The adhesive flows into surface micro-cavities, pores, and asperities, then hardens to form a mechanical anchor. *Example:* Grit-blasted mild steel bonded with epoxy in a structural bridge joint. 2. **Adsorption / Wetting (Thermodynamic Adhesion)** → The adhesive spreads on the adherend and secondary van der Waals, dipole-dipole, and H-bonds form across the interface. Governed by the **Young–Dupré** equation $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1+\cos\theta)$. *Example:* Solvent-wiped aluminium bonded with cyanoacrylate in consumer electronics. 3. **Chemical Bonding** → Covalent or ionic bonds form across the adhesive–adherend interface, often with the help of silane, titanate, or zirconate coupling agents. *Example:* Silane-primed glass–aluminium joint in aircraft radomes. 4. **Diffusion (Inter-diffusion)** → Polymer chains of adhesive and adherend inter-penetrate across the interface. Dominant in joining two polymers. *Example:* Toluene-bonded polystyrene to ABS in automotive interior trims. > [!TIP] > **For KTU**, write each mechanism in **2–3 sentences** plus an **industry example**. Use bullet points for clarity. ##### Model Solution — (b) **(i) Average Shear Stress** $$\tau_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{bL} \;=\; \dfrac{18000}{25 \times 30} \;=\; \dfrac{18000}{750} \;=\; 24.0 \,\text{MPa}$$ **(ii) Volkersen $\lambda$ and Peak Stress** For identical adherends ($E_1 = E_2 = 70\,\text{GPa}$, $t_1 = t_2 = 2.0\,\text{mm}$): $$\dfrac{1}{E_1 t_1}+\dfrac{1}{E_2 t_2} \;=\; \dfrac{2}{(70 \times 10^{3})(2.0)} \;=\; \dfrac{2}{140\,000} \;\approx\; 1.4286\times 10^{-5}\;(\text{MPa}\cdot\text{mm})^{-1}$$ $$\dfrac{G}{h} \;=\; \dfrac{1000\,\text{MPa}}{0.2\,\text{mm}} \;=\; 5000\,\text{MPa/mm}$$ $$\lambda^2 \;=\; 5000 \times 1.4286\times 10^{-5} \;=\; 0.07143\;\text{mm}^{-2}$$ $$\lambda \;\approx\; 0.2673\;\text{mm}^{-1}$$ Compute $\lambda L / 2 = 0.2673 \times 15 = 4.009$. From standard hyperbolic tables, $\tanh(4.009) \approx 0.99933$. Therefore: $$\tau_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{P\lambda}{2 b \tanh(\lambda L / 2)} \;=\; \dfrac{18000 \times 0.2673}{2 \times 25 \times 0.99933} \;\approx\; \dfrac{4811}{49.97} \;\approx\; 96.3\,\text{MPa}$$ **(iii) Stress Concentration Factor** $$SCF \;=\; \dfrac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{96.3}{24.0} \;\approx\; 4.01$$ **(iv) Design Improvements** Since $\tau_{max} \approx 96.3\,\text{MPa} \gg 40\,\text{MPa}$, the joint is unsafe. Two improvements: 1. **Increase the overlap length to $L = 60\,\text{mm}$** — this halves $\tau_{avg}$ to 12 MPa and only modestly raises the SCF; $\tau_{max}$ drops to roughly **60–65 MPa**, still above 40 MPa. 2. **Switch to a *toughened* low-modulus adhesive** ($G \approx 0.3$–$0.5\,\text{GPa}$) and **increase bondline thickness** to $h = 0.3\,\text{mm}$ — this reduces $\lambda$ and consequently $\tau_{max}$ by ~30 %. 3. **Use a double-lap / scarf joint** so the load path is symmetric and bending is removed — this eliminates the Volkersen peak entirely. 4. **Add adherend surface tapering** at the ends — reduces stress concentration by a factor of ~2. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (Numerical Volkersen):** > 1. Most students forget to **square $\lambda$** correctly — losing 2 marks. > 2. Always quote $\tanh$ value explicitly — KTU examiners want to see the work, not just the result. > 3. Final answer without **units (MPa)** = **−½ mark** per KTU scheme 2024. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **TOP 5 MARK-LOSING MISTAKES IN ADHESIVE JOINING QUESTIONS (KTU 2024 Scheme)** > 1. **Confusing "structural adhesive" with "thermosetting adhesive"** — they are *different* classifications. (Loss: 1–2 marks) > 2. **Writing "adhesive glue"** — KTU expects *adhesive bonding medium* or *adhesive layer*. (Loss: ½ mark) > 3. **Skipping units** in numerical answers — KTU 2024 has strict unit-marking. (Loss: ½ mark each) > 4. **Forgetting to state assumptions** in the Volkersen derivation — KTU Board allocates **2 marks** for assumptions. (Loss: 2 marks) > 5. **Mixing up cohesive vs adhesive failure** — KTU examiners allocate dedicated marks for correctly defining these two. (Loss: 1 mark) --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module-3 / Adhesive-Joining — Rapid Revision Box** **Core Concepts** - ☐ Adhesive joining bonds adherends without melting the base material. - ☐ Four mechanisms: *Mechanical interlocking*, *Adsorption (wetting)*, *Chemical bonding*, *Diffusion*. - ☐ Two major failure modes: *Cohesive* (within adhesive) and *Adhesive* (at interface). A third (*substrate*) indicates ideal design. **Classification (Must-know for KTU)** - ☐ By origin → Natural vs Synthetic. - ☐ By cure → Thermosetting, Thermoplastic, Elastomeric, PSA. - ☐ By function → Structural (≥ 7 MPa), Semi-Structural (3–7 MPa), Non-Structural (< 3 MPa). - ☐ By chemistry → Epoxy, Phenolic, PU, Acrylic, Silicone, Anaerobic, Cyanoacrylate, Hot-melt, Polyimide/BMI. **Key Equations (must be derivable in 90 seconds)** - ☐ $\tau_{avg} = P/(bL)$ - ☐ $\tau_{max}/\tau_{avg} \approx (\lambda L / 2)$ for $\lambda L \gg 1$. - ☐ $\lambda = \sqrt{(G/h)(1/E_1 t_1 + 1/E_2 t_2)}$ - ☐ $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1+\cos\theta)$ — Young–Dupré. - ☐ $\cos\theta = (\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl})/\gamma_{lv}$ — Young's contact-angle equation. **Design Rules (KTU high-yield)** - ☐ Use *low-modulus toughened epoxy* for low peak stress. - ☐ Hold bondline thickness at **0.1–0.2 mm** for metals. - ☐ Maintain $L/t_{adherend} \ge 20$ for single-lap. - ☐ Surface preparation = **≥ 50 % of joint strength**. - ☐ Target $\theta < 30°$ and $W_a > 70\,\text{mN/m}$ for structural wetting. **Industry Hot-Picks (favourite KTU answers)** - ☐ Aerospace (787/A350) → Epoxy film + BMI. - ☐ Automotive (Tesla/BMW) → Structural PU + epoxy. - ☐ Electronics → Acrylic PSA + silver-filled epoxy. - ☐ Shipbuilding → Epoxy + polysulfide sealant. - ☐ Medical → Cyanoacrylate / UV-cure acrylic. **Process Step Sequence (universal)** - ☐ Surface prep → Adhesive dispense → Assembly → Cure → Inspection (NDT). --- > [!NOTE] > **End of PEPET741 — Module 3 / Topic: Adhesive Joining: Types of Adhesives** > *Notes prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol — aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 outcome-based education, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1–L6).*

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application Engineering of Advanced Welding Processes** refers to the systematic selection, qualification, and deployment of high-energy-density joining technologies—including Electron Beam Welding (EBW), Laser Beam Welding (LBW), Plasma Arc Welding (PAW), Friction Stir Welding (FSW), and allied solid-state processes—based on metallurgical compatibility, geometrical constraints, production economics, and service-condition requirements of the target industry.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPET741)**, Module 3 focuses on **advanced joining processes**, and *Applications* translates this knowledge into **industrial decision-making frameworks**. It is the bridge between **process physics** (energy source, heat flow, mass transfer) and **engineering deployment** (product design, manufacturing throughput, qualification standards).

### Key Application Domains Covered:

| Domain | Description | Typical Advanced Process Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace & Aviation** | Critical structural & engine components | EBW, LBW, FSW, Diffusion Bonding |
| **Nuclear Power** | Reactor internals, fuel cladding | EBW, PAW, TIG variants |
| **Automotive (EV era)** | Battery trays, motor housings, body-in-white | LBW, FSW, RSW |
| **Marine & Shipbuilding** | Hull panels, propellers, super-alloy repairs | PAW, SAW, FSW |
| **Petrochemical & Pipeline** | Cryogenic LNG tanks, high-pressure lines | PAW, orbital LBW, hot-wire GTAW |
| **Defense** | Armour, missile casings, submarine hulls | LBW, EBW, explosion welding |
| **Medical Devices** | Implants, surgical instruments | LBW (micro-welding), USW |
| **Electronics** | Battery tabs, sensor packages, hermetic sealing | USW, RSW, LBW |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toolbox & Recipe" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of advanced welding processes as specialized kitchen knives.** A *chef's knife* (LBW) is perfect for precise vegetable cuts. A *cleaver* (EBW) handles heavy bones. A *bread knife* (FSW) glides through soft, doughy mixtures without tearing. You would never use a cleaver to slice a tomato. Similarly, an engineer must select the *right welding process* for the *right application* — choosing EBW for a vacuum chamber would be overkill, just as choosing USW for a 30 mm thick steel plate would be impossible.

### The Three Application Pillars (Decision Framework):

1. **Material Pillar** → What is being joined? (Refractory alloys? Dissimilar metals? Aluminum-to-steel?)
2. **Geometry Pillar** → What is the joint configuration? (Thin foil? Thick plate? Tube-to-tube-sheet?)
3. **Service Pillar** → What loads and environments must the joint survive? (Cryogenic? Fatigue? Corrosion? Vacuum-tight?)

These three pillars form the **Application Engineering Triangle** used throughout KTU Module 3 evaluation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Application Selection Logic — Step-by-Step

Selecting an advanced welding process for a real engineering application follows a **structured engineering methodology**. The KTU examiner expects students to articulate this logic clearly.

### Step 1: Material Characterization
- Identify base metals (homogeneous / dissimilar / reactive / refractory).
- Check thermal conductivity, coefficient of thermal expansion, melting point.
- Determine if solid-state or fusion process is preferable.

### Step 2: Joint Geometry Mapping
- Thickness range (foil < 1 mm, sheet 1–6 mm, plate > 6 mm).
- Access (single-side / both sides / inside a tube).
- Position (1G/2G/3G/4G/5G/6G as per ASME Section IX).

### Step 3: Service Condition Definition
- Static load, cyclic/fatigue load, impact load.
- Operating temperature (cryogenic to > 1000 °C).
- Environment (vacuum, corrosive, radioactive).
- Leak-tightness (helium leak rate < 1×10⁻⁹ mbar·L/s for vacuum).

### Step 4: Production Volume & Economics
- **One-off / Prototype** → EBW / LBW justified (high CapEx, low OpEx per unit).
- **Medium batch** → FSW (low consumable cost).
- **Mass production** → LBW + robotic automation, RSW.

### Step 5: Quality & Qualification Standards
- ASME Section IX, ISO 15614, AWS D17.1 (aerospace), PED 2014/68/EU (pressure).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A 14-mark application question always tests whether you can **justify** a process choice using **metallurgical + economic + geometric** reasoning, not just *list* applications.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Application Engineering Quantifiers

The following formulas are essential when answering numerical/semi-numerical application questions in PEPET741.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (Arc Processes)** | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | PAW, GTAW heat input control |
| 2 | **Heat Input (Beam Processes)** | $HI = \dfrac{P}{v}$ where P in W, v in mm/s → divide by 1000 for kJ/mm | kJ/mm | LBW, EBW |
| 3 | **Power Density** | $PD = \dfrac{P}{A_{spot}}$ | W/mm² | Process selection (PD > 10⁵ W/mm² = high-energy-density) |
| 4 | **Depth-to-Width Ratio (keyhole indicator)** | $\psi = \dfrac{d}{w}$ | dimensionless | LBW/EBW typically > 5:1 |
| 5 | **Cooling Rate (3D conduction, Rosenthal thick plate)** | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = 2\pi k \rho c \left(\dfrac{v \cdot t_{8/5}}{q}\right)^{2}$ (inversely related) | K/s | FSW, EBW microstructure prediction |
| 6 | **Welding Cost per Unit Length** | $C = \dfrac{C_{cap} + C_{cons} + C_{lab}}{L_{annual}}$ | ₹/mm | Process economic comparison |
| 7 | **Solidification Growth Rate** | $R = v \cos\theta$ | mm/s | EBW/LBW dendritic structure |
| 8 | **Temperature Gradient** | $G = \dfrac{\Delta T}{x}$ | K/mm | FSW nugget thermal mapping |
| 9 | **Friction Stir Welding Tool RPM** | $N_{opt} \approx \dfrac{v}{\pi D_{shoulder}} \cdot k$ | rpm | FSW parameter selection |
| 10 | **Joint Efficiency** | $\eta_{joint} = \dfrac{\sigma_{weld}}{\sigma_{base}} \times 100$ | % | Aerospace qualification |

**Where:**

- $\eta$ = arc efficiency (GTAW ≈ 0.6, PAW ≈ 0.7, SAW ≈ 0.9)
- $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $S$ = travel speed (mm/min)
- $v$ = travel speed (mm/s)
- $P$ = beam power (W)
- $A_{spot}$ = focused spot area (mm²)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity, $\rho$ = density, $c$ = specific heat
- $t_{8/5}$ = cooling time between 800 °C and 500 °C
- $\theta$ = angle between travel direction and dendrite growth direction

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> In the **aerospace sector**, every kilogram saved translates to **₹10,000–₹25,000 of fuel cost per flight hour**. EBW and LBW enable **narrow, deep, single-pass welds** in titanium and Inconel, eliminating multi-pass distortion and post-weld machining. This is *why* the GE9X engine uses **LBW for fan blade containment rings** and the Falcon 9 rocket uses **Friction Stir Welding for its aluminium-lithium cryogenic tanks**.

In **EV battery manufacturing**, LBW of copper-to-aluminium busbars (dissimilar metals) achieves **joint resistances < 50 µΩ**, which directly determines the vehicle's range. Solid-state FSW of aluminium battery trays eliminates filler wire, flux, and spatter — critical for **sealed IP67/IP6K9K battery enclosures**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Density and Application Classification

The classification of welding processes into **conventional** vs **high-energy-density** is fundamentally rooted in **power density at the workpiece surface**.

### Derivation Start:

A focused energy source deposits power $P$ over an effective interaction area $A_{spot}$. By definition:

$$PD = \dfrac{P}{A_{spot}}$$

For a circular spot of diameter $d_{spot}$:

$$A_{spot} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_{spot}^{2}}{4}$$

Substituting:

$$PD = \dfrac{4P}{\pi \cdot d_{spot}^{2}}$$

For Electron Beam Welding, typical values are:
- $P$ = 3000 W (3 kW)
- $d_{spot}$ = 0.5 mm = 0.5 × 10⁻³ m (but expressed in mm: 0.5 mm)

Substituting (in mm units for clean engineering numerics):

$$PD_{EBW} = \dfrac{4 \times 3000}{\pi \times (0.5)^{2}} = \dfrac{12000}{0.7854}$$

$$PD_{EBW} \approx 15279 \;\; \text{W/mm}^{2} \approx 1.53 \times 10^{4} \;\; \text{W/mm}^{2}$$

This falls in the range $10^{4}$ to $10^{7}$ W/mm², classifying EBW as **high-energy-density**.

For comparison, Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW):
- $P$ (heat in arc) ≈ 3000 W
- $d_{spot}$ (arc footprint) ≈ 10 mm

$$PD_{SMAW} = \dfrac{4 \times 3000}{\pi \times (10)^{2}} = \dfrac{12000}{314.16}$$

$$PD_{SMAW} \approx 38.2 \;\; \text{W/mm}^{2}$$

This is **~400× lower** than EBW — hence the **narrow HAZ and deep penetration** unique to beam processes.

### Classification Boundary Logic:

| Power Density Range | Process Class | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| $PD < 10^{3}$ W/mm² | Conventional Arc | SMAW, GMAW, FCAW, SAW |
| $10^{3} \leq PD < 10^{4}$ W/mm² | Intermediate / Plasma | PAW (transferred arc), Electroslag |
| $10^{4} \leq PD \leq 10^{7}$ W/mm² | High-Energy-Density | LBW, EBW |
| $PD > 10^{7}$ W/mm² (pulsed, femtosecond) | Ultra-fast | Femtosecond LBW (micro-electronics) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU often asks students to *calculate and justify* process selection based on PD. Memorize the formula $PD = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}$ and remember the order-of-magnitude boundaries.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Process vs. Application Suitability

This matrix is a **KTU-board favourite**. Use it for 14-mark application questions.

| Application / Requirement | EBW | LBW | PAW | FSW | Diffusion Bonding | USW |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace Ti / Inconel** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★ |
| **Aluminium automotive body** | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★ | ★★★ |
| **Dissimilar metals (Cu-Al)** | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Vacuum-tight joints** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★ |
| **Thick-section (>25 mm) steel** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★ | ✗ |
| **Nuclear Zr-alloy fuel cladding** | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ✗ | ★★★★★ | ✗ |
| **Underwater / field repair** | ✗ | ✗ | ★★ (hyperbaric) | ★★ (subsea FSW) | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Micro-electronics / foil** | ✗ | ★★★★★ | ✗ | ✗ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Battery tab welding** | ✗ | ★★★★★ | ✗ | ★★★ | ✗ | ★★★★★ |
| **Cryogenic LNG tank** | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ✗ |

**Legend:** ★★★★★ Excellent | ★★★★ Very Good | ★★★ Good | ★★ Marginal | ★ Poor | ✗ Not applicable

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical: Heat Input for a Titanium Aerospace Component

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A titanium Ti-6Al-4V aerospace bracket is to be welded by Plasma Arc Welding (PAW). Voltage = 28 V, Current = 180 A, Travel speed = 150 mm/min. Arc efficiency for PAW (keyhole mode) = 0.70. Calculate (a) Heat input per unit length in kJ/mm, (b) Comment on whether the value is suitable for Ti-6Al-4V without sensitizing the HAZ.

### Solution:

**Given:**
- $V$ = 28 V
- $I$ = 180 A
- $S$ = 150 mm/min
- $\eta$ = 0.70

**Step 1 — Apply the arc heat input formula:**

$$HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$$

$$HI = \dfrac{0.70 \times 28 \times 180 \times 60}{150 \times 1000}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator:**

$$0.70 \times 28 = 19.6$$

$$19.6 \times 180 = 3528$$

$$3528 \times 60 = 211680$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$150 \times 1000 = 150000$$

**Step 4 — Divide:**

$$HI = \dfrac{211680}{150000} = 1.4112 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Answer (a):** $HI \approx 1.41$ kJ/mm

**Step 5 — Comment for Ti-6Al-4V:**
For titanium, recommended maximum heat input is generally **0.6 to 1.5 kJ/mm** to avoid excessive grain growth in the $\beta$-transformed HAZ. The computed value of **1.41 kJ/mm is at the upper acceptable limit**. To improve safety margin:

- Reduce travel speed (counter-productive — increases HI),
- Reduce current/voltage, **or**
- Use **pulsed PAW** to maintain peak penetration while reducing average HI.

**Answer (b):** The HI is borderline acceptable; pulsed PAW or pulsed EBW would be a safer alternative for this critical aerospace Ti-alloy application.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Application Selection Engine

The following diagram maps the **decision flow** an engineer follows when applying advanced welding processes — aligned with KTU Module 3 outcomes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Brief: Industrial Need] --> B[Identify Material Family]
    B --> B1[Reactive Metals: Ti, Zr, Be]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: List any three industrial applications of Electron Beam Welding (EBW) and state why EBW is preferred for those applications.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Aerospace engine components (Ti-6Al-4V fan casings, Inconel turbine seals):** EBW produces **vacuum-quality welds** with **deep penetration in a single pass**, eliminating distortion common in multi-pass arc welding.
2. **Nuclear reactor fuel cladding (Zr-4 alloy end-plug welds):** EBW is performed **in vacuum**, preventing atmospheric contamination (O₂, N₂, H₂) that would otherwise cause **hydride embrittlement** in zirconium.
3. **Automotive transmission gears (dissimilar steel combinations):** EBW's narrow HAZ preserves the **case-hardened surface** of gears that would otherwise soften in conventional arc welding.

> Preferred because of **high power density (10⁴–10⁶ W/mm²)**, **vacuum environment**, and **minimal HAZ**, ensuring metallurgical cleanliness and dimensional accuracy.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Why is Friction Stir Welding (FSW) increasingly replacing GMAW in marine aluminium hull panel fabrication? Give two reasons.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Solid-state nature eliminates porosity and solidification cracking** — critical for marine structures exposed to seawater corrosion, where any crack acts as a stress concentrator.
2. **No filler wire, no shielding gas, no spatter, no fumes** → improves welder safety and reduces post-weld cleaning, with **up to 40% lower cost per metre** in long aluminium panel runs.
3. (Bonus) **Lower distortion** due to lower peak temperatures (~0.8 T_m vs 1.0 T_m in fusion welding), critical for maintaining hull flatness tolerances of ±1 mm/m.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare the applications of **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** and **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** for **aerospace Ti-alloy components**, with reference to (i) penetration mechanism, (ii) joint geometry achievable, (iii) atmospheric requirements, and (iv) production throughput.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | LBW | EBW |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Penetration mechanism | Photon absorption → surface heating + keyhole (via vapour recoil) | Kinetic energy of electrons → deep conduction + keyhole |
| (ii) Joint geometry | Up to ~25 mm in Ti (multi-mode fibre laser); best for sheet & moderate plate | Up to ~200 mm in Ti (single pass); best for thick plate & deep narrow joints |
| (iii) Atmospheric requirement | Helium shielding or vacuum-chamber-free operation possible; **ambient operation feasible** | **Mandatory vacuum** (10⁻² to 10⁻⁴ Pa); high pump-down time |
| (iv) Throughput | **Higher** — no vacuum, robotic-friendly, faster cycle time | **Lower** — vacuum pump-down adds 5–30 min per cycle |

**Application verdict for aerospace Ti-alloys:**
- For **thin-to-medium Ti brackets, honeycomb stiffeners, and fairings → LBW is preferred** (speed + flexibility + robotic compatibility).
- For **thick Ti bulkheads, engine mount beams, and rotor assemblies → EBW remains the gold standard** (single-pass penetration, vacuum purity).

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A **defense submarine hull** requires welding of **HY-80 high-strength steel plates of 32 mm thickness**. Compare the suitability of **PAW (keyhole mode)** vs **SAW (multi-pass)** for this application. Justify your choice with quantitative heat input and distortion reasoning.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** Thickness $t$ = 32 mm, HY-80 steel (Q&T condition, 550 MPa YS), submarine hull application (requires **low residual stress, no HAZ softening, Class A weld**).

**Heat Input Calculation:**

For SAW (typical): $V$ = 32 V, $I$ = 600 A, $S$ = 400 mm/min, $\eta_{SAW}$ = 0.90.

$$HI_{SAW} = \dfrac{0.90 \times 32 \times 600 \times 60}{400 \times 1000} = \dfrac{1036800}{400000} = 2.59 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}$$

For PAW keyhole (typical): $V$ = 30 V, $I$ = 250 A, $S$ = 200 mm/min, $\eta_{PAW}$ = 0.70.

$$HI_{PAW} = \dfrac{0.70 \times 30 \times 250 \times 60}{200 \times 1000} = \dfrac{315000}{200000} = 1.575 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Comparison:**

| Criterion | SAW (Multi-Pass) | PAW (Keyhole, Single Pass) |
|---|---|---|
| Heat input | ~2.59 kJ/mm (HIGH) | ~1.58 kJ/mm (LOWER) |
| Number of passes for 32 mm | 8–12 passes | **1 pass** (with edge prep) |
| Distortion | **High** (cumulative thermal cycles) | **Very Low** (single thermal cycle) |
| HAZ width | Wide, softened zone risk in HY-80 | Narrow, preserves Q&T properties |
| Productivity | Lower | **Higher** |
| Suitability for HY-80 | Marginal — risk of HAZ softening below 550 MPa | **Highly suitable** |

**Verdict:** **PAW keyhole mode is the recommended choice** for HY-80 submarine hull welding. The **single-pass deep penetration** reduces HAZ softening, controls distortion within ±1 mm/m tolerance, and preserves the **quenched-and-tempered microstructure** essential for **submarine pressure hull integrity under hydrostatic collapse pressure**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often **lose 2–3 marks** by *only listing advantages* without doing the **heat-input calculation** or addressing the **HY-80 metallurgical constraint**. Always show numerical work + metallurgical justification in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the application of **Diffusion Bonding** in manufacturing **titanium honeycomb sandwich panels** for next-generation aircraft floor structures. Explain the process parameters and metallurgical outcomes.

**Model Answer:**

**Why Diffusion Bonding for Ti honeycomb?**
- Conventional brazing fails because braze alloy **melts during subsequent fire incidents**.
- Adhesive bonding degrades above **180 °C**, unsuitable for supersonic aircraft brake-heated zones.
- Diffusion bonding creates a **metallurgical bond without melting**, with parent-metal-strength joints that survive > 600 °C.

**Process Parameters:**
- Temperature: 0.5 to 0.8 T_m of Ti = **700 °C to 950 °C**
- Pressure: 1 to 10 MPa (applied isostatically or via HIP)
- Time: 30 min to 4 h
- Atmosphere: Vacuum (10⁻³ Pa) or inert Ar
- Surface finish: < 0.4 µm Ra (critical — asperity contact drives diffusion)
- Interlayer (optional): Titanium foil or Ti-Cu eutectic for lower temperature bonding

**Metallurgical Outcome:**
- Grain boundary diffusion → **coherent metallic bond**
- No cast structure, no HAZ, **fatigue strength matches or exceeds base metal**
- Honeycomb core and face sheets become a **monolithic load-bearing structure**

**Application in Aircraft Floor:**
- Boeing 787 floor beams, Airbus A350 cargo floor panels → **20–30% weight saving** over conventional aluminium floors.
- Maintains **structural integrity up to 800 °C** for 15 min (fire certification per FAR 25.856).

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** With the rise of electric vehicles, **battery cell-to-busbar welding** has become critical. Compare **Ultrasonic Welding (USW)**, **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)**, and **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** for joining **aluminium cell tabs to copper busbars** in a high-volume gigafactory. Recommend the most suitable process with justification.

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | USW | LBW | RSW |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dissimilar Al-Cu compatibility | **Excellent** — solid-state, no intermetallic pool issues | Moderate — risk of **Al-Cu intermetallics** (CuAl₂) | Poor — high heat → thick intermetallic layer |
| Joint electrical resistance | **< 30 µΩ** | 50–80 µΩ | 80–120 µΩ |
| Cycle time | ~0.5 s/weld | ~1.5 s/weld | ~0.8 s/weld |
| Tool wear / consumable | Sonotrode wears every 50k cycles | Optical fibre, low wear | Electrodes wear every 2–5k cycles |
| Throughput | **Highest** (multi-spot sonotrodes) | High (galvanometer scanner) | Moderate |
| Capital cost | Medium | **High** | Low |
| Suitability for 0.2–0.3 mm Al + 0.3 mm Cu tabs | **Excellent** | Good (but needs Ni plating sometimes) | Poor (splash, expulsion) |

**Verdict:** **Ultrasonic Welding (USW) is the recommended process** for gigafactory Al-to-Cu cell tab welding because:
1. It produces the **lowest electrical resistance joints**, directly extending EV range.
2. **Solid-state bonding eliminates brittle Cu-Al intermetallic phases** that plague LBW and RSW.
3. **Fastest cycle time** (~0.5 s) aligns with gigafactory throughput targets of **>100 cells/min per station**.
4. **Lower operating cost per weld** despite moderate CapEx, due to consumable-free operation and long sonotrode life.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Many students **forget the intermetallic problem** in Cu-Al welding. Always mention **CuAl₂, Cu₉Al₄** brittle phases.
> 2. Some students confuse **USW cycle time** with RSW. Remember: **RSW has higher electrical resistance joints** and faster electrode wear.
> 3. **Never recommend adhesive or mechanical fastening** for cell-to-busbar — they add weight and degrade thermally.
> 4. In 14-mark questions, **always end with a numerical justification** (e.g., resistance values, cycle times).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Applications**

### Core Concepts:
- Application engineering = **Material + Geometry + Service** triad.
- **Power density** $PD = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}$ is the fundamental classifier of welding processes.
- **High-energy-density processes** (EBW, LBW) → power density > 10⁴ W/mm².
- **Solid-state processes** (FSW, USW, Diffusion Bonding) → avoid fusion-related defects.

### Process-to-Industry Mapping (Must Memorize):
- **EBW:** Aerospace thick sections, nuclear vacuum chambers, **mandatory vacuum**.
- **LBW:** Automotive (especially EV batteries), sheet-metal aerospace, hermetic seals.
- **PAW:** Thick-section steel (submarine hulls, pressure vessels), high-quality single-pass welds.
- **FSW:** Aluminium marine panels, aerospace bulkheads, EV battery trays — **Al alloys preferred**.
- **Diffusion Bonding:** Ti honeycomb, nuclear fuel cladding, super-alloy turbine blades.
- **USW:** Foil/tab welding — battery cells, electronics, multilayer Cu-Al stacks.
- **Explosion Welding:** Dissimilar metal clad plates (Ti-steel, Al-steel) for chemical/thermal reactors.

### Must-Know Numerical Formulas:
- $HI = \dfrac{\eta V I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ — arc processes (kJ/mm)
- $HI = \dfrac{P}{v \times 1000}$ — beam processes (kJ/mm)
- $PD = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d_{spot}^{2}}$ — power density (W/mm²)
- $t_{8/5}$ — cooling time, critical for HAZ microstructure
- Joint efficiency $\eta_{joint} = \dfrac{\sigma_{weld}}{\sigma_{base}} \times 100$

### Industry Standards to Reference (for full marks):
- ASME Section IX (welding qualification)
- ISO 15614 (procedure qualification)
- AWS D17.1 (aerospace fusion welding)
- AWS D17.2 (aerospace FSW)
- ITER RCC-MR (nuclear)
- EN 13480 (piping)
- ISO 25239 (FSW standard)

### Common Exam Traps:
- ❌ Recommending LBW where vacuum purity is mandatory (use EBW).
- ❌ Recommending FSW for high-melting-point steels (not feasible — tool wear).
- ❌ Forgetting **intermetallic phases** in dissimilar metal welding.
- ❌ Ignoring **atmospheric requirement** for reactive metals (Ti, Zr).
- ❌ Calculating heat input without **arc efficiency factor** $\eta$.

### Golden Rule for KTU Answers:
> *"Name the process → State the industrial application → Justify metallurgically → Justify economically → Cite the standard."*

This five-step structure guarantees **full marks** in any 14-mark application question on Module 3.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: High Speed Welding Using Magnetic Pulse

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Magnetic Pulse Welding (MPW)** is an advanced **solid-state, high-velocity impact welding** process in which two metallic workpieces are joined without any external application of heat, filler material, or shielding gas. The joining is accomplished by accelerating a *flyer* workpiece to extremely high velocities (typically **300 m/s to 1000 m/s**) using the **Lorentz force** generated by a short-duration, high-intensity pulsed electromagnetic field, and impacting it against a stationary *target* workpiece. The kinetic energy of impact produces a localized pressure wave that exceeds the yield strength of both materials, causing the surface asperities to collapse and form a **metallurgical bond** through atomic-level interaction and micro-vaporization of surface contaminants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** MPW is categorized under *High-Speed / Impact Welding* and is the **only** industrial welding process that uses *electromagnetic body forces* (not heat) as the primary energy source for joining.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two trains on parallel tracks. Now imagine one train is stationary (the **target**), and the other train (the **flyer**) is being pushed forward by a giant invisible magnet that has been switched on for a tiny fraction of a second. The moving train strikes the stationary one with such violence that the couplers fuse into a single solid joint — there is no molten metal, no flame, and no sparks.
