The "magnet" in MPW is actually a **copper coil** carrying an enormous pulse of current (up to **1–2 Mega-Ampere**) discharged from a capacitor bank. The invisible "push" is the **repulsive Lorentz force** between the coil current and the eddy currents induced in the flyer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Discharge voltage: **5–25 kV**
> - Peak current: **100 kA – 2 MA**
> - Pulse duration: **10–100 μs**
> - Impact velocity: **300–1000 m/s**
> - Vacuum permeability constant: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$

### Classification of the Process

| Aspect | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| Process type | Solid-state welding (no fusion zone) |
| Energy source | Pulsed electromagnetic field |
| Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) | **Negligible / None** |
| Joining mechanism | Atomic-level diffusion + micro-jetting at interface |
| Typical joint geometry | Tube-to-tube, tube-to-rod, sheet-to-sheet, wire-to-terminal |
| Environment | Ambient air (vacuum not mandatory) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics Behind Magnetic Pulse Welding

The working of MPW is based on **two intertwined electromagnetic principles**:

1. **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** — A time-varying magnetic field induces eddy currents in a nearby conductor.
2. **Lorentz Force Law** — A current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a mechanical force.

When the capacitor bank is discharged through the work coil, an enormous transient current $I(t)$ flows. This generates a transient magnetic field $B(t)$ that links with the flyer workpiece (which must be electrically conductive). By Lenz's Law, opposing eddy currents $I_e$ are induced in the flyer. The interaction between the coil current $I$ and the induced current $I_e$ produces a **repulsive radial force** that accelerates the flyer towards the target.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Energy Storage:** A high-voltage capacitor bank is charged to a preset voltage (typically **5–25 kV**) from a DC power supply. Stored energy: $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$.
- **Step 2 — Triggered Discharge:** A triggered spark gap (or thyristor) closes the circuit, dumping the capacitor energy into the work coil in a fraction of a millisecond.
- **Step 3 — Magnetic Field Generation:** A transient, axially-directed magnetic field $B(t)$ builds up inside the coil, reaching peak flux densities of **10–30 Tesla**.
- **Step 4 — Eddy Current Induction:** The rapidly changing flux induces large eddy currents in the flyer workpiece (must be a good electrical conductor — preferably **aluminum or copper**).
- **Step 5 — Lorentz Force Acceleration:** The repulsive force $F = I_e \cdot B \cdot L$ acts on the flyer, accelerating it inward toward the target. Acceleration reaches values of **$10^6 \, \text{m/s}^2$**.
- **Step 6 — High-Velocity Oblique Impact:** The flyer impacts the target at an oblique angle, with collision point velocity of **300–1000 m/s**.
- **Step 7 — Jetting & Bond Formation:** The collision produces a high-pressure jet of metal that ejects surface oxides and contaminants, exposing clean, nascent metal surfaces that bond under the contact pressure.
- **Step 8 — Wave Formation:** A characteristic **wavy (sinusoidal) interface** forms — the *fingerprint* of a successful MPW joint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" matters:** The wavy interface is the visual evidence of a true metallurgical bond. A straight, featureless interface may indicate a *weak* or *incomplete* bond. KTU examiners frequently ask students to identify good vs. bad MPW joints from micrographs.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Energy stored in capacitor bank | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $P_m = \dfrac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$ | **Magnetic pressure** acting on flyer surface | Pa |
| 3 | $F = B \cdot I \cdot L$ | Lorentz force on a current-carrying conductor | N |
| 4 | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy of accelerated flyer | J |
| 5 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, C \, V^2 \, \eta}{m}}$ | Final flyer velocity (with efficiency $\eta$) | m/s |
| 6 | $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Electrical time-constant of discharge circuit | s |
| 7 | $I(t) = I_0 \, e^{-t/\tau} \sin(\omega t)$ | Underdamped RLC discharge current | A |
| 8 | $\omega = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | Natural frequency of LC tank circuit | rad/s |
| 9 | $P_{\text{contact}} = \dfrac{\rho \, v^2}{2}$ | Dynamic contact pressure at impact (Hugoniot) | Pa |
| 10 | $B = \mu_0 \, n \, I$ | Axial flux density inside a solenoid coil | Tesla |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Joining aluminum tubes to steel fittings in chassis and EV battery modules; crimp-free Al-Cu busbar connections in electric vehicles.
- **Aerospace:** Joining dissimilar lightweight alloys (e.g., Al–Mg to Ti) without forming brittle intermetallics.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Hermetic sealing of copper-to-aluminum terminal connections in high-power busbars.
- **Nuclear Industry:** Joining Zircaloy tubes to stainless steel end-plugs for fuel assemblies.
- **HVAC & Refrigeration:** Joining copper tube-to-tube refrigerant lines without brazing filler metals.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Magnetic Pressure Acting on the Flyer

The **magnetic pressure** is the central physical quantity that determines flyer acceleration. We derive it from Maxwell's equations.

**Starting Point:** Consider a surface of area $A$ inside a uniform magnetic field of flux density $B$. The stored magnetic energy per unit volume is the energy density $u$:

$$
u = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} \quad \text{[Joules/m}^3\text{]}
$$

Since pressure is energy per unit volume, the equivalent magnetic pressure on the surface is:

$$
P_m = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}
$$

**Step 1 — Express B in terms of coil parameters:** For a solenoid of $n$ turns per unit length carrying current $I$:

$$
B = \mu_0 \, n \, I
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the pressure equation:**

$$
P_m = \frac{(\mu_0 \, n \, I)^2}{2\mu_0} = \frac{\mu_0 \, n^2 \, I^2}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the force on the flyer of area $A$:**

$$
F = P_m \cdot A = \frac{\mu_0 \, n^2 \, I^2 \, A}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Interpretation:** For typical values $n = 100$ turns/m, $I = 500{,}000$ A, $A = 10^{-3}$ m²:

$$
F = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(100)^2(5\times 10^5)^2(10^{-3})}{2}
$$

$$
F = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(10^4)(2.5\times 10^{11})(10^{-3})}{2}
$$

$$
F = \frac{(1.2566\times 10^{-6})(2.5\times 10^{12})}{2} \approx 1.57 \times 10^6 \text{ N}
$$

This force (~ **157 tonnes**) is delivered in roughly **20–50 μs**, which is why the flyer reaches hypersonic speeds.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Flyer Velocity from Energy Balance

The flyer is accelerated by the magnetic force over a **standoff distance** $d$. We use the **work-energy theorem**.

**Step 1 — Work done by magnetic force:**

$$
W = F \cdot d = P_m \cdot A \cdot d
$$

**Step 2 — Equate work to flyer kinetic energy** (with energy transfer efficiency $\eta$, typically 0.05–0.20):

$$
\eta \cdot \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for flyer velocity $v$:**

$$
v^2 = \frac{\eta \, C V^2}{m}
$$

$$
v = V \sqrt{\frac{\eta \, C}{m}}
$$

**Step 4 — For typical MPW values:** $C = 400 \,\mu F$, $V = 10$ kV, $m = 0.05$ kg, $\eta = 0.10$:

$$
v = 10{,}000 \sqrt{\frac{0.10 \times 400 \times 10^{-6}}{0.05}}
$$

$$
v = 10{,}000 \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 10^{-5}}{0.05}} = 10{,}000 \sqrt{8 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
v = 10{,}000 \times 0.02828 \approx 283 \text{ m/s}
$$

This comfortably lies within the **300–1000 m/s** welding window.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Effects

| Parameter ↑ | Effect on Velocity | Effect on Bond Quality | Practical Limit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Charging Voltage $V$ | Strong increase ($\propto V$) | Better bond up to a point | Coil insulation breakdown |
| Capacitance $C$ | Moderate increase | Higher impulse force | Capacitor bank cost |
| Standoff distance $d$ | Increases if more space | Slight improvement | Long coil inductance |
| Flyer mass $m$ | Decreases velocity | Harder to bond | Mechanical handling |
| Coil turns $n$ | Increases $P_m$ quadratically | Stronger bond | Coil heating |
| Inductance $L$ | Lower peak current | Smoother pulse | System size |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Threshold — The "Welding Window":** For MPW to produce a sound bond, the **impact velocity** must exceed a *minimum weldability velocity* (MWV) which depends on the material combination. Below this threshold, the bond is purely mechanical (weak). Above an upper threshold, the flyer may fragment or vaporize.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an MPW System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PSU[DC Power Supply Unit] --> CB[High Voltage Capacitor Bank]
    CB -->|Stores energy as CV²/2| CB
    CB -->|Triggered discharge| SG[Triggered Spark Gap / Thyristor Switch]
    SG -->|Transient current pulse| WC[Work Coil Solenoid]
    WC -->|Time varying magnetic field B t| FLYER[Flyer Workpiece]
    FLYER -->|Induced eddy currents Ie| FLYER
    WC -->|Lorentz repulsion force F| FLYER
    FLYER -->|High velocity impact 300 to 1000 m/s| TGT[Target Workpiece]
    TGT -->|Jetting cleans surface| JET[Metal Jet + Oxide Ejection]
    JET -->|Clean nascent surfaces| BOND[Metallurgical Wavy Bond]
    CTRL[Control Console with Timing Circuit] -->|Firing trigger| SG
    CTRL -->|Voltage setpoint| PSU
```

### 4.2 Process Sequence Flow (Step-by-Step Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Charge Capacitor Bank] --> S2[Step 2: Position Flyer at Standoff]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Trigger Spark Gap]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: LC Oscillation in Coil]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Eddy Current Induction in Flyer]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Lorentz Force Accelerates Flyer]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Oblique Impact at Target]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Jetting and Wavy Bond Formation]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Inspect Weld Interface Micrograph]
```

### 4.3 Joint Geometry Configurations Supported

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GEO[MPW Joint Geometry Families] --> G1[Tube to Tube Joint]
    GEO --> G2[Tube to Rod Joint]
    GEO --> G3[Sheet to Sheet Lap Joint]
    GEO --> G4[Wire to Terminal Joint]
    GEO --> G5[Plate to Plate Edge Joint]

    G1 --> G1A[Concentric configuration]
    G1 --> G1B[Offset configuration]
    G3 --> G3A[Parallel plate coils]
    G3 --> G3B[Strip line coil]
```

### 4.4 Wavy Interface — The Signature of a Good MPW Bond

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TOP[Flyer Material: Aluminum] --> BOND
    BOND[Wavy Interfacial Zone 5 to 50 micrometer amplitude] --> BOT
    BOT[Target Material: Copper or Steel]
    BOND --> NOTE[Wave amplitude is the KTU examiner's key indicator of bond quality]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation (KTU Style):** In the actual KTU university exam, you may be asked to *label* a schematic of an MPW system. Memorize the chain: *Capacitor → Switch → Coil → Magnetic Field → Eddy Current → Lorentz Force → Flyer → Target → Wavy Bond*. This is a **favourite 7-mark short answer** question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
*Define magnetic pulse welding. Why is it classified as a solid-state welding process?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Magnetic pulse welding (MPW) is a high-velocity impact welding process that uses the Lorentz force generated by a pulsed electromagnetic field to accelerate a flyer workpiece against a target workpiece, producing a solid-state metallurgical bond without melting the base metals. It is classified as a solid-state process because (i) the bulk material temperatures remain well below the melting point, (ii) there is no fusion zone, and (iii) the bond is formed by atomic-level plastic deformation and diffusion at the impact interface. The peak interface temperature may briefly reach the melting point due to adiabatic heating, but it does not propagate as a weld pool. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
*State any three advantages of magnetic pulse welding over conventional fusion welding.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Dissimilar metal joining** without brittle intermetallic formation (e.g., Al–Cu, Al–Steel).
2. **No Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** — preserves parent metal microstructure and mechanical properties.
3. **No filler metal, shielding gas, or flux** is required, making it environmentally clean and cost-effective for mass production.
4. **Very short cycle time** (~1 second) makes it ideal for automation in automotive and EV battery production.
**(Any three = 3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of the magnetic pulse welding process. Label all major components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the magnetic pressure acting on the flyer workpiece and explain how it determines the impact velocity. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

The MPW system consists of the following major components:

1. **DC Power Supply Unit (PSU):** Charges the capacitor bank to a preset high voltage (5–25 kV).
2. **Capacitor Bank (C):** Stores electrical energy $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$.
3. **Triggered Switch (Spark Gap / Thyristor):** Initiates the rapid discharge of the capacitor into the coil.
4. **Work Coil:** A copper solenoid positioned coaxially around the flyer. Generates a transient axial magnetic field.
5. **Flyer Workpiece:** The outer (or inner) part that is to be propelled; must be a good electrical conductor.
6. **Target Workpiece:** The stationary part that receives the high-velocity impact.
7. **Standoff Gap:** Pre-set air gap between flyer and target before firing.
8. **Control Console:** Sets voltage, monitors timing, triggers discharge.

**Working:** When the switch is closed, the capacitor discharges a high current pulse through the coil in **10–100 μs**. This sets up a rapidly increasing magnetic field. Eddy currents are induced in the flyer (Faraday's law). The interaction between the coil current and the eddy currents produces a **repulsive radial Lorentz force** (Lenz's law), which accelerates the flyer across the standoff gap. The flyer impacts the target at **300–1000 m/s** at an oblique angle, producing a metal jet that ejects surface oxides. Clean nascent metal surfaces come into contact under enormous pressure and form a **wavy metallurgical bond**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

Magnetic pressure is the mechanical pressure exerted by the magnetic field on the flyer surface.

**Step 1 — Magnetic Energy Density:** The energy density of a magnetic field is:

$$
u = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} \quad \text{[J/m}^3\text{]}
$$

**Step 2 — Pressure Equivalence:** Since pressure has units of energy per unit volume, the magnetic pressure on a surface is:

$$
P_m = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Express B using Ampere's Law for a coil** with $n$ turns per unit length carrying current $I$:

$$
B = \mu_0 \, n \, I
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$
P_m = \frac{(\mu_0 \, n \, I)^2}{2\mu_0} = \frac{\mu_0 \, n^2 I^2}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Link to Impact Velocity** by equating magnetic work to flyer kinetic energy (with efficiency $\eta$):

$$
\eta \cdot \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

$$
v = V \sqrt{\frac{\eta \, C}{m}}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** Higher $P_m$ produces a larger force $F = P_m \cdot A$, which does more work over the standoff distance, hence imparting a greater final velocity $v$. The velocity must lie within the **material-specific welding window** for a sound bond. **(7 marks)**

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the mechanism of bond formation in magnetic pulse welding, with emphasis on the role of jetting and wavy interface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain any four industrial applications of magnetic pulse welding with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

Bond formation in MPW occurs in four distinct stages:

1. **Approach Stage:** The flyer accelerates across the standoff gap under the Lorentz force. Initial contact is made at a small point.
2. **Jetting Stage:** The collision produces a pressure far exceeding the dynamic yield strength of both materials. The interface material is squeezed outward at hypersonic speed, forming a **metal jet**. This jet sweeps away surface oxides, adsorbed gases, and contaminants, exposing clean, nascent (chemically active) metal surfaces.
3. **Wavy Bond Formation:** The continued oblique collision creates a characteristic **sinusoidal / wavy interface** with wavelength of **5–50 μm** and amplitude of **2–10 μm**. This wave is the *fingerprint* of a true metallurgical bond formed by combined plastic deformation and atomic diffusion.
4. **Solidification / Locking:** The deformed material at the interface locks together by short-range atomic diffusion and micro-keying, completing the bond. Because no bulk melting occurs, no cast microstructure forms, and the bond inherits the parent metal strength.

> The wavy interface is therefore a direct visual indicator of bond quality and is the most cited feature in KTU answer keys. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

1. **Aluminum–Copper Busbar Joints in EVs:** MPW produces low-resistance, oxide-free Al–Cu connections used in battery packs and inverters, eliminating galvanic corrosion at the joint.
2. **Automotive Chassis Components:** Joining aluminum crash tubes to steel mounting brackets, providing lightweight structures with high fatigue strength and no HAZ softening.
3. **HVAC Refrigeration Tubing:** Joining copper-to-copper or copper-to-aluminum refrigerant lines without brazing filler, eliminating lead-based solders and improving thermal cycling life.
4. **Aerospace Dissimilar Alloys:** Joining aluminum–magnesium to titanium components in aircraft structures, where fusion welding would form brittle Al–Ti intermetallics.
5. **Nuclear Fuel Assembly End-Plugs:** Hermetic sealing of Zircaloy tubes to stainless steel end-plugs in pressurized water reactors.
**(Any four well-explained = 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing MPW with electromagnetic *forming*:** Forming only *shapes* the metal; MPW *joins* it. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you do not mention the *bond formation* step.
> 2. **Wrong formula for magnetic pressure:** It is $B^2 / (2\mu_0)$, **not** $B^2 / \mu_0$. The factor of 2 is frequently missed.
> 3. **Forgetting the welding window:** Stating that "more velocity = better weld" is **incorrect**. There is a *minimum* and *maximum* velocity threshold. Beyond the maximum, the flyer fragments.
> 4. **Ignoring the wavy interface:** A 14-mark question without a reference to the wavy interface / jetting is considered incomplete.
> 5. **Wrong units:** Mixing up kV and V, or T and mT, will lose you 0.5–1 mark even in a long answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Magnetic Pulse Welding (MPW) is a **solid-state, high-velocity impact welding** process.
- Joining energy source: **Pulsed electromagnetic field**, *not* heat.
- Key physical principle: **Lorentz force** between coil current and induced eddy currents.
- Peak current: **100 kA – 2 MA**; discharge voltage: **5–25 kV**; pulse duration: **10–100 μs**.
- Flyer impact velocity: **300–1000 m/s**.
- Magnetic pressure formula: $P_m = \dfrac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$.
- Stored capacitor energy: $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$.
- Flyer kinetic energy balance: $\eta \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}CV^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2$.
- Bond formation involves **jetting → wavy interface → atomic diffusion**.
- **No HAZ, no filler, no shielding gas, no fusion zone** — these are the *defining* features of MPW.
- Best suited for **dissimilar metal joining** (Al–Cu, Al–Steel, Al–Ti).
- Major industries: **EV / automotive, aerospace, HVAC, nuclear, electrical busbars**.
- A characteristic **wavy interfacial zone** is the *signature* of a sound MPW bond.
- Both workpieces need not be ferromagnetic; at least one must be a good electrical conductor (eddy current carrier).
- The coil is typically a single-turn or few-turn **copper** solenoid; field-shaping concentrators are used for non-axisymmetric joints.
- **Always** state the *welding window* concept when discussing process parameters — this is a high-frequency KTU examiner cue.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microwave Welding - High Speed Welding Using Microwave Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Microwave Welding - High Speed Welding Using Microwave Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Microwave Welding (MW)** is an advanced, non-conventional fusion joining process that utilizes high-frequency electromagnetic energy in the microwave spectrum (typically at the **2.45 GHz industrial, scientific, and medical (ISM) band**) to heat, melt, and fuse thermoplastic polymers, elastomers, and certain ceramic or composite materials. The heating mechanism is fundamentally **volumetric and dielectric in nature** — energy is deposited throughout the bulk of the material simultaneously rather than being conducted inward from the surface, which is the defining feature that distinguishes it from conventional resistance, friction, or hot-plate welding techniques.

In the strict KTU 2024 syllabus context, microwave welding is classified under the family of **electromagnetic/energy beam joining processes**, alongside induction welding and ultrasonic welding, and is specifically positioned as a *high-speed, contactless, selective, and energy-efficient* thermal joining technique.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Microwave welding is NOT a fusion process in the traditional arc-welding sense. It is a **dielectric heating-based fusion process** for thermoplastics and susceptor-assisted joining. The standard industrial microwave frequency is fixed at **2.45 GHz** (wavelength ≈ **12.24 cm**) by international ITU regulations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Microwave Oven on a Production Line"

Imagine heating a samosa in your home microwave oven. The microwave energy does not just heat the surface — it penetrates deep into the food, exciting water molecules throughout the volume, and the entire samosa heats up almost simultaneously from the inside out. This is the principle of **volumetric, molecular-level heating**.

Now, scale that exact physics up to an industrial assembly line:
- Instead of food, we place two plastic components with their mating surfaces in contact.
- A focused microwave field (delivered through a precisely tuned **applicator**) is applied for a few seconds.
- The molecules in the bulk of the polymer chain (typically polar C–H, C–O, or N–H groups) oscillate billions of times per second under the alternating field.
- This oscillation generates **dielectric loss heating** uniformly through the joint interface.
- The polymer crosses its **glass transition (Tg)** and **melting temperature (Tm)**, fuses, and on cooling forms a homogeneous weld.
- For non-polar materials (e.g., polypropylene, PTFE) that do not absorb microwaves, **susceptors** (electrically conductive particles) are embedded at the interface to convert microwave energy into localized heat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy summary:** It is essentially "cooking two parts together from the inside out" using the same physics that heats leftovers — but engineered to produce a controlled, structural metallurgical/chemical bond rather than a meal.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are universally referenced in KTU board questions on microwave welding:

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------|
| Industrial Microwave Frequency | **2.45 GHz** | Allocated ISM band; deeply penetrating |
| Wavelength in free space | **λ ≈ 12.24 cm** | Determines applicator dimensions |
| Wavelength in dielectric | **λd = λ₀ / √(ε′)** | Reduced by relative permittivity |
| Typical industrial power | **1 kW – 6 kW** | Magnetron-based sources |
| Penetration depth (Dp) | **~1 cm to 30 cm** | Material dependent |
| Electric field strength in cavity | **10⁴ – 10⁶ V/m** | Drives dipole rotation |
| Typical weld cycle time | **5 – 60 seconds** | Much faster than hot-plate welding |
| Industrial application temperature | **200 °C – 400 °C** | For most thermoplastics |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this:** *"2.45 GHz, 12.24 cm wavelength, dielectric volumetric heating"* — these three numbers will appear in nearly every KTU question paper on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Physics of Microwave–Material Interaction (The "Why")

The fundamental principle behind microwave welding is **dielectric heating**. When a polar dielectric material is placed in a high-frequency alternating electric field, the following sequence of events occurs:

1. **Polarization of Dipoles:** Polar molecules (or polar segments of polymer chains) align themselves with the instantaneous direction of the applied E-field.
2. **Continuous Reorientation:** At 2.45 GHz, the field reverses polarity **2.45 × 10⁹ times per second**. The dipoles attempt to follow this oscillation, but viscous drag and inertia in the polymer matrix cause them to lag behind.
3. **Intermolecular Friction:** The continuous "tug-of-war" between the driving field and the restoring molecular forces generates friction at the molecular scale.
4. **Dielectric Loss → Heat:** This friction converts electromagnetic energy directly into thermal energy (Joule equivalent) within the material volume.
5. **Volumetric Heating:** Unlike surface heating (conduction/convection), the heat is generated *everywhere* the field penetrates, leading to a near-uniform temperature rise.

### 2.2 Governing Equations and the Formula Sheet

The complete mathematical description of microwave heating rests on the following equations, every one of which is high-yield for KTU numerical questions:

#### 2.2.1 Power Density Dissipated in a Dielectric

The volumetric power density (W/m³) absorbed by a dielectric in a microwave field is given by:

$$P_v = \omega \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon'' \cdot E_{rms}^2$$

Where:
- $P_v$ = volumetric power density (W/m³)
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = angular frequency (rad/s)
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m = permittivity of free space
- $\varepsilon''$ = **dielectric loss factor** (dimensionless)
- $E_{rms}$ = root-mean-square electric field strength (V/m)

#### 2.2.2 Complex Permittivity

The material's response is characterized by the complex relative permittivity:

$$\varepsilon_r^* = \varepsilon' - j\varepsilon''$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon'$ = **dielectric constant** (energy storage capability)
- $\varepsilon''$ = **dielectric loss factor** (energy dissipation capability)
- $\tan \delta = \dfrac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}$ = **loss tangent** (key KTU parameter)

#### 2.2.3 Penetration Depth of Microwave Energy

The depth at which the incident power falls to **1/e (≈ 36.8 %)** of its surface value:

$$D_p = \frac{\lambda_0}{2\pi \sqrt{2\varepsilon'}} \left[ \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}\right)^2} - 1 \right]^{-1/2}$$

For low-loss materials ($\varepsilon'' \ll \varepsilon'$), this simplifies to the most commonly used KTU formula:

$$D_p \approx \frac{\lambda_0 \sqrt{\varepsilon'}}{2\pi \varepsilon''}$$

#### 2.2.4 Wavelength Inside the Dielectric

$$\lambda_d = \frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon'}}$$

#### 2.2.5 Total Power Coupled into the Workpiece

$$P_{abs} = P_v \cdot V = \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E_{rms}^2 \cdot V$$

Where $V$ is the volume of material inside the active microwave field region.

#### 2.2.6 Temperature Rise (Energy Balance)

$$P_{abs} \cdot t = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Solving for temperature rise:

$$\Delta T = \frac{P_{abs} \cdot t}{m \cdot c_p}$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of heated material (kg)
- $c_p$ = specific heat capacity (J/kg·K)
- $t$ = exposure time (s)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Consolidated Table)

| # | Formula | Description | KTU Frequency |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $f = 2.45$ GHz | ISM allocated frequency | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $\lambda_0 = c/f$ | Free-space wavelength (≈ 12.24 cm) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $\tan \delta = \varepsilon''/\varepsilon'$ | Loss tangent | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $P_v = \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E_{rms}^2$ | Volumetric power density | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $D_p \approx \lambda_0 \sqrt{\varepsilon'} / (2\pi \varepsilon'')$ | Penetration depth | ⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $\lambda_d = \lambda_0 / \sqrt{\varepsilon'}$ | Wavelength in material | ⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $\Delta T = P_{abs} \cdot t / (m c_p)$ | Adiabatic temperature rise | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | $P_{abs} = P_v \cdot V$ | Total absorbed power | ⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Microwave welding is the preferred joining method in **medical device manufacturing** (PVC blood bags, IV tubes), **automotive interior trim** (PP dashboard assembly), and **food packaging** (sealed polymer pouches). The volumetric heating eliminates thermal gradients that cause warping in conventional welding, and cycle times are typically 10×–100× faster than hot-plate methods for thin sections.

### 2.4 Why Microwave Welding is "High-Speed" — The Real Engineering Justification

The high-speed character arises from three coupled effects:

- **Simultaneous bulk heating** — no waiting for conduction from surface to core.
- **Selective deposition** — energy is concentrated at the joint interface (especially with susceptors), not wasted on the surrounding structure.
- **Rapid dielectric response** — at 2.45 GHz, the energy transfer reaches steady-state in microseconds, so the moment power is switched on, heating begins at full intensity.

Typical cycle times: **5 – 60 seconds** for industrial polymer welds, compared to **30 – 300 seconds** for hot-plate welding of the same joint.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free-Space Wavelength at 2.45 GHz

The free-space wavelength is required in almost every numerical problem. We derive it explicitly:

**Starting point:** All electromagnetic waves obey the wave equation in vacuum, propagating at the speed of light.

$$\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f}$$

Substituting the standard values:

$$\lambda_0 = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{2.45 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Step 1 — Convert denominator to standard form:**

$$\lambda_0 = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.45 \times 10^9} \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Combine powers of 10:**

$$\lambda_0 = \frac{3}{2.45} \times 10^{8-9} \text{ m} = \frac{3}{2.45} \times 10^{-1} \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerical value:**

$$\lambda_0 = 1.2245 \times 10^{-1} \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to centimetres:**

$$\boxed{\lambda_0 \approx 12.245 \text{ cm} \approx 12.24 \text{ cm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical value used in every KTU problem statement. Memorize it as **"12.24 cm"**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wavelength Inside a Dielectric Polymer

When microwaves enter a non-magnetic dielectric, the wavelength shrinks by a factor of $\sqrt{\varepsilon'}$.

**Starting point:** The phase velocity inside a dielectric is reduced:

$$v_d = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon'}}$$

**Step 1 — Relate wavelength to phase velocity and frequency:**

$$\lambda_d = \frac{v_d}{f} = \frac{c}{f\sqrt{\varepsilon'}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\lambda_0 = c/f$:**

$$\boxed{\lambda_d = \frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon'}}}$$

**Numerical example (typical for PVC, $\varepsilon' = 3.0$):**

$$\lambda_d = \frac{12.24 \text{ cm}}{\sqrt{3.0}} = \frac{12.24}{1.732} \approx 7.07 \text{ cm}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Volumetric Power Density (Dielectric Loss Equation)

This is the most important derivation in microwave welding and frequently tested for 7–14 marks.

**Starting point — Polarization current density in a lossy dielectric:**

When an alternating field $\vec{E}$ acts on a dielectric, the current density has two components: a capacitive (charging) part proportional to $\varepsilon'$ and a conductive (lossy) part proportional to $\omega \varepsilon''$.

$$\vec{J}_{total} = j\omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon' \vec{E} + \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' \vec{E}$$

**Step 1 — Time-averaged power dissipation (real part only):**

The real, time-averaged power dissipated per unit volume is the scalar product of $\vec{J}$ and $\vec{E}$ averaged over one RF cycle. For a sinusoidal field $E(t) = E_0 \cos(\omega t)$, the RMS value is $E_{rms} = E_0/\sqrt{2}$, and only the in-phase (loss) component contributes to net energy dissipation.

$$\langle P_v \rangle = \frac{1}{2} \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E_0^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert peak field to RMS field using $E_0 = \sqrt{2}\, E_{rms}$:**

$$\langle P_v \rangle = \frac{1}{2} \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' (\sqrt{2}\, E_{rms})^2$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the squared term:**

$$\langle P_v \rangle = \frac{1}{2} \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' \cdot 2 E_{rms}^2$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the factor of 2:**

$$\boxed{P_v = \omega \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon'' \, E_{rms}^2 \quad \text{[W/m}^3\text{]}}$$

**Step 5 — Write in expanded form using $\omega = 2\pi f$:**

$$P_v = 2\pi f \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon'' \, E_{rms}^2$$

> [!TIP]
> This single boxed equation is the *heart* of microwave heating analysis. KTU examiners often give you $f$, $\varepsilon''$, and $E_{rms}$ and ask you to compute $P_v$, then multiply by volume to get total absorbed power.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Total Power Absorbed and Cycle Time Calculation

A standard KTU numerical problem: *"Compute the time required to heat a polymer specimen to fusion temperature."*

**Given (typical exam problem):**
- Microwave frequency: $f = 2.45$ GHz
- Loss factor: $\varepsilon'' = 0.15$
- RMS electric field: $E_{rms} = 5 \times 10^4$ V/m
- Heated volume: $V = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ (i.e., 5 cm³)
- Initial temperature: $T_i = 25\,°C$
- Fusion (melting) temperature: $T_m = 180\,°C$
- Specific heat: $c_p = 1500$ J/kg·K
- Density: $\rho = 950$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Compute angular frequency:**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 2.45 \times 10^9 = 1.5394 \times 10^{10} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute volumetric power density:**

$$P_v = \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E_{rms}^2$$

Substituting values:

$$P_v = (1.5394 \times 10^{10}) \times (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times (0.15) \times (5 \times 10^4)^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute each factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_v &= (1.5394 \times 10^{10}) \times (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times (0.15) \times (2.5 \times 10^9) \\
&= (1.5394 \times 8.854 \times 0.15 \times 2.5) \times 10^{10-12+9} \\
&= (5.114) \times 10^{7} \text{ W/m}^3 \\
&\approx 5.11 \times 10^7 \text{ W/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total absorbed power:**

$$P_{abs} = P_v \cdot V = (5.11 \times 10^7) \times (5 \times 10^{-6}) = 255.7 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Mass of the heated specimen:**

$$m = \rho V = 950 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 4.75 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — Required temperature rise:**

$$\Delta T = T_m - T_i = 180 - 25 = 155 \text{ °C} = 155 \text{ K}$$

**Step 7 — Energy required (sensible heat only):**

$$Q = m c_p \Delta T = (4.75 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1500 \times 155$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 4.75 \times 10^{-3} \times 1500 \times 155 \\
&= 4.75 \times 1.5 \times 155 \\
&= 1104.4 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Time required (assuming 100 % coupling efficiency):**

$$t = \frac{Q}{P_{abs}} = \frac{1104.4}{255.7} \approx 4.32 \text{ s}$$

**Step 9 — Apply realistic coupling efficiency (η ≈ 60–80 % for industrial systems):**

Taking $\eta = 0.7$:

$$t_{real} = \frac{4.32}{0.7} \approx 6.17 \text{ s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** With a 2.45 GHz, 5 × 10⁴ V/m field, a 5 cm³ polymer specimen can be heated from 25 °C to its melting point in roughly **6 seconds**, illustrating the *high-speed* nature of microwave welding.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Penetration Depth for a Low-Loss Polymer

**Given:** $\varepsilon' = 2.6$ (polyethylene), $\varepsilon'' = 0.003$, $f = 2.45$ GHz.

**Step 1 — Check low-loss assumption:**

$$\tan \delta = \frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'} = \frac{0.003}{2.6} \approx 1.15 \times 10^{-3} \ll 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Apply simplified penetration depth formula:**

$$D_p \approx \frac{\lambda_0 \sqrt{\varepsilon'}}{2\pi \varepsilon''}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$D_p = \frac{(0.1224 \text{ m}) \times \sqrt{2.6}}{2\pi \times 0.003}$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_p &= \frac{0.1224 \times 1.612}{0.01885} \\
&= \frac{0.1973}{0.01885} \\
&\approx 10.47 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** For low-loss polymers, microwave penetration is very deep (multi-metre scale) — confirming that thin polymer sheets and films can be heated *uniformly throughout their thickness*, which is the foundation of the "volumetric" heating advantage.

---

### 3.6 KTU Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Influence

| Parameter | Effect on Weld Quality | Quantitative Relation |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| ↑ Frequency $f$ | ↑ Power density, ↓ Penetration depth | $P_v \propto f$; $D_p \propto 1/f$ |
| ↑ Loss factor $\varepsilon''$ | ↑ Heating rate, ↓ Penetration | $P_v \propto \varepsilon''$; $D_p \propto 1/\varepsilon''$ |
| ↑ Electric field $E$ | ↑ $P_v$ quadratically | $P_v \propto E^2$ |
| ↑ Mass $m$ | ↑ Energy required, ↓ $\Delta T$ per second | $\Delta T \propto 1/m$ |
| ↑ Specific heat $c_p$ | ↑ Energy needed per K rise | $\Delta T \propto 1/c_p$ |
| ↑ Exposure time $t$ | ↑ Temperature (linearly) | $\Delta T \propto t$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Microwave Welding System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the entire industrial microwave welding system from the energy source to the finished joint, satisfying the KTU requirement for a functional schematic:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[AC Mains Power Supply 3 Phase 415V] --> REC[High Voltage Rectifier 20kV DC]
    REC --> MAG[Magnetron Oscillator 2.45 GHz CW]
    MAG --> WG1[Rectangular WR 340 Waveguide]
    WG1 --> ISO[Ferrite Circulator Isolator]
    ISO --> TUN[Three Stub Tuner Impedance Matching]
    TUN --> APPL[Microwave Applicator Cavity TM010 or TE10 Mode]
    APPL --> WZ[Workpiece Zone with Susceptor at Interface]
    WZ --> CLAMP[Hydraulic Pneumatic Clamp 0.2 to 1 MPa]
    CLAMP --> CTRL[PLC Controller with Temperature and Pressure Feedback]
    CTRL --> SEN[Infrared Pyrometer and Load Cell Sensors]
    SEN --> CTRL
    APPL --> REF[Reflected Power Water Load Dummy]
    REF --> ISO
    MAG --> COOL[Water Cooling Jacket 25 L min]
    COOL --> MAG
    WZ --> OUT[Welded Joint Cooled and Ejected]
```

**Reading the diagram (left to right, top to bottom):**
1. Mains AC → high-voltage DC → magnetron converts DC to **2.45 GHz CW microwave energy**.
2. Energy travels through a **WR-340 rectangular waveguide** (standard for 2.45 GHz).
3. An **isolator** protects the magnetron from reflected power.
4. A **three-stub tuner** matches the impedance for maximum power transfer.
5. The **applicator cavity** focuses the field onto the joint.
6. **Susceptor particles** at the interface absorb and convert microwave energy to localized heat.
7. The **clamp** provides joining pressure during solidification.
8. **Sensors** feed back to a **PLC** that controls time, power, and pressure profile.

---

### 4.2 Process Flow — Microwave Welding Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Load and Align Parts] --> B[Step 2: Apply Clamping Pressure]
    B --> C[Step 3: Energize Magnetron 2.45 GHz]
    C --> D[Step 4: Dielectric Heating Volumetric]
    D --> E[Step 5: Interface Melts above Tg and Tm]
    E --> F[Step 6: Hold Pressure During Fusion]
    F --> G[Step 7: Switch Off Microwave Power]
    G --> H[Step 8: Cool under Pressure Solidification]
    H --> I[Step 9: Release Clamp Eject Welded Part]
```

---

### 4.3 Microwave–Material Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MW[2.45 GHz Microwave Field] --> P1{Polar Material}
    MW --> P2{Non Polar Material}
    P1 -- Dipole Rotation --> H1[Direct Dielectric Heating]
    H1 --> R1[Uniform Volumetric Weld]
    P2 -- No Dipole Response --> H2[Embedded Susceptor]
    H2 -- Eddy Current Loss --> R2[Localized Interfacial Heating]
    R2 --> R1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight from the diagram:** Polar polymers (PVC, PA, PET, PMMA) can be welded *directly* by microwaves. Non-polar polymers (PP, PE, PTFE) require **susceptors** — typically 1–10 % by mass of conductive carbon black, graphite, or metal powder — to absorb the field and convert it to heat at the joint.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]

**Q: Define microwave welding. State any two advantages of microwave welding over conventional hot-plate welding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Microwave welding is a non-conventional fusion joining process in which high-frequency electromagnetic energy at the industrial frequency of **2.45 GHz** is used to heat, melt, and fuse thermoplastic materials via **volumetric dielectric heating** of the bulk material or through susceptor-assisted interfacial heating at the joint interface.

**Advantages (1 mark, any two):**
1. **Volumetric and uniform heating** — eliminates surface-to-core thermal gradients, reducing warping and residual stress.
2. **High speed** — typical cycle times of 5–60 s versus 30–300 s for hot-plate welding.
3. **Selective / localised heating** — energy is concentrated at the joint, leaving the rest of the part cool.
4. **Contactless energy transfer** — no physical contact between the heat source and the workpiece, reducing contamination.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]

**Q: What is a susceptor in microwave welding? Why is it used for non-polar polymers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A **susceptor** is a material with high dielectric loss factor (typically conductive carbon black, graphite, stainless steel fibres, or ceramic ferrite powder) that is either embedded at the joint interface or coated as a thin layer, in order to preferentially absorb microwave energy and convert it to localized heat.

**Reason (1.5 marks):** Non-polar polymers such as polypropylene (PP), polyethylene (PE), and PTFE have a very low loss tangent ($\tan \delta \ll 0.01$) and therefore couple poorly with the 2.45 GHz field. The susceptor acts as an *intermediary absorber* — it heats up under the microwave field and then conducts heat into the surrounding non-polar matrix, raising it to fusion temperature. This enables welding of materials that would otherwise be microwave-transparent.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Slot]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **principle of microwave welding** with a clear block diagram of the system. Discuss the role of the magnetron, waveguide, isolator, tuner, and applicator in achieving controlled volumetric heating. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer workpiece of volume $5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³, density $950$ kg/m³, specific heat $1500$ J/kg·K, and dielectric loss factor $\varepsilon'' = 0.12$ is to be welded using 2.45 GHz microwaves with an RMS electric field of $4 \times 10^4$ V/m. If the initial temperature is 30 °C and the melting point is 175 °C, determine:
  (i) the volumetric power density deposited,  (ii) the total absorbed power, and  (iii) the time required to reach the melting point (assume 70 % coupling efficiency). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

1. **Principle (3 marks):** Microwave welding uses **dielectric heating**. Polar molecules in a thermoplastic align and reorient with the 2.45 GHz alternating electric field, generating intermolecular friction that converts electromagnetic energy to heat throughout the bulk volume. This is fundamentally different from surface heating methods.

2. **Magnetron (1 mark):** The **magnetron** is the microwave source — a crossed-field vacuum tube that converts DC electrical input into continuous-wave (CW) microwave energy at 2.45 GHz, typically at 1–6 kW output.

3. **Waveguide (1 mark):** The **WR-340 rectangular waveguide** (internal dimensions 86.36 mm × 43.18 mm) transports the microwave energy from the magnetron to the applicator with minimal loss at 2.45 GHz.

4. **Isolator and tuner (1 mark):** The **ferrite circulator/isolator** protects the magnetron from reflected power, while the **three-stub tuner** matches the load impedance to the waveguide characteristic impedance for maximum power transfer (minimising VSWR).

5. **Applicator (1 mark):** The **applicator cavity** (single-mode TE₁₀ or TM₀₁₀, or multi-mode) focuses the field onto the joint region, ensuring the required E-field strength of 10⁴–10⁶ V/m is achieved at the workpiece.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- $V = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³
- $\rho = 950$ kg/m³
- $c_p = 1500$ J/kg·K
- $\varepsilon'' = 0.12$
- $f = 2.45$ GHz → $\omega = 1.539 \times 10^{10}$ rad/s
- $E_{rms} = 4 \times 10^4$ V/m
- $T_i = 30\,°C$, $T_m = 175\,°C$
- $\eta = 0.70$

**(i) Volumetric Power Density (3 marks):**

$$P_v = \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E_{rms}^2$$

$$P_v = (1.539 \times 10^{10}) \times (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times (0.12) \times (4 \times 10^4)^2$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_v &= (1.539 \times 10^{10}) \times (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times (0.12) \times (1.6 \times 10^9) \\
&= (1.539 \times 8.854 \times 0.12 \times 1.6) \times 10^{10-12+9} \\
&= 2.617 \times 10^{7} \text{ W/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{P_v \approx 2.62 \times 10^{7} \text{ W/m}^3}$$

**(ii) Total Absorbed Power (2 marks):**

$$P_{abs} = P_v \cdot V = (2.617 \times 10^{7}) \times (5 \times 10^{-6}) = 130.8 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Time Required (2 marks):**

Mass: $m = \rho V = 950 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 4.75 \times 10^{-3}$ kg

Temperature rise: $\Delta T = 175 - 30 = 145$ K

Energy required: $Q = m c_p \Delta T = 4.75 \times 10^{-3} \times 1500 \times 145 = 1033$ J

Useful power: $P_{useful} = \eta \cdot P_{abs} = 0.7 \times 130.8 = 91.6$ W

$$t = \frac{Q}{P_{useful}} = \frac{1033}{91.6} \approx 11.3 \text{ s}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice — KTU Style]

**Q: (a)** With neat sketches, explain the **construction and working of a continuous-wave (CW) magnetron** used as the microwave source in industrial microwave welding systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **microwave welding and induction welding** based on principle, frequency, applicable materials, penetration depth, and joint strength. A PVC component (ε′ = 3.2, ε″ = 0.18) is exposed to 2.45 GHz microwaves of free-space field $5 \times 10^4$ V/m. Calculate the wavelength of the microwave inside the PVC. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

1. **Construction (3.5 marks):** The CW magnetron consists of a **cylindrical cathode** at the centre, surrounded by a **copper anode block** containing an even number of cylindrical resonant cavities (typically 8 or 16) arranged radially. A **strong DC magnetic field** is applied parallel to the cathode axis by permanent magnets, and a **DC electric field** is established radially between cathode (negative) and anode (positive, typically at 20 kV). A small antenna probe in one cavity couples microwave energy out into the waveguide.

2. **Working (3.5 marks):** Thermionic emission from the cathode produces a cylindrical cloud of electrons. The crossed E × B fields force the electrons to execute **cycloidal/spiral paths** past the cavity openings. This electron flow constitutes an **alternating current** at the resonant frequency of the cavities (determined by the cavity dimensions). Energy is transferred from the electrons to the cavity oscillations through **velocity modulation**, building up a sustained electromagnetic oscillation at 2.45 GHz. The output is coupled out via the antenna probe and launched into the waveguide.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Microwave Welding | Induction Welding |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Frequency | 2.45 GHz (microwave) | 100 kHz – 1 MHz (RF) |
| Principle | Dielectric heating of polar molecules | Eddy current heating in conductive/ferromagnetic parts |
| Applicable materials | Thermoplastics, polar polymers | Thermoplastics with embedded susceptors, conductive polymers, metals |
| Heating mechanism | Volumetric, molecular dipole rotation | Surface/skin depth eddy currents |
| Penetration | Centimetres (low-loss polymers) | Skin depth of order 0.1–1 mm |
| Joint strength | Comparable to parent material | Comparable to parent material |

**Wavelength Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $\varepsilon' = 3.2$, $f = 2.45$ GHz, $\lambda_0 = 12.24$ cm

$$\lambda_d = \frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon'}} = \frac{12.24}{\sqrt{3.2}} = \frac{12.24}{1.789} \approx 6.84 \text{ cm}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ½ → 2 transition** in the power density derivation (2 marks lost if peak vs RMS field is confused).
> 2. **Using free-space wavelength instead of in-material wavelength** when calculating applicator dimensions (3 marks lost).
> 3. **Stating 2.45 GHz without units** or saying "microwave frequency" without the numerical value (1 mark lost — examiners treat this as vague).
> 4. **Confusing dielectric constant (ε′) with loss factor (ε″)** in numerical problems — they are *different quantities* and must be assigned correctly.
> 5. **Ignoring coupling efficiency η** in cycle time calculations — KTU problems often give η explicitly; missing it costs the full final mark of part (iii).
> 6. **Writing "microwave welding is used for metals"** — this is FALSE for bulk metals (they reflect microwaves). Microwave welding is primarily for **thermoplastics** and **susceptor-assisted joints**. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- **Microwave welding = dielectric heating at 2.45 GHz** (ISM band, λ₀ ≈ 12.24 cm).
- **Mechanism:** Polar molecules rotate 2.45 × 10⁹ times/second → friction → volumetric heat.
- **Key materials:** Polar thermoplastics (PVC, PA, PET, PMMA) weld directly; non-polar (PP, PE, PTFE) need **susceptors**.
- **Master formula:** $P_v = \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E_{rms}^2$ — the single most-tested equation.
- **Penetration depth** is large for low-loss polymers (multi-metre); small for high-loss or lossy materials.
- **In-material wavelength:** $\lambda_d = \lambda_0 / \sqrt{\varepsilon'}$.
- **Cycle time:** $\Delta T = P_{abs} t / (m c_p)$ — apply coupling efficiency η.
- **System components (in order):** Mains → Rectifier → **Magnetron** → Waveguide → **Isolator** → **Tuner** → **Applicator** → Workpiece → Sensors → PLC.
- **Magnetron:** Crossed-field vacuum tube, 20 kV DC, 8/16 cavities, E × B interaction.
- **Advantages:** Fast (5–60 s), volumetric, contactless, selective, energy-efficient.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited to thermoplastics, requires careful shielding, magnetron has finite life, complex tuning.
- **Applications:** Medical tubing, automotive trim, food packaging, textile lamination, polymer film sealing.
- **Compare with induction:** MW → polar dielectric, MHz–GHz, volumetric; Induction → eddy current, 100 kHz–1 MHz, skin depth limited.
- **Numerical constants to memorize:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $f = 2.45$ GHz, $\lambda_0 = 12.24$ cm.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern Soldering and Brazing Techniques: Induction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modern Soldering and Brazing Techniques: Induction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Induction Soldering and Induction Brazing** are non-contact, high-precision joining processes in which the heat required to melt the filler metal (solder or brazing alloy) is generated *internally* within the base workpieces by means of **eddy currents** induced by a high-frequency alternating electromagnetic field. The energy is transferred from a water-cooled copper coil (the *induction coil* or *inductor*) to the workpiece through electromagnetic induction, exactly on the principle of an electrical transformer, where the coil acts as the primary and the electrically conductive workpiece acts as a single-turn short-circuited secondary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize:** "Induction brazing/soldering is a process in which joining is accomplished by heating the parts locally or generally with the heat generated by induced electrical currents caused by the alternating electromagnetic field produced by an induction coil, without any physical contact between the heat source and the workpiece."

### 1.2 Operating Frequency Bands (Standard Metrics)

| Band | Frequency Range | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **LF (Low Frequency)** | **1 kHz – 10 kHz** | Deep penetration heating, large components |
| **MF (Medium Frequency)** | **10 kHz – 100 kHz** | General brazing of steels, copper |
| **HF (High Frequency)** | **100 kHz – 10 MHz** | Surface heating, small parts, selective brazing |

Typical industrial installations operate at **10 kHz, 30 kHz, or 450 kHz** depending on the power rating and depth of heating required. Modern solid-state MOSFET/IGBT inverters have largely replaced the older spark-gap and thyristor (SCR) generators.

> [!NOTE]
> The melting point of the **filler metal** (solder typically < 450 °C, brazing alloy typically > 450 °C but < parent metal melting point) is the controlling temperature. The **parent/base metal is never melted** in either process. In induction brazing, the entire joint area is uniformly raised to the brazing temperature.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Induction Cooktop" Intuition

Imagine the induction cooktop in your hostel kitchen. There is no flame, no hot coil visible — yet a steel *kadai* placed on the glass top instantly becomes hot, while the glass surface itself remains cool. Why? Because a coil beneath the glass spins a high-frequency magnetic field, which *induces swirling electrical currents* (called **eddy currents**) inside the *kadai*. The *kadai*'s own electrical resistance converts this current into heat.

**Induction brazing works on EXACTLY the same principle, but inside a workpiece:**

- The **induction coil** = the cooktop coil
- The **workpiece joint** = the *kadai*
- The **eddy currents** = the same swirling currents, but tightly confined to the joint region
- The **water-cooled coil** stays cold while the joint glows red-hot in a few seconds

The only difference: in brazing, the heat must be concentrated *precisely at the joint interface* so that the filler metal (pre-placed as a ring, paste, or preform) melts and flows into the joint by capillary action. This precision is achieved by *shaping* the induction coil to match the geometry of the joint — a skilled *tooling designer* essentially draws the magnetic field lines around the joint.

### 1.4 Why Induction Is Classified as a "Modern" Technique

Traditional torch brazing relies on a flame — slow, oxidizing, and dependent on operator skill. Induction brazing, by contrast, is:

- **Clean** (no combustion gases, no oxidation in inert atmosphere setups)
- **Fast** (heat-up time typically **1 to 10 seconds**)
- **Localised** (heat-affected zone is **< 2 mm** in most applications)
- **Repeatable** (energy input is electrically controlled, not operator-dependent)
- **Automatable** (easily integrated into CNC rotary indexing or in-line production)

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If a KTU question asks "Why is induction brazing preferred in mass production?" — the answer lies in the **cycle time, repeatability, and localized heating**. Mention at least these three keywords.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Induction Heating — Four Step Logic Chain

The complete energy conversion in induction brazing can be decomposed into a clean four-step chain:

- **Step 1 — AC Excitation:** A high-frequency alternating current (typically sinusoidal, at frequency *f* in **Hz**) is passed through a water-cooled copper coil (the *inductor*). The coil is usually made of hollow rectangular or round copper tubing to permit internal water cooling.
- **Step 2 — Magnetic Field Generation:** According to Ampere's law, the AC current produces an alternating magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ in the space around the coil. For a simple solenoid of *N* turns and length *L*, the peak flux density inside the coil is given by:

$$B = \mu_0 \, \mu_r \, n \, I_{peak}$$

where $n = N/L$ is turns per unit length and $I_{peak}$ is the peak coil current. For high-frequency induction heating, the coil is often only **1 to 3 turns** because the induced voltage per turn is high.

- **Step 3 — Eddy Current Induction:** The time-varying magnetic flux passes through the nearby electrically conductive workpiece. By Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, an electromotive force (EMF) is induced:

$$\varepsilon = -\frac{d\Phi_B}{dt} = -N \, A \, \frac{dB}{dt}$$

This induced EMF drives circulating **eddy currents** in the workpiece, just as in a short-circuited secondary winding of a transformer. The eddy currents flow in closed loops on planes perpendicular to the magnetic field, exactly opposite in phase to the coil current (Lenz's law).

- **Step 4 — Joule Heating (I²R Losses):** The workpiece is not a perfect conductor. Its finite electrical resistivity $\rho$ (in **Ω·m**) opposes the eddy current flow. The resulting **Joule heating** dissipates electrical energy as thermal energy:

$$P = I_{eddy}^{2} \, R_{workpiece} = I_{eddy}^{2} \cdot \frac{\rho \, \ell}{A_c}$$

The heating rate can be enormous: a small steel joint can be raised from **25 °C to 1100 °C in less than 5 seconds**, generating a brazing temperature of approximately **1100 °C to 1200 °C** for silver brazing of steels.

### 2.2 The Two Critical Electromagnetic Effects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Skin Effect and Proximity Effect are the two pillars of induction heating design.** KTU frequently asks about these.

#### 2.2.1 Skin Effect

At high frequencies, AC current in a conductor does *not* flow uniformly across its cross-section. It tends to crowd toward the **outer surface** (the "skin") of the conductor because the magnetic flux linkages are minimum there. The current density $J$ decays exponentially with depth *x* from the surface:

$$J(x) = J_s \, e^{-x / \delta}$$

where $J_s$ is the surface current density and $\delta$ is the **reference depth** (or "skin depth") — defined as the depth at which the current density has fallen to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its surface value. The reference depth is given by the **fundamental formula of induction heating**:

$$\boxed{\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho}{\omega \mu_0 \mu_r}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}}$$

**Where:**
- $\delta$ = reference (skin) depth in **metres (m)**
- $\rho$ = electrical resistivity of the workpiece in **Ω·m**
- $f$ = operating frequency in **Hz**
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = angular frequency in **rad/s**
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m** (permeability of free space)
- $\mu_r$ = relative permeability of the workpiece (dimensionless, **≈ 1 for non-magnetic, 100–500 for carbon steel at low T**)

**Worked examples** (cold conditions, $\mu_r \approx 100$ for steel):
- For **steel** at **10 kHz**: $\delta \approx 0.5$ mm
- For **steel** at **450 kHz**: $\delta \approx 0.05$ mm
- For **copper** at **10 kHz**: $\delta \approx 0.65$ mm

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic:** "Higher frequency → thinner skin → shallower heating." This is the key to selecting frequency for surface vs. through heating.

#### 2.2.2 Proximity Effect

When two conductors carrying AC current are placed *near* each other, their current distributions are mutually distorted. Current concentrates on the sides of the conductors that are *facing each other* (for currents in the same direction) or on the *opposite* sides (for currents in opposite directions). In induction brazing, the **proximity effect between the induction coil and the workpiece** is the primary cause of power transfer to the workpiece — the field is squeezed into the gap between coil and part. This is why the **coupling distance** (air gap) between the coil and the joint dramatically affects heating efficiency: a 1 mm increase in gap can reduce power transfer by up to 30%.

### 2.3 Energy Transfer Efficiency — The Coupling Coefficient

The induction coil and the workpiece form a loosely coupled air-core transformer. The fraction of coil power actually delivered to the workpiece is called the **electrical efficiency** $\eta_e$, which depends on:

- **Coil-to-part gap** (smaller = better, ideal < 2 mm)
- **Material permeability** $\mu_r$ (ferrous > non-ferrous)
- **Material resistivity** $\rho$ (moderate is best — copper is *too* conductive and reflects energy, certain stainless steels heat poorly)
- **Operating frequency** matching the workpiece size

A typical industrial induction brazing system has an **overall electrical-to-thermal efficiency of 60–85%** — far higher than oxy-fuel brazing (~10–15% useful heat at the joint).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta = \sqrt{\rho \div \pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}$ | Reference (skin) depth | m |
| 2 | $P = I_{eddy}^{2} \cdot R$ | Heat generated in workpiece | W |
| 3 | $B = \mu_0 \mu_r n I_{peak}$ | Peak flux density inside coil | T |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = -N \, A \, dB \div dt$ | Induced EMF (Faraday) | V |
| 5 | $Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat to raise temperature | J |
| 6 | $t = Q \div P$ | Heat-up time | s |
| 7 | $\eta = P_{joint} \div P_{coil}$ | Coupling efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $R_a \propto \rho \div \delta$ | Effective AC resistance of workpiece | Ω |
| 9 | $f \propto 1 \div d^2$ (for round bar) | Frequency selection rule | Hz |
| 10 | $F = \sigma \, E^2$ | Power density (Poynting-derived) | W/m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Frequency Selection Heuristic (Industry Rule of Thumb):** $f \approx 0.4 \div d^2$ where *d* is the part diameter in cm and *f* is in **kHz**. Example: a 1 cm diameter shaft requires ~**400 kHz**; a 10 cm diameter forging requires only **~4 kHz**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Induction brazing is ubiquitous in:

- **Automotive** — brazing of hard-facing tips, HVAC fittings, fuel injector assemblies
- **Aerospace** — blade-to-disc joints in jet engines, heat exchanger panels
- **Tool manufacturing** — brazing tungsten carbide tips to steel shanks (mining/drilling tools)
- **Electronics** — hermetic sealing of packages, lead-frame soldering
- **Medical devices** — joining of stainless steel needle assemblies
- **HVAC and refrigeration** — copper-to-copper and copper-to-brass tube joints
- **Power transmission** — diamond segment brazing on cutting blades

The single largest tonnage use globally is **brazing of carbide cutting tool inserts** and **brazing of aluminium heat exchangers** in automotive air-conditioning systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Reference (Skin) Depth

The reference depth $\delta$ is the most important parameter in induction heating design. The KTU examiner will award full marks only if you can derive it from Maxwell's equations. Below is the full derivation.

**Starting Point — Maxwell's Equation (Faraday's Law in Differential Form):**

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$$

We assume a plane conductor occupying the half-space $x \geq 0$, with a uniform surface magnetic field $B_s \cos(\omega t)$ applied parallel to the surface (along the *y*-axis). The induced eddy currents will flow along the *z*-axis. Due to symmetry, all fields depend only on *x* and *t*. This is a **1-D semi-infinite problem**.

**Step 1 — Write the curl operator in 1-D form:**

The only non-zero component of $\nabla \times \vec{E}$ for $E = E_z(x,t)$ is along the *y*-direction:

$$\frac{\partial E_z}{\partial x} = -\frac{\partial B_y}{\partial t}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law (point form) in the conductor:**

$$\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E} \quad \Rightarrow \quad J_z = \sigma E_z$$

where $\sigma = 1/\rho$ is the electrical conductivity of the workpiece.

**Step 3 — Apply Ampere's Law (neglect displacement current at these frequencies):**

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J} \quad \Rightarrow \quad -\frac{\partial H_y}{\partial x} = J_z = \sigma E_z$$

**Step 4 — Combine the two curl equations to obtain a wave equation in $H_y$:**

Differentiate the Ampere equation with respect to *x*:

$$-\frac{\partial^{2} H_y}{\partial x^{2}} = \sigma \frac{\partial E_z}{\partial x}$$

Substitute Faraday's law $\partial E_z / \partial x = -\partial B_y / \partial t = -\mu_0 \mu_r \, \partial H_y / \partial t$:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} H_y}{\partial x^{2}} = \sigma \mu_0 \mu_r \, \frac{\partial H_y}{\partial t}$$

**Step 5 — Assume time-harmonic solution $H_y(x,t) = \text{Re}\{H(x) \, e^{j \omega t}\}$:**

Substituting into the PDE:

$$\frac{d^{2} H}{d x^{2}} = j \omega \sigma \mu_0 \mu_r \, H$$

Let $\alpha^{2} = j \omega \sigma \mu_0 \mu_r$. Note that $\sqrt{j} = (1 + j) / \sqrt{2}$, so:

$$\alpha = (1 + j) \sqrt{\frac{\omega \sigma \mu_0 \mu_r}{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Solve the ODE with the boundary condition $H(0) = H_s$ and $H(\infty) = 0$:**

$$H(x) = H_s \, e^{-\alpha x}$$

The real physical solution for the magnetic field amplitude inside the conductor is:

$$H_y(x,t) = H_s \, e^{-x/\delta} \, \cos(\omega t - x/\delta)$$

**Step 7 — Identify the decay constant $\delta$:**

The exponential decay constant is:

$$\frac{1}{\delta} = \text{Re}\{\alpha\} = \sqrt{\frac{\omega \sigma \mu_0 \mu_r}{2}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \sigma \mu_0 \mu_r}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho}{\omega \mu_0 \mu_r}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}$$

**Final Result (boxed for exam use):**

$$\boxed{\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}}$$

**Units Check:** $[\rho] = \Omega \cdot m$, $[f] = s^{-1}$, $[\mu_0] = H / m = \Omega \cdot s / m$. So:

$$\sqrt{\frac{\Omega \cdot m}{s^{-1} \cdot \Omega \cdot s / m}} = \sqrt{m^{2}} = m \quad \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If asked to "show the units" of skin depth, use the relationship $\mu_0$ has units of **H/m = Ω·s/m**. Always carry out the unit check — KTU examiners award partial marks for dimensional consistency.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Frequency on Heating

The following analytical matrix allows a designer to choose the right frequency for a given brazing task.

| Material | Bar Dia. (mm) | Recommended *f* (kHz) | Resulting $\delta$ (mm) | Heating Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Steel | 5 | 450 | 0.05 | Surface |
| Steel | 20 | 30 | 0.2 | Through (small parts) |
| Steel | 100 | 4 | 0.6 | Deep / through |
| Copper | 10 | 450 | 0.12 | Surface (reflections) |
| Aluminium | 10 | 450 | 0.17 | Surface (low efficiency) |
| Brass | 20 | 30 | 0.5 | Through |

> [!WARNING]
> **Non-magnetic materials (copper, aluminium, austenitic stainless steel) heat POORLY in induction** because $\mu_r \approx 1$ and $\rho$ is very low. To compensate, you must use **very high frequencies (MHz range)** or **ferritic filler inserts** to improve coupling.

### 3.3 Numerical Example — Heat-Up Time for a Steel Joint

**Given:**
- Joint mass: $m = 50 \text{ g} = 0.05 \text{ kg}$ (carbon steel joint)
- Specific heat: $c_p = 500 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
- Power delivered to joint: $P = 4000 \text{ W}$ (typical for a 5 kW generator at ~80% coupling)
- Temperature rise: from 25 °C to 1100 °C, so $\Delta T = 1075$ K

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the required sensible heat:**

$$Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T = 0.05 \times 500 \times 1075$$

$$Q = 26875 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the heat-up time (ignoring radiation losses for short duration):**

$$t = \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{26875}{4000} = 6.72 \text{ s}$$

**Result:** The joint reaches brazing temperature in **approximately 6.7 seconds** — about 10× faster than torch brazing, which justifies the "modern" classification of the process.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, radiation and convection losses add roughly 15–25% to the required time, so the realistic heat-up time is around **7.5 – 8.5 seconds**.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Induction vs Other Brazing Methods

| Parameter | Induction Brazing | Torch Brazing | Furnace Brazing | Resistance Brazing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heat-up time | **1 – 10 s** | 30 – 300 s | 300 – 1800 s | 5 – 30 s |
| Localisation | Excellent | Poor | Poor | Good |
| Atmosphere control | Possible (local purge) | Difficult | Easy (whole furnace) | Difficult |
| Operator skill | Low | High | Medium | Medium |
| Capital cost | High | Low | Medium | Low |
| Cycle cost | Low | Medium | High | Low |
| Suitable for mass production | **Excellent** | Poor | Good | Good |
| Joint size range | Small to medium | Any | Any | Small |
| Automation friendly | **Excellent** | Poor | Medium | Good |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Induction Brazing System Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the full functional architecture of a modern solid-state induction brazing workstation, showing the energy flow from mains to the brazed joint.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[MAINS SUPPLY 3 PHASE 50 HZ 415 V] --> R[RECTIFIER UNIT]
    R --> F[DC FILTER CAPACITOR BANK]
    F --> INV[SOLID STATE INVERTER IGBT MOSFET]
    INV --> TF[OUTPUT TRANSFORMER HV TO LV]
    TF --> CAP[CAPACITOR BANK TANK CIRCUIT]
    CAP --> COIL[INDUCTION COIL WORK HEAD WATER COOLED]
    COIL -.INDUCES EDDY CURRENTS.-> WP[WORKPIECE JOINT]
    WP --> FM[FILLER METAL MELTS AND FLOWS BY CAPILLARITY]
    FM --> JB[BRAZED JOINT FORMS]
    COIL --- WC1[WATER COOLING IN]
    COIL --- WC2[WATER COOLING OUT]
    INV --- CTRL[CONTROL UNIT PLC HMI]
    CTRL --- TS[TEMPERATURE SENSOR PYROMETER]
    CTRL --- PR[POSITION SENSOR]
    CTRL --> INV
    R --> CTRL
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- **M** → **R** → **F** → **INV** form the **power electronics chain** (AC → DC → high-frequency AC).
- The **inverter** is the heart of the system; modern units use IGBTs switching at 10–450 kHz.
- The **tank circuit** (CAP + COIL) is a resonant LC circuit that determines the operating frequency.
- The **workpiece** receives energy purely by *electromagnetic induction* (shown as a *dotted* line because it is a *field*, not a wire).
- **CTRL** forms the *closed-loop* — a pyrometer measures joint temperature and modulates the inverter power to maintain the exact brazing temperature profile.

### 4.2 Process Flowchart — Induction Brazing Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START DESIGN COIL TO MATCH JOINT GEOMETRY] --> B[CLEAN AND DEGREASE WORKPIECES]
    B --> C[APPLY FLUX OR PLACE FILLER PREFORM]
    C --> D[ASSEMBLE JOINT WITH FILLER IN PLACE]
    D --> E[POSITION WORKPIECE INSIDE OR NEAR INDUCTION COIL]
    E --> F[TURN ON HIGH FREQUENCY POWER]
    F --> G[EDDY CURRENTS HEAT JOINT REGION]
    G --> H[JOINT REACHES BRAZING TEMPERATURE]
    H --> I{FILLER FULLY MELTED AND FLOWING}
    I -->|NO| F
    I -->|YES| J[SWITCH OFF POWER OR REDUCE TO HOLD]
    J --> K[ALLOW JOINT TO COOL NATURALLY OR QUENCH]
    K --> L[REMOVE FLUX RESIDUE BY PICKLING OR WATER RINSE]
    L --> M[INSPECT JOINT VISUALLY AND BY NDT]
    M --> N[FINISHED BRAZED COMPONENT]
```

### 4.3 Coil Geometry vs Field Distribution — Schematic Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SG1[SINGLE TURN LOOP COIL]
      C1[COPPER TUBE LOOP] --> F1[CONCENTRATED FIELD AT JOINT]
    end
    subgraph SG2[HELICAL SOLENOID COIL]
      C2[COIL WITH 3 TO 5 TURNS] --> F2[CYLINDRICAL FIELD AROUND TUBE]
    end
    subgraph SG3[PANCake OR PUDDING COIL]
      C3[FLAT SPIRAL COIL] --> F3[FLAT FIELD FOR FLAT SURFACES]
    end
    subgraph SG4[CHANNEL OR SPLIT COIL]
      C4[CONDUCTOR WITH GAP] --> F4[LOCALISED FIELD AT FLANGE OR EDGE]
    end
    F1 --> J[BRAZED JOINT REGION]
    F2 --> J
    F3 --> J
    F4 --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule:** The coil geometry is *mirrored* on the joint geometry. A round tube joint uses a solenoid; a flat plate uses a pancake; a flange edge uses a *channel* coil. This shape-matching is what makes induction brazing so controllable.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define induction brazing. List any four advantages of induction brazing over torch brazing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — keyword-driven):**

Induction brazing is a non-contact joining process in which the heat required to melt the brazing filler metal is generated within the workpiece by **eddy currents** induced by a high-frequency alternating magnetic field produced by a water-cooled induction coil. The base metal is not melted; only the filler metal melts and flows by capillary action to form the joint. The process is classified under "modern" brazing techniques because of its clean, fast, and highly controllable nature.

**Advantages over torch brazing (1/2 mark each, any four):**
1. **Rapid localised heating** (cycle time of seconds rather than minutes)
2. **No open flame** → safe, no combustion products, can be used in clean rooms
3. **High repeatability** → power input is electrically controlled, not operator-dependent
4. **Easily automatable** → suitable for inline mass production
5. **Lower total heat input** → smaller heat-affected zone, less distortion
6. **Higher energy efficiency** at the joint (60–85% vs ~10–15% for torch)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the term "skin effect" in induction heating. State the formula for reference depth and explain the role of frequency.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Skin Effect (1 mark):** At high frequencies, alternating current does not distribute uniformly across a conductor's cross-section. Instead, it concentrates in a thin layer near the surface (the "skin"), while the interior carries almost no current. This is because the self-induced EMF is maximum at the centre, opposing the current flow there, leaving the surface as the path of least opposition.

**Reference Depth Formula (1 mark):**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}$$

**Role of Frequency (1 mark):** The reference depth is *inversely proportional to the square root of frequency*. As frequency increases, $\delta$ decreases, meaning the current is confined to a thinner surface layer. Therefore:
- **High frequency (HF/MF)** → surface heating, used for thin parts or shallow case-hardening
- **Low frequency (LF)** → deeper penetration, used for through-heating of large components

This relationship is the *primary tool* used by designers to select the operating frequency for a given brazing task.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle and major components of an induction brazing system. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the expression for reference (skin) depth starting from Maxwell's equations, and compute the skin depth for copper at 50 kHz given $\rho = 1.7 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega \cdot m$, $\mu_r = 1$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle and Components — 7 marks:**

The induction brazing system consists of the following major components, organized into a *power chain* and a *control chain*:

1. **Power Supply / Mains (3-phase, 50 Hz, 415 V):** Provides the bulk input power. For a typical 5–50 kW brazing station, three-phase AC mains is rectified to DC.

2. **Rectifier and Filter:** A 6-pulse or 12-pulse diode bridge converts the AC mains to smooth DC, which is stored in a large DC link capacitor bank.

3. **High-Frequency Inverter (IGBT / MOSFET):** This is the heart of the system. It chops the DC into a high-frequency square wave (or quasi-sinusoidal wave) at the operating frequency, typically **10 kHz to 450 kHz**. Modern inverters use IGBTs for medium frequencies and MOSFETs for high frequencies.

4. **Tank Circuit (Output Capacitor + Induction Coil):** The induction coil and a parallel capacitor form an LC resonant circuit. The coil is wound from hollow copper tube through which cooling water flows continuously.

5. **Induction Coil (Work Head):** A custom-shaped copper coil (1 to 5 turns) that delivers the magnetic field to the joint. Common shapes: solenoid, pancake, split-return, channel.

6. **Workpiece and Filler Metal:** The joint is pre-assembled with filler metal (paste, ring, or preform) and flux (if needed). It is positioned within or adjacent to the coil, with a coupling gap of ideally **1–3 mm**.

7. **Control System (PLC + HMI + Pyrometer):** A pyrometer measures the joint temperature in real-time and feeds back to the PLC, which modulates the inverter duty cycle to maintain the brazing temperature window (typically ±10 °C).

8. **Cooling System:** Closed-loop water chiller circulates deionised water through the coil, capacitors, and inverter heatsinks.

**Working Principle:** The high-frequency AC current in the coil produces a time-varying magnetic field. This field, by Faraday's law, induces eddy currents in the conductive workpiece. The workpiece's own resistance converts the eddy current energy to Joule heat. Within seconds, the joint reaches the brazing temperature, the filler melts, capillary action draws it into the joint gap, and a metallurgical bond is formed. The power is then switched off (or held at a lower level for soaking), and the joint is allowed to cool.
