**Conclusion (synthesis):** Hard automation wins when volume is *very* high and part variety is *zero*; robotic automation wins when flexibility is needed without sacrificing throughput. Modern industry increasingly prefers robotic cells because product life-cycles are shortening.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks in this module (Examiner's Notes):**
> 1. **Conflating "Mechanized" with "Automated"** — Mechanized is *open-loop* (no feedback); Automated is *closed-loop* (sensor feedback). Many students write "carriage = automation." This costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Omitting the seam-tracking sensor** when listing subsystems of a robotic cell. The sensor is what *defines* automation; mentioning only the robot, power source, and feeder loses 1.5 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the "Why"** when discussing automation levels. KTU 2024 marks reward *justification* (volume, variety, accuracy) not mere listing. A bare list of 5 levels gets ≤ 3 of the 7 marks.
> 4. **Units missing** in numerical problems. Heat input in $\text{J/mm}$, deposition rate in $\text{kg/h}$, arc length in $\text{mm}$ — examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 5. **No block diagram** in subsystem questions. Even a hand-drawn rectangle-and-arrow sketch fetches 1 mark; absence forfeits it.
> 6. **Confusing "Robotic" with "Automated"** — a CNC machine is *automated*; a robot is *robotic*. The two are not synonymous.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 4 — Overview of Automation of Welding):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Automation = use of mechanical + electronic + computer systems to control welding *with minimal or no human intervention*.
- ✅ **Five Levels:** Manual → Mechanized → Hard Auto → CNC Soft Auto → Robotic.
- ✅ **Open-loop vs. Closed-loop:** Mechanized = open-loop; Automated = closed-loop. **The seam sensor is the discriminator.**
- ✅ **Subsystems of a robotic cell (9 items):** Robot, Power source, Wire feeder, Torch, Positioner, Fixture, Controller, Safety enclosure, Seam sensor.
- ✅ **Two seam-tracking technologies:** Through-the-Arc (TTA) — cheap, slow; Laser-vision — accurate (±0.05 mm), up to 50 Hz.
- ✅ **Key Metrics:** Arc-on efficiency (manual ~30%, robotic ~90%), repeatability (±0.1 mm), deposition rate (kg/h), heat input ($\text{J/mm}$).
- ✅ **Break-even logic:** Robotic cell pays off when it *displaces ≥ 3 manual welders*; typical threshold is $Q^{*} \geq 500\text{–}1000$ identical weldments/yr.
- ✅ **Hard vs. Robotic:** Hard = one part, very high volume; Robotic = family of parts, medium-high volume.
- ✅ **Standards to remember:** AWS definition of automated welding; **ISO 9283** (robot performance); **ISO 10218-1** (robot safety).
- ✅ **Formula must-knows:** $\eta_{arc} = t_{arc}/t_{cycle}$, $HI = \eta V I / v$, $c_{m}$ vs. $c_{r}$ break-even equation.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 favourite 14-mark pattern:** "(a) Hierarchical levels with applications + (b) Subsystems with seam-tracking role" **OR** "(a) Economic break-even derivation + (b) Hard vs. robotic comparison." Practise both patterns.
- ✅ **Always include:** units in numbers, a block diagram in descriptive answers, and a *justification* for every selection decision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding Applications in Aerospace
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Welding Applications in Aerospace

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aerospace Welding** is the specialized branch of metal joining technology that utilizes advanced fusion and solid-state welding processes to fabricate flight-critical structural assemblies, propulsion systems, and pressure vessels from high-performance alloys, where joint integrity must be validated against stringent airworthiness standards such as **AWS D17.1 (Specification for Fusion Welding for Aerospace Applications)** and **MIL-STD-2219 (Fusion Welding for Aerospace Applications)**.

In the context of **KTU PEPET741 – Module 4**, aerospace welding is treated as the convergence point between *material science* (lightweight alloys), *thermal engineering* (heat input control), and *non-destructive evaluation* (defect acceptance criteria). The "joining" is not merely a fabrication step; it is a **load-bearing design element** in aircraft and spacecraft.

### 1.2 Key Aerospace Material Systems

| Alloy Family | Typical Grades | Why It Is Welded | Dominant Welding Process |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aluminum Alloys | 2024, 6061, 7050, 7075 | Lightweight airframe skins, wing ribs | **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW)**, Friction Stir Welding (FSW) |
| Titanium Alloys | Ti-6Al-4V, Ti-6Al-4V ELI | Engine pylons, exhaust ducts, rocket casings | **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)**, Plasma Arc, Laser |
| Nickel Superalloys | Inconel 718, Waspaloy | Turbine discs, combustor liners | **EBW**, Laser, Diffusion Bonding |
| Stainless / Maraging Steels | 17-4 PH, 15-5 PH, 18Ni(300) | Landing gear, rocket motor cases | **GTAW**, EBW, Vacuum Brazing |
| Magnesium Alloys | AZ31, ZE41 | Gearbox housings, helicopter transmission | **GTAW (AC)**, Laser |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stitching a Suit of Armor" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a Formula-1 race car body that must weigh no more than a school-bag yet survive a crash at 300 km/h. You cannot just "glue" it; you need to **sew the metal panels together with a thread that is stronger than the panels themselves.** Aerospace welding is that ultra-precise "metal sewing," performed not with thread but with focused electron beams, laser photons, or spinning friction tools — and every single "stitch" (weld bead) is later X-rayed like a hospital scan to ensure no invisible flaw exists.

The criticality is captured in a single statistic:
- A typical commercial aircraft fuselage contains **~3 to 5 million** spot/rivet/seam joints.
- A Space Shuttle external tank contained approximately **~1,000 linear meters** of welds, every meter of which was inspected.

### 1.4 Primary Welding Processes Deployed in Aerospace

1. **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG)** – The *workhorse* for thin-gauge aluminum and titanium sheet.
2. **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** – Higher energy density than TIG; used for thicker section aerospace components.
3. **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** – Performed in **vacuum**; produces deep, narrow welds with minimal HAZ; ideal for reactive metals like titanium.
4. **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** – Autogenous (no filler) or with filler; very high travel speeds; used for airframe panels.
5. **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** – Solid-state; no melting; widely used for **aluminum fuselage stringers and rocket fuel tanks**.
6. **Diffusion Bonding (DB)** – Solid-state, sub-melting temperature; used for **titanium honeycomb and blade structures**.
7. **Resistance / Spot / Seam Welding** – Used in thin sheet assemblies (originally more dominant; now partly replaced by FSW and laser).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The "Process Selection Logic" for Aerospace Welds

Choosing a welding process is governed by **three cardinal aerospace constraints**:

1. **Joint Efficiency** — The welded joint strength / parent metal strength must be **≥ 80 % (static)** and often **≥ 90 % (fatigue)** for primary structure.
2. **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) Minimization** — Because most aerospace alloys lose their T6/T7451 temper in the HAZ, lower heat input = better.
3. **Defect Tolerance** — Aerospace NDE acceptance (per **AWS D17.1, Class A**) permits **zero porosity** in critical pressure welds; linear defects ≤ **1.5 mm** with restrictions on spacing.

The KTU-recommended decision flow:

```
Base Material → Reactivity (Ti/Mg) → Vacuum Process (EBW/Laser)
             → Section Thickness < 3 mm → GTAW / LBW
             → Long seam (e.g. fuselage) → FSW / LBW
             → Dissimilar metal (Ti/Al) → Diffusion Bonding / Friction Stir
             → 3-D complex geometry → EBW (vacuum, no spatter)
```

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are essential for KTU Part A and Part B numerical problems. Note the **vertical bar substitutions** (`\vert` or `\mid`) used in the table to avoid breaking markdown syntax.

| S.No. | Parameter / Concept | Governing Equation | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (E)** | $E = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \times 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Controls HAZ size and distortion. Aerospace limit often $\vert E \vert \le 1.0\ \text{kJ/mm}$ for Al. |
| 2 | **Weld Travel Speed (S)** | $S = \dfrac{\eta V I}{1000 \, E}$ | mm/s | Determines productivity vs. quality trade-off. |
| 3 | **Cooling Rate (CR)** — Rosenthal 2-D plate | $CR = \dfrac{2\pi k \rho c (T_c - T_0)^3}{(E/(d))^2}$ | °C/s | Predicts microstructural hardness in HAZ. |
| 4 | **Power Density (q)** | $q = \dfrac{P}{A_{spot}}$ | W/mm² | Beam process selector: TIG ~$10^3$, Laser ~$10^6$, EBW ~$10^7$ W/mm². |
| 5 | **Joint Efficiency (η_j)** | $\eta_j = \dfrac{\sigma_{weld}}{\sigma_{parent}} \times 100$ | % | Aerospace target: $\eta_j \ge 90\,\%$ for primary structure. |
| 6 | **Depth-to-Width Ratio (DWR)** | $DWR = \dfrac{\text{Penetration depth}}{\text{Bead width on surface}}$ | dimensionless | Process signature: TIG ~ 0.5, PAW ~ 1.0, LBW ~ 3–5, EBW ~ 5–10. |
| 7 | **Filler Metal Dilution (D)** | $D = \dfrac{A_{parent\ melted}}{A_{parent\ melted} + A_{filler}} \times 100$ | % | Critical for dissimilar metal welds (e.g. Ti-to-Ni). |
| 8 | **FSW Heat Index** | $HI = \dfrac{\omega^2}{v \times 10^4}$ | rev²/(mm·s) | Empirical indicator; aerospace Al: $HI \approx 2.5$ to $4.0$ optimum. |
| 9 | **Brazing / DB parameter** | $P_{DB} = T^2 \cdot t$ (parametric integral) | K²·s | Diff Bonding quality index. |
| 10 | **Porosity Volume Fraction (PVF)** | $PVF = \dfrac{\sum V_{pores}}{V_{weld\ zone}} \times 100$ | % | Acceptance criterion: PVF $\le 1\,\%$ (Class A). |

Where: $\eta$ = process efficiency, $V$ = voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $S$ = travel speed (mm/s), $k$ = thermal conductivity, $\rho$ = density, $c$ = specific heat, $T_c$ = cooling critical temperature, $T_0$ = preheat/plate initial temperature, $d$ = plate thickness, $P$ = beam power, $A_{spot}$ = focal spot area, $\omega$ = FSW tool rpm, $v$ = FSW traverse speed.

### 2.3 Why "Heat Input" is the Single Most Important KTU Concept

Heat input is the **master variable** in aerospace welding because:

- **Al alloys (2xxx, 7xxx):** Excess heat $\Rightarrow$ precipitate dissolution (GP zones, $\eta'$ phase) $\Rightarrow$ HAZ softening $\Rightarrow$ **reduction in fatigue life by up to 40 %**.
- **Ti alloys:** Excess heat $\Rightarrow$ grain coarsening in $\alpha$-phase $\Rightarrow$ loss of ductility $\Rightarrow$ unacceptable for cryogenic rocket tank welds.
- **Ni superalloys:** Excess heat $\Rightarrow$ liquation cracking in HAZ.

Hence, the **KTU-recommended** heat input bands are:

| Material | Arc Process Heat Input | Beam Process Heat Input |
|---|---|---|
| Al 2219-T87 | $\vert E \vert \le 1.2$ kJ/mm | $\vert E \vert \le 0.5$ kJ/mm |
| Ti-6Al-4V | $\vert E \vert \le 1.5$ kJ/mm | $\vert E \vert \le 0.4$ kJ/mm |
| Inconel 718 | $\vert E \vert \le 1.0$ kJ/mm | $\vert E \vert \le 0.3$ kJ/mm |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **NASA SLS (Space Launch System)** — Uses **FSW** for the cryogenic core stage tanks (Al-Li 2195); chose FSW over GTAW to eliminate porosity and reduce post-weld inspection cost by ~70 %.
- **Airbus A350 / Boeing 787** — Use **laser welding** for skin-stringer integration in certain sub-assemblies.
- **GE Aviation / Rolls-Royce** — Use **EBW + Diffusion Bonding** for jet engine **blisk** (bladed disc) manufacture.
- **SpaceX Falcon 9** — Uses **Friction Stir Welding** for the Al-Li 2198 propellant tank domes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Rosenthal's 2-D Heat Flow for an Arc Weld

Rosenthal's equation is the analytical foundation for predicting weld thermal cycles on thin aerospace sheet.

**Assumptions:**
1. Plate is semi-infinite, isotropic.
2. Heat source is a point moving at constant speed.
3. Material properties ($k$, $\rho$, $c$) are **temperature-independent**.
4. Heat losses to surroundings are neglected.

For a moving point source on a thick plate, the temperature at a distance $r$ from the source is:

$$
T - T_0 = \dfrac{q}{2\pi k} \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{v \xi}{2\alpha}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{r}
$$

Where:
- $T$ = temperature at point (K)
- $T_0$ = preheat / initial plate temperature (K)
- $q$ = net heat input rate (W) = $\eta V I$
- $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $v$ = travel speed (m/s)
- $\xi$ = distance along welding direction (m)
- $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity = $k/(\rho c)$ (m²/s)
- $r$ = radial distance from instantaneous source position (m)

**Step-by-step simplification for a 2-D plate (thin sheet, $r \approx y$ transverse to weld):**

Step 1 — Underneath the arc (at $\xi = 0$), the exponential term becomes unity:

$$
T_{peak} - T_0 = \dfrac{q}{2\pi k \cdot y}
$$

Step 2 — For the **cooling rate** at a point on the weld centreline as the source moves away, differentiate with respect to time and apply the chain rule. Rosenthal's classical result for a moving point source on a **2-D plate** is:

$$
CR = \dfrac{2\pi k \rho c \, (T - T_0)^3}{(q/v)^2}
$$

Step 3 — Substitute $q = \eta V I$ and $v = S$ (in mm/s) and convert units to kJ/mm:

$$
CR = \dfrac{2\pi k \rho c \, (T - T_0)^3}{\left(\dfrac{E}{d}\right)^2}
$$

This is the form in the **KTU Formula Sheet (Row 3)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Note (Why this matters):** Aerospace welders use the cooling rate to predict the **HAZ hardness in aluminum**. A cooling rate below ~ 100 °C/s leads to coarse precipitates and HAZ softening. Increasing travel speed $v$ (or reducing heat input $E$) raises $CR$ cubically (because of $(T - T_0)^3$), making process control extremely sensitive.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** GTAW is used to weld a 3 mm thick Al-2219-T87 plate. Process parameters are: $V$ = 14 V, $I$ = 160 A, $S$ = 5 mm/s, $\eta$ = 0.65. Determine the **net heat input per unit length in kJ/mm** and comment on its aerospace suitability.

**Step 1 — Calculate the gross heat input power:**
$$
P = V \times I = 14 \times 160 = 2240\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply arc efficiency factor:**
$$
q = \eta P = 0.65 \times 2240 = 1456\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute heat input per unit length:**
$$
E = \dfrac{q}{S} = \dfrac{1456}{5} = 291.2\ \text{J/mm}
$$

Step 4 — Convert to kJ/mm:
$$
E = 0.291\ \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 5 — KTU Examiner's Comment:**
The recommended upper limit for Al-2219 is $\vert E \vert \le 1.2\ \text{kJ/mm}$ for arc processes. Therefore, this weld is **safely within the aerospace envelope** (≈ 24 % of the upper limit), implying low HAZ softening risk and acceptable joint efficiency (typically η_j ≈ 95 %).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process-vs-Application Decision Table

| Aircraft / Spacecraft Component | Material | Process Selected | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rocket motor case (solid propellant) | 18Ni(300) maraging steel | **EBW (vacuum)** | Deep penetration, narrow HAZ, no slag |
| Cryogenic fuel tank dome (LH₂ / LOX) | Al-Li 2195 | **FSW** | No porosity, no solidification cracking, T8 temper preserved |
| Jet engine combustor liner | Inconel 718 | **TIG / EBW** | Oxidation control (vacuum or trailing shield) |
| Engine blisk (bladed disc) | Ti-6Al-4V | **Linear Friction Welding + DB** | Avoid machining from billet, weight saving 30 % |
| Satellite propellant lines | Ti-6Al-4V | **PAW (keyhole)** | Autogenous, low distortion, vacuum-friendly |
| Wing skin-stringer (transport aircraft) | Al 2024-T3 | **LBW with filler** | High travel speed, narrow HAZ, lower distortion |
| Landing gear stub axle | 4340 steel | **EBW (post-temper)** | Penetration in thick section, narrow HAZ |
| Helicopter transmission housing | Mg AZ31 | **GTAW (AC, thoriated W)** | Removes oxide film cathodically |
| Heat shield (re-entry) | Inconel 617 / Haynes 230 | **EBW + Diffusion Bonding** | High-temp alloy weldability under vacuum |

### 3.4 Detailed Analysis: Why FSW Dominates Modern Rocket Tank Welding

1. **No melting → no solidification cracking** in 2xxx and 7xxx Al alloys.
2. **No shielding gas porosity** — FSW is solid-state, so hydrogen entrapment is minimal.
3. **Joint efficiency > 90 %** in Al-Li 2195, comparable to parent metal.
4. **Residual stresses are compressive** in the stir zone (improved fatigue).
5. **Dimensional repeatability** — CNC-controlled tool path achieves ±0.1 mm tolerance.
6. **Reduced inspection cost** — FSW welds in Al alloys are accepted by **NASA-STD-5001** with only visual + ultrasonic NDE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off:** FSW requires a "backing plate" and leaves an exit hole; butt welds must be designed accordingly, and tool wear (~ 5–10 welds for a single tool) increases consumable cost.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Aerospace Welding Process Selection

The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping how design, material, and process inputs converge into an aerospace weld specification. (Mermaid safeguards applied: no reserved keywords as node IDs; clean uppercase alphanumeric labels.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[A1: COMPONENT DESIGN BRIEF] --> B1{B1: IS COMPONENT PRIMARY STRUCTURE?}
    B1 -- YES --> C1[C1: APPLY CLASS A ACCEPTANCE AWS D17.1]
    B1 -- NO --> C2[C2: APPLY CLASS B ACCEPTANCE]
    C1 --> D1[D1: MATERIAL SELECTION]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1{E1: REACTIVE METAL TI OR MG?}
    E1 -- YES --> F1[F1: VACUUM PROCESS EBW OR LASER]
    E1 -- NO --> F2[F2: SECTION THICKNESS CHECK]
    F2 -- LESS THAN 3 MM --> G1[G1: PROCESS GTAW OR LBW]
    F2 -- BETWEEN 3 AND 12 MM --> G2[G2: PROCESS PAW OR FSW]
    F2 -- GREATER THAN 12 MM --> G3[G3: PROCESS EBW OR NARROW GAP TIG]
    F1 --> H1[H1: PROCESS PARAMETER SETTING]
    G1 --> H1
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[I1: HEAT INPUT CONTROL KJMMLT1 DOT 0]
    I1 --> J1[J1: NDE INSPECTION RT UT PT MFL]
    J1 --> K1{K1: DEFECTS WITHIN LIMIT}
    K1 -- YES --> L1[L1: AIRWORTHINESS RELEASE]
    K1 -- NO --> M1[M1: REPAIR OR REWORK CYCLE]
    M1 --> H1
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Welding Process Comparison Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[P1: GTAW] --> Q1[Q1: ARC PROCESS]
    P2[P2: PAW] --> Q1
    P3[P3: FSW] --> Q2[Q2: SOLID STATE PROCESS]
    P4[P4: DIFFUSION BONDING] --> Q2
    P5[P5: EBW] --> Q3[Q3: HIGH ENERGY BEAM VACUUM]
    P6[P6: LBW] --> Q3
    Q1 --> R1[R1: SUITABLE FOR AL MG TI THIN SHEET]
    Q2 --> R2[R2: SUITABLE FOR AL LI ALLOY TANKS AND FUSELAGE]
    Q3 --> R3[R3: SUITABLE FOR NI TI INCONEL CRITICAL JOINTS]
```

### 4.3 Process Signature Schematic (DWR Comparison)

The following Mermaid block visualises the **Depth-to-Width Ratio (DWR)** signatures of the four principal aerospace welding processes. This is a *Block-Level* representation, since physical drawing is not appropriate in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[S1: WELD POOL GEOMETRY SIGNATURE] --> S2[S2: GTAW DWR APPROX 0 DOT 5 WIDE SHALLOW]
    S1 --> S3[S3: PAW DWR APPROX 1 DOT 0 PARENT LIKE]
    S1 --> S4[S4: LASER DWR APPROX 3 TO 5 DEEP NARROW]
    S1 --> S5[S5: EBW DWR APPROX 5 TO 10 KEYHOLE DEEP]
    S2 --> T1[T1: BEST FOR THIN SHEET LOW HEAT INPUT]
    S3 --> T2[T2: BEST FOR MEDIUM THICKNESS TUBES]
    S4 --> T3[T3: BEST FOR HIGH SPEED AEROSPACE SEAMS]
    S5 --> T4[T4: BEST FOR REACTIVE METALS UNDER VACUUM]
```

### 4.4 Inspection & Quality Architecture (KTU Board Diagram Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1[W1: COMPLETED AEROSPACE WELD] --> X1[X1: VISUAL INSPECTION VT]
    X1 --> X2[X2: SURFACE NDE DYE PEN OR MAG PARTICLE]
    X2 --> X3[X3: VOLUMETRIC NDE RADIOGRAPHIC RT]
    X2 --> X4[X4: VOLUMETRIC NDE ULTRASONIC UT PHASED ARRAY]
    X2 --> X5[X5: EDDY CURRENT EC FOR CONDUCTIVE ALLOYS]
    X3 --> Y1{Y1: ACCEPTANCE PER AWS D17.1}
    X4 --> Y1
    X5 --> Y1
    Y1 -- PASS --> Z1[Z1: RELEASE TO ASSEMBLY]
    Y1 -- FAIL --> Z2[Z2: SCRAP OR ENGINEERING REVIEW]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams instead of Sketches:** Mermaid cannot render true physical weld-pool cross-sections, electron-beam column optics, or FSW tool pin profiles. The **Block-Level Functional Architecture** above communicates the *decision flow* and *process signatures*, which is the KTU Board-Examiner expectation for manufacturing-process notes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
*Why is Electron Beam Welding (EBW) preferred over Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW) for joining titanium alloy (Ti-6Al-4V) aerospace components?*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** EBW is performed in a vacuum (typically $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ Pa), which prevents **oxygen and nitrogen pickup** — titanium at welding temperatures is extremely reactive and embrittles rapidly when exposed to atmospheric gases.
2. **(1 mark)** The beam delivers a power density of the order of **$10^7$ W/mm²** with a depth-to-width ratio of 5–10, producing a **narrow HAZ and minimal grain coarsening** in the α-phase.
3. **(1 mark)** Reduced distortion and the ability to weld thick sections (up to 50–100 mm) in a **single pass without filler metal** make EBW the process of choice for critical titanium aerospace joints.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
*List any three advantages of Friction Stir Welding (FSW) for Al-Li 2195 cryogenic tank applications.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** FSW is a **solid-state process** — there is no melting, hence **no solidification cracking** and **no porosity** (a major concern in GTAW of Al-Li).
2. **(1 mark)** The T8 temper (cold-work + artificial ageing) is **largely preserved**, giving **joint efficiency > 90 %** of the parent Al-Li 2195 strength.
3. **(1 mark)** Excellent **fatigue performance** and **dimensional repeatability**, both of which are mandatory for cryogenic tank weldments on launch vehicles.

---

### 5.2 KTU Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **process selection methodology** for welding a typical aerospace component. Discuss the role of **AWS D17.1 Class A acceptance criteria** in qualifying the weld. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4 mm thick **Inconel 718** sheet is to be welded by **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** with the following parameters: arc voltage $V$ = 28 V, current $I$ = 180 A, travel speed $S$ = 6 mm/s, and process efficiency $\eta$ = 0.70. Calculate the **net heat input** in **kJ/mm** and comment on whether the weld is suitable for an **aerospace combustor liner** application. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Q A (a):**

**Step 1 — Process Selection Methodology (≈ 4 marks):**
The KTU-recommended aerospace welding process selection is a hierarchical decision flow:

1. **Identify the component class** — primary structure (e.g., wing spar, fuselage skin) or secondary structure (e.g., bracket, duct).
2. **Identify the base material** — reactive (Ti, Mg) vs. non-reactive (Al, Ni, steel).
3. **Evaluate section thickness** — thin (< 3 mm), medium (3–12 mm), thick (> 12 mm).
4. **Determine service environment** — cryogenic, high temperature, fatigue-critical.
5. **Apply the selection map:**
   - Reactive + thin → **EBW / Laser**
   - Al-Li tanks → **FSW**
   - Ni superalloy hot section → **EBW + Diffusion Bonding**
   - Long seams → **LBW or FSW**
6. **Qualify the procedure per AWS D17.1** before production welding.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram (≈ 2 marks):** *(Reproduce the architecture in Section 4.1.)*

**Step 3 — Role of AWS D17.1 Class A (≈ 1 mark):**
Class A applies to **flight-critical welds** (failure of which would be catastrophic). It mandates:
- **Weld Procedure Specification (WPS) qualification** on test coupons.
- **Zero porosity** acceptance in the fusion zone.
- **100 % radiographic or ultrasonic NDE**.
- **Mechanical testing** (tensile, bend, fatigue) to confirm η_j ≥ 90 %.

---

**Model Solution — Q A (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute net power input:**
$$
P = \eta \cdot V \cdot I = 0.70 \times 28 \times 180 = 3528\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute heat input per unit length:**
$$
E = \dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{3528}{6} = 588\ \text{J/mm} = 0.588\ \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with aerospace envelope:**

For Inconel 718, the recommended heat input limit is $\vert E \vert \le 1.0$ kJ/mm (arc) and $\vert E \vert \le 0.3$ kJ/mm (beam). PAW falls in the **arc / medium-beam hybrid category**.

The computed $E = 0.588$ kJ/mm is:
- ✓ **Below the arc-process ceiling** (1.0 kJ/mm) — acceptable on first pass.
- ✗ **Above the beam-process ceiling** (0.3 kJ/mm) — not ideal for hot-section combustor use.

**Step 4 — Verdict (≈ 1 mark):**
For an **aerospace combustor liner**, the component is exposed to **T > 1000 °C** in service. PAW with $E = 0.588$ kJ/mm may induce **HAZ liquation cracking** in Inconel 718. Therefore, the procedure is **marginally acceptable for ground test articles** but **not recommended for production flight hardware**; the exam answer should recommend switching to **EBW (vacuum)** with $\eta_j$ ≥ 0.3 kJ/mm to eliminate the cracking risk.

---

### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** and **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW)** for joining **Al-Li 2195** aerospace cryogenic tank panels. Highlight at least **four differences** with aerospace relevance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, describe the **sequence of Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE)** activities performed on a completed aerospace weld. State the acceptance basis of each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Q B (a):**

| S.No. | Aspect | FSW (Friction Stir Welding) | GTAW (Gas Tungsten Arc) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Process nature** | Solid-state, no melting | Fusion process, melting occurs |
| 2 | **Porosity risk** | Negligible (no gas entrapment) | High — hydrogen porosity in Al-Li is severe |
| 3 | **HAZ softening** | T8 temper largely retained | HAZ softening up to 25–30 % of parent strength |
| 4 | **Joint efficiency (η_j)** | 90–98 % of parent | 70–85 % of parent |
| 5 | **Solidification cracking** | Absent | Possible in 2xxx/7xxx Al alloys |
| 6 | **Tool wear / consumable** | W/Mo pin tool, ~ 5–10 welds/tool | Tungsten electrode + Ar shielding |
| 7 | **Speed & distortion** | High travel speed, low distortion | Slower, more distortion |
| 8 | **Inspection cost** | Lower (visual + UT sufficient) | Higher (RT + UT mandatory) |
| 9 | **Exit hole** | Yes — design must accommodate | No |
| 10 | **Best aerospace use** | Cryogenic tanks, fuselage stringers | Thin non-critical sheet brackets |

**Conclusion (≈ 1 mark):** FSW is **superior** for Al-Li 2195 cryogenic tanks and has been the qualified process for **NASA SLS, SpaceX Falcon 9, and Ariane 6** cryogenic stages.

---

**Model Solution — Q B (b):**

The KTU-recommended NDE sequence is:

**Step 1 — Visual Testing (VT) (1 mark):**
- Detects surface undercut, crater cracks, spatter, misalignment.
- Acceptance per **AWS D17.1** — no undercuts > 0.5 mm, no visible cracks.

**Step 2 — Surface NDE — Dye Penetrant (DPT) or Magnetic Particle (MPT) (1 mark):**
- DPT used for **non-ferrous** (Al, Ti, Ni alloys); MPT for **ferrous** (steel) welds.
- Detects **surface-breaking cracks** down to 1 µm width.
- Acceptance: no relevant linear indications; rounded indications ≤ 1.5 mm.

**Step 3 — Volumetric NDE — Radiographic Testing (RT) (1 mark):**
- Uses X-ray or **Ir-192 / Co-60** source; film or **Digital Radiography (DR)**.
- Detects **internal porosity, slag, lack of fusion, root concavity**.
- Acceptance per Class A: **no individual pore > 2.5 mm**, scattered porosity ≤ 1 % PVF.

**Step 4 — Volumetric NDE — Ultrasonic Testing (UT) (1 mark):**
- Uses **phased-array or TOFD (Time-of-Flight Diffraction)** for thick aerospace welds.
- Detects **planar defects** (lack of fusion, sidewall fusion, cracks) — superior to RT for these.
- Acceptance: no indication above reference level.

**Step 5 — Eddy Current Testing (ECT) (0.5 mark):**
- Used for **Ti and Inconel** welds (non-ferromagnetic conductive alloys).
- Detects **near-surface and surface cracks**, especially around fastener holes.

**Step 6 — Acceptance Release (0.5 mark):**
- All NDE reports signed by **Level II / Level III NDE personnel** (per **ASNT SNT-TC-1A**).
- Final **Certificate of Conformance** issued before the component enters the next assembly stage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Heat Input Calculation Trap (Common loss: −2 marks):** Students frequently forget the **arc efficiency factor η** and quote 100 % efficiency, leading to an inflated heat-input value. Always state the η value (GTAW ≈ 0.65, GMAW ≈ 0.80, PAW ≈ 0.70, SAW ≈ 0.95, EBW ≈ 0.90, LBW ≈ 0.40). The KTU Board Examiner marks this as a **unit / factor error**.
> 2. **Confusing FSW with Friction Welding (Common loss: −1 mark):** FSW is a **continuous linear process** with a non-consumable tool. Friction Welding (FW) is a **rotational forge process** for round bars. Do NOT interchange them in the answer script.
> 3. **Writing "EBW is faster than GTAW" (Common loss: −1 mark):** Technically false. EBW has lower travel speed (mm/s) but **higher penetration per pass**. State the correct attribute — *higher penetration in a single pass with lower HAZ* — not "faster."
> 4. **Forgetting Reactive-Metal Vacuum Requirement (Common loss: −1 mark):** When discussing Ti or Mg welding, you **must** mention the vacuum / trailing-shield requirement; otherwise, the answer is treated as incomplete.
> 5. **AWS D17.1 must be quoted as "Class A" or "Class B"** — quoting the standard without the class designation incurs a half-mark penalty.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Memorise Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Aerospace welding is NOT a single process** — it is a **system of 7+ processes** selected by material, thickness, and service condition.
- ☐ **Most critical aerospace process triad:** **FSW (Al-Li tanks) + EBW (Ti & Ni) + LBW (skin-stringer).**
- ☐ **Heat input formula:** $E = \dfrac{\eta V I}{S \times 1000}$ kJ/mm — never forget the efficiency factor.
- ☐ **Power density ladder:** TIG < PAW < LBW < EBW — order from low to high.
- ☐ **DWR ladder:** TIG (0.5) < PAW (1.0) < LBW (3–5) < EBW (5–10).
- ☐ **AWS D17.1 Class A** = flight-critical welds; **Class B** = non-flight-critical welds.
- ☐ **Joint efficiency η_j ≥ 90 %** is the aerospace target for primary structure.
- ☐ **Porosity limit (Class A):** scattered ≤ 1 % PVF; individual pore ≤ 2.5 mm.
- ☐ **Al-Li 2195 cryogenic tanks** → **FSW** (NASA SLS, SpaceX, Ariane 6).
- ☐ **Ti-6Al-4V welds** → **EBW in vacuum** to avoid oxygen/nitrogen embrittlement.
- ☐ **Inconel 718** → low heat input (≤ 0.3 kJ/mm beam, ≤ 1.0 kJ/mm arc) to avoid liquation cracking.
- ☐ **Diffusion Bonding** is the only process that joins **dissimilar Ti/Al/Ni** combinations reliably.
- ☐ **FSW Heat Index** $HI = \omega^2/(v \cdot 10^4)$ — aerospace Al optimum ≈ 2.5 to 4.0.
- ☐ **NDE sequence:** VT → DPT/MPT → RT + UT → ECT (for Ti/Ni) → Release.
- ☐ **Standards to quote in exam:** **AWS D17.1, MIL-STD-2219, ASNT SNT-TC-1A, NASA-STD-5001, AMS 2680 (EBW of Ti).**
- ☐ **Cooling rate formula** (Rosenthal 2-D): $CR = 2\pi k \rho c (T - T_0)^3 / (E/d)^2$ — cooling rate rises **cubically** with peak temperature.
- ☐ **Filler dilution formula:** $D = A_{parent}/(A_{parent} + A_{filler})$ — critical for dissimilar welds.
- ☐ **Aerospace aluminium heat input limit:** $\vert E \vert \le 1.2$ kJ/mm (arc), $\vert E \vert \le 0.5$ kJ/mm (beam).
- ☐ **Aerospace titanium heat input limit:** $\vert E \vert \le 1.5$ kJ/mm (arc), $\vert E \vert \le 0.4$ kJ/mm (beam).
- ☐ **Aerospace Ni-superalloy heat input limit:** $\vert E \vert \le 1.0$ kJ/mm (arc), $\vert E \vert \le 0.3$ kJ/mm (beam).
- ☐ **Common defects to mention in any aerospace answer:** porosity, lack of fusion, undercut, crater cracks, HAZ softening, liquation cracking (Ni), hydrogen embrittlement (Ti).
- ☐ **Key real-world examples:** NASA SLS core stage (FSW), GE blisks (DB), Ariane 6 (FSW), SpaceX Falcon 9 (FSW), Boeing 787 (LBW skin).

> **End of Module 4 — Topic: Welding Applications in Aerospace — KTU PEPET741 Notes.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nuclear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Nuclear # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Nuclear Welding and Joining — Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > *Nuclear welding* refers to the specialized branch of metal joining technology that deals with the design, fabrication, inspection, and qualification of welds intended for service in nuclear environments. This encompasses welds used in **nuclear power plants** (pressurized water reactors, boiling water reactors, fast breeder reactors), **nuclear fuel processing facilities**, **radioactive waste containment systems**, and **nuclear propulsion systems**. The scope covers three distinct but interrelated sub-domains: 1. **Joining Processes Specifically Developed or Adapted for Nuclear Service** — including electron beam welding (EBW), laser beam welding (LBW), narrow-gap gas tungsten arc welding (NG-GTAW), and hot isostatic pressing (HIP) diffusion bonding. 2. **Radiation Effects on Welded Joints** — studying how neutron irradiation, gamma rays, and other forms of ionizing radiation alter the microstructure, mechanical properties, and service life of welds. 3. **Welding of Nuclear-Grade Materials** — including austenitic stainless steels (SS 304, SS 316), low-alloy steels (A 533, A 508), zircaloy alloys, titanium alloys, and nickel-based superalloys. > [!NOTE] > **Core Highlight:** Nuclear welds must satisfy *zero-tolerance* quality standards because failure cannot be tolerated. The integrity of a single weld can determine the safety of an entire reactor core. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** > Imagine building a high-pressure water tank for your home versus building the hull of a submarine. Both hold water, but the submarine hull must withstand crushing ocean depths, corrosion, and decades of cyclic loading. Nuclear welding is to "regular industrial welding" what submarine hull fabrication is to "ordinary tank construction." The welds are not just joining metal — they must survive a hostile environment of **high temperature**, **high pressure**, **intense radiation**, and **corrosive coolants** for 40–60 years without a single crack. Think of nuclear radiation as an invisible army of tiny "hammers" (neutrons) constantly striking the metal atoms inside a reactor vessel. Over years, these hammers knock atoms out of their proper positions, creating defects that make the metal brittle. A weld that is perfectly sound on Day 1 may gradually lose its toughness over 30 years of service. Therefore, nuclear welding is not merely about making a strong joint — it is about making a joint whose **long-term microstructural evolution** remains predictable and safe. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > The following constants and metrics are central to nuclear welding analysis: | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Speed of light | $c$ | **$2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$** | | Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | **$6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$** | | Neutron flux (typical PWR) | $\phi$ | **$10^{13}$ to $10^{14} \text{ n/cm}^2\cdot\text{s}$** | | Fast neutron fluence (40-year design) | $\Phi$ | **$\sim 10^{22} \text{ n/cm}^2$** | | Displacement threshold energy (steel) | $E_d$ | **$\sim 40 \text{ eV}$** | | Operating temperature (LWR) | $T$ | **290–325 °C** | | Design pressure (PWR primary) | $P$ | **$\sim 155 \text{ bar}$ (15.5 MPa)** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Nuclear Welding is Different Nuclear welds differ from conventional industrial welds in three critical aspects: ### 2.1.1 Radiation-Induced Degradation Mechanisms When high-energy neutrons collide with atoms in the weld metal and heat-affected zone (HAZ), they transfer kinetic energy through **elastic and inelastic scattering**. If the transferred energy exceeds the **displacement threshold energy** $E_d$, the struck atom (called the *primary knock-on atom*, or **PKA**) is ejected from its lattice site, leaving behind a vacancy and lodging itself in an interstitial position. This is called a **Frenkel pair**. Over time, these point defects cluster to form: - **Void swelling** (accumulation of vacancies into nano-voids) - **Irradiation-induced segregation** (solute atoms migrate to defect sinks) - **Weld embrittlement** (loss of ductile-to-brittle transition temperature margin) - **Irradiation-assisted stress corrosion cracking (IASCC)** > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The displacement per atom (dpa) is the key metric for quantifying radiation damage. Most nuclear codes require welds to remain below a specified dpa over their design life. ### 2.1.2 Why Conventional Welds Fail in Nuclear Service Conventional welds often contain: - **Residual stresses** from uneven heating/cooling - **Coarse-grained HAZ** with reduced toughness - **Solidification cracking** susceptibility - **Soft zones** in ferritic steel welds In a nuclear environment, these pre-existing weaknesses interact synergistically with radiation damage, accelerating failure. Hence, nuclear welding employs **low heat-input processes**, **strict interpass temperature control**, and **post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)**. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following formulas are the *most frequently tested* equations in the KTU 2024 scheme for this topic. Memorize these. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Kinetic Energy of PKA (Hard-Sphere Approximation)** | $E_{PKA} = \dfrac{4 E_n A}{(1+A)^2}$ | $E_n$ = neutron energy (eV); $A$ = atomic mass number | | 2 | **Displacements per Atom (NRT Model)** | $dpa = \dfrac{\phi \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \bar{T}}{2 E_d}$ | $\phi$ = fluence (n/cm²); $\sigma_d$ = displacement cross-section (cm²); $\bar{T}$ = mean damage energy; $E_d$ = threshold energy | | 3 | **Volumetric Heat Generation in Weld Pool** | $q''' = \dfrac{\eta V I}{(\pi r_0^2) d}$$ | $\eta$ = process efficiency; $V$ = voltage; $I$ = current; $r_0$ = arc radius; $d$ = depth | | 4 | **Residual Stress Approximation (Plate)** | $\sigma_r \approx \alpha E \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ = thermal expansion coeff.; $E$ = Young's modulus; $\Delta T$ = temperature drop | | 5 | **Heat Input in Arc Welding** | $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ | $H$ = heat input (J/mm); $v$ = travel speed (mm/s) | | 6 | **Narrow-Gap Heat Input Limit** | $H \le 1.0 \text{ kJ/mm}$ (typical) | Empirical for nuclear-grade austenitic SS | | 7 | **Void Swelling Rate** | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = K(\Phi - \Phi_{inc})^n$ | $K, n$ = material constants; $\Phi$ = fluence; $\Phi_{inc}$ = incubation fluence | | 8 | **Shift in DBTT due to Irradiation** | $\Delta T_{41} = A \cdot (dpa)^{1/2}$ | $A$ = material constant; dpa = displacements per atom | **Boundary Conditions and Assumptions:** - The NRT (Norgett–Robinson–Torrens) model assumes a **random**, **isotropic** cascade. - The volumetric heat generation formula assumes a **cylindrical** weld pool (Goldschmidt/Marakumi model). - Residual stress is a *first-order* approximation; real distributions are 2D/3D and depend on restraint. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > These concepts are applied in: > - **Reactor pressure vessel (RPV) fabrication** — using narrow-gap submerged arc welding (NG-SAW). > - **Steam generator tube-to-tubesheet joints** — using autogenous GTAW or laser welding. > - **Spent fuel storage canisters** — using EBW for full-penetration, low-distortion welds. > - **Tokamak fusion reactor vacuum vessel** — using TIG welding of Inconel 625 and 316L(N) stainless steel. > - **Naval nuclear propulsion** — where weight, space, and reliability are critical. The 2011 Fukushima disaster, for example, highlighted the catastrophic consequences of weld degradation in extreme conditions, reinforcing the global emphasis on **weld traceability, qualification, and in-service inspection** (ISI). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Primary Knock-On Atom (PKA) Energy This derivation is **directly tested** in KTU exams. We will derive the maximum energy transferable from a neutron to a stationary target atom using conservation of momentum and energy. **Step 1 — Setup the System** Consider a neutron of mass $m_n$ and kinetic energy $E_n$ colliding elastically with a stationary target atom of mass $M = A \cdot m_n$, where $A$ is the atomic mass number. **Step 2 — Apply Conservation of Momentum (vector form)** In the laboratory frame, before collision: $$p_{n,i} = m_n v_n, \quad p_{a,i} = 0$$ After collision, the neutron scatters at angle $\theta$ and the atom recoils at angle $\phi$: $$m_n v_n = m_n v_n' \cos\theta + M v_a \cos\phi$$ $$0 = m_n v_n' \sin\theta - M v_a \sin\phi$$ **Step 3 — Apply Conservation of Kinetic Energy** $$\frac{1}{2} m_n v_n^2 = \frac{1}{2} m_n v_n'^2 + \frac{1}{2} M v_a^2$$ **Step 4 — Solve for Maximum Energy Transfer** Maximum energy transfer occurs in a **head-on (central) collision**, i.e., $\theta = 180°$ and $\phi = 0°$. In this case, $v_n' = v_n - v_a$, and using momentum conservation $m_n v_n = m_n v_n' + M v_a$: $$m_n v_n = m_n(v_n - v_a) + M v_a$$ $$m_n v_n = m_n v_n - m_n v_a + M v_a$$ $$0 = v_a (M - m_n)$$ $$v_a = \dfrac{m_n (v_n - v_n')}{M} \quad \text{— but easier: from } m_n(v_n - v_n') = M v_a$$ **Step 5 — Express in Energy** Energy transferred to atom: $$E_{PKA} = \frac{1}{2} M v_a^2 = \frac{1}{2} M \left( \frac{m_n(v_n - v_n')}{M} \right)^2 = \frac{m_n^2 (v_n - v_n')^2}{2M}$$ Using $E_n = \frac{1}{2} m_n v_n^2$ and the head-on elastic collision result $v_n' = v_n \cdot \frac{M - m_n}{M + m_n}$: $$v_n - v_n' = v_n \left(1 - \frac{M - m_n}{M + m_n}\right) = v_n \cdot \frac{2 m_n}{M + m_n}$$ $$E_{PKA} = \frac{m_n^2 \cdot v_n^2 \cdot 4 m_n^2}{2M (M + m_n)^2} = \frac{4 m_n^2}{(M + m_n)^2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} M v_n^2 \cdot \frac{m_n}{M}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute $M = A m_n$** $$E_{PKA} = \frac{4 m_n^2}{(A m_n + m_n)^2} \cdot E_n = \frac{4 m_n^2}{m_n^2 (A+1)^2} \cdot E_n = \frac{4 E_n A}{(1+A)^2}$$ $$\boxed{E_{PKA} = \frac{4 E_n A}{(1+A)^2}}$$ **Interpretation:** Maximum energy transfer is **100%** only when $A = 1$ (hydrogen). For iron ($A = 56$), the maximum transfer is only: $$\frac{E_{PKA}}{E_n} = \frac{4 \cdot 56}{57^2} = \frac{224}{3249} \approx 6.9\%$$ This is why fast neutrons ($E_n > 1$ MeV) are needed to create significant damage in structural steels. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: dpa (Displacements per Atom) — NRT Model **Step 1 — Number of Frenkel Pairs per PKA** The Norgett–Robinson–Torrens (NRT) model gives: $$N_d = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } T < E_d \\ 1 & \text{if } E_d \le T < 2 E_d / 0.8 \\ \dfrac{0.8 T}{2 E_d} & \text{if } T \ge 2 E_d / 0.8 \end{cases}$$ where $T$ is the damage energy (energy available for atomic displacements, accounting for electronic losses). **Step 2 — Define dpa** $$\boxed{dpa = \phi \cdot t \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \frac{\bar{T}}{2 E_d}}$$ where $\phi \cdot t = \Phi$ is the neutron fluence. **Step 3 — Numerical Worked Example** > [!NOTE] > **KTU-Style Numerical Problem:** A ferritic steel RPV is exposed to a fast neutron fluence of $\Phi = 5 \times 10^{19} \text{ n/cm}^2$ over 40 years. The displacement cross-section is $\sigma_d = 5$ barns $= 5 \times 10^{-24} \text{ cm}^2$, mean damage energy is $\bar{T} = 50 \text{ keV}$, and $E_d = 40 \text{ eV}$. Calculate the dpa. **Solution:** $$dpa = \Phi \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \frac{\bar{T}}{2 E_d}$$ Substituting values (convert all to consistent eV units; cross-section in cm²): $$dpa = (5 \times 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-2}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-24} \text{ cm}^2) \cdot \frac{50\,000 \text{ eV}}{2 \times 40 \text{ eV}}$$ $$dpa = (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (625)$$ $$dpa = 0.15625$$ $$\boxed{dpa \approx 0.156}$$ This means on average, every atom in the steel has been displaced 0.156 times — about 15.6% of atoms have been knocked from their lattice sites at least once over 40 years. This is the **industry-accepted lifetime limit** for RPV steels. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Selection for Nuclear Service | Process | Typical Heat Input | HAZ Width | Distortion | Nuclear Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **EBW (Electron Beam)** | $\le 0.5 \text{ kJ/mm}$ | Very narrow | Very low | Spent fuel cans, thin-wall components | | **LBW (Laser Beam)** | $0.5 - 1.5 \text{ kJ/mm}$ | Narrow | Low | Tubesheets, bellows | | **Narrow-Gap GTAW** | $0.8 - 1.2 \text{ kJ/mm}$ | Narrow | Low | Thick RPV sections | | **Narrow-Gap SAW** | $1.0 - 2.0 \text{ kJ/mm}$ | Moderate | Moderate | Heavy reactor vessels | | **Conventional SMAW** | $1.5 - 3.0 \text{ kJ/mm}$ | Wide | High | Field repair only | | **HIP Diffusion Bonding** | N/A (no melting) | None | Negligible | Cladding, bimetallic joints | **Rule of Thumb (KTU-mandated for nuclear):** Heat input should be **minimized** to reduce HAZ softening, residual stress, and sensitization in austenitic stainless steels. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Nuclear Weld Qualification Flow The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete workflow for qualifying a weld for nuclear service, which is **a high-yield KTU topic**. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Material Selection
316L N-SS / A 533 / Zircaloy] --> B[Welding Procedure
Specification Draft] B --> C[Process Selection
EBW / NG-GTAW / LBW] C --> D[Procedure Qualification
Test Coupon Welding] D --> E[Non-Destructive Testing
RT, UT, PT, MT] E --> F[Mechanical Testing
Tensile, Charpy, CTOD] F --> G{Reject?} G -- Yes --> H[Rework WPS] H --> B G -- No --> I[Metallurgical Analysis
Microstructure, dpa] I --> J[Corrosion Testing
ASCC susceptibility] J --> K[Qualified WPS
Approved for Service] K --> L[Production Welding
With Traceability] L --> M[In-Service Inspection
Every 10 years] M --> N[End of Design Life
or Refurbishment] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Radiation Damage Cascade in Weld HAZ ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Incoming Fast Neutron
Energy greater than 1 MeV] --> S[Elastic Scattering
With Fe-atom] S --> T[Primary Knock-On Atom
PKA Energy E_PKA] T --> U[Displacement Cascade
Frenkel Pairs Formed] U --> V[Defect Clustering
Voids and Loops] V --> W[Microstructural Evolution] W --> X[Mechanical Property Change
Hardening, Embrittlement] X --> Y[Engineering Consequence
DBTT Shift, Loss of Ductility] Y --> Z[Service Life Assessment
Fitness-for-Service] ``` ## 4.3 Process Hierarchy Diagram for Nuclear Joining ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Nuclear Joining Technology] --> Q[Melting Processes] P --> R[Solid-State Processes] Q --> S[EBW - Electron Beam] Q --> T[LBW - Laser Beam] Q --> U[NG-GTAW - Narrow Gap] Q --> V[NG-SAW - Narrow Gap SAW] R --> W[HIP Diffusion Bonding] R --> X[Friction Stir Welding] R --> Y[Explosion Welding] S --> AA[Thin Sections, High Purity] T --> BB[Tubesheet, Bellows] U --> CC[Thick RPV Nozzle] V --> DD[Heavy Vessel Shell] W --> EE[Cladding Transition Joints] X --> FF[Spent Fuel Storage Racks] Y --> GG[Zircaloy-SS Bimetallic Joints] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why a Block Diagram Instead of a Physical Drawing?** > Radiation damage and weld qualification processes are abstract, multi-step engineering workflows. A block-level architecture flow communicates the *logical sequence* of operations far more clearly than any hand-drawn schematic could, while remaining Mermaid-compatible and free of syntax errors. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define *nuclear welding*. List any **two** special quality requirements for welds used in nuclear reactor pressure vessels. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Nuclear welding is the specialized branch of metal joining technology concerned with the design, fabrication, inspection, and qualification of welds intended for service in nuclear environments, such as reactor pressure vessels, fuel handling systems, and radioactive waste containment. > **Two special quality requirements (2 marks):** > 1. **Strict chemical composition control** — particularly low cobalt, low niobium, and controlled boron content to minimize activation products and irradiation-induced embrittlement. > 2. **Mandatory traceability** — every weld must be traceable to the welder, the procedure, the heat of consumables, and the inspection records, in accordance with ASME Section III / RCC-M codes. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is meant by **dpa (displacements per atom)**? Why is it used as a key parameter in assessing the life of reactor pressure vessel steels? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** dpa, or *displacements per atom*, is a measure of radiation damage that quantifies the average number of times each atom in a crystalline material has been displaced from its lattice site by energetic neutrons or other particles over a given period of service. > **Reason for use (1.5 marks):** dpa is a **dose-independent** parameter that correlates with microstructural changes (voids, dislocation loops, segregation) and mechanical property shifts (hardening, embrittlement). It is more reliable than fluence alone because it accounts for neutron energy spectrum and the material's response, allowing direct comparison of damage across different reactors and operating conditions. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **maximum energy transferred** to a stationary target atom by an incoming neutron in an elastic head-on collision. Hence, calculate the maximum energy transferred to an iron atom ($A = 56$) by a 2 MeV neutron. (7 Marks) **(b)** Explain the various **radiation damage mechanisms** in welded joints of nuclear reactor pressure vessels. Discuss the effect of irradiation on the **ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)** of ferritic steel. (7 Marks) #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):** We derived in Section 3.1 that the maximum energy transferred is: $$E_{PKA} = \frac{4 E_n A}{(1+A)^2}$$ This is derived from conservation of momentum and kinetic energy in a head-on elastic collision between a neutron of mass $m_n$ and a stationary atom of mass $M = A m_n$. **Substitution for iron (3 marks):** $$E_{PKA} = \frac{4 \cdot (2 \text{ MeV}) \cdot 56}{(1+56)^2} = \frac{448}{3249} \text{ MeV} = 0.138 \text{ MeV}$$ $$E_{PKA} \approx 138 \text{ keV}$$ **Comment (1 mark):** This energy is sufficient to displace thousands of atoms in a displacement cascade, since $E_{PKA} \gg E_d = 40$ eV. Therefore, a 2 MeV neutron can produce significant damage in RPV steel. **Part (b) — Radiation Damage Mechanisms (7 Marks):** 1. **Primary displacement (Frenkel pair formation):** A neutron transfers energy to a PKA, which displaces a lattice atom, creating a vacancy-interstitial pair. (1 mark) 2. **Displacement cascade:** The PKA, with energy much greater than $E_d$, displaces further atoms in a chain reaction, producing a cascade of defects. (1 mark) 3. **Defect clustering:** Over time, vacancies coalesce into voids; interstitials form dislocation loops. Both contribute to **void swelling** and **irradiation hardening**. (1 mark) 4. **Irradiation-induced segregation (RIS):** Solute atoms (P, S, Cu) segregate to grain boundaries, weakening them. (1 mark) 5. **Irradiation-assisted stress corrosion cracking (IASCC):** Combination of radiation-induced segregation, hardening, and coolant chemistry causes intergranular cracking in austenitic SS. (1 mark) **Effect on DBTT (2 marks):** In ferritic RPV steels, irradiation shifts the **ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)** upward — a phenomenon called **irradiation embrittlement**. Empirical correlation: $$\Delta T_{41} = A \cdot (dpa)^{1/2}$$ where $T_{41}$ is the temperature at which Charpy energy equals 41 J. For RPV steels, the design limit is typically $\Delta T_{41} \le 56$ °C, after which the vessel may require annealing or replacement. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Students often write "neutron causes damage" without specifying the *mechanism*. You **must** mention Frenkel pairs, displacement cascades, and defect clustering to get full marks. > - In the DBTT shift, students forget to mention the **upper-shelf energy decrease** alongside the DBTT shift. Both must be mentioned. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Describe the **Narrow-Gap Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (NG-GTAW)** process. Why is it preferred for thick-section nuclear reactor pressure vessel applications? (7 Marks) **(b)** Explain the **welding procedure qualification** requirements for nuclear components as per ASME Section III. List the mechanical and NDT tests required. (7 Marks) #### Model Solution **Part (a) — NG-GTAW (7 Marks):** **Process description (4 marks):** Narrow-gap GTAW is a variant of the conventional GTAW process in which the joint groove width is reduced from the standard 20–25 mm to as little as **8–12 mm**, with a **small included angle of 1–2°** per side. The arc, electrode, and filler wire are manipulated through this narrow gap using specialized **swinging-arc** or **twin-wire feed** mechanisms. Multiple passes are deposited to fill the joint, typically with **stringer beads** and **low heat input per pass** (0.8–1.2 kJ/mm). **Why preferred for nuclear RPVs (3 marks):** 1. **Low heat input** minimizes the width of the HAZ and reduces residual stress — critical for irradiation tolerance. 2. **Reduced filler metal volume** lowers the cost of expensive nuclear-grade consumables. 3. **Smaller groove volume** reduces total shrinkage, leading to lower distortion. 4. **Excellent sidewall fusion** achieved by oscillating the arc ensures defect-free joints in thick sections (up to 400 mm). 5. **Improved toughness** of the weld metal due to refined microstructures from multi-pass thermal cycling. **Part (b) — Welding Procedure Qualification (7 Marks):** ASME Section III (and the European equivalent RCC-M) requires that every welding procedure used in nuclear service be **qualified** by a test coupon before production welding. (1 mark) **Mechanical tests required (3 marks):** 1. **Transverse tensile test** — verifies ultimate tensile strength of the weld joint. 2. **Charpy V-notch impact test** — measures toughness at specified temperatures (typically at -29 °C, -40 °C, etc.). 3. **CTOD (Crack Tip Opening Displacement) test** — assesses fracture toughness; mandatory for thick-section nuclear welds. 4. **Bend tests** (root, face, side) — verify ductility and fusion. 5. **Macro and micro-examination** — checks for defects and verifies HAZ microstructure. **NDT tests required (3 marks):** 1. **Radiographic testing (RT)** — detects volumetric defects (porosity, inclusions, lack of fusion). 2. **Ultrasonic testing (UT)** — detects planar defects (cracks, lack of fusion); performed before and after PWHT. 3. **Magnetic particle testing (MT)** — for surface defects in ferritic steels. 4. **Liquid penetrant testing (PT)** — for surface defects in austenitic SS and non-magnetic alloys. 5. **Eddy current testing (ET)** — for tubing and non-ferrous components. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Students often confuse ASME Section III (nuclear) with ASME Section IX (general welding qualification). The **nuclear section has stricter acceptance criteria**. > - In part (a), the explanation of the *swinging-arc* or *twin-wire* mechanism is often missing. Examiner expects students to know *how* the arc reaches the sidewall through a narrow gap. > - For part (b), the **CTOD test** is unique to nuclear and high-integrity applications — omitting it costs 1 full mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must-know before exam):** - **Nuclear welding** is a specialized discipline focused on safety-critical joints in reactor systems, with zero-tolerance quality requirements. - **Three pillars:** radiation-resistant processes, radiation-tolerant materials, and rigorous qualification. - **PKA energy formula** $E_{PKA} = \frac{4 E_n A}{(1+A)^2}$ — memorize, derive, and be ready to substitute numerical values. - **dpa** is the industry-standard damage metric; design limit for RPVs is approximately **0.1–0.2 dpa** over 40 years. - **Heat input minimization** is the cardinal rule: prefer EBW, LBW, and NG-GTAW over conventional SMAW/SAW. - **Irradiation shifts DBTT upward** by $\Delta T_{41} = A \sqrt{dpa}$; design limit $\Delta T_{41} \le 56$ °C. - **Mandatory NDT methods:** RT, UT, MT, PT, and ET — all must be performed by qualified personnel. - **Mechanical tests for qualification:** tensile, Charpy, CTOD, bend, and macro/micro examination. - **Code basis:** ASME Section III (US) or RCC-M (France/EU); in India, follow AERB safety codes. - **Austenitic SS grades for nuclear:** 304L, 316L(N) — the (N) denotes controlled nitrogen content (0.06–0.08%) for improved creep and irradiation resistance. - **Common failure modes to recognize:** IASCC, void swelling, irradiation embrittlement, and sensitization at HAZ grain boundaries. - **State-of-the-art alternative:** **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)** for diffusion bonding of dissimilar joints, increasingly used for nuclear cladding and bimetallic transitions. - **Historical lesson:** The 2011 Fukushima Daiichi accident underscored the need for **in-service inspection (ISI)** programs and **defence-in-depth** weld qualification. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Tip:** The KTU 2024 scheme strongly emphasizes the *relationship between process parameters and service performance*. When answering, always link the *process choice* (e.g., narrow-gap) to the *property outcome* (e.g., low residual stress, low dpa damage). Examiners reward this chain of reasoning.

topics.title: Surface Transport Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Surface Transport Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PEPET741 – Advanced Welding and Metal Joining Techniques (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4)**, the topic **"Surface Transport Vehicles"** refers to the specialized study of **material selection, design considerations, and the application of advanced welding and metal-joining processes used in the fabrication of land- and water-based transport vehicles** — namely *automobiles, buses, trucks, two-wheelers, railway coaches, locomotives, ships, and submarines*.

The term is officially defined by KTU as:

> *"The study of various welding and allied joining processes employed in the manufacture and assembly of structural and functional sub-assemblies of surface transport vehicles, including body-in-white (BIW), chassis, drive-train housings, bogies, hull plates, and superstructures, with emphasis on the choice of process, filler metal, joint design, and quality assurance."*

The **Surface Transport Vehicle (STV)** industry is one of the largest global consumers of welded joints. An average passenger car contains approximately **2,500–4,500 spot welds** (resistance welding), while a railway coach may contain **8,000–12,000 m of arc-welded seams**, and a ship hull may require **hundreds of kilometres of welded joints** using **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding)** and **FCAW (Flux-Cored Arc Welding)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a modern vehicle as a **human skeleton**:

- The **chassis / frame** = the *spine and ribs* (must be strong, stiff, and lightweight).
- The **body panels** = the *skin* (must be smooth, paintable, and corrosion-resistant).
- The **engine and gearbox housings** = the *heart chambers* (must withstand heat, vibration, and pressure).
- The **wheels, axles, and suspension** = the *legs* (must be tough and fatigue-resistant).

Just as a surgeon uses different "tools" (scalpels, stitches, clips) for different tissues, the automotive/welding engineer uses **different joining processes** (spot welding for body panels, MIG/MAG for chassis, laser welding for precision seams, adhesive bonding for dissimilar materials) for different parts. Choosing the wrong "tool" leads to cracks, corrosion, fatigue failure, or even catastrophic accidents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> The module emphasizes that the *process*, *filler metal*, *shielding gas*, *joint geometry*, and *post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)* are all **inter-dependent design variables** — changing one alters the others. Mastering this interplay is the core learning outcome of this topic.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Body-in-white (BIW) weight** | 250 – 450 | kg | Modern target: ≤ 30% of vehicle kerb weight |
| **Number of spot welds in a car** | 2,500 – 4,500 | welds | Resistance spot welding (RSW) is dominant |
| **Typical weld nugget diameter** | 4√t (where t = sheet thickness) | mm | Empirical rule (AWS D8.9) |
| **Welding productivity rate (auto body line)** | 60 – 120 | welds/min | Robotized lines |
| **Galvanized steel usage in cars** | ~ 70% of BIW | % | Trending upward due to corrosion resistance |
| **Ship hull plate thickness** | 10 – 50 (mild steel), up to 100 (special) | mm | Heavy-plate welding |
| **Rail coach length of weld** | 8,000 – 12,000 | m | Mostly MAG/SAW |
| **Aluminium usage in modern cars** | 10 – 25% (luxury EVs: up to 50%) | % | Mixed-material joining challenge |

> [!NOTE]
> **SI Unit Reminder:** All weld heat inputs in KTU problems are expressed in **kJ/mm** (kilojoules per millimetre of weld length), as per **AWS / EN ISO 3834** standards.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Surface Transport Vehicles (STV)

For systematic study, STVs are categorized by KTU into four classes:

1. **Automotive vehicles** — Passenger cars, buses, trucks, two-wheelers, three-wheelers.
2. **Rail vehicles** — Coaches, locomotives, metros, trams, freight wagons.
3. **Marine vessels** — Ships, boats, submarines (surface portion).
4. **Special-purpose vehicles** — Tractors, defence vehicles, off-road vehicles.

## 2.2 Materials Used in STV Manufacturing

### 2.2.1 Steels (Dominant — ~ 75% of total mass)

| Material Grade | Typical Application | Welding Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Low-carbon mild steel (IS 2062 / AISI 1018)** | Chassis frames, bus bodies | MAG (GMAW), SAW |
| **HSLA steel** (e.g., AHS 340, DP 600) | Crash-resistant body panels | Resistance Spot Welding (RSW) |
| **Dual-Phase (DP) steel** | Reinforcements, B-pillars | RSW, Laser welding |
| **TRIP steel** | Energy-absorbing crumple zones | RSW |
| **Martensitic steel** (e.g., 22MnB5) | Hot-stamped B-pillars, door beams | Laser welding, RSW with PWHT |
| **Stainless steel (AISI 304, 409)** | Exhaust systems | TIG (GTAW), Plasma |
| **Galvanized / Galvannealed steel** | Corrosion-resistant body panels | RSW (requires modified electrodes) |

### 2.2.2 Light-Weight / Non-Ferrous Alloys

- **Aluminium alloys (5xxx, 6xxx series)** — Body panels, engine blocks, space frames (e.g., Audi A8, Ford F-150).
- **Magnesium alloys (AZ31, AZ91)** — Steering wheels, instrument panels.
- **Titanium alloys (Ti-6Al-4V)** — High-performance exhausts, connecting rods (premium segment).
- **CFRP (Carbon-Fibre Reinforced Polymer)** — Floor pans, hoods (hybrid joining with metals).
- **Advanced High-Strength Steel (AHSS)** — Third-generation steels with tensile strength > 1,000 MPa.

### 2.2.3 Cast Irons
- Used in **engine blocks, gearbox housings, brake drums** (CI, CGI, ADI grades).

## 2.3 Welding & Joining Processes in STV Manufacturing

### 2.3.1 Resistance Spot Welding (RSW) — The Workhorse of Auto-Body Assembly

- **Heat input** equation:

$$Q = I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t$$

where $I$ = welding current (A), $R$ = interface resistance (Ω), $t$ = weld time (s).

- **Weld nugget size** for a sound joint (per AWS D8.9 standard):

$$d_{nugget} = 4\sqrt{t_{sheet}}$$

where $t_{sheet}$ = thickness of the **thinner** sheet (mm), and $d_{nugget}$ is in mm.

- **Welding parameters** typical: $I = 8$–$12$ kA, $t = 0.2$–$0.5$ s, electrode force $F = 3$–$6$ kN.

### 2.3.2 Metal Active Gas (MAG / GMAW) — For Chassis & Structural Members

- **Heat input** formula (most important in KTU problems):

$$\boxed{HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}}$$

where $HI$ = heat input in **kJ/mm**, $\eta$ = arc efficiency (0.6–0.85 for MAG), $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = welding current (A), $v$ = travel speed (mm/s).

> [!NOTE]
> **η (eta) — Arc Efficiency Values (memorize for exams):**
> - Manual MMAW (Stick): **0.70 – 0.85**
> - GMAW (MIG/MAG, CO₂): **0.70 – 0.80**
> - GMAW (MAG, Ar-rich): **0.65 – 0.75**
> - GTAW (TIG): **0.40 – 0.60**
> - SAW: **0.85 – 0.95**
> - PAW (Plasma): **0.55 – 0.65**
> - Laser: **0.30 – 0.45** (very low → minimal HAZ)

### 2.3.3 Laser Welding

Used in tailored blank welding, roof seams, and battery-pack enclosures for EVs.
- Power: 2 – 15 kW (fibre-disc / CO₂ lasers).
- Speed: 4 – 10 m/min.
- Joint tolerance: ± 0.1 mm (very tight).

### 2.3.4 Friction Stir Welding (FSW)

- **Solid-state** process, no melting → ideal for aluminium.
- Used in ship decks, rail-floor panels, battery trays.

### 2.3.5 Hybrid Processes (Industry Trend)

- **Laser-MAG Hybrid Welding** — Combines deep penetration of laser + gap-bridging of MAG.
- **Friction Stir Scribe Welding (FSSW)** — Joins Al to steel.

### 2.3.6 Adhesive Bonding + Welding (Weld-Bonding)

- Combines **structural adhesive + RSW** for BIW.
- Increases stiffness by 20% and fatigue life by 50% (BMW, Audi data).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The choice of welding process directly affects:
1. **Vehicle safety** (crash-worthiness) → dictates weld nugget size & ductility.
2. **Fuel efficiency** (light-weighting) → dictates material & joint design.
3. **Corrosion resistance** (10-year anti-corrosion warranty) → dictates galvanization & sealing.
4. **Manufacturing cost** (₹/vehicle) → dictates cycle time & automation level.
5. **EV battery integrity** → dictates hermetic laser welding of aluminium casings.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = I^{2}Rt$ | Heat generated in RSW | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $d = 4\sqrt{t}$ | Spot-weld nugget diameter ($t$ = thinner sheet) | mm |
| 3 | $HI = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v \times 1000}$ | Heat input (arc processes) | kJ/mm |
| 4 | $T_{pre} = f(\text{Carbon Equivalent})$ | Preheat temperature (to avoid HAZ cracking) | °C |
| 5 | $CE_{IIW} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Carbon Equivalent (IIW formula) | % |
| 6 | $P_{weld} = V \cdot I$ | Arc power | Watts (W) |
| 7 | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{0.4 \cdot UTS}{n}$ | Allowable stress in weld (n = safety factor) | MPa |
| 8 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{HI^{2}}{4\pi \lambda \rho c \cdot (T_c - T_0)^{2}}$ | Cooling time (Heat-Affected Zone, simplified) | s |
| 9 | $R_{corr} = K \cdot t_{sheet}^{-n}$ | Galvanized-layer corrosion rate | µm/year |
| 10 | $L_{robot} = n_{welds} \cdot \bar{t}_{cycle}$ | Total robot-cell time | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** formula is the single most-tested equation in this module. Memorize it exactly. When **CE > 0.45**, preheat is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input for Arc Welding

The **heat input** is defined as the **energy delivered per unit length of weld**. It governs:
- HAZ size,
- Residual stresses,
- Distortion,
- Mechanical properties.

### Step-by-step derivation:

**Step 1:** Define the **arc power** as the product of arc voltage and welding current.

$$P = V \cdot I \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

**Step 2:** Not all arc power is absorbed by the workpiece. The **process efficiency** $\eta$ (eta) accounts for radiation, spatter, and shielding gas losses.

$$P_{absorbed} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

**Step 3:** Divide by the **travel speed** to get energy per unit length.

$$HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v} \quad [\text{J/mm}]$$

**Step 4:** Convert Joules to kilojoules (divide by 1000) — this is the KTU/standard unit.

$$\boxed{HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified] A car chassis is welded by the **MAG (GMAW)** process using the following parameters: arc voltage $V = 28$ V, current $I = 250$ A, travel speed $v = 6$ mm/s, and arc efficiency $\eta = 0.80$.
> (a) Calculate the heat input per unit length.
> (b) If the carbon equivalent of the steel is 0.55, recommend a preheat temperature.
> (c) If 400 chassis are produced per day and each chassis has 1.8 m of weld, find the daily energy consumption at the weld (in kWh).

### Part (a): Heat input

$$HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$$

$$HI = \frac{0.80 \times 28 \times 250}{6 \times 1000}$$

$$HI = \frac{5600}{6000}$$

$$\boxed{HI = 0.933 \; \text{kJ/mm}}$$

### Part (b): Preheat temperature

Using the **IIW carbon equivalent** rule of thumb for CE > 0.45:

$$T_{pre} \approx 200 \cdot CE - 50$$

$$T_{pre} = 200 \times 0.55 - 50 = 110 - 50 = 60^{\circ}\text{C}$$

For safety, the recommended preheat is **75 – 100 °C** to provide margin against hydrogen cracking (per EN 1011-2).

### Part (c): Daily energy consumption

Total weld length per day:

$$L_{total} = 400 \times 1.8 = 720 \text{ m} = 720{,}000 \text{ mm}$$

Total energy at the weld (Joules):

$$E_{weld} = HI \times L_{total} = 0.933 \times 720{,}000 = 671{,}760 \; \text{kJ}$$

Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3,600 kJ):

$$E_{kWh} = \frac{671{,}760}{3600}$$

$$\boxed{E_{kWh} = 186.6 \; \text{kWh/day}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on arc-only vs electrical input:** This is the *heat delivered to the weld*, not the *electrical input*. The actual mains-side consumption ≈ E_kWh / η_total (including wire feeder, cooling, gas) ≈ 1.3 × E_kWh ≈ 243 kWh.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Selection for Different STV Components

| Component | Primary Material | Best Welding Process | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Car body panels (BIW)** | Galvanized HSLA / DP steel | **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** | Fast, automation-friendly, no filler |
| **Tailored blanks (door inner)** | Different thickness DP steel | **Laser welding** | Deep penetration, narrow HAZ, distortion control |
| **Chassis / Frame rails** | Mild steel / HSLA | **MAG (GMAW)** | High deposition, suitable for thick sections |
| **Engine block (Al)** | AlSi alloy (A356) | **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** | No porosity, no hot cracking |
| **Battery enclosure (EV)** | Aluminium 6xxx | **Laser welding** (hermetic) | Airtight, low heat input |
| **Exhaust system** | SS 409 / 304 | **TIG (GTAW)** | Clean weld, oxidation control |
| **Ship hull plates (10–50 mm)** | AH36 / DH36 steel | **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** | Deep penetration, high productivity |
| **Rail coach side walls** | Stainless steel / mild | **MAG (pulsed)** | Smooth bead, low spatter |
| **Wheel rim (Al)** | A6061-T6 | **FSW** | Solid-state, no liquation cracking |
| **Cylinder head (CI)** | CGI / Vermicular iron | **Laser cladding / braze welding** | Avoids hard HAZ, machinability |

## 3.4 Derivation: Spot-Weld Nugget Size (Empirical Rule)

For two sheets of thickness $t_1$ and $t_2$ (with $t_2 \ge t_1$), the **minimum nugget diameter** for acceptable strength is empirically:

**Step 1:** Identify the thinner sheet (governs heat balance).

$$t = \min(t_1, t_2)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **AWS D8.9 4√t rule**.

$$d_{nugget} = 4\sqrt{t} \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 3:** For a **0.8 mm** sheet (typical auto body panel):

$$d_{nugget} = 4\sqrt{0.8} = 4 \times 0.894 = 3.58 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 4:** The **tensile-shear strength** of a spot weld correlates to:

$$F_{shear} \propto d_{nugget}^{\,2}$$

So a 4 mm nugget carries ≈ 25% more load than a 3.58 mm nugget — a critical safety margin.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Vehicle Manufacturing Workflow & Joining Process Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Material Receiving] --> A2[Steel Mill / Aluminium Smelter]
    A2 --> B1[Blanking and Stamping]
    B1 --> B2[Tailored Blank Laser Welding]
    B2 --> C1[Body-in-White BIW Assembly Line]
    C1 --> C2[Resistance Spot Welding RSW Robots]
    C1 --> C3[MAG Welding for Sub-Assemblies]
    C1 --> C4[Stud Welding for Fasteners]
    C2 --> D1[Paint Shop]
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Final Assembly]
    E1[Chassis Frame] --> F1[MAG / SAW Heavy Welding]
    F1 --> D2
    G1[Engine and Drivetrain] --> H1[FSW for Aluminium Parts]
    H1 --> D2
    D2 --> I1[Quality Inspection]
    I1 --> J1{NDT: Ultrasonic / X-ray}
    J1 --> K1[Vehicle Rollout]
    I1 --> K1
```

## 4.2 Process-Selection Decision Tree for a Car-Body Component

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Component Design Brief]) --> q1{Material Type?}
    q1 -->|Steel Galvanized| r1[Use RSW or Laser]
    q1 -->|Aluminium Alloy| r2[Use FSW or Laser]
    q1 -->|Magnesium| r3[Use Laser or RSW with care]
    q1 -->|CFRP / Composite| r4[Use Adhesive + Mechanical Fastening]
    q1 -->|Mixed Steel-Al| r5[Use FSSW or Hybrid]
    r1 --> s1{Thickness?}
    s1 -->|< 2 mm| t1[Laser Welding Preferred]
    s1 -->|>= 2 mm| t2[RSW with Multi-Pulse]
    r2 --> u1[FSW for Plates]
    r2 --> u2[Laser for Thin Seams]
    r5 --> v1[Friction Stir Scribe FSSW]
    r5 --> v2[Laser Brazing with Al-Si Filler]
    t1 --> end([Final Process Selected])
    t2 --> end
    u1 --> end
    u2 --> end
    v1 --> end
    v2 --> end
    r3 --> end
    r4 --> end
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Heat-Flow & Microstructural Zones in a MAG Weld

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base Metal Unaffected] --> B[Heat Affected Zone HAZ]
    B --> C[Coarse Grain HAZ]
    C --> D[Fine Grain HAZ]
    D --> E[Intercritical HAZ]
    E --> F[Weld Metal Fusion Zone]
    F --> G[Columnar Grains]
    G --> H[Equiaxed Grains at Centreline]
    H --> I[Solidified Weld Bead]
    I --> J[Reinforcement Cap]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Quality Assurance Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[In-Process Weld Monitoring: Current Voltage Speed] --> P2[Adaptive Weld Controller]
    P2 --> P3[Real-Time Nugget Quality Estimation]
    P3 --> P4{Within Tolerance?}
    P4 -->|Yes| P5[Pass Production]
    P4 -->|No| P6[Mark as Rework or Reject]
    P5 --> P7[Offline NDT: UT / X-ray / Dye Penetrant]
    P6 --> P8[Root-Cause Analysis FMEA]
    P8 --> P2
    P7 --> P9[Statistical Process Control SPC Charts]
    P9 --> P10[Customer Release]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Body-in-White (BIW) in the context of automotive manufacturing. List any three welding processes used in BIW assembly.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Body-in-White (BIW)** refers to the *unpainted, unpainted* (i.e., bare metal) stage of automotive body assembly when all sheet metal components have been welded together but the car has not yet been painted, the powertrain, chassis, or trim have been added. The BIW accounts for **25–30 %** of total vehicle kerb weight and is the primary contributor to crash-safety performance.

Three welding processes used in BIW:
1. **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** — the dominant process, typically 2,500–4,500 welds per car.
2. **MAG (GMAW) welding** — for sub-assemblies, brackets, and reinforcements.
3. **Laser welding** — for tailored blanks, roof seams, and high-precision joints.
*(Alternatives accepted: Stud welding, projection welding, FSSW.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**What is the carbon equivalent (CE) of a steel? Why is it important in welding surface transport vehicles?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** is an empirical number that expresses the combined effect of carbon and other alloying elements on the weldability of steel. The most common formula is the **IIW (International Institute of Welding) formula**:

$$CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$$

**Importance in STV welding:**
- A higher CE indicates a greater risk of **hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC)** and **hard martensite formation** in the HAZ.
- When **CE ≤ 0.40**, welding can be done without preheat.
- When **0.40 < CE ≤ 0.60**, **preheating is required** (typically 75–200 °C).
- When **CE > 0.60**, **post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)** is mandatory.

In STV, AHSS (CE often 0.5–0.7) and ship-hull DH36 plates are highly CE-sensitive, making this metric essential for crash-safe, defect-free welds.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, describe the **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** process. Explain its sequence of operations and state any **four** important process parameters that govern weld quality in automotive BIW assembly.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] For an automotive production line, the RSW of a galvanized-steel joint uses: $t_1 = 1.2$ mm and $t_2 = 1.6$ mm, current $I = 9.5$ kA, weld time $t = 0.32$ s, interface resistance $R = 280 \; \mu\Omega$, and a duty cycle of 30 %. Calculate: (i) the heat generated per spot weld, (ii) the minimum acceptable nugget diameter as per the **4√t** rule, and (iii) the average power dissipation per spot.

### **Model Solution (a):**

**Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** is a *resistance-heating-based, fusion* welding process in which two or more sheets are fused together at discrete points (spots) by the heat generated from the **resistance to electric current flow** through the sheets, concentrated by water-cooled copper electrodes under pressure.

**Sequence of operations (6 stages):**

1. **Squeeze** — Electrodes close and apply force (no current).
