2. **Weld (Heat)** — Current passes, faying surfaces heat up, a molten nugget forms.
3. **Hold (Forge / Cool)** — Current off, electrodes hold to solidify the nugget.
4. **Off** — Electrodes retract; the spot is complete.
5. **Move to next location** — Indexing of the part or the welding head.
6. **Repeat** — Cycle time 0.5 – 1.5 s per spot on a robotized line.

**Four key process parameters governing weld quality:**
- **Welding current (I)** — 8–12 kA; governs nugget size; over-current → expulsion, under-current → stick weld.
- **Weld time (t)** — 0.2–0.5 s; longer time → bigger nugget but risk of overheating.
- **Electrode force (F)** — 3–6 kN; too low → splash, too high → cold joint.
- **Electrode geometry & material** — Class 2 Cu–Cr–Zr; dome/truncated-cone tip; governs current density and indent depth.

### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
$I = 9.5$ kA = $9{,}500$ A, $t_{weld} = 0.32$ s, $R = 280 \; \mu\Omega = 280 \times 10^{-6}$ Ω, $t_1 = 1.2$ mm, $t_2 = 1.6$ mm.

**(i) Heat generated per spot weld (Joule's law):**

$$Q = I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t_{weld}$$

$$Q = (9{,}500)^{2} \times 280 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.32$$

$$Q = 90{,}250{,}000 \times 280 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.32$$

$$Q = 90{,}250{,}000 \times 8.96 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 8{,}086 \; \text{J} \approx 8.09 \; \text{kJ per spot}}$$

**(ii) Minimum acceptable nugget diameter (4√t rule, $t$ = thinner sheet):**

$$d_{min} = 4\sqrt{1.2} = 4 \times 1.0954$$

$$\boxed{d_{min} = 4.38 \; \text{mm}}$$

**(iii) Average power dissipation per spot:**

$$P_{avg} = \frac{Q}{t_{weld}} = \frac{8{,}086}{0.32}$$

$$\boxed{P_{avg} = 25{,}269 \; \text{W} \approx 25.3 \; \text{kW per spot}}$$

If the line operates at a 30 % duty cycle (welding only 30 % of total time), the **time-averaged power** is:

$$P_{line,avg} = 25.3 \times 0.30 = 7.58 \; \text{kW}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** process with a suitable diagram. Discuss **four** advantages of FSW for manufacturing aluminium-intensive surface transport vehicles (ships, rail coaches, EVs).
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] In the MAG welding of a truck chassis, the operating parameters are: arc voltage $V = 30$ V, current $I = 280$ A, travel speed $v = 5.5$ mm/s, and arc efficiency $\eta = 0.78$. The carbon equivalent of the steel is 0.50.
> (i) Compute the heat input in kJ/mm.
> (ii) Predict the preheat temperature required.
> (iii) If the truck has 4.5 m of MAG weld and the plant produces 220 trucks/day, find the total weld-side energy consumed in kWh/day.

### **Model Solution (a):**

**Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** is a **solid-state** joining process developed by **The Welding Institute (TWI), UK, in 1991**. A *non-consumable* rotating tool consisting of a **shoulder** and a **pin (probe)** is plunged into the joint line between two clamped workpieces. The frictional heat generated softens (but does not melt) the material, and the tool's mechanical stirring action causes plastic flow, forging the two pieces together as the tool traverses the joint.

**Diagram description (text schematic):**

- **Tool shoulder (top)** rotates and applies downward forging force.
- **Pin (probe)** stirs the plasticized material.
- **Advancing side** — material flows from front to back.
- **Retreating side** — material flows opposite direction.
- **Stir zone (nugget)** — recrystallized fine-grain microstructure.
- **Thermo-mechanically affected zone (TMAZ)** — deformed grains.
- **Heat-affected zone (HAZ)** — no plastic deformation, only thermal cycle.

**Four advantages for STV aluminium manufacturing:**

1. **No melting → no solidification defects** — eliminates porosity, hot cracking, and liquation cracking in Al-Mg-Si (6xxx) alloys.
2. **Lower distortion** — heat input is much lower than arc welding (typical FSW HI ≈ 0.4–1.0 kJ/mm vs 1.0–2.5 for MAG).
3. **Excellent mechanical properties** — fine recrystallized grains give superior fatigue and tensile strength.
4. **No shielding gas or filler wire required** — environmentally cleaner, lower consumable cost (relevant in shipbuilding and rail).
5. *(Bonus)* — easily automatable / robotized for ship-deck panels and rail floor panels.

### **Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Heat input:**

$$HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$$

$$HI = \frac{0.78 \times 30 \times 280}{5.5 \times 1000}$$

$$HI = \frac{6{,}552}{5{,}500}$$

$$\boxed{HI = 1.191 \; \text{kJ/mm}}$$

**(ii) Preheat temperature (using $T_{pre} \approx 200 \cdot CE - 50$):**

$$T_{pre} = 200 \times 0.50 - 50 = 50^{\circ}\text{C}$$

In practice, EN 1011-2 recommends a **minimum preheat of 75 °C** to provide safety margin against hydrogen cracking. So the recommended preheat is **75 – 100 °C**.

**(iii) Total weld-side energy per day:**

$$L_{total} = 220 \times 4.5 = 990 \; \text{m} = 990{,}000 \; \text{mm}$$

$$E_{weld} = HI \times L_{total} = 1.191 \times 990{,}000 = 1{,}179{,}090 \; \text{kJ}$$

Convert to kWh:

$$E_{kWh} = \frac{1{,}179{,}090}{3{,}600}$$

$$\boxed{E_{kWh} = 327.5 \; \text{kWh/day}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Travel speed is often given in mm/min, not mm/s. **Always convert to mm/s** when using the heat-input formula.
> 2. **Mixing up $t$ (weld time) and $t$ (sheet thickness)** in RSW questions — the 4√t rule uses **sheet thickness**, not weld time.
> 3. **Using $\eta = 1$ (100% efficiency)** — KTU expects you to use the correct arc efficiency. 1.0 is *never* correct; it is 0.6–0.95 depending on the process.
> 4. **Forgetting to apply the thinner sheet rule** for spot-weld sizing — always use $t = \min(t_1, t_2)$.
> 5. **Skipping the 1000 in the heat-input formula** — $HI$ in kJ/mm requires dividing by 1000, otherwise the answer is 1000× too large.
> 6. **Carbon Equivalent formula errors** — Mn is divided by **6**, Ni/Cu by **15**, Cr/Mo/V by **5**. Memorize this!

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Surface Transport Vehicles (STVs)** = automobiles + rail + marine vehicles; STV industry is the **largest single consumer** of welded joints in the world.
- [x] **Body-in-White (BIW)** = unpainted car body; typically 2,500–4,500 spot welds; **25–30 %** of vehicle kerb weight.
- [x] **Primary processes:** RSW (body), MAG/GMAW (chassis), Laser (tailored blanks), FSW (aluminium), SAW (ship hull), TIG (exhaust).
- [x] **Spot weld nugget rule (AWS D8.9):** $d_{nugget} = 4\sqrt{t_{min}}$ where $t_{min}$ is the thinner sheet in mm.
- [x] **Heat input formula:** $HI = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v \times 1000}$ kJ/mm. **Units are critical** — $v$ must be in mm/s.
- [x] **Arc efficiency (η) values to memorize:** SAW 0.85–0.95, MMAW 0.70–0.85, GMAW-CO₂ 0.70–0.80, GTAW 0.40–0.60, Laser 0.30–0.45.
- [x] **Carbon Equivalent (IIW):** $CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$. CE > 0.45 → preheat mandatory.
- [x] **Preheat temperature (rule of thumb):** $T_{pre} \approx 200 \cdot CE - 50$ °C.
- [x] **AHSS (Advanced High-Strength Steel)** and **DP/TRIP/Martensitic steels** dominate modern crash-safety structures.
- [x] **Aluminium 5xxx/6xxx** → FSW or Laser (no porosity); cannot be RSW easily (high resistivity, oxide layer issues).
- [x] **Galvanized steel** → RSW requires **higher current** (≈ 10–15 % more) due to zinc coating, and frequent electrode tip dressing.
- [x] **EV battery enclosures** → laser-welded aluminium (hermetic seal, low heat input to protect cells).
- [x] **Ship hulls** (10–50 mm AH36) → SAW (high productivity, deep penetration); one-sided welding with ceramic backing.
- [x] **Weld-bonding** (adhesive + RSW) → 20 % higher stiffness, 50 % higher fatigue life vs RSW alone (used by BMW, Audi).
- [x] **NDT methods in STV:** Visual → Dye Penetrant → Ultrasonic → X-ray; SPC charts for spot-weld current monitoring.
- [x] **Common failure modes:** HAZ hydrogen cracking, lamellar tearing, fatigue at spot-weld toes, galvanic corrosion at Al-steel joints.
- [x] **SI units** for heat input = **kJ/mm**, not kJ/m (the latter is 1000× smaller, a common KTU answer-key trap).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joining of Advanced Materials: Joining of Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Joining of Advanced Materials: Joining of Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: JOINING OF ADVANCED MATERIALS — JOINING OF COMPOSITES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite Material (KTU Definition):** A heterogeneous material composed of two or more distinct constituent phases — a **matrix** (continuous phase) and a **reinforcement** (dispersed phase) — combined at a macroscopic level to produce a material with properties superior to those of its individual components.

For the purpose of joining, KTU 2024 Scheme classifies advanced composites into three primary families:

| Family | Abbrev. | Matrix | Reinforcement Examples | Typical Service |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Polymer Matrix Composite | PMC | Thermoset / Thermoplastic | Glass, Carbon, Aramid fibres | Aerospace, automotive, marine |
| Metal Matrix Composite | MMC | Al, Ti, Mg, Cu | SiC, Al₂O₃, B₄C, C fibres | Automotive, defence, electronics |
| Ceramic Matrix Composite | CMC | SiC, Al₂O₃, ZrO₂ | C, SiC fibres | Jet engines, thermal protection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Joining of composites** is defined as the science and engineering of creating a permanent or semi-permanent mechanical/chemical bond between two composite parts, or between a composite and a metallic/component, without compromising the in-service properties of the parent materials.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Concrete & Steel" Analogy

Imagine **concrete** (brittle, strong in compression) reinforced with **steel rods** (tough, strong in tension). You cannot simply weld the steel rods of two reinforced concrete slabs using conventional arc welding — the heat would crack the concrete, melt the steel interface unevenly, and destroy the bond between the two phases.

**Composites behave exactly like this.** They are deliberately designed with **two materials of different chemistry, melting points, and thermal expansion coefficients**. Applying a conventional welding arc is like trying to mend a brick wall with a blowtorch — the bricks crack, the mortar crumbles, and the joint is weaker than the original.

> [!TIP]
> **The KTU Exam Mantra:** "Composite joining is the art of transferring load across a *bond* without breaking the *bond within the parent material*." The reinforcement-matrix interface inside the composite is sacred — any joining process must preserve it.

### 1.3 Why Conventional Welding Fails on Composites — The Four Pillars of Difficulty

1. **Thermal Incompatibility** — Reinforcements (e.g., SiC) and matrices (e.g., Aluminium) have vastly different melting/softening points. The matrix melts while the reinforcement remains solid, creating a **viscous slurry** that cannot form a sound weld.
2. **Chemical Reactions at Interface** — High heat promotes **unwanted interfacial reactions** (e.g., Al–SiC reaction forms brittle Al₄C₃, which catastrophically degrades MMC strength).
3. **Reinforcement Damage** — Fibres can burn, oxidize, dissolve, or become misaligned during thermal cycling.
4. **Residual Stresses** — Mismatch in **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)** generates micro-cracks at the interface upon cooling.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Composite Joining

The following metrics are used universally in KTU 2024 Scheme question papers for grading joint performance:

- **Lap Shear Strength (LSS):** Measured in **MPa**, force per unit overlap area.
- **Joint Efficiency (η):** Expressed as a **%**, ratio of joint strength to base material strength.
- **Peel Strength:** Measured in **N/mm** or **N/cm**, resistance to cleavage loading.
- **Cure Temperature (T_cure):** Adhesive processing temperature in **°C**.
- **Glass Transition Temperature (T_g):** Critical polymer softening point in **°C**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Composite Joining Methods

The KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 4) groups all composite joining techniques into **three major categories**. Each method exploits either a *chemical* (adhesion), *mechanical* (fastening), or *thermal* (fusion / solid-state) bonding mechanism.

#### 2.1.1 Mechanical Fastening (Primary Load-Bearing Method)

Bolts, rivets, screws, and pins transfer load through **bearing stress** on the composite. Used where:
- Disassembly is required
- High peel/cleavage loads exist
- Joint must carry through-thickness loads

> **Why and How it Works:** The fastener shank pushes against the hole wall. The composite locally crushes in "bearing failure" mode. A clearance hole is mandatory, and the hole edge is the weakest point. **Why preferred for thermoset PMCs:** No thermal damage, no chemical attack, repeatable assembly.

#### 2.1.2 Adhesive Bonding (Most Common Method)

A polymeric adhesive (epoxy, phenolic, polyurethane, cyanate ester) is cured between two faying surfaces. Load is transferred through **shear in the adhesive layer**.

**Why and How it Works:**
- *Why:* Distributes stress over a wide area (eliminates stress concentrations inherent to fastener holes), adds **< 2 % weight**, seals against moisture, joins dissimilar materials, and dampens vibration.
- *How:* Surface preparation → adhesive application → assembly → cure under heat and pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Preparation is the #1 determinant of adhesive bond strength** in composite joining. KTU examiners frequently test this. The standard treatments are:
> 1. **Solvent degreasing** (acetone wipe) — removes release agents
> 2. **Abrasion** (scuff-sanding with 320-grit SiC paper)
> 3. **Plasma / Corona treatment** — raises surface energy for thermoset matrices
> 4. **Silanization** — applies a chemical coupling agent (e.g., 3-aminopropyl-triethoxysilane) for silica-based reinforcements

#### 2.1.3 Fusion & Solid-State Welding (Only for Thermoplastic Matrices and MMCs)

These are the methods closest to traditional welding but applied with extreme care:

| Process | Composite Type | Heat Source | Solid-State? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** | MMC (Al/SiC, Al/Al₂O₃) | Frictional + Plastic deformation | Yes |
| **Ultrasonic Welding** | Thermoplastic PMC | High-frequency vibration (20–40 kHz) | Yes (mostly) |
| **Induction Welding** | Thermoplastic PMC | Electromagnetic induction | No (fusion) |
| **Resistance Welding** | Thermoplastic PMC | Joule heating via embedded mesh | No (fusion) |
| **Hot Plate / Hot Tool Welding** | Thermoplastic PMC | Heated platen | No (fusion) |
| **Laser Transmission Welding** | Thermoplastic PMC | Laser through transparent part | No (fusion) |
| **TIG/MIG on MMCs** | MMC | Arc (rare, problematic) | No (fusion) |

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities below are testable in KTU 2024 University ESE and internal assessments. Master this table for Part A and Part B scoring.

| Symbol / Quantity | Formula / Expression | Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lap Shear Strength (LSS) | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{w \cdot L_{o}}$ | **MPa** | Shear stress in single-lap joint; $F$ = load, $w$ = width, $L_{o}$ = overlap length |
| Joint Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma_{joint}}{\sigma_{parent}} \times 100$ | **%** | Strength ratio of joint to base composite |
| Adhesive Shear Stress (Volkersen) | $\tau(x) = \dfrac{P \cdot \lambda}{2 w} \left[ \dfrac{\cosh(\lambda x)}{\sinh(\lambda L)} + \dfrac{\sigma_{th}}{\sigma_{0}} \right]$ | MPa | Distributed shear along lap; $L$ = overlap half-length, $\lambda$ = Goland–Reissner constant |
| Peel Load Capacity | $P_{peel} = w \cdot G_{a} \cdot \theta_{c}$ | **N** | Cleavage resistance; $G_{a}$ = adhesive shear modulus, $\theta_{c}$ = critical peel angle |
| Thermal Residual Stress (CTE mismatch) | $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu}$ | **MPa** | Residual stress from CTE mismatch; $E$ = modulus, $\Delta \alpha$ = CTE difference, $\Delta T$ = cooling range, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio |
| Bearing Stress (mechanical fastening) | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{F}{d \cdot t}$ | **MPa** | Bearing stress on hole; $d$ = fastener diameter, $t$ = laminate thickness |
| Goland–Reissner Bending Factor $k$ | $k = \dfrac{1}{1 + 10 t^{3} E / (L^{2} E_{ad} t_{ad})}$ | dimensionless | Accounts for bending of adherends in single-lap joints |
| Volkersen $\lambda$ | $\lambda^{2} = \dfrac{G_{a}}{t_{a}} \left( \dfrac{1 - \nu}{E t} \right)$ | $m^{-2}$ | Controls shear-lag distribution along the overlap |
| Heat Generation in FSW | $Q = \dfrac{2 \pi \mu F N r_{m}}{v}$ | **W** | Power input; $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $F$ = axial force, $N$ = rpm, $r_{m}$ = mean radius, $v$ = traverse speed |
| Rule of Mixtures (composite modulus) | $E_{c} = V_{f} E_{f} + (1 - V_{f}) E_{m}$ | **GPa** | Estimating effective elastic modulus; $V_{f}$ = fibre volume fraction |
| Optimal Overlap Length | $L_{o,opt} \approx 0.5 \cdot t \sqrt{\dfrac{E \cdot t_{a}}{G_{a}}}$ | **mm** | Minimizes peak shear for given adherend thickness |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility — Where These Methods Are Used

| Industry | Dominant Joining Method | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Commercial Aircraft (Boeing 787, Airbus A350) | Adhesive bonding + hybrid rivet-bonding (Hi-Lok / lock-bolt) | Fatigue life, weight saving, smooth aerodynamic skin |
| Wind Turbine Blades (50 m+ long) | Adhesive bonding of GFRP + CFM shells | Cannot be mechanically fastened over large area |
| Formula-1 Monocoque | Adhesive + Z-pinning (transverse reinforcement) | Crash energy absorption |
| Spacecraft Heat Shields (CMC) | Mechanical fastening with floating coupling | Tolerates ±600 °C thermal cycling |
| Automotive Battery Trays (CFRP-Al hybrid) | Friction stir welding at Al/PMC interface | Hermetic seal, structural strength |
| Helicopter Rotor Hubs (MMC) | Friction stir welded Al-SiC | Eliminates 1,200+ rivets, cuts weight by 30 % |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Volkersen Shear-Lag Analysis for Single-Lap Adhesive Joint

The **Volkersen (1938)** model is the foundational equation for shear stress distribution in a single-lap adhesive joint. The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently asks students to *derive* or *apply* this result in Part B.

#### 3.1.1 Physical Setup & Assumptions

Consider two isotropic adherends of equal thickness $t$ and width $w$, bonded with an adhesive of thickness $t_{a}$ and shear modulus $G_{a}$, over an overlap length $2L$ (half-length $L$). The applied tensile load is $P$.

**Assumptions (KTU standard):**
1. Adhesive carries *only* shear stress (no transverse normal or peel stress).
2. Adherend bending is neglected.
3. Adherends behave linear-elastically ($E$ constant).
4. Adhesive shear strain is linear through its thickness.

#### 3.1.2 Equilibrium of a Differential Adherend Element

Take a small element of the top adherend of length $dx$. The load changes by $dP$ due to shear transferred from the adhesive.

$$
\frac{dP}{dx} = \tau(x) \cdot w
$$

The adhesive shear strain $\gamma$ relates to the differential slip between the adherends:

$$
\gamma(x) = \frac{du_{1}}{dx} - \frac{du_{2}}{dx} = \frac{\tau(x)}{G_{a}}
$$

where $u_1$ and $u_2$ are the axial displacements of the top and bottom adherend respectively. Differentiating once more and using Hooke's law for the adherends ($P = E t w \cdot du/dx$):

$$
\frac{d^{2}P}{dx^{2}} = w \cdot \frac{d\tau}{dx} = \frac{G_a \cdot w}{t_{a}} \left( \frac{1}{E_{1} t_{1}} + \frac{1}{E_{2} t_{2}} \right) P
$$

For symmetric adherends ($E_1 t_1 = E_2 t_2 = E t$):

$$
\frac{d^{2}P}{dx^{2}} = \lambda^{2} P, \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda^{2} = \frac{2 G_a}{t_a E t}
$$

#### 3.1.3 General Solution and Boundary Conditions

The general solution of the second-order ODE is:

$$
P(x) = A \cosh(\lambda x) + B \sinh(\lambda x)
$$

Apply boundary conditions:
- At $x = -L$: $P = P$ (full applied load)
- At $x = +L$: $P = 0$ (free end of overlap)

Substituting both, we get:

$$
A = \frac{P}{2 \cosh(\lambda L)}, \quad B = \frac{P}{2 \sinh(\lambda L)}
$$

#### 3.1.4 Final Volkersen Shear Stress Distribution

$$
\tau(x) = \frac{1}{w} \frac{dP}{dx} = \frac{P \lambda}{2 w} \left[ \frac{\cosh(\lambda x)}{\sinh(\lambda L)} - \frac{\sinh(\lambda x)}{\cosh(\lambda L)} \right]
$$

This can be rewritten more compactly using a hyperbolic identity:

$$
\tau(x) = \frac{P \lambda}{2 w} \cdot \frac{\cosh(\lambda x)}{\sinh(\lambda L)} \quad \text{(symmetric form, common in KTU textbooks)}
$$

**Maximum shear stress occurs at the ends** ($x = \pm L$):

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{P \lambda}{2 w} \cdot \coth(\lambda L) \approx \frac{P \lambda}{2 w} \quad (\text{when } \lambda L > 4)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight (KTU favourite):** Because $\coth(\lambda L) \to 1$ for long overlaps, simply increasing the overlap length does **not** reduce peak stress once $\lambda L > 4$. This is why joints are designed with a *finite* optimal overlap, not an infinite one.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermal Residual Stress in a Composite Adhesive Joint

When a PMC and a metal are bonded at the cure temperature $T_{cure}$ and cooled to ambient $T_{amb}$, the CTE mismatch $\Delta \alpha = \alpha_{PMC} - \alpha_{metal}$ generates in-plane residual stress.

#### 3.2.1 Strain Compatibility (1D Model)

For a symmetric joint cooled by $\Delta T = T_{cure} - T_{amb}$, the total strain in each adherend is the sum of mechanical and thermal contributions:

$$
\epsilon_{metal} = \frac{\sigma_m}{E_m} + \alpha_m \Delta T
$$

$$
\epsilon_{PMC} = \frac{\sigma_p}{E_p} + \alpha_p \Delta T
$$

Force equilibrium demands the stresses balance across the bondline:

$$
\sigma_m \cdot t_m = \sigma_p \cdot t_p \quad \text{(equal-and-opposite forces)}
$$

#### 3.2.2 Solving for the Residual Stress

Strain compatibility at the bondline:

$$
\frac{\sigma_m}{E_m} + \alpha_m \Delta T = \frac{\sigma_p}{E_p} + \alpha_p \Delta T
$$

Substituting $\sigma_p = -\sigma_m (t_m / t_p)$ and rearranging:

$$
\sigma_m = \frac{(\alpha_p - \alpha_m) \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{E_m} + \dfrac{t_m}{t_p E_p}}
$$

The **classical closed-form result for equal thicknesses** ($t_m = t_p = t$) is:

$$
\sigma_{th} = \frac{E_m E_p}{E_m + E_p} \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

Accounting for plane-strain correction (Poisson's ratio $\nu$):

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{th} = \frac{E_m E_p \, \Delta \alpha \, \Delta T}{(E_m + E_p)(1 - \nu)}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **exact equation tabulated in Section 2.2**. For example, bonding a CFRP laminate ($\alpha_p = 0.2 \times 10^{-6}$/K, $E_p = 140$ GPa) to aluminium ($\alpha_m = 23 \times 10^{-6}$/K, $E_m = 70$ GPa) and cooling by $\Delta T = 120$ K yields a residual stress of approximately **61 MPa** — comparable to the adhesive shear strength itself.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Joint Efficiency for a Mechanically Fastened Composite

A single bolt in double-shear on a laminate of thickness $t$ with fastener diameter $d$. The joint efficiency in bearing is:

$$
\eta_{bearing} = \frac{F_{ult, joint}}{F_{ult, parent}} = \frac{\sigma_{b, allow} \cdot d \cdot t}{\sigma_{tens, parent} \cdot w \cdot t} = \frac{\sigma_{b, allow} \cdot d}{\sigma_{tens, parent} \cdot w}
$$

For a typical CFRP-epoxy laminate ($\sigma_{tens} = 1500$ MPa, $\sigma_{b, allow} = 700$ MPa, $d = 4$ mm, $w = 12$ mm):

$$
\eta_{bearing} = \frac{700 \cdot 4}{1500 \cdot 12} = \frac{2800}{18000} = 0.155 = 15.5\%
$$

This dramatic number illustrates why **mechanical fastening alone is rarely used** for primary composite structures — the joint efficiency is less than one-quarter of the parent material.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes in Composite Joints

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions often include a "compare the failure modes of adhesive vs. mechanical joints in a PMC." This matrix is the standard examiner reference.

| Failure Mode | Adhesive Joint | Mechanically Fastened Joint | Hybrid (Bonded + Bolted) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cohesive (in adhesive)** | Most common if bondline is correct thickness | N/A | Reduced — adhesive shared |
| **Adhesive (interfacial)** | Common if surface prep is poor | N/A | Reduced |
| **Cohesive in adherend (tear-out / net-tension)** | Possible if adhesive is *too strong* | **Most common** in single-bolt | Common |
| **Bearing** | N/A | **Most common** in multi-bolt | Common |
| **Cleavage / Peel** | Dominant at joint ends | Dominant at hole edges | Both reduced by 60–80 % |
| **Fatigue** | Crack propagation along bondline | Crack at hole edge | Hybrid gives 3–10× life |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a composite material. Why is the joining of composites fundamentally different from the welding of plain carbon steel?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **composite material** is a heterogeneous, multi-phase engineering material composed of a continuous **matrix phase** and a dispersed **reinforcement phase** (typically fibres, whiskers, or particles), combined to yield properties unattainable by either phase alone. The interface between matrix and reinforcement — the **interphase** — is the load-transferring region.

Joining of composites is fundamentally different from welding of carbon steel for four reasons:
1. **Presence of a second phase:** The reinforcement does not melt at the matrix softening point, so a conventional fusion weld cannot form a homogeneous pool.
2. **CTE mismatch:** Differential thermal contraction on cooling induces residual micro-cracks at the interface.
3. **Chemical reactivity:** Interfacial reactions (e.g., Al–SiC → Al₄C₃) degrade strength.
4. **Anisotropy:** Fibre-dominated properties mean joint design must respect fibre orientation; a butt joint across 0° fibres is far weaker than one across 45° fibres.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any three surface preparation techniques used before adhesive bonding of polymer matrix composites. Why is solvent wiping alone insufficient?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three standard techniques are:
1. **Solvent degreasing** (acetone or methyl-ethyl-ketone wipe) — removes mould-release agents, oils, and loose contamination.
2. **Mechanical abrasion** (320–400 grit SiC paper, Scotch-Brite pad) — creates micro-roughness for mechanical interlocking.
3. **Chemical / Plasma treatment** — raises surface free energy and introduces polar functional groups (-OH, -COOH) for chemical bonding.

Solvent wiping alone removes *only soluble* contamination; it cannot remove **silicone-based release agents** that have chemically bonded to the surface, cannot create micromechanical interlocking sites, and cannot raise the surface energy to the ≥ 50 mJ/m² required for adhesive wetting. A typical bonded joint loses 40–70 % of its lap shear strength if abrasion is omitted.

---

### 5.2 Part B — University ESE Question (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Composite Joining:**
> - **Do NOT** describe TIG/MIG welding of PMCs. It is **impossible** and examiners deduct full marks.
> - **Do NOT** confuse thermoplastic and thermoset matrices. Only thermoplastic composites can be fusion-welded. Thermoset composites *must* be adhesively bonded or mechanically fastened.
> - **Always** mention the four failure modes (cohesive, adhesive, substrate, mixed) when discussing joint strength.
> - Show units for every numerical answer; "MPa" and "%" are mandatory.
> - The Volkersen equation is the most-tested formula in this module. Memorize it.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Adhesive Bonding Focus*

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **Volkersen shear-lag model** for a single-lap adhesive joint in a PMC. Derive the expression for the shear stress distribution and state the boundary conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CFRP-epoxy laminate is bonded to an aluminium alloy 6061-T6 plate using a 0.15 mm thick epoxy film adhesive. The overlap length is 25 mm, joint width 25 mm, adhesive shear modulus $G_a = 1.2$ GPa, adherend modulus $E = 70$ GPa (Aluminium), adherend thickness $t = 2.0$ mm. Calculate the lap shear strength of the joint if the joint fails at a peak load of 8.5 kN. Comment on whether the joint efficiency is acceptable (CFRP parent tensile strength = 1500 MPa). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Schematic (1 mark):** Show two adherends with overlap region, coordinate $x$ from joint centre, applied load $P$, distributed shear $\tau(x)$.
2. **Assumptions (1 mark):** State all four Volkersen assumptions (see Section 3.1.1 above).
3. **Equilibrium equation (2 marks):** 
$$
\frac{dP}{dx} = \tau(x) w
$$
Differentiate and substitute Hooke's law for adherend + shear strain for adhesive to obtain
$$
\frac{d^2 P}{dx^2} = \lambda^2 P, \quad \lambda^2 = \frac{2 G_a}{t_a E t}
$$
4. **General solution and B.C. (1 mark):** Apply $P(-L) = P$ and $P(L) = 0$ to get
$$
P(x) = \frac{P \cosh(\lambda x)}{\cosh(\lambda L)} - \frac{P \sinh(\lambda x)}{\sinh(\lambda L)}
$$
5. **Final shear distribution (1 mark):**
$$
\tau(x) = \frac{P \lambda}{2 w} \left[ \frac{\cosh(\lambda x)}{\sinh(\lambda L)} \right], \quad \tau_{max} = \frac{P \lambda}{2 w} \coth(\lambda L)
$$
6. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Shear stress is maximum at the joint ends ($x = \pm L$) and zero at the centre. This explains why single-lap joints always initiate failure from the ends.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the Volkersen $\lambda$ parameter (2 marks):**

$$
\lambda^{2} = \frac{2 G_a}{t_a E t} = \frac{2 \times 1.2 \times 10^{9}}{0.15 \times 10^{-3} \times 70 \times 10^{9} \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\lambda^{2} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{9}}{2.1 \times 10^{7}} = 114.3 \;\; m^{-2}
$$

$$
\lambda = 10.69 \;\; m^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\lambda L$ (1 mark):**

Overlap is $2L = 25$ mm, so $L = 12.5$ mm = $0.0125$ m.

$$
\lambda L = 10.69 \times 0.0125 = 0.134
$$

Since $\lambda L \ll 4$, the joint is *short* and the $\coth(\lambda L)$ factor must be retained:

$$
\coth(0.134) = \frac{\cosh(0.134)}{\sinh(0.134)} = \frac{1.0090}{0.1345} = 7.502
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate peak shear stress (1 mark):**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{P \lambda}{2 w} \cdot \coth(\lambda L) = \frac{8500 \times 10.69}{2 \times 0.025} \times 7.502
$$

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{90{,}865}{0.050} \times 7.502 = 1.817 \times 10^{6} \times 7.502 = 13.63 \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Lap Shear Strength (LSS) (1 mark):**

The nominal lap shear strength is the average shear over the overlap area:

$$
LSS = \frac{F}{w \cdot L_o} = \frac{8500}{0.025 \times 0.025} = \frac{8500}{6.25 \times 10^{-4}} = 13.6 \times 10^{6} \;\; \text{Pa} = 13.6 \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Joint Efficiency and Comment (2 marks):**

Cross-section of the adherend: $A = w \cdot t = 25 \times 2.0 = 50 \;\; \text{mm}^2$

Stress in CFRP at failure load:

$$
\sigma_{CFRP} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{8500}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 170 \times 10^{6} \;\; \text{Pa} = 170 \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

Joint efficiency:

$$
\eta = \frac{170}{1500} \times 100 = 11.33\%
$$

**Comment:** A joint efficiency of ~11 % is **not acceptable** for primary aerospace structure (target ≥ 50 %). Remedies include (i) increasing overlap length, (ii) using a scarf or stepped-lap geometry, (iii) using a toughened epoxy adhesive with $G_a$ ≥ 0.5 GPa at room temperature, and (iv) adding a hybridization step (bonded + bolted) to improve peel resistance.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Advanced Welding Focus (MMC / Thermoplastic)*

**(a)** Explain why **friction stir welding (FSW)** is the preferred method for joining aluminium-based metal matrix composites (e.g., Al/SiC). List four microstructural defects commonly observed in fusion-welded MMCs and the FSW parameters that minimize them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermoplastic GFRP composite (polypropylene matrix, 30 wt% glass fibre) is to be joined to an identical laminate using **ultrasonic welding**. Describe the working principle, identify the three key process parameters and their effect on weld strength, and state the typical lap shear strength achievable. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Why FSW is preferred (4 marks):**

1. **Solid-state process** — peak temperature stays below the matrix melting point (typically 0.6–0.8 $T_m$ of Al). The SiC reinforcement does not dissolve, so the brittle Al₄C₃ interfacial reaction product is suppressed.
2. **No filler metal required** — eliminates the problem of reinforcement segregation at the weld pool.
3. **Microstructural refinement** — the rotating tool fragments and re-distributes SiC particles uniformly, sometimes increasing local hardness by 15–25 %.
4. **Low residual stress** — solid-state bonding means thermal gradients are much smaller than in arc welding, reducing distortion and CTE-induced cracking.

**Four fusion-weld defects and FSW remedies (3 marks):**

| Defect in MMC Fusion Weld | Cause | FSW Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| Porosity | Dissolution of H₂ in Al melt | Solid-state — no melting |
| Al₄C₃ formation at SiC/Al interface | Reaction above 700 °C | Tool limits T to 450–550 °C |
| Particle segregation to grain boundaries | Stirring by Marangoni flow | Mechanical mixing by tool pin |
| Solidification cracking | Wide mushy zone in MMC | Narrow, near-isothermal HAZ |

**Tool parameters that minimize defects:** tool rotation speed (typically 500–1500 rpm), traverse speed (50–500 mm/min), axial plunge depth (0.1–0.3 mm), and tool tilt angle (1–3°).

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Working principle (3 marks):**
In ultrasonic welding of thermoplastics, high-frequency electrical energy (20–40 kHz) is converted to mechanical vibration by a piezoelectric transducer. The vibration is transmitted through a **sonotrode (horn)** to the upper laminate. The stack is pressed against an anvil. Frictional heat at the interface (typically 80–200 °C) raises the polymer above its melting/softening range, and pressure consolidates the bondline. Bonding times are 1–4 seconds — among the fastest composite-joining methods.

**Three key process parameters and effects (3 marks):**

1. **Weld pressure (0.2–0.6 MPa):** Too low → insufficient consolidation and voids. Too high → fibre crushing and resin squeeze-out. Optimum maximizes LSS.
2. **Weld time (1–4 s):** Too short → insufficient heat for melting. Too long → polymer degradation and charring. LSS rises asymptotically and then falls.
3. **Amplitude (30–90 µm peak-to-peak):** Higher amplitude generates more frictional heat but can also damage the fibres. A typical optimum for PP-GF30 is ~50 µm at 35 kHz.

**Typical achievable LSS (1 mark):**
For PP-glass fibre laminates, ultrasonic welding typically produces lap shear strengths of **12–22 MPa**, corresponding to joint efficiencies of **65–80 %** of the parent laminate strength — substantially higher than adhesive bonding of the same material.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Joining of Composites**

- **Composite family dictates joining method.** Thermoset PMC → adhesive or mechanical. Thermoplastic PMC → fusion welding. MMC → friction stir or vacuum brazing. CMC → active brazing or mechanical with floating coupling.
- **The Volkersen equation** is the single most important formula in this module: 
$$
\tau(x) = \frac{P \lambda}{2 w} \cdot \frac{\cosh(\lambda x)}{\sinh(\lambda L)}
$$
Peak shear is at the joint ends, **not** the centre.
- **Joint efficiency benchmarks:** Adhesive — 30–60 %; Mechanical — 10–25 %; Hybrid bond-bolt — 60–80 %; Friction stir on MMC — 70–90 %; Ultrasonic on thermoplastic — 65–80 %.
- **Surface preparation is the #1 determinant of adhesive bond strength.** Always state "degrease → abrade → prime" in any bonding answer.
- **CTE mismatch generates residual stress** 
$$
\sigma_{th} = \frac{E_m E_p \, \Delta \alpha \, \Delta T}{(E_m + E_p)(1 - \nu)}
$$
which is comparable to adhesive strength and can cause pre-mature failure.
- **The four failure modes** for any composite joint: cohesive (in adhesive), adhesive (at interface), substrate (in adherend), and mixed.
- **Never** propose TIG/MIG/Gas welding of polymer matrix composites. It is physically impossible and you will receive zero marks.
- **Rule of Mixtures** is a quick first estimate for composite modulus: 
$$
E_c = V_f E_f + (1 - V_f) E_m
$$
- **Bearing stress formula** $\sigma_b = F / (d \cdot t)$ is required for all mechanical fastening numericals.
- **Friction stir welding** is a *solid-state* process; the matrix is plasticized, not melted — this is the key reason it works on MMCs.
- **Ultrasonic welding** is the fastest polymer composite joining method (1–4 s cycle time) but limited to small-area joints (< 200 mm²) and thermoplastic matrices only.
- **Cure temperature** of the adhesive must be ≥ 20 °C above the maximum service temperature for dimensional stability.
- **Failure surface inspection** is mandatory in KTU lab viva questions: cohesive failure (adhesive on both sides) = good bond; adhesive failure (clean substrate) = poor surface prep; substrate failure (composite fibre damage) = joint stronger than parent.
- **Glass transition temperature (T_g)** is the upper limit for a polymer-matrix composite in service; above $T_g$ the matrix softens and the joint fails.
- **Three major joining mechanism categories:** Mechanical, Adhesive, and Thermal (fusion or solid-state). Hybrid combinations are the modern industrial standard.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joining of Smart Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Joining of Smart Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart Materials (Intelligent/Responsive Materials):** Engineered materials that possess one or more properties (shape, stiffness, viscosity, electric resistance, magnetic susceptibility, etc.) that can be significantly altered in a controlled fashion by external stimuli such as stress, temperature, moisture, pH, electric or magnetic fields.

In the context of **PEPET741 (Advanced Welding and Metal Joining Techniques)**, smart materials are classified as **functional materials** whose performance envelope is governed by their **microstructural phase state**, **crystallographic orientation**, or **domain structure**. The phrase **"Joining of Smart Materials"** refers to the engineered assembly of two or more smart-material components (or joining a smart material to a conventional structural material) using mechanical, thermal, chemical, or hybrid joining processes while **preserving the stimulus-response functionality** of the base material in the joint region.

The principal classes of smart materials encountered in KTU 2024 syllabus include:

| Class | Representative Material | Stimulus | Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) | Nitinol (Ni-Ti), Cu-Zn-Al, Cu-Al-Ni | Temperature / Stress | Shape recovery (SME / TWSME) |
| Piezoelectric | PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate), PVDF | Electric field / Stress | Strain / Voltage generation |
| Magnetostrictive | Terfenol-D (Tb-Dy-Fe) | Magnetic field | Strain |
| Electrorheological (ER) / MR fluids | Carbonyl iron in oil | E / B field | Viscosity change |
| Shape Memory Polymer (SMP) | Polyurethane networks | Temperature | Shape recovery |
| Self-Healing | Microcapsule-based polymers | Damage (crack) | Autonomic repair |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Muscle-Bone" Analogy:** Imagine a robotic prosthetic hand. The **bones** are made from a regular metal like titanium (structural material), but the **fingers that bend and grip** are made from a *smart* material like Nitinol. When you heat the Nitinol finger with a small electric current, it "remembers" its original curved shape and bends — just like a human muscle contracting.

**Now, the welding problem:** If you try to *weld* this Nitinol finger to the titanium bone using a conventional arc, you essentially **burn away the very memory** that makes the finger smart. Joining smart materials is therefore less about achieving high joint strength and more about **performing surgery without killing the patient** — the smart functionality in the Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) and Fusion Zone (FZ) must remain intact.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Smart-Material Joining

- **Transformation Temperatures ($A_s, A_f, M_s, M_f$):** Austenite start/finish, Martensite start/finish temperatures — the **"personality"** of an SMA, with typical values in the range **$-100^\circ\text{C}$ to $+100^\circ\text{C}$**.
- **Heat Input ($E$):** Energy deposited per unit length, governed by **Eq. (2.1)**.
- **Intercritical Temperature Window ($\Delta T_{safe}$):** Allowed thermal excursion before permanent loss of functionality.
- **Functionality Retention Index ($\eta_R$):** Ratio of post-join response to as-received response, defined in **Section 2**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Central Joining Dilemma: Why Smart Materials Are Difficult to Weld

Smart materials, particularly **Shape Memory Alloys (SMAs)** like Nitinol, are highly sensitive to thermal cycling. The fundamental reasons conventional fusion welding fails:

1. **Phase Transformation Disruption:** Nitinol's functional behavior depends on a reversible solid-state phase change between **austenite (B2 cubic)** and **martensite (B19' monoclinic)** phases. Welding temperatures exceed the austenitizing temperature ($>A_f$), causing **compositional shifts (Ni loss via evaporation)** that depress the transformation temperature and **destroy the "memory"**.
2. **Sensitivity to Contamination:** Ti in Nitinol has a strong affinity for **O, N, H, C** — even a few hundred ppm degrades the ductility and shape-memory response.
3. **Grain Growth and Recrystallization:** Coarse grains in the HAZ degrade pseudoelastic response.
4. **Residual Stress Formation:** Differential thermal contraction between fusion zone and base metal causes locked-in stresses that bias the shape-memory response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Key Insight:** Smart-material joining is **not about strength alone**; it is a *functionality-preservation* problem. Examiners expect students to explicitly mention **temperature control, atmosphere control, and microstructural preservation**.

### 2.2 Joining Methods Classification for Smart Materials

**Step-by-Step logic decomposition:**

- **Step 1:** Identify the type of smart material (SMA, piezoelectric, magnetostrictive, etc.).
- **Step 2:** Determine the **critical transformation temperature** $T_{cr}$ below which the material remains functionally intact.
- **Step 3:** Select a process whose **peak interface temperature** $T_{peak} < T_{cr}$, or whose **dwell time** at $T > T_{cr}$ is short enough to prevent permanent property loss.
- **Step 4:** Choose a **shielding environment** (vacuum, high-purity Ar) to prevent interstitial contamination.
- **Step 5:** Apply a **post-join functional training cycle** (for SMAs: constraint annealing + thermal cycling to "educate" the joint).

### 2.3 Suitable Joining Processes for Smart Materials

| Process | Suitability | Critical Control Parameter | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** | Excellent for thin Nitinol wires/foils | Pulse shaping, peak power | HAZ still occurs |
| **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** | Excellent — vacuum is inherent | Accelerating voltage, beam current | Capital cost |
| **Tungsten Inert Gas (TIG/GTAW)** | Conditional — Ni-rich filler helps | Low heat input, Ar-purged backing | Contamination risk |
| **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** | Better than TIG — constricted arc | Keyhole mode stability | Still fusion-based |
| **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** | Excellent — solid-state | Tool rotation, traverse speed | Tool wear on Ti-rich materials |
| **Diffusion Bonding (DB)** | Excellent for SMA-to-SMA or SMA-to-metal | Bonding temperature, pressure, time | Long cycle time |
| **Ultrasonic Welding (USW)** | Excellent for thin foils/wires | Weld time, pressure, amplitude | Limited to thin sections |
| **Resistance Spot / Projection Welding** | Good for thin foils | Electrode force, weld time | Surface prep critical |
| **Brazing (vacuum, Au- or Ag-based filler)** | Good for ceramic piezoelectrics | Braze temperature $\ll A_f$ | Filler compatibility |
| **Adhesive Bonding (epoxy, silicone)** | Good for piezo/polymer smarts | Surface prep, cure cycle | Temperature limit $\sim 200^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **Mechanical (riveting, crimping, swaging)** | Universal | Joint geometry | Stress concentration |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the governing equations you must memorize for KTU ESE answers on smart-material joining.

| # | Quantity / Equation | Symbol | Typical Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Input (arc processes) | $E$ | J/mm | $E = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ |
| 2 | Voltage (arc) | $V$ | V | typically **8 – 24 V** for TIG |
| 3 | Current | $I$ | A | typically **30 – 150 A** for Nitinol |
| 4 | Travel speed | $v$ | mm/s | typically **2 – 10 mm/s** |
| 5 | Arc efficiency | $\eta$ | dimensionless | **0.6 (TIG)**, **0.8 (PAW)**, **0.3 (GMAW)** |
| 6 | Heat Input (resistance) | $E_R$ | J | $E_R = I^2 R \, t$ |
| 7 | Functionality Retention | $\eta_R$ | dimensionless | $\eta_R = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{recovered}^{post}}{\varepsilon_{recovered}^{as-received}}$ |
| 8 | Ni evaporation correction | $\Delta c_{Ni}$ | at.% | Empirical; depends on $E$ |
| 9 | Clausius–Clapeyron (transformation shift) | $\dfrac{dT_0}{d\sigma}$ | K/MPa | **$\sim 0.6$ K/MPa** for Nitinol |
| 10 | Transformation strain (max recoverable) | $\varepsilon_{rec}$ | dimensionless | up to **$\sim 8\%$** for single-crystal Nitinol |
| 11 | Diffusion Bonding time | $t_{DB}$ | s | $t_{DB} = \dfrac{k T x^2}{D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)}$ (simplified) |
| 12 | FSW peak temperature | $T_{peak}$ | °C | empirical, **$\le 0.8 T_m$** |
| 13 | Adiabatic shear-band criterion | $\beta$ | dimensionless | $\beta = \dfrac{\rho c_v \dot{\varepsilon} \Delta T}{\sigma_{flow}}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Pipe Rule Compliance:** For all absolute values in formulas above, e.g., $\vert T - T_0 \vert$, use **\vert** or **\mid** rather than the pipe character to prevent table breakage.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** SMA-actuated morphing wing skins joined to CFRP or aluminum spars.
- **Medical Devices:** Nitinol guidewires, stents, and orthodontic arches joined using **laser welding** in hermetically sealed assemblies.
- **MEMS / Robotics:** Piezoelectric stacks bonded to flexure structures via **diffusion bonding** or **conductive epoxy**.
- **Civil (Structural Health Monitoring):** PZT patches surface-bonded to bridges via **structural adhesives**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input for Arc-Based Joining of Nitinol

The **arc heat input** governs the HAZ size, the dwell time above $A_f$, and the resultant Ni loss. Derivation from energy conservation at the weld pool:

**Step 1 — Define the electrical power delivered by the arc:**

$$
P_{arc} = V \cdot I
$$

where $V$ is the arc voltage (volts) and $I$ is the welding current (amperes).

**Step 2 — Apply the arc transfer efficiency** $\eta$, which represents the fraction of electrical power actually delivered to the workpiece (the rest is lost to electrode cooling, spatter, radiation, and convection):

$$
P_{workpiece} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I
$$

**Step 3 — Define heat input per unit length of weld** as the workpiece power divided by the torch travel speed $v$ (mm/s):

$$
E = \frac{\eta \, V \, I}{v}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting representative KTU-board values** for Nitinol DCEN-TIG welding:

$$
E = \frac{(0.6)(12 \,\text{V})(60 \,\text{A})}{3 \,\text{mm/s}}
$$

$$
E = \frac{432 \,\text{W}}{3 \,\text{mm/s}}
$$

$$
E = 144 \,\text{J/mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to the safe limit.** For Nitinol, $T_{peak}$ in the HAZ must remain below approximately **$A_f + 50\,^\circ\text{C}$** to avoid excessive Ni evaporation. Empirical studies show $E_{safe} \le 200\,\text{J/mm}$ for **Nitinol sheet thickness $t \le 1$ mm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic for Step 4:** Multiplying volts × amperes gives watts (J/s); dividing by mm/s gives J/mm. The constant $\eta = 0.6$ is the **standard KTU convention** for non-pulsed TIG.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Functionality Retention Index $\eta_R$ After Joining

For an SMA, the recovered strain upon heating above $A_f$ is the **functional output**. Post-welding, this output diminishes due to microstructural disruption.

**Step 1 — Define as-recovered strain** (the "ideal" performance):

$$
\varepsilon_{rec}^{AR} = \varepsilon_{L} - \varepsilon_{T}
$$

where $\varepsilon_L$ is the strain after loading in martensite and $\varepsilon_T$ is the residual strain after unloading.

**Step 2 — Define post-weld recovered strain** measured from a tensile-recovery test on a welded specimen:

$$
\varepsilon_{rec}^{PW} = \varepsilon_{L}^{PW} - \varepsilon_{T}^{PW}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the retention index:**

$$
\eta_R = \frac{\varepsilon_{rec}^{PW}}{\varepsilon_{rec}^{AR}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration:** Suppose a base Nitinol wire recovers **6.0%** strain, but the welded wire recovers only **4.2%** under identical loading conditions:

$$
\eta_R = \frac{0.042}{0.060}
$$

$$
\eta_R = 0.70 \quad \text{(i.e., 70% retention)}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

- $\eta_R \ge 0.9$ → Excellent (typically diffusion bonding, EBW, FSW).
- $0.7 \le \eta_R < 0.9$ → Acceptable (laser or pulsed-TIG with strict shielding).
- $\eta_R < 0.7$ → Poor; joint must be relocated outside functional zone.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ni Evaporation Loss & Transformation Temperature Shift

**Step 1 — Empirical Ni loss model** for arc welds (from KTU-recommended standard reference data):

$$
\Delta c_{Ni} \approx k \cdot \frac{E}{t}
$$

where $k$ is an empirical coefficient ($\approx 0.04$ at.%-mm/J for TIG on Nitinol) and $t$ is the sheet thickness.

**Step 2 — Compute** for $E = 144\,\text{J/mm}$, $t = 1\,\text{mm}$:

$$
\Delta c_{Ni} = (0.04) \cdot \frac{144}{1}
$$

$$
\Delta c_{Ni} = 5.76 \,\text{at.\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the empirical Nitinol transformation-temperature sensitivity:**

$$
\Delta A_f \approx -100 \, \frac{^\circ\text{C}}{\text{at.\% Ni}}
$$

**Step 4 — Resulting shift:**

$$
\Delta A_f = (-100)(+5.76)
$$

$$
\Delta A_f = -576 \, ^\circ\text{C}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Step 4 is the "trap" answer KTU examiners test.** A student who mechanically computes $\Delta c_{Ni} = 5.76$ at.% and stops at "Ni was lost" **fails to demonstrate board-level understanding**. The correct interpretation is: *Such a large $\Delta A_f$ would drive $A_f$ far below ambient, completely destroying high-temperature shape memory; therefore, the heat input MUST be reduced by an order of magnitude (use $E \le 30\,\text{J/mm}$ with pulsed current).*

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Critical Pulse-On Time for Pulsed-TIG on Nitinol

To keep the HAZ above $A_f$ for the minimum time, use pulsed current with on-time $t_{on}$ chosen to limit Ni diffusion.

**Step 1 — Define thermal diffusion length** in time $t_{on}$:

$$
\delta_{th} = \sqrt{4 \alpha \, t_{on}}
$$

where $\alpha$ is thermal diffusivity ($\sim 4 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ for Nitinol).

**Step 2 — Require** $\delta_{th} \le t_{sheet}$:

$$
\sqrt{4 \alpha \, t_{on}} \le t_{sheet}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $t_{on}$:**

$$
t_{on} \le \frac{t_{sheet}^2}{4 \alpha}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate** for $t_{sheet} = 1\,\text{mm} = 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$:

$$
t_{on} \le \frac{(10^{-3})^2}{4 \cdot (4 \times 10^{-6})}
$$

$$
t_{on} \le \frac{10^{-6}}{16 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
t_{on} \le 0.0625 \,\text{s}
$$

$$
t_{on} \le 62.5 \,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Pulse frequency selection:** Combined with a typical off-time $t_{off} \approx 5 \cdot t_{on}$, the pulse frequency becomes:

$$
f = \frac{1}{t_{on} + t_{off}} = \frac{1}{6 t_{on}}
$$

$$
f = \frac{1}{6 \cdot 0.0625} \approx 2.67 \,\text{Hz}
$$

**Engineering takeaway:** **Low-frequency pulsed TIG at 2 – 5 Hz** with $t_{on} \le 60\,\text{ms}$ is the standard practice for welding Nitinol sheets without destroying functionality.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process-Selection Flowchart for Joining Smart Materials

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Smart Material] --> B{Type?}
    B -->|SMA - Nitinol| C[Determine Transformation Temps As, Af]
    B -->|Piezoelectric - PZT| D[Determine Curie Temp Tc]
    B -->|SMP - Polymer| E[Determine Glass Transition Tg]
    C --> F{Thickness?}
    F -->|<= 1 mm| G[Laser Welding or Pulsed TIG]
    F -->|1 to 3 mm| H[EBW or Plasma Arc]
    F -->|> 3 mm| I[FSW or Diffusion Bonding]
    D --> J[Brazing below Tc or Adhesive Bonding]
    E --> K[Adhesive or Ultrasonic Welding]
    G --> L[Shield with High Purity Argon]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> M[Verify Functionality Retention]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{eta R >= 0.85?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Accept Joint]
    N -->|No| P[Relocate Joint or Change Process]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Joining-Process Influence on SMA Microstructure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Process Inputs]
        I1[Heat Input E]
        I2[Shielding Atmosphere]
        I3[Travel Speed v]
    end

    subgraph HAZ[Heat Affected Zone]
        H1[Temperature Excursion dt]
        H2[Ni Evaporation]
        H3[Grain Coarsening]
    end

    subgraph FZ[Fusion Zone]
        F1[Cast Structure]
        F2[Interstitials O N H pickup]
        F3[Compositional Shift]
    end

    subgraph Output[Functional Output]
        O1[Transformation Temp Shift dT0]
        O2[Recoverable Strain epsilon rec]
        O3[Retention Index eta R]
    end

    I1 --> H1
    I1 --> H2
    I2 --> F2
    I3 --> H1
    H1 --> H3
    H2 --> O1
    F1 --> O3
    F3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Comparative Decision Matrix (Text-Based Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Joining Process Decision] --> B{Fusion Process Acceptable?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Section Thickness}
    B -->|No| D[Solid State Route]
    C -->|Thin Foil| E[Laser or Ultrasonic]
    C -->|Medium| F[EBW or FSW]
    C -->|Thick| G[FSW or Diffusion Bonding]
    D --> H{Geometry}
    H -->|Lap Joint| I[Resistance Spot or Adhesive]
    H -->|Butt Joint| J[FSW or DB]
    H -->|Wire to Terminal| K[Laser Spot or Crimping]
    E --> L[Verify Functionality Retention]
    F --> L
    G --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Post Join Training Cycle]
    M --> N[Final Acceptance]
```

### 4.4 HAZ Temperature-Time Profile Schematic

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Schematic:** When a Nitinol specimen is traversed by a moving heat source, the thermal cycle experienced by any point in the HAZ follows the classical **Rosenthal thin-plate solution**. The peak temperature $T_p$ at distance $y$ from the weld centerline decays as:

$$
T_p(y) - T_0 = \frac{E}{2 \pi \lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{y} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{v y}{2 \alpha}\right)
$$

where $\lambda$ is thermal conductivity, $\alpha$ thermal diffusivity, $v$ travel speed, and $T_0$ the preheat. The boundary condition for safe operation is $T_p(y_{HAZ}) \le A_f + 50\,\text{K}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Define Shape Memory Alloys (SMAs). Why is conventional arc welding generally unsuitable for joining Nitinol without modification?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** is a metallic material that can recover its original shape upon heating above a characteristic transformation temperature, after being deformed in its low-temperature martensitic phase — a phenomenon termed the **Shape Memory Effect (SME)**. The most common engineering SMA is **Nitinol**, an equiatomic Ni-Ti intermetallic.

Conventional arc welding is unsuitable for unmodified Nitinol joining because:

1. **Thermal excursion destroys the B2 austenite ↔ B19' martensite transformation** that is the basis of SME.
2. **Ni evaporates preferentially** at arc temperatures, shifting composition and depressing the transformation temperature $A_f$ far below the operating range.
3. **Interstitial contamination** by O, N, H, and C from ambient atmosphere embrittles the joint.
4. The large **HAZ** experiences grain coarsening, which degrades pseudoelastic response and fatigue life.

*Mark distribution (3):* Definition — 1 mark; thermal disruption — 1 mark; contamination — 1 mark.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** List any three solid-state joining processes recommended for smart materials. State one specific advantage of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Process | Specific Advantage for Smart Materials |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** | Peak temperature $\le 0.8\,T_m$ (solid-state); avoids fusion, preserves SMA transformation cycle. |
| 2 | **Diffusion Bonding (DB)** | Bonding temperature can be kept **below $A_f$** with sufficient pressure-time product; near-net-shape joints. |
| 3 | **Ultrasonic Welding (USW)** | Joining occurs in **< 1 second** at low bulk temperature; ideal for thin Nitinol foils and wire terminations. |

*Mark distribution (3):* One mark per correctly identified process + advantage.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with a typical split **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Joining Process Selection & Quantitative Heat-Input Control*

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various joining processes suitable for **Nitinol (Ni-Ti)** Shape Memory Alloys, classifying them into fusion, solid-state, and mechanical categories. Highlight the critical process parameters that must be controlled to preserve the shape-memory functionality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **1.2 mm thick** Nitinol sheet is to be joined using pulsed DCEN-TIG welding. The selected parameters are: $V = 11\,\text{V}$, $I_{peak} = 80\,\text{A}$, pulse on-time $t_{on} = 50\,\text{ms}$, pulse off-time $t_{off} = 250\,\text{ms}$, and torch travel speed $v = 2.5\,\text{mm/s}$. Arc efficiency $\eta = 0.6$. Compute:
- (i) the effective heat input $E$ in J/mm, and
- (ii) the duty cycle and pulse frequency. Comment on whether the chosen parameters will preserve functionality, given the empirical safe limit $E_{safe} \le 200\,\text{J/mm}$ for this thickness. **(7 marks)**

##### **MODEL SOLUTION**

**(a) Detailed Discussion — Joining Processes for Nitinol (7 marks)**

**1. Fusion Processes (3 marks):**

- **Pulsed DCEN-TIG with high-purity Ar shielding and backing-gas:** Most widely used. Pulsing limits HAZ; backing gas prevents root-side oxidation. Requires Ni-rich filler (e.g., Ni-56 at.% Ti) to compensate for Ni loss.
- **Laser Beam Welding (LBW), Nd:YAG or fiber laser:** Low HAZ, autogenous, suitable for thin foils (50 – 500 µm). Pulse shaping (ramp-up/ramp-down) reduces thermal shock.
- **Electron Beam Welding (EBW):** Performed in vacuum — inherently oxygen-free. Highest depth-to-width ratio; suitable for aerospace-quality joints.
- **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW):** Constricted arc gives higher energy density than TIG; better for medium thickness (2 – 5 mm).

**2. Solid-State Processes (2.5 marks):**

- **Friction Stir Welding (FSW):** Tool rotation locally plasticizes the material below $T_m$; no melting. Peak $T \le 0.8\,T_m$. Preserves B2 austenite. Tool material choice critical (W-Re or polycrystalline diamond).
- **Diffusion Bonding (DB):** Bonding at $T = 0.6\,T_m$ to $0.8\,T_m$, with high pressure in vacuum. No HAZ. Excellent for SMA-to-SMA and SMA-to-metal joints. Long cycle (1 – 4 hours).
- **Ultrasonic Welding (USW):** No external heat; frictional heat at interface. Ideal for wires (< 2 mm dia.) and foils.
- **Explosion Welding:** For cladding Nitinol onto conventional steels.

**3. Mechanical Processes (1.5 marks):**

- **Crimping, Swaging, Riveting:** No thermal input; preserves all functional properties. Limited to non-critical load paths.
- **Bolted Joints:** Universal but introduce stress concentrations that may bias the SME response.

**Critical Control Parameters (compiled):**

- Heat input $E \le 200\,\text{J/mm}$ for $t \le 1$ mm; reduce proportionally with thickness.
- Shielding gas: **> 99.999% Ar**, both top and back; O$_2$ content **< 50 ppm**.
- Interpass temperature $< 100\,^\circ\text{C}$.
- Post-weld **shape-memory training** cycle (constraint anneal at $500\,^\circ\text{C}$ for 30 min + 100 thermal cycles).

**(b) Quantitative Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $V = 11\,\text{V}$, $I_{peak} = 80\,\text{A}$, $t_{on} = 50\,\text{ms} = 0.050\,\text{s}$, $t_{off} = 250\,\text{ms} = 0.250\,\text{s}$, $v = 2.5\,\text{mm/s}$, $\eta = 0.6$.

**Step 1 — Compute mean current during pulsing:**

The mean current delivered to the workpiece over a full cycle is:

$$
I_{mean} = I_{peak} \cdot \frac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}}
$$

$$
I_{mean} = 80 \cdot \frac{0.050}{0.050 + 0.250}
$$

$$
I_{mean} = 80 \cdot \frac{0.050}{0.300}
$$

$$
I_{mean} = 80 \cdot 0.1667
$$

$$
I_{mean} \approx 13.33 \,\text{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute effective heat input:**

$$
E = \frac{\eta \, V \, I_{mean}}{v}
$$

$$
E = \frac{(0.6)(11)(13.33)}{2.5}
$$

$$
E = \frac{87.98}{2.5}
$$

$$
E \approx 35.2 \,\text{J/mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute duty cycle:**

$$
D = \frac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}} = \frac{0.050}{0.300} = 0.1667 \quad \text{or} \quad 16.67\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute pulse frequency:**

$$
f = \frac{1}{t_{on} + t_{off}} = \frac{1}{0.300} \approx 3.33 \,\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — Functionality verdict:**

Since $E = 35.2\,\text{J/mm} \ll E_{safe} = 200\,\text{J/mm}$, the chosen parameters will **preserve the shape-memory functionality** with a wide safety margin. The pulse frequency of $\sim 3.3$ Hz is in the recommended **2 – 5 Hz** range, and the duty cycle of 16.67% ensures the HAZ cools between pulses, limiting cumulative Ni evaporation.

*Mark split:* (i) Computation — 3 marks; (ii) Duty + frequency — 2 marks; (iii) Functionality comment with safe-limit justification — 2 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice — Piezoelectric Bonding, Adhesives & Diffusion Bonding*

**(a)** Explain the challenges involved in joining **piezoelectric ceramics (e.g., PZT)** to metallic substrates. Discuss the suitability of **vacuum brazing** and **conductive-epoxy adhesive bonding** for this application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two Nitinol sheets each of **0.8 mm thickness** are to be diffusion-bonded. The bonding temperature is $T = 850\,^\circ\text{C}$, applied pressure $P = 10\,\text{MPa}$, and an activation energy for interfacial diffusion $Q = 140\,\text{kJ/mol}$. Assuming the diffusion coefficient follows $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ with $D_0 = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ and $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol\cdot K)}$, compute:
- (i) the diffusion coefficient $D$ at $850\,^\circ\text{C}$, and
- (ii) the characteristic diffusion length $\ell = \sqrt{D \cdot t}$ for a bonding time of **45 minutes**, and state whether full-interface bonding is expected. **(7 marks)**

##### **MODEL SOLUTION**

**(a) Challenges in Joining PZT to Metals (7 marks)**

**Material Nature of PZT:**

PZT is a **brittle perovskite ceramic** with a Curie temperature $T_C \approx 200 - 350\,^\circ\text{C}$ (composition-dependent). Above $T_C$, the piezoelectric domains randomize and functionality is **irreversibly lost**.

**Key Challenges:**

1. **Thermal-expansion mismatch:** CTE of PZT ($\sim 4 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{K}^{-1}$) differs markedly from typical metals (steel $\sim 12 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{K}^{-1}$); residual stresses at the interface can crack the ceramic.
2. **Curie-temperature ceiling:** Any process exposing PZT above $T_C$ destroys piezoelectricity.
3. **Brittleness:** Mechanical fastening causes chipping; fusion welding is impossible.
4. **Poling degradation:** PZT must be electrically *poled* (DC field $\sim 3\,\text{kV/mm}$ at elevated $T$) to align dipoles; heating above $T_C$ randomizes them.
5. **Surface preparation:** Both ceramic and metal must be metallized (e.g., Ag or Ni sputtering) for reliable brazing.

**Vacuum Brazing (3 marks):**

- **Suitability:** High — conducted in vacuum (no oxidation), with low-melting Ag-Cu (e.g., Ag-28%Cu, eutectic at $780\,^\circ\text{C}$) or Au-based fillers that remain **below $T_C$** of most PZT grades.
- **Advantage:** Strong, hermetic joints; compatible with metallized ceramic.
- **Limitation:** $T$ must be monitored with a thermocouple; filler must wet metallized layer.

**Conductive-Epoxy Adhesive Bonding (2 marks):**

- **Suitability:** Excellent for **low-temperature applications** ($\le 150\,^\circ\text{C}$). Epoxies loaded with Ag flakes give conductivity for electrical grounding.
- **Advantage:** Room-temperature cure, no thermal stress, accommodates CTE mismatch via elastic compliance.
- **Limitation:** Outgassing in vacuum; lower mechanical strength; temperature ceiling typically $\sim 200\,^\circ\text{C}$.

**Other suitable processes (brief mention):** Anodic bonding (for Si-based piezo MEMS); solder bonding with low-melting Bi-Sn alloys.

**(b) Diffusion-Bonding Calculations (7 marks)**

**Given:** $T = 850\,^\circ\text{C} = 850 + 273 = 1123\,\text{K}$, $D_0 = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $Q = 140{,}000\,\text{J/mol}$, $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol\cdot K)}$, $t = 45\,\text{min} = 2700\,\text{s}$.

**Step 1 — Compute exponent:**

$$
\frac{Q}{RT} = \frac{140{,}000}{(8.314)(1123)}
$$

$$
\frac{Q}{RT} = \frac{140{,}000}{9336.6}
$$

$$
\frac{Q}{RT} \approx 14.998 \approx 15.00
$$

**Step 2 — Compute diffusion coefficient:**

$$
D = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)
$$

$$
D = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \exp(-15.00)
$$

$$
D = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 3.059 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
D \approx 3.67 \times 10^{-12} \,\text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute diffusion length:**

$$
\ell = \sqrt{D \cdot t}
$$

$$
\ell = \sqrt{(3.67 \times 10^{-12})(2700)}
$$

$$
\ell = \sqrt{9.91 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
\ell \approx 9.95 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{m}
$$

$$
\ell \approx 99.5 \,\mu\text{m} \approx 0.10 \,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

For a **0.8 mm** thick sheet, the surface roughness of typical Ni-Ti is $R_a \approx 0.2 - 1.0\,\mu\text{m}$, far below $\ell \approx 100\,\mu\text{m}$. The diffusion length is **ample** to close interfacial voids by creep + diffusion, so **full-interface bonding is expected** within 45 minutes.

*Mark split:* (i) Diffusion coefficient — 3 marks; (ii) Diffusion length — 3 marks; (iii) Bonding feasibility statement — 1 mark.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in "Joining of Smart Materials" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $A_s, A_f, M_s, M_f$:** Examiners WILL deduct 1 mark each if you mis-label austenite-start vs. austenite-finish. Mnemonic: **"S = Start, F = Finish; A = Austenite (hot), M = Martensite (cold)."**
> 2. **Forgetting to apply $\eta$ in heat-input problems:** Writing $E = VI/v$ without the arc efficiency is the **single most common KTU valuation error** in this module.
> 3. **Treating "shape memory" as "elastic deformation":** It is a **diffusionless, solid-state phase transformation** — do not describe it as spring-back.
> 4. **PZT joining — ignoring $T_C$:** Any process that heats PZT above its Curie temperature permanently destroys piezoelectricity; mention this explicitly.
> 5. **Diffusion bonding — units of $D$:** Use **m²/s** consistently; do not mix cm²/s.
> 6. **Filler selection in brazing:** State the filler composition (e.g., BAg-8, Au-18Ni) — vague answers like "silver brazing" lose marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PEPET741 — Joining of Smart Materials**

- **Definition:** Smart materials respond reversibly to external stimuli; joining must preserve *functionality*, not merely *strength*.
- **Principal classes:** SMAs (Nitinol), piezoelectrics (PZT), magnetostrictives (Terfenol-D), SMPs, ER/MR fluids, self-healing polymers.
- **Why conventional welding fails SMAs:** Ni evaporation, interstitial pickup, HAZ phase disruption, grain coarsening.
- **Critical temperatures:** $A_s, A_f, M_s, M_f$ (SMA); $T_C$ (piezoelectric); $T_g$ (SMP); $T_m$ (reference for solid-state processes).
- **Heat-input formula:** $E = \eta V I / v$ — always include $\eta$.
- **Functionality retention index:** $\eta_R = \varepsilon_{rec}^{PW} / \varepsilon_{rec}^{AR}$; target $\ge 0.85$.
- **Pulse-TIG parameters for Nitinol:** $E \le 200\,\text{J/mm}$ (1 mm sheet); $t_{on} \le 60\,\text{ms}$; frequency 2 – 5 Hz; duty $\le 20\%$.
- **Shielding:** 99.999% Ar, O$_2$ < 50 ppm, mandatory backing-gas on root side.
- **Solid-state preference order for SMAs:** **FSW ≈ DB ≈ EBW > Pulsed-TIG > USW > LBW > Mechanical** (depends on geometry).
- **Piezo joining:** Keep $T < T_C$; use vacuum brazing with Ag-Cu or Au-based filler; or use Ag-loaded conductive epoxy if service $T \le 150\,^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Diffusion bonding:** $T \approx 0.6 - 0.8\,T_m$; $\ell = \sqrt{Dt}$ must exceed surface roughness $R_a$.
- **Ni evaporation rule of thumb:** $\Delta A_f \approx -100\,^\circ\text{C}$ per at.% Ni lost above stoichiometric 50%.
- **Post-join training:** Constraint anneal $\sim 500\,^\circ\text{C}$ / 30 min + 100 thermal cycles restores SME in FZ/HAZ.
- **Crystallographic structures to remember:** B2 (cubic austenite) $\leftrightarrow$ B19' (monoclinic martensite) for Nitinol; perovskite tetragonal/rhombohedral for PZT.
- **Common KTU pitfall:** Forgetting $\eta$ in $E$ — costs 1 mark per occurrence.
- **Maximum recoverable strain:** $\varepsilon_{rec} \le 8\%$ (single-crystal Nitinol); polycrystalline typically 4 – 6%.
- **Memory of recovery:** Always pair the heat-input value with the corresponding *sheet thickness* — examiners penalize dimensionless statements.
- **Engineering applications:** Aerospace morphing wings (SMA-TIG/FSW), medical stents (laser welding under Ar), MEMS actuators (diffusion bonding or epoxy), civil SHM (adhesive PZT patches).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joining of Nanomaterials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET741 - ADVANCED WELDING AND METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Joining of Nanomaterials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Joining of Nanomaterials** is defined as the controlled, atomically-precise bonding process used to create mechanical, electrical, thermal, or chemical continuity between two or more nano-scale structural entities (such as **carbon nanotubes, graphene sheets, nanowires, nanoparticles, or nanofilms**) without significantly disrupting their intrinsic nano-scale properties.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this field sits at the convergence of *nanotechnology*, *materials science*, and *manufacturing engineering*, addressing the assembly challenges of sub-100 nm features where conventional metallurgical bonding assumptions (bulk diffusion, macroscopic surface tension, equilibrium phase formation) often break down.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024):** "Joining of nanomaterials encompasses all techniques — physical, chemical, mechanical, or thermal — capable of producing robust interconnections between 0D, 1D, 2D, or 3D nanostructures while preserving their characteristic quantum, electrical, optical, or mechanical behaviour."

### 1.2 Nanomaterial Classification (Recap)

Nanomaterials are classified by the dimensionality of their confined electron motion:

| Dimensionality | Type | Typical Examples | Typical Size Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0-D** | Quantum dots, nanoparticles | Au nanoparticles, quantum dots | 1–100 nm (all directions) |
| **1-D** | Nanowires, nanotubes, nanorods | CNTs, Si nanowires | Diameter **< 100 nm** |
| **2-D** | Nanosheets, nanofilms | Graphene, MoS₂, h-BN | Thickness **< 100 nm** |
| **3-D** | Bulk nanostructured materials | Nanocrystalline Al, MOFs | Grain size **< 100 nm** |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine building a **delicate sandcastle on a beach** — but each grain of sand behaves like an entire castle on its own, and touching them the wrong way causes them to either crumble or fuse into useless glass.

That is exactly what **joining nanomaterials** is like:

- **Conventional welding** is like melting an iceberg with a blowtorch — you have plenty of mass, so the process is forgiving.
- **Nanomaterial joining** is like trying to solder two spider webs together with a candle — even the slightest heat, vibration, or contamination can destroy the very property you wanted to keep.

So the engineer must choose techniques that deliver **just enough energy to bond**, but **not enough to destroy**.

### 1.4 Why Joining Nanomaterials is Fundamentally Different

> [!NOTE]
> Standard textbook welding assumes:
> 1. Continuum mechanics holds (true when grain size *≪* component size)
> 2. Surface area/volume ratio is moderate
> 3. Phases are bulk-equilibrium
>
> At the nano-scale, **all three assumptions fail**, which is why new joining science is required.

The driving differences are summarised below:

| Macro-Welding Assumption | Nano-Scale Reality |
|---|---|
| Melting point is a fixed material constant | Melting point **decreases** sharply with size (Gibbs-Thomson effect) |
| Surface oxides are removed easily | Native oxide layer can be **thicker than the nanowire itself** |
| Diffusion follows Fick's laws | Surface diffusion dominates bulk diffusion |
| Joint strength ≈ parent strength | Joint strength often **exceeds** parent strength (defect-free interfaces) |
| Joint is invisible in device | Joint **IS** the device (single-CNT transistor) |

### 1.5 Engineering Significance (Standard Metric Callout)

The characteristic metric used to evaluate nanomaterial joints is the **Joint Efficiency Ratio (JER)**:

$$\eta_{JER} = \frac{\sigma_{joint}}{\sigma_{parent}}$$

where $\sigma_{joint}$ and $\sigma_{parent}$ are the mechanical strengths of the joint and parent nanostructure respectively.

A value **> 1** is possible in nano-joining because the joint zone can be defect-free single crystal, while the parent nanomaterial contains inherent vacancies and grain boundaries. This is the **opposite** of macro-welding where JER is always **< 1**.
